topics.title: Definitions of Monomer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions of Monomer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition of a Monomer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monomer (IUPAC-aligned working definition):** A *monomer* is a small, low-molecular-weight, reactive molecule possessing **one or more functional groups** capable of forming covalent linkages with itself or with other molecules under suitable polymerisation conditions, thereby generating a **macromolecule (polymer)** whose constitutional repeating units are derived from the original monomer(s).

The term originates from Greek: **mono** = one, **meros** = part — literally, *a single part* that joins with many identical or complementary parts to build a *poly* (many) meric structure.

Mathematically expressed, a monomer is denoted as $M$ (or $M_i$), and the polymerisation process converts $n$ monomer units into a polymer chain:

$$nM \longrightarrow (M)_n$$

where:
- $M$ = monomer molecule
- $n$ = degree of polymerisation ($\overline{DP_n}$)
- $(M)_n$ = polymer chain containing $n$ repeat units

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The LEGO Brick

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Imagine a **LEGO brick** as a monomer. A single brick is small, simple, and on its own has no purpose as a structure. But when thousands of bricks (monomers) click together through their studs (functional groups), they build a wall, a tower, or an entire building (a polymer). The *shape of the stud* on the brick is its **functionality** — it dictates how many neighbours one brick can connect to, which in turn decides whether the final structure will be a *linear chain* (like a row of bricks), a *branched tree*, or a *3D crosslinked network*.

## 1.3 Why "Definitions" Carries Plural Weight in KTU

KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate that "monomer" is **not a one-line definition**. It is a **taxonomic concept** with layered classifications:

| Layer | Classification Axis |
|---|---|
| **L1** | Based on **functionality** (number of reactive sites) |
| **L2** | Based on **chemical structure** (vinyl, diene, cyclic, etc.) |
| **L3** | Based on **origin/source** (natural vs synthetic vs bio-based) |
| **L4** | Based on **polymerisation behaviour** (condensation vs addition) |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU ESE Part-B answers, *always* present the definition first, then immediately back it up with a **classification table + a numerical example**. This single habit can elevate a 7-mark answer to a full-mark one.

## 1.4 The Concept of **Functionality (f)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functionality** is the *number of reactive sites (functional groups)* on a single monomer molecule that can participate in polymerisation. It is the single most important descriptor of a monomer's behaviour.

- A monomer with **f = 1** is **monofunctional** — can only terminate a chain (a chain stopper).
- A monomer with **f = 2** is **difunctional** — yields **linear** polymers.
- A monomer with **f = 3** is **trifunctional** — yields **branched or lightly crosslinked** polymers.
- A monomer with **f ≥ 3** is **polyfunctional** — yields highly crosslinked, network (thermoset) polymers.

This parameter directly governs the polymer's **topology, solubility, mechanical strength, and thermal behaviour**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Expanded Classification of Monomers

### A. Classification Based on **Functionality (f)**

This is the **single most-tested classification in KTU** as it directly predicts polymer architecture.

| Functionality (f) | Monomer Type | Example Monomer | Resulting Polymer Topology |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **f = 1** | Monofunctional | Acrylic acid (CH₂=CH–COOH), styrene (chain stopper) | No polymer — only chain termination |
| **f = 2** | Difunctional | Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂), hexamethylene diamine | Linear / straight-chain polymer |
| **f = 2** | Difunctional (bifunctional) | Glycol + dicarboxylic acid (AA + BB type) | Linear condensation polymer |
| **f = 3** | Trifunctional | Glycerol (3 –OH groups), 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid | Branched polymer |
| **f ≥ 4** | Polyfunctional | Pentaerythritol C(CH₂OH)₄, divinylbenzene (when both vinyls react) | Crosslinked, network, thermoset polymer |

### B. Classification Based on **Chemical Structure**

| Monomer Family | General Formula | Reactive Group | Example Monomers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vinyl monomers** | CH₂=CH–X | C=C double bond | Ethylene, styrene, vinyl chloride, methyl methacrylate |
| **Diene monomers** | CH₂=C(R)–CH=CH₂ | Two conjugated C=C bonds | Butadiene, isoprene, chloroprene |
| **Cyclic monomers** | Ring (3–7 membered) | Ring strain opens on polymerisation | ε-Caprolactam (→ nylon-6), ethylene oxide, tetrahydrofuran |
| **Bifunctional/AA–BB** | HO–R–OH and HOOC–R′–COOH | –OH and –COOH pairs | Ethylene glycol + adipic acid → PET |
| **Acrylic monomers** | CH₂=CH–COOR | C=C + ester | Methyl acrylate, butyl acrylate |
| **Aromatic monomers** | C₆H₅–X | Ring + functional group | Phenol (–OH), aniline (–NH₂) |

### C. Classification Based on **Origin / Source**

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural monomers** are derived from living organisms (plants, animals, microbes).
> **Synthetic monomers** are produced by industrial chemical processes.
> **Bio-based monomers** are synthetically produced but sourced from renewable biological feedstocks (green chemistry).

| Origin | Examples | Polymer Produced |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural** | Glucose, amino acids, isoprene, lactic acid | Starch, proteins, natural rubber, PLA |
| **Synthetic (petrochemical)** | Ethylene, styrene, vinyl chloride, butadiene | PE, PS, PVC, polybutadiene |
| **Bio-based** | Bio-ethylene (from sugarcane ethanol), bio-BDO | Bio-PE, bio-PBS |

## 2.2 The Functionality Theorem (Carothers' Insight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carothers' rule (1931):** For a system of monomers, **the average functionality $\overline{f}$** determines the polymer architecture *before* any reaction occurs.
>
> $$\overline{f} = \frac{\sum N_i \, f_i}{\sum N_i}$$
>
> where:
> - $N_i$ = number of moles of monomer *i*
> - $f_i$ = functionality of monomer *i*
> - $\sum N_i$ = total number of monomer moles

### Decision Boundaries (Critical for KTU)

| Condition | Predicted Architecture |
|:---:|---|
| $\overline{f} < 2$ | **No polymerisation possible** — only small molecules form |
| $\overline{f} = 2$ (exactly) | **Linear polymer** — chain growth terminates naturally |
| $2 < \overline{f} < 3$ | **Branched / lightly crosslinked** polymer — soluble, fusible |
| $\overline{f} \geq 3$ | **Highly crosslinked network** — thermoset, infusible, insoluble |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorise these equations — they appear almost every KTU cycle in Part-A or Part-B.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Engineering Utility |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $nM \rightarrow (M)_n$ | $M$ = monomer, $n$ = DP | Fundamental polymerisation stoichiometry |
| 2 | $\overline{f} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Carothers' average functionality | Predicts architecture *a priori* |
| 3 | $\overline{X_n} = \frac{1}{1-p}$ (step-growth, ideal) | $p$ = extent of reaction, $\overline{X_n}$ = number-average DP | Drives Carothers' equation for condensation polymers |
| 4 | Gel point: $p_c = \frac{2}{\overline{f}}$ | $p_c$ = critical extent of reaction at gelation | Determines onset of crosslinked network |
| 5 | Molecular weight: $\overline{M_n} = \overline{DP_n} \times M_0$ | $M_0$ = repeat unit molar mass | Converts DP to absolute MW |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linear polymers (f = 2):** Used in fibres, films, and thermoplastics (PE, PP, PET, nylon-6,6) — recyclable, melt-processable.
- **Branched polymers (f between 2 and 3):** Used in coatings, adhesives, and impact-resistant plastics (LDPE).
- **Crosslinked polymers (f ≥ 3):** Used in thermosets, vulcanised rubber, ion-exchange resins, and structural composites — superior mechanical strength and chemical resistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Average Functionality Computation

**Problem:** A polymerisation mixture contains **4 moles of glycerol** (functionality 3) and **6 moles of phthalic anhydride** (functionality 2). Calculate the average functionality $\overline{f}$ and predict the polymer architecture.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify monomer types and their functionalities**

Glycerol (HOCH₂–CHOH–CH₂OH) possesses **three hydroxyl groups** $\Rightarrow f_1 = 3$.

Phthalic anhydride (an aromatic cyclic anhydride) possesses **two reactive carbonyl sites** when ring-opened $\Rightarrow f_2 = 2$.

$$f_1 = 3, \quad f_2 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Carothers' average functionality formula**

$$\overline{f} = \frac{\sum N_i \, f_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{N_1 f_1 + N_2 f_2}{N_1 + N_2}$$

$$\overline{f} = \frac{(4 \times 3) + (6 \times 2)}{4 + 6}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator separately**

$$\text{Numerator} = 12 + 12 = 24$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 10$$

$$\overline{f} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4$$

**Step 4 — Apply the decision rule for architecture**

Since $2 < \overline{f} = 2.4 < 3$, the predicted architecture is:

> **Branched / lightly crosslinked polymer** — soluble and fusible (a typical alkyd resin).

**Step 5 — Verify by gel-point analysis (optional)**

$$p_c = \frac{2}{\overline{f}} = \frac{2}{2.4} = 0.833$$

The system gels when **83.3%** of functional groups have reacted — a useful industrial control parameter.

## 3.2 Example 2 — Distinguishing Functionality vs. Polymer Type

**Problem:** Explain, with reasoning, why **ethylene (CH₂=CH₂)** gives a linear polymer but **divinylbenzene (DVB)** gives a crosslinked network, even though both contain C=C bonds.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Analyse ethylene structure**

$$H_2C = CH_2$$

Number of reactive C=C bonds = **1** $\Rightarrow f = 2$ (two carbon radicals can grow on either side). Polymerisation yields **–CH₂–CH₂–CH₂–CH₂–** (linear polyethylene).

**Step 2 — Analyse divinylbenzene (DVB) structure**

$$CH_2 = CH - C_6H_4 - CH = CH_2$$

Number of reactive C=C bonds = **2** $\Rightarrow f = 4$ (each vinyl adds two reactive carbons, total four reactive ends). Polymerisation enables growth in **multiple directions simultaneously**, producing a **3D crosslinked network**.

**Step 3 — Generalise the rule**

| Monomer | Reactive C=C Bonds | Functionality (f) | Result |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| Ethylene | 1 | 2 | Linear PE |
| Styrene | 1 | 2 | Linear PS |
| Divinylbenzene | 2 | 4 | Crosslinked network (ion-exchange resin matrix) |
| Trivinylbenzene | 3 | 6 | Highly crosslinked, rigid thermoset |

> [!NOTE]
> The *number of independent polymerisable functional groups* — not the total reactive atoms — defines **f**.

## 3.3 Example 3 — Repeat Unit Mass Derivation

**Problem:** Vinyl chloride (CH₂=CHCl, M = 62.5 g/mol) is polymerised to PVC with a number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{DP_n} = 1000$. Calculate $\overline{M_n}$.

### Solution

$$\overline{M_n} = \overline{DP_n} \times M_0$$

$$\overline{M_n} = 1000 \times 62.5 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\overline{M_n} = 62{,}500 \text{ g/mol} = 62.5 \text{ kDa}$$

> [!TIP]
> Use this same template for any addition-polymer question in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Monomers (Knowledge Map)

The following **Mermaid flowchart** captures the full taxonomy of monomers as expected from a KTU top-scorer's answer sheet.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["MONOMER"]:::root --> B["BY FUNCTIONALITY"]
    A --> C["BY CHEMICAL STRUCTURE"]
    A --> D["BY ORIGIN"]
    A --> E["BY POLYMERISATION TYPE"]

    B --> B1["MONOFUNCTIONAL f equals 1"]
    B --> B2["DIFUNCTIONAL f equals 2"]
    B --> B3["TRIFUNCTIONAL f equals 3"]
    B --> B4["POLYFUNCTIONAL f greater than 3"]

    B1 --> B1A["Acrylic Acid Chain Stopper"]
    B2 --> B2A["Linear Polymer Ethylene Glycol"]
    B2 --> B2B["Bifunctional AA plus BB Nylon 66"]
    B3 --> B3A["Branched Polymer Glycerol Phthalic"]
    B4 --> B4A["Crosslinked Network Divinylbenzene"]

    C --> C1["VINYL CH2 equals CHX"]
    C --> C2["DIENE Two Conjugated C=C"]
    C --> C3["CYCLIC Ring Strain Driven"]
    C --> C4["AROMATIC Ring plus Functional Group"]
    C --> C5["ACRYLIC CH2 equals CHCOOR"]

    C1 --> C1A["Ethylene Styrene VCM"]
    C2 --> C2A["Butadiene Isoprene Chloroprene"]
    C3 --> C3A["Caprolactam Ethylene Oxide"]
    C4 --> C4A["Phenol Aniline"]
    C5 --> C5A["Methyl Acrylate MMA"]

    D --> D1["NATURAL"]
    D --> D2["SYNTHETIC PETROCHEMICAL"]
    D --> D3["BIO BASED RENEWABLE"]

    D1 --> D1A["Glucose Amino Acids Isoprene"]
    D2 --> D2A["Ethylene Styrene VCM"]
    D3 --> D3A["Bio Ethylene from Sugarcane"]

    E --> E1["ADDITION Unsaturated"]
    E --> E2["CONDENSATION Two plus Functional"]

    E1 --> E1A["Vinyl and Diene Monomers"]
    E2 --> E2A["Diol plus Diacid or Diamine plus Diacid"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    class B1A,B2A,B2B,B3A,B4A,C1A,C2A,C3A,C4A,C5A,D1A,D2A,D3A,E1A,E2A fill:#fde9a9,stroke:#a07b1f,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this chart for KTU answers:** Start at the *root* node `MONOMER`, traverse down *one* classification branch, then *quote one industrially relevant example*. This three-step pattern (Definition → Classification → Example) is the universal KTU full-mark template.

## 4.2 Functional-Group-to-Architecture Mapping (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the **functionality count (f)** of monomers drives the **final polymer architecture** — a question that appears almost every KTU ESE cycle.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT["MONOMER INPUT STAGE"]
        M1["MONOFUNCTIONAL f equals 1"]
        M2["DIFUNCTIONAL f equals 2"]
        M3["TRIFUNCTIONAL f equals 3"]
        M4["POLYFUNCTIONAL f greater than 3"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["POLYMERISATION REACTION"]
        R1["Chain Terminator only"]
        R2["Linear Chain Growth"]
        R3["Branched Growth"]
        R4["Three Dimensional Network Formation"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["POLYMER ARCHITECTURE"]
        P1["No Polymer Small Molecule"]
        P2["Linear Thermoplastic eg PE Nylon PET"]
        P3["Branched Soluble Fusible eg LDPE Alkyd"]
        P4["Crosslinked Thermoset eg Vulcanised Rubber DVB Network"]
    end

    M1 --> R1 --> P1
    M2 --> R2 --> P2
    M3 --> R3 --> P3
    M4 --> R4 --> P4

    classDef inputBox fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    classDef processBox fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a07b1f,color:#000000
    classDef outputBox fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000
    class M1,M2,M3,M4 inputBox
    class R1,R2,R3,R4 processBox
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 outputBox
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram is the **single most high-yield image** for KTU Module-1. Examiners love to see it reproduced or described in words in the 14-mark question on "monomer classification".

## 4.3 Repeat-Unit Formation Visualised

```mermaid
graph LR
    M["MONOMER CH2 equals CHX"] -->|Addition Polymerisation| RU["REPEAT UNIT minus CH2 minus CHX minus"]
    RU -->|n times| P["POLYMER minus CH2 minus CHX minus n"]

    classDef monoStyle fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef ruStyle fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    classDef polyStyle fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000
    class M monoStyle
    class RU ruStyle
    class P polyStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a monomer. With one example each, distinguish between monofunctional and difunctional monomers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **monomer** is a low-molecular-weight, reactive molecule containing one or more functional groups capable of undergoing polymerisation to form a macromolecule (polymer). The general reaction is $nM \rightarrow (M)_n$.

- **Monofunctional monomer (f = 1):** Contains only **one** reactive functional group. Example: *acrylic acid* (CH₂=CH–COOH). Cannot form a polymer on its own — acts as a chain terminator.
- **Difunctional monomer (f = 2):** Contains **two** reactive functional groups. Example: *ethylene glycol* (HO–CH₂–CH₂–OH) or *ethylene* (CH₂=CH₂). Forms **linear polymers**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse *functionality* with *number of atoms*. Functionality counts **independent reactive groups**, not atoms. Methyl methacrylate has 8 carbons but $f = 2$ (one C=C bond).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List four classes of monomers based on chemical structure, giving one industrially important example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Monomer Class | Example | Polymer Formed |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vinyl** (CH₂=CHX) | Vinyl chloride | PVC (pipes, cables) |
| 2 | **Diene** (CH₂=C(R)–CH=CH₂) | Isoprene | Natural rubber |
| 3 | **Cyclic** (ring opens on polymerisation) | ε-Caprolactam | Nylon-6 (fibres, textiles) |
| 4 | **Acrylic** (CH₂=CH–COOR) | Methyl methacrylate | PMMA (Plexiglas, lenses) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Classify monomers based on **(i) functionality** and **(ii) chemical structure**. Give **two examples** for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymerisation mixture contains **3 mol glycerol** (f = 3) and **9 mol adipic acid** (f = 2). Calculate the **average functionality** $\overline{f}$, the **gel point** $p_c$, and predict the resulting polymer architecture with industrial examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Monomers**

**(i) Classification by Functionality (f):**

| Class | f | Example 1 | Example 2 | Polymer Formed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Monofunctional | 1 | Acrylic acid | Styrene (in excess acts as terminator) | None / chain-capped |
| Difunctional | 2 | Ethylene | Hexamethylenediamine | Linear (PE, nylon) |
| Trifunctional | 3 | Glycerol | 1,3,5-Benzenetricarboxylic acid | Branched |
| Polyfunctional | ≥ 4 | Pentaerythritol | Divinylbenzene | Crosslinked network |

**(ii) Classification by Chemical Structure:**

| Class | Reactive Group | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vinyl | C=C | Ethylene | Vinyl chloride |
| Diene | Two conjugated C=C | Butadiene | Isoprene |
| Cyclic | Ring strain | ε-Caprolactam | Ethylene oxide |
| Bifunctional AA–BB | –OH / –COOH pair | Ethylene glycol | Adipic acid |

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $N_1 = 3$ mol glycerol, $f_1 = 3$; $N_2 = 9$ mol adipic acid, $f_2 = 2$.

**Step 1 — Compute average functionality $\overline{f}$:**

$$\overline{f} = \frac{N_1 f_1 + N_2 f_2}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{(3)(3) + (9)(2)}{3 + 9}$$

$$\overline{f} = \frac{9 + 18}{12} = \frac{27}{12} = 2.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute gel point $p_c$:**

$$p_c = \frac{2}{\overline{f}} = \frac{2}{2.25} = 0.889$$

**Step 3 — Architecture prediction:**

Since $2 < \overline{f} = 2.25 < 3$, the system yields a **branched / lightly crosslinked polymer** — soluble and fusible, typical of **alkyd resins** used in paints, varnishes, and surface coatings.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $p_c = 2/\overline{f}$ but forget to convert $\overline{f}$ to **2.25** correctly. A single arithmetic slip here costs the full 7 marks. *Always show denominator simplification step-by-step.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Define the term **monomer**. Explain the concept of **functionality** with suitable examples. Why does functionality determine whether a polymer is *linear, branched, or crosslinked*? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **flowchart**, explain the classification of monomers based on **origin** (natural, synthetic, bio-based). Discuss **three industrial polymers** with their monomers and applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Monomer & Functionality**

A **monomer** is a small, reactive molecule capable of covalently bonding with itself or other molecules to form a polymer: $nM \rightarrow (M)_n$.

**Functionality (f):** The number of reactive sites (functional groups) on a monomer molecule that can participate in polymerisation.

- **f = 1:** Monofunctional — example: *acrylic acid*. Cannot polymerise alone.
- **f = 2:** Difunctional — example: *ethylene glycol*. Gives linear polymer (e.g., PET).
- **f = 3:** Trifunctional — example: *glycerol*. Gives branched polymer (e.g., alkyd).
- **f ≥ 3:** Polyfunctional — example: *divinylbenzene*. Gives crosslinked thermoset.

**Why functionality dictates architecture:**

Each functional group is a *potential growth point*. If a monomer has only **two** reactive sites, the chain can grow in only **two directions** → linear. If it has **three**, branching into a third direction becomes possible. With **four or more**, growth in multiple directions creates a **3D network**. Hence:

$$\overline{f} = 2 \Rightarrow \text{Linear};\quad 2 < \overline{f} < 3 \Rightarrow \text{Branched};\quad \overline{f} \geq 3 \Rightarrow \text{Crosslinked}$$

**(b) Classification by Origin — Flowchart & Industrial Polymers**

**Origin-based classification:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    O["MONOMER ORIGIN"]
    O --> ON["NATURAL from living organisms"]
    O --> OS["SYNTHETIC from petrochemicals"]
    O --> OB["BIO BASED renewable feedstocks"]

    ON --> ON1["Glucose"]
    ON --> ON2["Amino Acids"]
    ON --> ON3["Isoprene"]

    OS --> OS1["Ethylene"]
    OS --> OS2["Vinyl Chloride"]
    OS --> OS3["Styrene"]

    OB --> OB1["Bio Ethylene"]
    OB --> OB2["Lactic Acid"]
```

**Three industrial polymers with their monomers and applications:**

| Polymer | Monomer | Origin | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyethylene (PE)** | Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) | Synthetic (petro) | Carry bags, bottles, pipes |
| **Natural Rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene)** | Isoprene (CH₂=C(CH₃)–CH=CH₂) | Natural (latex from *Hevea brasiliensis*) | Tyres, gaskets, elastomers |
| **Poly(lactic acid) (PLA)** | Lactic acid (CH₃CH(OH)COOH) | Bio-based (corn starch fermentation) | Biodegradable packaging, 3D printing, medical sutures |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **natural monomers** (e.g., isoprene from latex) with **natural polymers** (e.g., rubber itself). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. The monomer is the *small molecule*; the polymer is the *macromolecule derived from it*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> Print this section and stick it on your study wall one week before the KTU ESE.

- **Definition core:** Monomer = small, reactive, low-MW molecule that links to form a polymer via the reaction $nM \rightarrow (M)_n$.
- **Functionality (f)** is the *count of independent reactive groups*, not atoms.
- **Carothers' average functionality:** $\overline{f} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ — determines polymer architecture.
- **Gel point equation:** $p_c = \frac{2}{\overline{f}}$ — predicts when the system forms an infinite crosslinked network.
- **Architecture decision rules:** $f = 2 \Rightarrow$ linear; $2 < f < 3 \Rightarrow$ branched; $f \geq 3 \Rightarrow$ crosslinked thermoset.
- **Classification axes (4):** Functionality, Chemical structure, Origin, Polymerisation type.
- **Vinyl monomers** dominate commercial addition polymers (PE, PP, PVC, PS, PMMA).
- **Cyclic monomers** (ε-caprolactam, ethylene oxide) polymerise via **ring-opening**.
- **AA + BB type monomers** (e.g., ethylene glycol + adipic acid) form **step-growth condensation polymers** like PET and nylon.
- **Repeat unit mass** $M_0$ relates to $\overline{M_n}$ via $\overline{M_n} = \overline{DP_n} \times M_0$.
- **Industrial examples to memorise:** PE (ethylene), PVC (vinyl chloride), PS (styrene), PMMA (methyl methacrylate), Nylon-6 (caprolactam), Nylon-6,6 (HMDA + adipic acid), PET (ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid), Natural rubber (isoprene).
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Definition → Classification → Example → Industrial application = full-mark template for any KTU polymer question.
- **Common confusion to avoid:** Monofunctional monomers **stop** polymerisation; they do **not** start it. Chain initiators (like peroxides) are *catalysts*, not monomers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeat Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Repeat Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Repeat Unit** (also called the **Mer**, **Structural Unit**, or **Monomer Unit**) is the smallest identifiable chemical structural unit whose repetition, through covalent bonds, constitutes the complete macromolecular chain of a polymer. In the IUPAC-recommended nomenclature, the repeat unit is the constitutional unit (CRU - Constitutional Repeating Unit) that, when joined sequentially head-to-tail in large numbers, forms the polymer backbone.

Mathematically, if a polymer chain consists of $n$ repeat units, the chain can be represented as:

$$\text{Polymer Chain} = \sim [M]_n \sim$$

where $[M]$ is the repeat unit and $n$ is the **Degree of Polymerization (DP or $X_n$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The repeat unit is the *fundamental counting block* of a polymer. For **addition (chain-growth) polymers**, the repeat unit is **identical** to the monomer (e.g., ethylene $\rightarrow$ polyethylene). For **condensation (step-growth) polymers**, the repeat unit is **not** identical to the monomer; the actual structural residue remains after elimination of small molecules (e.g., water, HCl).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **freight train**. Each freight train is composed of hundreds of identical **wagons (repeat units)** coupled together. No single wagon is the train — but the **wagon design is the fundamental repeating module**.

Similarly:
- The **train** = the polymer molecule
- The **wagon** = the repeat unit
- The **number of wagons** = degree of polymerization ($X_n$)
- The **type of wagon** (boxcar, tanker, flatbed) = the chemical identity (polyethylene, PVC, nylon, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> - **Monomer** = the small molecule fed into the reactor (e.g., vinyl chloride, $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHCl}$).
> - **Repeat Unit** = the chemical residue that remains *built into* the polymer chain after polymerisation.
> - In addition polymerisation, monomer $\equiv$ repeat unit (no atoms lost).
> - In condensation polymerisation, repeat unit $\neq$ monomer (small by-product eliminated).

### 1.3 Real-World Examples of Repeat Units

| Polymer | Monomer (Feed) | Repeat Unit In-Chain | Mass Lost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polyethylene (PE) | $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}_2$ | $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-$ | 0 (addition) |
| Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) | $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHCl}$ | $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHCl}-$ | 0 (addition) |
| Polystyrene (PS) | $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHC}_6\text{H}_5$ | $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-$ | 0 (addition) |
| Nylon 6,6 | Hexamethylenediamine + Adipic acid | $-\text{NH}(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{NH-CO}(\text{CH}_2)_4\text{CO}-$ | $2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| PET | Ethylene glycol + Terephthalic acid | $-\text{O-CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{-O-CO-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-CO}-$ | $2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identification of the Repeat Unit**
- Locate the two-carbon backbone segment in an addition polymer.
- For a monosubstituted vinyl monomer $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHX}$, the repeat unit is $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHX}-$.
- For condensation polymers, identify the residue after loss of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{HCl}$, or $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$.

**Step 2 — Calculation of Repeat Unit Molecular Weight ($M_0$)**
- **Addition polymer:** $M_0 = M_{\text{monomer}}$
- **Condensation polymer:** $M_0 = (\text{Sum of monomer masses}) - (\text{Mass of by-product molecules eliminated})$
- Per the Carothers equation, for a bifunctional condensation: $M_0 = M_{\text{monomer}_1} + M_{\text{monomer}_2} - M_{\text{by-product}}$

**Step 3 — Linking to Degree of Polymerization**
- The **number-average degree of polymerisation** is:
$$X_n = \frac{\text{Number of repeat units per chain (average)}}{1} = \frac{M_n}{M_0}$$
where $M_n$ is the number-average molecular weight of the polymer sample.

**Step 4 — Engineering Relevance**
- $X_n$ governs **mechanical strength, viscosity, melt flow index, glass transition, and processability**.
- Industrial PE grades have $X_n$ ranging from $\mathbf{10^3}$ to $\mathbf{10^5}$.
- Ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) used in bulletproof vests has $X_n \approx 10^5\text{–}10^6$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam high-yield** — they appear almost every semester.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | $X_n$ = number-average DP, $M_n$ = number-avg MW, $M_0$ = repeat unit MW | dimensionless / g·mol⁻¹ |
| 2 | $X_w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_0}$ | Weight-average DP | dimensionless |
| 3 | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | g·mol⁻¹ |
| 4 | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g·mol⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Polydispersity Index (always $\geq 1$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $M_0^{\text{add}} = M_{\text{monomer}}$ | Addition polymer | g·mol⁻¹ |
| 7 | $M_0^{\text{cond}} = M_{A} + M_{B} - 18\,n_{w}$ | Condensation (Carothers); $n_w$ = water molecules lost | g·mol⁻¹ |
| 8 | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers DP (step-growth) at conversion $p$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $X_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$ | DP with stoichiometric imbalance ratio $r \leq 1$ | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plastics industry** uses repeat unit MW to calculate intrinsic viscosity via the **Mark-Houwink equation** $[\eta] = K M^a$.
- **Composite design** needs the repeat unit structure to predict crystallinity (isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic) and therefore stiffness.
- **Recycling streams** are characterised by average $X_n$ because mechanical degradation reduces chain length.
- **Biomedical implants** (PMMA bone cement, PDMS) require precise $X_n$ for controlled degradation rates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Repeat Unit Molecular Weight for Condensation Polymers

Consider a generic **A–B type condensation** (e.g., an amino acid polymerising to nylon-type polyamide):

$$\text{H}_2\text{N–R–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R–COOH} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–R–CO–NH–R–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 1 —** Each condensation step joins two monomers and eliminates **one water molecule** (molar mass $M_w = 18\,\text{g/mol}$).

**Step 2 —** For $n$ monomers forming one chain, there are $(n-1)$ bonds formed, so $(n-1)$ water molecules are released.

**Step 3 —** Mass balance for the polymer chain:

$$M_{\text{polymer}} = n \cdot M_{\text{monomer}} - (n-1) \cdot 18$$

**Step 4 —** For large $n$, $(n-1) \approx n$, so the equation simplifies to:

$$M_{\text{polymer}} \approx n \cdot (M_{\text{monomer}} - 18)$$

**Step 5 —** The expression in parentheses is the **repeat unit molecular weight**:

$$M_0 = M_{\text{monomer}} - 18$$

For an **A–A / B–B condensation** (e.g., nylon 6,6 from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid):

$$M_0 = M_{\text{diamine}} + M_{\text{diacid}} - 2 \cdot 18$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Polyethylene (Addition Polymer)

**Given:** $M_n$ of a polyethylene sample $= 1.4 \times 10^5\,\text{g/mol}$. Monomer = ethylene, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$.

**Step 1 —** Atomic masses: C = 12, H = 1.
**Step 2 —** Monomer mass: $M_{\text{ethylene}} = 2(12) + 4(1) = 28\,\text{g/mol}$.
**Step 3 —** Since PE is an addition polymer: $M_0 = 28\,\text{g/mol}$.
**Step 4 —** Calculate degree of polymerisation:

$$X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^5}{28} = 5000$$

> **Conclusion:** A single PE chain in this sample contains **5000 repeat units on average**.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Nylon 6,6 (Condensation Polymer)

**Given:** Hexamethylenediamine $\text{H}_2\text{N(CH}_2)_6\text{NH}_2$ ($M = 116\,\text{g/mol}$) + Adipic acid $\text{HOOC(CH}_2)_4\text{COOH}$ ($M = 146\,\text{g/mol}$). Polymer $M_n = 25{,}000\,\text{g/mol}$.

**Step 1 —** Compute $M_0$:

$$M_0 = 116 + 146 - (2 \times 18) = 262 - 36 = 226\,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 2 —** Compute $X_n$:

$$X_n = \frac{25{,}000}{226} = 110.6 \approx 111$$

**Step 3 —** Verification using Carothers equation with conversion $p = 0.99$:

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{1}{1-0.99} = 100$$

The small difference (111 vs 100) is reconciled by including a stoichiometric imbalance ratio $r < 1$ in the full Carothers formula.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Polymer Repeat Units

| Polymer | Repeat Unit Structure | $M_0$ (g/mol) | Type | Atoms Lost per Repeat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polyethylene | $-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2-$ | **28** | Addition | 0 |
| Polypropylene | $-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-$ | **42** | Addition | 0 |
| PVC | $-\text{CH}_2\text{CHCl}-$ | **62.5** | Addition | 0 |
| Polystyrene | $-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-$ | **104** | Addition | 0 |
| PMMA | $-\text{CH}_2\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3)-$ | **100** | Addition | 0 |
| Nylon 6,6 | $-\text{NH(CH}_2)_6\text{NHCO(CH}_2)_4\text{CO}-$ | **226** | Condensation | $2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| PET | $-\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OOC-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-CO}-$ | **192** | Condensation | $2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| Nylon 6 | $-\text{NH(CH}_2)_5\text{CO}-$ | **113** | Condensation | $1\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Concept of Repeat Unit Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Monomer Feed] --> B{Reaction Type?}
    B -- Addition --> C[Chain Growth]
    B -- Condensation --> D[Step Growth + Byproduct]
    C --> E[Repeat Unit = Monomer]
    D --> F[Repeat Unit = Monomer - Byproduct]
    E --> G[Polymer Chain n times Repeat Unit]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Degree of Polymerisation Xn]
    H --> I[Molecular Weight Mn]
    I --> J[Mechanical and Thermal Properties]

    classDef feed fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000

    class A feed
    class B,C,D proc
    class E,F,G,H,I out
    class J endNode
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Repeat Unit in a Polymer Backbone

```mermaid

    subgraph backboneA [Polyethylene Backbone]
        ru1A["CH2 CH2"] --> ru2A["CH2 CH2"] --> ru3A["CH2 CH2"] --> ru4A["... ... ..."]
    end

        ru1B["NH CH2 6 NH CO CH2 4 CO"] --> ru2B["NH CH2 6 NH CO CH2 4 CO"] --> ru3B["... ... ..."]
    end

    subgraph backboneC [PVC Backbone]
        ru1C["CH2 CHCl"] --> ru2C["CH2 CHCl"] --> ru3C["CH2 CHCl"] --> ru4C["... ... ..."]
    end

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Repeat Unit and Molecular Weight Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Repeat Unit Molecular Weight M0] -->|Divide| Xn[Degree of Polymerisation Xn]
    Mn[Number Avg Molecular Weight Mn] -->|Divide by M0| Xn
    Mw[Weight Avg Molecular Weight Mw] -->|Divide by M0| Xw[Weight Avg DP Xw]
    Mn --> PDI[Polydispersity Index Mw over Mn]
    Mw --> PDI
    PDI --> QC[Quality Control of Polymerisation]
    Xn --> Mech[Mech Properties]
    Xn --> Therm[Thermal Properties]
    Xn --> Proc[Processability]

    classDef core fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef derived fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#000

    class M0,Mn,Mw core
    class Xn,Xw,PDI derived
    class Mech,Therm,Proc,QC app
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "repeat unit" of a polymer. How does it differ from a monomer in the case of a condensation polymerisation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **repeat unit** is the smallest chemical structural unit whose regular sequential repetition constitutes the polymer chain. In **addition polymerisation**, the repeat unit is structurally identical to the starting monomer (e.g., vinyl chloride $\rightarrow$ PVC repeat unit $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHCl}-$). In **condensation polymerisation**, however, the repeat unit differs from the monomer because small molecules (water, HCl, methanol) are eliminated during the reaction. For example, in nylon 6,6, the repeat unit $-\text{NH}(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{NH-CO}(\text{CH}_2)_4\text{CO}-$ is formed by the loss of two water molecules from one diamine and one diacid monomer. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*The number-average molecular weight of a polystyrene sample is $1.04 \times 10^5\,\text{g/mol}$. Calculate the degree of polymerisation. (Monomer: styrene, $\text{C}_8\text{H}_8$, $M = 104\,\text{g/mol}$.)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Since polystyrene is an addition polymer, the repeat unit molecular weight equals the monomer molecular weight:
$$M_0 = 104\,\text{g/mol}$$

Degree of polymerisation:
$$X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{1.04 \times 10^5}{104} = 1000$$

**$\therefore$ The polystyrene sample has $X_n = 1000$ repeat units per chain on average. (3 marks)**

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Addition + Condensation DP Problems**

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*A sample of polyethylene has a number-average molecular weight of $2.8 \times 10^5\,\text{g/mol}$. (i) Identify the repeat unit, (ii) calculate its molecular weight, and (iii) determine the degree of polymerisation. Also explain with a reaction scheme why $M_0 = M_{\text{monomer}}$ for addition polymers.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Repeat unit identification:** Polyethylene is synthesised from ethylene monomer $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}_2$ via addition polymerisation. The repeat unit is $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-$, with a head-to-tail arrangement.

**(ii) Repeat unit molecular weight:**

$$M_0 = 2 \times 12 + 4 \times 1 = 24 + 4 = 28\,\text{g/mol}$$

**(iii) Degree of polymerisation:**

$$X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{2.8 \times 10^5}{28} = 10{,}000$$

**Reaction scheme explaining $M_0 = M_{\text{monomer}}$:**

$$n\,\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}_2 \longrightarrow \sim[\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2]_n \sim$$

In addition polymerisation, the $\pi$-bond of the vinyl group opens up and joins monomers together. No atoms are lost; therefore the sum of $n$ repeat unit masses equals $n$ times the monomer mass, giving $M_0 = 28\,\text{g/mol} = M_{\text{monomer}}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Nylon 6,6 is prepared from hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid with elimination of water.*
*(i) Write the repeat unit structure.*
*(ii) Calculate its molecular weight.*
*(iii) If the number-average molecular weight of a commercial nylon 6,6 grade is $22{,}600\,\text{g/mol}$, find the degree of polymerisation.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Repeat unit structure:**

$$\sim[\text{NH-}(\text{CH}_2)_6\text{-NH-CO-}(\text{CH}_2)_4\text{-CO}]_n \sim$$

**(ii) Molecular weight of repeat unit:**

$$M_0 = M_{\text{diamine}} + M_{\text{diacid}} - 2\,M_{\text{water}}$$

$$M_0 = 116 + 146 - 2(18) = 262 - 36 = 226\,\text{g/mol}$$

**(iii) Degree of polymerisation:**

$$X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{22{,}600}{226} = 100$$

**Verification via Carothers equation (conversion $p = 0.99$):**

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100 \;\checkmark$$

Hence, the nylon 6,6 chain has approximately **100 repeat units**, consistent with the Carothers prediction for 99% conversion in a stoichiometric step-growth reaction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]**
*Define the term "polydispersity index" (PDI). A polymer sample consists of three fractions: 30% of chains with $M = 10{,}000$, 50% with $M = 50{,}000$, and 20% with $M = 1{,}00{,}000$. Calculate $M_n$, $M_w$, and PDI. Comment on the nature of the polymerisation process.*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** PDI $= \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ is a measure of the breadth of the molecular weight distribution. A value of PDI = 1 denotes a perfectly monodisperse polymer (theoretically only in living polymerisations); higher values indicate broader distributions.

**Step 1 — Assume 100 g of sample (mole-based calculation needs $N_i$):**
Let us take 1 g of sample and convert mass fractions to number fractions using moles.

$$N_1 = \frac{0.30 \times 1}{10{,}000} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol}$$
$$N_2 = \frac{0.50 \times 1}{50{,}000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol}$$
$$N_3 = \frac{0.20 \times 1}{1{,}00{,}000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Number-average molecular weight:**

$$M_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^{-5})(10{,}000) + (1.0 \times 10^{-5})(50{,}000) + (2.0 \times 10^{-6})(1{,}00{,}000)}{(3.0 \times 10^{-5}) + (1.0 \times 10^{-5}) + (2.0 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$M_n = \frac{0.30 + 0.50 + 0.20}{4.2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.00}{4.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 23{,}810\,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Weight-average molecular weight:**

$$M_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{(3.0 \times 10^{-5})(10^8) + (1.0 \times 10^{-5})(2.5 \times 10^9) + (2.0 \times 10^{-6})(10^{10})}{1.00}$$

$$M_w = 3000 + 25{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 48{,}000\,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Polydispersity index:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{48{,}000}{23{,}810} \approx 2.02$$

**Comment:** A PDI of ~2 is characteristic of a **step-growth (condensation) polymerisation** in which chain-termination is statistical. Free-radical addition polymerisations typically give PDI between 1.5 and 2.0, while living/anionic polymerisations yield PDI approaching 1.0. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2022]**
*Explain with a neat sketch how the repeat unit of PVC is formed from its monomer. Given that $M_n$ of a PVC sample is $1.25 \times 10^5\,\text{g/mol}$, calculate the number of repeat units in an average chain. (Monomer: vinyl chloride, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{Cl}$, $M = 62.5\,\text{g/mol}$.)*

**Model Solution:**

**Repeat unit formation in PVC:**

Monomer: $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHCl}$ (vinyl chloride)

Reaction:
$$n\,\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHCl} \xrightarrow{\text{Initiator}} \sim[\text{CH}_2-\text{CHCl}]_n \sim$$

The $\pi$-bond of the vinyl group breaks; the carbon atoms form new $\sigma$-bonds with adjacent monomers, giving a saturated head-to-tail backbone. The substituent (Cl) projects laterally from the chain.

**Repeat unit structure:** $-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHCl}-$ (carbon–carbon single bond backbone with alternating H and Cl substituents).

**Calculation of repeat unit molecular weight:**

$$M_0 = 2(12) + 3(1) + 35.5 = 24 + 3 + 35.5 = 62.5\,\text{g/mol}$$

Since PVC is an addition polymer, $M_0 = M_{\text{monomer}} = 62.5\,\text{g/mol}$.

**Number of repeat units per chain:**

$$X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^5}{62.5} = 2000$$

**$\therefore$ An average PVC chain in this sample contains $X_n = 2000$ repeat units.** **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse monomer mass with repeat unit mass in condensation polymers.** Many students write $M_0 = 116 + 146 = 262$ for nylon 6,6, forgetting to subtract the two eliminated water molecules ($-36$). This costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $M_n$ must be in g·mol⁻¹ (not kg·mol⁻¹). Read the question carefully.
> 3. **PDI problems require computing $N_i$ from mass fractions, not using mass fractions directly** in the $M_n$ formula. Using mass fractions gives a wrong (mass-average-like) answer and costs **3–4 marks**.
> 4. **Always specify polymerisation type** in viva questions. Examiners award a mark for the correct classification (addition vs. condensation).
> 5. **Skipping the reaction scheme** in repeat-unit questions loses the 2 marks usually allocated for the diagrammatic step. Always draw the conversion arrow from monomer to repeat unit.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Repeat Unit (Mer)** = smallest repeating constitutional unit in a polymer chain; represented as $[M]$ in $\sim[M]_n\sim$.
- **Addition polymerisation:** $M_0 = M_{\text{monomer}}$; no by-product.
- **Condensation polymerisation:** $M_0 = \sum M_{\text{monomers}} - (\text{mass of eliminated by-product})$.
- **Degree of Polymerisation (DP):** $X_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$; high $X_n$ $\Rightarrow$ stronger, tougher polymer.
- **Number-average MW:** $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ — biased toward small chains.
- **Weight-average MW:** $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ — biased toward large chains.
- **PDI:** $\dfrac{M_w}{M_n} \geq 1$; PDI = 1 (monodisperse, ideal living polymerisation); PDI ≈ 2 typical for step-growth; PDI = 1.5–2.0 typical for free-radical.
- **Carothers equation:** $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (equal stoichiometry, conversion $p$).
- **Industrial benchmarks:** PE → $M_0 = 28$, PVC → $M_0 = 62.5$, PS → $M_0 = 104$, Nylon 6,6 → $M_0 = 226$, PET → $M_0 = 192$.
- **Head-to-tail addition** is the predominant regiochemistry in vinyl polymerisation; head-to-head is a minor defect.
- **Quick exam trick:** For any "find $X_n$" question, the only operations needed are (1) compute $M_0$ using the right polymerisation class, and (2) divide $M_n$ by $M_0$. Memorise the atomic masses: C = 12, H = 1, O = 16, N = 14, Cl = 35.5.
- **Viva favourite:** *"Why is the DP of commercial polymers never infinite?"* — Answer: Statistical chain-termination; 100% conversion is thermodynamically and kinetically impossible in finite time, and stoichiometric imbalance caps the maximum achievable $X_n$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oligomer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Oligomer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oligomer:** A molecular species of intermediate relative molecular mass (molar mass) whose structure essentially comprises a small number of units derived, actually or conceptually, from molecules of lower relative molecular mass. By convention, an oligomer is a substance whose **degree of polymerisation (DP)** lies between **2 and 20** (some sources extend the upper limit to ~50 for borderline cases). When the number of repeating units is **n = 1**, the molecule is the **monomer**; when **n ≥ 20 (or 50)**, the molecule is a true **high polymer**.

The term is derived from the Greek words *"oligos"* (meaning "few") and *"meros"* (meaning "part"). The general structural formula of an oligomer obtained from a monomer **M** is:

$$\text{Oligomer} = M_{n} \quad \text{where} \quad 2 \leq n \leq 20$$

| Species | Degree of Polymerisation (n) | Molar Mass Range (typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Monomer | 1 | < 100 g/mol |
| Dimer | 2 | ~100–500 g/mol |
| Trimer | 3 | ~150–750 g/mol |
| **Oligomer** | **2 – 20** | **~100 – 10,000 g/mol** |
| Polymer | > 20 (or > 50) | > 10,000 g/mol |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Train Compartments):**
> - **Monomer** = a **single train engine** (1 unit, no carriages). It cannot "transport bulk freight."
> - **Oligomer** = a **short train with 2–20 carriages** linked together. It has the *idea* of being a long chain, but is still small, manageable, and the individual carriages are clearly visible.
> - **Polymer** = a **long, massive freight train with thousands of carriages**. The individual units are practically indistinguishable, and the material exhibits completely new bulk properties (strength, viscosity, elasticity).

Think of oligomers as the **"in-between molecules"** — they are the chemists' stepping stones. A polymer chemist often synthesises an oligomer first (because it is easy to characterise precisely) and then uses it as a **macro-monomer** or **prepolymer** to build the final high-molecular-weight polymer.

### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

The concept of oligomers became industrially important with the rise of **step-growth polymerisation** (Wallace Carothers, 1929, DuPont). In the **nylon-6,6** synthesis, an intermediate *hexamethylenediamine-adipic acid salt* (often called "nylon salt") is a 1:1 adduct — essentially an oligomeric precursor. Carothers also coined the term **"superpolymer"** for materials with DP > 100, distinguishing them from oligomeric intermediates.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise this KTU-favourite line:** *"If it drips, it's an oligomer; if it stands on its own, it's a polymer."* — A loose heuristic relating low molar mass (fluid) to oligomeric character.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Oligomers

Oligomers are classified based on the **number of repeating units** and the **uniformity of composition**:

| Sub-class | DP (n) | Special Name | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Monomer | 1 | Monomer | Ethylene CH₂=CH₂ |
| Dimer | 2 | Dimer | Cyclohexene (from 2 butadiene) |
| Trimer | 3 | Trimer | Trioxane (cyclic trimer of formaldehyde) |
| Tetramer | 4 | Tetramer | Cyclooctatetraene (from 4 acetylene) |
| n-mer | n | n-mer | General |

**Based on composition:**
- **Homooligomer:** Built from a single type of monomer. Example: oligoethylene (CH₃–(CH₂)ₙ–CH₃).
- **Cooligomer (or Heterooligomer):** Built from two or more different monomers. Example: oligo(styrene-*co*-acrylonitrile).
- **Telechelic Oligomer:** An oligomer bearing **reactive functional groups at both chain ends** (Greek *tele* = "far", *chelos* = "claw"). Used as a prepolymer in polyurethane and epoxy resin synthesis.

### 2.2 Degree of Polymerisation (DP) — The Core Numerical Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Polymerisation (DP) or *X̄ₙ*:** The average number of monomer units in a polymer/oligomer chain. It is the most fundamental quantity in polymer science.

$$X_n = \bar{X}_n = \frac{\text{Number of monomer units}}{\text{Number of molecules}}$$

For an oligomeric mixture, the number-average DP is the relevant parameter.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verified and appear frequently in KTU PCPOT205 Module 1 questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $X_n = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N}$ | DP from consumed monomers (Carothers equation form) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$ | Extent of reaction (fraction of functional groups reacted) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Number-average DP in step-growth polymerisation (Carothers) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $M_n = X_n \cdot M_0$ | Number-average molar mass of polymer/oligomer | g/mol |
| 5 | $M_w = X_w \cdot M_0$ | Weight-average molar mass | g/mol |
| 6 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n} = \dfrac{X_w}{X_n}$ | Polydispersity Index (measure of molar mass distribution breadth) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molar mass (statistical) | g/mol |
| 8 | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molar mass (statistical) | g/mol |

**Boundary conditions for oligomer classification:**
- Monomer limit: $X_n = 1 \Rightarrow p = 0$
- Dimer limit: $X_n = 2 \Rightarrow p = 0.5$
- Trimer limit: $X_n = 3 \Rightarrow p \approx 0.667$
- Oligomer upper limit: $X_n = 20 \Rightarrow p = 0.95$
- Polymer threshold: $X_n \geq 20 \Rightarrow p \geq 0.95$

### 2.4 Why Oligomers Exist — The "Why" Behind the Concept

- **Kinetic control:** Polymerisation reactions can be stopped deliberately at low conversion (e.g., by quenching, catalyst poisoning, or monomer depletion) to obtain oligomers of well-defined chain length.
- **Thermodynamic stability:** Some cyclic oligomers (e.g., cyclic trimer of formaldehyde = trioxane) are thermodynamically more stable than their linear counterparts.
- **Synthetic convenience:** Oligomers have low melt viscosity, are easy to purify, and can be characterised by standard techniques like GC-MS, vapour-pressure osmometry, and MALDI-TOF.
- **Reactive intermediates:** Telechelic oligomers are the "Lego blocks" of step-growth polymerisation — they are pre-made, well-characterised chains that simply need to be linked together.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Polyurethane industry:** Uses **telechelic hydroxyl-terminated polyether oligomers** (e.g., PEG, PPG) of molar mass 500–5000 g/mol as soft segments.
2. **Epoxy resins:** Based on **epoxy-terminated oligomers** of bisphenol-A diglycidyl ether (BADGE) — typically DP 0.2 (n = 0.2 to 30) — these are oligomers in the strict sense.
3. **Dentistry:** **Bis-GMA** is a dimethacrylate oligomer used in dental composites.
4. **Cosmetics:** **Oligopeptides** (e.g., Matrixyl®) are short chains of 3–10 amino acids used in anti-aging skin care.
5. **Molecular biology:** **Oligonucleotides** (DNA/RNA primers) of 18–25 bases are oligomers synthesised by solid-phase synthesis.
6. **Food industry:** **Oligosaccharides** (e.g., fructo-oligosaccharides, FOS) are prebiotic dietary fibres.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Carothers Equation for $X_n$

The most important equation in step-growth polymerisation is the **Carothers equation**, which directly relates the degree of polymerisation to the extent of reaction. Since oligomers are early-stage products of step-growth reactions, this derivation is essential.

**Starting Definitions:**

Let $N_0$ = total number of monomer molecules initially present.
Let $N$ = total number of molecules (of *all* sizes) present at time $t$.
Let $p$ = fraction of functional groups that have reacted (extent of reaction).

**Step 1 — Count unreacted functional groups:**

The total number of functional groups originally present (assuming difunctional monomers) is $2 N_0$ (each monomer contributes 2 reactive groups).

The number of functional groups that have **reacted** is $2 (N_0 - N)$ (because each bond formed consumes 2 functional groups, and each reaction reduces the molecule count by 1).

**Step 2 — Express the extent of reaction:**

$$p = \frac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Number of functional groups initially present}} = \frac{2 (N_0 - N)}{2 N_0}$$

$$p = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$:**

$$N = N_0 (1 - p)$$

**Step 4 — Define the number-average degree of polymerisation:**

The number-average DP is the *initial* number of monomer units divided by the *current* number of molecules (since each molecule, regardless of size, represents one unit in the count):

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4:**

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0 (1 - p)}$$

$$\boxed{X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}}$$

This is the **Carothers equation**. It applies to step-growth polymerisation of *difunctional* monomers (A–B type or A–A + B–B type with exact stoichiometric balance).

**Step 6 — Verify the oligomer boundary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_n = 2 \text{ (dimer)} \Rightarrow 1-p = 0.5 \Rightarrow p = 0.50 \\
X_n = 3 \text{ (trimer)} \Rightarrow 1-p = 0.333 \Rightarrow p = 0.667 \\
X_n = 20 \text{ (oligomer upper limit)} \Rightarrow 1-p = 0.05 \Rightarrow p = 0.95
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to number-average molar mass:**

If $M_0$ is the molar mass of the repeating unit (monomer residue), then:

$$M_n = X_n \cdot M_0 = \frac{M_0}{1 - p}$$

For an oligomeric mixture with $p = 0.95$ and $M_0 = 100$ g/mol:

$$M_n = \frac{100 \text{ g/mol}}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{100}{0.05} = 2000 \text{ g/mol}$$

This is a typical oligomeric molar mass, confirming the Carothers framework.

### 3.2 Derivation: Polydispersity Index for the Most Probable Distribution (Flory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In step-growth polymerisation, oligomers and polymers follow the **Flory Most Probable Distribution** (also called the *Schulz–Flory distribution*). The PDI for this distribution is theoretically **2.0** (regardless of conversion), but *oligomeric* samples often show lower PDI (≈ 1.1–1.5) because short chains can be fractionated easily.

**Starting probability:** A given monomer unit has probability $(1-p)$ of being unreacted at one end. The probability of finding an *n*-mer (chain of exactly $n$ units) is:

$$P(n) = (1 - p) \, p^{(n-1)}$$

This is a **geometric distribution**.

**Number-average DP:**

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

**Weight-average DP:**

$$X_w = \frac{1 + p}{1 - p}$$

**Proof of the $X_w$ formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_w &= \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n \cdot w_n = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{n^2 (1-p)^2 p^{n-1}}{1} \\
&= (1-p)^2 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^2 p^{n-1} \\
&= (1-p)^2 \cdot \frac{1 + p}{(1-p)^3} \quad \text{(using identity } \sum n^2 x^{n-1} = \frac{1+x}{(1-x)^3} \text{)} \\
&= \frac{1 + p}{1 - p}
\end{aligned}$$

**PDI derivation:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{X_w}{X_n} = \frac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1 + p$$

For a true step-growth polymer at high conversion ($p \to 1$): $\text{PDI} \to 2$.

For an oligomeric mixture at $p = 0.5$: $\text{PDI} = 1.5$.
For an oligomeric mixture at $p = 0.95$: $\text{PDI} = 1.95$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** In a step-growth polymerisation of ethylene glycol with adipic acid, the initial monomer concentration is 2.0 mol. After 30 minutes, the number of molecules present is 1.8 mol. Calculate: (a) extent of reaction $p$, (b) number-average DP $X_n$, (c) number-average molar mass $M_n$, (d) classify the product as monomer/oligomer/polymer.

**Given:** $N_0 = 2.0$ mol, $N = 1.8$ mol, $M_0 = (62 + 146) / 2 = 104$ g/mol (for the repeat unit of the resulting polyester, –O–CH₂CH₂–O–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO–)

Wait, let me reconsider. The repeating unit of poly(ethylene adipate) is:
$$-(\text{OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OCO(CH}_2)_4\text{CO})-$$

The molar mass of this repeating unit is:
- C: 6 × 12 = 72
- H: 8 × 1 = 8  
- O: 4 × 16 = 64
- Total: 72 + 8 + 64 = 144 g/mol

But if we consider the "monomer residue" concept (i.e., ½ of the combined monomer pair), then $M_0 = (62 + 146)/2 = 104$ g/mol.

Let me use the simpler convention: $M_0$ = 144 g/mol (the true repeat unit).

**Solution:**

(a) Extent of reaction:
$$p = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0} = \frac{2.0 - 1.8}{2.0} = \frac{0.2}{2.0} = 0.10$$

(b) Number-average DP:
$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.90} = 1.111$$

(c) Number-average molar mass:
$$M_n = X_n \cdot M_0 = 1.111 \times 144 = 160 \text{ g/mol}$$

(d) Classification: Since $X_n = 1.111 \approx 1$, the product is essentially still in the **monomer–dimer mixture** region. To obtain true oligomers ($X_n = 2$–$20$), one needs $p = 0.5$ to $0.95$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Monomer vs. Oligomer vs. Polymer

| Property | Monomer | **Oligomer** | High Polymer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Degree of polymerisation | 1 | 2 – 20 | > 20 (often > 100) |
| Typical $M_n$ | < 200 g/mol | 200 – 10,000 g/mol | > 10,000 g/mol |
| Physical state (RT) | Volatile liquid or gas | Low-viscosity liquid / waxy solid | Solid (often high strength) |
| Chain-end effects | All molecules are "ends" | End groups dominate properties | End groups negligible |
| Glass transition | Not defined | Often a vague, broad transition | Sharp, well-defined $T_g$ |
| Viscosity behaviour | Newtonian, low $\eta$ | Newtonian to mildly shear-thinning | Strongly non-Newtonian, viscoelastic |
| Melt flow | Flows freely | Flows under gravity | Requires heat/pressure |
| Mechanical strength | None | Low | Engineering-relevant |
| Typical characterisation | GC, HPLC | MALDI-TOF, GPC, NMR | GPC, light scattering, viscometry |
| Synthesis | — | Kinetic arrest of step-growth | High-conversion polymerisation |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "oligomer". How is it distinguished from a monomer and a polymer? Give two examples of naturally occurring oligomers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An oligomer is a molecular species of intermediate relative molecular mass whose structure comprises a small number of repeating units (typically 2 to 20) derived from molecules of lower relative molecular mass. The term comes from the Greek *oligos* (few) and *meros* (part).
>
> **Distinction (1 mark):**
> - A **monomer** has degree of polymerisation (DP) = 1, i.e., a single repeat unit.
> - An **oligomer** has DP between 2 and 20, with a typical molar mass range of ~100–10,000 g/mol.
> - A **polymer** has DP > 20, with molar mass > 10,000 g/mol, and exhibits characteristic bulk properties like high tensile strength and viscoelasticity.
>
> **Natural examples (1 mark):** 
> - **Oligopeptides** (e.g., glutathione, a tripeptide of Glu–Cys–Gly).
> - **Oligosaccharides** (e.g., raffinose, a trisaccharide found in sugar beet).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is a telechelic oligomer? Explain with a suitable example and state its industrial importance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A telechelic oligomer is an oligomer that bears **reactive functional groups at both chain ends** (from Greek *tele* = "far" and *chelos* = "claw"). The two end groups are usually identical (e.g., –OH, –NH₂, –COOH, –NCO, –SH).
>
> **Example (1 mark):** **Hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene (HTPB)** is a classic telechelic oligomer with –OH groups at both ends, used as a prepolymer in polyurethane propellant binders. Other examples include carboxyl-terminated polybutadiene (CTPB) and amine-terminated polyether (Jeffamine®).
>
> **Industrial importance (1 mark):** Telechelic oligomers are the **preformed building blocks** of step-growth polymerisation. They are easy to characterise (low PDI, known $M_n$), have predictable reactivity, and allow the polymer chemist to design segmented polymers (e.g., polyurethanes, polyesters, polyamides) with precise hard-segment/soft-segment ratios.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusion of DP limits:** Many students incorrectly state the oligomer–polymer boundary as DP = 100. The IUPAC convention is **DP = 20** (some sources use 50). Writing 100 will cost ½ mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $X_n$ and $X_w$:** The Carothers equation gives the **number-average** DP ($X_n$). Do not substitute it into weight-average formulas.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** $M_0$ must be in **g/mol** and $M_n$ must be in **g/mol** (or kg/mol with consistent unit conversion).
> 4. **Failing to state assumptions:** Always state the assumptions of step-growth kinetics (equal reactivity of functional groups, no cyclisation, stoichiometric balance).
> 5. **Calculation errors at low $p$:** When $p$ is small (e.g., 0.10), $X_n$ is close to 1, and the product is a monomer-dimer mixture, NOT a true oligomer. State this clearly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose Either A or B**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End-Semester Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Carothers equation for the number-average degree of polymerisation ($X_n$) in step-growth polymerisation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a polyesterification of ethylene glycol with adipic acid, the initial moles of each functional group (–OH and –COOH) are 2.0 mol. After a certain time, the number-average degree of polymerisation is found to be 8. Calculate: (i) the extent of reaction $p$, (ii) the moles of water eliminated, (iii) the number-average molar mass $M_n$ (take $M_0 = 144$ g/mol for the repeat unit), and (iv) classify the resulting product (monomer/oligomer/polymer). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of the Carothers equation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. **Equal reactivity:** All functional groups (e.g., –COOH, –OH) have the same probability of reaction, regardless of chain length.
2. **Difunctional monomers:** Each monomer molecule has exactly two reactive functional groups (A–A + B–B or A–B type).
3. **Stoichiometric balance:** Equimolar concentrations of A and B groups.
4. **No cyclisation or side reactions.**
5. **Step-growth kinetics:** Any two oligomeric species can react with each other.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules, $N$ = number of molecules (of all sizes) at time $t$, $p$ = extent of reaction.

Number of functional groups initially present = $2 N_0$.
Number of functional groups reacted = $2 (N_0 - N)$ (since each bond formation reduces the molecule count by 1 and consumes 2 functional groups).

**Step 1:** Define the extent of reaction:

$$p = \frac{2(N_0 - N)}{2 N_0} = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $N$:

$$N = N_0 (1 - p)$$

**Step 3:** Number-average DP is the total number of repeat units divided by the total number of chains:

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $N$:

$$\boxed{X_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

**Verification (0 marks bonus, optional):** For $p = 0.99$, $X_n = 100$ (a high polymer); for $p = 0.5$, $X_n = 2$ (a dimer, the smallest oligomer).

---

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** Initial moles of each functional group = 2.0 mol → $N_0 = 2.0$ mol (assuming 1:1 stoichiometry, the initial number of monomer pairs/molecules is 2.0 mol). $X_n = 8$, $M_0 = 144$ g/mol.

**(i) Extent of reaction (2 marks):**

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p} \implies 1 - p = \frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

$$\boxed{p = 1 - 0.125 = 0.875 \text{ (or } 87.5\%)}$$

**(ii) Moles of water eliminated (2 marks):**

Number of bonds formed = $N_0 - N = N_0(1 - (1-p)) = N_0 p = 2.0 \times 0.875 = 1.75$ mol.

Each ester bond formation releases one molecule of H₂O. Therefore:

$$\boxed{n_{H_2O} = 1.75 \text{ mol}}$$

**(iii) Number-average molar mass (2 marks):**

$$M_n = X_n \cdot M_0 = 8 \times 144 \text{ g/mol} = \boxed{1152 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**(iv) Classification (1 mark):**

Since $X_n = 8$, and the oligomer range is $2 \leq X_n \leq 20$, the product is an **oligomer** (specifically, a *cooligomeric polyester* of medium chain length, often called a "prepolymer").

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End-Semester]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **telechelic oligomer** with a neat labelled sketch. Discuss its role in the synthesis of polyurethanes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of polystyrene is found to have a number-average molar mass of 10,400 g/mol. The molar mass of the styrene repeat unit is 104 g/mol. (i) Calculate the number-average degree of polymerisation $X_n$. (ii) Is this a monomer, oligomer, or polymer? (iii) The weight-average molar mass is found to be 20,800 g/mol. Calculate the PDI and comment on the breadth of the molar mass distribution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Telechelic oligomer in polyurethane synthesis (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A telechelic oligomer is a short polymer chain (typically 2–20 repeat units) bearing **two reactive functional end groups**, one at each terminus. Common end groups include –OH, –NH₂, –COOH, –NCO, –SH, and –CH=CH₂.

**Labelled sketch (2 marks):**

$$\text{HO} - [\text{Repeat Unit}]_n - \text{OH} \quad \text{(a dihydroxy telechelic oligomer)}$$

```
   [F1]---[R1]---[R2]---[R3]---[···]---[Rn]---[F2]
   F1 = Functional group 1 (e.g. -OH)
   F2 = Functional group 2 (e.g. -OH)
   Ri = Repeat unit i
   n   = 2 to 20
```

**Role in polyurethane synthesis (3 marks):**

Polyurethanes are synthesised by the **step-growth reaction of a diol-terminated oligomer (the "polyol") with a diisocyanate (the "hard segment")**:

$$\text{HO} - [\text{Oligomer}]_n - \text{OH} + 2 \text{ OCN-R-NCO} \longrightarrow \text{Polyurethane}$$

- The **telechelic polyol** (e.g., polyether diol, polyester diol) forms the **soft segment** of the polyurethane, providing flexibility and low-temperature performance.
- The **diisocyanate** (e.g., MDI, TDI, HDI) forms the **hard segment**, providing rigidity and high-temperature strength.
- By choosing the oligomer's $M_n$ (typically 500–5000 g/mol), the chemist can precisely tune the soft/hard segment ratio and hence the mechanical properties of the final polyurethane.
- Common examples: **hydroxyl-terminated polyether (PTMEG)**, **hydroxyl-terminated polyester (PCL diol)**, and **hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene (HTPB)** used in solid rocket propellant binders.

---

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $M_n = 10,400$ g/mol, $M_0 = 104$ g/mol (styrene: C₈H₈).

**(i) Number-average DP (2 marks):**

$$X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{10,400 \text{ g/mol}}{104 \text{ g/mol}} = \boxed{100}$$

**(ii) Classification (1 mark):**

Since $X_n = 100 > 20$, the sample is a **high polymer** (not an oligomer or monomer). It is a *typical commercial polystyrene* used in packaging, insulation, etc.

**(iii) PDI and distribution breadth (4 marks):**

Given $M_w = 20,800$ g/mol:

$$X_w = \frac{M_w}{M_0} = \frac{20,800 \text{ g/mol}}{104 \text{ g/mol}} = 200$$

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{M_w}{M_n} = \frac{20,800}{10,400} = \boxed{2.0}$$

**Comment (2 marks):** A PDI of exactly 2.0 is the theoretical value for a **Flory most-probable distribution** expected from an ideal step-growth polymerisation. The molar mass distribution is therefore relatively **broad** (compared to "living" polymerisation products, which can have PDI as low as 1.01–1.10). The sample is **polydisperse** — chains range in length from dimers to very long chains.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **OLIGOMER — 60-SECOND REVISION SHEET**

- [x] **Definition:** Intermediate molar mass species with **DP = 2 to 20** (Greek *oligos* = few).
- [x] **Boundary:** $X_n = 1$ (monomer) → $X_n = 2$–$20$ (oligomer) → $X_n > 20$ (polymer).
- [x] **Carothers equation:** $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ — valid for step-growth, difunctional, stoichiometric, equal-reactivity systems.
- [x] **Molar mass:** $M_n = X_n \cdot M_0$; typical oligomer range **100–10,000 g/mol**.
- [x] **Flory most-probable distribution:** PDI = 1 + p → for an oligomeric mixture at $p = 0.95$, PDI ≈ 1.95; for a high polymer at $p = 0.99$, PDI → 2.0.
- [x] **Telechelic oligomers:** Have reactive groups at BOTH ends. Used as prepolymers in PU, epoxy, and polyester syntheses.
- [x] **Cyclic oligomers:** Formed by intramolecular cyclisation. Example: trioxane (cyclic trimer of formaldehyde), ε-caprolactone cyclic oligomers.
- [x] **Characterisation techniques:** GC-MS (dimers/trimers), MALDI-TOF (up to ~5000 g/mol), GPC (oligomers and polymers), NMR (end-group analysis).
- [x] **Examples to memorise:**
  - Oligoethylene glycol (OEG) — telechelic diol
  - Trioxane — cyclic trimer of formaldehyde
  - Cyclohexene — cyclic dimer of 1,3-butadiene
  - Matrixyl® — cosmetic pentapeptide
  - HTPB — hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene (rocket propellant binder)
- [x] **Common KTU mistakes to avoid:** Confusing $X_n$ with $X_w$; stating oligomer–polymer boundary as 100 (use 20); forgetting to state assumptions of Carothers equation; using inconsistent units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polymer # Module 1: Introduction to Polymers — The "Polymer" Topic ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymer (IUPAC-aligned, KTU 2024 Definition):** A **polymer** is a high-molar-mass macromolecule composed of a large number of repeating structural units termed **monomeric units** (or *mers*), which are derived from low-molar-mass molecules known as **monomers**. The process by which these monomers are chemically linked into long chains is called **polymerisation**. The literal meaning is rooted in Greek: *poly* (many) + *meros* (parts) = **"many parts."** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Train Analogy:** Imagine a long goods train. > - Each **wagon** is a **monomer** (small unit). > - The **entire train** is the **polymer** (macromolecule). > - The **coupling link** between wagons is the **covalent bond** formed during polymerisation. > - Different wagon designs (open, tank, flatbed) = different monomers (e.g., ethylene, styrene, vinyl chloride) → producing different polymers (polyethylene, polystyrene, PVC). Just as a 50-wagon train is longer and heavier than a 10-wagon train, a polymer with a greater **number of repeating units** has a higher **molecular weight** and different mechanical properties. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Highlights) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---| | Repeat unit molar mass | $M_0$ | 28 – 200 g/mol | | Degree of Polymerisation | $DP$ or $\overline{X}_n$ | $10^2$ – $10^5$ | | Number-average molar mass | $\overline{M}_n$ | $10^4$ – $10^6$ g/mol | | Weight-average molar mass | $\overline{M}_w$ | $10^4$ – $10^7$ g/mol | | Polydispersity Index | $PDI = \overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n$ | 1.0 (monodisperse) – 20+ | ### 1.4 Terminology Hierarchy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Monomer** → Small molecule (e.g., ethylene, $CH_2=CH_2$) > **Repeat Unit (Monomeric Unit / Mer)** → The smallest structural unit whose repetition defines the polymer chain (e.g., $-CH_2-CH_2-$ in polyethylene). > **Polymer / Macromolecule** → The complete long-chain entity. **Why the distinction matters:** For polymers made from two different monomers (e.g., nylon-66 from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid), the *monomer* and the *repeat unit* are **not identical**. The repeat unit is the chemically meaningful structural segment. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Polymers (KTU Module 1 Core) Polymers are classified along multiple axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the following taxonomy: ### 2.1.1 By Origin 1. **Natural Polymers** — Found in nature. Examples: *cellulose, starch, natural rubber (polyisoprene), proteins, DNA, silk.* 2. **Synthetic Polymers** — Man-made via chemical polymerisation. Examples: *polyethylene (PE), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), polystyrene (PS), nylon, polyester.* 3. **Semi-synthetic (Modified Natural)** — Chemically altered natural polymers. Examples: *cellulose acetate, vulcanised rubber, rayon.* ### 2.1.2 By Structure / Topology 1. **Linear Polymers** — Long, unbranched chains (e.g., HDPE, PVC). 2. **Branched Polymers** — Linear backbone with side branches (e.g., LDPE, amylopectin). 3. **Cross-linked (Network) Polymers** — Covalent bonds linking multiple chains into a 3D network (e.g., vulcanised rubber, thermosets like Bakelite). ### 2.1.3 By Thermal Response (KTU High-Yield) 1. **Thermoplastic Polymers** — Linear/branched chains held by weak secondary forces. **Soften on heating, harden on cooling, can be remoulded.** Examples: PE, PP, PS, PVC, PET. 2. **Thermosetting Polymers** — Heavily cross-linked networks formed irreversibly during moulding. **Cannot be remelted/remoulded.** Examples: Bakelite, urea-formaldehyde, epoxy, melamine. 3. **Elastomers** — Lightly cross-linked polymers with long, flexible chains. **Exhibit large elastic recovery.** Examples: natural rubber, neoprene, silicone rubber. ### 2.1.4 By Polymerisation Mechanism 1. **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymers** — Formed by successive addition of monomers with unsaturated bonds (C=C, C≡C). No by-product. Examples: PE, PP, PS, PVC, PMMA. 2. **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymers** — Formed by stepwise reaction between bifunctional monomers with elimination of small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃). Examples: nylon, polyester, Bakelite. ### 2.1.5 By Homogeneity of Repeating Units 1. **Homopolymer** — Single type of repeat unit (e.g., polyethylene = all $-CH_2-CH_2-$). 2. **Copolymer** — Two or more different repeat units arranged in a chain. Sub-types: - Random (e.g., SBR rubber) - Alternating (e.g., nylon-66) - Block (e.g., SBS thermoplastic elastomer) - Graft (e.g., impact-modified PS) ### 2.1.6 By Chemical Nature - **Organic Polymers** — Backbone contains carbon (majority). - **Inorganic Polymers** — Backbone contains no carbon (e.g., silicones with $-Si-O-Si-O-$ backbone). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations) | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{M_0}{M_n}$ | Degree of Polymerisation (number-average) | dimensionless | | 2 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i}{\sum N_i \, / \, X_i}$ | Statistical definition of $\overline{X}_n$ | dimensionless | | 3 | $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \dfrac{\sum w_i}{\sum (w_i / M_i)}$ | Number-average molar mass | g/mol | | 4 | $\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \dfrac{\sum w_i M_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Weight-average molar mass | g/mol | | 5 | $\overline{X}_w = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{M_0}$ | Weight-average degree of polymerisation | dimensionless | | 6 | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Polydispersity Index (1 = monodisperse) | dimensionless | **Engineering Utility:** $PDI$ is the single most reported "fingerprint" of a polymer batch in industry. A low PDI (≈1.0–1.2) is desired for high-performance fibres and biomedical applications; a higher PDI (2–10) is acceptable for commodity packaging plastics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Boundary Condition:** > $\overline{M}_w \geq \overline{M}_n$, with **equality only when all chains have identical molar mass** (monodisperse, ideal case — never achieved in real synthesis). Therefore $PDI \geq 1$ **always**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number-Average Molar Mass ($\overline{M}_n$) ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup Suppose we have a polymer sample composed of $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, k$ distinct molecular species.
