For the $i$-th species: - $N_i$ = number of molecules (chains) - $M_i$ = molar mass of each chain - $w_i$ = total mass contribution ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Mass of the $i$-th species:** The total mass contributed by species $i$ is simply the number of molecules times the molar mass per molecule: $$w_i = N_i \, M_i$$ **Step 2 — Total mass of the sample:** Sum over all species present in the sample: $$w_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i$$ **Step 3 — Total number of molecules:** Sum of molecules across all species: $$N_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i$$ **Step 4 — Definition of number-average molar mass:** The "number-average" is the *arithmetic mean* of molar masses weighted by the number of molecules. By definition: $$\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i}$$ **Step 5 — Re-express in terms of mass fractions:** The mass fraction of species $i$ is defined as: $$x_i = \dfrac{w_i}{w_{total}} = \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i}$$ Substituting $w_i = N_i M_i$ and $N_i = w_i / M_i$ into the original equation: $$\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i} = \dfrac{\sum w_i}{\sum \left( w_i / M_i \right)}$$ This is the **mass-fraction form**, the form most easily measured experimentally by osmometry. ### 3.1.3 Numerical Illustration > A polymer sample contains three species: > - 5 molecules of $M_1 = 10{,}000$ g/mol > - 3 molecules of $M_2 = 50{,}000$ g/mol > - 2 molecules of $M_3 = 100{,}000$ g/mol **Compute numerator:** $$\sum N_i M_i = (5)(10{,}000) + (3)(50{,}000) + (2)(100{,}000)$$ $$= 50{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 200{,}000 = 400{,}000 \text{ g/mol (numerator in mass units)}$$ **Compute denominator:** $$\sum N_i = 5 + 3 + 2 = 10 \text{ molecules}$$ **Compute $\overline{M}_n$:** $$\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{400{,}000}{10} = 40{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Weight-Average Molar Mass ($\overline{M}_w$) ### 3.2.1 The "Why" Behind It $\overline{M}_n$ weighs each chain **equally regardless of size**. But big chains dominate the *bulk physical properties* (viscosity, strength, light scattering). $\overline{M}_w$ corrects this by weighting each species by its **mass contribution**, which inherently favours heavier chains. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the weighted contribution:** The "moment" of the mass distribution is $\sum w_i M_i$. We divide by the total mass $\sum w_i$ to normalise: $$\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i}$$ **Step 2 — Re-express using $N_i$ (count-based form):** Substituting $w_i = N_i M_i$: $$\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i \cdot M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$$ This is the **count-based form** — physically measured by light scattering or sedimentation. ### 3.2.3 Numerical Illustration (continuing the previous data) **Numerator:** $$\sum N_i M_i^2 = (5)(10{,}000)^2 + (3)(50{,}000)^2 + (2)(100{,}000)^2$$ $$= (5)(1 \times 10^8) + (3)(2.5 \times 10^9) + (2)(1 \times 10^{10})$$ $$= 5 \times 10^8 + 7.5 \times 10^9 + 2 \times 10^{10}$$ $$= 5 \times 10^8 + 75 \times 10^8 + 200 \times 10^8 = 280 \times 10^8$$ **Denominator:** (already computed) $$\sum N_i M_i = 400{,}000$$ **Compute $\overline{M}_w$:** $$\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{280 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^5} = \dfrac{2.8 \times 10^{10}}{4 \times 10^5} = 7 \times 10^4 = 70{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$ ### 3.2.4 Compute Polydispersity Index: $$PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \dfrac{70{,}000}{40{,}000} = 1.75$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check:** $\overline{M}_w > \overline{M}_n$ — **always true**, because $M_i^2$ weighting amplifies larger species. This sample has a *moderately broad* molecular-weight distribution. ## 3.3 Derivation: Degree of Polymerisation ($\overline{X}_n$) For a homopolymer, each repeat unit has molar mass $M_0$. For a chain of $X$ repeat units: $$M = X \cdot M_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = \dfrac{M}{M_0}$$ Averaging over the distribution: $$\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}, \qquad \overline{X}_w = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{M_0}$$ **Example:** For polyethylene, $M_0 = 28$ g/mol ($-CH_2-CH_2-$). If $\overline{M}_n = 280{,}000$ g/mol, then: $$\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{280{,}000}{28} = 10{,}000 \text{ repeat units per chain (on average)}$$ ### 3.4 Functionality Concept (Brief, KTU-relevant) > **Functionality ($f$):** Number of reactive sites per monomer molecule. > - Bifunctional ($f=2$) → linear polymer (e.g., ethylene in PE, adipic acid in nylon). > - Trifunctional or higher ($f \geq 3$) → branched/cross-linked polymer (e.g., glycerol in alkyd resins, phenol in Bakelite). **Carothers' rule (preview for Module 2):** Average functionality $\bar{f} > 2$ is required to form a network (cross-linked) polymer. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Polymers ```mermaid flowchart TD R0["POLYMERS"]:::root R0 --> A1["By Origin"] R0 --> A2["By Structure"] R0 --> A3["By Thermal Response"] R0 --> A4["By Polymerisation"] R0 --> A5["By Repeat Unit"] A1 --> A1a["Natural
cellulose, rubber"] A1 --> A1b["Synthetic
PE, PVC, nylon"] A1 --> A1c["Semi-synthetic
cellulose acetate"] A2 --> A2a["Linear
HDPE, PVC"] A2 --> A2b["Branched
LDPE, amylopectin"] A2 --> A2c["Cross-linked
vulcanised rubber, Bakelite"] A3 --> A3a["Thermoplastic
PE, PP, PS"] A3 --> A3b["Thermosetting
epoxy, urea-formaldehyde"] A3 --> A3c["Elastomer
natural rubber, silicone"] A4 --> A4a["Addition
PE, PS, PVC"] A4 --> A4b["Condensation
nylon, polyester"] A5 --> A5a["Homopolymer
single repeat unit"] A5 --> A5b["Copolymer
two or more repeat units"] A5b --> A5b1["Random"] A5b --> A5b2["Alternating"] A5b --> A5b3["Block"] A5b --> A5b4["Graft"] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Molecular Weight Distribution Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR M0["Monomer
CH2=CH2
M0 = 28 g/mol"]:::mono RU["Repeat Unit
CH2-CH2
M0 = 28 g/mol"]:::ru P1["Polymer Chain
n repeat units
M = n x M0"]:::chain DIST["Distribution of Chains
N1, N2, N3 ... Nk
with masses M1, M2 ... Mk"]:::dist Mn["Mn computed
sum NiMi / sum Ni"]:::calc Mw["Mw computed
sum NiMi^2 / sum NiMi"]:::calc PDI["PDI = Mw / Mn
greater than 1"]:::pdi M0 -->|polymerisation| RU RU -->|repeats n times| P1 P1 -->|many chains of varying n| DIST DIST --> Mn DIST --> Mw Mn --> PDI Mw --> PDI classDef mono fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000 classDef ru fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 classDef chain fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000 classDef dist fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#fff classDef pdi fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Copolymer Architectures (ASCII Block Diagram) ``` (A) HOMOPOLYMER: -AAAAA-AAAAA-AAAAA- (B) RANDOM COPOLYMER: -AABABABBBAABABBAA- (C) ALTERNATING COPOLYMER: -ABABABABABABABAB- (D) BLOCK COPOLYMER: -AAAAAA-BBBBBB-AAAAAA- (E) GRAFT COPOLYMER: -AAAAAAAAAAAAA- | | | B B B B B B B B B ``` | Architecture | Example Polymer | Industrial Application | |---|---|---| | Random | SBR (styrene–butadiene rubber) | Tyre treads | | Alternating | Nylon-66 | Textile fibres | | Block | SBS (styrene–butadiene–styrene) | Thermoplastic elastomers, shoe soles | | Graft | HIPS (high-impact polystyrene) | Toughened packaging | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Define the term "polymer" with a suitable example. Distinguish between a monomer and a repeat unit.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **polymer** is a high-molar-mass macromolecule composed of a large number of repeating structural units called *monomeric units*, formed by the chemical linking of small molecules called *monomers* (3 marks for definition + 1-line example). - **Monomer:** The small, low-molar-mass molecule that participates in polymerisation. *Example:* ethylene ($CH_2=CH_2$). - **Repeat Unit (Mer):** The smallest structural unit whose repetition constitutes the polymer chain. *Example:* $-CH_2-CH_2-$ in polyethylene. For homopolymers, the repeat unit has the same chemical formula as the monomer (minus the unsaturation). For copolymers (e.g., nylon-66), monomer and repeat unit **differ** in identity. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** **What is meant by "degree of polymerisation"? How is it related to the number-average molar mass?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **degree of polymerisation (DP or $\overline{X}_n$)** is the average number of repeat units (monomeric units) present per polymer chain. It is mathematically related to the number-average molar mass by: $$\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$$ where $M_0$ is the molar mass of a single repeat unit. For a homopolymer, $\overline{X}_n$ is dimensionless and typically ranges from $10^2$ to $10^5$. For example, commercial polyethylene with $\overline{M}_n = 280{,}000$ g/mol and $M_0 = 28$ g/mol has $\overline{X}_n = 10{,}000$. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### ✅ Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Classification + Molecular Weight **Q3(a). [7 Marks]** Classify polymers on the basis of (i) thermal response and (ii) polymerisation mechanism. Give two examples for each class and mention at least one characteristic application. **Q3(b). [7 Marks]** A polymer sample consists of four species with the following data: | Species | Number of molecules $N_i$ | Molar mass $M_i$ (g/mol) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 10 | 5,000 | | 2 | 20 | 25,000 | | 3 | 15 | 75,000 | | 4 | 5 | 150,000 | Calculate $\overline{M}_n$, $\overline{M}_w$, and the Polydispersity Index. Comment on the breadth of the distribution. --- ### ✅ Question B (14 Marks): Copolymers + Functionality Concept **Q4(a). [7 Marks]** Differentiate between homopolymers and copolymers. With neat schematic diagrams, describe any four types of copolymer architectures and give one industrial example for each. **Q4(b). [7 Marks]** Define the term **functionality** of a monomer. Explain how the average functionality of a monomer mixture determines whether the resulting polymer is linear, branched, or cross-linked. Compute the average functionality of a mixture containing 2 mol of glycerol ($f=3$) and 3 mol of ethylene glycol ($f=2$). --- ## 5.3 Model Solutions (Part B) ### Model Solution to Q3(a) — Classification **(i) Classification by Thermal Response:** 1. **Thermoplastic Polymers:** Linear or branched chains held by weak secondary forces (van der Waals, dipole). They **soften on heating and harden on cooling reversibly**; can be remoulded multiple times. - *Example 1:* Polyethylene (PE) → plastic bags, bottles. - *Example 2:* Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) → pipes, cable insulation. 2. **Thermosetting Polymers (Thermosets):** Heavily cross-linked 3D networks formed irreversibly during moulding via covalent cross-links. **Cannot be remelted or remoulded.** - *Example 1:* Bakelite (phenol-formaldehyde resin) → electrical switches, handles. - *Example 2:* Epoxy resin → adhesives, composite matrices. 3. **Elastomers:** Lightly cross-linked polymers with long, flexible chains above their glass-transition temperature. Exhibit **large reversible elastic deformation** (>>100%). - *Example 1:* Vulcanised natural rubber → tyres, gaskets. - *Example 2:* Silicone rubber → medical implants, sealants. **(ii) Classification by Polymerisation Mechanism:** 1. **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerisation:** Monomers with unsaturated bonds (C=C, C≡C) add successively to an active chain end. **No small-molecule by-product.** Initiated by radicals, ions, or metal catalysts. - *Example 1:* Polyethylene from ethylene. - *Example 2:* Polystyrene from styrene. 2. **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerisation:** Bifunctional monomers react stepwise with the elimination of small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃). By-product is removed to drive equilibrium forward. - *Example 1:* Nylon-66 from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid (by-product: H₂O). - *Example 2:* PET from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid (by-product: H₂O). --- ### Model Solution to Q3(b) — Molecular Weight Calculation **Given data (reproduced):** | Species | $N_i$ | $M_i$ (g/mol) | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^2$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 10 | 5,000 | 50,000 | $2.5 \times 10^8$ | | 2 | 20 | 25,000 | 500,000 | $1.25 \times 10^{10}$ | | 3 | 15 | 75,000 | 1,125,000 | $8.4375 \times 10^{10}$ | | 4 | 5 | 150,000 | 750,000 | $1.125 \times 10^{11}$ | | **Σ** | **50** | — | **2,425,000** | **2.125 × 10¹¹** | **Calculation of $\overline{M}_n$:** $$\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \dfrac{2{,}425{,}000}{50} = 48{,}500 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Calculation of $\overline{M}_w$:** $$\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \dfrac{2.125 \times 10^{11}}{2.425 \times 10^6} = 87{,}629 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Calculation of PDI:** $$PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \dfrac{87{,}629}{48{,}500} \approx 1.81$$ **Comment:** A PDI of 1.81 indicates a **moderately broad** molecular-weight distribution, typical of polymers synthesised by condensation polymerisation or free-radical addition polymerisation. Living/controlled polymerisation techniques (e.g., ATRP, RAFT) can produce PDI values closer to 1.0–1.1. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting that $N_i M_i$ carries units of g/mol (numerator mass), and dividing by $N_i$ (dimensionless count) yields g/mol — *do not write kg/mol*. > 2. **Sign error:** $\overline{M}_w > \overline{M}_n$ is a **mandatory** inequality; if a student computes $\overline{M}_w < \overline{M}_n$, they have done a mathematical error (lose 2 marks). > 3. **Forgetting PDI formula:** PDI is **not** requested often by name, but examiners *expect* a "comment on breadth." Always compute and interpret it. --- ### Model Solution to Q4(a) — Copolymers A **homopolymer** is built from a single type of repeat unit — its chain contains only one kind of structural segment (e.g., $-AAAAA-$). A **copolymer** is built from two or more chemically distinct repeat units (e.g., $-AABABBAA-$). **Four copolymer architectures:** ``` (a) RANDOM: -AABABBABBABAABBA- (b) ALTERNATING: -ABABABABABABABABAB- (c) BLOCK: -AAAAAAA-BBBBBB-AAAAAAA- (d) GRAFT: -AAAAAAAAAAAA- | | | B B B B B B ``` | Architecture | Schematic feature | Industrial Example | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Random** | A and B units distributed statistically | SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) | Tyre treads | | **Alternating** | Strict A-B-A-B-... sequence | Nylon-66 | Fibres, ropes | | **Block** | Long A-segments and B-segments joined end-to-end | SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene) | Thermoplastic elastomers, shoe soles | | **Graft** | B side-chains grafted onto A backbone | HIPS (High-Impact Polystyrene) | Toughened packaging, toys | --- ### Model Solution to Q4(b) — Functionality **Definition (3 marks):** The **functionality ($f$)** of a monomer is defined as the number of reactive sites (functional groups) available for participation in polymerisation per molecule. A monomer with $f = 2$ is *bifunctional*; with $f = 3$ it is *trifunctional*. **Effect of average functionality on polymer architecture (3 marks):** | $\bar{f}$ of monomer mixture | Resulting polymer architecture | |---|---| | $\bar{f} = 2.00$ | Linear polymer | | $2 < \bar{f} < 2$ (slightly above 2) | Branched polymer | | $\bar{f} \gg 2$ (well above critical value) | Cross-linked network (thermoset/elastomer) | **Numerical Calculation (1 mark):** $$\bar{f} = \dfrac{\sum n_i f_i}{\sum n_i} = \dfrac{(2)(3) + (3)(2)}{2 + 3} = \dfrac{6 + 6}{5} = \dfrac{12}{5} = 2.4$$ Since $\bar{f} = 2.4 > 2$, the resulting polymer will be a **branched/cross-linked network**, not a linear chain. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Polymer" Topic (Module 1)** - ✅ **Polymer** = many *mers* (parts) joined by covalent bonds. Greek etymology. - ✅ **Monomer** ≠ **Repeat Unit** in general. They coincide only for homopolymers made from a single difunctional monomer. - ✅ Five primary classification axes: Origin, Structure (Linear/Branched/Cross-linked), Thermal Response (Thermoplastic/Thermoset/Elastomer), Mechanism (Addition/Condensation), and Composition (Homo/Copolymer). - ✅ **Thermoplastic vs Thermoset vs Elastomer:** Remouldable | Not remouldable | Elastic recovery. - ✅ **Copolymer architectures:** Random, Alternating, Block, Graft — each with distinct industrial uses. - ✅ **Number-average molar mass:** $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ — measured by **osmometry**. - ✅ **Weight-average molar mass:** $\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ — measured by **light scattering**. - ✅ **PDI = $\overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n \geq 1$**, with equality only for monodisperse (theoretical) systems. - ✅ **Degree of Polymerisation:** $\overline{X}_n = \overline{M}_n / M_0$ — dimensionless count of repeat units per chain. - ✅ **Functionality rule:** $\bar{f} = 2$ → linear; $\bar{f} > 2$ → branched/cross-linked (Carothers' principle). - ✅ **Mandator inequality** to remember on the answer script: $\overline{M}_w \geq \overline{M}_n$ — a violation = calculation error, lose marks. - ✅ **Polyethylene example to memorize:** $M_0 = 28$ g/mol; $\overline{M}_n = 280{,}000$ → $\overline{X}_n = 10{,}000$.

topics.title: Degree of Polymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degree of Polymerization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Degree of Polymerization (DP or X)** is formally defined as the number of **repeating structural monomer units (mer units)** covalently linked together to form a single, long-chain macromolecule. It is the most fundamental quantitative descriptor of polymer chain length and is directly proportional to the molecular weight of the polymer.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the molecular weight of the polymer macromolecule ($M$) to the molecular weight of the single repeating unit ($M_0$):

$$DP = X = \frac{M}{M_0}$$

Since a real polymer sample is **polydisperse** (containing chains of varying lengths), the term is rigorously expressed in two statistical averages:
- **Number-Average Degree of Polymerization** ($X_n$)
- **Weight-Average Degree of Polymerization** ($X_w$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term Degree of Polymerization is not a single number for a polymer sample — it is a statistical distribution. The most common exam-trap is assuming all chains in a sample have identical DP. **Always use averages.**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **passenger train**. The *locomotive* is the initiator, and each *railway coach attached behind it* is one monomer unit.
- A train with **100 coaches** → DP = 100
- A train with **1,000 coaches** → DP = 1,000

Now consider a railway yard that contains trains of different lengths — short trains with 50 coaches, medium trains with 500 coaches, and long trains with 5,000 coaches. The yard's overall statistics are described by the **average degree of polymerization**.

In the same way, a real polymer sample is a "yard" of macromolecular "trains" of varying lengths, and we describe them using number-averages and weight-averages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology**
> - **Monomer:** The small, single repeat unit (e.g., ethylene, $CH_2=CH_2$).
> - **Mer:** The structural unit in the polymer chain (e.g., $-CH_2-CH_2-$ in polyethylene).
> - **Polymer:** A high molecular weight macromolecule composed of $X$ mers joined end-to-end.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

- $M_0$ = Molecular weight of the **repeating unit** (mer). Units: **g/mol** or **kg/kmol**.
- $M$ = Molecular weight of the **polymer chain**. Units: **g/mol**.
- $X$ or $DP$ = **Dimensionless** integer.
- $X_n$ = **Number-average** degree of polymerization.
- $X_w$ = **Weight-average** degree of polymerization.
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** = $X_w / X_n$. PDI = 1.0 for perfectly monodisperse samples.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Degree of Polymerization

The DP of a polymer is not a fixed value; it depends on the mechanism of polymerization. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects mastery of the following logical steps:

1. **Identify the polymerization type** (Step-growth vs. Chain-growth).
2. **Determine the extent of reaction ($p$)** — the fraction of functional groups that have reacted. $p$ ranges from 0 to 1.
3. **Apply the appropriate Carothers' equation** to relate DP to $p$.
4. **Account for chain transfer and termination mechanisms** in free-radical polymerizations.
5. **Convert DP to molecular weight** using $M = X \cdot M_0$.

### 2.1.1 Step-Growth (Condensation) Polymerization

In step-growth polymerization, **any two molecular species** (monomer + monomer, monomer + oligomer, oligomer + oligomer) can react. The DP grows slowly at first, then rapidly near the end of the reaction.

**Carothers' Equation (Fundamental relationship):**

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

- When $p = 0$ (no reaction) → $X_n = 1$ (only monomers)
- When $p = 0.99$ (99% conversion) → $X_n = 100$
- When $p = 0.999$ (99.9% conversion) → $X_n = 1000$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Achieving high molecular weight in step-growth polymerization requires **extremely high conversion** (typically $> 99\%$). Even a small amount of unreacted monomer drastically reduces DP. This is why PET, nylon, and polyesters need precise stoichiometry and high-purity monomers.

For **non-stoichiometric (off-ratio) systems** with two monomers A and B, where the stoichiometric imbalance ratio is $r$:

$$X_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

Where $r = N_A / N_B$ (ratio of functional groups, with $r \leq 1$).

### 2.1.2 Chain-Growth (Addition) Polymerization

In chain-growth polymerization, polymer chains grow by rapid addition of monomers to **active chain ends** (radicals, ions, or metal catalysts). The relationship between DP and $p$ depends on the termination mechanism.

**For free-radical polymerization with bimolecular termination:**

- **Termination by combination** (two growing chains fuse):

$$X_n = \frac{2}{2 - p(2 + f)}$$

- **Termination by disproportionation** (chain transfer of H atom):

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p(1 + f)}$$

Where $f$ is the **fraction of chains terminated by chain transfer** events (with initiator-derived radicals, solvent, etc.).

**Kinetic-chain length ($\nu$) and DP relationship:**

$$X_n = 2\nu \quad \text{(combination)} \qquad X_n = \nu \quad \text{(disproportionation)}$$

Where the kinetic chain length is:

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{R_p}{R_t}$$

Here $R_p$ = rate of propagation, $R_i$ = rate of initiation, $R_t$ = rate of termination.

### 2.1.3 Mayo Equation (with Chain Transfer Agent)

When a chain transfer agent (CTA) like a solvent or a regulator is present, DP is reduced:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + C_s \cdot \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \cdot \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

Where:
- $C_s$ = chain transfer constant to solvent
- $C_I$ = chain transfer constant to initiator
- $[S], [I], [M]$ = concentrations of solvent, initiator, monomer
- $(X_n)_0$ = DP in the absence of CTA

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Equation** | **Application** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic DP | $X = M / M_0$ | Single chain | Dimensionless |
| Number-average DP | $X_n = M_n / M_0$ | Statistical average (count basis) | Dimensionless |
| Weight-average DP | $X_w = M_w / M_0$ | Statistical average (mass basis) | Dimensionless |
| Carothers' (step-growth) | $X_n = 1 / (1 - p)$ | Equal-ratio condensation | Dimensionless |
| Carothers' (off-ratio) | $X_n = (1+r) / (1+r-2rp)$ | Non-stoichiometric step-growth | Dimensionless |
| Combination termination | $X_n = 2 / [2 - p(2+f)]$ | Free-radical, chain fusion | Dimensionless |
| Disproportionation | $X_n = 1 / [1 - p(1+f)]$ | Free-radical, H-transfer | Dimensionless |
| Kinetic chain length | $\nu = R_p / R_i$ | Chain-growth kinetics | Dimensionless |
| Mayo equation | $1/X_n = 1/(X_n)_0 + C_s[S]/[M]$ | With chain transfer | Dimensionless |
| Polydispersity Index | $PDI = X_w / X_n$ | Molecular weight distribution | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The DP directly controls the **mechanical, thermal, rheological, and processing** properties of polymers. For example, increasing the DP of polyethylene from 1,000 to 100,000 transforms it from a low-melting wax into a tough engineering plastic suitable for containers and pipes. The textile industry controls the DP of PET fibers to achieve specific tenacity and dye uptake. In paints and coatings, low DP oligomers (prepolymers) are used for low viscosity, then crosslinked in-situ to achieve the final high-DP network.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Carothers' Equation (Step-Growth)

We will derive the relationship $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ for a simple A–B type condensation polymerization (e.g., HOOC–R–COOH + HO–R'–OH → polyester + water).

### Step 1: Define the initial system

Let:
- $N_0$ = total initial number of monomer molecules in the reaction mixture at $t = 0$
- $N$ = total number of molecules (of all sizes) remaining at time $t$

### Step 2: Define the extent of reaction

The extent of reaction $p$ is defined as the **fraction of functional groups that have reacted** at time $t$:

$$p = \frac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Number of functional groups initially present}}$$

### Step 3: Count the number of bonds formed

Each bond formed joins **two molecules** into one. The number of new bonds (condensation linkages) formed at time $t$ is:

$$\text{Bonds formed} = p \cdot N_0$$

(Because each molecule initially has at least one functional group; the count is direct.)

### Step 4: Apply the law of conservation of mass

Initially there were $N_0$ molecules. Each bond formation reduces the molecule count by exactly 1 (two molecules become one). Therefore:

$$N = N_0 - (\text{Number of bonds formed})$$

Substituting the result from Step 3:

$$N = N_0 - p \cdot N_0 = N_0(1 - p)$$

### Step 5: Define the number-average degree of polymerization

By definition, the number-average DP is the initial number of monomer units divided by the number of molecules present at time $t$:

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Step 4 into Step 5

Substituting $N = N_0(1-p)$:

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1 - p)}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

This is the celebrated **Carothers' Equation** for equimolar step-growth polymerization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** The equation tells us that to reach $X_n = 100$ (a useful polymer), we need $p = 0.99$ (99% conversion). To reach $X_n = 10{,}000$ (a high-performance polymer), we need $p = 0.9999$ (99.99% conversion). The polymer chain length grows *asymptotically* as $p \to 1$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Mayo Equation

### Step 1: Identify all chain-ending events

In free-radical polymerization, a growing chain radical (P•) can be terminated by:
1. **Bimolecular termination** with another radical → $R_t$ (gives $X_n$ = $(X_n)_0$ in absence of CTA)
2. **Chain transfer to solvent** (S–H + P• → P–H + S•) → reduces DP
3. **Chain transfer to initiator** → reduces DP
4. **Chain transfer to monomer** (often small, neglected here)

### Step 2: Rate of chain propagation

The rate at which monomer units are added to chains:

$$R_p = k_p [M \cdot] [M]$$

### Step 3: Rate of chain termination (no CTA)

$$R_t = 2 k_t [M \cdot]^2$$

### Step 4: Number-average DP without chain transfer

The DP equals the number of monomer units added per chain initiated:

$$(X_n)_0 = \frac{R_p}{R_i}$$

In steady state, $R_i = R_t$, so:

$$(X_n)_0 = \frac{R_p}{R_t} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 k_t [M \cdot]}$$

### Step 5: With chain transfer to solvent

Now there is an additional chain-ending pathway:

$$R_{tr,S} = k_{tr,S} [M \cdot] [S]$$

The total rate of chain formation (chain "starts" + "transfers") is $R_i + R_{tr,S}$, and the total rate of monomer addition is still $R_p$. The new DP is:

$$X_n = \frac{R_p}{R_i + R_{tr,S}}$$

### Step 6: Invert and add similar terms

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{R_i}{R_p} + \frac{R_{tr,S}}{R_p} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + \frac{k_{tr,S} [S]}{k_p [M]}$$

### Step 7: Introduce the chain transfer constant

Define $C_s = k_{tr,S} / k_p$:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + C_s \frac{[S]}{[M]}$$

### Step 8: Add initiator contribution

A similar derivation for chain transfer to initiator (with constant $C_I$) yields the **full Mayo equation**:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + C_s \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Carothers' Equation)

**Problem:** Nylon-6,6 is synthesized by step-growth polymerization of adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine in 1:1 molar ratio. If the extent of reaction $p = 0.995$, calculate the number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$.

**Solution:**

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.995}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1}{0.005}$$

$$X_n = 200$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means an average of 200 monomer units are linked per chain — typical for a textile-grade nylon-6,6 fiber. Engineering-grade nylon requires $p > 0.999$, giving $X_n > 1000$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Off-Ratio Carothers)

**Problem:** A step-growth polymerization uses 1.05 mol of diamine and 1.00 mol of diacid. If the reaction is driven to $p = 0.98$, find $X_n$.

**Solution:**

$$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{1.00}{1.05} = 0.9524$$

$$X_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1 + 0.9524}{1 + 0.9524 - 2(0.9524)(0.98)}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1.9524}{1 + 0.9524 - 1.8667}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1.9524}{0.0857}$$

$$X_n \approx 22.78$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The off-ratio dramatically reduces $X_n$ compared to the equimolar case ($X_n = 50$ for $p = 0.98$ at $r = 1$). **Stoichiometric balance is critical.**

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Step-Growth (Condensation)** | **Chain-Growth (Addition)** |
|---|---|---|
| Chain growth rate | Slow, gradual | Fast, instantaneous |
| Monomer disappearance | Early in reaction | Late in reaction |
| DP at low conversion | Low (1–10) | High ($10^3$–$10^5$) even at low $p$ |
| Equation (ideal) | $X_n = 1/(1-p)$ | $X_n = 2/[2-p(2+f)]$ |
| Stoichiometry effect | Critical | Less critical |
| By-products | Yes (e.g., H₂O, HCl) | No |
| Chain transfer importance | Less | High |
| Example polymers | PET, Nylon-6,6, PC | PE, PP, PS, PVC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Degree of Polymerization Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input: Monomer Identity] --> nodeB[Calculate M0 Molecular Weight of Repeat Unit]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Polymerization Mechanism}
    nodeC -->|Step Growth| nodeD[Determine Extent p and Stoichiometric Ratio r]
    nodeC -->|Chain Growth| nodeE[Determine Termination Type and Chain Transfer]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Apply Carothers Equation Xn equals 1 over 1 minus p]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Apply Termination Specific Equation]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Apply Mayo Equation for CTA Effect]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Output: Number Average DP Xn]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute Weight Average Xw and PDI]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Final Output: Molecular Weight M equals Xn times M0]
    nodeI --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Carothers Equation Behavior (DP vs Conversion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[p equals 0.0] -->|Xn equals 1| nodeB[Only monomer]
    nodeC[p equals 0.90] -->|Xn equals 10| nodeD[Short oligomers]
    nodeE[p equals 0.99] -->|Xn equals 100| nodeF[Polymer begins]
    nodeG[p equals 0.999] -->|Xn equals 1000| nodeH[Engineering plastic]
    nodeI[p equals 0.9999] -->|Xn equals 10000| nodeJ[High performance polymer]
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeJ
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Polymer Chain Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Initiator Residue] --> nodeB[Repeating Unit 1]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Repeating Unit 2]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Repeating Unit 3]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Dotted Continuation]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Repeating Unit n minus 1]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Repeating Unit n]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Chain End Group]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Total Mer Count equals DP equals Xn]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** Every polymer chain consists of:
> 1. **One initiator/initiation residue** (from step-growth or chain-growth)
> 2. **X repeating units (mers)** in the middle
> 3. **Two chain ends** (one is the initiator; the other is the termination product)
>
> For a chain of DP = $n$, there are exactly $n$ mers. The molecular weight of the chain is $M = n \cdot M_0 + M_{\text{end groups}}$ (end groups often neglected for high $n$).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Effects Influencing Degree of Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Degree of Polymerization] --> nodeB[Mechanism Factor]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Conversion Factor p]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Stoichiometry Factor r]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Chain Transfer Factor]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Temperature Factor]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Step growth uses Carothers]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Chain growth uses Termination Equation]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Higher p gives higher DP]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[r closer to 1 gives higher DP]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Higher CTA gives lower DP]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Higher T changes kp and kt and CTA]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Final DP Value]
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "degree of polymerization". How is it related to the number-average molecular weight of a polymer?**

**Model Answer:**
The degree of polymerization (DP or $X$) is defined as the total number of repeating structural monomer units (mers) in a polymer chain. For a polydisperse polymer sample, the number-average degree of polymerization is defined as:

$$X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0}$$

where $M_n$ is the number-average molecular weight and $M_0$ is the molecular weight of the repeating unit. It is a dimensionless quantity. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Carothers' equation for the degree of polymerization in a step-growth polymerization. What does it signify?**

**Model Answer:**
For a step-growth polymerization with equal stoichiometric ratio and extent of reaction $p$:

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

It signifies that high molecular weight polymers can only be obtained at very high extents of reaction (typically $p > 0.99$). It also shows the asymptotic relationship between $X_n$ and $p$. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Carothers' equation relating the number-average degree of polymerization to the extent of reaction for an equimolar step-growth polymerization.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Let $N_0$ be the initial number of monomer molecules at time $t=0$.

**Step 2:** Let $N$ be the number of molecules (of all sizes) at time $t$.

**Step 3:** The extent of reaction $p$ is the fraction of functional groups that have reacted. Therefore, the number of bonds formed = $p N_0$.

**Step 4:** Each bond formation reduces the total molecule count by 1 (two molecules become one):

$$N = N_0 - p N_0 = N_0 (1 - p)$$

**Step 5:** By definition of number-average DP:

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

This is the Carothers' equation. (7 marks)

**(b) A polyester is synthesized by reacting 1.2 mol of ethylene glycol with 1.0 mol of terephthalic acid. If the reaction is carried out to $p = 0.97$, calculate (i) the number-average degree of polymerization, and (ii) the number-average molecular weight of the resulting polyester. (Given: $M_0$ of repeat unit = 192 g/mol)** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Degree of Polymerization:**

Stoichiometric ratio (with glycol as the excess component):

$$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{1.0}{1.2} = 0.833$$

Apply the off-ratio Carothers' equation:

$$X_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1 + 0.833}{1 + 0.833 - 2(0.833)(0.97)}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1.833}{1 + 0.833 - 1.616}$$

$$X_n = \frac{1.833}{0.217}$$

$$X_n = 8.45 \approx 8$$

**(ii) Number-Average Molecular Weight:**

$$M_n = X_n \times M_0 = 8.45 \times 192 = 1622.4 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_n \approx 1622 \text{ g/mol}}$$

(7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the concept of chain transfer in free-radical polymerization. Derive the Mayo equation for degree of polymerization in the presence of a chain transfer agent.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Chain Transfer:**
Chain transfer is a chain-ending event in which a growing polymer radical $\text{P}\cdot$ abstracts an atom (typically H or Cl) from a chain transfer agent (CTA) such as solvent, initiator, monomer, or a deliberately added regulator. The result is:
- The original growing chain is terminated (becomes a dead polymer).
- A new radical is generated from the CTA, which can start a new chain.

This reduces the number-average degree of polymerization.

**Derivation of Mayo Equation:**

In the absence of chain transfer:

$$(X_n)_0 = \frac{R_p}{R_i}$$

When chain transfer to solvent is present, the additional chain-ending rate is:

$$R_{tr,S} = k_{tr,S} [\text{M}\cdot] [S]$$

The new DP is:

$$X_n = \frac{R_p}{R_i + R_{tr,S}}$$

Inverting:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{R_i + R_{tr,S}}{R_p} = \frac{R_i}{R_p} + \frac{R_{tr,S}}{R_p}$$

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + \frac{k_{tr,S} [S]}{k_p [M]}$$

Defining the chain transfer constant $C_s = k_{tr,S} / k_p$:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + C_s \frac{[S]}{[M]}$$

Adding the initiator contribution gives the full Mayo equation:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + C_s \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

(7 marks)

**(b) In the bulk polymerization of styrene initiated by AIBN, the following data are obtained: $C_M = 0.85 \times 10^{-4}$, $C_I = 0.025$, $[I] = 0.01$ mol/L, $[M] = 8.0$ mol/L, and the DP in absence of any transfer = 500. Calculate the actual $X_n$ of polystyrene. (Neglect solvent term.)** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The Mayo equation (with monomer and initiator transfer):

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + C_M \frac{[M]}{[M]} + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

Wait — the chain transfer to monomer term in standard form is $C_M \cdot \frac{[M]}{[M]}$ when written in the denominator convention. Let us use the conventional form:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{(X_n)_0} + C_M + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

(This is the simplified form, valid when transfer is to monomer only, $[M]$ cancels in the ratio.)

Substituting values:

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = \frac{1}{500} + (0.85 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.025) \cdot \frac{0.01}{8.0}$$

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = 0.0020 + 0.000085 + 0.0000313$$

$$\frac{1}{X_n} = 0.0021163$$

$$X_n = \frac{1}{0.0021163} = 472.5$$

$$\boxed{X_n \approx 472}$$

The chain transfer events have reduced the DP from 500 to about 472. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $M_0$ (molecular weight of mer) with $M_{\text{monomer}}$ (molecular weight of monomer).** For polymers like polyethylene, these are equal. But for PVC, $M_{\text{monomer}}$ (vinyl chloride = 62.5) and $M_0$ (–CH₂–CHCl– = 62.5) are equal; however, for polymers where monomer loses a small molecule (e.g., water in condensation), $M_0$ may differ. Always read the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting stoichiometry in off-ratio problems.** When reactants are not in 1:1 ratio, students often blindly use $X_n = 1/(1-p)$, losing 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Mixing $X_n$ and $X_w$.** Numerical problems specifying number-average molecular weight $M_n$ must use $X_n$, not $X_w$.
> 4. **Sign errors in the off-ratio Carothers' equation.** The denominator is $1 + r - 2rp$, not $1 - r + 2rp$. Re-derive it from scratch in the exam if uncertain.
> 5. **Ignoring the difference between combination and disproportionation** in free-radical polymerizations — this leads to wrong factor of 2 in the answer.
> 6. **Not specifying units** in the final answer. Always write "g/mol" or "kg/kmol" for molecular weights and "dimensionless" for DP.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DP definition:** Number of repeating mers in a polymer chain. $X = M / M_0$.
- **Two statistical averages:** $X_n$ (number-average) and $X_w$ (weight-average).
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI):** $X_w / X_n$. Equals 1.0 for monodisperse; > 1.0 for real polymers.
- **Carothers' equation (equal stoichiometry):** $X_n = 1 / (1 - p)$.
- **Off-ratio Carothers:** $X_n = (1 + r) / (1 + r - 2rp)$.
- **Chain-growth combination:** $X_n = 2 / [2 - p(2 + f)]$.
- **Chain-growth disproportionation:** $X_n = 1 / [1 - p(1 + f)]$.
- **Kinetic chain length:** $\nu = R_p / R_i = R_p / R_t$.
- **Mayo equation:** $1/X_n = 1/(X_n)_0 + C_s [S]/[M] + C_I [I]/[M]$.
- **High conversion is critical** in step-growth: $X_n = 100$ requires $p = 0.99$.
- **Step-growth needs exact stoichiometry** — imbalance drastically reduces $X_n$.
- **Chain transfer reduces DP** — useful for controlling molecular weight in industry (e.g., using CCl₄ as CTA in PVC).
- **PDI depends on mechanism:** Step-growth typically gives PDI ≈ 2; chain-growth gives PDI between 1.5 and 2.0; living polymerization gives PDI → 1.
- **Always quote the equation you are using** with proper variables defined.
- **Numericals must show full step-by-step substitution** — partial substitution is penalized in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of Polymer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Representation of Polymer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **polymer** is a high molecular weight compound formed by the chemical combination of a large number of small repeating structural units called **monomers**. The fundamental representations of a polymer are standardized methods used to express its chemical identity, composition, and architecture in a concise, unambiguous, and reproducible form.

According to **IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry)** recommendations, a polymer is represented through three hierarchical levels:

1. **Monomer (M):** The small, reactive molecule from which the polymer is synthesized.
2. **Mer / Repeat Unit:** The smallest structural unit whose repetition describes the constitution of the polymer chain.
3. **Constitutional Repeat Unit (CRU):** The smallest unit whose repetition produces the complete constitutional (atomic connectivity) sequence of the polymer chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The representation of polymers is foundational to Module 1 because every subsequent topic — polymerisation mechanisms, molecular weight calculations, classification, and properties — depends on correctly identifying and writing the repeat unit, the mer, and the end groups of the polymer chain.

## 1.2 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition | Role in Representation |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer** | Low molecular weight starting molecule | Becomes the residue inside the chain |
| **Mer** | The single repeating unit of the chain | Used for simplified chemical representation |
| **Repeat Unit** | The structural fragment that recurs in the chain | Basis of CRU identification |
| **Constitutional Repeat Unit (CRU)** | The IUPAC-endorsed smallest constitutional repeating sequence | Universal IUPAC representation |
| **End Group** | The terminal atoms/groups of a finite chain | Defines the linear formula $M_n = n \cdot M_0 + M_e$ |
| **Degree of Polymerisation (DP, $\bar{X}_n$)** | Number of repeat units per chain | Determines the formula $M_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Train of Bricks" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a long passenger train. Each passenger coach is identical — a sleeper, an AC coach, a pantry car. The train is the **polymer**, the type of coach is the **monomer**, a single coach is the **mer/repeat unit**, and the locomotive + the guard's van at the two ends are the **end groups**. If you describe the train by showing just **one coach with a "..." in between**, you have written the **polymer representation**. The fact that the chain has a beginning and an end (end groups) is what makes the *theoretical* infinite chain different from a *real* finite polymer molecule.

This analogy clarifies a vital KTU point: **real polymers are finite**, so their representation must, in principle, account for the two terminal end groups. However, since the chain length $\bar{X}_n$ is large (typically $10^3$ to $10^5$), the contribution of the end groups to the molecular formula is usually neglected in KTU-level problems, except when explicitly asked.

## 1.4 Why Polymer Representation is Different from Small-Molecule Chemistry

In small-molecule chemistry, a compound is fully described by one **exact structural formula** (e.g., ethanol: $CH_3CH_2OH$). Polymers, however, are **mixtures of chains** of different lengths (polydisperse), and the chains are built by repeating one or more units. Hence polymer representation is a **statistical, simplified, convention-based** exercise — not a single molecular drawing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Standard Methods of Representing a Polymer

KTU 2024 Module-1 expects a student to be able to convert a monomer (or a pair of monomers) into all five representational forms. They are listed below in the order of increasing compactness.

### Method 1 — Full Structural Formula (Dot-and-Line Representation)

The complete Lewis structure showing **every atom, every bond, and every end group**. This is the most explicit and least ambiguous form. It is rarely drawn in industry but is essential for understanding the topology (head-to-tail vs head-to-head linkages).

**Example:** Polyethylene (PE) from ethylene ($CH_2=CH_2$)

$$H-CH_2-CH_2-(\cdots-CH_2-CH_2-)_n-CH_2-CH_2-H$$

The terminal $H$ atoms are the **end groups**.

### Method 2 — Repeat-Unit Representation (CRU-Based)

The polymer is written as a single bracketed CRU followed by the subscript $\boldsymbol{n}$.

$$\text{Polyethylene} \;\longrightarrow\; \left[-CH_2-CH_2-\right]_n$$

The CRU is identified using the **IUPAC Seniority Rules** (drawn later in Section 4). For vinyl polymers ($CH_2=CHX$), the CRU is always written as $-CH_2-CHX-$ (head-to-tail orientation) because that is the kinetically and thermodynamically preferred mode of addition.

### Method 3 — Condensed (Abbreviated) Structural Formula

A compact text form that uses parentheses and the subscript to convey repetition. This is the most commonly used form in textbooks and in KTU answer scripts.

$$\left[CH_2-CH_2\right]_n \quad \text{or} \quad -(CH_2CH_2)_n-$$

### Method 4 — Schematic / Block Representation

A symbolic form used in textbooks, slides, and reaction schemes where the CRU is replaced by a circle, a square, or a letter. This is used when the chemistry of the *chain architecture* (block, graft, alternating) is more important than the *atomic detail*.

$$\bigcirc - \bigcirc - \bigcirc - \cdots - \bigcirc \quad \text{or} \quad [A]_n$$

### Method 5 — Empirical / Molecular Formula Representation

Derived by multiplying the empirical formula of the CRU by the degree of polymerisation. It ignores end groups and is useful for **combustion analysis**, **elemental analysis**, and **molecular weight** problems.

$$\text{Polyethylene empirical formula per CRU} = C_2H_4 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Polymer formula} = C_{2n}H_{4n}$$

## 2.2 Rules for Writing the Constitutional Repeat Unit (CRU) — IUPAC Hierarchy

For a polymer made from a single vinylic monomer $CH_2=CHX$, the CRU is written by:

> 1. Drawing the **backbone carbon chain** first (with the substituted carbon placed in the *least-substituted end* if the chain is symmetric, but in vinyl polymers the substituted $CHX$ carbon is written second for unambiguous head-to-tail depiction).
> 2. Surrounding the unit with **square brackets** $[ \; ]$.
> 3. Adding the **subscript $n$** (or $\bar{X}_n$ for the average degree of polymerisation) outside the bracket on the lower right.

**Worked mini-example (Polystyrene from styrene $CH_2=CH-C_6H_5$):**

$$\text{Styrene} \;\Rightarrow\; \left[-CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)-\right]_n$$

## 2.3 Special Case — Condensation Polymers (Two Different Monomers)

When two different monomers react (e.g., a diacid + a diol forming a polyester), the CRU contains **residues from both monomers** and is written by alternating the segments inside one bracket. The bracket encloses the **smallest unit whose repetition reconstructs the chain**.

**Example:** Nylon-6,6 from hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid

$$\text{HMDA: } H_2N-(CH_2)_6-NH_2 \quad \text{Adipic acid: } HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH$$

$$\text{CRU of Nylon-6,6} \;=\; \left[-NH-(CH_2)_6-NH-CO-(CH_2)_4-CO-\right]_n$$

With loss of $H_2O$ at each amide bond (the by-product of condensation), the empirical CRU is $\text{C}_{12}H_{22}N_2O_2$.

## 2.4 The "End-Group" Correction to the Molecular Formula

For a *real* finite polymer chain, the molecular formula is **not** simply $n \times M_0$. It contains two end groups of mass $M_e$ in total:

$$M_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n + M_e$$

Where:
- $M_0$ = molar mass of one CRU (or one mer) in g/mol
- $\bar{X}_n$ = number-average degree of polymerisation
- $M_e$ = total molar mass of the two end groups (often $-H$ on one end and $-OH$ or $-X$ on the other; sometimes initiator residue)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** In most numerical problems the end-group term is *negligible* (because $M_0\bar{X}_n \gg M_e$). Always check whether the problem statement says "neglect end groups" or "include end groups". If "include" is specified, you **must** add $M_e$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module-1 Essential)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CRU mass of a homopolymer | $M_0 = \sum (\text{atomic masses of CRU})$ | g/mol | Always for a single repeating unit |
| 2 | Number-average DP | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | Given $M_n$, find number of mers |
| 3 | Mass-average DP | $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | Given $M_w$, find weighted number of mers |
| 4 | End-group-corrected $M_n$ | $M_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n + M_e$ | g/mol | When end groups are *not* negligible |
| 5 | Repeat unit (mer) mass for empirical form | $M_{\text{mer}} = \dfrac{M_0}{N_{\text{CRU per mer}}}$ | g/mol | For polycondensates, CRU may equal 1 mer |
| 6 | Conversion: monomer to CRU | Subtract atoms lost (e.g., $H_2O$, $HCl$, $NH_3$) per bond | g/mol | Condensation polymers |
| 7 | Composition in copolymers | $\text{mol\% of A} = \dfrac{n_A}{n_A+n_B}\times 100$ | % | Random/alternating/graft representation |
| 8 | Empirical-to-molecular conversion | $(\text{empirical formula})\times n$ | — | From CRU empirical formula to full chain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Master the conversion from a *monomer name/structure* to a *CRU* and to a *repeat-unit mass $M_0$*. These three conversions are asked almost every KTU Module-1 exam.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of Correct Representation

- **Materials selection in packaging:** Drawing the CRU of PE vs PET vs PVC tells an engineer immediately whether the polymer is *saturated aliphatic* (flexible, chemically inert) or *aromatic* (rigid, high $T_g$).
- **Recycling coding:** The international resin codes (1=PET, 2=HDPE, 3=PVC, 4=LDPE, 5=PP, 6=PS, 7=Other) are nothing but a shorthand for the **CRU** of each polymer.
- **Process design:** The presence of polar side groups ($-Cl$, $-OH$, $-CN$) in the CRU determines whether the polymer is amenable to *extrusion*, *injection moulding*, or *solvent casting*.
- **Failure analysis:** When a polymer degrades, the end groups and the CRU determine the *backbone stability* (e.g., PVC loses $HCl$ from its $-CHCl-$ unit, a fact deducible only from the CRU).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through the **canonical derivations** and **worked numerical problems** a KTU 2024 student must be able to reproduce on the answer script. Each algebraic step is shown explicitly with the **logic of conversion** written in plain language.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From a Vinylic Monomer to a CRU (Free-Radical Polymerisation)

**Statement:** Show that the CRU of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) derived from the monomer methyl methacrylate, $CH_2=C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)$, is $\left[-CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)-\right]_n$.

**Step 1 — Identify the reactive double bond.**

In $CH_2=C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)$, the $C=C$ is between the carbon bearing two hydrogens (the $*CH_2*$ end) and the carbon bearing the methyl and the ester group (the $*C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)*$ end). This $\pi$-bond is the polymerisable site.

**Step 2 — Open the $\pi$-bond and join carbons in head-to-tail fashion.**

The growing radical attacks the $CH_2$ end (the less-substituted carbon) preferentially. A new $C-C$ $\sigma$-bond is formed between the $CH_2$ of the incoming monomer and the substituted carbon of the previous unit.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Monomer: } & CH_2{=}C(CH_3)(COOCH_3) \\
\text{CRU: } & \;\;\, \big\downarrow \\
& \left[-CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)-\right]_n
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Confirm the CRU mass.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0(\text{PMMA}) &= 5(12) + 8(1) + 2(16) \\
&= 60 + 8 + 32 \\
&= 100 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic check:** The monomer $C_5H_8O_2$ has mass $100$ g/mol. The CRU after opening the $\pi$-bond (which contributes no mass change) is also $100$ g/mol. This is the **defining feature of addition polymerisation** — *the CRU and the monomer have the same empirical formula and the same molar mass*. This contrasts with condensation polymerisation, where the CRU mass is *less* than the sum of the two monomer masses.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From a Diacid + Diamine Pair to a CRU (Condensation Polymerisation)

**Statement:** Derive the CRU of Nylon-6,6 from hexamethylenediamine (HMDA) and adipic acid.

**Step 1 — Write the full monomer structures.**

$$\text{HMDA: } H_2N-(CH_2)_6-NH_2 \quad ; \quad \text{Adipic acid: } HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH$$

**Step 2 — Identify the functional groups and the small-molecule by-product.**

The amine $-NH_2$ of HMDA reacts with the carboxylic acid $-COOH$ of adipic acid to form an amide (peptide) bond $-NH-CO-$ with the elimination of one $H_2O$ per bond.

**Step 3 — Form the amide linkages and lose water.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cdots H_2N-(CH_2)_6-NH_2 + HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH \cdots \\
\Downarrow \\
\cdots -NH-(CH_2)_6-NH-CO-(CH_2)_4-CO- \cdots + 2H_2O
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Enclose the CRU in brackets and add the subscript.**

$$\left[-NH-(CH_2)_6-NH-CO-(CH_2)_4-CO-\right]_n$$

**Step 5 — Compute the CRU molar mass.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0(\text{Nylon-6,6}) &= 12(12) + 22(1) + 2(14) + 2(16) \\
&= 144 + 22 + 28 + 32 \\
&= 226 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic check (mass balance):**

$$\begin{aligned}
M(\text{HMDA}) + M(\text{Adipic acid}) &= 116 + 146 \\
&= 262 \;\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Loss of } 2H_2O &= 2 \times 18 = 36 \;\text{g/mol} \\
\text{Hence, } M_0(\text{Nylon-6,6}) &= 262 - 36 \\
&= 226 \;\text{g/mol} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

This **262 − 36 = 226** arithmetic is the most frequently tested mass-balance in KTU Module-1 problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Molecular Formula of a Polymer with End Groups

**Problem:** A sample of polystyrene is reported to have a number-average molecular weight $M_n = 104{,}000$ g/mol. The chain is initiated by a benzoyl peroxide initiator, giving a phenyl end group on one end and a hydrogen on the other. Compute the number-average degree of polymerisation $\bar{X}_n$.

**Step 1 — Identify the CRU and its molar mass.**

The CRU of polystyrene is $-CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)-$, with

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0(\text{PS}) &= 8(12) + 8(1) \\
&= 96 + 8 \\
&= 104 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the end groups and their total mass.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_e &= M(\text{phenyl from initiator}) + M(\text{hydrogen}) \\
&= 77 + 1 = 78 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — careful: the *terminal* phenyl group is a $C_6H_5-$ fragment (mass 77), and the *other* end is an $H$ (mass 1). For a high-MW chain, $M_e \ll M_n$, so this 78 g/mol is **negligible compared to 104 000 g/mol**. KTU convention: neglect $M_e$ unless the chain is very short (oligomer, $M_n < 5{,}000$ g/mol).

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_n &= M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{M_n}{M_0} \\
&= \dfrac{104{,}000}{104} \\
&= 1000
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic of conversion:** $104{,}000/104$ is exactly $1000$ because $104 \times 1000 = 104{,}000$. So the average chain contains 1000 styrene mers.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — From a Copolymer's Monomer Composition to a CRU

**Problem:** A poly(styrene-co-methyl methacrylate) random copolymer contains 35 mol% styrene and 65 mol% methyl methacrylate. Write a representative segment of the chain and compute the average CRU mass.

**Step 1 — Write the two mer units.**

$$\text{Styrene mer (S)}: -CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)- \quad M(S) = 104 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\text{MMA mer (M)}: -CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)- \quad M(M) = 100 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Draw a random sequence (one possibility).**

$$\cdots -CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)-CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)-CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)-CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)- \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mole-fraction-weighted average CRU mass.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\langle M_0 \rangle &= 0.35 \times 104 + 0.65 \times 100 \\
&= 36.4 + 65.0 \\
&= 101.4 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Express the full polymer chain.**

$$\left[-\left(CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)\right)_{0.35}-\text{co}-\left(CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)\right)_{0.65}-\right]_{\bar{X}_n}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference for the Ten Most Common Polymers in KTU Module-1

| Polymer | Monomer | CRU | $M_0$ (g/mol) | Empirical Formula | Polymer Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyethylene (PE)** | $CH_2=CH_2$ | $[-CH_2-CH_2-]_n$ | **28** | $C_2H_4$ | Addition (vinyl) |
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | $CH_2=CH-CH_3$ | $[-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-]_n$ | **42** | $C_3H_6$ | Addition (vinyl) |
| **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** | $CH_2=CHCl$ | $[-CH_2-CHCl-]_n$ | **62.5** | $C_2H_3Cl$ | Addition (vinyl) |
| **Polystyrene (PS)** | $CH_2=CH-C_6H_5$ | $[-CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)-]_n$ | **104** | $C_8H_8$ | Addition (vinyl) |
| **PMMA (Perspex)** | $CH_2=C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)$ | $[-CH_2-C(CH_3)(COOCH_3)-]_n$ | **100** | $C_5H_8O_2$ | Addition (vinyl) |
| **PTFE (Teflon)** | $CF_2=CF_2$ | $[-CF_2-CF_2-]_n$ | **100** | $C_2F_4$ | Addition (vinyl) |
| **Nylon-6,6** | HMDA + Adipic acid | $[-NH-(CH_2)_6-NH-CO-(CH_2)_4-CO-]_n$ | **226** | $C_{12}H_{22}N_2O_2$ | Condensation |
| **PET** | EG + Terephthalic acid | $[-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-CO-C_6H_4-CO-]_n$ | **192** | $C_{10}H_8O_4$ | Condensation |
| **Polycarbonate (PC)** | Bisphenol A + Phosgene | $[-O-C_6H_4-C(CH_3)_2-C_6H_4-O-CO-]_n$ | **254** | $C_{16}H_{14}O_3$ | Condensation (step-growth) |
| **Natural rubber (cis-polyisoprene)** | Isoprene $C_5H_8$ | $[-CH_2-C(CH_3)=CH-CH_2-]_n$ | **68** | $C_5H_8$ | Addition (1,4-) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the matrix:** The "Empirical Formula" column gives the formula of **one mer / one CRU**. Multiply by $n$ to get the full chain formula. Notice that for **addition polymers** the empirical formula of the CRU is identical to the molecular formula of the monomer (no atoms are lost). For **condensation polymers** the empirical formula of the CRU contains *fewer atoms* than the sum of the two monomer formulas (because $H_2O$, $HCl$, $NH_3$, etc. are eliminated).

## 3.6 Edge-Case Derivation — When the CRU ≠ the Mer

**Statement:** A student writes the CRU of poly(ethylene terephthalate) as $[-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-]$ and claims $M_0 = 44$ g/mol. Show why this is wrong, and derive the correct CRU.

**Step 1 — Identify the monomers.**

Ethylene glycol: $HO-CH_2-CH_2-OH$ (MW = 62 g/mol)
Terephthalic acid: $HOOC-C_6H_4-COOH$ (MW = 166 g/mol)

**Step 2 — Realise the chain alternates segments from both monomers.**

The $-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-$ segment (from the glycol) by itself is **not** a CRU, because the chain does not continue with another $-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-$ segment. It continues with the diacid residue $-CO-C_6H_4-CO-$.

**Step 3 — Write the correct CRU (one glycol + one diacid residue, less $H_2O$).**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0(\text{PET}) &= M(\text{glycol}) + M(\text{diacid}) - 2 M(H_2O) \\
&= 62 + 166 - 36 \\
&= 192 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

The correct CRU is

$$\left[-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-CO-C_6H_4-CO-\right]_n$$

**Logic of conversion:** The student lost marks because they treated only *one* monomer's residue as the CRU. In a condensation polymer made from **two** monomers, the CRU must contain **both** residues, with the small-molecule by-product subtracted. This is a **favourite KTU 2024 trap question**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The mermaid diagrams below are written with the **Alpha-Node Rule** (every node ID begins with a letter, never a reserved keyword) and the **Clean-Label Rule** (no markdown formatting inside double-quoted node text).

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Hierarchical Pyramid of Polymer Representation

This block diagram shows the five canonical ways of representing a polymer, arranged as a pyramid from the **most detailed** (top) to the **most compact** (bottom). Each level is *derived from* the one above it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["LEVEL 5 EMPIRICAL FORMULA C2H4N REPEATED N TIMES"] --> B1["LEVEL 4 SCHEMATIC BLOCKS CIRCLE REPRESENTS ONE MER"]
    B1 --> C1["LEVEL 3 CONDENSED FORMULA OPEN BRACKET CH2 CH2 CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    C1 --> D1["LEVEL 2 CRU INSIDE SQUARE BRACKET WITH SUBSCRIPT N"]
    D1 --> E1["LEVEL 1 FULL LEWIS STRUCTURE WITH EVERY ATOM AND BOND"]
    E1 --> F1["MONOMER CH2 DOUBLE BOND CH2 STARTING MATERIAL"]
    F1 --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Start from the *top* (Monomer) and go down: the full Lewis structure (Level 1) is the *most explicit*; the empirical formula (Level 5) is the *most compact*. The arrow loop on the right shows that all five are *equivalent representations* of the *same* polyethylene molecule.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Decision Flow for Writing a CRU

This block diagram is the **algorithm** a KTU student should follow when given a monomer (or monomer pair) and asked to write the CRU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START GIVEN MONOMER OR MONOMER PAIR"] --> Q1{"IS REACTION ADDITION OR CONDENSATION"}
    Q1 -- ADDITION --> P1["OPEN PI BOND HEAD TO TAIL"]
    P1 --> P2["WRITE CRU AS DASH CH2 CHX DASH IN BRACKETS"]
    P2 --> P3["M0 EQUALS MONOMER MASS"]
    Q1 -- CONDENSATION --> C1["IDENTIFY TWO FUNCTIONAL GROUPS"]
    C1 --> C2["JOIN WITH LOSS OF H2O OR HCL OR NH3"]
    C2 --> C3["WRITE CRU WITH BOTH RESIDUES IN ONE BRACKET"]
    C3 --> C4["M0 EQUALS SUM MINUS BYPRODUCT MASS"]
    P3 --> END1["FINAL CRU READY"]
    C4 --> END1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Branching decision tree. The two divergent paths (Addition vs Condensation) converge into a single outcome — the **final CRU** — but the *computational path* and the *mass-balance* differ in the two cases.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Anatomy of a Real Polymer Chain

This block diagram shows a *finite* polymer chain with end groups, the backbone, the side groups, and the mer segmentation. It is the conceptual picture you should hold in mind when answering "draw the polymer" type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LEFT["LEFT END GROUP R1 INITIATOR FRAGMENT"] --> BB1["BACKBONE SEGMENT MER 1 CH2 CHX"]
    BB1 --> BB2["BACKBONE SEGMENT MER 2 CH2 CHX"]
    BB2 --> BB3["BACKBONE SEGMENT MER 3 CH2 CHX"]
    BB3 --> DOTS["DOTTED LINE REPEATING SEGMENTS UP TO N MINUS 2 MERS"]
    DOTS --> BBM["BACKBONE SEGMENT MER N CH2 CHX"]
    BBM --> RIGHT["RIGHT END GROUP R2 OFTEN HYDROGEN OR HALIDE"]
    BB1 --- SIDE1["SIDE GROUP X H CH3 CL C6H5 ETC"]
    BB2 --- SIDE2["SIDE GROUP X"]
    BB3 --- SIDE3["SIDE GROUP X"]
    BBM --- SIDEN["SIDE GROUP X"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *horizontal* chain is the polymer backbone. The *vertical* branches are the side groups. The two *terminal* blocks are the end groups. The dotted block in the middle is the conventional **"..."** symbol meaning *the pattern continues*.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Mapping of Monomer → CRU for the Five Most Common KTU Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["MONOMER ETHYLENE CH2 DOUBLE BOND CH2"] --> C1["CRU POLYETHYLENE BRACKET DASH CH2 CH2 DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    M2["MONOMER PROPYLENE CH2 DOUBLE BOND CH CH3"] --> C2["CRU POLYPROPYLENE BRACKET DASH CH2 CH CH3 DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    M3["MONOMER VINYL CHLORIDE CH2 DOUBLE BOND CH CL"] --> C3["CRU PVC BRACKET DASH CH2 CH CL DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    M4["MONOMER STYRENE CH2 DOUBLE BOND CH C6H5"] --> C4["CRU POLYSTYRENE BRACKET DASH CH2 CH C6H5 DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    M5["MONOMER TETRAFLUORO ETHYLENE CF2 DOUBLE BOND CF2"] --> C5["CRU PTFE BRACKET DASH CF2 CF2 DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    M6["MONOMER ISOPRENE CH2 DOUBLE BOND C CH3 CH DOUBLE BOND CH2"] --> C6["CRU POLYISOPRENE BRACKET DASH CH2 C CH3 DOUBLE BOND CH CH2 DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    M7["MONOMER PAIR HMDA + ADIPIC ACID"] --> C7["CRU NYLON 6 6 BRACKET DASH NH CH2 SIX NH CO CH2 FOUR CO DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
    M8["MONOMER PAIR EG + TEREPHTHALIC ACID"] --> C8["CRU PET BRACKET DASH O CH2 CH2 O CO C6H4 CO DASH CLOSE BRACKET N"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Eight parallel transformation paths. The left column is the input (monomer or monomer pair); the right column is the output (CRU). Each transformation is a **one-to-one mapping** in KTU Module-1 problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** In representation-of-polymer questions, students most commonly lose marks in three places:
> 1. **Writing the CRU as the mer of only one monomer** in a condensation polymer (e.g., writing PET's CRU as just $[-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-]_n$ and forgetting the terephthalate segment).
> 2. **Drawing head-to-head / tail-to-tail** linkages instead of the correct *head-to-tail* orientation.
> 3. **Forgetting the by-product subtraction** ($H_2O$, $HCl$, $NH_3$) when computing $M_0$ of a condensation polymer.
> Always double-check the **head** and the **tail** of your CRU, and always do the **mass-balance check** ($M_0 = M_{\text{monomer(s)}} - \text{by-product mass}$) before submitting.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the term "Constitutional Repeat Unit" (CRU). Represent the polymer prepared from vinyl chloride using the CRU notation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Constitutional Repeat Unit (CRU)** is the *smallest structural unit* of a polymer chain whose regular repetition produces the complete constitutional (connectivity) sequence of the polymer, as defined by IUPAC.

For **polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** prepared from vinyl chloride ($CH_2=CHCl$):

- **Polymerisation type:** Addition (chain-growth).
- **CRU opening:** The $C=C$ $\pi$-bond of $CH_2=CHCl$ opens; the substituted $CHCl$ carbon and the $CH_2$ carbon join in **head-to-tail** fashion.
- **CRU notation:**

$$\text{CRU of PVC} = \left[-CH_2-CHCl-\right]_n$$

- **Molar mass of one CRU:** $M_0 = 24 + 2 + 35.5 = 61.5$ g/mol (using Cl = 35.5).

**Mark split:** Definition of CRU = 1 mark; Polymerisation type identification = 0.5 mark; Correct CRU = 1 mark; $M_0$ value = 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question 2** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**What is a "mer" in polymer chemistry? Differentiate between a homopolymer and a copolymer with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **mer** is the *single repeating unit* of a polymer chain. It is the structural fragment obtained from one monomer molecule after the polymerisation reaction.

| Property | Homopolymer | Copolymer |
|---|---|---|
| Number of distinct monomers | **One** | **Two or more** |
| Composition along the chain | Uniform | Variable (random, alternating, block, graft) |
| Example | Polyethylene, PVC | Styrene–butadiene rubber (SBR) |
| Representation | $[A]_n$ | $[A\text{-}co\text{-}B]_n$ (random), $[A\text{-}alt\text{-}B]_n$ (alternating), $[A]_m[B]_n$ (block) |

**Example — Homopolymer:** Polypropylene from propylene only — $[-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-]_n$.

**Example — Copolymer:** Styrene–butadiene rubber (SBR) — random copolymer of styrene and 1,3-butadiene used in tyre treads.

**Mark split:** Definition of mer = 1 mark; Homopolymer example = 0.5 mark; Copolymer example + distinguishing feature = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With suitable examples, explain the **five different methods of representing a polymer**. Discuss the merits and demerits of each method. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polyester is prepared from **ethylene glycol (EG)** and **terephthalic acid (TPA)**. Draw the CRU of the resulting polymer (PET), and compute its number-average molecular weight $M_n$ for a degree of polymerisation $\bar{X}_n = 250$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The **five methods** of representing a polymer are:

**Method 1 — Full structural (Lewis) formula:** Every atom and bond shown explicitly. *Merit:* unambiguous, shows end groups. *Demerit:* extremely tedious for high-MW polymers; impractical for $n > 20$.

**Example:** $H-[CH_2-CH_2]_n-H$ (PE)

**Method 2 — Repeat-unit representation (CRU-based):** Single CRU inside square brackets with subscript $n$. *Merit:* IUPAC-recommended; compact; shows chemical identity. *Demerit:* does not show end groups or 3D arrangement.

**Example:** $[-CH_2-CH_2-]_n$ (PE)

**Method 3 — Condensed (abbreviated) structural formula:** Uses parentheses and a subscript; e.g., $-(CH_2CH_2)_n-$. *Merit:* very compact; commonly used in industrial datasheets. *Demerit:* can be confused with small-molecule notation if the brackets/subscript are misread.

**Method 4 — Schematic / block representation:** The CRU is replaced by a circle, square, or letter; e.g., $\bigcirc-\bigcirc-\bigcirc-\cdots$ or $[A]_n$. *Merit:* useful for showing chain architecture (block, graft, star). *Demerit:* chemistry is hidden; not useful for quantitative analysis.

**Method 5 — Empirical formula representation:** The empirical formula of the CRU is multiplied by $n$. *Merit:* essential for combustion/elemental analysis. *Demerit:* provides no structural information; cannot distinguish between isomers.

**Example:** Empirical formula of PE per CRU = $C_2H_4$; full chain = $C_{2n}H_{4n}$.

> **Mark split for (a):** 1 mark for naming each method (5 × 1 = 5); 1 mark for the example; 1 mark for the comparative merit/demerit discussion.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the monomers.**

- Ethylene glycol: $HO-CH_2-CH_2-OH$, $M = 62$ g/mol
- Terephthalic acid: $HOOC-C_6H_4-COOH$, $M = 166$ g/mol

**Step 2 — Identify the reaction type.**

Both are bifunctional. Each $-OH$ of EG reacts with one $-COOH$ of TPA, eliminating $H_2O$. Since each monomer has **two** functional groups, the chain grows in both directions. This is **step-growth condensation polymerisation**.

**Step 3 — Write the CRU.**

The smallest unit that, when repeated, regenerates the entire PET chain is:

$$\text{CRU of PET} = \left[-O-CH_2-CH_2-O-CO-C_6H_4-CO-\right]_n$$

**Step 4 — Compute the molar mass of one CRU.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= M(\text{EG}) + M(\text{TPA}) - 2M(H_2O) \\
&= 62 + 166 - 2(18) \\
&= 62 + 166 - 36 \\
&= 192 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $M_n$ for $\bar{X}_n = 250$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_n &= M_0 \times \bar{X}_n \\
&= 192 \times 250 \\
&= 48{,}000 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Mark split for (b):** 1 mark for identifying monomers; 1 mark for the CRU; 2 marks for the mass balance; 1 mark for $M_0$; 2 marks for the final $M_n$ computation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the terms **monomer, mer, repeat unit, and constitutional repeat unit**. Illustrate the four definitions with the example of **polypropylene** prepared from propylene. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sample of **Nylon-6,6** has a number-average degree of polymerisation $\bar{X}_n = 100$. If the chain is initiated such that the two end groups together contribute $44$ g/mol, calculate the **number-average molecular weight $M_n$** of the sample. Show the **mass balance** for the CRU formation step. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Definitions:**

1. **Monomer:** The low-molecular-weight, polymerisable starting molecule.
2. **Mer:** A single repeating unit of the polymer chain (sometimes used interchangeably with repeat unit).
3. **Repeat unit:** The structural unit that recurs along the chain; the smallest segment that, when concatenated, regenerates the polymer.
4. **Constitutional Repeat Unit (CRU):** The IUPAC-defined *smallest constitutional* (atomic-connectivity) unit whose regular repetition reproduces the complete chain constitution.

**Illustration with Polypropylene (PP):**

- **Monomer:** Propylene, $CH_2=CH-CH_3$
- **Mer / Repeat unit:** $-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-$
- **CRU (same as mer for vinyl polymers):** $\left[-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-\right]_n$

The relationship is:

$$\underbrace{CH_2=CH-CH_3}_{\text{Monomer}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Addition}}\; \underbrace{\left[-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-\right]_n}_{\text{CRU of PP}}$$

> **Mark split for (a):** 1 mark per definition (4 × 1 = 4); 2 marks for the PP illustration (writing the monomer + CRU); 1 mark for the relationship statement.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the monomers and the reaction.**

Nylon-6,6 is formed by the condensation of **hexamethylenediamine (HMDA)** and **adipic acid**, eliminating two $H_2O$ molecules per CRU.

$$\text{HMDA: } H_2N-(CH_2)_6-NH_2 \quad M = 116 \text{ g/mol}$$
$$\text{Adipic acid: } HOOC-(CH_2)_4-COOH \quad M = 146 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Write the CRU and compute $M_0$.**

$$\text{CRU} = \left[-NH-(CH_2)_6-NH-CO-(CH_2)_4-CO-\right]_n$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= M(\text{HMDA}) + M(\text{Adipic acid}) - 2M(H_2O) \\
&= 116 + 146 - 2(18) \\
&= 116 + 146 - 36 \\
&= 226 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the end-group-corrected formula.**

The problem states that the two end groups together contribute $M_e = 44$ g/mol. Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_n &= M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n + M_e \\
&= 226 \times 100 + 44 \\
&= 22{,}600 + 44 \\
&= 22{,}644 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Mass-balance verification.**

Sum of monomer masses: $116 + 146 = 262$ g/mol. Less 2 $H_2O$: $262 - 36 = 226$ g/mol. This matches $M_0$. ✓

> **Mark split for (b):** 2 marks for writing the CRU; 1 mark for the mass balance; 1 mark for $M_0$; 2 marks for the corrected $M_n$ with end groups; 1 mark for the verification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist as the **last 5-minute revision** before entering the KTU examination hall.

- [ ] A **polymer** is a high-MW compound of repeating *mers* derived from one or more *monomers*.
- [ ] The **CRU (Constitutional Repeat Unit)** is the IUPAC-endorsed smallest constitutional repeating sequence.
- [ ] The **five representations** of a polymer are: full Lewis, CRU-based, condensed, schematic, and empirical formula.
- [ ] For **addition (chain-growth) polymers**, the CRU molar mass $M_0$ **equals** the monomer molar mass.
- [ ] For **condensation (step-growth) polymers**, the CRU molar mass $M_0$ **equals** the sum of the two monomer molar masses **minus** the by-product ($H_2O$, $HCl$, $NH_3$, etc.).
- [ ] For vinyl polymers $CH_2=CHX$, the CRU is always written as **$-CH_2-CHX-$** (head-to-tail) inside square brackets with subscript $n$.
- [ ] The degree of polymerisation relates $M_n$ to $M_0$ via $\bar{X}_n = M_n / M_0$ (neglecting end groups) or $\bar{X}_n = (M_n - M_e)/M_0$ (including end groups).
- [ ] The end-group correction term $M_e$ is *negligible* for high-MW polymers ($M_n > 5{,}000$ g/mol) but **must be included** when explicitly asked.
- [ ] **Copolymers** are written as $[A\text{-}co\text{-}B]_n$ (random), $[A\text{-}alt\text{-}B]_n$ (alternating), $[A]_m[B]_n$ (block), and shown with a *graft* side chain for graft copolymers.
- [ ] The empirical formula per CRU is the same as the monomer's molecular formula for **addition** polymers, but **less than** the sum of the monomers for **condensation** polymers.
- [ ] Common KTU examples to memorise: **PE** ($M_0 = 28$), **PP** ($42$), **PVC** ($62.5$), **PS** ($104$), **PMMA** ($100$), **PTFE** ($100$), **Nylon-6,6** ($226$), **PET** ($192$).
- [ ] Always do the **mass-balance check** at the end of a condensation polymer problem: $M_0 = M_{\text{monomer A}} + M_{\text{monomer B}} - \text{mass of by-product(s)}$.
- [ ] The IUPAC seniority rule for CRU identification is beyond Module-1 scope; however, for vinyl polymers, the head-to-tail $-CH_2-CHX-$ is the universally accepted CRU.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functionality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functionality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In polymer chemistry, **functionality** is a fundamental molecular property that quantifies the number of reactive sites (or bonding sites) available on a monomer molecule, which can participate in polymerization reactions to form covalent linkages with other monomers.

Mathematically and chemically, functionality ($\mathbf{f}$) of a monomer is defined as:

$$\mathbf{f} = \text{Number of reactive functional groups (or bonding sites) per monomer molecule}$$

A reactive site is any atom, group of atoms, or bond (typically a multiple bond such as C=C, C≡C, C=O) that is capable of undergoing a chemical reaction with a complementary site on another monomer to form a new covalent bond, thereby extending the polymer chain or network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Staudinger's Functionality Principle (1932):** The functionality of a monomer is defined as the number of covalent bonds that a single molecule of the monomer can form with neighbouring monomers during the polymerization process. This principle is the foundation for predicting whether a monomer system will yield linear, branched, or cross-linked polymers.

### 1.2 Classification of Monomers Based on Functionality
