The functionality of a monomer is the single most critical parameter that determines the architecture of the resulting polymer.

| **Functionality Class** | **Symbolic Value** | **Polymer Architecture Produced** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Mono-functional | $\mathbf{f = 1}$ | **No polymer** — only dimers or oligomers possible |
| Di-functional | $\mathbf{f = 2}$ | **Linear** thermoplastic polymers |
| Tri-functional | $\mathbf{f = 3}$ | **Branched** or lightly cross-linked polymers |
| Poly-functional | $\mathbf{f \geq 4}$ | **Highly cross-linked** network (thermoset) polymers |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a children's building-block game. The number of "studs" or "connector pegs" on each block is analogous to its functionality:

- A block with **1 stud** (mono-functional) can only connect to one other block — it forms a small isolated piece, **never** a long chain.
- A block with **2 studs** (di-functional) can link end-to-end, like a train, producing a **long linear chain**.
- A block with **3 studs** (tri-functional) acts like a junction in a railway network — it produces **branches** and intersections.
- A block with **4 or more studs** (poly-functional) creates a rigid **3D network** where every block is locked in place, like a tightly knit fishing net.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world implication:** This is why epoxy resins (functionality ≥ 3) form hard, infusible thermosets, while polyethylene (functionality = 2) can be melted and remoulded as a thermoplastic.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Average functionality ($\mathbf{\bar{f}}$ or $\mathbf{f_{avg}}$)**: The weighted mean functionality of a monomer mixture.
- **Extent of reaction ($\mathbf{p}$)**: The fraction of total functional groups that have reacted.
- **Degree of polymerization ($\mathbf{\bar{X}_n}$)**: The average number of monomer units per polymer chain.
- **Gel point ($\mathbf{p_c}$)**: The critical extent of reaction at which the polymer system forms an infinite 3D network.

### 1.5 Functionality — The Two Distinct Interpretations

It is crucial for KTU examinations to distinguish between two subtly different interpretations of functionality:

**Interpretation A — Functionality based on functional groups:**
Count the number of reactive functional groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH, –NH₂) on the molecule. For example, glycerol (HOCH₂–CHOH–CH₂OH) has **3 hydroxyl groups**, so its functionality is **3**.

**Interpretation B — Functionality based on double bonds:**
For vinyl polymerizations, a C=C double bond is considered to provide **2 reactive sites** (one on each carbon), because both carbons can propagate the chain. For example, ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) has functionality = **2**, even though it has only one "functional group" (the double bond).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In vinyl/alkene polymerizations, the functionality is **always equal to 2** for any compound containing one C=C bond, regardless of other substituents. A diene (two C=C bonds) has functionality = **4** (e.g., 1,3-butadiene → synthetic rubber). Students frequently confuse the two interpretations — remember which type of polymerization is being discussed.

---

### 1.6 Worked Examples of Functionality

| **Monomer** | **Structure** | **Reactive Sites** | **Functionality** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Styrene | CH₂=CH–C₆H₅ | 1 C=C bond (2 sites) | **2** |
| Vinyl chloride | CH₂=CHCl | 1 C=C bond (2 sites) | **2** |
| 1,3-Butadiene | CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂ | 2 C=C bonds (4 sites) | **4** |
| Ethylene glycol | HO–CH₂–CH₂–OH | 2 –OH groups | **2** |
| Glycerol | HOCH₂–CHOH–CH₂OH | 3 –OH groups | **3** |
| Adipic acid | HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH | 2 –COOH groups | **2** |
| Phenol | C₆H₅–OH | 3 reactive H (ortho, ortho, para) | **3** |
| p-Cresol | CH₃–C₆H₄–OH | 2 reactive H (both ortho) | **2** |
| Divinyl benzene | (CH₂=CH)₂–C₆H₄ | 2 C=C bonds (4 sites) | **4** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Phenol's trifunctionality** (3 reactive ring positions) is the key reason phenol-formaldehyde resins (Bakelite) form densely cross-linked thermosets, whereas p-cresol (methyl group blocks the para position) is **only difunctional** and yields linear Novolac chains.

---

### 1.7 Critical Threshold — The "Functionality = 2" Rule

For step-growth (condensation) polymerizations, there is a fundamental rule:

> **If the average functionality of the monomer system is ≤ 2, only linear (or no) polymers are produced. If the average functionality is > 2, branched and cross-linked polymers result.**

This threshold governs every aspect of polymer architecture design and is the most heavily tested concept in KTU Module 1 questions on functionality.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid for KTU exams:** "**Two is True, More is Network**" — functionality = 2 gives linear chains, functionality > 2 gives cross-linked networks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Average Functionality in a Multi-Monomer System

When two or more monomers are mixed, each with its own functionality, the **average functionality** of the entire system is calculated as the mole-fraction-weighted mean:

$$\bar{f} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i \, f_i}{\sum_{i} N_i} = \sum_{i} x_i \, f_i$$

where:
- $\mathbf{N_i}$ = number of moles of monomer *i*
- $\mathbf{f_i}$ = functionality of monomer *i*
- $\mathbf{x_i}$ = mole fraction of monomer *i* in the mixture

This formula is central to all KTU problems involving monomer mixtures and is the gateway to applying Carothers' equation (see Section 3).

### 2.2 Why Average Functionality Matters

A real polymerisation reactor never contains a single pure monomer. Commercial polymerisations use:
- **Mixtures of di-functional and tri-functional** monomers (e.g., glycerol + phthalic acid → alkyd resins)
- **Mixtures of mono-functional chain stoppers** with di-functional monomers (to control molecular weight)
- **Slight excesses of one di-functional monomer** in A–B type polymerizations (e.g., nylon-6,6: excess hexamethylenediamine controls DP)

In all these cases, the **average functionality** of the mixture determines the maximum attainable molecular weight and whether gelation will occur.

### 2.3 Critical Analysis Steps for Predicting Polymer Architecture

When a KTU question provides a monomer mixture, follow this logical decision tree:

**Step 1:** Identify each monomer and its functionality $f_i$.
- Vinyl group (C=C) → contributes **2** to functionality.
- Each –OH, –COOH, –NH₂, –NCO, –COCl → contributes **1**.

**Step 2:** Compute the average functionality $\bar{f}$ using the mole-fraction formula.

**Step 3:** Apply the architectural rule:
- $\bar{f} = 1$ → no polymer (only dimer).
- $\bar{f} = 2$ → linear polymer (thermoplastic).
- $\bar{f} > 2$ → branched / cross-linked polymer (thermoset); gelation is possible.

**Step 4:** Compute the **critical extent of reaction** $p_c = 2/\bar{f}$ at which gelation occurs.

**Step 5:** Apply Carothers' equation: $\bar{X}_n = 2 / (2 - p\bar{f})$ to find the degree of polymerization at any given $p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the average functionality must exceed 2 for gelation:** Each branch point (functionality ≥ 3) creates a junction from which multiple chains can grow. Only when these branch points become numerous and interconnected enough (when $p\bar{f} \to 2$) does an "infinite network" form. This is the mathematical essence of the gel point.

### 2.4 Functionality vs. Functionality in Step-Growth vs. Chain-Growth

This is a frequently confused point in KTU exams:

| **Polymerization Type** | **Functionality Source** | **Typical Value** |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Step-growth (condensation) | Number of reactive end-groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂) | Often 2 or 3 |
| Chain-growth (vinyl/alkene) | Each C=C double bond contributes 2 | Always **2** per C=C |
| Chain-growth (diene) | Two C=C bonds contribute 4 | Often **4** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical consequence:** In free-radical polymerisation, even adding a small amount of a divinyl monomer (functionality 4) to a vinyl monomer (functionality 2) instantly creates branching and can lead to cross-linking — this is how "cross-linked polystyrene" ion-exchange resins are manufactured.

### 2.5 Effect of Functionality on Molecular Weight

Carothers' equation (derived fully in Section 3) gives the number-average degree of polymerization:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}}$$

This shows the **dramatic effect of $\bar{f}$ on molecular weight**:

- As $p \to 1$ and $\bar{f} = 2$, $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$ (high polymer possible).
- As $p \to 1$ and $\bar{f} = 1.5$, $\bar{X}_n \to 4$ (only tetramer).
- As $\bar{f} \to 2$ and $p \to 1$, the system gels.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Polymer chemists deliberately adjust the average functionality (e.g., by adding mono-functional chain terminators) to "dial in" a desired molecular weight. This is the principle behind "molecular-weight regulators" in industrial polyester and nylon production.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Functionality of pure monomer | $f$ = number of reactive sites | Dimensionless integer | Determines polymer architecture |
| Average functionality (mixture) | $\bar{f} = \sum N_i f_i \,/\, \sum N_i$ | $N_i$ = mol; $f_i$ = dimensionless | Used in multi-monomer systems |
| Extent of reaction | $p = (N_0 - N)/N_0$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of groups reacted |
| Carothers' DP equation | $\bar{X}_n = 2/(2 - p\bar{f})$ | Dimensionless | Average chain length |
| Gel point (Carothers) | $p_c = 2/\bar{f}$ | Dimensionless | Critical extent for gelation |
| Flory gel point (statistical) | $p_c = 1/[1 + (f-2)\bar{X}_n]$ | Dimensionless | More accurate than Carothers' |
| Polymer architecture rule | $\bar{f} \le 2$ → linear; $\bar{f} > 2$ → network | — | Decides thermoplastic vs thermoset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme CO mapping:** This topic maps to **CO1** — *Understand the basic principles of polymerisation and the concept of functionality.* Mastery of these formulas is essential for direct 3-mark questions and the 14-mark Part B derivations.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of functionality underpins virtually every commercial polymer synthesis:

1. **Alkyd resins (paints):** Glycerol ($\bar{f} = 3$) + phthalic anhydride ($\bar{f} = 2$) gives a cross-linked film.
2. **Nylon-6,6:** Hexamethylenediamine ($f = 2$) + adipic acid ($f = 2$) gives $\bar{f} = 2$ → strictly linear fibre.
3. **Phenolic resins (Bakelite):** Phenol ($f = 3$) + formaldehyde ($f = 2$) gives $\bar{f} > 2$ when excess formaldehyde is used → cross-linked thermoset.
4. **Epoxy networks:** Bisphenol-A diglycidyl ether ($f = 2$ epoxide) + triamine hardener ($f = 3$ or 6) → cross-linked thermoset.
5. **Polyurethane foams:** Diisocyanate ($f = 2$) + triol ($f = 3$) + water (chain extender) → branched/cross-linked foamed polymer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry tip for KTU Viva:** If asked "Why is melamine-formaldehyde used for tableware while urea-formaldehyde is used as adhesive?" — the answer lies in **functionality**: melamine has 6 reactive –NH sites (high $\bar{f}$), giving densely cross-linked, hard, heat-resistant thermoset; urea has only 4 (lower $\bar{f}$), giving a softer, more soluble network.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Functionality of a Binary Monomer Mixture

**Statement:** For a mixture containing $N_A$ moles of monomer A (functionality $f_A$) and $N_B$ moles of monomer B (functionality $f_B$), derive the expression for average functionality.

**Step 1 — Define total moles of each type of functional group:**

Moles of A-type functional groups = $N_A \times f_A$

Moles of B-type functional groups = $N_B \times f_B$

**Step 2 — Sum the contributions of all functional groups (regardless of type, since each group can potentially form a bond):**

$$\text{Total functional groups} = N_A \, f_A + N_B \, f_B$$

**Step 3 — Sum the total number of monomer molecules:**

$$\text{Total monomer molecules} = N_A + N_B$$

**Step 4 — By definition, average functionality is total functional groups divided by total monomer molecules:**

$$\bar{f} = \frac{N_A f_A + N_B f_B}{N_A + N_B}$$

**Step 5 — In terms of mole fractions, $x_A = N_A/(N_A + N_B)$ and $x_B = N_B/(N_A + N_B)$:**

$$\bar{f} = x_A \, f_A + x_B \, f_B$$

> This result is the *defining* equation for average functionality and is the starting point for all Carothers' equation applications.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carothers' Equation for Degree of Polymerization

**Statement:** Derive the relationship $\bar{X}_n = 2/(2 - p\bar{f})$ for step-growth polymerization.

**Step 1 — Define initial conditions:**

Let $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules in the system.

Each monomer of average functionality $\bar{f}$ contributes $\bar{f}$ reactive groups. So:

$$\text{Initial number of functional groups} = N_0 \times \bar{f}$$

**Step 2 — Define the extent of reaction $p$:**

$p$ is the fraction of functional groups that have reacted at time $t$.

$$\text{Number of reacted groups} = p \times N_0 \times \bar{f}$$

$$\text{Number of unreacted groups} = (1 - p) \times N_0 \times \bar{f}$$

**Step 3 — Count the number of bonds formed:**

Each covalent bond is formed by the reaction of **two** functional groups (one from each monomer). Therefore:

$$\text{Number of new bonds} = \frac{p \times N_0 \times \bar{f}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Count the number of molecules remaining:**

Initially there are $N_0$ molecules. Every bond formed joins two previously separate molecules, thereby reducing the total molecule count by 1 (ignoring cyclisation, which is the Carothers assumption). Therefore:

$$N = N_0 - \frac{p \times N_0 \times \bar{f}}{2}$$

$$N = N_0 \left( 1 - \frac{p\bar{f}}{2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the number-average degree of polymerization:**

By definition, $\bar{X}_n$ is the ratio of initial monomer molecules to remaining molecules:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{p\bar{f}}{2} \right)}$$

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{p\bar{f}}{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Rationalise the denominator (multiply numerator and denominator by 2):**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}}$$

> This is the celebrated **Carothers equation** (1936), central to step-growth polymerization kinetics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:**
> - When $p\bar{f} \ll 1$: $\bar{X}_n \approx 1$ (mostly monomers).
> - When $p\bar{f} \to 2$ (from below): $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$ — this defines the **gel point**.
> - When $p\bar{f} > 2$: physically meaningless in this linear formula; corresponds to network formation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gel Point According to Carothers

**Statement:** Derive the critical extent of reaction $p_c$ at which gelation occurs.

**Step 1 — At the gel point, an infinite molecular weight is just reached:**

This corresponds to $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$.

**Step 2 — Examine the denominator of Carothers' equation:**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}} \longrightarrow \infty$$

This requires the denominator to approach zero:

$$2 - p_c \bar{f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $p_c$:**

$$p_c = \frac{2}{\bar{f}}$$

> **Carothers' gel point formula.** For a system with $\bar{f} > 2$, gelation occurs at a finite $p < 1$. For $\bar{f} \le 2$, gelation never occurs (infinite molecular weight is approached only at $p = 1$).

**Example:** A 1 : 2 molar mixture of glycerol ($\bar{f}_A = 3$) and phthalic anhydride ($\bar{f}_B = 2$):

$\bar{f} = (1 \times 3 + 2 \times 2)/(1+2) = 7/3 \approx 2.33$

$p_c = 2/(7/3) = 6/7 \approx 0.857$ — gel forms when ~85.7% of groups have reacted.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Flory's Statistical Gel Point (Mentioned in KTU Syllabus)

**Statement:** For a monomer of functionality $f$, derive the statistical gel point $p_c = 1/(f-1)$.

**Step 1 — Consider a single functional group on a branched monomer:**

The probability that any one of its remaining $(f-1)$ functional groups has reacted is $p$ (extent of reaction).

**Step 2 — For a chain to propagate *through* this branch point (rather than terminate), all $(f-1)$ other groups must have reacted:**

The probability that this branch point is a true "propagation junction" is:

$$P_{\text{junction}} = p^{f-1}$$

**Step 3 — For an infinite network to form, the chain-branching coefficient must reach unity:**

$$p_c^{\,f-1} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_c = 1$$

This naive result is corrected by considering the **branching coefficient** $\alpha$ — the probability that a randomly chosen functional group leads, on average, to another branch point.

**Step 4 — Flory's corrected statistical gel point for a single monomer of functionality $f$:**

$$p_c = \frac{1}{f-1}$$

> For $f = 3$: $p_c = 1/2$ (gel forms at 50% reaction). For $f = 4$: $p_c = 1/3$. For $f = 2$: $p_c = 1$ (no gel possible in pure linear polymerisation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Flory's statistical gel point ($p_c = 1/(f-1)$) gives *lower* values than Carothers' (which gives $p_c = 2/f$). In practice, gelation occurs at the **Flory** value because of network connectivity statistics. Both formulas are KTU-recognised; Carothers' is more often used in textbook problems.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A monomer mixture contains 2 mol of glycerol (functionality 3) and 3 mol of ethylene glycol (functionality 2). Calculate (a) average functionality, (b) gel point (Carothers), (c) $\bar{X}_n$ at 80% reaction.

**Part (a) — Average functionality:**

$$\bar{f} = \frac{(2)(3) + (3)(2)}{2+3} = \frac{6+6}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4$$

**Part (b) — Carothers gel point:**

$$p_c = \frac{2}{\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{2.4} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$

**Part (c) — Degree of polymerization at 80% conversion:**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{2 - (0.80)(2.4)} = \frac{2}{2 - 1.92} = \frac{2}{0.08} = 25$$

> **Interpretation:** At 80% reaction (just below the gel point of 83.3%), the system has an average chain length of 25 monomer units. Above 83.3% reaction, the system gels into an infinite 3D network.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Functionality Effects at a Glance

| **$\bar{f}$** | **Architecture** | **$p_c$ (Carothers)** | **Maximum $\bar{X}_n$ at $p=1$** | **Example** |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1.0 | Dimer only | $\infty$ (no gel) | 2 | Acetic acid + ethanol |
| 1.5 | Short oligomers | $\infty$ (no gel) | 4 | Chain-stopper added |
| 2.0 | Strictly linear | 1.0 (no gel) | $\infty$ | Nylon-6,6 |
| 2.4 | Branched | 0.83 | $\infty$ (gels first) | Glycerol + ethylene glycol |
| 3.0 | Cross-linked | 0.67 | $\infty$ (gels first) | Phenol + formaldehyde |
| 4.0 | Dense network | 0.50 | $\infty$ (gels first) | Divinyl benzene |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Polymer Architecture Based on Functionality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[START: Identify monomer system] --> node2[Determine functionality f of each monomer]
    node2 --> node3[Single monomer or mixture?]
    node3 -- Mixture --> node4[Compute average functionality f-bar]
    node3 -- Pure monomer --> node5[Use f directly as f-bar]
    node4 --> node6{Is f-bar greater than 2?}
    node5 --> node6
    node6 -- NO, f-bar equals 2 --> node7[LINEAR POLYMER produced: Thermoplastic]
    node6 -- NO, f-bar less than 2 --> node8[NO POLYMER or only OLIGOMERS: Dimer Trimer Tetramer]
    node6 -- YES, f-bar greater than 2 --> node9[BRANCHED or CROSS-LINKED polymer produced]
    node9 --> node10[Compute gel point p-c equals 2 divided by f-bar]
    node10 --> node11{Is extent of reaction p greater than p-c?}
    node11 -- NO --> node12[Soluble branched polymer: Pre-gel stage]
    node11 -- YES --> node13[INFINITE 3D NETWORK: GEL formed: Thermoset]
    node7 --> node14[Apply Carothers equation: X-n equals 2 divided by 2 minus p times f-bar]
    node12 --> node14
    node8 --> node14
    node13 --> node15[STOP: Network analysis complete]
    node14 --> node15
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Cross-Linked Polymer Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MonoA[MONOMER A: f equals 2: LINEAR CONTRIBUTOR]
        A1[Reactive site 1] --- A2[Reactive site 2]
    end
    subgraph MonoB[MONOMER B: f equals 3: BRANCH POINT]
        B1[Reactive site 1] --- B2[Reactive site 2]
        B2 --- B3[Reactive site 3]
    end
    subgraph MonoC[MONOMER C: f equals 4: NETWORK HUB]
        C1[Reactive site 1] --- C2[Reactive site 2]
        C2 --- C3[Reactive site 3]
        C3 --- C4[Reactive site 4]
    end
    MonoA -- bond --> MonoB
    MonoB -- bond --> MonoC
    MonoC -- bond --> MonoA
    MonoA -- bond --> MonoC
    MonoB -- bond --> MonoA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** MonoA (functionality 2) acts as a chain segment; MonoB (functionality 3) creates a branch point; MonoC (functionality 4) is a network hub. The interconnections illustrate how monomers of higher functionality yield a 3D network.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Functionality on Molecular Weight Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Constant p equals 0.95: 95 percent reaction] --> nodeB[Compute X-n for each f-bar]
    nodeB --> nodeC1[f-bar equals 1.5: X-n equals 4]
    nodeB --> nodeC2[f-bar equals 1.8: X-n equals 6.67]
    nodeB --> nodeC3[f-bar equals 1.9: X-n equals 9.5]
    nodeB --> nodeC4[f-bar equals 1.99: X-n equals 66.7]
    nodeB --> nodeC5[f-bar equals 2.0: X-n equals 40]
    nodeB --> nodeC6[f-bar equals 2.01: SYSTEM HAS ALREADY GELLED]
```

> **Engineering insight:** As $\bar{f}$ approaches 2 from below, $\bar{X}_n$ rises sharply; the moment $\bar{f}$ exceeds 2, gelation occurs *before* 100% conversion. This is why polymer chemists carefully control functionality to avoid premature gelation.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Phenol-Formaldehyde Resin: Functionality in Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Phenol C6H5OH] --> nodeO1[Ortho position 1: Reactive H]
    nodeP --> nodeO2[Ortho position 2: Reactive H]
    nodeP --> nodePA[Para position: Reactive H]
    nodeO1 -- CH2OH attack --> nodeR1[Substituted phenol 1: f still 2]
    nodeO2 -- CH2OH attack --> nodeR2[Substituted phenol 2: f equals 1]
    nodePA -- CH2OH attack --> nodeR3[Substituted phenol 3: f still 2]
    nodeR1 --> nodeNET[Cross-linked network: RESIT stage]
    nodeR2 --> nodeNET
    nodeR3 --> nodeNET
```

> **Industrial relevance:** Phenol-formaldehyde (PF) resins pass through three stages: **Resol** (linear/soluble, f = 2 dominant), **Resitol** (partially cross-linked, still fusible), and **Resit** (fully cross-linked, infusible thermoset). The transition is governed by functionality crossing 2.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, May Session]**
**Define functionality of a monomer. State Staudinger's principle of functionality. With two examples, show how functionality determines the nature of the polymer formed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):* Functionality of a monomer is the number of reactive sites (functional groups or bonds) available on the monomer molecule that can participate in polymerization to form covalent bonds with other monomers.

*Staudinger's principle (1 mark):* According to Staudinger (1932), the functionality of a monomer is the number of covalent bonds a single molecule of that monomer can form with neighbouring monomers during polymerization.

*Two examples (1 mark):*
- **Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂), functionality = 2:** polymerises to give linear polyethylene (thermoplastic).
- **Glycerol (HOCH₂–CHOH–CH₂OH), functionality = 3:** when reacted with a diacid, produces a cross-linked, three-dimensional network (alkyd resin).

*Polymer nature rule:* Monofunctional → no polymer; difunctional → linear; trifunctional/polyfunctional → cross-linked polymer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, December Session]**
**Distinguish between functionality based on functional groups and functionality based on double bonds. Illustrate with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Functionality based on functional groups (1 mark):* In step-growth (condensation) polymerization, functionality is counted as the number of reactive end-groups such as –OH, –COOH, –NH₂, etc. Example: **adipic acid, HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH, has functionality = 2** (two –COOH groups).

*Functionality based on double bonds (1 mark):* In chain-growth (vinyl) polymerization, each C=C double bond contributes 2 reactive sites (one on each carbon). Example: **styrene, CH₂=CH–C₆H₅, has functionality = 2** (one C=C double bond provides 2 sites).

*Distinction (1 mark):* The first interpretation applies to condensation polymerisations where monomers are joined by the reaction of end-groups; the second applies to addition polymerisations where propagation occurs across a C=C bond. Both definitions, however, share the same physical meaning: the number of covalent bonds one monomer can form with its neighbours.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Sample]`

**(a)** Define the term "functionality" of a monomer. Explain how the functionality of a monomer affects the structure and properties of the resulting polymer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monomer mixture consists of 2 mol of glycerol (functionality 3) and 3 mol of phthalic anhydride (functionality 2). Calculate (i) the average functionality of the mixture, (ii) the Carothers gel point, and (iii) the number-average degree of polymerization at 70% extent of reaction. Comment on the nature of the polymer formed. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Definition and Effect of Functionality (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Functionality of a monomer is the number of reactive sites (functional groups or reactive bonds) on the monomer molecule that can participate in the polymerization reaction to form covalent linkages with neighbouring monomers. Formally, by Staudinger's principle, the functionality $f$ of a monomer is the total number of covalent bonds that one molecule of the monomer can form with other monomer molecules during the polymerization process.

**Effect of functionality on polymer structure (3 marks):**

The functionality of the monomer (or average functionality of a monomer mixture) is the single most important parameter governing polymer architecture:

- **Mono-functional ($f = 1$):** Such a monomer has only one reactive site. When it reacts, it can form a dimer at most, but never a polymer chain. Example: acetic acid (CH₃COOH) can react with ethanol to give ethyl acetate, but cannot form a polymer.

- **Di-functional ($f = 2$):** The monomer has two reactive sites. Polymerization proceeds by linking monomers end-to-end, producing a strictly **linear chain** polymer. Examples: ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) → polyethylene; adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine → nylon-6,6. Such polymers are **thermoplastic** — they soften on heating and can be remoulded.

- **Tri-functional ($f = 3$):** The monomer has three reactive sites and acts as a branch point. Polymerization produces **branched** or **cross-linked** structures. Examples: glycerol + phthalic anhydride → alkyd resin (paint binder); phenol + formaldehyde (with excess formaldehyde) → phenolic resin (Bakelite). Such polymers tend to be **thermosetting** — once formed, they cannot be remelted.

- **Poly-functional ($f \ge 4$):** Such monomers act as network hubs. Polymerization gives a rigid, three-dimensional **infusible network** with high mechanical strength, chemical resistance, and thermal stability. Examples: melamine-formaldehyde resins; divinylbenzene-crosslinked polystyrene.

**Effect on properties (2 marks):**

Functionality directly controls:
1. **Polymer architecture** — linear (f = 2), branched (f = 3), cross-linked (f ≥ 4).
2. **Solubility** — high functionality reduces solubility due to network rigidity.
3. **Thermal behaviour** — cross-linked (high f) polymers are thermosets; linear (f = 2) polymers are thermoplastics.
4. **Mechanical strength** — increases with cross-link density.
5. **Molecular weight** — controlled by Carothers' equation; high f leads to gelation before 100% conversion.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N_A = 2$ mol glycerol, $f_A = 3$
- $N_B = 3$ mol phthalic anhydride, $f_B = 2$
- $p = 0.70$ for part (iii)

**(i) Average functionality (2 marks):**

$$\bar{f} = \frac{N_A f_A + N_B f_B}{N_A + N_B} = \frac{(2)(3) + (3)(2)}{2+3} = \frac{6+6}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4$$

**(ii) Carothers' gel point (2 marks):**

$$p_c = \frac{2}{\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{2.4} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833$$

**(iii) Number-average degree of polymerization at p = 0.70 (2 marks):**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{2 - (0.70)(2.4)} = \frac{2}{2 - 1.68} = \frac{2}{0.32} = 6.25$$

**Comment on polymer nature (1 mark):**

Since $\bar{f} = 2.4 > 2$, the system produces a **cross-linked polymer** (thermoset alkyd resin). At 70% conversion (which is below the gel point of 83.3%), the system exists in the pre-gel stage as soluble, branched polymer chains of average length 6.25 monomer units. Beyond 83.3% conversion, gelation occurs and an infinite 3D network forms.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Sample]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of average functionality with suitable examples. Derive the Carothers' equation for the degree of polymerization in a step-growth polymerization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A phenolic resin is synthesised from a 1:1 mole mixture of phenol (functionality 3) and formaldehyde (functionality 2). Calculate (i) the average functionality of the system, (ii) the Carothers gel point, and (iii) $\bar{X}_n$ at 90% reaction. What type of polymer is produced? State two industrial applications of this polymer. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Average Functionality and Carothers' Equation Derivation (7 marks)

**Concept of average functionality (3 marks):**

In most industrial polymerisations, the reaction mixture contains two or more different monomers, each with its own functionality. To predict the architecture and molecular weight of the resulting polymer, we need the **average functionality** $\bar{f}$ of the entire monomer system.

Average functionality is defined as the total number of functional groups contributed by all monomers divided by the total number of monomer molecules:

$$\bar{f} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i f_i}{\sum_{i} N_i}$$

where $N_i$ is the number of moles of monomer $i$ and $f_i$ is its functionality.

**Examples:**
- **Mixture of glycerol and ethylene glycol:** Glycerol has 3 –OH groups (f = 3); ethylene glycol has 2 –OH groups (f = 2). A 1:1 mole mixture gives $\bar{f} = (1 \times 3 + 1 \times 2)/2 = 2.5$.
- **Phenol + formaldehyde (1:1.5):** Phenol (f = 3), formaldehyde (f = 2). $\bar{f} = (1 \times 3 + 1.5 \times 2)/2.5 = 6/2.5 = 2.4$.

**Derivation of Carothers' equation (4 marks):**

Consider a step-growth polymerisation with $N_0$ initial monomer molecules of average functionality $\bar{f}$. The total number of functional groups initially present is $N_0 \bar{f}$.

Let $p$ be the extent of reaction (fraction of functional groups that have reacted). The number of functional groups that have reacted is $p \times N_0 \bar{f}$.

Since each covalent bond is formed by the reaction of two functional groups, the number of new bonds formed is:

$$\text{Bonds} = \frac{p N_0 \bar{f}}{2}$$

Each bond formed joins two previously separate molecules into one, reducing the molecule count by one. Therefore, the number of molecules remaining at extent of reaction $p$ is:

$$N = N_0 - \frac{p N_0 \bar{f}}{2} = N_0 \left( 1 - \frac{p \bar{f}}{2} \right)$$

The number-average degree of polymerisation is:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{p\bar{f}}{2} \right)} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{p\bar{f}}{2}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}}$$

This is **Carothers' equation**, valid for step-growth polymerisations of monomers with average functionality $\bar{f}$.

#### Part (b) — Phenol-Formaldehyde Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N_P = 1$ mol phenol, $f_P = 3$
- $N_F = 1$ mol formaldehyde, $f_F = 2$
- $p = 0.90$ for part (iii)

**(i) Average functionality (2 marks):**

$$\bar{f} = \frac{(1)(3) + (1)(2)}{1+1} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$

**(ii) Carothers' gel point (2 marks):**

$$p_c = \frac{2}{\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{2.5} = 0.80$$

**(iii) Degree of polymerization at 90% reaction (2 marks):**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{2 - (0.90)(2.5)} = \frac{2}{2 - 2.25} = \frac{2}{-0.25}$$

The denominator is **negative**, indicating that the system has already passed the gel point ($p = 0.90 > p_c = 0.80$). At this point, Carothers' linear equation breaks down; the system is a fully cross-linked, insoluble, infusible network.

> Note: This result confirms that at 90% reaction, the system has already gelled — the formula gives a negative value, which physically means the polymer is an infinite network.

**Polymer type (0.5 mark):**
Phenol-formaldehyde resin with $\bar{f} = 2.5 > 2$ produces a **thermosetting, cross-linked polymer** known as **Bakelite** (in the fully cured "Resit" stage).

**Industrial applications (0.5 mark):**
1. **Electrical switchboards and insulators** — excellent heat resistance and electrical insulation.
2. **Adhesives and laminates** (e.g., plywood binders, brake linings) — high mechanical strength and chemical resistance.

*(Other valid applications: cookware handles, billiard balls, moulded articles, foundry resins.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Functionality Problems (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing functionality interpretations:** For vinyl monomers, the C=C bond contributes 2 to functionality — not 1. Many students write "ethylene has functionality 1" because they see "one double bond." Lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use mole fractions or total moles** when computing $\bar{f}$ for a mixture. The correct formula uses $N_i$ (moles) in both numerator and denominator, not weights. Lose 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Using $p_c = 1/(f-1)$ (Flory) instead of $p_c = 2/\bar{f}$ (Carothers) in a Carothers' equation problem** — the syllabus expects the Carothers form unless explicitly stated otherwise. Lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Not stating the polymer architecture** after computing $\bar{f}$ — students often stop at the calculation. Always conclude with "linear / branched / cross-linked" and identify thermoplastic vs. thermoset. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Sign error in Carothers' equation** — when $p\bar{f} > 2$ (already past gel point), the equation gives a negative value; students sometimes report a meaningless negative $\bar{X}_n$ without recognising that the system has gelled. Always cross-check $p$ vs. $p_c$ first. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Not defining $\bar{f}$ clearly** before substituting into the formula in long-answer questions. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — although $\bar{X}_n$ is dimensionless, KTU examiners still expect you to write "dimensionless" or "no units" to demonstrate rigour.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Functionality" (Module 1):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Functionality $f$ of a monomer = number of reactive sites / covalent bonds it can form with neighbours (Staudinger, 1932).

- ✅ **Architecture rule:** $f = 1$ → no polymer; $f = 2$ → **linear** (thermoplastic); $f = 3$ → **branched/cross-linked**; $f \ge 4$ → **dense 3D network** (thermoset).

- ✅ **Vinyl monomers** (one C=C bond) always have $f = 2$; **dienes** (two C=C bonds) have $f = 4$.

- ✅ **Average functionality** of a mixture: $\bar{f} = \sum N_i f_i / \sum N_i = \sum x_i f_i$ (mole-fraction weighted mean).

- ✅ **Carothers' equation:** $\bar{X}_n = 2/(2 - p\bar{f})$ — relates DP to extent of reaction and average functionality.

- ✅ **Carothers' gel point:** $p_c = 2/\bar{f}$ — extent of reaction at which gelation occurs. Valid only if $\bar{f} > 2$.

- ✅ **Flory's statistical gel point:** $p_c = 1/(f-1)$ — more accurate, lower than Carothers' value.

- ✅ **Threshold insight:** Polymerisation to high molecular weight requires $p\bar{f} \to 2$ from below; cross-linking requires $p\bar{f} \ge 2$.

- ✅ **Cross-link density** is controlled by the mole fraction of tri-functional (or higher) monomers in the formulation.

- ✅ **Phenol ($f = 3$)** + formaldehyde ($f = 2$) → phenolic resin (Bakelite); the only case where a $1:1$ ratio gives $\bar{f} = 2.5$ (thermoset).

- ✅ **Glycerol ($f = 3$)** + phthalic anhydride ($f = 2$) → alkyd resin; functionality and gelation govern paint film formation.

- ✅ **Nylon-6,6:** hexamethylenediamine ($f = 2$) + adipic acid ($f = 2$) → $\bar{f} = 2$ → strictly linear fibre.

- ✅ **Mono-functional monomers** (e.g., acetic acid in PET synthesis) act as **chain terminators / molecular-weight regulators**.

- ✅ **Industrial control of $\bar{f}$:** Adding a small amount of mono-functional monomer *decreases* $\bar{f}$, suppresses gelation, and limits $\bar{X}_n$.

- ✅ **Carothers' equation is valid** only for $p\bar{f} < 2$ (pre-gel stage). Beyond the gel point, statistical network theories (Flory–Stockmayer) must be used.

- ✅ **Two key memory aids:**
  - *"Two is True, More is Network"* — $f = 2$ linear, $f > 2$ cross-linked.
  - *"Average counts, extremes don't"* — when computing $\bar{f}$ for mixtures, always use the **mole-fraction weighted mean**, never the arithmetic mean of functionalities.

- ✅ **Most-tested KTU question types:** (1) Compute $\bar{f}$ for a given mixture; (2) Apply Carothers' equation; (3) Predict architecture from $\bar{f}$; (4) Identify gel point; (5) Compare functionality interpretations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bi-Functional Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bi-Functional Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bi-Functional Systems (Definition):** > Bi-functional systems in polymer chemistry refer to monomeric systems in which **each monomer molecule carries exactly two reactive functional groups** capable of participating in step-growth (condensation) polymerization. These two functional groups may be **chemically identical (homofunctional, e.g., AA or BB)** or **chemically different (heterofunctional, e.g., AB)**, and the resulting polymerisation proceeds through successive condensation reactions between complementary functional groups to build linear macromolecular chains. Bi-functional monomer systems are the simplest and most fundamental class of monomers in **step-growth (condensation) polymerisation**, and they form the theoretical foundation for the **Carothers equation** that relates the *number-average degree of polymerisation* ($\bar{X}_n$) to the *extent of reaction* ($p$). ## 1.2 Classification of Bi-Functional Systems Bi-functional polymerisation systems are categorised into two main types: > [!NOTE] > **Type 1 — AA + BB System (Two Different Bifunctional Monomers)** > Two distinct monomers, each carrying two identical functional groups, react with each other. > - **Example:** Adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH) + Hexamethylenediamine (H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂) → Nylon 6,6 > [!NOTE] > **Type 2 — AB System (Single Bifunctional Monomer)** > A single monomer carries two different functional groups that can react with each other. > - **Example:** 6-Aminocaproic acid (H₂N–(CH₂)₅–COOH) → Nylon 6 > - **Example:** Hydroxy acids (HO–R–COOH) → Polyesters ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Picture > [!TIP] > **"The Train Coupling Analogy":** > Imagine a railway yard with two kinds of wagons: > - **Type AA wagons** have couplers on both ends that only mate with **Type B** couplers. > - **Type BB wagons** have only **B-type** couplers on both ends. > - **Type AB wagons** have one **A-coupler** and one **B-coupler** on the same wagon. > > The "reaction" is the *clicking* of an A-coupler with a B-coupler. As more and more couplings happen, the wagons form longer and longer trains. The **extent of reaction (p)** is essentially the *percentage of A-couplers that have already clicked with a B-coupler*. To get a very long train (high molecular weight), almost every coupler must have clicked (p must approach 1). > > If there are *more AA wagons than BB wagons* (non-stoichiometric, r < 1), then some AA wagons will be left with both A-couplers free — these become "dead ends" and cap the maximum train length. ## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Standard Symbols) - **$N_0$** = Total number of monomer molecules initially present (in moles) - **$N_A, N_B$** = Number of unreacted A and B functional groups at time $t$ - **$N_{A0}, N_{B0}$** = Initial number of A and B functional groups - **$p$** = Extent of reaction (dimensionless fraction, $0 \leq p \leq 1$) - **$\bar{X}_n$** = Number-average degree of polymerisation (dimensionless) - **$r$** = Stoichiometric ratio = $N_{A0}/N_{B0}$, with $r \leq 1$ (unitless) - **$\bar{M}_n$** = Number-average molecular weight (g·mol⁻¹) - **$M_0$** = Mean molecular weight of one structural repeat unit (g·mol⁻¹) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Step-Growth Polymerisation Step-growth polymerisation obeys four cardinal rules: 1. **Any two molecular species can react** (monomer–monomer, monomer–oligomer, oligomer–oligomer) — there is no special "active centre" propagation. 2. **The functional group reactivity is independent of chain length** (the **equal-reactivity principle** of Flory). 3. **The polymer chain grows slowly** — high molecular weight is achieved only at very high conversion (p > 0.99). 4. **By-products (small molecules such as H₂O, HCl)** are usually eliminated in true condensation reactions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight (Why high $p$ is mandatory):** > From $\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$, achieving $\bar{X}_n = 100$ requires $p = 0.99$ (i.e. **99 % conversion**), and $\bar{X}_n = 1000$ demands $p = 0.999$ (**99.9 % conversion**). This is the *stringent conversion requirement* unique to step-growth polymerisation. ## 2.2 The Carothers Equation — Logical Derivation Pathway The Carothers equation relates $\bar{X}_n$ to $p$ and is derived as follows: **Step 1:** Define the *number of monomer molecules* at any conversion. **Step 2:** Each A–B bond formed reduces the molecule count by exactly one (because two molecules are joined into one). **Step 3:** Count the number of bonds formed at conversion $p$. **Step 4:** Apply $\bar{X}_n = N_0/N$ (definition of number-average DP). **Step 5:** Express in terms of $r$ and $p$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Reference for Bi-Functional Step-Growth Systems** | **System Type** | **Carothers Equation for $\bar{X}_n$** | **Carothers Equation for $\bar{M}_n$** | **Condition / Special Case** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | AB (single monomer) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{M_0}{1-p}$ | Stoichiometric balance is automatic | | AA + BB (stoichiometric, r = 1) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{M_0}{1-p}$ | $N_{A0} = N_{B0}$ initially | | AA + BB (non-stoichiometric, r < 1) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | $r = N_{A0}/N_{B0}$, A is the minority group | | Maximum achievable DP (excess of one reagent) | $\bar{X}_{n,\max} = \dfrac{1+r}{1-r}$ | $\bar{M}_{n,\max} = M_0 \cdot \dfrac{1+r}{1-r}$ | Reached only when $p \to 1$ | | Fraction of unreacted A groups | $(1-p)$ | — | At conversion $p$ | | Fraction of unreacted B groups | $\dfrac{1-rp}{r}$ (if $r < 1$) | — | At conversion $p$ | | Number of molecules at time $t$ | $N = N_0(1-p)$ (AB); $N = N_{A0} + N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0}$ (AA+BB) | — | Foundational definition | **Where:** - **$M_0$** = Mean molecular weight of the structural repeat unit (e.g., for Nylon 6,6, $M_0 = 226$ g·mol⁻¹; for Nylon 6, $M_0 = 113$ g·mol⁻¹) - **$r$** = Stoichiometric imbalance ratio ($N_{A0} \leq N_{B0}$, hence $r \leq 1$) - **$p$** = Fraction of A groups that have reacted ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Nylon 6,6** (AA+BB): Used in tyre cords, gears, and conveyor belts. - **Nylon 6** (AB): Used in textiles, carpets, and engineering plastics. - **Polyethylene terephthalate (PET)** — formed from ethylene glycol (BB) + terephthalic acid (AA). - **Polycarbonate** — formed from bisphenol A + phosgene (AA+BB). - **Molecular weight control** through stoichiometric imbalance is the **industrial method** for producing resins of defined viscosity (e.g., alkyd resins, epoxy prepolymers). > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook (Examiner's Favourite):** > *"p = 0.99 → Xₙ = 100, p = 0.999 → Xₙ = 1000"* — a classic 3-mark KTU question! # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Case 1: Derivation for AB Bi-Functional System Let the monomer be of the form **A–R–B**, where A and B are mutually reactive functional groups. Let there be $N_0$ such molecules initially. **Given / Assumed:** - Initial A groups = $N_{A0} = N_0$ (one A per monomer) - Initial B groups = $N_{B0} = N_0$ (one B per monomer) - $p$ = fraction of A groups that have reacted (by definition, also equals fraction of B groups that have reacted because each A–B bond consumes one of each) - $M_0$ = molar mass of the structural repeat unit **Derivation:** $$\text{Number of A–B bonds formed at extent } p = p \cdot N_0$$ $$\text{Since each bond reduces the molecule count by 1:}$$ $$N = N_0 - (\text{number of bonds}) = N_0 - pN_0 = N_0(1-p)$$ $$\text{By definition of number-average degree of polymerisation:}$$ $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$ $$\text{Number-average molecular weight:}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n = \frac{M_0}{1-p}}$$ ## 3.2 Case 2: Derivation for AA + BB Bi-Functional System (General, with Stoichiometric Imbalance) Let the system consist of: - $N_{A0}$ molecules of the form **A–R–A** (each contributing 2 A groups) - $N_{B0}$ molecules of the form **B–R′–B** (each contributing 2 B groups) - Total initial molecules: $N_0 = N_{A0} + N_{B0}$ **Total functional groups initially:** - A groups: $2N_{A0}$ - B groups: $2N_{B0}$ **Define stoichiometric ratio:** $$r = \frac{N_{A0}}{N_{B0}} \quad \text{with} \quad r \leq 1$$ (We conventionally let A be the minority group, so $r \leq 1$.) **At extent of reaction $p$ (fraction of A groups that have reacted):** - Number of A–B bonds formed = (fraction of A reacted) × (initial number of A groups) = $p \cdot 2N_{A0}$ - These same bonds also consumed $2pN_{A0}$ of the B groups. **Remaining unreacted groups:** - A groups remaining: $N_A = 2N_{A0} - 2pN_{A0} = 2N_{A0}(1-p)$ - B groups remaining: $N_B = 2N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0} = 2N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0}$ **Each linear polymer molecule has exactly 2 chain ends** (one A-type, one B-type, except in the non-stoichiometric case where some ends are uncapped). The number of chain ends equals the total number of unreacted functional groups: $$\text{Total ends} = N_A + N_B = 2N_{A0}(1-p) + 2N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0}$$ $$\text{Since each molecule has 2 ends, the number of molecules is:}$$ $$N = \frac{N_A + N_B}{2} = \frac{2N_{A0}(1-p) + 2N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0}}{2}$$ $$N = N_{A0}(1-p) + N_{B0} - pN_{A0}$$ $$N = N_{A0} - pN_{A0} + N_{B0} - pN_{A0}$$ $$N = N_{A0} + N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0}$$ **Apply the definition of $\bar{X}_n$:** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_{A0} + N_{B0}}{N_{A0} + N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0}}$$ **Divide numerator and denominator by $N_{B0}$:** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{\dfrac{N_{A0}}{N_{B0}} + 1}{\dfrac{N_{A0}}{N_{B0}} + 1 - 2p \cdot \dfrac{N_{A0}}{N_{B0}}}$$ $$\text{Substitute } r = \frac{N_{A0}}{N_{B0}}:$$ $$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}$$ **For stoichiometric case ($r = 1$):** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+1}{1+1-2p} = \frac{2}{2-2p} = \frac{1}{1-p} \quad \checkmark$$ This matches the AB case, as expected. **Maximum DP at $p \to 1$:** $$\boxed{\bar{X}_{n,\max} = \frac{1+r}{1-r}}$$ **Number-average molecular weight:** $$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Quick Limiting-Case Check (KTU Favourite):** > - If $r = 1$ (perfect stoichiometry) and $p = 0.99$ → $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-0.99) = 100$ > - If $r = 0.99$ (1 % AA in deficit) and $p = 0.99$ → $\bar{X}_n = (1.99)/(1.99 - 2(0.99)(0.99)) = 1.99/0.0298 \approx 66.8$ > - If $r = 0.99$ and $p \to 1$ → $\bar{X}_{n,\max} = 1.99/0.01 = 199$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (Typical University Exam — 7 Mark):** A stoichiometric mixture of adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine is polymerised. Calculate $\bar{X}_n$ and $\bar{M}_n$ at $p = 0.95$ and $p = 0.99$. Given $M_0 = 226$ g·mol⁻¹ for Nylon 6,6. **Solution:** **At p = 0.95 (stoichiometric, r = 1):** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{1}{1-0.95} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$ $$\bar{M}_n = \frac{M_0}{1-p} = \frac{226}{0.05} = 4520 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$$ **At p = 0.99:** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-0.99} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$ $$\bar{M}_n = \frac{226}{0.01} = 22600 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$$ **Comment:** To achieve an engineering-grade Nylon 6,6 ($\bar{M}_n \approx 20{,}000$ g·mol⁻¹), one must push conversion beyond 99 %. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AB vs AA+BB | **Parameter** | **AB System** | **AA+BB System (stoichiometric)** | **AA+BB System (non-stoichiometric, r < 1)** | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Number of monomer types | 1 | 2 | 2 | | Carothers equation | $1/(1-p)$ | $1/(1-p)$ | $(1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ | | Maximum theoretical $\bar{X}_n$ at $p=1$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $(1+r)/(1-r)$ — **finite!** | | Stoichiometry automatically satisfied? | **Yes** | Only if measured precisely | No (must control) | | Industrial molecular-weight control | Difficult | Difficult | **Easy** (via r) | | Example | Nylon 6, PET from hydroxy acids | Nylon 6,6, PET from EG+TPA | Alkyd resins, epoxy prepolymers | ## 3.5 Fraction of End-Groups Analysis (Vital for Characterisation) In an AA+BB system with $r < 1$ at conversion $p$: - **Fraction of A groups unreacted:** $(1-p)$ - **Fraction of B groups unreacted:** $\dfrac{2N_{B0} - 2pN_{A0}}{2N_{B0}} = 1 - \dfrac{pN_{A0}}{N_{B0}} = 1 - rp$ When $p = 1$ (full consumption of minority A groups): - Unreacted A groups: 0 % - Unreacted B groups: $(1 - r) \times 100$ % This residual excess of B groups is what **caps the chain growth** in non-stoichiometric systems and is the working principle behind **monofunctional chain stoppers** used industrially to control $\bar{M}_n$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — AA + BB Step-Growth Polymerisation ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[AA Monomer
A R A] -->|k| bond1[A B linkage] B1[BB Monomer
B R B] -->|k| bond1 bond1 --> chain1[Linear Dimer
A R A B R B] chain1 -->|k| bond2[A B linkage] A2[AA Monomer
A R A] -->|k| bond2 bond2 --> trimer1[Trimer
A R A B R B A R A] trimer1 -->|k| bond3 B2[BB Monomer
B R B] -->|k| bond3 bond3 --> tetramer1[Tetramer
A R A B R B A R A B R B] tetramer1 -.->|further k| polymer[Long Polymer Chain
Degree of Polymerisation increases as p to power 1] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — AB Step-Growth Polymerisation ```mermaid flowchart LR m1[AB Monomer
A R B] -->|k| d1[Dimer A R B A R B] m2[AB Monomer
A R B] -->|k| d1 d1 -->|k| t1[Trimer A R B A R B A R B] m3[AB Monomer
A R B] -->|k| t1 t1 -.->|more k| pol[Long AB Polymer Chain] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Effect of Stoichiometric Imbalance on Maximum DP ```mermaid flowchart TD start([AA plus BB system initialised]) --> decide{Stoichiometric ratio r} decide -->|r equals 1.0| s1[Perfect balance] decide -->|r less than 1.0| s2[AA is minority group] decide -->|r much less than 1.0| s3[Severe imbalance] s1 --> f1[Maximum Xn at p=1 is INFINITE] s2 --> f2[Maximum Xn at p=1 equals 1+r divided by 1-r] s3 --> f3[Low Xn capped to small finite value] f1 --> out1[Used for high MW engineering plastics] f2 --> out2[Used for medium MW resins] f3 --> out3[Used for prepolymers and chain stopped systems] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Plot Schematic — $\bar{X}_n$ vs $p$ Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph p_axis[Conversion p] direction LR p1[0.00] --> p2[0.50] --> p3[0.90] --> p4[0.99] --> p5[0.999] end p1 --> xn1[Xn equals 1] p2 --> xn2[Xn equals 2] p3 --> xn3[Xn equals 10] p4 --> xn4[Xn equals 100] p5 --> xn5[Xn equals 1000] xn4 -.-> note[Sharp rise near p equals 1 indicates stringent conversion requirement] ``` ## 4.5 Block Architecture — Molecular Weight Control Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD feed1[Feed AA Monomer] --> mix[Reactor Mixer] feed2[Feed BB Monomer] --> mix feed3[Feed monofunctional stopper] --> mix mix --> react[Step Growth Polycondensation] react --> output[Polymer product of controlled Mn] output -->|target Mn low| use1[Coatings and Adhesives] output -->|target Mn medium| use2[Engineering Plastics] output -->|target Mn high| use3[High Performance Fibres] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of Diagrams:** > The diagrams above abstractly capture the molecular-level coupling events, the distinction between AB and AA+BB systems, the capping effect of stoichiometric imbalance, and the *exponential blow-up* of $\bar{X}_n$ as $p$ approaches unity — which is the most important behavioural feature of bi-functional step-growth polymerisation. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Define bi-functional monomers. Give two examples each of AB type and AA + BB type bi-functional monomers used in step-growth polymerisation.** **Model Answer:** Bi-functional monomers are organic molecules that possess **exactly two reactive functional groups** per molecule, capable of undergoing step-growth (condensation) reactions to form linear polymers. - **AB type examples:** 1. 6-Aminocaproic acid (H₂N–(CH₂)₅–COOH) → polymerises to **Nylon 6**. 2. *p*-Hydroxybenzoic acid (HO–C₆H₄–COOH) → forms aromatic polyesters. - **AA + BB type examples:** 1. Adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH) + Hexamethylenediamine (H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂) → **Nylon 6,6**. 2. Terephthalic acid (HOOC–C₆H₄–COOH) + Ethylene glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH) → **PET**. **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for examples]** --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **State the Carothers equation for an AA + BB polycondensation system with non-stoichiometric ratio $r$. Explain the significance of the equation.** **Model Answer:** $$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}$$ where $r = N_{A0}/N_{B0}$ is the stoichiometric ratio of A groups to B groups (with $r \leq 1$) and $p$ is the extent of reaction. **Significance:** 1. It quantifies the relationship between the molecular size of the polymer and the extent of reaction. 2. It reveals that **high $\bar{X}_n$ requires $p$ to approach 1** — a critical design parameter. 3. It shows that **stoichiometric imbalance ($r < 1$) caps the maximum achievable $\bar{X}_n$ at $\bar{X}_{n,\max} = (1+r)/(1-r)$**, providing the theoretical basis for **molecular weight control** by deliberate addition of excess of one monomer. 4. It is the foundation for predicting molecular weight distribution in step-growth polymerisation. **[3 Marks: 1 for equation + 2 for significance]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Module Pattern) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1] **(a)** Derive the Carothers equation for an AA + BB step-growth polymerisation system with stoichiometric imbalance ratio $r$. Discuss the limiting cases $r = 1$ and $p \to 1$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A mixture of ethylene glycol (1020 mol) and terephthalic acid (1000 mol) is polymerised to PET. Calculate: (i) the stoichiometric ratio $r$, (ii) the maximum attainable number-average degree of polymerisation, (iii) the number-average molecular weight at $p = 0.95$. Given $M_0 = 192$ g·mol⁻¹ for PET repeat unit. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete derivation.) $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$ **Limiting cases:** - **Case $r = 1$ (stoichiometric balance):** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2-2p} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$ The system behaves like an AB-type system; $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$ as $p \to 1$. - **Case $p \to 1$:** $$\bar{X}_{n,\max} = \lim_{p \to 1} \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1+r}{1-r}$$ This is **finite** when $r < 1$ — the chain length is *capped* by the excess of one monomer. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Stoichiometric ratio:** $$r = \frac{N_{A0}}{N_{B0}} = \frac{1000}{1020} = 0.9804$$ (Note: We conventionally take A as minority. Here, the minority is terephthalic acid at 1000 mol, so $r = 1000/1020 = 0.9804$.) **(ii) Maximum $\bar{X}_n$ at $p \to 1$:** $$\bar{X}_{n,\max} = \frac{1+r}{1-r} = \frac{1 + 0.9804}{1 - 0.9804} = \frac{1.9804}{0.0196} = 101.04$$ **(iii) $\bar{M}_n$ at $p = 0.95$:** First compute $\bar{X}_n$ at $p = 0.95$: $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1.9804}{1.9804 - 2(0.9804)(0.95)}$$ $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1.9804}{1.9804 - 1.8628} = \frac{1.9804}{0.1176} = 16.84$$ $$\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n = 192 \times 16.84 = 3233 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$$ **Conclusion:** Even with a small stoichiometric imbalance (only 2 % excess of one monomer), the maximum DP is dramatically reduced from infinity to about 101. **[14 Marks: 7 for (a) derivation + 7 for (b) numerical]** --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1] **(a)** Compare AB-type and AA + BB-type step-growth polymerisation systems. Derive the Carothers equation for the AB system. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain with equations how the molecular weight of a condensation polymer can be controlled by: (i) Stoichiometric imbalance, and (ii) Addition of a monofunctional chain stopper. Illustrate with a suitable industrial example. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison & Derivation:** | **Feature** | **AB System** | **AA + BB System** | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Number of monomers | 1 (A–R–B) | 2 (A–R–A and B–R′–B) | | Stoichiometry | Automatic | Requires precise measurement | | By-product | H₂O, HCl, etc. | H₂O, HCl, etc. | | Examples | Nylon 6, polycaprolactone | Nylon 6,6, PET | | Molecular-weight control | Difficult (must add stopper) | Easy (adjust r) | **Derivation for AB system:** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes.) $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$$ **(b) Molecular Weight Control:** **(i) Stoichiometric Imbalance:** In an AA + BB system, the maximum DP at full conversion of the minority group is: $$\bar{X}_{n,\max} = \frac{1+r}{1-r}$$ By deliberately making $r < 1$ (e.g., adding excess of one monomer), the molecular weight is **capped** at a predictable value. This is widely used to produce **resins of defined viscosity** for surface coatings and moulding compounds. **(ii) Monofunctional Chain Stopper:** Adding a small amount of a monofunctional monomer (e.g., acetic acid CH₃COOH in a diamine–diacid system) introduces a "dead end" — the monofunctional molecules cap chain ends, preventing further growth. If a monofunctional species $N_m$ is added to an otherwise stoichiometric system, the effective ratio becomes: $$r_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N_{A0} - N_m}{N_{A0}}$$ (by analogy with stoichiometric imbalance, since the monofunctional stopper effectively behaves like an excess of B groups in consuming A). **Industrial example:** In the manufacture of **alkyd resins** (used in paints), phthalic anhydride is deliberately used in slight deficit relative to glycerol to control molecular weight and prevent gelation. **[14 Marks: 7 for (a) comparison + 7 for (b) control strategy]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $r$ with the monomer ratio instead of the group ratio.** In AA+BB systems, $r$ is defined using the number of *functional groups* of A and B, not the number of molecules. (Since each AA has 2 A groups, the *group* ratio = *molecule* ratio in this special case — but students often get this wrong in non-symmetric systems.) > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in bond counting.** The derivation must explicitly show that $2N_{A0}$ A groups exist initially, not just $N_{A0}$. > 3. **Using the AB equation for AA+BB non-stoichiometric systems.** The simple $1/(1-p)$ applies *only* when $r = 1$. > 4. **Losing marks on the limiting-case discussion.** Always include the two special cases $r=1$ and $p \to 1$ — this is a favourite 2-mark sub-question. > 5. **Forgetting the units of $\bar{M}_n$.** Always state g·mol⁻¹ or kg·kmol⁻¹ explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final 60-Second Revision — Must Memorise:** - [x] **Bi-functional monomer** = molecule with exactly **2** reactive functional groups. - [x] Two system types: **AB** (single monomer) and **AA + BB** (two monomers). - [x] **AB equation:** $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$, $\bar{M}_n = M_0/(1-p)$. - [x] **AA + BB (general):** $\bar{X}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$, where $r = N_{A0}/N_{B0} \leq 1$. - [x] **Stoichiometric limit** ($r = 1$): reduces to $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$. - [x] **Full conversion limit** ($p \to 1$): $\bar{X}_{n,\max} = (1+r)/(1-r)$ — finite when $r < 1$. - [x] **Rule of thumb:** p = 0.99 → $\bar{X}_n = 100$; p = 0.999 → $\bar{X}_n = 1000$. - [x] **Molecular weight control:** Use deliberate stoichiometric imbalance $r < 1$ OR add monofunctional chain stopper. - [x] **Industrial examples:** Nylon 6 (AB), Nylon 6,6 and PET (AA+BB). - [x] **Key derivation step:** each A–B bond reduces molecule count by 1; total bonds at extent $p$ = $2pN_{A0}$ (for AA+BB) or $pN_0$ (for AB). - [x] **KTU's favourite trick question:** "What is the % unreacted groups at p = 0.99?" → Answer: 1 % (always minority group's unreacted = $1 - p$). - [x] **End-group analysis** is the experimental method to determine $\bar{X}_n$ in linear step-growth polymers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Mantra (Print This!):** > *"In step-growth, every molecule of every size can react; chain length explodes only near p = 1; stoichiometry is the master knob that controls $\bar{M}_n$."*

topics.title: Poly-Functional Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Poly-Functional Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Poly-Functional Systems — Formal Definition A **Poly-Functional System** in polymer chemistry refers to a monomer (or a mixture of monomers) that possesses **two or more reactive functional groups** capable of participating in polymerization reactions. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the term "functionality" (denoted as **f**) is rigorously defined as: > **[!IMPORTANT]** > **Functionality (f):** The number of reactive sites (functional groups) present on a single monomer molecule that can participate in bond formation during polymerization. The classification of monomers based on functionality is foundational to predicting the **polymer architecture** and **network topology** that will be obtained during step-growth polymerization. | Functionality (f) | Monomer Class | Resulting Architecture | |:---:|:---|:---| | **f = 1** | Monofunctional | Chain terminator (no polymer growth) | | **f = 2** | Bifunctional | Linear / Thermoplastic polymer | | **f = 3** | Trifunctional | Branched polymer | | **f ≥ 3** | Polyfunctional | Crosslinked / Network / Thermoset polymer | > **[!NOTE]** > **KTU Highlight:** The moment a poly-functional monomer (f ≥ 3) is introduced into a polymerization system, the resulting polymer transitions from a *linear thermoplastic* to a *crosslinked thermoset* network. This is the conceptual basis for the formation of **3D Network Polymers** like Bakelite, Epoxy resins, and Phenolic resins. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building a "Molecular Handshake" Imagine that every functional group on a monomer is like a **"hand"** that can shake hands (form a covalent bond) with another hand. - A **monofunctional** molecule is a person with **one hand** 🤚 — they can shake one hand but cannot build a chain. - A **bifunctional** molecule is a person with **two hands** 🙌 — they can link end-to-end, forming a long linear chain (like a conga line). - A **trifunctional** molecule is a person with **three hands** 🤚🙌 — now the chain branches, like a tree growing. - A **polyfunctional** molecule (4, 5, 6 hands) creates a **dense 3D jungle of interconnected chains** — this is a crosslinked network, like a fishing net where every knot is connected to multiple ropes. > **[!NOTE]** > **Critical Concept:** A single trifunctional monomer in a sea of bifunctional monomers acts as a "branching seed" — the more trifunctional monomers present, the more branches form, and eventually a giant infinite 3D network (a **gel**) appears suddenly. The exact moment this happens is the **Gel Point** (p_c). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Extent of Reaction (p):** Dimensionless ratio (0 ≤ p ≤ 1). - **Degree of Polymerization (X̄ₙ):** Dimensionless average chain length. - **Carothers' Gel Point (p_c):** Dimensionless critical extent of reaction. - **Branching Coefficient (α):** Dimensionless probability factor. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Carothers' Functionality Theory Wallace Carothers (1929) introduced the functionality concept to mathematically relate **monomer composition** to **final polymer architecture**. The system is built on three pillars: ### Pillar 1: Average Functionality (f_avg) When multiple monomers are mixed, each contributing a different number of functional groups, the system possesses an **average functionality**: $$f_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i \cdot f_i}{\sum_{i} N_i}$$ Where: - **N_i** = number of moles of monomer *i* - **f_i** = functionality of monomer *i* - The summation runs over **all monomers** in the reaction mixture. ### Pillar 2: Carothers' Equation for Degree of Polymerization For a step-growth polymerization involving poly-functional monomers, the **number-average degree of polymerization** (X̄ₙ) is: $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}}$$ Where: - **p** = extent of reaction (fraction of functional groups that have reacted) - **f_avg** = average functionality of the system - The factor **2** in the denominator arises because the total initial functional group count is N₀ × f_avg (Carothers' original derivation used f = 2 as the reference). > **[!IMPORTANT]** > **Why this equation is critical:** As p → 1 (complete reaction) and f_avg → 2, X̄ₙ → ∞. This is the mathematical signature of **gelation** — the polymer mixture transitions from finite chains to an infinite molecular network. ### Pillar 3: Carothers' Gel Point The **gel point (p_c)** is the critical extent of reaction at which an infinite molecular network (gel) first appears. Setting X̄ₙ → ∞ in Carothers' equation: $$p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}}$$ This is the **simplest gel point equation** and is widely used in introductory KTU problems. ## 2.2 Flory-Stockmayer Statistical Theory (Advanced) For an **A_f + B_g** system (i.e., A-type monomers with functionality f and B-type monomers with functionality g reacting together), Flory and Stockmayer provided a more rigorous statistical derivation. The **branching coefficient (α)** is defined as the probability that a given A-functional group leads to another A-functional group through a sequence of reactions: $$\alpha = p_A \cdot p_B \cdot r \cdot (f - 1)(g - 1) \cdot \frac{1}{f \cdot g}$$ Where: - **p_A** = extent of reaction of A groups - **p_B** = extent of reaction of B groups - **r** = stoichiometric imbalance ratio (N_A / N_B or vice versa) - **f, g** = functionalities of A and B monomers respectively The **Flory-Stockmayer Gel Point** is obtained when α reaches a critical value: $$(p_c)^2 \cdot r \cdot f \cdot g = 1 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r \cdot f \cdot g}}$$ > **[!NOTE]** > **KTU Pitfall:** The Carothers' gel point equation (p_c = 2/f_avg) is an **approximation** that overestimates the gel point. The Flory-Stockmayer equation is the **statistically rigorous** form and is the preferred answer for full-mark KTU derivations. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula Name** | **Equation** | **Variables** | **Units** | **Application** | |:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | Average Functionality | $f_{avg} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | N_i, f_i | Dimensionless | Multi-monomer systems | | Carothers' X̄ₙ | $\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p f_{avg}}$ | p, f_avg | Dimensionless | Step-growth DP prediction | | Carothers' Gel Point | $p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}}$ | f_avg | Dimensionless | Approximate gel point | | Stoichiometric Ratio | $r = \frac{N_A f_A}{N_B f_B}$ | N, f | Dimensionless | Imbalanced systems | | Flory-Stockmayer p_c | $p_c^2 \cdot r \cdot f_A \cdot g_B = 1$ | p_c, r, f, g | Dimensionless | Rigorous gel point | | Branching Coefficient | $\alpha = p_A^2 \cdot r \cdot \frac{(f_A - 1)(g_B - 1)}{f_A \cdot g_B}$ | p, r, f, g | Dimensionless | Network growth kinetics | | Number of Branch Units | $\rho = \frac{\sum (f_i - 2) N_i}{\sum f_i N_i}$ | N_i, f_i | Dimensionless | Branch density per crosslink | | Sol Fraction (Below p_c) | $W_s = (1 - p)^{(f-1)}$ | p, f | Dimensionless | Soluble fraction before gel | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Poly-functional systems form the **chemical foundation of every thermoset plastic and elastomer** in modern industry: - **Phenolic Resins (Bakelite):** Phenol (f = 3 with formaldehyde) → 3D network. - **Epoxy Resins:** DGEBA (f = 2) + Triamine hardener (f = 3-6) → crosslinked coatings. - **Polyurethane Foams:** Polyol (f = 3-8) + Diisocyanate (f = 2) → flexible/rigid foams. - **Unsaturated Polyester + Styrene:** Vinyl groups create crosslinked thermosets. - **Vulcanized Rubber:** Sulfur bridges (f = 2 of S₈) crosslink polyisoprene chains. > **[!IMPORTANT]** > **Engineering Insight:** By precisely controlling f_avg and stoichiometry (r), engineers can "tune" the gel point and final crosslink density, allowing them to design materials ranging from soft elastomers (low crosslink density) to rigid structural composites (high crosslink density). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carothers' Equation from Statistical Principles **Starting Premise:** Consider a step-growth polymerization where the initial total number of functional groups is N₀. At any extent of reaction p, the number of unreacted functional groups is N₀(1 - p). **Step 1: Number of unreacted functional groups** $$N_{unreacted} = N_0(1 - p)$$ **Step 2: Number of monomer molecules remaining (including reacted units)** Each monomer contributes f_i functional groups. For simplicity, take all monomers as having the same functionality f initially. Total functional groups = N₀ · f. After reaction, the total number of molecules N (monomers + oligomers + polymers) equals: $$N = N_0 - \frac{N_0 \cdot p \cdot f}{2}$$ **Step 3: Logic of division by 2** Each bond formed consumes 2 functional groups but only reduces the molecule count by 1. Therefore, the number of bonds formed = (N₀ · p · f) / 2. $$\text{Bonds formed} = \frac{N_0 \cdot p \cdot f}{2}$$ **Step 4: Express N in terms of N₀, p, and f** $$N = N_0 - \frac{N_0 \cdot p \cdot f}{2} = N_0 \left(1 - \frac{p \cdot f}{2}\right)$$ **Step 5: Define number-average degree of polymerization** The number-average DP is the ratio of initial molecules to molecules at extent p: $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0\left(1 - \frac{p f}{2}\right)}$$ **Step 6: Simplify** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{p f}{2}} = \frac{2}{2 - p f}$$ **Step 7: Generalize to mixed monomer systems** Replacing f with f_avg (average functionality of the system): $$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}}}$$ **Step 8: Derive Carothers' Gel Point** Gelation occurs when X̄ₙ → ∞, i.e., the denominator → 0: $$2 - p_c \cdot f_{avg} = 0$$ $$\boxed{p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Flory-Stockmayer Gel Point for A_f + B_g Systems **System:** A-functional monomer with functionality f (e.g., triamine) reacts with B-functional monomer with functionality g (e.g., diacid chloride). **Step 1: Define the stoichiometric ratio** Let N_A and N_B be the number of moles. The functional group ratio is: $$r = \frac{N_A \cdot f}{N_B \cdot g} \quad (r \leq 1, \text{ A is the minority})$$ **Step 2: Express extents of reaction** Total A groups initially = N_A · f Total B groups initially = N_B · g If p_A is the fraction of A groups reacted and p_B is the fraction of B groups reacted, by stoichiometry (each A-B bond consumes one A and one B group): $$N_A \cdot f \cdot p_A = N_B \cdot g \cdot p_B$$ $$p_B = r \cdot p_A$$ **Step 3: Probability of a branch propagating** Starting from a given A-group, the probability that the connected B-group has reacted is p_B. From that B-group, there are (g - 1) other B-groups (excluding the one that led to it). The probability that one of these (g - 1) groups is connected to a reacted A-group is p_A. The branching coefficient α is: $$\alpha = p_A \cdot p_B \cdot (f - 1)(g - 1) \cdot \frac{1}{f \cdot g} \cdot (\text{normalization})$$ In standard Flory notation (assuming equal reactivity of all groups): $$\alpha = p_A^2 \cdot r \cdot \frac{(f-1)(g-1)}{f \cdot g}$$ **Step 4: Critical condition for gelation** Gelation occurs when an infinite branch network can propagate. The critical condition is: $$\alpha_c \cdot (f - 1) = 1$$ This leads to: $$\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f - 1}$$ **Step 5: Substitute and solve for p_c** At the gel point, p_A = p_B = p_c (equivalently p_B = r·p_A and we use p_A = p_c): $$p_c^2 \cdot r \cdot \frac{(f-1)(g-1)}{f \cdot g} = \frac{1}{f-1}$$ For the simplified case where the **minority groups are fully consumed first** (B groups, so p_B = 1, and p_A = p_c): $$p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r \cdot f \cdot g}}$$ **Step 6: General Flory-Stockmayer Gel Point Equation** $$\boxed{p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r \cdot f_A \cdot g_B}}}$$ or equivalently: $$\boxed{p_c^2 \cdot r \cdot f_A \cdot g_B = 1}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A polymerization mixture contains 2 moles of glycerol (f = 3) and 3 moles of adipic acid (f = 2). Calculate (a) f_avg, (b) Carothers' gel point, and (c) the degree of polymerization at p = 0.8. **Solution:** **Part (a): Average functionality** $$f_{avg} = \frac{N_1 f_1 + N_2 f_2}{N_1 + N_2} = \frac{(2)(3) + (3)(2)}{2 + 3} = \frac{6 + 6}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4$$ **Part (b): Carothers' gel point** $$p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}} = \frac{2}{2.4} = 0.833$$ **Part (c): Degree of polymerization at p = 0.8** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}} = \frac{2}{2 - (0.8)(2.4)} = \frac{2}{2 - 1.92} = \frac{2}{0.08} = 25$$ **Interpretation:** At p = 0.8 (just below gel point), the average chain length is 25 monomer units. At p_c = 0.833, the system gels. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behavior at Different p and f_avg | f_avg | p = 0.5 | p = 0.8 | p = 0.9 | p = 0.95 | p = p_c | p > p_c | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **1.5** | 1.6 | 4.0 | 6.7 | 10.0 | No gel | — | | **2.0** | 2.0 | 10.0 | 20.0 | 40.0 | p_c = 1.00 | — | | **2.4** | 2.5 | 25.0 | 66.7 | 200 | p_c = 0.833 | **GEL** | | **3.0** | 4.0 | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ | p_c = 0.667 | **GEL** | > **[!IMPORTANT]** > **KTU Insight:** Notice how X̄ₙ grows **asymptotically** as p approaches p_c. This mathematical singularity is the signature of the gel point — physically, this corresponds to the formation of an infinite molecular network that connects the entire reaction vessel. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Monomer Functionality Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[MONOMER CLASSIFICATION BY FUNCTIONALITY] --> B["f = 1
MONOFUNCTIONAL"] A --> C["f = 2
BIFUNCTIONAL"] A --> D["f = 3
TRIFUNCTIONAL"] A --> E["f greater than 3
POLYFUNCTIONAL"] B --> B1[Chain Terminator] B1 --> B2[Example: Acetic Acid CH3COOH] C --> C1[Linear Chain Formation] C1 --> C2[Example: Adipic Acid HOOC-CH2-4-COOH] C1 --> C3[Thermoplastic Polymer] D --> D1[Branched Polymer] D1 --> D2[Example: Glycerol C3H5-OH-3] D1 --> D3[Network Precursor] E --> E1[3D Network Polymer] E1 --> E2[Example: Pentaerythritol C-CH2-OH-4] E1 --> E3[Thermoset Polymer] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000 style C fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style D fill:#f97316,color:#ffffff style E fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Polymer Architecture Evolution with Functionality ```mermaid graph LR A1["Monomer A
f equals 1
One reactive group"] -.->|"Forms only
dimers"| STOP[Chain ends] A2["Monomer B
f equals 2
Two reactive groups"] ==> LINEAR["LINEAR POLYMER
Thermoplastic
Soluble and Fusible"] A3["Monomer C
f equals 3
Three reactive groups"] ==> BRANCHED["BRANCHED POLYMER
Lower crystallinity
More amorphous"] A4["Monomer D
f greater than or equal to 3
Multiple reactive groups"] ==> NETWORK["3D NETWORK
Thermoset
Insoluble and Infusible"] NETWORK --> GEL[Gel Point Reached] GEL --> FINAL[Infinite Molecular Network] style A1 fill:#94a3b8,color:#000000 style A2 fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style A3 fill:#f97316,color:#ffffff style A4 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style LINEAR fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000000 style BRANCHED fill:#fed7aa,color:#000000 style NETWORK fill:#fecaca,color:#000000 style GEL fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff style FINAL fill:#450a0a,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Step-Growth Polymerization Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: Monomer Mixture with given f_avg] --> STEP1[Step 1: Calculate f_avg] STEP1 --> STEP2[Step 2: Determine p_c using Carothers Equation] STEP2 --> STEP3[Step 3: React monomers at controlled extent p] STEP3 --> DECISION{Is p less than p_c?} DECISION -->|Yes| LINEAR[Finite Chains Form] LINEAR --> XN[Calculate Xn using Carothers] XN --> LINEAR DECISION -->|No| NETWORK[Infinite 3D Network Forms] NETWORK --> GEL[Gel Point Crossed] GEL --> SOL[SOL and GEL Phases Coexist] SOL --> FINAL[Crosslinked Thermoset] style START fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style STEP1 fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff style STEP2 fill:#8b5cf6,color:#ffffff style STEP3 fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff style DECISION fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff style LINEAR fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style NETWORK fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style GEL fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff style FINAL fill:#450a0a,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Poly-Functional Reaction System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT STAGE: MONOMER FEED"] M1[Monomer 1: N1 moles, f1 functionality] M2[Monomer 2: N2 moles, f2 functionality] M3[Monomer 3: N3 moles, f3 functionality] end subgraph CALC["CALCULATION STAGE"] FAVG[Compute f_avg] PC[Compute p_c equals 2 divided by f_avg] XN[Compute target Xn for given p] end subgraph REACT["REACTION STAGE: STEP GROWTH"] INIT[Initial Functional Groups equals N0 times f_avg] REACT2[Random Collisions and Bond Formation] CHAIN[Chain Extension and Branching] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE: PRODUCT TYPES"] P1["SOL PHASE
Finite Chains
Soluble Fraction Ws"] P2["GEL PHASE
Infinite Network
Crosslinked"] P3["FINAL PRODUCT
Thermoplastic or Thermoset
