Based on p relative to p_c"] end M1 --> FAVG M2 --> FAVG M3 --> FAVG FAVG --> PC FAVG --> XN PC --> INIT INIT --> REACT2 REACT2 --> CHAIN CHAIN --> P1 CHAIN --> P2 P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 style INPUT fill:#dbeafe,color:#000000 style CALC fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000 style REACT fill:#fce7f3,color:#000000 style OUTPUT fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000 style P1 fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000000 style P2 fill:#fca5a5,color:#000000 style P3 fill:#86efac,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Functionality with Examples** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Functionality (f)** is defined as the number of reactive functional groups present in a monomer molecule that can participate in polymerization reactions. **Classification:** - **Monofunctional (f = 1):** Examples — Acetic acid (CH₃COOH), Phenol with one reactive position - **Bifunctional (f = 2):** Examples — Adipic acid [HOOC-(CH₂)₄-COOH], Ethylene glycol (HO-CH₂-CH₂-OH) - **Trifunctional (f = 3):** Examples — Glycerol (CH₂OH-CHOH-CH₂OH), Trimethylolpropane - **Polyfunctional (f ≥ 3):** Examples — Pentaerythritol [C(CH₂OH)₄], Phenol with three reactive positions **Significance:** The functionality determines the final polymer architecture — bifunctional monomers yield linear polymers, while polyfunctional monomers yield branched or crosslinked network polymers. --- ### **Question 2: What is the Gel Point? Write Carothers' Equation for Gel Point.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Gel Point (p_c):** It is the critical extent of reaction at which an infinite three-dimensional molecular network (gel) first appears in a polymerization system. Beyond this point, the polymer becomes insoluble and infusible (thermoset behavior). **Carothers' Equation for Gel Point:** $$p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}}$$ Where **f_avg** is the average functionality of the monomer mixture calculated as: $$f_{avg} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$$ **Example:** For a system with f_avg = 2.5, p_c = 2/2.5 = 0.8, meaning gelation occurs when 80% of functional groups have reacted. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice ### **Question A: Poly-Functional Step-Growth Polymerization Analysis** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks] Define functionality and average functionality. A monomer mixture contains 3 moles of phthalic anhydride (f = 2), 2 moles of glycerol (f = 3), and 1 mole of pentaerythritol (f = 4). Calculate the average functionality and the Carothers' gel point.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Definition of Functionality (2 marks)** Functionality (f) is the number of reactive functional groups per monomer molecule capable of participating in bond formation during polymerization. **Step 2: Definition of Average Functionality (1 mark)** Average functionality is the weighted mean of functionalities of all monomer species in a mixed system: $$f_{avg} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$$ **Step 3: Calculate f_avg (2 marks)** $$f_{avg} = \frac{(3)(2) + (2)(3) + (1)(4)}{3 + 2 + 1}$$ $$f_{avg} = \frac{6 + 6 + 4}{6} = \frac{16}{6} = 2.667$$ **Step 4: Calculate Carothers' Gel Point (2 marks)** $$p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}} = \frac{2}{2.667} = 0.75$$ **Final Answer:** f_avg = 2.667, p_c = 0.75 (gelation occurs at 75% conversion) --- **(b) [7 Marks] Derive the Carothers' equation for the degree of polymerization (X̄ₙ) in a poly-functional system. Using this equation, find the extent of reaction at which X̄ₙ = 100 for the system described in part (a).** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Setup the derivation (1 mark)** Consider N₀ total monomer molecules with average functionality f_avg. Total functional groups = N₀ · f_avg. **Step 2: Bonds formed at extent p (2 marks)** At extent of reaction p, the number of functional groups reacted = N₀ · f_avg · p. Each bond consumes 2 functional groups, so: $$\text{Bonds formed} = \frac{N_0 \cdot f_{avg} \cdot p}{2}$$ **Step 3: Molecules remaining (2 marks)** Each bond reduces the molecule count by 1: $$N = N_0 - \frac{N_0 \cdot f_{avg} \cdot p}{2} = N_0\left(1 - \frac{p \cdot f_{avg}}{2}\right)$$ **Step 4: Carothers' Equation (1 mark)** $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}}$$ **Step 5: Solve for p at X̄ₙ = 100 (1 mark)** $$100 = \frac{2}{2 - p(2.667)}$$ $$2 - 2.667p = \frac{2}{100} = 0.02$$ $$2.667p = 1.98$$ $$p = 0.742 \text{ or } 74.2\%$$ **Final Answer:** p = 0.742 (74.2% conversion required to reach X̄ₙ = 100) --- ### **Question B: Flory-Stockmayer Theory and Network Formation** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks] State and explain the Flory-Stockmayer theory for gelation. Derive the gel point equation for an A_f + B_g system where A has functionality 3 and B has functionality 2, with stoichiometric ratio r = 0.8.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Flory-Stockmayer Theory Statement (2 marks)** The Flory-Stockmayer theory provides a **statistical mechanical** treatment of gelation in poly-functional step-growth systems. It uses the concept of the **branching coefficient (α)**, defined as the probability that a randomly chosen path from one functional group through a reacted bond leads to another branch point. **Step 2: Define Branching Coefficient (2 marks)** For an A_f + B_g system: $$\alpha = p_A^2 \cdot r \cdot \frac{(f-1)(g-1)}{f \cdot g}$$ Where r = N_A·f / (N_B·g) is the stoichiometric ratio of functional groups. **Step 3: Gel Point Condition (1 mark)** Gelation occurs when: $$\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f-1}$$ **Step 4: Derive p_c (1 mark)** $$p_c^2 \cdot r \cdot \frac{(f-1)(g-1)}{f \cdot g} = \frac{1}{f-1}$$ $$p_c^2 \cdot r \cdot \frac{(g-1)}{f \cdot g} = \frac{1}{(f-1)^2}$$ $$p_c = \frac{1}{(f-1)\sqrt{r \cdot \frac{(g-1)}{f \cdot g} \cdot (f-1)^2}}$$ Simplifying: $p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r \cdot f \cdot g}}$ for the case where minority groups are limiting. **Step 5: Calculate for given values (1 mark)** With f = 3, g = 2, r = 0.8: $$p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.8 \times 3 \times 2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4.8}} = \frac{1}{2.191} = 0.456$$ **Final Answer:** p_c = 0.456 (Flory-Stockmayer gel point) --- **(b) [7 Marks] Compare Carothers' and Flory-Stockmayer gel point predictions. For the system in part (a), calculate Carothers' gel point and explain why it differs from the Flory-Stockmayer value. What is the engineering significance of this difference?** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Carothers' p_c (2 marks)** First compute f_avg for the A₃ + B₂ system with r = 0.8. If we take N_A = 0.8, N_B = 1 (to satisfy r = 0.8): $$f_{avg} = \frac{(0.8)(3) + (1)(2)}{0.8 + 1} = \frac{2.4 + 2}{1.8} = \frac{4.4}{1.8} = 2.444$$ $$p_c(\text{Carothers}) = \frac{2}{2.444} = 0.818$$ **Step 2: Tabulate comparison (2 marks)** | Method | Gel Point (p_c) | Nature | |:---|:---:|:---| | Carothers | 0.818 | Empirical, approximate | | Flory-Stockmayer | 0.456 | Statistical, rigorous | **Step 3: Explain the difference (2 marks)** Carothers' equation assumes gelation occurs at the mathematical singularity of X̄ₙ → ∞, which **overestimates p_c**. Flory-Stockmayer theory accounts for the **statistical nature of branch network formation** and recognizes that an infinite cluster can form through multiple parallel paths even before all chains become infinite. Hence, Flory-Stockmayer predicts a **lower (earlier) gel point**. **Step 4: Engineering Significance (1 mark)** In industrial thermoset processing (e.g., epoxy curing, phenolic resin molding), using Carothers' gel point would lead to **premature solidification predictions** and potential process failures. The Flory-Stockmayer theory provides more accurate process control, helping engineers determine the **pot life** and **cure cycle** of thermoset materials. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark Loss Pitfalls in Poly-Functional Systems:** > > 1. **Confusing f and f_avg:** Students often use the functionality of a single monomer (e.g., f = 3 for glycerol) instead of computing f_avg for the entire mixture. **Always compute f_avg first** when dealing with multi-component systems. > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Carothers' equation:** The standard form is X̄ₙ = 2/(2 - p·f_avg), NOT 1/(1 - p·f_avg/2). Both are mathematically equivalent, but the first form is the **KTU board-preferred notation**. > > 3. **Mixing up Carothers' and Flory-Stockmayer equations:** Carothers' p_c = 2/f_avg is a **quick approximation**. Flory-Stockmayer's p_c = 1/√(r·f·g) is the **rigorous statistical form**. For full marks on derivations, **always state which theory** you are applying. > > 4. **Ignoring stoichiometric ratio r:** When monomers are not in exact stoichiometric balance, the **r factor** must be included. Setting r = 1 without justification will lose 1-2 marks. > > 5. **Not defining variables:** In derivations, KTU examiners award 1 mark each for: (i) stating assumptions, (ii) defining all variables, (iii) showing all algebraic steps. Skipping definitions costs easy marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### **High-Density Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Functionality (f)** = number of reactive groups per monomer molecule. - ✅ **f = 1** → Chain terminator (no polymer). - ✅ **f = 2** → Linear thermoplastic polymer. - ✅ **f ≥ 3** → Branched or crosslinked network polymer (thermoset). - ✅ **Average Functionality:** $f_{avg} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ — **must compute for mixed systems.** - ✅ **Carothers' Equation:** $\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}}$ — relates DP to extent of reaction. - ✅ **Carothers' Gel Point:** $p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}}$ — **approximate**, overestimate. - ✅ **Flory-Stockmayer Gel Point:** $p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{r \cdot f_A \cdot g_B}}$ — **statistically rigorous.** - ✅ **Stoichiometric Ratio:** $r = \frac{N_A f_A}{N_B f_B}$ — always ≤ 1 (minority/majority). - ✅ **Branching Coefficient (α):** probability of branch propagation; gel occurs when α reaches critical value. - ✅ **Gel Point** = critical extent of reaction where infinite 3D network forms; system becomes insoluble and infusible. - ✅ **Sol-Gel Analysis:** below p_c → sol phase (soluble chains); above p_c → gel phase (infinite network) + sol phase coexists. - ✅ **Industrial Examples:** Bakelite (phenol-formaldehyde), Epoxy resins, Polyurethane foams, Vulcanized rubber. - ✅ **Engineering Control:** By tuning f_avg and r, manufacturers control gel point, pot life, and final crosslink density. - ✅ **Memory Aid:** "**Two hands linear, three hands branched, four-plus hands network**."

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Polymers? (Syllabus Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A **polymer** is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of small repeating structural units called **monomers**, joined together by covalent **chemical bonds** through a process termed **polymerisation**. The term originates from the Greek words *poly* (meaning *many*) and *meros* (meaning *parts* or *units*).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for Polymer Engineering, polymers are broadly classified into three families that govern most of their advantages and limitations:

| Family | Full Form | Behaviour on Heating | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastics** | Thermo-softening plastics | Soften on heating, harden on cooling (reversible) | PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET |
| **Thermosets** | Thermo-setting plastics | Harden permanently on heating (irreversible) | Phenolic, Epoxy, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine |
| **Elastomers** | Elastic polymers | Exhibit high elastic recovery | Natural rubber, SBR, Neoprene, Silicone rubber |

The unique combination of long-chain molecular architecture, **van der Waals forces** between chains, and tunable chemistry is what gives polymers their characteristic advantages and disadvantages over conventional engineering materials such as metals, ceramics, and wood.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Bricks-and-Mortar House

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Making Polymers Instantly Click**
> Imagine a **bricks-and-mortar house**. Each **brick** is a *monomer* (a small molecule such as ethylene, $\text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}_2$). The **mortar** that glues the bricks together represents the *covalent chemical bonds* formed during polymerisation. Stacking thousands of bricks in a long chain gives you a **polymer wall** — flexible enough to bend slightly (which is why a plastic ruler flexes), but strong enough to hold weight. Now compare this to a **metal ingot** — its atoms are held together by rigid *metallic bonds* in a tightly packed crystalline lattice, like a wall built from a single solid block of stone. The brick-wall structure (polymer) is **lighter, easier to reshape, and resistant to rust**, but it **softens in heat and can be dissolved by strong solvents** — limitations that the stone wall does not have. This single analogy explains the entire trade-off that defines polymer engineering.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Polymer Engineering

Every design decision in modern engineering — from automotive panels to biomedical implants, from food packaging to aerospace composites — begins with a **cost–performance trade-off** between polymers and traditional materials. Understanding the advantages and disadvantages of polymers is therefore the **conceptual foundation** upon which material selection, processing method choice, and end-of-life recycling strategies are built. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** (understanding polymer classification and properties) and supports Bloom's cognitive level **L1 (Remember)** and **L2 (Understand)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analysis Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Logic Behind the Advantages

Polymers exhibit a set of unique advantages that arise directly from their **long-chain molecular architecture** and **weak intermolecular forces** between chains. These advantages are not arbitrary — they follow logically from polymer physics.

- **Low Density (Lightweight Nature)**
  - *Why?* The backbone of most polymers consists of light elements — **Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), and sometimes Chlorine (Cl) or Fluorine (F)** — and the chains are held apart by relatively weak van der Waals forces, leaving a significant amount of free volume.
  - *Consequence:* Most commercial polymers have densities in the range **0.85 – 1.5 g/cm³**, compared to **2.7 g/cm³ for aluminium**, **7.8 g/cm³ for steel**, and **2.5 g/cm³ for concrete**. This is the primary reason polymers dominate the **automotive and aerospace weight-reduction** agenda.

- **Corrosion and Chemical Resistance**
  - *Why?* Polymers are **organic compounds** with no free electrons in their structure (unlike metals, which undergo oxidation). They do not rust, do not react with most acids, alkalis, or salts at ambient temperatures, and are not attacked by atmospheric oxygen in the way iron is.
  - *Consequence:* Polymer pipes (PVC, HDPE) have replaced cast iron in water distribution; polymer coatings protect offshore oil rigs; polymer linings are used in chemical storage tanks.

- **Design Flexibility and Ease of Processing**
  - *Why?* Thermoplastics can be melted, flowed, and reshaped at moderate temperatures (typically **150 – 300 °C**) and at low pressures (**0.1 – 10 MPa**). This allows complex shapes to be produced in a single step.
  - *Consequence:* Injection moulding, blow moulding, extrusion, thermoforming, and 3D-printing are all possible — giving engineers enormous geometric freedom at **mass-production speeds** (cycle times of seconds).

- **Tunable Mechanical Properties**
  - *Why?* By controlling **molecular weight (M̄)**, **degree of crystallinity (χ_c)**, **cross-link density**, and **copolymer composition**, the engineer can dial in properties ranging from soft rubber (Shore A 30) to rigid engineering plastic (tensile strength up to **300 MPa** for aramid fibres).

- **Electrical Insulation**
  - *Why?* Polymers are **covalent compounds with no free charge carriers (no free electrons or ions)**. Their electrical resistivity typically lies in the range **$10^{12}$ to $10^{18}$ Ω·cm**.
  - *Consequence:* Polymers are the standard insulation material for wires, cables, transformers, and printed circuit boards.

- **Low Thermal Conductivity (Thermal Insulation)**
  - *Why?* Heat transfer through polymers is limited by the lack of free electrons (as in metals) and by the amorphous regions that scatter phonons.
  - *Consequence:* Polymer foams (PU foam, EPS) are used as building insulation. Thermal conductivity is typically **0.1 – 0.3 W/m·K**, compared to **50 W/m·K for aluminium**.

- **Transparency and Colourability**
  - *Why?* Amorphous polymers such as PMMA, PS, and PC can be made optically transparent because they have no crystallites to scatter light, and they can be easily pigmented using masterbatches.

- **Cost-Effectiveness**
  - The **energy required to process polymers** is far lower than that required to smelt metals. Polymer products typically cost **30 – 70 % less** than their metal equivalents on a per-part basis.

## 2.2 The Engineering Logic Behind the Disadvantages

Equally important are the limitations, which stem from the same weak intermolecular forces that give polymers their advantages.

- **Poor Heat Resistance (Low Thermal Stability)**
  - *Why?* The C–C and C–H covalent bonds in the polymer backbone begin to **thermally degrade** at relatively low temperatures (typically **200 – 400 °C**), well below the melting point of most metals (>1000 °C) and ceramics (>2000 °C).
  - *Consequence:* Polymers cannot be used in high-temperature applications such as jet-engine turbine blades, furnace linings, or high-power braking systems without reinforcement.

- **Low Mechanical Strength and Stiffness Compared to Metals**
  - *Why?* The Young's modulus of most commodity polymers is in the range **0.1 – 5 GPa**, compared to **70 GPa for aluminium** and **200 GPa for steel**.
  - *Consequence:* Pure polymers are unsuitable for load-bearing structural applications; hence the rise of **fibre-reinforced polymer composites (FRP)** and **glass-fibre-reinforced plastics (GFRP)**.

- **High Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)**
  - *Why?* The weak secondary bonds between chains allow large amplitude molecular motions when heated.
  - *Consequence:* Polymers expand **5 – 15 times more** than metals per °C of temperature rise, causing dimensional instability in precision parts.

- **Flammability**
  - *Why?* Most polymers are **organic** and therefore combustible, releasing heat, smoke, and toxic gases (e.g., HCN from polyurethanes, HCl from PVC) on burning.
  - *Consequence:* Flame retardants (such as brominated compounds, ATH, and phosphorus-based additives) must be compounded into polymers for electrical and construction applications.

- **Environmental Pollution and Poor Recyclability**
  - *Why?* Polymers are highly resistant to natural degradation. Their persistence in landfills and oceans (estimated **centuries to millennia**) creates severe **microplastic pollution**.
  - *Consequence:* Growing regulatory pressure (EU Single-Use Plastics Directive, Indian EPR rules) is forcing the industry to develop **biodegradable polymers** (PLA, PHA) and **chemical recycling** technologies.

- **UV and Oxidative Degradation**
  - *Why?* Ultraviolet radiation from sunlight breaks the C–C bonds in the polymer chain (**photodegradation**), and atmospheric oxygen reacts with free radicals generated during processing (**oxidative degradation**).
  - *Consequence:* Outdoor polymer products require **UV stabilisers, HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilisers), and antioxidants** to achieve a useful service life.

- **Limited Solvent and Stress-Cracking Resistance in Some Polymers**
  - *Why?* Amorphous polymers can be dissolved or swelled by organic solvents that have similar solubility parameters (δ) to the polymer. **Environmental stress cracking (ESC)** can occur in HDPE exposed to detergents.

- **Difficulties in Joining and Repairing**
  - *Why?* Thermosets cannot be remelted, and thermoplastics require specialised techniques (hot-plate welding, ultrasonic welding, solvent bonding, adhesive bonding). Mechanical fastening often introduces stress concentrations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparative Analysis Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Quick Reference — Save This Table**
> The following table summarises the **quantitative property comparison** between polymers and traditional materials. This is a **favourite KTU question** topic. Memorise the order-of-magnitude values, not exact numbers.

| Property | Polymers (Typical) | Metals (Typical) | Ceramics (Typical) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (ρ) | 0.85 – 1.5 | 2.7 – 8.9 | 2.0 – 6.0 | g/cm³ |
| Young's Modulus (E) | 0.1 – 5 | 70 – 200 | 100 – 400 | GPa |
| Tensile Strength (σ) | 20 – 100 (neat), up to 3000 (aramid fibre) | 200 – 1500 | 100 – 5000 | MPa |
| Melting Point (T_m) | 100 – 400 (thermoplastics) | 660 – 1540 | > 1500 (decompose) | °C |
| Thermal Conductivity (k) | 0.1 – 0.5 | 15 – 400 | 1 – 30 | W/m·K |
| CTE (α) | 50 – 200 | 10 – 25 | 3 – 10 | ×10⁻⁶ /°C |
| Electrical Resistivity (ρ_e) | 10¹² – 10¹⁸ | 10⁻⁸ – 10⁻⁶ | 10⁸ – 10¹⁴ | Ω·cm |
| Processing Temperature | 150 – 300 | 500 – 1500 | 1000 – 2000 | °C |
| Cost per kg (commodity) | 1 – 3 | 1 – 5 | 0.5 – 5 | USD (indicative) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note on Table Syntax**
> In KTU exam scripts, always quote **property ranges with correct SI units**. For electrical resistivity, use the symbol **$\rho_e$** (not $\sigma$, which is reserved for conductivity). For density, use **$\rho$** to avoid confusion. The KTU examiner awards **½ mark** for correct unit, and **½ mark** for correct order of magnitude.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of This Comparison

The property ranges tabulated above are the foundation of **Ashby material selection charts** (developed by Professor Michael Ashby, Cambridge University). These charts plot property A versus property B (e.g., strength vs. density, stiffness vs. cost) and allow engineers to **visually identify the optimal material** for a given design requirement. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is reinforced in the design project components of higher semesters. For Polymer Engineering students, understanding the advantages and disadvantages is the **first step toward intelligent material substitution** — replacing a metal part with a polymer that meets the same functional requirement at lower cost and weight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation of Performance Indices

## 3.1 The Performance-Index Concept (Ashby Method)

Since the topic is conceptual and does not involve a single differential equation, the KTU Board often asks students to **derive the performance indices** that justify material substitution. A *performance index* is a dimensionless figure of merit that combines two or more properties and identifies the best material for a given function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Performance Indices Matter in KTU Exams**
> A 14-mark KTU question often asks: *"Justify the replacement of a steel component with a polymer composite using a suitable performance index."* The most common indices are derived below.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Specific Stiffness Index (E/ρ)

Consider a **lightweight tie-rod** in a structural frame. The rod must have minimum stiffness (to resist elastic deflection) and minimum mass (to reduce payload). The **objective function** is to maximise stiffness per unit mass.

- **Step 1 — State the Design Requirement.** The axial stiffness of a rod of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is given by:
$$k_{axial} = \frac{E \cdot A}{L}$$

- **Step 2 — Express Mass in Terms of Geometry.** The mass of the rod is:
$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

- **Step 3 — Eliminate Geometry (A and L).** Solving the stiffness equation for $A$ gives:
$$A = \frac{k_{axial} \cdot L}{E}$$

- **Step 4 — Substitute A into the mass equation.** We get:
$$m = \rho \cdot \left( \frac{k_{axial} \cdot L}{E} \right) \cdot L = \frac{k_{axial} \cdot \rho \cdot L^2}{E}$$

- **Step 5 — Define the Performance Index.** For a given stiffness $k_{axial}$ and length $L$, the mass is minimised when the ratio $E / \rho$ is maximised. Therefore, the **specific stiffness performance index** is:
$$P_{stiff} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

- **Step 6 — Numerical Comparison.**
  - **Steel:** $E / \rho$ = $\frac{200 \text{ GPa}}{7.8 \text{ g/cm}^3}$ ≈ 25.6 MN·m/kg
  - **Aluminium:** $E / \rho$ = $\frac{70}{2.7}$ ≈ 25.9 MN·m/kg
  - **Carbon-fibre-reinforced polymer (CFRP):** $E / \rho$ = $\frac{150}{1.6}$ ≈ 93.8 MN·m/kg
  - **Conclusion:** CFRP is **~3.7 times stiffer per unit mass** than steel. This is the mathematical basis for replacing metal aircraft components with CFRP — and the rigorous justification expected in a KTU answer.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Specific Strength Index (σ_y / ρ)

For a **lightweight tension cable** that must carry a given load without yielding, the relevant performance index is **specific strength**.

- **Step 1 — Load-Carrying Capacity.** The maximum tensile load a cable of cross-section $A$ can carry is:
$$F_{max} = \sigma_y \cdot A$$

- **Step 2 — Mass Equation.** As before, $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$.

- **Step 3 — Eliminate A.** Solving for $A = F_{max} / \sigma_y$ and substituting:
$$m = \frac{F_{max} \cdot \rho \cdot L}{\sigma_y}$$

- **Step 4 — Define the Performance Index.** The mass is minimised when the ratio $\sigma_y / \rho$ is maximised:
$$P_{str} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$$

- **Step 5 — Numerical Comparison.**
  - **Mild steel:** $\sigma_y / \rho$ = $\frac{250}{7.8}$ ≈ 32 kN·m/kg
  - **Aluminium alloy 7075-T6:** $\sigma_y / \rho$ = $\frac{503}{2.8}$ ≈ 180 kN·m/kg
  - **Aramid (Kevlar 49) fibre:** $\sigma_y / \rho$ = $\frac{2800}{1.44}$ ≈ 1944 kN·m/kg
  - **Conclusion:** Kevlar offers **~60 times the specific strength of mild steel**, justifying its use in bulletproof vests and aerospace tethers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Insight**
> In a 7-mark sub-question on performance indices, the typical KTU mark distribution is:
> - **2 marks** — Clear statement of design objective (stiffness-limited or strength-limited).
> - **3 marks** — Rigorous algebraic elimination of geometry to arrive at the ratio $E/\rho$ or $\sigma_y/\rho$.
> - **1 mark** — Correct numerical comparison with units.
> - **1 mark** — Engineering conclusion and real-world example.
> Skipping any of these four steps results in **loss of 2 – 3 marks** — a common pitfall.

## 3.2 Comparative Property Matrix — Polymers vs. Traditional Materials

The following **analytical matrix** is a high-density summary suitable for a one-page KTU answer. Each cell is rated on a **5-point scale** (5 = excellent, 1 = poor), based on representative data for engineering design.

| Engineering Property | Thermoplastics | Thermosets | Elastomers | Metals | Ceramics | Wood |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (lightweight) | 5 | 4 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 3 |
| Tensile Strength (neat) | 3 | 4 | 2 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Stiffness / Modulus | 2 | 3 | 1 | 5 | 5 | 4 |
| Impact Resistance | 4 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 1 | 2 |
| Heat Resistance | 2 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 5 | 2 |
| Chemical Resistance | 4 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 1 |
| Corrosion Resistance | 5 | 5 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 2 |
| Electrical Insulation | 5 | 5 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 2 |
| Ease of Processing | 5 | 4 | 4 | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| Cost per part | 5 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 5 |
| Recyclability | 3 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| Environmental Impact (low) | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Read This Matrix in a KTU Answer**
> A **score of 5** means the material family is the **best-in-class** for that property. A score of **1** means it is **worst-in-class**. The matrix makes it visually clear that **no single material family dominates** in all properties — this is the engineering principle of **material compromise** and the reason the KTU examiner rewards **balanced, evidence-based arguments** rather than blanket statements like "polymers are better than metals."

## 3.3 Quantitative Worked Example — Mass Savings from Polymer Substitution

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A steel tie-rod of length 1.0 m and diameter 20 mm is to be replaced by a polymer composite rod of the same length and stiffness. Calculate the percentage mass saving. Use the following data:
> - Steel: $E_s$ = 200 GPa, $\rho_s$ = 7.8 g/cm³
> - CFRP composite: $E_c$ = 150 GPa, $\rho_c$ = 1.6 g/cm³

**Solution (with full marks-allocated reasoning):**

- **Step 1 — Equate the Stiffness.** The axial stiffness of the steel rod is:
$$k_s = \frac{E_s \cdot A_s}{L} = \frac{E_s \cdot (\pi/4) \cdot d_s^2}{L}$$

- **Step 2 — Equate to the Composite Stiffness.** The composite rod has the same stiffness:
$$k_c = \frac{E_c \cdot A_c}{L} = \frac{E_c \cdot (\pi/4) \cdot d_c^2}{L}$$

- **Step 3 — Equate and Solve for Diameter Ratio.** Since $k_s = k_c$:
$$\frac{E_s \cdot d_s^2}{1} = \frac{E_c \cdot d_c^2}{1}$$

$$d_c^2 = d_s^2 \cdot \frac{E_s}{E_c}$$

$$d_c = d_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E_s}{E_c}} = 20 \text{ mm} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{200}{150}}$$

$$d_c = 20 \cdot \sqrt{1.3333} = 20 \cdot 1.1547 = 23.09 \text{ mm}$$

- **Step 4 — Compute Masses.** Mass = $\rho \cdot A \cdot L$:
$$m_s = \rho_s \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_s^2 \cdot L = 7.8 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (20)^2 \cdot 1000 \text{ mm units}$$

Let us work in **cm** for consistency:
$$m_s = 7.8 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (2.0)^2 \cdot 100 = 7.8 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 314.16 / 1 \approx 2449 \text{ g}$$

$$m_c = 1.6 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (2.309)^2 \cdot 100 = 1.6 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 5.331 \approx 26.81 \text{ g (per unit length factor cancels)}$$

- **Step 5 — Compute Percentage Mass Saving.**
$$\text{Mass saving} = \frac{m_s - m_c}{m_s} \times 100\% = \frac{2449 - 26.81 \cdot (314.16/314.16)}{2449} \times 100\%$$

Reverting to the simple ratio:
$$\frac{m_c}{m_s} = \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_s} \cdot \frac{d_c^2}{d_s^2} = \frac{1.6}{7.8} \cdot \frac{E_s}{E_c} = 0.2051 \cdot 1.3333 = 0.2735$$

$$\text{Mass saving} = (1 - 0.2735) \times 100\% = 72.65\%$$

- **Step 6 — Engineering Conclusion.** Replacing the steel rod with a CFRP rod of equal stiffness yields a **mass saving of ~72.65 %** — equivalent to removing roughly **1.76 kg from every 2.43 kg of original mass**. This is a powerful illustration of the polymer advantage quantified in KTU Board style.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Material Family Classification

The following Mermaid diagram presents the classification of engineering materials and highlights where polymers fit in the broader engineering landscape.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Materials]:::root
    A --> B[Metals]:::metal
    A --> C[Ceramics and Glasses]:::ceramic
    A --> D[Polymers]:::polymer
    A --> E[Composites]:::comp
    A --> F[Natural Materials]:::nat

    B --> B1[Ferrous - Steel Cast Iron]
    B --> B2[Non-ferrous - Al Cu Ti Mg]

    C --> C1[Traditional - Clay SiC]
    C --> C2[Advanced - Al2O3 ZrO2]

    D --> D1[Thermoplastics]:::tp
    D --> D2[Thermosets]:::ts
    D --> D3[Elastomers]:::el

    D1 --> D1a[PE PP PVC PS PET PMMA]
    D2 --> D2a[Phenolic Epoxy Polyester Urea]
    D3 --> D3a[Natural Rubber SBR Silicone Neoprene]

    E --> E1[FRP - Fibre Reinforced Polymer]
    E --> E2[PMC - Polymer Matrix Composite]
    E --> E3[CMC - Ceramic Matrix Composite]

    F --> F1[Wood Bamboo Leather]
    F --> F2[Cotton Silk Natural Fibres]

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px
    classDef metal fill:#94a3b8,color:#000000,stroke:#475569,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ceramic fill:#fda4af,color:#000000,stroke:#9f1239,stroke-width:2px
    classDef polymer fill:#86efac,color:#000000,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    classDef comp fill:#c4b5fd,color:#000000,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nat fill:#fde68a,color:#000000,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tp fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000000,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef ts fill:#fed7aa,color:#000000,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:1px
    classDef el fill:#fbcfe8,color:#000000,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**
> Polymers occupy the green-shaded branch (D). Within polymers, three sub-families exist — **thermoplastics (D1)**, **thermosets (D2)**, and **elastomers (D3)** — each with its own characteristic examples. **Composites (E)** are a hybrid family that often combine polymers with fibres, hence the relevance to this course.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Advantages vs. Disadvantages Trade-off Map

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **engineering trade-offs** that a Polymer Engineering student must internalise. The arrows indicate causal relationships (e.g., weak intermolecular forces → low melting point → poor heat resistance).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Structural Origin Weak Intermolecular Forces]:::cause
    SP --> A1[Lightweight]:::adv
    SP --> A2[Corrosion Resistance]:::adv
    SP --> A3[Easy Processing]:::adv
    SP --> A4[Electrical Insulation]:::adv
    SP --> A5[Transparency and Colourability]:::adv

    SP --> D1[Poor Heat Resistance]:::dis
    SP --> D2[Low Stiffness vs Metals]:::dis
    SP --> D3[High CTE]:::dis
    SP --> D4[UV and Oxidative Degradation]:::dis

    AP[Organic C–C C–H Backbone]:::cause
    AP --> A6[Low Cost from Abundant Feedstock]:::adv
    AP --> A7[Tunable via Copolymerisation]:::adv
    AP --> D5[Flammability]:::dis
    AP --> D6[Environmental Persistence]:::dis
    AP --> D7[Limited Solvent Resistance]:::dis

    AR[Architecture Long Chain Entanglement]:::cause
    AR --> A8[High Impact Strength in Elastomers]:::adv
    AR --> A8b[Recyclable Thermoplastics]:::adv
    AR --> D8[Stress Cracking in HDPE]:::dis
    AR --> D9[Difficult Joining and Repair]:::dis

    classDef cause fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
    classDef adv fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000000,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef dis fill:#fecaca,color:#000000,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Use This Trade-off Map in a KTU Answer**
> The diagram visually links **structural origins** (yellow nodes) to **consequences** (green for advantages, red for disadvantages). In a 14-mark question, the examiner expects the student to **state the structural origin first, then derive the consequence** — not just list properties in isolation. This structure-oriented answer style typically scores **2 – 3 marks higher** than a flat listing.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Polymer Selection Decision Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart guides a Polymer Engineering student through the **decision logic** for selecting a polymer based on the advantages and disadvantages framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the application load-bearing?}:::q
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Will the part be exposed to T greater than 200 deg C?}:::q
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Does the part need to flex or absorb impact?}:::q

    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Consider thermosets phenolics polyimides or ceramics or metals]:::rec
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is corrosion a major concern?}:::q
    Q4 -- Yes --> R2[Choose fluoropolymers PVDF PTFE or PP]:::rec
    Q4 -- No --> R5[Choose commodity thermoplastics PE PP PVC]:::rec

    Q3 -- Yes --> Q5{Is chemical resistance needed?}:::q
    Q3 -- No --> Q6{Is optical transparency needed?}:::q

    Q5 -- Yes --> R3[Choose elastomer EPDM silicone for chemical resistance]:::rec
    Q5 -- No --> R4[Choose natural rubber SBR polyurethane]:::rec

    Q6 -- Yes --> R6[Choose PMMA PC PS for clarity]:::rec
    Q6 -- No --> R7[Choose commodity thermoplastics based on cost]:::rec

    classDef q fill:#fde68a,color:#000000,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rec fill:#bae6fd,color:#000000,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision-Flow Takeaway**
> Notice how the **advantages** of polymers (lightweight, corrosion resistance, transparency) drive the *Yes* branches, while the **disadvantages** (poor heat resistance, low stiffness) drive the *No* branches. The KTU examiner may set a case-study question where you are asked to walk through this logic for a specific application (e.g., *Choose a polymer for a chemical storage tank*). Practice this flow on three real-world examples before the exam.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any six advantages of polymers over conventional engineering materials such as metals and ceramics.**

**Model Answer (verbatim, KTU-style):**

The six key advantages of polymers over metals and ceramics are:

1. **Low density / Lightweight nature** — densities range from 0.85 to 1.5 g/cm³, compared to 2.7 g/cm³ for aluminium and 7.8 g/cm³ for steel.
2. **Corrosion resistance** — polymers are organic, with no free electrons, so they do not rust or oxidise.
3. **Ease of processing** — thermoplastics can be moulded at low temperatures (150 – 300 °C) and pressures (0.1 – 10 MPa), allowing complex shapes in one step.
4. **Electrical insulation** — electrical resistivity in the range $10^{12}$ to $10^{18}$ Ω·cm.
5. **Chemical resistance** — resistant to most acids, alkalis, and salts at ambient conditions.
6. **Design flexibility and colourability** — can be produced in transparent, translucent, or any colour, with smooth surface finish.

*(½ mark each — total 3 marks)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Mention any six disadvantages or limitations of polymers as engineering materials.**

**Model Answer (verbatim, KTU-style):**

The six major disadvantages of polymers are:

1. **Poor heat resistance** — thermal degradation begins at 200 – 400 °C, well below metal melting points.
2. **Low stiffness** — Young's modulus (0.1 – 5 GPa) is 1 – 2 orders of magnitude lower than metals.
3. **High coefficient of thermal expansion** — 5 – 15 times higher than metals, causing dimensional instability.
4. **Flammability** — most polymers are combustible and release toxic gases (HCl, HCN) on burning.
5. **Environmental persistence** — non-biodegradable; contribute to microplastic pollution lasting centuries.
6. **UV and oxidative degradation** — sunlight and atmospheric oxygen degrade polymer chains, reducing service life unless stabilised.

*(½ mark each — total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **advantages of polymers** over traditional engineering materials (metals, ceramics, wood). For each advantage, briefly state the **molecular or structural reason** that gives rise to it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **steel pipe** of length 5 m, outer diameter 50 mm, and wall thickness 5 mm is to be replaced by an **HDPE pipe** of equal length and equal **burst-pressure capacity**. Calculate the required wall thickness of the HDPE pipe and the percentage mass saving. Use the following data:

| Material | Density (g/cm³) | Tensile Yield Strength (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Steel | 7.8 | 250 |
| HDPE | 0.95 | 25 |

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — Advantages of Polymers

| S. No. | Advantage | Molecular / Structural Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Low density (0.85 – 1.5 g/cm³) | Light C, H, O atoms in backbone; weak van der Waals forces leave free volume. |
| 2 | Corrosion resistance | Organic nature; no free electrons to undergo oxidation. |
| 3 | Ease of processing | Weak secondary bonds allow flow at moderate temperatures (150 – 300 °C). |
| 4 | Electrical insulation | No free charge carriers; electrical resistivity $10^{12}$ – $10^{18}$ Ω·cm. |
| 5 | Low thermal conductivity | Absence of free electrons and phonon scattering in amorphous regions. |
| 6 | Design flexibility and colourability | Amorphous polymers have no crystallites; pigments disperse easily. |
| 7 | Tunable mechanical properties | Control of $M̄$, $\chi_c$, cross-link density, copolymer composition. |
| 8 | Cost-effectiveness | Low processing energy; mass-production cycle times of seconds. |

*(1 mark for each correct pair — total 7 marks; half-mark may be awarded for partial correctness.)*

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — Pipe Mass Saving Calculation

**Step 1 — Burst Pressure of a Thin-Walled Pipe.** The hoop stress in a thin-walled pipe is:
$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

where $p$ is the internal pressure, $r$ is the inner radius, and $t$ is the wall thickness.

**Step 2 — Equate Burst Pressures.** For the steel pipe to have the same burst pressure as the HDPE pipe, the **yield stress divided by the ratio $r/t$** must be equal:
$$\frac{\sigma_{y,steel}}{r_s / t_s} = \frac{\sigma_{y,HDPE}}{r_H / t_H}$$

**Step 3 — Assume Equal Inner Diameter.** We assume both pipes have the same inner diameter $d_i = 50 - 2(5) = 40$ mm (steel) and the same bore is preserved:
$$r_s = 20 \text{ mm (inner radius of steel pipe)}$$
$$r_H = 20 \text{ mm (inner radius of HDPE pipe — same bore)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for HDPE Wall Thickness.**
$$\frac{250}{20 / 5} = \frac{25}{20 / t_H}$$

$$\frac{250}{4} = \frac{25 \cdot t_H}{20}$$

$$62.5 = 1.25 \cdot t_H$$

$$t_H = \frac{62.5}{1.25} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Wait — this is impractical for a pipe!** A 50 mm wall on a 40 mm bore is a solid rod, not a pipe. This means HDPE cannot match the steel pipe's burst pressure at the **same bore**. The correct engineering interpretation is: **HDPE is unsuitable for high-pressure applications**, and the engineer should select a different polymer (e.g., reinforced thermoplastic, or simply accept a lower pressure rating). In a KTU answer, this is the **engineering insight worth 1 mark**.

**Step 5 — Recalculate Assuming Equal Outer Diameter.** Suppose the HDPE pipe has the **same outer diameter 50 mm** as the steel pipe. Then:
$$r_{H,outer} = 25 \text{ mm}$$

For the same burst pressure, $\sigma_{y,steel} \cdot t_s / r_s = \sigma_{y,HDPE} \cdot t_H / r_H$:
$$250 \cdot \frac{5}{20} = 25 \cdot \frac{t_H}{22.5}$$
$$62.5 = 1.111 \cdot t_H$$
$$t_H = 56.25 \text{ mm (again impractical)}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Conclusion.** HDPE has a tensile strength ~10 times lower than steel. To match the burst pressure, the wall thickness must be 10 times greater, which negates the lightweight advantage. The **correct engineering recommendation** is to use HDPE only for **low-pressure applications** (water distribution, drainage) and to use **reinforced thermoplastic pipes (RTP)** or **metals** for high-pressure service.

**Step 7 — Mass Comparison at Equal Bore (if the question forces a numerical answer).** The volume of material per unit length is $\pi (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$. For the steel pipe: $\pi (25^2 - 20^2) = \pi (625 - 400) = 225\pi$ mm². For an HDPE pipe with 50 mm wall: $\pi (70^2 - 20^2) = \pi (4900 - 400) = 4500\pi$ mm².
- Mass per unit length: steel = $7.8 \cdot 225\pi$ = 5519 g/m; HDPE = $0.95 \cdot 4500\pi$ = 13,428 g/m. **HDPE is heavier in this unrealistic case.**
- This confirms the engineering conclusion: **the polymer advantage disappears when the design constraint is high strength**.

*(7 marks — awarded for: 2 marks for correct hoop-stress formula, 2 marks for correct substitution and calculation, 2 marks for engineering interpretation, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **disadvantages and limitations** of polymers, and explain the **molecular reasons** behind each limitation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **suitable performance index**, justify the **replacement of an aluminium component** with a **carbon-fibre-reinforced polymer (CFRP)** component for a stiffness-critical application. Use the following data and assume both components have the **same geometry**:

| Material | Young's Modulus, E (GPa) | Density, ρ (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|
| Aluminium 7075-T6 | 72 | 2.81 |
| CFRP (unidirectional) | 135 | 1.55 |

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — Disadvantages of Polymers

| S. No. | Disadvantage | Molecular / Structural Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Poor heat resistance | C–C and C–H bonds in backbone break at 200 – 400 °C; metals and ceramics have much stronger bonds. |
| 2 | Low stiffness | Weak secondary forces between chains give modulus of 0.1 – 5 GPa vs 70 – 200 GPa for metals. |
| 3 | High coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) | Large-amplitude molecular motions with temperature; weak secondary bonds expand easily. |
| 4 | Flammability | Organic nature; combustion releases CO₂, H₂O, and toxic gases (HCl from PVC, HCN from PU). |
| 5 | Environmental persistence | Long-chain molecules are not attacked by most microorganisms; degradation takes centuries. |
| 6 | UV and oxidative degradation | UV photons break C–C bonds; oxygen reacts with free radicals generated during processing. |
| 7 | Difficulty in joining/repairing | Thermosets cannot be remelted; thermoplastics need specialised welding techniques. |
| 8 | Solvent and stress-cracking sensitivity | Amorphous polymers dissolve in solvents with similar solubility parameter δ. |

*(1 mark for each correct pair — total 7 marks; half-mark may be awarded for partial correctness.)*

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — Performance-Index Justification

**Step 1 — State the Design Objective.** The component is **stiffness-critical**, meaning it must resist elastic deformation under load. The objective is to **minimise mass while maintaining stiffness**.

**Step 2 — Derive the Specific Stiffness Performance Index.** As derived in Section 3.1.1, for equal stiffness at equal geometry, the mass scales as:
$$m \propto \frac{\rho}{E}$$

Therefore, the **specific stiffness** is the reciprocal:
$$P_{stiff} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Compute for Aluminium.**
$$P_{Al} = \frac{72 \text{ GPa}}{2.81 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 25.62 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute for CFRP.**
$$P_{CFRP} = \frac{135}{1.55} = 87.10 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Improvement Ratio.**
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{P_{CFRP}}{P_{Al}} = \frac{87.10}{25.62} = 3.40$$

**Step 6 — Mass Saving at Equal Stiffness.** If we redesign the component to match the **stiffness of the aluminium part** with CFRP, the mass saving is:
$$\text{Mass saving} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{3.40}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.294) \times 100\% = 70.6\%$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Conclusion.** Replacing aluminium with CFRP in a stiffness-critical application yields a **~70.6 % mass saving** for the same stiffness, which is a decisive advantage in aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350), high-performance automotive (Formula 1 monocoques), and sports equipment (bicycle frames, tennis rackets). The **cost penalty** (CFRP is ~10 – 20× costlier per kg than aluminium) is offset by the mass saving, which reduces fuel consumption and emissions over the component's lifetime.

*(7 marks — awarded for: 2 marks for derivation of E/ρ, 2 marks for numerical computation with units, 2 marks for ratio and mass saving, 1 mark for engineering conclusion with real-world example.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark per occurrence):** Writing $E$ in GPa and $\rho$ in kg/m³ in the same ratio without conversion. Always keep units consistent: either (GPa, g/cm³) or (Pa, kg/m³), but not mixed.
> 2. **Forgetting to Justify the Choice of Performance Index (–1 mark):** A 7-mark sub-question on justification must begin with the **explicit statement** that the design is "stiffness-critical" (or "strength-critical"). Skipping this step costs a mark.
> 3. **Confusing Density ρ with Electrical Resistivity $\rho_e$ (–1 mark):** Use the symbol $\rho_m$ (mass density) or $\rho$ with the unit **g/cm³** to disambiguate. KTU examiners are strict on this.
> 4. **Listing Without Explaining (–2 to –3 marks):** A common mistake is writing *"Polymers are lightweight and corrosion-resistant"* without explaining **why** (molecular origin). The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises the **why** — students who skip the molecular reasoning lose 30 – 40 % of the marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the Cost Factor in Polymer Substitution (–1 mark):** A complete engineering answer must mention that **mass saving alone does not justify polymer substitution**; the **life-cycle cost** (including manufacturing, maintenance, and end-of-life disposal) must also be considered.
> 6. **Conflating "Plastic" with "Polymer" (–½ mark):** Not all polymers are plastics (e.g., natural rubber is an elastomer; Kevlar is a high-performance polymer fibre). Use the precise term **"polymer"** unless specifically referring to a plastic formulation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorise Before Walking into the KTU Exam Hall**

- **Definition to Memorise:** A polymer is a high-molecular-weight compound made of many repeating **monomer** units joined by **covalent bonds** through **polymerisation**.

- **Three Polymer Families (with behaviour on heating):**
  - **Thermoplastics** — soften on heating, harden on cooling (reversible). Examples: PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET, PMMA.
  - **Thermosets** — harden permanently on heating (irreversible). Examples: Phenolic, Epoxy, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine.
  - **Elastomers** — exhibit high elastic recovery. Examples: Natural rubber, SBR, Neoprene, Silicone.

- **Top 8 Advantages to Memorise (in order of KTU importance):**
  1. Low density (0.85 – 1.5 g/cm³) — lightweight.
  2. Corrosion resistance — no free electrons, no rust.
  3. Ease of processing — low T (150 – 300 °C), low P (0.1 – 10 MPa).
  4. Electrical insulation — resistivity $10^{12}$ – $10^{18}$ Ω·cm.
  5. Chemical resistance — inert to most acids, alkalis, salts.
  6. Design flexibility — complex shapes, transparent, colourable.
  7. Tunable properties — control $M̄$, $\chi_c$, cross-link density.
  8. Cost-effectiveness — low energy to process.

- **Top 6 Disadvantages to Memorise (in order of KTU importance):**
  1. Poor heat resistance — degrades at 200 – 400 °C.
  2. Low stiffness — E = 0.1 – 5 GPa vs 70 – 200 GPa for metals.
  3. High CTE — 50 – 200 × 10⁻⁶ /°C vs 10 – 25 × 10⁻⁶ /°C for metals.
  4. Flammability — releases toxic gases (HCl, HCN, CO).
  5. Environmental persistence — non-biodegradable; microplastics.
  6. UV and oxidative degradation — requires stabilisers (HALS, antioxidants).

- **Two Performance Indices to Derive On-Demand:**
  - **Stiffness-limited design:** $P_{stiff} = E / \rho$ (specific stiffness).
  - **Strength-limited design:** $P_{str} = \sigma_y / \rho$ (specific strength).

- **Typical Density Values to Quote (with units):**
  - Polymers: 0.85 – 1.5 g/cm³
  - Aluminium: 2.7 g/cm³
  - Steel: 7.8 g/cm³
  - CFRP: 1.55 g/cm³
  - Kevlar (Aramid): 1.44 g/cm³

- **Typical Young's Modulus Values to Quote (with units):**
  - Polyethylene: 0.2 – 1 GPa
  - PVC: 2.5 – 3 GPa
  - Nylon 6,6: 2 – 4 GPa
  - CFRP (unidirectional): 135 GPa
  - Aluminium: 70 GPa
  - Steel: 200 GPa

- **Typical Electrical Resistivity Values to Quote:**
  - Polymers: $10^{12}$ – $10^{18}$ Ω·cm
  - Metals: $10^{-8}$ – $10^{-6}$ Ω·cm
  - Difference: ~20 orders of magnitude — this is the basis for polymer use as **insulators**.

- **Three Real-World Examples to Quote (for full-mark engineering applications):**
  1. **Boeing 787 Dreamliner** — 50 % composite material by weight; **20 % fuel saving** vs the aluminium 767.
  2. **HDPE pipes** — replacing cast-iron water mains; **100-year service life** with no corrosion.
  3. **Kevlar aramid fibre** — used in bulletproof vests; **specific strength ~60× that of mild steel**.

- **One Sentence to Impress the Examiner:** *"Polymers offer a compelling combination of low density, corrosion resistance, and design flexibility, but their low stiffness, poor heat resistance, and environmental persistence demand careful material selection guided by performance indices such as $E/\rho$ and life-cycle cost analysis."* — This single sentence, if written as the opening of a 14-mark answer, immediately signals **conceptual depth** and typically scores **½ – 1 bonus mark** on impression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Polymers and Structure of Monomer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Polymers and Structure of Monomer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Polymer?

A **polymer** is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of small repeating structural units called **monomers**, joined together by covalent bonds through the process of **polymerization**. The term is derived from the Greek words *poly* (meaning "many") and *meros* (meaning "parts").

$$ \text{Monomer} \xrightarrow{\text{Polymerization}} \text{Polymer} $$

A typical polymer chain may contain anywhere from a few hundred to several million repeating units, resulting in molecular weights ranging from **10⁴ to 10⁷ g/mol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** According to the KTU PCPOT205 syllabus, a polymer is defined as a substance composed of macromolecules characterized by the multiple repetition of one or more species of atoms or groups of atoms (constitutional repeating units). The IUPAC preferred term is **macromolecule**.

## 1.2 What is a Monomer?

A **monomer** is the smallest repeating chemical unit that reacts with other similar or dissimilar molecules to form a polymer. Monomers are characterized by their **functionality** — the number of reactive sites (usually double bonds or functional groups) available for chain propagation.

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Monomer** | Single small molecule | Ethylene (CH₂=CH₂) |
| **Repeat Unit** | Structural unit that repeats in the chain | –CH₂–CH₂– |
| **Mer** | Synonym for repeat unit | –CH₂–CH₂– |
| **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** | Number of repeat units | 1000 (for LDPE) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Train & Bogies):** Imagine a polymer as a long freight train. Each **bogie (wagon)** is a *monomer*. The *chain of bogies* connected by couplings is the *polymer chain*. If you join hundreds of identical bogies, you get a *homopolymer* (like a goods train with same wagons). If you alternate different types of bogies (e.g., passenger + goods), you get a *copolymer* (like a mixed train). The *length of the train* is analogous to the molecular weight of the polymer.

## 1.3 Need for Classification

Polymers exhibit an enormous diversity of properties — from rigid solids to flexible elastomers, from water-soluble to chemically inert. With thousands of polymers in industrial use, a systematic **classification** is essential for:
- **Selection** based on end-use application
- **Predicting** physical and mechanical behavior
- **Designing** new polymeric materials
- **KTU Board Perspective:** Direct 7–14 mark questions on classification appear almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Polymers — Comprehensive Framework

Polymers can be classified based on **seven primary criteria**, each of which is a high-yield KTU topic.

---

### 2.1.1 Based on Origin

| Type | Definition | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Natural Polymers** | Found in nature (plants/animals) | Cellulose, Starch, Proteins, Natural Rubber, Silk, Wool, DNA |
| **Semi-Synthetic Polymers** | Chemically modified natural polymers | Cellulose Acetate (CA), Nitrocellulose, Vulcanized Rubber, Rayon |
| **Synthetic Polymers** | Entirely man-made from petrochemicals | Polyethylene (PE), PVC, Nylon-6,6, Bakelite, Teflon |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Vulcanized rubber (sulfur-crosslinked natural rubber) is classified as **semi-synthetic** because natural rubber is the starting material and sulfur is added to crosslink it. This is a frequently asked distinction question.

---

### 2.1.2 Based on Polymerization Mechanism (Reaction Type)

This is the **most important classification for KTU**.

**(a) Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymers:**
- Formed by repeated addition of monomers containing C=C double bonds
- **No by-product** is eliminated
- Initiated by free radicals, ions, or coordination catalysts
- Examples: Polyethylene, Polypropylene, PVC, Polystyrene, PTFE

$$ n \text{CH}_2 {=} \text{CHX} \xrightarrow{\text{Initiator}} (\text{--CH}_2\text{--CHX--})_n $$

**(b) Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymers:**
- Formed by condensation reactions between bifunctional monomers
- **Small molecules (H₂O, HCl, NH₃)** are eliminated as by-products
- Each monomer must have at least two reactive functional groups
- Examples: Nylon-6,6, PET, Bakelite, Polyester

$$ n \text{H}_2\text{N--R--NH}_2 + n \text{HOOC--R'--COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H--[NH--R--NH--CO--R'--CO]}_n \text{--OH} + (2n-1)\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

---

### 2.1.3 Based on Structure (Chain Architecture)

| Structure Type | Description | Property | Example |
|---------------|-------------|----------|---------|
| **Linear Polymer** | Long, straight chains with no branches | High density, high tensile strength, high melting point | HDPE, Nylon, PVC |
| **Branched Polymer** | Main chain with side branches | Lower density, lower tensile strength | LDPE |
| **Cross-Linked Polymer** | Covalent bonds between chains | Rigid, hard, infusible | Vulcanized rubber, Bakelite |
| **Network Polymer** | Highly cross-linked 3D structure | Hard, brittle, thermosetting | Epoxy, Phenol-formaldehyde |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy (Branching Intuition):** A *linear polymer* is like a row of soldiers standing shoulder-to-shoulder. A *branched polymer* is like a tree with secondary branches coming off the main trunk. A *cross-linked* structure is like a fishing net where horizontal ropes are tied to vertical ropes. A *network polymer* is like a dense 3D scaffold with every strand interlocked.

---

### 2.1.4 Based on Thermal Response

| Type | Behavior on Heating | Remoldable? | Examples |
|------|---------------------|-------------|----------|
| **Thermoplastic** | Softens on heating, hardens on cooling (reversible) | Yes | PE, PP, PS, PVC, Nylon |
| **Thermosetting** | Sets permanently on heating; cannot be remelted | No | Bakelite, Melamine, Epoxy, Urea-formaldehyde |
| **Elastomers** | High elastic recovery; cross-linked but lightly | No (but reversible elasticity) | Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone rubber |

---

### 2.1.5 Based on Tacticity (Stereoregularity)

Tacticity refers to the spatial arrangement of side groups along the polymer backbone.

- **Isotactic:** All side groups on the **same side** of the backbone → Highly crystalline, high melting point. Example: Isotactic Polypropylene (iPP) — used in car bumpers.
- **Syndiotactic:** Side groups alternate in a **regular alternating pattern** → Also crystalline. Example: Syndiotactic Polystyrene.
- **Atactic:** Side groups arranged **randomly** → Amorphous, soft. Example: Atactic Polypropylene (aPP) — used as adhesive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Ziegler-Natta catalysts (and metallocenes) are used to control stereoregularity during polymerization. This directly determines whether the resulting polymer is **isotactic, syndiotactic, or atactic**.

---

### 2.1.6 Based on Backbone Composition

| Type | Backbone Composition | Example |
|------|---------------------|---------|
| **Homochain Polymer** | All backbone atoms are carbon | Polyethylene, PVC |
| **Heterochain Polymer** | Backbone contains C, O, N, Si, etc. | Nylon (C–N), PET (C–O), Silicones (Si–O) |

---

### 2.1.7 Based on Polarity

- **Polar Polymers:** Have permanent dipole moments → High dielectric constant, used in capacitors (PVC, PVDF, Nylon)
- **Non-polar Polymers:** No net dipole → Excellent electrical insulators (PE, PTFE, PS)

---

## 2.2 Structure of the Monomer — Functionality Concept

The **functionality (f)** of a monomer is the number of bonds it can form with other monomers.

| Functionality | Type | Behavior |
|--------------|------|----------|
| **f = 1** | Monofunctional | Terminates chain growth (chain stopper) |
| **f = 2** | Bifunctional | Produces **linear** polymers |
| **f ≥ 3** | Trifunctional/Tetrafunctional | Produces **branched or cross-linked/network** polymers |

### Common Monomer Functional Groups

| Monomer Class | Example | Reactive Group |
|--------------|---------|----------------|
| **Vinyl monomers** | CH₂=CHX (X = H, Cl, CH₃, C₆H₅) | C=C double bond |
| **Dienes** | CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂ (Butadiene) | Two conjugated C=C bonds |
| **Diols** | HO–(CH₂)₂–OH (Ethylene glycol) | –OH groups |
| **Diacids** | HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH (Adipic acid) | –COOH groups |
| **Diamines** | H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂ (Hexamethylenediamine) | –NH₂ groups |
| **Amino acids** | H₂N–R–COOH | –NH₂ and –COOH |
| **Lactams** | Caprolactam (cyclic) | Ring opening |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are mandatory for any numerical question on this topic in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------|
| $DP$ (Degree of Polymerization) | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | Number of repeat units per chain |
| $\overline{M}_n$ (Number-average MW) | $\overline{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Statistical average based on number of molecules |
| $\overline{M}_w$ (Weight-average MW) | $\overline{M}_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Statistical average based on weight contribution |
| **PDI** (Polydispersity Index) | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Measure of molecular weight distribution width |
| $w_i$ (Weight fraction) | $w_i = \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Fraction of total weight from species *i* |
| $x_i$ (Mole fraction) | $x_i = \dfrac{N_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Fraction of total molecules from species *i* |

Where:
- $N_i$ = Number of molecules with molecular weight $M_i$
- $M_0$ = Molecular weight of the repeat unit
- For a monodisperse polymer: $PDI = 1$; for commercial polymers: $PDI \geq 2$

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_n$)

Consider a polymer sample consisting of $N$ total molecules divided into $k$ different molecular weight species. Let:
- Species 1: $N_1$ molecules, each of mass $M_1$
- Species 2: $N_2$ molecules, each of mass $M_2$
- Species 3: $N_3$ molecules, each of mass $M_3$
- ... and so on, up to $N_k$ molecules of mass $M_k$.

**Step 1: Total mass of all molecules in the sample**

$$ m_{total} = N_1 M_1 + N_2 M_2 + N_3 M_3 + \cdots + N_k M_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i $$

**Step 2: Total number of molecules in the sample**

$$ N_{total} = N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + \cdots + N_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i $$

**Step 3: By definition, the number-average molecular weight is the total mass divided by the total number of molecules**

$$ \boxed{\overline{M}_n = \frac{m_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i}} $$

This is essentially a **mole-fraction-weighted arithmetic mean** of the molecular weights.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Weight-Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M}_w$)

The weight-average molecular weight is a more complex statistic that weights each molecular weight $M_i$ by the *total mass* of species $i$ in the sample (rather than by number).

**Step 1: Fraction of total mass contributed by species $i$**

$$ w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i} $$

This is the weight fraction of species $i$. By definition, $\sum w_i = 1$.

**Step 2: Weight-average is a weighted mean where each $M_i$ is weighted by $w_i$**

$$ \overline{M}_w = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i M_i $$

**Step 3: Substitute $w_i$ expression**

$$ \overline{M}_w = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i} \right) M_i $$

**Step 4: Since the denominator is common (independent of $i$), it can be factored out**

$$ \boxed{\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}} $$

**Key Insight:** Since $M_i^2$ appears in the numerator, **$\overline{M}_w$ is more sensitive to high-molecular-weight species** than $\overline{M}_n$. This is why $\overline{M}_w \geq \overline{M}_n$ always.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Typical Style)

> **Problem:** A polymer sample contains the following molecular species:
> - 30 molecules of $M_1 = 10{,}000$ g/mol
> - 50 molecules of $M_2 = 20{,}000$ g/mol
> - 20 molecules of $M_3 = 50{,}000$ g/mol
>
> Calculate (a) $\overline{M}_n$, (b) $\overline{M}_w$, and (c) PDI.

### Solution

**Step 1: Tabulate the data**

| Species $i$ | $N_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^2$ |
|-------------|-------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | 30 | 10,000 | $30 \times 10^4 = 3.0 \times 10^5$ | $30 \times (10^4)^2 = 3.0 \times 10^9$ |
| 2 | 50 | 20,000 | $50 \times 2 \times 10^4 = 1.0 \times 10^6$ | $50 \times (2 \times 10^4)^2 = 2.0 \times 10^{10}$ |
| 3 | 20 | 50,000 | $20 \times 5 \times 10^4 = 1.0 \times 10^6$ | $20 \times (5 \times 10^4)^2 = 5.0 \times 10^{10}$ |
| **Sum** | $\sum N_i = 100$ | — | $\sum N_i M_i = 2.3 \times 10^6$ | $\sum N_i M_i^2 = 7.3 \times 10^{10}$ |

**Step 2: Calculate $\overline{M}_n$**

$$ \overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^6}{100} = 2.3 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol} $$

$$ \overline{M}_n = 23{,}000 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 3: Calculate $\overline{M}_w$**

$$ \overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{7.3 \times 10^{10}}{2.3 \times 10^6} = 3.174 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol} $$

$$ \overline{M}_w = 31{,}739 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 4: Calculate PDI**

$$ PDI = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \frac{31{,}739}{23{,}000} = 1.38 $$

**Step 5: Interpretation for KTU answer writing**

> Since $PDI = 1.38 > 1$, the polymer sample is **polydisperse** (i.e., it has a distribution of molecular weights rather than a single value). A monodisperse polymer would have $PDI = 1$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Degree of Polymerization

> **Problem:** Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC) has a number-average molecular weight of $\overline{M}_n = 62{,}500$ g/mol. The repeat unit is –CH₂–CHCl– with $M_0 = 62.5$ g/mol. Calculate the degree of polymerization.

**Solution:**

$$ DP = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \frac{62{,}500 \text{ g/mol}}{62.5 \text{ g/mol}} = 1000 $$

$$ \boxed{DP = 1000 \text{ repeat units per chain on average}} $$
