# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between addition and condensation polymerization with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Addition Polymerization | Condensation Polymerization |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| **By-product** | No by-product eliminated | Small molecule (H₂O, HCl, NH₃) eliminated |
| **Monomer type** | Vinyl monomers with C=C bond | Bifunctional monomers (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂) |
| **Mechanism** | Chain-growth (radical/ionic/coordination) | Step-growth |
| **Initiation** | Requires initiator or catalyst | May or may not require catalyst |
| **Examples** | (i) Polyethylene from ethylene (ii) PVC from vinyl chloride | (i) Nylon-6,6 from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid (ii) PET from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid |
| **Repeat unit** | Same as monomer composition | Different from monomers (atoms lost in by-product) |

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the following terms with examples: (i) Thermoplastic (ii) Thermosetting (iii) Elastomer.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Thermoplastic:** Polymers that **soften on heating and harden on cooling**, and this process is **reversible and repeatable**. They have linear or branched structures with weak intermolecular forces. Examples: Polyethylene (PE), Polystyrene (PS), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Nylon.

**(ii) Thermosetting:** Polymers that undergo **irreversible cross-linking** upon heating, forming a hard, rigid, three-dimensional network. Once set, they **cannot be remelted or remolded**. Examples: Bakelite (phenol-formaldehyde), Melamine, Epoxy resin, Urea-formaldehyde.

**(iii) Elastomer:** Polymers that exhibit **large elastic recovery** when deformed — they can be stretched to several times their original length and return to shape. They are lightly cross-linked. Examples: Natural rubber (polyisoprene), Neoprene, Silicone rubber.

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either Question A OR Question B**.

---

### **Question A** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**A.** (a) Classify polymers based on (i) structure, (ii) thermal behavior, and (iii) tacticity. Give suitable examples for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A polymer sample has the following composition:**
- 30% by weight of molecules with MW = 20,000 g/mol
- 50% by weight of molecules with MW = 50,000 g/mol
- 20% by weight of molecules with MW = 100,000 g/mol

**Calculate the number-average and weight-average molecular weights, and the PDI.** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for A(a):**

**(i) Classification based on Structure:**

1. **Linear polymers** – long straight chains with no branches. Chains pack closely → high crystallinity, high density, high tensile strength. Example: **HDPE**, Nylon-6,6.

2. **Branched chain polymers** – main chain with side branches. Packing is irregular → lower density, lower crystallinity. Example: **LDPE**.

3. **Cross-linked polymers** – covalent bonds formed between adjacent chains (often by sulfur in rubber vulcanization). Improves elasticity and strength. Example: **Vulcanized natural rubber**.

4. **Network polymers** – extensive cross-linking in three dimensions. Hard, rigid, infusible. Example: **Bakelite**.

**(ii) Classification based on Thermal Behavior:**

1. **Thermoplastic** – Soften on heating, harden on cooling. Remoldable. Linear/branched structure. Ex: **PE, PP, PS, PVC, Nylon**.
2. **Thermosetting** – Undergo permanent cross-linking on heating. Cannot be remelted. Ex: **Bakelite, Melamine, Epoxy**.
3. **Elastomers** – Highly elastic, lightly cross-linked. Ex: **Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone**.

**(iii) Classification based on Tacticity:**

1. **Isotactic** – All side groups on the same side of the backbone → Crystalline → High melting point. Ex: **Isotactic polypropylene** (iPP).
2. **Syndiotactic** – Side groups alternate regularly → Crystalline. Ex: **Syndiotactic polystyrene** (sPS).
3. **Atactic** – Side groups randomly arranged → Amorphous, soft, low melting point. Ex: **Atactic polypropylene** (aPP).

#### **Model Solution for A(b):**

**Step 1:** Given weight fractions directly: $w_1 = 0.30$, $w_2 = 0.50$, $w_3 = 0.20$ with $M_1 = 20{,}000$, $M_2 = 50{,}000$, $M_3 = 100{,}000$.

**Step 2: Calculate $\overline{M}_w$ (this is simply the weight-fraction-weighted mean)**

$$ \overline{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i = (0.30)(20{,}000) + (0.50)(50{,}000) + (0.20)(100{,}000) $$

$$ \overline{M}_w = 6{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 51{,}000 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 3: Find $N_i$ ratios.** Since $w_i = N_i M_i / \sum N_i M_i$, then $N_i \propto w_i / M_i$.

$$ N_1 : N_2 : N_3 = \frac{w_1}{M_1} : \frac{w_2}{M_2} : \frac{w_3}{M_3} = \frac{0.30}{20{,}000} : \frac{0.50}{50{,}000} : \frac{0.20}{100{,}000} $$

Normalize (multiply by 100,000):

$$ N_1 : N_2 : N_3 = 1.5 : 1.0 : 0.2 $$

$$ \text{Or equivalently, take } N_1 = 15,\ N_2 = 10,\ N_3 = 2 $$

**Step 4: Compute $\overline{M}_n$**

| Species | $N_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i M_i$ |
|---------|-------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | 15 | 20,000 | 300,000 |
| 2 | 10 | 50,000 | 500,000 |
| 3 | 2 | 100,000 | 200,000 |
| **Sum** | **27** | — | **1,000,000** |

$$ \overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{27} = 37{,}037 \text{ g/mol} $$

**Step 5: Calculate PDI**

$$ PDI = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} = \frac{51{,}000}{37{,}037} = 1.377 $$

**Final Answer:** $\overline{M}_w = 51{,}000$ g/mol, $\overline{M}_n = 37{,}037$ g/mol, $PDI = 1.377$. Since $PDI > 1$, the polymer is polydisperse.

---

### **Question B** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**B.** (a) Explain with neat diagrams the concept of **tacticity** in polymers. How does it affect polymer properties? Discuss the role of **Ziegler–Natta catalysts** in controlling stereoregularity. **(7 marks)**

**(b) What is **functionality** of a monomer? Explain how functionality determines polymer structure. Discuss how the functionality of monomers influences the resulting polymer architecture with examples. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for B(a):**

**Step 1: Definition of Tacticity**
Tacticity (or stereoregularity) refers to the **spatial arrangement of side groups (substituents) along the polymer backbone**. It is a stereochemical property of vinyl polymers of the form –[CH₂–CHX]ₙ–.

**Step 2: Three Types of Tacticity**

**Isotactic Polymer:** All X groups are arranged on the **same side** of the polymer backbone. This regular arrangement allows chains to pack closely → **highly crystalline**, high melting point, high tensile strength. Example: Isotactic polypropylene (iPP) melts at ~165 °C.

**Syndiotactic Polymer:** X groups **alternate regularly** on opposite sides of the backbone. Also crystalline but with different packing. Example: Syndiotactic polystyrene.

**Atactic Polymer:** X groups are arranged **randomly** on both sides. Cannot crystallize → **amorphous, soft, low melting point, soluble**. Example: Atactic polypropylene (aPP) is rubbery at room temperature.

**Step 3: Effect on Properties**

| Property | Isotactic | Syndiotactic | Atactic |
|----------|-----------|--------------|---------|
| Crystallinity | High | Moderate-High | Low (amorphous) |
| Density | High | Moderate | Low |
| Melting Point | High | High | Low |
| Mechanical Strength | High | High | Low |
| Solubility | Low | Low | High |
| Optical Clarity | Opaque | Opaque | Transparent |

**Step 4: Role of Ziegler–Natta Catalysts**
Ziegler-Natta catalysts (e.g., **TiCl₃ + Al(C₂H₅)₃** or **MgCl₂-supported TiCl₄**) are **stereospecific coordination catalysts** used during polymerization to control the orientation of each monomer as it adds to the growing chain. They force each new monomer unit to add in a specific stereochemical orientation, producing **isotactic** polymers from propylene. Karl Ziegler and Giulio Natta received the 1963 Nobel Prize for this discovery. Modern metallocene catalysts offer even better stereocontrol.

#### **Model Solution for B(b):**

**Step 1: Definition of Functionality**
The **functionality (f)** of a monomer is defined as the **number of reactive sites** (functional groups or double bonds) per monomer molecule that can participate in the polymerization reaction to form covalent bonds with other monomers.

**Step 2: Classification Based on Functionality**

- **Monofunctional monomers (f = 1):** Have only one reactive group. They **terminate chain growth** and cannot form polymers on their own. Example: Monochloroacetic acid (ClCH₂COOH), alcohols.
- **Bifunctional monomers (f = 2):** Have two reactive groups. They form **linear polymers** with no cross-linking. Example: Adipic acid (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH), Ethylene glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH), Ethylene diamine.
- **Trifunctional monomers (f = 3):** Have three reactive groups. They produce **branched or cross-linked (network) polymers**. Example: Glycerol (HOCH₂CH(OH)CH₂OH), Phenol (used with formaldehyde).

**Step 3: Relation Between Functionality and Structure**

For a polymer network to form (cross-linked), the **average functionality of the monomer mixture must be ≥ 2** for branching, and the system as a whole must follow the **Carothers' equation**:

$$ DP_n = \frac{2}{2 - \overline{f}} $$

Where $\overline{f}$ is the average functionality of the monomer mixture.

- If $\overline{f} = 2$: $DP_n = \infty$ (very long linear chains, no cross-linking — gelation does not occur).
- If $\overline{f} > 2$: $DP_n$ becomes finite again, and 3D network (gel) forms.
- If $\overline{f} < 2$: Polymerization does not occur (only small oligomers).

**Step 4: Examples of Architecture Determination**

- **Nylon-6,6:** Hexamethylenediamine (f = 2) + Adipic acid (f = 2) → Both f = 2 → **Linear polymer**.
- **Bakelite:** Phenol (f = 3, ortho and para positions are reactive) + Formaldehyde (f = 2) → Cross-linked **network polymer**.
- **Glyptal (alkyd resin):** Glycerol (f = 3) + Phthalic anhydride (f = 2) → **Cross-linked polyester** with 3D network.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose 2–5 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing monomer vs repeat unit:** In condensation polymers, the repeat unit is **NOT identical** to the monomer because by-product atoms are lost. Example: PET repeat unit is –O–CH₂CH₂–O–CO–C₆H₄–CO–, derived from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid, not from any single monomer.
>
> 2. **Writing "synthetic rubber" instead of "synthetic elastomer":** While often used interchangeably, the technically correct KTU board term is **synthetic elastomer** (e.g., Neoprene, SBR).
>
> 3. **Ignoring units in $\overline{M}_n$ / $\overline{M}_w$ calculations:** Always write units (g/mol). Missing units = -1 mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Stating "vulcanized rubber is a synthetic polymer":** WRONG. Vulcanized rubber is **semi-synthetic** because natural rubber is the starting raw material.
>
> 5. **Mixing up isotactic with syndiotactic diagrams:** The most common drawing error. Isotactic = all substituents on one side. Syndiotactic = strictly alternating.
>
> 6. **In tacticity question, forgetting to mention Ziegler-Natta catalyst:** Without discussing the catalyst, the answer is incomplete — you will lose 2 marks.
>
> 7. **PDI computation sign error:** Forgetting to square $M_i$ in the numerator of $\overline{M}_w$ is the most common arithmetic mistake.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Polymer** = Macromolecule made of many repeating units; $\overline{M}_n$ ranges $10^4$–$10^7$ g/mol.
- [x] **Monomer** = Small reactive building block; characterized by **functionality (f)**.
- [x] **Repeat Unit (Mer)** = Structural unit repeated along the chain; obtained by joining monomers.
- [x] **Degree of Polymerization (DP)** = Number of repeat units per chain = $\overline{M}_n / M_0$.
- [x] **Natural polymers** = Found in nature (cellulose, starch, rubber, proteins, DNA).
- [x] **Semi-synthetic** = Chemically modified natural polymers (cellulose acetate, vulcanized rubber).
- [x] **Synthetic** = Entirely man-made (PE, PVC, nylon, PET, bakelite).
- [x] **Addition (Chain-growth) polymerization** = No by-product; vinyl monomers; radical/ionic/coordination mechanism.
- [x] **Condensation (Step-growth) polymerization** = Small molecule by-product; bifunctional monomers.
- [x] **Linear polymer** = Straight chains, high density, crystalline (HDPE, Nylon).
- [x] **Branched polymer** = Side branches; lower density (LDPE).
- [x] **Cross-linked** = Inter-chain covalent bonds; elastic (vulcanized rubber).
- [x] **Network polymer** = 3D interlocked network; hard and infusible (Bakelite).
- [x] **Thermoplastic** = Remoldable on heating (PE, PP, PVC).
- [x] **Thermosetting** = Permanent set; cannot be remelted (Bakelite, Epoxy).
- [x] **Elastomer** = High elastic recovery; lightly cross-linked (Natural rubber, Neoprene).
- [x] **Isotactic** = All substituents same side → crystalline, high MP (iPP).
- [x] **Syndiotactic** = Alternating substituents → crystalline.
- [x] **Atactic** = Random substituents → amorphous (aPP).
- [x] **Homochain** = All-carbon backbone (PE, PP).
- [x] **Heterochain** = Backbone has O, N, Si (Nylon, PET, Silicone).
- [x] **Polar polymer** = Permanent dipoles; used in capacitors (PVC, PVDF).
- [x] **Non-polar polymer** = Excellent insulators (PE, PTFE, PS).
- [x] **$\overline{M}_n$ formula** = $\sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ (number-weighted).
- [x] **$\overline{M}_w$ formula** = $\sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ (weight-weighted).
- [x] **PDI** = $\overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n \geq 1$; equal to 1 only for monodisperse.
- [x] **Ziegler–Natta catalyst** controls stereoregularity → produces isotactic polymers.
- [x] **Carothers' equation** $DP_n = 2/(2-\overline{f})$ relates functionality to chain length and network formation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeat Unit and Polymer of the Following: Polyethylene
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Repeat Unit and Polymer of the Following: Polyethylene

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Polymer and Repeat Unit

A **polymer** is a high-molecular-weight macromolecule composed of a large number of small repeating structural units (called **mers**) linked together through covalent chemical bonds. The term *polymer* originates from the Greek words *poly* (meaning "many") and *meros* (meaning "parts").

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A polymer is a long-chain molecule made by the chemical linking of a very large number of identical small molecules called **monomers**. The small repeating unit that constitutes the polymer chain is called the **Repeat Unit** (also known as the **Structural Repeating Unit, SRU**).

The **Repeat Unit (RU)** is the smallest defined unit of a polymer chain whose repetition produces the complete polymer structure (excluding end groups). It is essentially the monomer residue that remains after the polymerisation reaction.

## 1.2 Polyethylene — Formal Definition

**Polyethylene (PE)** is the simplest, most widely produced, and commercially significant addition polymer in the world, synthesised by the polymerisation of the monomer **ethylene (ethene, CH₂=CH₂)**.

- **Chemical formula of monomer:** C₂H₄ (or CH₂=CH₂)
- **Repeat unit:** –[CH₂–CH₂]–
- **General formula of polymer:** –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ– where **n** is the **degree of polymerisation (DP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Polyethylene is a **homo-polymer** (formed from a single type of monomer) and an **addition polymer** (formed without the loss of any small molecule). It belongs to the class of **polyolefins**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Beaded Necklace" Model

Imagine a **necklace** where you thread thousands of identical **beads** onto a string one by one.

| Analogy Element | Polymer Science Counterpart |
|----------------|------------------------------|
| One single bead | **Monomer** (Ethylene, CH₂=CH₂) |
| The hole in the bead | The **reactive double bond (C=C)** |
| The string passing through | The new **C–C single bond** formed |
| Pattern on the string | **Repeat Unit** (–CH₂–CH₂–) |
| Full necklace | **Polymer chain** (Polyethylene) |
| Length of the necklace | **Degree of Polymerisation (n)** |

Each bead (ethylene) "opens up" its double bond, and the resulting "activated" units (now –CH₂–CH₂–) join hands to form the long chain (polyethylene). The repeat unit, therefore, is the **structural fingerprint** that recurs over and over in the chain.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics for Polyethylene

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quantitative Parameters (must be memorised for KTU):**
> - **Molecular weight of ethylene monomer (M₀):** **28 g/mol**
> - **Molecular weight of repeat unit of PE (M₀):** **28 g/mol** (since repeat unit = one –CH₂–CH₂– unit)
> - **Number of repeat units (n) in commercial PE:** typically **10,000 to 100,000+**
> - **Density of PE:** ranges from **0.91 g/cm³ (LDPE)** to **0.97 g/cm³ (HDPE)**
> - **Glass transition temperature (T_g):** approximately **–120 °C**
> - **Melting temperature (T_m):** **115 °C to 137 °C** (depending on crystallinity)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Repeat Unit

Polyethylene is formed when **n** molecules of ethylene undergo **addition (chain-growth) polymerisation**. The π-bond of the C=C double bond breaks, and a new σ-bond forms between successive carbon atoms, yielding a saturated hydrocarbon chain.

The polymerisation reaction can be represented as:

$$ n \cdot \text{CH}_2 = \text{CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Initiator / Catalyst}} -(\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_n- $$

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Repeat Unit Identification

1. **Write the monomer structure:** CH₂=CH₂ (ethene)
2. **Identify the polymerisable functional group:** the **C=C double bond** is the reactive site.
3. **Open the double bond:** Replace "=" with two single bonds projecting outward.
4. **Place the monomer in brackets:** The bracketed unit is the **repeat unit (SRU)**: –[CH₂–CH₂]–
5. **Attach subscript 'n' to indicate repetition:** –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–

> [!NOTE]
> In **polyethylene**, the **repeat unit is identical to the monomer** because ethylene adds without the loss of any atom. This is a key KTU distinction — for polymers like PET or nylon, the repeat unit differs from the monomer because small molecules (water, HCl) are eliminated during polymerisation (condensation polymerisation).

## 2.2 Classification of Polyethylene by Chain Architecture

The mechanical, thermal, and rheological properties of polyethylene depend entirely on **chain branching**, which in turn is controlled by the polymerisation method. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically expects knowledge of the following grades:

| Grade | Full Name | Polymerisation Method | Branching | Crystallinity | Density (g/cm³) |
|-------|-----------|----------------------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **LDPE** | Low-Density Polyethylene | Free-radical (high pressure, 1000–3000 atm) | Long & short chain branches | Low (~50–60%) | 0.910–0.925 |
| **HDPE** | High-Density Polyethylene | Ziegler–Natta / Phillips coordination catalysis (low pressure) | Essentially linear | High (~70–80%) | 0.941–0.965 |
| **LLDPE** | Linear Low-Density Polyethylene | Copolymerisation with α-olefins (1-butene, 1-hexene) | Short, uniform branches | Moderate | 0.918–0.940 |
| **UHMWPE** | Ultra-High Molecular Weight PE | Modified Ziegler catalysis | Linear | Very high | 0.930–0.945 |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are repeatedly tested in KTU University Examinations. Memorise the units and conditions carefully.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $M_0 = 2 \times 12 + 4 \times 1 = 28$ | Molecular weight of repeat unit (–CH₂–CH₂–) | g/mol |
| 2 | $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \times \bar{X}_n$ | Number-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 3 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$ | Number-average degree of polymerisation | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\bar{M}_w = M_0 \times \bar{X}_w$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 5 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Polydispersity Index | Dimensionless, PDI ≥ 1 |
| 6 | $\rho_{\text{PE}} = \dfrac{\text{mass of PE}}{\text{volume of PE}}$ | Density of polyethylene | g/cm³ |
| 7 | $T_m(\text{°C}) = \dfrac{414 \times 10^3}{\bar{M}_n} + 273$ | Approx. melting point relationship (Flory) | K |
| 8 | $n = \dfrac{\text{Total mass of polymer}}{\text{Molecular weight of monomer (28)}}$ | Number of repeat units | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table formatting rule followed:** Vertical bars are replaced with `\vert` where required to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LDPE**: Plastic bags, squeeze bottles, cling films, agricultural mulch films.
- **HDPE**: Bottles, piping, geomembranes, houseware, toys.
- **LLDPE**: Stretch films, liners, packaging where puncture resistance is needed.
- **UHMWPE**: Medical implants (hip/knee replacements), bulletproof vests, high-performance gears.

The structural simplicity of polyethylene (only C and H atoms) makes it **chemically inert, electrically insulating, and recyclable (Resin code 2 / 4)** — properties that are direct consequences of the –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ– repeat unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight of the Repeat Unit (M₀)

The repeat unit of polyethylene is **–CH₂–CH₂–**, which contains:
- **2 carbon atoms** (atomic mass of C = 12 g/mol)
- **4 hydrogen atoms** (atomic mass of H = 1 g/mol)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (2 \times 12) + (4 \times 1) \\
    &= 24 + 4 \\
    &= 28 \; \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The carbon backbone contributes 24 g/mol, and the saturated hydrogens add 4 g/mol. The total is 28 g/mol — coincidentally equal to the molecular weight of ethylene monomer, because no atoms are lost in addition polymerisation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Degree of Polymerisation (DP or X_n) from M_n

The **number-average degree of polymerisation** $(\bar{X}_n)$ is defined as the average number of repeat units per polymer chain. It is obtained by dividing the number-average molecular weight by the molecular weight of a single repeat unit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n &= \frac{\text{Average mass of one polymer chain}}{\text{Mass of one repeat unit}} \\
          &= \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} \\
          &= \frac{\bar{M}_n}{28}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** If the total mass of a chain is 280,000 g/mol and each repeat unit weighs 28 g/mol, then the chain contains 280,000 ÷ 28 = 10,000 repeat units. This number (10,000) is the DP.

### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A commercial HDPE sample has a number-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_n = 1.40 \times 10^5$ g/mol. Calculate its degree of polymerisation.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n &= \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} \\
          &= \frac{1.40 \times 10^5 \; \text{g/mol}}{28 \; \text{g/mol}} \\
          &= 5000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\bar{X}_n = 5000$. The HDPE chain contains **5000 repeat units** on average.

## 3.3 Derivation: End-Group Analysis (For Initiator-Traced Polymerisation)

When polymerisation is initiated by a radical initiator **I•** (e.g., benzoyl peroxide), the initiator residue attaches to one end of the chain. If the other end is a hydrogen (termination by H-abstraction), the full chain structure is:

$$ \text{I} - (\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_n - \text{H} $$

The molecular weight of such a single chain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{chain}} &= M_{\text{end groups}} + n \times M_0 \\
                &= M_I + 1 + (n \times 28)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M_I$ is the molecular weight of the initiator fragment.

> **Logic (engineering view):** For high molecular weight PE ($n > 1000$), the end-group contribution becomes negligible (< 0.1%) and is ignored in commercial calculations. This is why the simplified formula $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \times \bar{X}_n$ works perfectly in industry.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Repeat Unit Identification Across Polymerisation Types

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Students frequently confuse *monomer* and *repeat unit*. The following matrix eliminates this confusion permanently.

| Polymerisation Type | Monomer | Repeat Unit | Is M₀ = M(monomer)? | Example |
|---------------------|---------|-------------|---------------------|---------|
| **Addition (chain-growth)** | CH₂=CH₂ | –(CH₂–CH₂)– | ✅ **Yes** | **Polyethylene** |
| Addition | CH₂=CHCl | –(CH₂–CHCl)– | ✅ Yes | PVC |
| Addition | CH₂=CH–C₆H₅ | –(CH₂–CH(C₆H₅))– | ✅ Yes | Polystyrene |
| **Condensation (step-growth)** | HO–(CH₂)₂–OH + HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH | –(O–(CH₂)₂–O–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO)– | ❌ **No** (loses H₂O) | PET |

> **Key Take-away:** For **polyethylene** (and all addition polymers), the **molecular weight of the repeat unit equals the molecular weight of the monomer**. This is unique to addition polymerisation and is a guaranteed KTU short-answer question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Degree of Polymerisation on Properties

| Degree of Polymerisation (n) | Molecular Weight (g/mol) | Physical State at 25 °C | Typical Application |
|------------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| < 100 | < 2,800 | Waxy solid / viscous liquid | Lubricants, waxes |
| 100 – 1,000 | 2,800 – 28,000 | Soft, flexible solid | Adhesives, hot-melt glues |
| 1,000 – 10,000 | 28,000 – 2.8 × 10⁵ | Tough plastic (LDPE, LLDPE) | Films, packaging |
| > 10,000 | > 2.8 × 10⁵ | Rigid, strong plastic (HDPE) | Pipes, containers |
| > 100,000 | > 2.8 × 10⁶ | Ultra-tough fibre (UHMWPE) | Body armour, implants |

> **Logic:** A higher **n** means more entanglements, stronger van der Waals forces, higher crystallinity, and consequently better mechanical strength. This is the central design principle of polymer engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: From Monomer to Polymer Chain of Polyethylene

The following Mermaid block diagrams the entire logical chain: monomer identification → double-bond opening → repeat unit formation → polymerisation → property emergence. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MONOMER: Ethylene CH2=CH2"] --> nodeB{"Reactive Site: C=C Double Bond"}
    nodeB --> nodeC["Activation: Initiator or Catalyst attacks pi-bond"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["REPEAT UNIT: CH2-CH2 with two open valencies"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Step 1: Two repeat units join by new C-C sigma bond"}
    nodeE --> nodeF["Dimer: CH2-CH2-CH2-CH2"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"Step 2: Continue adding repeat units n times"}
    nodeG --> nodeH["POLYMER CHAIN: -CH2-CH2-CH2-CH2-...-CH2-CH2-"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Degree of Polymerisation n controls properties"}
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Low n: Waxy / Liquid"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Medium n: LDPE / LLDPE Films"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["High n: HDPE Rigid Products"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["Very High n: UHMWPE Advanced Engineering"]

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeH fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeJ fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeK fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeL fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeM fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Polyethylene by Chain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["POLYETHYLENE FAMILY"] --> nodeQ{"Polymerisation Method"}
    nodeQ --> nodeR["LDPE: High Pressure Free Radical"]
    nodeQ --> nodeS["HDPE: Ziegler-Natta Low Pressure"]
    nodeQ --> nodeT["LLDPE: Copolymerisation with 1-olefins"]
    nodeQ --> nodeU["UHMWPE: Modified Ziegler Catalyst"]

    nodeR --> nodeR1["Long and short chain branches"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2["Low crystallinity, low density"]

    nodeS --> nodeS1["Linear chain, minimal branches"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2["High crystallinity, high density"]

    nodeT --> nodeT1["Short uniform branches on linear backbone"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2["High tensile strength films"]

    nodeU --> nodeU1["Extremely high molecular weight linear chain"]
    nodeU1 --> nodeU2["Used in medical implants and body armour"]

    style nodeP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeR fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeS fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeU fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How the Repeat Unit Dictates End-Use Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["REPEAT UNIT -CH2-CH2-"] --> node2["Non-polar saturated hydrocarbon backbone"]
    node2 --> node3["Weak van der Waals forces between chains"]
    node3 --> node4["Low intermolecular attraction"]
    node4 --> node5["Flexible chains with high chain mobility"]
    node5 --> node6["Engineering Properties Emerge"]
    node6 --> node7["Chemically inert: resistant to acids and bases"]
    node6 --> node8["Excellent electrical insulator"]
    node6 --> node9["Low water absorption hydrophobic"]
    node6 --> node10["Recyclable resin code 2 or 4"]

    style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style node3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style node4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style node5 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style node6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node7 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style node8 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style node9 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style node10 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram is important for KTU viva:** Examiners love asking *"Why is polyethylene chemically inert?"* The answer lies in its **saturated C–C and C–H bonds**, which have no polar groups for chemical attack — a direct consequence of the simple –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ– repeat unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define the term "repeat unit" of a polymer. Taking polyethylene as an example, draw the structure of its repeat unit and the polymer chain.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded as follows: 1 for definition, 1 for structure, 1 for explanation):**

A **repeat unit (structural repeating unit, SRU)** is the smallest structural unit of a polymer whose repetition along the chain produces the complete polymer molecule. It is the residue of the monomer that becomes part of the polymer chain.

For polyethylene, the monomer is **ethylene (CH₂=CH₂)**. The repeat unit is:

$$ -(\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)- $$

The polymer chain is represented as:

$$ -(\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_n- $$

where **n** is the degree of polymerisation. The repeat unit is formed by the opening of the C=C double bond of the ethylene monomer during addition polymerisation. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Distinguish between a monomer and a repeat unit. Is the molecular weight of the repeat unit of polyethylene the same as that of its monomer? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Monomer | Repeat Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| Definition | The small molecule that reacts to form a polymer | The smallest unit that repeats in a polymer chain |
| Independent existence | Exists as a separate small molecule | Exists only as part of the polymer chain |
| Molecular weight | C₂H₄ = 28 g/mol | –(CH₂–CH₂)– = 28 g/mol |

**Yes, for polyethylene the molecular weight of the repeat unit (28 g/mol) equals the molecular weight of the monomer (28 g/mol).** This is because polyethylene is formed by **addition polymerisation**, in which the C=C double bond opens up and no atoms are eliminated. The repeat unit therefore contains exactly the same atoms as the monomer. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the polymerisation of ethylene to polyethylene. Discuss the mechanism of free-radical polymerisation and write the chemical equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the different types of polyethylene (LDPE, HDPE, LLDPE, UHMWPE) based on their method of preparation, structure, and properties. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a): Polymerisation Mechanism**

Polyethylene is produced by the **addition (chain-growth) polymerisation** of ethylene (CH₂=CH₂). The most common industrial method is **free-radical polymerisation**, which proceeds in three stages:

**Step 1 — Initiation (1 mark)**

A radical initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, **(C₆H₅COO)₂**) decomposes under heat to form initiator radicals (I•), which attack the C=C double bond of ethylene:

$$ \text{I}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2 = \text{CH}_2 \rightarrow \text{I} - \text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2^{\bullet} $$

**Step 2 — Propagation (2 marks)**

The newly formed chain radical attacks another ethylene monomer, transferring the active site to the chain end. This repeats **n** times:

$$ \text{I} - (\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)^{\bullet} + n \cdot \text{CH}_2 = \text{CH}_2 \rightarrow \text{I} - (\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_{n+1}^{\bullet} $$

**Step 3 — Termination (2 marks)**

Two growing chain radicals combine (coupling) to form the final polymer:

$$ \text{I} - (\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_m^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}(\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_n - \text{I} \rightarrow \text{I} - (\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_{m+n} - \text{I} $$

**Overall Reaction (2 marks):**

$$ n \cdot \text{CH}_2 = \text{CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Initiator, } \Delta} -(\text{CH}_2 - \text{CH}_2)_n- $$

> **Key Point:** High-pressure (1000–3000 atm) and high temperature (200–300 °C) conditions favour free-radical polymerisation and produce **LDPE** with extensive chain branching.

---

#### **Solution to Question A (b): Types of Polyethylene**

**1. LDPE — Low-Density Polyethylene (2 marks)**
- **Preparation:** Free-radical polymerisation at high pressure (1000–3000 atm) and temperature (~300 °C).
- **Structure:** Highly branched (long and short chain branches) due to "backbiting" of radicals.
- **Properties:** Low crystallinity (50–60%), density 0.91–0.925 g/cm³, flexible, transparent films.
- **Application:** Plastic bags, squeeze bottles, cling films.

**2. HDPE — High-Density Polyethylene (2 marks)**
- **Preparation:** Ziegler–Natta or Phillips catalysis at low pressure (1–10 atm) and temperature (~100 °C).
- **Structure:** Essentially **linear** with negligible branching.
- **Properties:** High crystallinity (70–80%), density 0.94–0.97 g/cm³, rigid, tough.
- **Application:** Bottles, pipes, houseware, toys.

**3. LLDPE — Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (1.5 marks)**
- **Preparation:** Copolymerisation of ethylene with α-olefins (1-butene, 1-hexene) using Ziegler–Natta catalysts.
- **Structure:** Linear backbone with **short, uniform** branches (introduced by the comonomer).
- **Properties:** Density 0.918–0.940 g/cm³, higher tensile strength and puncture resistance than LDPE.
- **Application:** Stretch films, packaging, geomembranes.

**4. UHMWPE — Ultra-High Molecular Weight PE (1.5 marks)**
- **Preparation:** Modified Ziegler catalysis yielding extremely long chains ($M_w > 3 \times 10^6$ g/mol).
- **Structure:** Linear, very long chains, no branching, very high crystallinity.
- **Properties:** Extremely tough, abrasion-resistant, biocompatible.
- **Application:** Orthopaedic implants (hip/knee replacements), bulletproof vests, industrial liners.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Define the terms: (i) Degree of polymerisation, (ii) Number-average molecular weight, (iii) Repeat unit. A commercial HDPE sample has $\bar{M}_n = 1.96 \times 10^5$ g/mol. Calculate its degree of polymerisation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the relationship between the degree of polymerisation and the physical properties of polyethylene? Explain with suitable examples how the end-use of PE changes with molecular weight. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a): Definitions and Calculation**

**Definitions (3 marks — 1 each):**

**(i) Degree of Polymerisation (DP or $\bar{X}_n$):** The average number of repeat units present in a polymer chain. It is dimensionless and is calculated as:

$$ \bar{X}_n = \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} $$

**(ii) Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$):** The total weight of all polymer molecules divided by the total number of polymer molecules:

$$ \bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} $$

where $N_i$ is the number of molecules having molecular weight $M_i$.

**(iii) Repeat Unit:** The smallest structural unit whose repetition along the chain generates the complete polymer. For polyethylene, the repeat unit is –(CH₂–CH₂)– with molecular weight $M_0 = 28$ g/mol.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= 28 \; \text{g/mol} \quad \text{(repeat unit of polyethylene)} \\
\bar{M}_n &= 1.96 \times 10^5 \; \text{g/mol} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} \\
          &= \frac{1.96 \times 10^5}{28} \\
          &= 7000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The degree of polymerisation of the HDPE sample is **7000**, meaning each chain contains 7000 repeat units on average. (7 marks total)

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b): DP vs Physical Properties**

The **degree of polymerisation (DP)** is the single most important parameter that determines the physical state, mechanical strength, and end-use of polyethylene. As DP increases, the following changes occur (7 marks — allocate 1 mark per row + 2 marks for applications):

| Degree of Polymerisation (n) | Molecular Weight Range | Physical State | Mechanical Properties | Typical Application |
|------------------------------|------------------------|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| < 100 | < 2,800 g/mol | Waxy solid / liquid | Negligible strength | Lubricants, paraffin wax |
| 100 – 1,000 | 2,800 – 28,000 g/mol | Soft, flexible solid | Low strength | Hot-melt adhesives |
| 1,000 – 10,000 | 28,000 – 280,000 g/mol | Tough plastic | Moderate strength | LDPE/LLDPE films, packaging |
| 10,000 – 100,000 | 2.8 × 10⁵ – 2.8 × 10⁶ g/mol | Rigid, strong plastic | High tensile strength | HDPE pipes, containers |
| > 100,000 | > 2.8 × 10⁶ g/mol | Ultra-strong fibre-like | Exceptional toughness | UHMWPE implants, armour |

**Explanation (2 marks):** As **n** increases:
- Chain entanglements increase → improved tensile strength.
- Intermolecular van der Waals forces increase → higher melting point.
- Crystallinity increases → harder and stiffer material.
- Melt viscosity increases → harder to process (requires special equipment).

> **Engineering trade-off:** Higher DP gives superior properties but makes processing difficult. The polymer engineer must select the optimal DP that balances performance and processability for the intended application.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Writing the repeat unit without brackets and subscript 'n'** — always write **–(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–**, never just –CH₂–CH₂–.
> 2. **Forgetting the repeating 'n' subscript** on the repeat unit (results in loss of 1 mark).
> 3. **Confusing monomer molecular weight (28 g/mol) with repeat unit molecular weight (28 g/mol)** in the case of condensation polymers like PET (192 g/mol ≠ monomer mass). For PE they are equal, but you must justify why.
> 4. **Mixing up LDPE and HDPE conditions** — LDPE is high-pressure free-radical; HDPE is low-pressure Ziegler–Natta.
> 5. **Wrong units in molecular weight calculations** — always quote in **g/mol** explicitly.
> 6. **Calculating $\bar{X}_n$ without showing the substitution step** — KTU examiners deduct marks for skipping mathematical steps.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Polyethylene** is synthesised from the monomer **ethylene (CH₂=CH₂)** via **addition polymerisation**.
- ✅ The **repeat unit** of polyethylene is **–(CH₂–CH₂)–** with $M_0 = 28$ g/mol.
- ✅ For PE, **$M_0$ (repeat unit) = $M$ (monomer) = 28 g/mol** because no atoms are lost.
- ✅ General formula: **–(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–** where **n = degree of polymerisation (DP)**.
- ✅ DP calculation: $\bar{X}_n = \bar{M}_n / M_0 = \bar{M}_n / 28$.
- ✅ **LDPE**: free-radical, high pressure, branched, low density (0.91–0.925 g/cm³).
- ✅ **HDPE**: Ziegler–Natta, low pressure, linear, high density (0.94–0.97 g/cm³).
- ✅ **LLDPE**: copolymer with 1-olefins, linear with short branches.
- ✅ **UHMWPE**: extremely high molecular weight (> 10⁶ g/mol), used in implants and armour.
- ✅ **Higher DP → stronger, tougher, more crystalline, but harder to process**.
- ✅ Polyethylene is **non-polar, chemically inert, hydrophobic, electrically insulating, and recyclable** (Resin code 2/4).
- ✅ Always write repeat units in **brackets with subscript 'n'**: –(CH₂–CH₂)ₙ–.
- ✅ Key equations to memorise: $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \times \bar{X}_n$ and PDI = $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polypropylene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polypropylene # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polypropylene (PP)** is a **semicrystalline, thermoplastic addition polymer** synthesized from the monomer **propylene (propene, CH₂=CH–CH₃)**. It belongs to the family of **polyolefins** and is produced commercially by **chain-growth (addition) polymerization** using **coordination catalysts** — primarily the **Ziegler–Natta (Z-N) catalysts** and, more recently, **metallocene catalysts**. The repeating constitutional unit of polypropylene is: $$-[\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)]_n-$$ where **n** is the **degree of polymerization (DP)**. The pendant **methyl (–CH₃) group** on every alternate backbone carbon gives polypropylene its characteristic **stereoregularity**, which directly governs its **crystallinity, density, and mechanical performance**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCPOT205 – Module 1):** Students must be able to identify the monomer, repeat unit, polymerization mechanism (coordination/insertion), tacticity (isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic), key properties, and major applications of polypropylene. --- ## 1.2 Historical Background The commercialization of polypropylene is one of the landmark achievements in 20th-century polymer science: - **1951 – Paul Hogan and Robert Banks** (Phillips Petroleum) first polymerized propylene using a chromium-based catalyst, but the product was a soft, rubbery, low-crystallinity material (predominantly **atactic PP**). - **1953 – Karl Ziegler (Germany)** developed transition-metal catalysts (TiCl₄ + Al(C₂H₅)₃) capable of polymerizing ethylene at low pressure. - **1954 – Giulio Natta (Italy)** extended Ziegler's work to propylene and successfully produced a **stereoregular (isotactic) polypropylene** using a **TiCl₃ / Al(C₂H₅)₃** catalyst system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ziegler and Natta shared the 1963 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** *"for their discoveries in the field of the chemistry and technology of high polymers."* Their work gave birth to the **Ziegler–Natta (Z-N) catalysis** that revolutionized the entire polyolefin industry. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a string of identical wooden beads (the **–CH₂–CH₂–** units) where, on every second bead, a small colored **tag (the –CH₃ group)** is glued sticking out. Now imagine threading thousands of such beads into a long chain. - If **all the tags point in the same direction** (say, all to the right), the chain can **pack tightly** like a neatly coiled rope — this is **isotactic PP**, which is **strong, stiff, and crystalline**. - If the tags **alternate left-right-left-right** in a perfect rhythm, the chain also packs reasonably well — this is **syndiotactic PP**. - If the tags **point randomly in every direction**, the chain is a tangled, messy ball — this is **atactic PP**, which is **soft, waxy, and amorphous** (essentially a useless rubbery waste by-product in early Z-N polymerization). > [!NOTE] > **The methyl group is tiny but decisive.** A 0.1 nm change in spatial arrangement transforms a worthless wax into a high-performance engineering plastic — this is the essence of **stereoregular polymerization**. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Polypropylene Polypropylene is classified into three principal commercial grades based on its **molecular architecture and stereochemistry**: | Grade | Tacticity | Crystallinity | Key Characteristic | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Homopolymer (PP-H)** | Isotactic (> 95 %) | High (40 – 60 %) | Rigid, strong, high heat resistance | | **Random Copolymer (PP-R)** | Isotactic + random ethylene co-units (1 – 7 %) | Lower than homopolymer | Better transparency & impact resistance | | **Impact (Block) Copolymer (PP-B / PP-I)** | Isotactic PP matrix + dispersed EPR rubber phase | Medium | Excellent low-temperature impact strength | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A common KTU question asks to differentiate **atactic, isotactic, and syndiotactic PP** with respect to crystallinity and melting point. Always link *tacticity → crystallinity → Tm → mechanical strength*. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Monomer: Propylene (Propene) **IUPAC Name:** Prop-1-ene **Molecular Formula:** C₃H₆ **Molar Mass:** **42.08 g/mol** **Boiling Point:** **–47.6 °C** (gaseous at room temperature) **Structure:** CH₂=CH–CH₃ > [!IMPORTANT] > Propylene is a **substituted vinyl monomer**. The presence of the **electron-donating methyl group** makes the double bond slightly **electron-rich** and influences the polymerization kinetics, the stability of the propagating chain end, and the **stereochemical outcome** of each monomer insertion step. --- ## 2.2 Polymerization Mechanism — Ziegler–Natta Catalysis Polypropylene is industrially produced by **coordination–insertion (stereospecific) polymerization** at the **surface of a transition-metal catalyst particle**. The mechanism proceeds through the **Cossee–Arlman mechanism**, widely accepted as the operative pathway. ### 2.2.1 Step-wise Mechanistic Logic 1. **Catalyst Activation** – The solid TiCl₃ (or MgCl₂-supported TiCl₄) catalyst is partially reduced by the co-catalyst **triethylaluminium, Al(C₂H₅)₃**, generating **active coordinatively-unsaturated Ti(III) sites** on the crystal surface. 2. **Complexation** – The π-bond of the propylene monomer **coordinates** to the vacant site on the active Ti center, forming a π-complex. 3. **Insertion (Chain Growth)** – The pre-ordinated **Ti–Polymer bond (Ti–C bond)** migrates/inserts into the coordinated C=C double bond. This is a **cis-insertion** that places the new monomer unit at the metal–polymer interface. 4. **Stereochemical Control** – The active site is **chiral (asymmetric)** due to its specific arrangement of Cl and alkyl ligands on the TiCl₃ surface. This chirality forces each incoming monomer to adopt a **preferred orientation**, producing **stereoregular (isotactic) sequences**. 5. **Chain Propagation** – The newly vacant coordination site is re-occupied by the next propylene molecule, and **step 2–4 repeat** until chain transfer or termination occurs. 6. **Chain Transfer / Termination** – Propagation may be terminated by: - **β-Hydride elimination** (most common) - **Transfer to monomer** - **Transfer to co-catalyst (Al-alkyl)** - **Transfer to H₂** (used industrially to control molecular weight — **the "Melt Flow Rate" of PP** is regulated by H₂ addition) > [!NOTE] > **Why stereoregular?** > The **chiral environment of the active site** is the molecular "mould". Each monomer fits into this mould in only one orientation, like a key in a lock, ensuring every pendant –CH₃ ends up on the same side of the polymer backbone. --- ## 2.3 Tacticity — The Heart of Polypropylene Stereochemistry **Tacticity** refers to the **spatial arrangement (configuration)** of the pendant methyl groups along the polymer chain, measured relative to the extended (planar zigzag) backbone conformation. ### 2.3.1 The Three Tactic Configurations | Configuration | Methyl Group Arrangement | Crystallinity | Melting Point (Tm) | Density | Mechanical Behaviour | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Isotactic (iPP)** | All –CH₃ on the **same side** | **High (50–60 %)** | **165 – 171 °C** | **0.90 – 0.91 g/cm³** | Rigid, strong, hard | | **Syndiotactic (sPP)** | –CH₃ alternating sides (ABAB…) | **Moderate (30–40 %)** | **~130 °C** | **0.89 – 0.90 g/cm³** | Tough, transparent, slow to crystallize | | **Atactic (aPP)** | –CH₃ randomly distributed | **Nil (amorphous)** | **No sharp Tm** | **~0.85 g/cm³** | Soft, tacky, rubbery, **commercially useless as a plastic** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Must-Know:** The tacticity of PP is determined experimentally by **¹³C Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy** in solution, where the **pentad sequence distribution** (mmmm, mmmr, mmrr, …) of the methyl carbon is analyzed. The most important industrial metric is the **Isotactic Index (I.I.)** = percentage of isotactic pentads. --- ## 2.4 Molecular Weight and Its Distribution The mechanical and rheological properties of polypropylene are critically dependent on its **number-average molecular weight (Mn)** and **weight-average molecular weight (Mw)**. | Property | Symbol | Formula | Typical Industrial Range for PP | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Number-average molecular weight | $M_n$ | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | **50,000 – 250,000 g/mol** | | Weight-average molecular weight | $M_w$ | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | **300,000 – 700,000 g/mol** | | Polydispersity Index | PDI | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | **2 – 10** (Z-N PP is broad) | | Degree of Polymerization | $X_n$ | $X_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | **1,200 – 6,000** | | Melt Flow Rate | MFR | $MFR = \dfrac{\text{mass extruded in 10 min}}{2.16 \text{ kg at 230 °C}}$ | **0.3 – 35 g/10 min** | > [!NOTE] > **MFR is the single most important industrial specification** for a commercial PP grade. A **low MFR** (e.g., 0.3) indicates high Mw and is used for **extrusion/blow-moulding**; a **high MFR** (e.g., 35) indicates low Mw and is used for **injection moulding of thin-walled parts**. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Description / Engineering Utility | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | $X_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ where $M_0 = 42.08$ g/mol for propylene | Average number of repeat units per chain. | | 2 | $M_n = M_0 \cdot \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{conversion}}$ | For free-radical addition; useful for sizing reactors. | | 3 | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Z-N PP typically **2 – 10**; metallocene PP is near **2.0** (narrow). | | 4 | $MFR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{10\,\text{min}}}{2.16\,\text{kg load, 230°C}}$ | Industrial rheology specification. | | 5 | $T_g(\text{atactic PP}) \approx -18\,°C$ | Glass transition of fully amorphous PP. | | 6 | $T_m(\text{isotactic PP}) \approx 165 - 171\,°C$ | Peak DSC melting temperature. | | 7 | $\%\text{Crystallinity} = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^\circ} \times 100$ | $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{100% cryst. PP}) = 209\,\text{J/g}$ | | 8 | $\text{Stereo specific efficiency} = \dfrac{[mmmm]}{[mmmm]+[mmmr]+\cdots} \times 100$ | Isotactic Index (I.I.) measured by ¹³C NMR. | | 9 | Rate of polymerization: $R_p = k_p [M] [C^*]$ | Coordination polymerization kinetics. | | 10 | $\rho_{\text{iPP}} \approx 0.90 - 0.91$ g/cm³ | Among the **lightest commodity plastics** (floats on water). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility of PP:** Because of its **low density, high Tm, excellent chemical resistance, and low cost**, PP is the **second most widely produced commodity plastic in the world** (after polyethylene), with global production exceeding **80 million tonnes/year**. Major engineering sectors: **automotive (bumpers, dashboards), packaging (hinged caps, films, containers), medical (syringes, vials — autoclavable), textiles (non-wovens, carpets, ropes), and electrical (cable insulation).** --- ## 2.6 Crystal Structure of Isotactic Polypropylene Isotactic PP crystallizes predominantly in the **α-form (alpha form)**, which is the thermodynamically most stable polymorph: - **Crystal System:** Monoclinic - **Unit Cell Parameters:** a = 0.665 nm, b = 2.096 nm, c = 0.650 nm, β = 99.3° - **Helical Conformation:** **3₁ helix** — 3 repeat units per turn, with a pitch of **0.65 nm**. This 3₁ helix arises because of the steric repulsion between the methyl groups, which forces the backbone to twist. > [!NOTE] > A less common but technologically important polymorph is the **β-form (trigonal, hexagonal)**, obtained by adding specific **β-nucleating agents** (e.g., quinacridone, calcium pimelate). β-PP has **improved impact strength and ductility** and is used in microporous battery separators and films. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Degree of Polymerization from Molecular Weight A sample of isotactic polypropylene has a **number-average molecular weight Mₙ = 1,68,000 g/mol**. Calculate its **number-average degree of polymerization (Xₙ)** and estimate the **number of –CH₃ groups per chain**. ### Step 1: Identify the molar mass of the repeat unit (M₀) The repeat unit of polypropylene is –[CH₂–CH(CH₃)]–. $$ M_0 = 2(12) + 4(1) + (12) + 3(1) = 12 + 4 + 12 + 3 $$ $$ M_0 = 12.01 + 4.04 + 12.01 + 3.03 = 42.08 \;\; \text{g/mol} $$ ### Step 2: Apply the definition of degree of polymerization $$ X_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0} $$ $$ X_n = \frac{1{,}68{,}000 \;\; \text{g/mol}}{42.08 \;\; \text{g/mol}} $$ $$ X_n \approx 3992 \;\; \text{repeat units} $$ ### Step 3: Count the pendant –CH₃ groups per chain Since **each repeat unit of PP carries exactly one –CH₃ group**, the number of methyl groups per chain equals the degree of polymerization. $$ N_{CH_3} = X_n \approx 3992 \;\; \text{groups per chain} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** A single PP chain is about **3992 monomer units long**, with each unit bearing a –CH₃ side group, giving a chain length of approximately **0.65 nm × 3992 ≈ 2.6 μm** when fully extended in the 3₁ helical conformation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Crystallinity from DSC Melting Enthalpy A polypropylene sample shows a melting endotherm with **ΔHf = 105 J/g** in a DSC scan. Determine its **percentage crystallinity**, taking the enthalpy of fusion of 100 % crystalline isotactic PP as **ΔHf° = 209 J/g**. ### Step 1: State the crystallinity formula $$ \%\,Crystallinity = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \times 100 $$ ### Step 2: Substitute numerical values $$ \%\,Crystallinity = \frac{105\;\text{J/g}}{209\;\text{J/g}} \times 100 $$ $$ \%\,Crystallinity \approx 50.2\% $$ ### Step 3: Engineering interpretation A crystallinity of ~50 % places this PP in the **isotactic homopolymer** range (typical: 40 – 60 %). Such a sample is suitable for **injection moulding of rigid packaging and automotive parts** and will exhibit a tensile strength of about **30 – 38 MPa**, an elongation at break of **~10 – 20 %**, and a heat-deflection temperature of about **100 °C** at 0.45 MPa load. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Comonomer (Ethylene) on Tm — The Random Copolymer Equation When a small amount of ethylene is randomly co-polymerized with propylene, the resulting **PP-R random copolymer** has a depressed melting point. The relevant relationship is the **Flory equation for random copolymer melting point depression**: $$ \frac{1}{T_m} - \frac{1}{T_m^{\circ}} = -\frac{R}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \ln X_A $$ where: - $T_m$ = melting point of the copolymer (K) - $T_m^{\circ}$ = melting point of the homopolymer iPP = **165 °C = 438.15 K** - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ = enthalpy of fusion per mole of crystallizable repeat unit ≈ **8.4 kJ/mol** - $X_A$ = mole fraction of crystallizable propylene units in the copolymer ### Worked Numerical Example For a PP-R random copolymer with **3 mol% ethylene**, calculate $T_m$ (in °C). **Step 1:** Calculate $X_A$ (mole fraction of propylene crystallizable units) $$ X_A = 1 - 0.03 = 0.97 $$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the right-hand side of the Flory equation $$ -\frac{R}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \ln X_A = -\frac{8.314}{8400} \ln(0.97) $$ $$ = -9.898 \times 10^{-4} \times (-0.03046) $$ $$ = +3.015 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{K}^{-1} $$ **Step 3:** Solve for $1/T_m$ $$ \frac{1}{T_m} = \frac{1}{438.15} + 3.015 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_m} = 2.2823 \times 10^{-3} + 0.03015 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_m} = 2.3125 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{K}^{-1} $$ **Step 4:** Invert to obtain $T_m$ in Kelvin, then convert to °C $$ T_m = \frac{1}{2.3125 \times 10^{-3}} = 432.43 \;\; \text{K} $$ $$ T_m = 432.43 - 273.15 = 159.28 \;\; °C $$ ### Engineering Interpretation > [!NOTE] > The incorporation of just **3 mol% ethylene depresses the melting point by ~6 °C**. This is why PP-R pipes (used in **hot- and cold-water plumbing**) can be heat-fused at lower temperatures while still retaining useful mechanical strength. Increasing ethylene beyond ~7 mol% renders the copolymer fully amorphous and it becomes an **elastomer (EPR rubber)**, not a plastic. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tacticity ↔ Properties ↔ Application | Property | Isotactic PP (iPP) | Syndiotactic PP (sPP) | Atactic PP (aPP) | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Stereoregularity | High (mmmm > 95 %) | Moderate (rrrr high) | Random | | Crystallinity | 50 – 60 % | 30 – 40 % | 0 % (amorphous) | | Tm (°C) | 165 – 171 | ~130 | None (softens gradually) | | Tg (°C) | –10 to –20 | ~ –8 | ~ –18 | | Density (g/cm³) | 0.90 – 0.91 | 0.89 – 0.90 | ~ 0.85 | | Stiffness (Tensile Modulus, GPa) | **1.4 – 1.8** | ~ 0.8 | < 0.1 | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | **30 – 38** | ~ 20 | Negligible | | Transparency | Translucent (spherulites scatter light) | Transparent | Transparent (amorphous) | | Solvent Resistance | Excellent | Good | Poor | | Primary Application | Automotive, packaging, fibres | Films, medical, transparent mouldings | Adhesive tackifier, waste by-product | | Catalyst | Z-N (TiCl₄/MgCl₂) or metallocene | Specific metallocene (C₂-symmetric) | Free radical (degraded products) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fischer / Sawhorse Projection of Polypropylene Tacticity The following Mermaid block illustrates the three stereoisomers of polypropylene, showing the spatial arrangement of the methyl group relative to the polymer backbone. ```mermaid graph LR classDef isotactic fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000 classDef syndiotactic fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000 classDef atactic fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000 subgraph Backbone_Chain[POLYPROPYLENE BACKBONE] direction LR node1["C1
CH2"]:::isoChain node2["C2

CH-CH3

CH-CH3

CH-CH3

CH-CH3
RANDOM"] ata1 --- ata2 --- ata3 --- ata4 end ``` --- ## 4.2 Cossee-Arlman Coordination-Insertion Mechanism Flow ```mermaid graph TD classDef catalyst fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef monomer fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 classDef product fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef process fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 A["Active Site
Ti-CH2-Polymer

CH2=CH-CH3
approaches Ti center"]:::monomer C["Pi-Complex Formation
Monomer coordinates

New C-C bond forms

One Repeat Unit
CH2-CH(CH3)-"]:::product F["New Vacant Site
Available for
Next Monomer"]:::catalyst A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> B G["Chiral Active Site
Imposes Stereospecific
Orientation of Monomer"]:::catalyst G -.-> C ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Z-N PP Production ```mermaid graph TB classDef input fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef process fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef control fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 M["Propylene Monomer
CH2=CH-CH3
Purity >= 99.5 percent"]:::input CAT["Ziegler-Natta Catalyst
TiCl4 on MgCl2 Support

Molecular Weight
Control Agent"]:::control REACTOR["POLYMERIZATION REACTOR

N2 or liquid propylene diluent"]:::process KILL["Catalyst KILLER
Methanol / Water
Quenches active sites"]:::process CENTRIFUGE["CENTRIFUGE / WASHING

+ Catalyst residues"]:::process DRYER["FLUIDIZED-BED DRYER

Residual volatiles < 50 ppm"]:::process EXTRUDER["EXTRUDER + PELLETIZER

Antioxidant + UV stabilizer
+ Nucleating agent"]:::process PP_OUT["POLYPROPYLENE PELLETS
MFR 0.3 to 35 g/10 min
Tacticity > 95 percent isotactic"]:::output M --> REACTOR CAT --> REACTOR H2 --> REACTOR REACTOR --> KILL KILL --> CENTRIFUGE CENTRIFUGE --> DRYER DRYER --> EXTRUDER EXTRUDER --> PP_OUT ``` --- ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right PP Grade ```mermaid graph TD Q1{Required Heat
Resistance and
Stiffness Level?} Q1 -->|High Tm and Rigidity| A1["ISOTACTIC HOMOPOLYMER PP-H
Tm 165 to 171 C
Use: Hot-fill containers, auto parts"] Q1 -->|Moderate transparency
and toughness| A2["RANDOM COPOLYMER PP-R
1 to 7 percent Ethylene
Use: Clear packaging, hot-water pipes"] Q1 -->|High impact
at low T| A3["IMPACT BLOCK COPOLYMER PP-B
