Low Extractables| A4["SYNDIOTACTIC PP
Metallocene catalyst
Use: Medical devices, films"] ``` --- ## 4.5 Comparative Property Spider Map (Mermaid xychart) ```mermaid graph LR classDef iPP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B classDef sPP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4 classDef aPP fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000 PPaxis["Polypropylene Tacticity Comparison"]:::iPP PPaxis --> iPPnode["ISOTACTIC PP
Tm 168 C
Density 0.91
Modulus 1.6 GPa
Crystallinity 55 percent"]:::iPP PPaxis --> sPPnode["SYNDIOTACTIC PP
Tm 130 C
Density 0.90
Modulus 0.8 GPa
Crystallinity 35 percent"]:::sPP PPaxis --> aPPnode["ATACTIC PP
No Tm
Density 0.85
Modulus less than 0.1 GPa
Amorphous"]:::aPP ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] **Differentiate between isotactic, syndiotactic, and atactic polypropylene with respect to (i) arrangement of methyl groups, (ii) crystallinity, and (iii) one key application of each.** **Model Answer:** | Tacticity | (i) Methyl Group Arrangement | (ii) Crystallinity | (iii) Application | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Isotactic** | All –CH₃ on the same side of the backbone | High (50 – 60 %), helical 3₁ conformation | Moulded automotive parts, packaging containers | | **Syndiotactic** | –CH₃ alternating regularly on opposite sides | Moderate (30 – 40 %) | Transparent films, medical devices | | **Atactic** | –CH₃ randomly arranged | Nil (amorphous) | Adhesive tackifier, by-product of early Z-N production | > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (3 M):** 1 M per row. Examiner awards **1 M for crystallinity**, **1 M for methyl arrangement**, and **1 M for application**. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] **What is the role of triethylaluminium (Al(C₂H₅)₃) in Ziegler–Natta polymerization of propylene?** **Model Answer:** Triethylaluminium acts as the **co-catalyst (activator)** in Z-N polymerization. Its functions are: 1. **Alkylation of the active site** — it transfers an ethyl group to the Ti center, converting TiCl₄/TiCl₃ to a catalytically active **Ti–C (Ti–ethyl) bond**, which is the true initiator of chain growth. 2. **Reduction of Ti(IV) → Ti(III)** — partially reduces the transition metal, generating coordinatively unsaturated sites capable of monomer coordination. 3. **Scavenger of impurities** — it reacts with trace water and oxygen, protecting the active sites. 4. **Chain transfer agent** — it participates in chain transfer to aluminium, which can broaden or moderate the molecular weight distribution. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution (3 M):** 1 M for "co-catalyst", 1 M for alkylation + reduction of Ti, 1 M for scavenging / chain transfer role. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model] #### (a) **Explain the coordination–insertion mechanism of propylene polymerization on a Ziegler–Natta catalyst. Use the Cossee–Arlman mechanism and clearly show how stereoregular (isotactic) polymer is produced. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Introduction (1 M):** Polypropylene is commercially produced by **chain-growth coordination polymerization** using heterogeneous Z-N catalysts (TiCl₄ supported on MgCl₂, activated by Al(C₂H₅)₃). The mechanism follows the **Cossee–Arlman model**. **Step 2 — Catalyst Activation (1 M):** The solid TiCl₄/MgCl₂ catalyst is reacted with the co-catalyst Al(C₂H₅)₃. This performs two functions: - **Alkylation** — transfers an ethyl group to Ti, forming the active **Ti–C₂H₅** bond. - **Reduction** — reduces Ti(IV) to Ti(III), creating a **coordinatively unsaturated site** next to the Ti–C bond. **Step 3 — Monomer Complexation (1 M):** The propylene monomer (CH₂=CH–CH₃) approaches the active site and coordinates through its π-bond to the vacant coordination site on Ti, forming a **π-complex**. **Step 4 — Cis-Insertion (2 M):** The Ti–Polymer bond (Ti–CH₂–Pₙ) undergoes a **concerted migratory insertion** of the coordinated C=C double bond. This is a **four-centred transition state** in which the new C–C bond forms on the same face as the existing Ti–C bond (cis-insertion). The new monomer unit is added to the chain, and the vacant coordination site is regenerated on the opposite side of the growing chain. **Step 5 — Stereoregulation (1 M):** The active site on the TiCl₃/MgCl₂ surface is **chiral (asymmetric)** due to the specific arrangement of Cl ligands and surface atoms. This chiral pocket forces each incoming propylene to adopt a **single preferred orientation** (e.g., the methyl group always reinserts on the same face), producing an **isotactic** sequence in which all pendant –CH₃ groups are on the same side of the backbone. **Step 6 — Repetition and Termination (1 M):** Steps 3–5 repeat for each new monomer, propagating the chain. Termination occurs mainly by **β-hydride elimination** or **chain transfer to H₂** (used to control molecular weight industrially). **Final Result:** A stereoregular, semicrystalline, high-melting isotactic polypropylene chain. --- #### (b) **Discuss the effect of tacticity on the physical and mechanical properties of polypropylene. How does the Isotactic Index (I.I.) of a commercial PP grade influence its end-use application? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Tacticity Definition (1 M):** Tacticity is the **stereochemical configuration** of the pendant –CH₃ group on every alternate backbone carbon, relative to the planar zigzag conformation of the chain. **Step 2 — Effect on Crystallinity and Tm (2 M):** | Tacticity | Crystallinity | Tm (°C) | Density (g/cm³) | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Isotactic (I.I. > 95 %) | 50 – 60 % | 165 – 171 | 0.90 – 0.91 | | Syndiotactic | 30 – 40 % | ~ 130 | 0.89 – 0.90 | | Atactic | Nil | None | 0.85 | Isotactic chains can pack into ordered **3₁ helices** and crystallize into lamellae. Atactic chains cannot pack, remaining amorphous. **Step 3 — Effect on Mechanical Properties (2 M):** - **iPP** — tensile strength 30 – 38 MPa, modulus 1.4 – 1.8 GPa, hardness Rockwell R95, good creep resistance → **rigid mouldings, fibres**. - **sPP** — lower strength but high clarity and toughness → **transparent films, medical ware**. - **aPP** — soft, tacky, weak → **not used as a plastic**, only as adhesive additive. **Step 4 — Effect on Thermal and Chemical Properties (1 M):** Higher isotacticity → higher Tm → better heat-deflection temperature → better chemical resistance. The high Tm of iPP allows **autoclaving at 121 °C**, critical for **medical disposables**. **Step 5 — Isotactic Index & End-Use (1 M):** The **Isotactic Index (I.I.) = % of isotactic pentads (mmmm) measured by ¹³C NMR**. Commercial grades are classified as: | I.I. Range | Grade | Typical End-Use | |:---|:---|:---| | 95 – 99 % | Homopolymer PP-H | Fibres, rigid packaging, automotive | | 90 – 95 % | High-crystallinity PP | Food containers, hot-fill | | < 90 % (with comonomer) | Random / Block copolymer | Pipes, battery cases, films | A higher I.I. → higher modulus and heat resistance → engineering applications. Lower I.I. (with ethylene comonomer) → impact resistance and clarity → packaging and pipe applications. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern] #### (a) **With neat sketches, describe the slurry (suspension) and gas-phase processes for the industrial manufacture of polypropylene. Compare their advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Slurry (Suspension) Process (3 M):** - The earliest commercial Z-N PP process. - **Reactor:** Stirred-tank or loop reactor, with **liquid propylene itself acting as the diluent** (also called the **"bulk slurry"** process) or with an inert hydrocarbon diluent (hexane, heptane). - **Conditions:** 60 – 80 °C, 10 – 40 bar. - The catalyst and growing polymer particles are suspended in the liquid; unreacted propylene is flashed off and recycled. - **Atactic PP** dissolves in the liquid diluent and is removed as a by-product. **Step 2 — Gas-Phase Process (2 M):** - Modern process (e.g., **Novolen, Unipol PP**). - **Reactor:** Vertical or horizontal fluidized-bed reactor. - **Conditions:** 60 – 90 °C, 20 – 35 bar. - **No liquid diluent** — gaseous propylene fluidizes the polymer particles, which grow around the catalyst. - **Atactic PP is not produced** in appreciable amounts due to higher stereospecificity of modern catalysts; no removal step needed. **Step 3 — Comparison Table (2 M):** | Feature | Slurry Process | Gas-Phase Process | |:---|:---|:---| | Diluent | Liquid propylene or hydrocarbon | None (gaseous monomer) | | Temperature / Pressure | 60 – 80 °C, 10 – 40 bar | 60 – 90 °C, 20 – 35 bar | | Atactic PP removal | Required (centrifuge/wash) | Not required | | Catalyst productivity | Moderate | Very high (modern MgCl₂-supported) | | Energy consumption | Higher (diluent recovery) | Lower | | Product uniformity | Moderate | High | | Environmental load | Higher (solvent emissions) | Lower | | Capital cost | Higher | Lower | > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Modern PP plants predominantly use **gas-phase processes** because of their higher energy efficiency, lower emissions, and superior catalyst productivity. --- #### (b) **List any five important mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties of isotactic polypropylene. Explain why PP is preferred over polyethylene (PE) for hot-water pipe applications and autoclaved medical devices. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Five Important Properties of iPP (3 M):** 1. **Low density (0.90 – 0.91 g/cm³)** — the **lightest commodity plastic**; floats on water. 2. **High melting point (165 – 171 °C)** — highest among commodity polyolefins. 3. **High tensile strength (30 – 38 MPa)** and **flexural modulus (1.4 – 1.8 GPa)**. 4. **Excellent chemical resistance** — resistant to acids, alkalis, alcohols, and most organic solvents at room temperature. 5. **Good electrical insulation** — high dielectric strength and low dielectric loss. 6. **Excellent fatigue resistance** — can survive millions of flex cycles, used in **integral hinges** (e.g., flip-top bottle caps). **Step 2 — PP vs PE for Hot-Water Pipes (2 M):** | Property | PP (Random Copolymer) | HDPE | |:---|:---|:---| | Melting point (°C) | 160 – 165 (PP-R) | 130 – 135 | | Max continuous service temp (°C) | 95 (PP-R) | 60 – 70 | | Pressure rating at 95 °C | High | Low (softens) | | Heat-fusion jointing | Excellent | Good | | Creep resistance at 70 °C | Better | Poorer | **PP-R pipes** can carry hot water continuously at **70 – 95 °C** without softening, whereas HDPE deforms above 60 – 70 °C. The higher Tm of PP makes it the **only commodity polyolefin suitable for hot-water plumbing**. **Step 3 — PP for Autoclaved Medical Devices (2 M):** - Medical devices (syringes, vials, surgical trays) are sterilized in **steam autoclaves at 121 °C / 15 psi for 20 min**. - **PP (Tm ≈ 165 °C)** retains its shape and mechanical integrity at 121 °C, while **PE (Tm ≈ 130 °C)** softens and deforms. - PP is **chemically inert**, **non-toxic**, and does not release harmful extractables, making it **USP Class VI / FDA-compliant** for medical use. - PP is **transparent** in thin sections, allowing visual inspection of contents. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Polypropylene Module):** > 1. **Confusing "tacticity" with "crystallinity".** Tacticity is a *configurational* property of the chain; crystallinity is a *physical* state arising from chain packing. Many students write "atactic PP is crystalline" — this loses 1 – 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to specify which polymorph** (α, β, or γ) of iPP they are discussing in questions on crystallization. > 3. **Stating the wrong molar mass** of the repeat unit (using 42 g/mol is correct, not 41 or 43). > 4. **Confusing the Cossee–Arlman mechanism with free-radical polymerization.** Z-N polymerization is **coordination–insertion, not radical**; no peroxides or initiators. > 5. **Not mentioning β-hydride elimination** as the dominant chain-termination step in Z-N PP. > 6. **Wrong attribution of Nobel Prize** — Ziegler and Natta shared the **1963** Nobel Prize in Chemistry, not Physics, and not 1960. > 7. **Failing to convert DSC crystallinity ΔHf values** to percentage using the correct ΔHf° = 209 J/g reference. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Polypropylene (PP)** is an **addition polymer of propylene (C₃H₆)**, belonging to the **polyolefin family**, with repeat unit **–[CH₂–CH(CH₃)]ₙ–** and M₀ = **42.08 g/mol**. - [x] Commercial PP is produced by **Ziegler–Natta (Z-N) coordination–insertion polymerization** following the **Cossee–Arlman mechanism**. - [x] The **methyl group on every alternate carbon** gives rise to **tacticity** — the spatial arrangement that determines crystallinity. - [x] Three tacticities: **Isotactic (iPP) – Syndiotactic (sPP) – Atactic (aPP)**. iPP is commercially dominant. - [x] **Isotactic PP** is **semicrystalline (50 – 60 % crystallinity)**, melts at **165 – 171 °C**, density **0.90 – 0.91 g/cm³**. - [x] **Atactic PP** is **amorphous**, waxy, and **commercially useless as a plastic**; used as adhesive tackifier. - [x] **Crystal structure:** α-form monoclinic, **3₁ helix**, unit cell a = 0.665 nm, b = 2.096 nm, c = 0.650 nm, β = 99.3°. - [x] **Ziegler & Natta** shared the **1963 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** for stereospecific polymerization of polyolefins. - [x] **Co-catalyst:** Al(C₂H₅)₃ (triethylaluminium) — alkylates and reduces the Ti active site. - [x] **Isotactic Index (I.I.)** = % of isotactic pentads (**mmmm**) measured by **¹³C NMR** — industrial quality metric. - [x] **Melt Flow Rate (MFR)** measured at **230 °C / 2.16 kg** is the key industrial rheology specification. - [x] **PP-R** random copolymer (1 – 7 % ethylene) — used in **hot-water pipes** (Tm depression follows the **Flory equation**). - [x] **PP-B** block (impact) copolymer — has **EPR rubber phase** dispersed in iPP matrix; excellent low-T impact strength. - [x] **Modern processes:** gas-phase (Novolen/Unipol) preferred over slurry due to lower energy and higher catalyst productivity. - [x] **Key applications:** packaging, automotive, fibres, medical (autoclavable), pipes, electrical insulation. - [x] **PP vs PE:** PP has higher **Tm, modulus, and chemical resistance**, making it suitable for **hot-water pipes, autoclaved medical devices, and microwaveable containers**, where HDPE/LDPE cannot perform. - [x] **Atactic PP is removed as a by-product** in slurry processes; modern high-stereospecificity catalysts minimize aPP formation. - [x] **Crystallinity formula:** $\%C = (\Delta H_f / 209\text{ J/g}) \times 100$. - [x] **Hydrogen** is the industrial molecular-weight regulator (chain transfer agent) in Z-N PP. - [x] **Metallocene (single-site) catalysts** give **narrower MWD (PDI ≈ 2)** and enable production of **syndiotactic and high-impact PP** grades unattainable with classical Z-N catalysts.

topics.title: Polystyrene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polystyrene # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **POLYSTYRENE (PS)** is a synthetic **vinyl aromatic thermoplastic polymer** synthesized by the **chain-growth (addition) polymerization** of the monomer **styrene (vinyl benzene, C₆H₅–CH=CH₂)**. The repeating structural unit in the polymer backbone is the **styryl unit –[–CH₂–CH(C₆H₅)–]ₙ–**, where a pendant **phenyl (benzene) ring** is attached to every alternate backbone carbon. The commercial PS produced industrially is predominantly **atactic** (random stereochemistry), which renders it **amorphous, optically transparent, and glassy** at room temperature. The general molecular architecture of polystyrene can be represented as: $$ \text{n CH}_2\text{=CH(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)} \xrightarrow{\text{Radical/Aionic/Cationic}} -[\text{CH}_2-\text{CH(C}_6\texttext{H}_5\text{)}]_n- $$ where the degree of polymerization **n** typically ranges from **n ≈ 1,000 to 3,000** in commercial grades, giving a number-average molecular weight (Mₙ) in the range of **10⁵ to 3 × 10⁵ g/mol**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Molecular Lego Tower of Phenyl Plates"** 🧱 > > Imagine each styrene monomer as a **Lego brick with a small flat "phenyl-plate" attached on one side**. When the bricks are snapped together head-to-tail during polymerization, they form a long chain. However, because the phenyl-plate is bulky, it cannot be stacked in a perfectly ordered fashion — it jumbles randomly to the left or right. This **randomness of plate orientation** is exactly what makes commercial polystyrene **"atactic"** (without order), giving it its characteristic **clarity, brittleness, and rigidity**. **Simple Explanation for a First-Time Reader:** - Styrene is essentially a vinyl group (CH₂=CH–) with a benzene ring attached to one end. - When millions of these styrene molecules are chemically "zipped" together under heat and an initiator, they form long, spaghetti-like chains. - Because the benzene ring is bulky and non-polar, the chains cannot pack tightly into crystals — they remain disordered (amorphous), and the material is **transparent, hard, and brittle** like glass. - This is the same plastic used in **disposable coffee cup lids, CD cases, and protective foam packing peanuts**. --- ## 1.3 Classification of Polystyrene within Polymer Science | Property Class | Polystyrene's Position | |---|---| | **Origin** | Synthetic (man-made, petrochemical-derived) | | **Polymerization Type** | Chain-growth (addition) | | **Thermoplastic / Thermoset** | **Thermoplastic** (reprocessable by heat) | | **Tacticity** | Predominantly **Atactic** (amorphous) | | **Aromatic content** | **Aromatic** (pendant phenyl ring) | | **Polarity** | **Non-polar** (weak van der Waals forces) | | **Crystallinity** | **Amorphous** (X-ray halo, no sharp diffraction peaks) | | **Optical Behaviour** | Transparent in thin sections | --- ## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context > [!NOTE] > **Brief Timeline for Context:** > - **1839:** Eduard Simon first isolated styrene monomer from storax resin. > - **1930s:** IG Farben (Germany) and Dow Chemical (USA) began **commercial production of polystyrene** by free-radical bulk polymerization. > - **Today:** Polystyrene is one of the **top 5 commodity plastics worldwide**, with annual global production exceeding **15 million metric tonnes**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics Used by KTU Examiners:** > - **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):** ~ **100 °C** > - **Density (amorphous):** ~ **1.04 – 1.06 g/cm³** > - **Tensile Strength:** ~ **30 – 50 MPa** > - **Elongation at Break:** ~ **1 – 3 %** (brittle) > - **Refractive Index:** ~ **1.59 – 1.60** (excellent optical clarity) > - **Monomomer Boiling Point:** **145 °C** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Monomer — Styrene (Vinyl Benzene) Styrene (IUPAC: **ethenylbenzene**, C₈H₈) is the **sole monomer** for polystyrene. Its structure consists of a vinyl group (–CH=CH₂) directly bonded to a phenyl ring. $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5 - \text{CH} = \text{CH}_2 $$ **Key features of the styrene monomer that drive its polymerization behaviour:** 1. **Weakly electron-donating phenyl group:** The benzene ring stabilizes the propagating radical (or anion) by **resonance delocalization** into the aromatic ring. This makes styrene a "**stabilized monomer**" that polymerizes rapidly. 2. **Conjugation of vinyl with aromatic ring:** The double bond is in **conjugation** with the π-electrons of the phenyl group, which lowers the activation energy of polymerization. 3. **Steric bulk of phenyl ring:** Once incorporated into the chain, the pendant phenyl group creates significant **steric hindrance**, restricting chain mobility and raising T_g. --- ## 2.2 Polymerization Mechanisms for Polystyrene Polystyrene is one of the few polymers that can be produced by **all four major chain-growth mechanisms** — this is a frequently asked KTU question. ### (a) Free-Radical Polymerization (Industrial Standard) Steps: 1. **Initiation:** Thermal homolysis of an initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, AIBN) produces a primary radical R•, which adds across the C=C of styrene to form a styryl radical. $$ \text{R–O–O–R} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{R–O}^{\bullet} $$ $$ \text{R–O}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)} \longrightarrow \text{R–O–CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) $$ 2. **Propagation:** The styryl radical adds successive styrene monomers head-to-tail. $$ \sim\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)} \longrightarrow \sim\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) $$ 3. **Termination:** Mainly by **combination** (most common for styrene) or disproportionation. **Why this matters industrially:** Free-radical polymerization gives a **random, atactic** product — exactly the commercially useful transparent PS. ### (b) Anionic Polymerization (Living Polymerization → Syndiotactic PS) When initiated by strong bases such as n-butyllithium in the absence of oxygen and moisture, styrene undergoes **living anionic polymerization**. This allows precise control over molecular weight (Mₙ is set by the [Monomer]/[Initiator] ratio) and produces **low-polydispersity** PS (M_w/Mₙ → 1). ### (c) Cationic Polymerization Protonic or Lewis acids (H₂SO₄, BF₃, SnCl₄) initiate polymerization through a **carbocationic** mechanism. The phenyl group stabilizes the cation by resonance, making cationic polymerization feasible. ### (d) Ziegler–Natta / Metallocene Catalysis Stereospecific catalysts (e.g., TiCl₄/AlR₃) produce **isotactic or syndiotactic PS** with semi-crystalline character, higher T_g, and improved mechanical/thermal properties. > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Insight:** The same monomer (styrene) gives dramatically different polymers depending on the mechanism: > - **Radical → Atactic PS (amorphous, transparent, brittle)** > - **Anionic (with specific catalysts) → Syndiotactic PS (semicrystalline, high T_g ≈ 270 °C)** > - **Ziegler–Natta → Isotactic PS (semicrystalline, T_m ≈ 240 °C)** --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Repeating unit | –[CH₂–CH(C₆H₅)]– | – | Styryl unit (MW = 104.15 g/mol) | | 2 | Degree of Polymerization (D.P.) | $D.P. = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit | | 3 | Rate of Radical Polymerization | $R_p = k_p \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} [M]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Classical Smith-Ewart equation | | 4 | Kinetic Chain Length (ν) | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{k_p[M]}{(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$ | dimensionless | Average monomer units per radical | | 5 | Number-Average DP from ν (Termination by combination) | $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$ | – | Each chain has 2 radicals combined | | 6 | Number-Average DP from ν (Termination by disproportionation) | $\overline{X}_n = \nu$ | – | One chain per radical | | 7 | Polystyrene T_g (atactic) | $T_g \approx 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | Williams-Landel-Ferry reference polymer | | 8 | Polystyrene T_g – M relationship (Fox-Flory) | $T_g = T_g^{\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$ | K | K ≈ 1.7 × 10⁵ g/mol for PS | | 9 | Steric Parameter (Tacticity dependence) | $\sigma = 1 - 2P$ | – | σ = 0 → atactic; σ = 1 → isotactic | | 10 | Density of amorphous PS | $\rho \approx 1.05\ \text{g/cm}^3$ | g/cm³ | Compare crystalline PE: 1.00 g/cm³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM TRAP ALERT:** Always use $M_0$ = **104.15 g/mol** for the styryl repeating unit. A common student error is to use the molecular weight of styrene monomer (which is also 104.15) — coincidentally they are the same for vinyl polymers where the repeat unit = the monomer, but for cross-linked or condensation polymers this would be wrong. Confirm by the repeating unit structure, not the monomer. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transparent housings:** PS's high refractive index (1.59) and clarity make it ideal for **lenses, labware, and display panels**. - **Insulation:** Expanded polystyrene (EPS) foam has thermal conductivity of only **0.033 W/m·K**, used in building insulation. - **Food packaging:** The FDA-approved grades are used in yogurt cups, egg cartons, and disposable cutlery (though environmentally controversial). - **Electrical insulation:** Low dielectric loss makes PS useful in capacitors and high-frequency electronics. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number-Average Degree of Polymerization (X̄ₙ) from Kinetic Chain Length This is a **classic KTU derivation question** (frequently worth 7 marks). **Given:** - Steady-state radical concentration: $[M^{\bullet}]_{ss} = \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$ - Rate of polymerization: $R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]_{ss}$ - Rate of initiation: $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ - Kinetic chain length: $\nu = \dfrac{\text{rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{rate of radical generation}} = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i}$ **Step 1 — Substitute the expressions for R_p and R_i:** $$ \nu = \dfrac{k_p [M] \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}}{2 f k_d [I]} $$ **Step 2 — Simplify the radical and initiator terms:** $$ \nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 f k_d [I]} \cdot \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} $$ $$ \nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}} $$ **Step 3 — Final compact form of kinetic chain length:** $$ \boxed{\nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}} $$ **Step 4 — Relate ν to the number-average degree of polymerization X̄ₙ:** For **termination by combination** (predominant in styrene free-radical polymerization): - Two growing chains merge → **1 polymer molecule** per 2 radicals consumed. $$ \overline{X}_n = 2\nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}} $$ For **termination by disproportionation**: - Each radical yields **1 chain** (H-atom transfer). $$ \overline{X}_n = \nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Higher monomer concentration and higher $k_p$ favour longer chains (higher Mₙ), while higher initiator concentration and faster termination depress the molecular weight. This is exactly why industrial suspension polymerization of styrene carefully controls [I] and temperature to target Mₙ ≈ 200,000 g/mol. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Fox–Flory Equation Applied to Polystyrene The **Fox–Flory equation** relates the glass transition temperature of a polymer to its number-average molecular weight: $$ T_g = T_g^{\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n} $$ where: - $T_g^{\infty}$ = T_g at infinite molecular weight (≈ 100 °C for atactic PS) - $K$ ≈ **1.7 × 10⁵ g·K/mol** (a polymer-specific constant for PS) **Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):** > **Question:** Calculate the T_g of a polystyrene sample with $\overline{M}_n = 1.0 \times 10^4$ g/mol. Given $T_g^{\infty} = 373\ \text{K}$ and $K = 1.7 \times 10^5\ \text{g·K/mol}$. **Solution:** $$ T_g = T_g^{\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n} $$ $$ T_g = 373\ \text{K} - \dfrac{1.7 \times 10^5\ \text{g·K/mol}}{1.0 \times 10^4\ \text{g/mol}} $$ $$ T_g = 373\ \text{K} - 17\ \text{K} $$ $$ \boxed{T_g = 356\ \text{K} = 83\ ^\circ\text{C}} $$ > **Interpretation:** Low-Mₙ PS has noticeably lower T_g because the chain ends have greater free volume, increasing segmental mobility. As Mₙ → ∞, T_g asymptotically approaches 100 °C. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Tacticity vs Properties of Polystyrene | Property | Atactic PS (aPS) | Isotactic PS (iPS) | Syndiotactic PS (sPS) | |---|---|---|---| | **Catalyst required** | Radical (no stereocontrol) | Ziegler–Natta / Metallocene | Metallocene (e.g., Cp*₂ZrCl₂/MAO) | | **Tacticity parameter σ** | 0 (random) | 1 (perfect isotactic) | –1 (perfect syndiotactic) | | **Crystallinity** | Amorphous | Semi-crystalline | Semi-crystalline | | **T_g / T_m** | T_g ≈ 100 °C | T_m ≈ 240 °C | T_m ≈ 270 °C, T_g ≈ 100 °C | | **Optical clarity** | Transparent | Opaque | Opaque | | **Heat resistance** | Low (softens at 100 °C) | High | Very high | | **Industrial volume** | > 90 % of PS market | Niche | Niche (Dow Versify, Idemitsu) | --- ## 3.4 Mass-Molecular Weight Conversion for Polystyrene A student should be able to convert between D.P., Mₙ, and the mass of a polymer sample. **Given:** For a PS sample, $n = 1500$ (number of repeat units per chain), $M_0 = 104.15$ g/mol, sample mass = 100 g. Find the number of polymer molecules. **Step 1 — Molecular weight of one chain:** $$ M_{\text{chain}} = n \times M_0 = 1500 \times 104.15 $$ $$ M_{\text{chain}} = 1.56 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol} $$ **Step 2 — Number of moles of chains:** $$ N_{\text{chains}} = \dfrac{\text{mass}}{M_{\text{chain}}} = \dfrac{100\ \text{g}}{1.56 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}} $$ $$ N_{\text{chains}} = 6.41 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol} $$ **Step 3 — Number of molecules:** $$ N = N_{\text{chains}} \times N_A = 6.41 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} $$ $$ \boxed{N \approx 3.86 \times 10^{20}\ \text{molecules}} $$ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Polymerization Reaction Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Styrene Monomer
C6H5-CH=CH2] --> B[Initiator
BPO or AIBN] B --> C[Heat 80-120 degC] C --> D[Primary Radical R-O*] D --> E[Styryl Radical
R-O-CH2-CH*(C6H5)] E --> F[Propagation Step
Add n Styrene Units] F --> G[Polystyrene Chain
-CH2-CH(C6H5)n-] G --> H{Termination Mode} H --> I[Combination
2 chains fuse] H --> J[Disproportionation
H atom transfer] I --> K[Final Atactic PS
MW 100000-300000 g/mol] J --> K ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Polystyrene Families ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RawMaterial[Feedstock] A1[Styrene Monomer] --> A2[Co-monomers and Additives] end subgraph Process[Polymerization Routes] B1[Free Radical
Bulk Suspension] --> C1[Atactic PS] B2[Ziegler Natta
or Metallocene] --> C2[Isotactic PS] B3[Syndiospecific
Metallocene] --> C3[Syndiotactic PS] end A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A1 --> B3 A2 --> B1 C1 --> D1[General Purpose PS
GPPS] C1 --> D2[High Impact PS
HIPS via rubber blending] C1 --> D3[Expanded PS
EPS via foaming agent] C1 --> D4[Extruded PS Foam
XPS] C1 --> D5[Syntactic Foam] D1 --> E1[Packaging
CD Cases, Labware] D2 --> E2[Appliance Housings
Refrigerator Liners] D3 --> E3[Insulation
Cups, Protective Packing] D4 --> E4[Building Thermal
Insulation Boards] C2 --> E5[High Heat Applications] C3 --> E6[Engineering Plastics] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Pendant Phenyl Group Orientation in PS Chains ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AtacticPS[Atactic Polystyrene - Random Phenyl Orientation] A1[CH2] --> A2[CH-Ph right] A2 --> A3[CH2] A3 --> A4[CH-Ph left] A4 --> A5[CH2] A5 --> A6[CH-Ph right] end subgraph IsotacticPS[Isotactic Polystyrene - All Phenyl Same Side] I1[CH2] --> I2[CH-Ph up] I2 --> I3[CH2] I3 --> I4[CH-Ph up] I4 --> I5[CH2] I5 --> I6[CH-Ph up] end subgraph SyndiotacticPS[Syndiotactic Polystyrene - Alternating Sides] S1[CH2] --> S2[CH-Ph up] S2 --> S3[CH2] S3 --> S4[CH-Ph down] S4 --> S5[CH2] S5 --> S6[CH-Ph up] end ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow: Suspension Polymerization of Styrene (Industrial) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Styrene Monomer
Purity 99.5 percent] --> P2[Mixing with Water
and Suspension Agent PVA] P3[Initiator
Benzoyl Peroxide] --> P2 P2 --> P4[Reactor 80-90 degC
Stirred Suspension] P4 --> P5[Polymer Beads Formed
0.1 to 2 mm diameter] P5 --> P6[Centrifuge and Wash] P6 --> P7[Drying Tunnel
Hot Air 60 degC] P7 --> P8[Sieving by Size] P8 --> P9[Final PS Beads
Ready for Compounding] P4 --> P10[Unreacted Monomer
Recovery by Steam Stripping] P10 --> P1 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Model Style]** **Q1. Define the term "tacticity" and explain how it applies to polystyrene. Mention the type of tacticity found in commercially produced PS.** **Model Answer:** Tacticity refers to the **stereoregular arrangement of side groups** (substituents) along the polymer backbone. In vinyl polymers, the chiral centre is every alternate backbone carbon bearing a substituent. For polystyrene, the substituent is the bulky phenyl (–C₆H₅) group: - **Isotactic PS:** All phenyl groups on the **same side** of the backbone plane. - **Syndiotactic PS:** Phenyl groups alternate sides in a regular pattern. - **Atactic PS:** Phenyl groups are **randomly arranged**. Commercially produced polystyrene (by free-radical polymerization) is **atactic**, which makes it **amorphous, transparent, and brittle**. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. Differentiate between the three industrial variants: GPPS, HIPS, and EPS.** **Model Answer:** | Property | GPPS | HIPS | EPS | |---|---|---|---| | **Full name** | General Purpose Polystyrene | High Impact Polystyrene | Expanded Polystyrene | | **Composition** | Pure PS homopolymer | PS + ~5–10 % polybutadiene rubber | PS + blowing agent (pentane) | | **Key feature** | Transparent, rigid, brittle | Opaque, tough, impact-resistant | Foamed, low density (0.03–0.05 g/cm³) | | **Application** | CD cases, labware | Refrigerator liners, toys | Packaging peanuts, insulation | | **Production** | Bulk / suspension polymerization | Bulk polymerization with rubber | Suspension polymerization + steam expansion | **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]` **(a)** Discuss in detail the **free-radical polymerization mechanism** for the synthesis of polystyrene. Include balanced equations for initiation, propagation, and termination steps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A polystyrene sample has a number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n = 2.0 \times 10^5$ g/mol. The monomer is styrene (C₈H₈, M₀ = 104.15 g/mol). Calculate the **degree of polymerization**, the **number of chains per gram**, and explain why commercial PS has a broad molecular weight distribution. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Free-Radical Polymerization of Styrene (7 marks)** **Step 1 – Initiation (2 marks):** A peroxide initiator such as benzoyl peroxide undergoes homolytic cleavage on heating: $$ (\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CO–O})_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta, 60-80\ ^\circ\text{C}} 2\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^{\bullet} $$ The benzoyloxy radical then adds to a styrene monomer: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO–CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) $$ **Step 2 – Propagation (3 marks):** The chain radical adds successive styrene monomers head-to-tail. The chain end remains a stabilized benzylic radical: $$ \sim\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) + \text{CH}_2\text{=CH(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)} \longrightarrow \sim\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) $$ The phenyl group **resonance-stabilizes** the propagating radical, which is why styrene polymerizes readily via free-radical mechanism. The propagation rate constant $k_p$ for styrene at 60 °C is ≈ **176 L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹**. **Step 3 – Termination (2 marks):** Termination in PS occurs predominantly by **combination** (90 %), with minor disproportionation: - **Combination:** Two growing chains fuse to give one dead chain. $$ \sim\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) + \overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)\text{–CH}_2\sim \longrightarrow \sim\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{CH}_2\sim $$ - **Disproportionation:** H-atom transfer between two chains. $$ 2\ \sim\text{CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) \longrightarrow \sim\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) + \sim\text{CH}=\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5) $$ **(b) Calculations (7 marks)** **Step 1 – Degree of polymerization (2 marks):** $$ \overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}}{104.15\ \text{g/mol}} $$ $$ \boxed{\overline{X}_n \approx 1920\ \text{repeat units per chain}} $$ **Step 2 – Number of chains per gram (3 marks):** $$ N_{\text{chains/g}} = \dfrac{N_A}{\overline{M}_n} = \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}}{2.0 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}} $$ $$ N_{\text{chains/g}} = 3.01 \times 10^{18}\ \text{chains per gram of PS} $$ **Step 3 – Why polydispersity is broad (2 marks):** Commercial radical PS has $\overline{M}_w/\overline{M}_n \approx 1.5$–**3.0** because: - Initiation is stochastic (random chain start times). - Termination events (combination/disproportionation) occur at different chain lengths. - Chain transfer to monomer, polymer, and solvent adds further heterogeneity. This gives a **broad molecular weight distribution** (high polydispersity index, PDI), which is why anionic "living" polymerization is preferred when narrow-distribution PS is needed. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Compare the **structure, properties, and applications** of **atactic, isotactic, and syndiotactic polystyrene**. Discuss why isotactic and syndiotactic PS require special catalysts while atactic PS can be made by simple free-radical polymerization. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A student dissolves 1.5 g of polystyrene in toluene and measures the viscosity. Using the **Mark–Houwink equation** $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$ with K = 1.7 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g and α = 0.69, determine the **viscosity-average molecular weight** $[\eta]$ measured as **0.72 dL/g**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Comparison of Polystyrene Tacticity (7 marks)** | Aspect | Atactic PS | Isotactic PS | Syndiotactic PS | |---|---|---|---| | Catalyst | None (radical auto-stereorandom) | Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄/AlR₃) or Metallocene | Syndiospecific metallocene (e.g., Cp*₂ZrCl₂/MAO) | | Phenyl orientation | Random | All one side | Alternating sides | | Crystallinity | Amorphous | Semi-crystalline | Semi-crystalline | | T_g | ~100 °C | ~100 °C | ~100 °C | | T_m | None | ~240 °C | ~270 °C | | Optical | Transparent | Opaque | Opaque | | Heat resistance | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | **Why special catalysts are needed (3 marks):** The phenyl group is bulky and does not naturally orient in a regular way during free-radical polymerization. To enforce stereoregular addition, the catalyst must provide a **chiral environment** at the active site. Ziegler–Natta catalysts with chiral ligands (or chiral metallocenes) pre-organize the monomer insertion step, leading to **stereospecific chain growth**. Without such catalysts, the random addition yields atactic PS — which is fortunately the most commercially useful form for transparent applications. **Applications (2 marks):** - Atactic PS: packaging, disposable items, labware. - Isotactic/Syndiotactic PS: high-heat engineering parts, automotive under-hood components, electrical connectors. **(b) Mark–Houwink Calculation (7 marks)** **Given:** - $[\eta] = 0.72\ \text{dL/g}$ - $K = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{dL/g}$ - $\alpha = 0.69$ **Step 1 – Rearrange the Mark–Houwink equation to solve for M:** $$ [\eta] = K M^{\alpha} \quad\Rightarrow\quad M^{\alpha} = \dfrac{[\eta]}{K} $$ $$ M = \left(\dfrac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/\alpha} $$ **Step 2 – Substitute numerical values (3 marks):** $$ \dfrac{[\eta]}{K} = \dfrac{0.72}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}} = 4235.3\ \text{dL/g ÷ (dL/g)} = 4235.3 $$ **Step 3 – Raise to the power 1/α (2 marks):** $$ M_{\eta} = (4235.3)^{1/0.69} = (4235.3)^{1.449} $$ Taking logarithms: $$ \log_{10}(M_{\eta}) = 1.449 \times \log_{10}(4235.3) $$ $$ \log_{10}(M_{\eta}) = 1.449 \times 3.6268 = 5.2553 $$ $$ M_{\eta} = 10^{5.2553} \approx 1.80 \times 10^{5} $$ $$ \boxed{M_{\eta} \approx 1.80 \times 10^5\ \text{g/mol}} $$ **Step 4 – Interpretation (2 marks):** This is the **viscosity-average molecular weight** ($M_\eta$). It lies between $M_n$ and $M_w$ and is weighted towards higher molecular weights because of the exponent α ≈ 0.69. For this PS, $M_\eta \approx 180{,}000$ g/mol, which is a typical commercial grade. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** for K in the Mark–Houwink equation — K must be in the same dL/g units as [η]. > 2. **Mixing up $M_0$ and $M_{\text{monomer}}$** — for PS they are numerically equal, but the principle is to always use the **repeat unit molecular weight** for D.P. calculations. > 3. **Stating atactic PS is "crystalline"** — it is amorphous; only stereoregular PS is semicrystalline. > 4. **Confusing the glass transition temperature (T_g = 100 °C) with the melting point** — atactic PS has no T_m, only T_g. > 5. **Omitting the role of chain transfer** in determining MWD — full marks require acknowledging that chain transfer to monomer and polymer broadens the polydispersity of commercial PS. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **⚡ HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Polystyrene** ### Core Identity - **Monomer:** Styrene (vinyl benzene, C₆H₅–CH=CH₂, MW = 104.15 g/mol) - **Repeat unit:** –[CH₂–CH(C₆H₅)]–ₙ– - **Polymer type:** Vinyl aromatic thermoplastic - **Tacticity:** Atactic (commercial), isotactic or syndiotactic (special catalysts) ### Key Numerical Values - T_g (atactic) = **100 °C** - Density (amorphous) = **1.05 g/cm³** - Refractive index = **1.59** - Tensile strength = **30–50 MPa** - Elongation at break = **1–3 %** (brittle) ### Polymerization Mechanisms - **Free-radical** (industrial): initiator (BPO/AIBN) → atactic PS - **Anionic** (living): n-BuLi → narrow MWD PS - **Cationic:** Lewis acids, e.g., BF₃ - **Ziegler–Natta / Metallocene:** stereoregular PS ### Key Equations - $R_p = k_p \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} [M]$ - $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$ (combination) or $\nu$ (disproportionation) - $T_g = T_g^{\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$ (Fox–Flory) - $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$ (Mark–Houwink) - $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ ### Commercial Variants - **GPPS** — clear, brittle - **HIPS** — rubber-toughened - **EPS** — foamed insulation - **XPS** — extruded foam boards - **SPS** — syndiotactic engineering plastic ### Major Applications - Disposable packaging, CD cases, labware (GPPS) - Refrigerator liners, toys (HIPS) - Thermal insulation, protective packaging (EPS) - High-heat engineering parts (iPS, sPS) ### Exam-Ready Definitions - **Atactic:** Random side-group arrangement → amorphous - **Syndiotactic:** Alternating side-group arrangement → semi-crystalline - **Isotactic:** Same-side arrangement → semi-crystalline - **K value:** Polymerization rate constant ratio for monomer reactivity - **Tacticity parameter σ = 1 − 2P**, where P = probability of isotactic placement > **End of Polystyrene Notes — Module 1, PCPOT205**

topics.title: Polyacrylonitrile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polyacrylonitrile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)** is a synthetic, **semicrystalline, atactic vinyl homopolymer** derived from the chain-growth polymerization of the monomer **acrylonitrile** (vinyl cyanide, propenenitrile, $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CN}$). The polymer chain consists of a saturated carbon backbone bearing a polar **nitrile (–C$\equiv$N)** pendant group on every alternate carbon atom.

The repeat unit is:

$$-\!\left[\text{CH}_2\!-\!\text{CH}(\text{CN})\right]\!-\!_{n}$$

with a **theoretical repeat unit molar mass of 53.06 g·mol$^{-1}$**. The high electronegativity of the nitrile group and its strong dipole interactions render PAN a **strongly cohesive, hydrogen-bond-accepting** polymer with a glass transition temperature ($T_g$) in the range **85–105 °C** and a melting temperature ($T_m$) of **~317 °C** — although it undergoes **thermo-oxidative degradation (cyclization)** before it can be melt-processed, which is a defining feature of its thermal behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** PAN is the **single most important commercial precursor for carbon fiber**, accounting for **over 90%** of worldwide carbon fiber production. Always remember the three-step thermal conversion: **Stabilization → Carbonization → Graphitization**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Wool That Turns to Steel"

Imagine a long string of identical **paper clips** hooked end-to-end — that is your PAN chain, with every "clip" carrying a tiny magnet-like **–C$\equiv$N dipole**. While the chain itself is soft and resembles wool (which is why PAN fibers are sold as "**acrylic**" — brand names *Orlon, Acrilan, Dralon, Courtelle*), the moment you bake the fiber in **air at 250 °C**, the magnets lock together into a rigid ladder structure (cyclization). If you then push the temperature to **1500 °C in nitrogen**, the dangling atoms (H, N) escape, leaving behind a near-pure **carbon wire** — stronger, for its weight, than steel. That is the magic of PAN: the same polymer is a sweater today and the body of a fighter jet tomorrow.

### 1.3 Nomenclature Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Monomer synonyms (KTU Examiner's must-know):** Acrylonitrile = Vinyl Cyanide = 2-Propenenitrile = Cyanoethylene = $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHCN}$  
> **Polymer synonyms:** Polyacrylonitrile = PAN = Orlon (historical trade name)

| Property | Value | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| CAS Number of Monomer | 107-13-1 | IUPAC Registry |
| Repeat Unit Formula | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_3\text{N}$ | – |
| Molecular Weight of Repeat Unit | **53.06 g/mol** | – |
| Density (amorphous) | **1.184 g/cm³** | ASTM D792 |
| Refractive Index, $n_D$ | **1.514 – 1.520** | – |
| Glass Transition ($T_g$) | **85–105 °C** | DSC |
| Melting Point ($T_m$) | **~317 °C** (decomposes) | DTA |
| LOI (Limiting Oxygen Index) | **~18 %** | ASTM D2863 |
| Carbon Yield on Pyrolysis | **~50–55 %** | – |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind PAN's Unusual Properties — A Structured Analysis

PAN occupies a special corner of the vinyl polymer map because of the **nitrile group's dual nature**: it is both **strongly electron-withdrawing** (by induction) and **weakly electron-withdrawing** (by resonance, through the C=C of the monomer). This electronic asymmetry drives every single property of PAN.

**Step 1 — Monomer Reactivity.**  
Acrylonitrile is an **electron-poor vinyl monomer** (Q = 0.48, e = 1.23 in the Alfrey–Price scheme). The nitrile group stabilises the propagating radical through **resonance delocalisation** and also inductively destabilises carbocations but stabilises carbanions. Net effect: PAN is **easily made by free-radical polymerisation**, harder by cationic, and almost ideal for **anionic** polymerisation.

**Step 2 — Tacticity.**  
Free-radical polymerisation of acrylonitrile is essentially **stereorandom (atactic)** because the nitrile group is **linear (cylindrical symmetry) and not bulky enough to enforce isotactic or syndiotactic placement**. Catalytic (coordination) Ziegler–Natta or metallocene catalysts, however, can give highly isotactic PAN, which is semicrystalline.

**Step 3 — Strong Dipole–Dipole Cohesion.**  
Each repeat unit carries a –C$\equiv$N dipole of moment **~3.6 D**. Adjacent chains align antiparallel to maximise dipole–dipole interaction, producing **strong intermolecular forces without hydrogen bonding**. This explains PAN's high $T_g$, its insolubility in almost all common organic solvents, and its excellent barrier properties.

**Step 4 — Thermo-oxidative Cyclisation.**  
Heating PAN in air at **~200–300 °C** triggers a **conjugated ladder polymer** (the famous "PAN ladder") via nitrile–nitrile cyclisation, releasing heat (exothermic, ~200 kJ/mol of repeat unit). This is **why PAN cannot be melt-spun like polyethylene or polypropylene**; the cyclisation occurs before flow, so PAN is processed exclusively by **solution (wet/dry-jet wet) spinning** in polar aprotic solvents (DMF, DMSO, NaSCN/H$_2$O, ethylene carbonate, or ionic liquids).

**Step 5 — Carbonisation Pathway.**  
The stabilised ladder polymer, when heated to **1000–1500 °C in inert atmosphere (N$_2$ or Ar)**, expels HCN, NH$_3$, H$_2$, CO$_2$, leaving a **turbostratic carbon sheet**. Further heating to **>2500 °C** (graphitisation) gives high-modulus carbon fiber with tensile strength **3–7 GPa** and modulus **230–600 GPa**.

### 2.2 Polymerisation Mechanisms Practically Used for PAN

PAN is industrially produced almost exclusively by **free-radical polymerisation**, but the academic and KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to know all four routes:

1. **Free Radical (dominant route):** AIBN or BPO initiator, 50–80 °C, solution (DMF/DMSO) or aqueous-slurry (precipitation) polymerisation. Yields atactic, high-MW PAN.
2. **Anionic (living):** BuLi, NaCN, Grignard reagents. Gives narrow-MWD, stereoregular (mostly isotactic) PAN.
3. **Coordination / Insertion:** Ziegler–Natta (TiCl$_3$/AlR$_3$) or metallocenes. Yields highly isotactic, semicrystalline PAN.
4. **Plasma / Radiation-induced:** Used for surface grafting and specialty applications; not for bulk PAN.

### 2.3 Copolymers of PAN — Why Industry Never Uses Pure PAN Homopolymer

Pure PAN is **not dyeable** (no dye site) and **brittle in fiber form**. Industry therefore uses **copolymers of acrylonitrile with 5–15 % of one or two comonomers**:

- **Methyl acrylate (MA):** improves dyeability, flexibility, and processing.
- **Itaconic acid (IA):** accelerates oxidative stabilisation; lowers cyclisation temperature.
- **Styrene, vinyl acetate, methyl methacrylate:** modify $T_g$ and stiffness.

These are called **modacrylics** if comonomer content is 35–85 %, otherwise **acrylic fibers** (<15 % comonomer).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-locked** for KTU PCPOT205. Memorise the form, not the symbols.

| Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Degree of Polymerisation (number average) | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit (**53.06 g/mol** for PAN) |
| Number-average DP for chain polymerisation (termination by combination) | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{2 R_p}{R_t + R_{tr}}$ | $R_p, R_t, R_{tr}$ are rates of propagation, termination, chain transfer |
| Number-average DP for chain polymerisation (termination by disproportionation) | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr}}$ | PAN is combination-dominant |
| Mayo–Lewis Copolymer Equation (instantaneous composition) | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \dfrac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}$ | $r_1, r_2$ = monomer reactivity ratios |
| Terminal model reactivity ratio | $r_1 = \dfrac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}$ | For AN + MA: $r_{AN} \approx 1.02$, $r_{MA} \approx 0.70$ |
| Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (intrinsic viscosity) | $[\eta] = K \overline{M}_v^{a}$ | For PAN in DMF at 25 °C: $K = 2.33 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g, $a = 0.75$ |
| Flory–Fox relation for $T_g$ of copolymer | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | $w_i$ = weight fraction, $T$ in **Kelvin** |
| Carothers equation (step-growth, less relevant for PAN but in syllabus) | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = extent of reaction |
| Theoretical carbon yield from PAN | $\%\text{C}_{\text{theor}} = \dfrac{36}{53.06} \times 100 \approx 67.8\,\%$ | Real yield 50–55 % (losses as HCN, NH$_3$) |
| Mass loss on full carbonisation | $\Delta m = m_0\!\left(1 - \dfrac{12}{53.06}\right) \approx 0.774\,m_0$ | Each repeat unit retains 12 of 53 g as carbon |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PAN underpins four multi-billion-dollar industries:

1. **Carbon fiber composites** — aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350), wind-turbine blades, pressure vessels, sports goods.
2. **Acrylic textile fibers** — sweaters, socks, blankets, carpets, awnings (replaces wool in many uses).
3. **Hollow-fiber membranes** — ultrafiltration, hemodialysis, water purification.
4. **Precursor for activated carbon, hard carbon (sodium-ion battery anodes), and carbon molecular sieves.**

> [!TIP]
> **One-line exam answer (KTU-style):** *PAN is a vinyl addition polymer of acrylonitrile, semicrystalline, atactic (FRP), with strong dipole interactions due to the pendant nitrile group, and is the dominant precursor for high-performance carbon fiber.*

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Carbon Yield from PAN

A KTU-favourite 7- to 14-mark derivation. Show every algebraic step clearly.

**Given:** Repeat unit of PAN = $\text{C}_3\text{H}_3\text{N}$, repeat-unit molar mass = $M_0 = 53.06\ \text{g/mol}$.  
**Assumption (ideal):** On complete pyrolysis, **all nitrogen leaves as HCN/NH$_3$ and all hydrogen leaves as H$_2$**, leaving a residue of **pure carbon** ($\text{C}$).

**Step 1 — Mass of carbon per repeat unit.**  
A single repeat unit $\text{C}_3\text{H}_3\text{N}$ contains **3 carbon atoms**, each of atomic mass **12.01 g/mol**.

$$
m_C \;=\; 3 \times 12.01 \;=\; 36.03\ \text{g per mol of repeat units}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical carbon mass fraction.**

$$
\%C_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_C}{M_0} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{36.03}{53.06} \times 100
$$

$$
\%C_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; 0.6791 \times 100 \;\approx\; 67.9\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Mass retained on full carbonisation (per gram of PAN).**

$$
m_{\text{retained}} \;=\; \dfrac{36.03}{53.06} \;\approx\; 0.679\ \text{g of C per g of PAN}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass lost on full carbonisation.**

$$
\Delta m_{\text{lost}} \;=\; 1 - 0.679 \;\approx\; 0.321\ \text{g per g of PAN}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**  
Real industrial carbon fiber yield is **50–55 %**, lower than the 67.9 % theoretical maximum, because some carbon escapes as **HCN, CH$_4$, CO, and CO$_2$** during the carbonisation step. The itaconic-acid comonomer in commercial PAN raises the *real* yield by **3–5 %** because it promotes early cyclisation and reduces volatile carbon loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU valuation tip:** Examiners give **full 7 marks** only when you (a) write $M_0 = 53.06$ explicitly, (b) state the assumption about complete loss of N and H, and (c) comment on the gap between theoretical and real yield.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mayo–Lewis Copolymer Composition for AN + MA System

The Mayo–Lewis terminal model is the **most-tested** copolymer equation in PCPOT205.

**Given feed:** Acrylonitrile ($M_1$) + Methyl acrylate ($M_2$) with reactivity ratios $r_1 = 1.02$ and $r_2 = 0.70$.  
**Feed mol fraction:** $f_1 = 0.85$ (i.e. $[M_1]/([M_1] + [M_2]) = 0.85$, so $[M_2]/[M_1] = 0.176$).

**Step 1 — Write the Mayo–Lewis equation.**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f_2$.**

$$
f_2 \;=\; 1 - f_1 \;=\; 1 - 0.85 \;=\; 0.15
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term in the numerator and denominator.**

Numerator term 1: $r_1 f_1^2 = 1.02 \times (0.85)^2 = 1.02 \times 0.7225 = 0.7370$

Numerator term 2: $f_1 f_2 = 0.85 \times 0.15 = 0.1275$

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; 0.7370 + 0.1275 \;=\; 0.8645
$$

Denominator term 3: $r_2 f_2^2 = 0.70 \times (0.15)^2 = 0.70 \times 0.0225 = 0.01575$

$$
\text{Denominator} \;=\; 0.7370 + 2(0.1275) + 0.01575 \;=\; 0.7370 + 0.2550 + 0.01575 \;=\; 1.0078
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $F_1$, the instantaneous copolymer mole fraction of AN.**

$$
F_1 \;=\; \dfrac{0.8645}{1.0078} \;\approx\; 0.858
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The copolymer is **slightly richer in AN (0.858) than the feed (0.850)** because $r_1 \cdot r_2 \approx 0.71 < 1$, indicating a **slight azeotropic drift** toward the AN side. For $r_1 \cdot r_2 \to 1$, the system would be ideal-random; here it is **close to but not exactly** ideal-random.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise:** Azeotropic composition in Mayo–Lewis occurs when $f_1 = \dfrac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}$. For AN + MA, $f_{1,\text{az}} = \dfrac{1 - 0.70}{2 - 1.02 - 0.70} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.28} \approx 1.07$ — i.e. **no real azeotrope exists** (azeotrope would be at $> 100\,\%$, impossible). Hence the system shows a **composition drift**, which is why industry uses **semicontinuous monomer addition** to keep $f_1$ constant.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Estimating PAN Molecular Weight from Intrinsic Viscosity (Mark–Houwink)

**Given:** A PAN sample dissolved in DMF at 25 °C has intrinsic viscosity $[\eta] = 1.25\ \text{dL/g}$. Using Mark–Houwink constants $K = 2.33 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{dL/g}$ and $a = 0.75$ for this solvent/temperature pair.

**Step 1 — Mark–Houwink equation.**

$$
[\eta] \;=\; K \cdot \overline{M}_v^{a}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
1.25 \;=\; (2.33 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot \overline{M}_v^{0.75}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\overline{M}_v^{0.75}$.**

$$
\overline{M}_v^{0.75} \;=\; \dfrac{1.25}{2.33 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.25}{0.000233}
$$

$$
\overline{M}_v^{0.75} \;=\; 5\,364.8
$$

**Step 4 — Take both sides to the power $1/0.75 = 4/3$.**

$$
\overline{M}_v \;=\; (5\,364.8)^{4/3}
$$

Let us compute $(5364.8)^{1/3}$ first, then raise to the 4th power.

$$
(5\,364.8)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 17.49
$$

$$
\overline{M}_v \;=\; (17.49)^{4} \;\approx\; 9.35 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\overline{M}_v \;\approx\; 9.35 \times 10^{4}\ \text{g/mol} \;=\; 93\,500\ \text{Da}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to degree of polymerisation.**

$$
\overline{X}_n \;\approx\; \dfrac{9.35 \times 10^{4}}{53.06} \;\approx\; 1\,763
$$

This corresponds to a **medium-MW fiber-grade PAN**, suitable for textile applications. Carbon-fiber-grade PAN typically has $\overline{M}_v$ in the range **(1.5 – 5) $\times 10^{5}$ g/mol**, i.e. $\overline{X}_n \approx 3\,000$ – $9\,500$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why viscosity-average, not number-average?** Viscosity experiments are quick, need only ~50 mg of sample, and $\overline{M}_v$ is closer to $\overline{M}_w$ (which dominates fiber mechanical properties) than $\overline{M}_n$ is.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — "When to Use What Equation" (Decision Table)

| Scenario in Exam | Correct Formula | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Asked: "DP of chain polymer, terminates by combination" | $\overline{X}_n = 2R_p/(R_t + R_{tr})$ | Students forget factor 2 |
| Asked: "Copolymer composition from feed" | Mayo–Lewis (full form) | Confusing $F_1$ with $f_1$ |
| Asked: "Theoretical carbon yield from PAN" | $\dfrac{3 \times 12}{53.06} \times 100$ | Using monomer MW (53.06) but not the repeat unit |
| Asked: "MW from viscosity" | Mark–Houwink, raise to $1/a$ | Forgetting to invert $a$ |
| Asked: "Why PAN is not melt-processable?" | Cyclisation below $T_m$ | Saying "high $T_m$" instead of "decomposes before flow" |
| Asked: "PAN solvent for spinning" | DMF / DMSO / NaSCN(aq) | Saying acetone (PAN is insoluble in acetone) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define Polyacrylonitrile. Write the structure of its repeat unit and name the monomer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) is a **synthetic, semicrystalline vinyl addition polymer** obtained by the chain-growth polymerisation of the monomer **acrylonitrile (vinyl cyanide, $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH}\!-\!\text{CN}$)**.

$$
\text{Repeat unit:}\quad -\!\left[\text{CH}_2\!-\!\text{CH}(\text{CN})\right]\!-\!_{n}
$$

Molecular weight of repeat unit = **53.06 g/mol**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
List **any four** important applications of Polyacrylonitrile.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
(i) **Acrylic textile fibers** (Orlon, Acrilan, Dralon) for sweaters, carpets and blankets.  
(ii) **Precursor for high-performance carbon fiber** (aerospace, wind turbines, sports goods).  
(iii) **Hollow-fiber membranes** for ultrafiltration, hemodialysis, and water purification.  
(iv) **Precursor for activated carbon and hard carbon anodes** for sodium-ion batteries.  
(v) **Copolymers**: SAN, ABS, modacrylics for engineering plastics.  
(Any four → 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module-1 question offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** of equal marks. Both are presented below for parallel practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Synthesis, Mechanism and Carbon-Fiber Conversion*

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **free-radical polymerisation of acrylonitrile** to give PAN. Include the three mechanistic steps (initiation, propagation, termination) with chemical equations and state the role of the solvent and temperature.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reaction overview (1 mark).**  
Acrylonitrile (AN, $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHCN}$) undergoes **chain-growth addition polymerisation** initiated by a thermal free-radical initiator such as **AIBN** (azobisisobutyronitrile) or **BPO** (benzoyl peroxide). Solvents commonly used are **DMF, DMSO, or aqueous NaSCN**; temperature **50–80 °C**; reaction time 2–6 h.

**Step 2 — Initiation (2 marks).**  
AIBN decomposes homolytically:

$$
\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C(CN)\!-\!N\!=\!N\!-\!C(CN)(CH}_3\text{)}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; 2\,(\text{CH}_3\text{)}_2\dot{\text{C}}\text{CN} \;+\; \text{N}_2
$$

The resulting cyano-isopropyl radical ($\text{R}^{\bullet}$) attacks the AN double bond:

$$
\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHCN} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R}\!-\!\text{CH}_2\!-\!\dot{\text{CH}}\text{CN} \quad (\equiv \text{M}_1^{\bullet})
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation (2 marks).**

$$
\text{M}_1^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHCN} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{M}_2^{\bullet}
$$

$$
\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHCN} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{M}_{n+1}^{\bullet}
$$

The nitrile group delocalises the radical, lowering $k_p$ slightly compared to styrene but increasing selectivity.

**Step 4 — Termination (2 marks).** Termination occurs predominantly by **combination** (because of the stable benzylic-type radical formed after the –CN):

$$
\text{M}_m^{\bullet} + \text{M}_n^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{M}_{m+n}\quad(\text{dead polymer})
$$

Minor disproportionation also gives one saturated and one unsaturated chain end. PAN is therefore a **combination-dominant** system.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **conversion of PAN fiber to carbon fiber** through stabilisation, carbonisation and (optional) graphitisation. Mention the chemistry of each step.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Spinning (1 mark).**  
PAN copolymer (typically ~92 % AN + 6 % MA + 2 % IA) is dissolved in DMF and **wet-spun** or **dry-jet wet-spun** into a coagulation bath to give a white acrylic tow.

**Step 2 — Stabilisation (3 marks).**  
The tow is heated in **air at 200–300 °C** for 30–120 min under tension. This triggers:
- **Cyclisation:** Adjacent –C$\equiv$N groups react to form **conjugated 6-membered pyridine-like rings** (the **ladder polymer**), an exothermic process (~200 kJ/mol).
- **Dehydrogenation:** Loss of H$_2$ from the backbone, increasing aromaticity.
- **Oxidation:** Incorporation of O as –C=O, –OH, –COOH groups; this is critical for the next step.

The product is a **black, infusible, oxygen-containing ladder polymer** that can survive further high-temperature processing without melting.

**Step 3 — Carbonisation (2 marks).**  
Heating in an **inert atmosphere (N$_2$ or Ar) to 1000–1500 °C** expels HCN, NH$_3$, H$_2$, CO, CO$_2$, leaving a **turbostratic carbon fiber** with tensile strength 3–5 GPa.

**Step 4 — Graphitisation (1 mark).**  
Optional further heating to **2500–3000 °C** converts turbostratic carbon into **graphitic sheets** aligned along the fiber axis, raising modulus to 500–600 GPa (high-modulus grade) or even 700+ GPa.

**Final step:** Surface treatment (electrolytic oxidation) and epoxy sizing for compatibility with polymer-matrix composites.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "**PAN is heated to make carbon fiber**" without specifying *temperature, atmosphere, and chemistry*. You will lose 3–4 marks. Always write the three explicit stages with conditions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Properties, Applications, and Copolymers*

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **properties of polyacrylonitrile** in detail — mechanical, thermal, chemical, optical and electrical — and explain how the pendant nitrile group controls each property.

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanical properties (2 marks):**  
PAN has **moderate tensile strength (50 MPa for dry fiber)** but **low elongation at break (15–30 %)**. The strong dipole interactions give **high loop and knot strength**, which is why PAN is used in knitwear. However, the **atactic structure** prevents the high crystallinity of HDPE or isotactic PP, so PAN is not used as a structural engineering plastic.

**Thermal properties (2 marks):**  
$T_g$ = **85–105 °C**; $T_m$ = ~317 °C (theoretical, never reached because of cyclisation). On heating in air, **thermo-oxidative cyclisation starts at ~200 °C** and proceeds exothermically. PAN is therefore classified as a **"non-melt processable" polymer** and must be solution-spun.

**Chemical resistance (1 mark):**  
Excellent resistance to **acids, alkalis, oxidising agents, and most organic solvents**. Soluble only in **polar aprotic solvents** (DMF, DMSO, DMAc, ethylene carbonate, concentrated aqueous NaSCN and ZnCl$_2$, ionic liquids).

**Optical and electrical (1 mark):**  
Refractive index **1.514–1.520**; transparent in thin films; yellows on prolonged UV exposure. Dielectric constant **~4.5 at 1 kHz** (high because of the –CN dipole), so PAN is used as a **capacitor dielectric** in specialty applications.

**Why the nitrile group is the master-key (1 mark):**  
The –C$\equiv$N has a **large dipole moment (3.6 D) and linear geometry**. It produces: (i) strong **dipole–dipole cohesion** (high $T_g$, high strength), (ii) **specific solvation only by polar aprotic solvents**, (iii) **chemical inertness** (C$\equiv$N triple bond is one of the most stable organic functional groups), and (iv) **cyclisation reactivity** at moderate temperatures — the basis of carbon-fiber manufacture.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a detailed note on **PAN-based copolymers** with examples and their commercial significance. Include the Mayo–Lewis copolymer composition concept.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why pure PAN is not used (1 mark).**  
Pure (homopolymer) PAN is **difficult to dye** (no chromophore or dye site), **brittle in fiber form**, and **undergoes sudden, exothermic, runaway cyclisation** during stabilisation. Copolymerisation with 5–15 % of a comonomer solves all three problems.

**Step 2 — Common comonomers (2 marks).**  
- **Methyl acrylate (MA):** 4–10 % — increases chain flexibility, opens up amorphous regions for dye diffusion.  
- **Itaconic acid (IA):** 1–3 % — provides –COOH groups that **lower the cyclisation temperature by ~30–40 °C** and broaden the exotherm, preventing runaway.  
- **Styrene, methyl methacrylate, vinyl acetate, acrylamide** — used in modacrylics (35–85 % comonomer) for flame-retardant textiles and wigs.

**Step 3 — Mayo–Lewis terminal model (2 marks).**  
For a binary copolymer, the instantaneous copolymer composition is:

$$
F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}
$$

where $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$. For AN ($M_1$) + MA ($M_2$): $r_1 = 1.02$, $r_2 = 0.70$. Because $r_1 \cdot r_2 \approx 0.71 \approx 1$, the system is **near-ideal random** but shows a slight drift; therefore industry uses **semicontinuous monomer addition** to keep feed composition constant.

**Step 4 — Commercial grades and uses (2 marks).**  
| Grade | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Acrylic fiber | >85 % AN, <15 % MA + IA | Sweaters, carpets, blankets, awnings |
| Modacrylic | 35–85 % AN, rest vinyl chloride or vinylidene chloride | Flame-retardant apparel, wigs, fake fur |
| SAN | ~70 % styrene + 30 % AN | Food containers, kitchenware, instrument lenses |
| ABS | 40–60 % AN + butadiene + styrene | Automotive, LEGO, electronic housings |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark loss zones:**
> 1. Confusing *acrylic* (PAN-based fiber, <15 % comonomer) with *modacrylic* (35–85 % comonomer) — examiners *will* deduct 1 mark.
> 2. Writing "AN + MA is ideal copolymer" — strictly, only when $r_1 r_2 = 1$ AND $r_1 = r_2 = 1$. Here only the product ≈ 1.
> 3. Forgetting to state that PAN is **never processed by melt extrusion** — students often miss this 1-mark easy gain.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this 15-point checklist before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

- [ ] PAN = polymer of **acrylonitrile (vinyl cyanide)** = repeat unit $\text{-[CH}_2\text{-CH(CN)]-}$, $M_0 = \mathbf{53.06\ g/mol}$.
- [ ] Monomer is **electron-poor** (Q = 0.48, e = 1.23) → free-radical and anionic polymerisation are dominant.
- [ ] FRP gives **atactic** PAN; Ziegler–Natta gives **isotactic** PAN.
- [ ] Pendant **–C$\equiv$N dipole moment ≈ 3.6 D** drives all cohesion and solubility behavior.
- [ ] $T_g$ of PAN = **85–105 °C**; $T_m$ theoretical ≈ **317 °C** but polymer cyclises below $T_m$.
- [ ] PAN is **non-melt processable** → always **solution-spun** from **DMF, DMSO, DMAc, NaSCN(aq), ionic liquid**.
- [ ] **Theoretical carbon yield = 67.9 %** (from repeat unit mass balance); real yield **50–55 %**.
- [ ] Carbon-fiber route: **Stabilisation (200–300 °C, air) → Carbonisation (1000–1500 °C, N$_2$) → optional Graphitisation (>2500 °C)**.
- [ ] Stabilisation chemistry: **cyclisation, dehydrogenation, oxidation → ladder polymer**.
- [ ] Commercial PAN is a **copolymer** with **methyl acrylate (4–10 %)** and **itaconic acid (1–3 %)**.
- [ ] Acrylic fiber = <15 % comonomer; Modacrylic = 35–85 % comonomer; SAN, ABS are also AN-based.
- [ ] **Mark–Houwink for PAN in DMF at 25 °C:** $K = 2.33 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g, $a = 0.75$.
- [ ] Major uses: **carbon fiber precursor, acrylic textiles, hollow-fiber membranes, hard-carbon battery anodes**.
- [ ] PAN is **self-extinguishing** (LOI ~18 % — only just); modacrylics with vinyl chloride raise LOI to ~27 %.
- [ ] **Never confuse "acrylic" (PAN-based) with "acrylic paint"** (PMA-based) or "acrylic sheet" (PMMA-based).

> [!TIP]
> **Two-sentence memory hook for any PAN question in KTU exam:**  
> *"PAN is a polar vinyl polymer with –CN pendant groups that ladder-polymerise on heating — which is exactly why it is the world's #1 precursor for carbon fiber, and why you can never melt-spin it."*  
> If you write these two sentences somewhere relevant, you will collect the **concept mark** even if the rest of the calculation is slightly off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polycarbonate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Polycarbonate # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Polycarbonate (PC)** is a high-performance engineering thermoplastic belonging to the family of polyesters of carbonic acid, characterized by the presence of the **carbonate functional group** $(-\text{O}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{O}-)$ as the dominant, repeating linkage along its macromolecular backbone. The most commercially significant member of this family is **Bisphenol-A Polycarbonate (BPA-PC)**, which is synthesized by the polycondensation of **Bisphenol-A (2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)propane)** with **phosgene** (or via transesterification with diphenyl carbonate). The general structural repeat unit of BPA-polycarbonate is represented as: $$\left[-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\right]_n$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Syllabus Insight (KTU 2024):** The IUPAC-recommended name is **poly(oxycarbonyloxy-1,4-phenyleneisopropylidene-1,4-phenylene)**. The subscript $n$ (degree of polymerization) typically ranges from **100 to 300**, giving commercial PCs a number-average molecular weight ($\overline{M_n}$) of **20,000 – 50,000 g/mol** and weight-average molecular weight ($\overline{M_w}$) of **40,000 – 100,000 g/mol**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distinguishing Feature:** Unlike many other engineering thermoplastics, polycarbonate is an **amorphous** polymer. Therefore, it does not exhibit a true melting point ($T_m$). Instead, it undergoes a glass transition at **$T_g \approx 145-150\,^\circ\text{C}$**, which is the highest among the major transparent thermoplastics. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine polycarbonate as a **"chain-link fence made of transparent bulletproof glass"**. - The **bisphenol-A units** act like the *vertical, rigid glass panels* — stiff, bulky, and resistant to bending. The two phenyl rings fused to the central quaternary carbon create a sterically hindered, structurally rigid segment. - The **carbonate linkages** are the *small, flexible metal connectors* holding the rigid glass panels together. They allow slight rotation, giving the chain a tiny bit of "give" so it does not shatter under impact. - The **methyl groups** ($\text{CH}_3$)$_2$ on the central carbon are like *tiny spacers* preventing the two phenyl rings from packing too closely — this is what keeps the polymer **optically transparent** (no crystalline regions to scatter light). - When struck, the rigid BPA segments absorb and dissipate the impact energy without propagating cracks, much like laminated automotive windshields. This unique combination of *rigid aromatic segments* + *flexible carbonate hinges* + *steric disruption of crystallinity* is what gives polycarbonate its signature balance of **transparency + toughness + heat resistance**. > [!TIP] > **Why is it called "poly-carbon-ate"?** The name comes from the hypothetical carbonic acid $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$. When both hydroxyl groups of this acid are replaced by organic R-groups via ester bonds, you get a "poly-ester of carbonic acid" = **poly-carbon-ate**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** **$147\,^\circ\text{C}$** (≈ 420 K) - **Density ($\rho$):** **$1.20\,\text{g/cm}^3$** (one of the lowest among engineering plastics) - **Light Transmittance:** **$\geq 90\%$** (in the visible range, 400–700 nm) - **Refractive Index ($n_D$):** **1.586** - **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT @ 1.82 MPa):** **$135-140\,^\circ\text{C}$** - **Notched Izod Impact Strength:** **600–850 J/m** (outstanding among transparent polymers) - **Tensile Strength at Yield:** **55–75 MPa** - **Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI):** **$\approx 25\%$** (self-extinguishing) - **Dielectric Strength:** **$\approx 30\,\text{kV/mm}$** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural & Molecular Analysis The polycarbonate repeat unit consists of three distinct architectural elements: | # | Structural Element | Chemical Identity | Functional Role | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Bisphenol-A moiety** | $-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-$ | Provides **rigidity, thermal stability, and impact resistance** via aromatic conjugation and steric bulk | | 2 | **Phenolic ether oxygen** | $-\text{O}-$ (linking BPA to carbonyl) | Provides **bond rotation freedom** and a site for H-bonding | | 3 | **Carbonyl group** | $-\text{C}(=\text{O})-$ | The reactive "core" that defines the **carbonate** linkage and undergoes nucleophilic substitution during synthesis | ### 2.1.1 Why BPA-PC is Amorphous The isopropylidene bridge $\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2$ creates a **kink** in the chain. The two phenyl rings attached to this carbon are forced into a *non-coplanar* arrangement. This irregular geometry prevents the chains from packing into an ordered crystalline lattice. The result: - **No $T_m$** (only a $T_g$) - **High optical transparency** (no spherulitic light scattering) - **Excellent dimensional stability** (no shrinkage from crystallization) ## 2.2 Commercial Synthesis Routes ### Route A: Interfacial Polycondensation (Schotten–Baumann Reaction) — *Dominant Industrial Method* This is the workhorse process used by **SABIC (Lexan®)**, **Covestro (Makrolon®)**, and most Asian manufacturers. **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Phase 1 (Aqueous):** Bisphenol-A is dissolved in aqueous **NaOH** to form the **sodium bisphenolate** (more nucleophilic): $$\text{HO}-\text{Ar}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{Ar}-\text{OH} + 2\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaO}-\text{Ar}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{Ar}-\text{ONa} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ 2. **Phase 2 (Organic):** Phosgene ($\text{COCl}_2$) is dissolved in an **immiscible organic solvent** (typically **dichloromethane $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$** or chlorobenzene). 3. **Phase Transfer:** A **phase-transfer catalyst** such as *triethylamine* $(\text{Et}_3\text{N})$ or a *quaternary ammonium salt* (e.g., benzyltriethylammonium chloride) shuttles the bisphenolate anion across the aqueous–organic interface. 4. **Schotten–Baumann Acylation:** At the interface, the nucleophilic phenolate oxygen attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of phosgene, displacing chloride: $$\text{ArO}^- + \text{Cl}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{ArO}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}^-$$ 5. **Chain Propagation:** The chlorocarbonate end-group then reacts with another bisphenolate, building the polymer: $$\text{ArO}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{Cl} + \text{NaO}-\text{Ar}' \rightarrow \text{ArO}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{O}-\text{Ar}' + \text{NaCl}$$ 6. **Acid Neutralization:** The liberated HCl is neutralized by excess NaOH, driving the equilibrium forward. **Operating conditions:** Temperature $25-40\,^\circ\text{C}$, atmospheric pressure, residence time 1–2 hours. The product PC dissolves in the organic layer and is later isolated by precipitation, washing, and drying. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why interfacial?** Phosgene is a toxic, gaseous reactant. By confining it to a separate organic phase, the reaction is more controllable, safer, and gives higher $\overline{M_w}$ than bulk methods.
