The NaOH aqueous phase serves a dual role — reactant (deprotonates BPA) and acid scavenger (neutralizes HCl). ### Route B: Melt Transesterification (Non-Phosgene Route) Increasingly important due to environmental and regulatory pressures against phosgene. **Logic:** - Reactants: **BPA + Diphenyl carbonate** $(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O})_2\text{C}=\text{O}$ - Catalyst: **Lithium acetylacetonate** or alkali metal hydroxides (10–100 ppm) - Temperature: Progressively ramped from $180\,^\circ\text{C}$ → $300\,^\circ\text{C}$ - Byproduct: **Phenol** (removed under vacuum to drive equilibrium to high $\overline{M_w}$) - No solvent required; pure melt process **Reaction summary:** $$n\,\text{HO}-\text{Ar}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{Ar}-\text{OH} + n\,(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O})_2\text{CO} \rightarrow \left[-\text{O}-\text{Ar}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{Ar}-\text{O}-\text{CO}-\right]_n + 2n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The non-phosgene, melt route is the **"green chemistry"** preferred process for new plants built after 2010. It eliminates toxic phosgene but requires a sophisticated reactor design to remove phenol efficiently at high vacuum. ### Route C: Ring-Opening Polymerization (ROP) of Cyclic Carbonates A laboratory-scale specialty method. Cyclic oligomeric carbonates are first prepared, purified, then ring-opened anionically or via insertion catalysts. Not industrially dominant. ## 2.3 Engineering Properties Deep-Dive | Property Class | Specific Property | Value/Range | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Mechanical** | Notched Izod Impact | **600–850 J/m** | ~30× higher than PMMA; comparable to toughened grades | | **Mechanical** | Tensile Modulus | **2.0–2.4 GPa** | Stiff but not as rigid as glass fiber-filled nylons | | **Thermal** | $T_g$ | **147 °C** | Highest among transparent thermoplastics; enables hot-fill, autoclave sterilization | | **Thermal** | HDT (1.82 MPa) | **135–140 °C** | Load-bearing capability at elevated temperatures | | **Optical** | Transmittance | **$\geq 90\%$** | Comparable to PMMA; used in glazing | | **Optical** | Refractive Index | **1.586** | High — used in optical lenses (eyeglasses, DVDs) | | **Electrical** | Volume Resistivity | **$10^{16}\,\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$** | Excellent insulator | | **Electrical** | Dielectric Constant (1 MHz) | **2.9** | Low signal loss in high-frequency applications | | **Chemical** | Acid Resistance | Poor to concentrated acids | Limits use in chemical plant piping | | **Chemical** | Solvent Resistance | Sensitive to **ketones, aromatic hydrocarbons, chlorinated solvents** | Cause stress cracking | | **Flame** | Limiting Oxygen Index | **25%** | Self-extinguishing (no drip) per UL94 V-2 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Stress-cracking failure in PC is a recurrent viva question. Even *residual molded-in stresses* combined with exposure to mild solvents (e.g., acetone vapor) can cause catastrophic brittle failure. Annealing at $120-130\,^\circ\text{C}$ relieves these stresses. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Repeat Unit Molecular Weight (BPA-PC) | $\displaystyle M_0 = 254.27\;\text{g/mol}$ | — | Based on $-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{CO}-$ | | Number-Average Degree of Polymerization | $\displaystyle \overline{DP}_n = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | For $\overline{M}_n = 30{,}000$, $\overline{DP}_n \approx 118$ | | Carothers Equation (for stoichiometric imbalance) | $\displaystyle \overline{DP}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | $r = N_A/N_B$ (mole ratio), $p$ = extent of reaction | For $r=1,\;p\to 1$, $\overline{DP}_n \to \infty$ | | Glass Transition (Fox-Flory) | $\displaystyle T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$ | $T_{g,\infty} \approx 150\,^\circ\text{C}$, $K \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ | Valid for $\overline{M}_n$ below critical entanglement $M_c$ | | Tg (Cowie-Fox alternative) | $\displaystyle T_g(\overline{M}_n) = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{C}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Similar to Fox-Flory, with empirical $C$ | Used for low-MW PC oligomers | | Density-Property Check | $\displaystyle \rho = \frac{M_0 \cdot N}{V_{\text{cell}}}$ | $N$ = chains/cell, $V_{\text{cell}}$ = unit-cell volume | For amorphous PC, $\rho \approx 1.20\,\text{g/cm}^3$ | | Molar Refractivity (Lorentz-Lorenz) | $\displaystyle R = \frac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \frac{M}{\rho}$ | $n$ = refractive index, $M$ = molar mass, $\rho$ = density | $R_{\text{PC}} \approx 31.4\,\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Tip:** Be ready to write the **Carothers equation** for an A–B + A–A type polycondensation if a calculation problem is asked. For BPA + phosgene, both are *difunctional* (functionality $f=2$), so Carothers applies directly. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive:** Headlamp lenses, instrument panels, sun-roof glazing (replaces glass → $40\%$ weight reduction) - **Electronics:** Connectors, capacitor housings, mobile phone chassis (high $T_g$ + low dielectric loss) - **Optical Data Storage:** CDs, DVDs, Blu-ray discs (refractive index precision + dimensional stability) - **Medical:** Hemodialyzers, surgical instrument housings, IV connectors (steam sterilizable, biocompatible grades) - **Construction & Architecture:** Sound walls, transparent roofing, security glazing (ballistic-rated) - **Aerospace:** Cabin interior panels, helmet visors (F-22, F-35 fighter jets) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number-Average Molecular Weight from Carothers' Equation for BPA-PC Synthesis The BPA + phosgene system is a classic **A–A + B–B** type polycondensation (where A and B are mutually reactive end-groups: phenolate and chloroformate). **Step 1: Define stoichiometry.** Let $N_A$ = moles of bisphenolate end-groups (from BPA) and $N_B$ = moles of chloroformate end-groups (from phosgene). Define the **stoichiometric ratio**: $$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B}, \quad 0 < r \leq 1$$ (BPA is conventionally the limiting reagent; phosgene is in slight excess in practice, but for theoretical treatment, we allow $r \leq 1$.) **Step 2: Define extent of reaction $p$.** $p$ = fraction of A groups that have reacted at time $t$. Number of unreacted A groups = $N_A(1-p)$. Number of unreacted B groups = $N_B - N_A p$ (since each A consumes one B). **Step 3: Total number of molecules remaining in the system.** At extent $p$, total molecules = (unreacted A end-groups as "molecules") + (unreacted B end-groups as "molecules") + polymer chains. For an A–A / B–B system, each polymer chain has one A end and one B end. So total molecules: $$N_{\text{total}} = N_A(1-p) + N_B - N_A p + \text{chains}$$ Since each chain consumes one A and one B, the number of chains = number of A–B bonds formed = $N_A p$. Therefore: $$N_{\text{total}} = N_A(1-p) + N_B - N_A p + N_A p = N_A(1-p) + N_B$$ **Step 4: Apply Carothers' equation.** Number-average degree of polymerization is the *initial* total number of molecules divided by the *current* total: $$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A(1-p) + N_B}$$ Substitute $N_A = r N_B$: $$\begin{aligned} \overline{DP}_n &= \frac{r N_B + N_B}{r N_B(1-p) + N_B} \\ &= \frac{N_B(r+1)}{N_B \left[r(1-p) + 1\right]} \\ &= \frac{1+r}{1 + r(1-p)} \\ &= \frac{1+r}{1 + r - r p} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Carothers result (A–A + B–B):** $$\boxed{\;\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the $2rp$ in the denominator?** In the *Step-Growth Polymerization* canonical Carothers form, when we count bonds (each bond consumes 2 functional groups), the formula becomes $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$. The factor of **2** appears because every condensation event consumes *one A* AND *one B*. For perfectly stoichiometric $r=1$: $$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{2}{2-2p} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$ > which is the famous $\overline{DP}_n = 1/(1-p)$ result — divergence at $p \to 1$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Applying Carothers to Real BPA-PC Production A reactor is charged with $N_B$ = 1.00 mol of BPA and $N_A$ = 1.02 mol of phosgene (slight excess of A). The reaction proceeds to $p = 0.99$. **Step 1: Compute $r$.** $$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{1.02}{1.00} = 1.02$$ **Step 2: Substitute into Carothers' equation.** $$\begin{aligned} \overline{DP}_n &= \frac{1 + 1.02}{1 + 1.02 - 2(1.02)(0.99)} \\ &= \frac{2.02}{1 + 1.02 - 2.0196} \\ &= \frac{2.02}{2.02 - 2.0196} \\ &= \frac{2.02}{0.0004} \\ &= 5050 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Compute $\overline{M}_n$.** $$\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \cdot M_0 = 5050 \times 254.27\;\text{g/mol} \approx 1.28 \times 10^6\;\text{g/mol}$$ **Interpretation:** Even a 2% stoichiometric imbalance at $p = 0.99$ limits $\overline{DP}_n$ to ~5000. To achieve the commercial range of $\overline{M}_n \approx 30{,}000$ (i.e., $\overline{DP}_n \approx 118$), the *purity* of monomers must be extraordinarily high. Industrial phosgene is typically $\geq 99.99\%$ pure, and BPA is purified to ppb levels of mono-functional impurities (e.g., $p$-isopropenylphenol). ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PC vs. Other Transparent Polymers | Property | **PC (BPA)** | **PMMA** | **PS** | **PET (amorphous)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | $T_g$ ($^\circ\text{C}$) | **147** | 105 | 100 | 78 | | Notched Izod (J/m) | **600–850** | 16–20 | 21 | 45 | | Light Transmittance (%) | **90** | 92 | 88 | 90 | | Refractive Index | **1.586** | 1.492 | 1.590 | 1.575 | | Density (g/cm³) | **1.20** | 1.18 | 1.05 | 1.34 | | Heat Deflection Temp ($^\circ\text{C}$) | **138** | 95 | 95 | 70 | | Chemical Resistance | Moderate | Fair | Poor | Good | | Cost (relative) | High | Medium | Low | Low | > [!TIP] > **Examination insight:** When asked to *justify* why PC is chosen for bullet-resistant / aircraft canopies, the answer must cite **all four**: high $T_g$ (thermal stability at high altitudes), high impact strength (energy absorption), high transparency (visibility), and low density (mass efficiency). PMMA has higher clarity but is brittle; PET is cheaper but has low HDT. ## 3.4 Derivative: Tg vs. Molecular Weight (Fox–Flory) — Worked Numerical The Fox-Flory equation for the molecular weight dependence of $T_g$: $$T_g(\overline{M}_n) = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$$ **Given:** $T_{g,\infty} = 150\,^\circ\text{C}$ for BPA-PC, $K = 1.0 \times 10^5\,^\circ\text{C}\cdot\text{g/mol}$. Find $T_g$ at $\overline{M}_n = 10{,}000$ and at $\overline{M}_n = 50{,}000$. **Case (a): $\overline{M}_n = 10{,}000$** $$T_g = 150 - \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^4} = 150 - 10 = 140\,^\circ\text{C}$$ **Case (b): $\overline{M}_n = 50{,}000$** $$T_g = 150 - \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{5.0 \times 10^4} = 150 - 2 = 148\,^\circ\text{C}$$ > This shows that the **entanglement molecular weight** ($M_c \approx 4{,}800$ for PC) is critical: once $\overline{M}_n \gg M_c$, $T_g$ plateaus and the polymer achieves its full thermal-mechanical performance. ## 3.5 Synthesis Mass Balance — Interfacial Route For 1.00 kg of BPA-PC produced (yield 100%): **Inputs:** - BPA: $1000\;\text{g} / 254.27\;\text{g/mol} = 3.933\;\text{mol}$ - Phosgene (theoretical): $3.933\;\text{mol} \times 98.92\;\text{g/mol} = 389.1\;\text{g}$ - NaOH: $3.933 \times 2 = 7.866\;\text{mol} = 314.6\;\text{g}$ **Byproducts:** - NaCl: $7.866\;\text{mol} \times 58.44\;\text{g/mol} = 459.7\;\text{g}$ - H₂O: $7.866\;\text{mol} \times 18.02\;\text{g/mol} = 141.7\;\text{g}$ This is why interfacial plants must integrate **brine electrolysis** (to recycle NaCl → NaOH + Cl₂ → phosgene) for economic and environmental reasons. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Bisphenol-A Polycarbonate Repeat Unit & Architectural Features ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BPA Moiety
aromatic rigid segment] --> B[Isopropylidene bridge
C CH3 2 kink] B --> C[Phenyl ring 1] B --> D[Phenyl ring 2] C --> E[Phenolic ether O] D --> F[Phenolic ether O] E --> G[Carbonate carbonyl
C double bond O] F --> G G --> H[Repeat unit termination
back to next BPA] style A fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#7f6000,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **blue BPA box** represents the rigid aromatic core contributing *thermal stability* and *impact resistance*. - The **orange isopropylidene bridge** is the kink that prevents crystallization (amorphous). - The **green carbonate carbonyl** is the reactive, defining functional group (legacy of the name "poly-carbon-ate"). - The **pink termination** loops back to the next BPA, illustrating the *repeating nature* of the polymer. ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Interfacial Polycondensation of BPA-PC ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Phosgene gas feed
COCl2 cylinder] --> M1 P2[Bisphenol A solid
BPA pellets] --> D1[Dissolution in NaOH
aqueous phase] D1 --> M1{Two-phase reactor
CH2Cl2 organic
plus aqueous} P3[Triethylamine
phase transfer catalyst] --> M1 M1 --> R1[Schotten Baumann
condensation at interface
25 to 40 C] R1 --> R2[Polymer dissolution
in organic layer] R2 --> S1[Phase separation
decant aqueous brine] S1 --> S2[Precipitation in
non solvent methanol] S2 --> S3[Centrifugation
washing] S3 --> S4[Drying under vacuum
80 C 24 h] S4 --> S5[Product PC pellets
Lexan or Makrolon grade] R1 --> W1[Waste HCl neutralized
by excess NaOH] W1 --> W2[Brine effluent
to electrolysis recycle] style M1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b45f06,stroke-width:2px style R1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style S5 fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px style W2 fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47,stroke-width:2px ``` **Block-by-block interpretation:** - **Inputs (left):** Three feed streams converge into the reactor: phosgene, BPA-NaOH solution, and the phase-transfer catalyst. - **Reactor (yellow):** The two-phase reactor is the heart of the process, operating at mild conditions. - **Reaction (green):** Schotten–Baumann condensation is the chemistry step. - **Workup (blue):** Polymer separation, precipitation, washing, drying. - **Output (blue pellet):** The finished polycarbonate pellets ready for compounding/granulation. - **Waste (pink):** HCl is captured as NaCl brine, which modern plants recycle to make more NaOH and Cl₂ (for more phosgene) — a closed-loop economy. ## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: Property–Structure Relationships in BPA-PC ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Structure BPA plus carbonate] --> P1[Property High Tg 147 C] S1 --> P2[Property High impact strength 600 J per m] S1 --> P3[Property Transparency over 90 percent] S1 --> P4[Property Amorphous no Tm] S1 --> P5[Property Self extinguishing LOI 25] S2[Sub-feature Rigid aromatic rings] --> P1 S2 --> P2 S3[Sub-feature Isopropylidene kink] --> P4 S3 --> P3 S4[Sub-feature Polar carbonate group] --> P5 S4 --> P6[Property Stress cracking in ketones] style S1 fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px style P1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style P4 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style P5 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px style P6 fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47,stroke-width:2px ``` **Pedagogical takeaway:** Each *property* traces back to a *specific structural feature*. This cause–effect mapping is exactly what KTU examiners expect in a 7-mark descriptive answer. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]** **Q1.** Define polycarbonate. Write the structure of the repeat unit of bisphenol-A polycarbonate and identify the **two** functional groups present in it. **Model Answer:** Polycarbonate is a thermoplastic polymer containing the **carbonate functional group** $(-\text{O}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\text{O}-)$ as the repeating unit in its backbone. The bisphenol-A polycarbonate repeat unit is: $$\left[-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{C}(=\text{O})-\right]_n$$ The two functional groups present are: 1. **Aromatic phenyl (benzene) rings** – part of the bisphenol-A moiety 2. **Carbonate ester group** – the defining linkage > *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for structure, 0.5 marks for functional-group identification.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]** **Q2.** List **any three** distinguishing properties of polycarbonate that make it superior to PMMA for engineering applications. **Model Answer:** | # | Property | PC Value | PMMA Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Glass Transition Temperature** | $147\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $105\,^\circ\text{C}$ | PC retains properties at higher service temperatures | | 2 | **Notched Izod Impact Strength** | $600-850\;\text{J/m}$ | $16-20\;\text{J/m}$ | PC is ~30× tougher; used in safety glazing | | 3 | **Heat Deflection Temperature** | $135-140\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $95\,^\circ\text{C}$ | PC can be steam-sterilized (medical) and hot-filled | *(Award 1 mark per valid property with comparative value.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]** ### Question A **(a)** Explain the **interfacial polycondensation process** for the manufacture of bisphenol-A polycarbonate from bisphenol-A and phosgene. Discuss the role of (i) NaOH, (ii) the organic solvent, and (iii) the phase-transfer catalyst. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare bisphenol-A polycarbonate with PMMA and polystyrene in terms of (i) glass transition temperature, (ii) impact strength, and (iii) optical transparency. Justify the use of PC in **bullet-resistant glazing** with reference to its structure. **(7 marks)** ### Question B (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Describe the **melt transesterification route** for polycarbonate synthesis. How does it differ from the interfacial route in terms of (i) monomer chemistry, (ii) byproducts, (iii) environmental impact, and (iv) molecular weight control? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Carothers equation** for an A–A + B–B polycondensation. A polycarbonate reactor uses $N_B = 1.00\;\text{mol}$ of BPA and $N_A = 1.01\;\text{mol}$ of phosgene. If the reaction proceeds to $p = 0.98$, calculate the number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{DP}_n$ and $\overline{M}_n$. Given $M_0 = 254.27\;\text{g/mol}$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solutions #### Solution to Question A (a) — Interfacial Polycondensation **Step 1 (1 mark):** In this process, bisphenol-A is dissolved in an aqueous solution of **NaOH** (typically 5–10% w/w) to form the water-soluble **sodium bisphenolate** salt: $$\text{HO-Ar-C(CH}_3)_2\text{-Ar-OH} + 2\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaO-Ar-C(CH}_3)_2\text{-Ar-ONa} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Phosgene gas is dissolved in an **immiscible organic solvent** — typically **dichloromethane** ($\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$) — that does not mix with the aqueous BPA solution. The two immiscible phases form an *interface*. **Step 3 (1 mark):** A **phase-transfer catalyst** — usually **triethylamine** $(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5)_3\text{N}$ or a quaternary ammonium salt — is added. This catalyst shuttles the bisphenolate anion ($\text{ArO}^-$) from the aqueous phase across the interface into the organic phase where it reacts with phosgene. **Step 4 (2 marks):** The reaction — a **Schotten–Baumann acylation** — occurs at the interface: $$\text{ArO}^- + \text{COCl}_2 \rightarrow \text{ArO-CO-Cl} + \text{Cl}^-$$ $$\text{ArO-CO-Cl} + \text{NaO-Ar'} \rightarrow \text{ArO-CO-O-Ar'} + \text{NaCl}$$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Polymerization continues by repetition, building the polycarbonate chain. The reaction is conducted at **$25-40\,^\circ\text{C}$**, atmospheric pressure, and the **HCl byproduct is neutralized by excess NaOH**, shifting the equilibrium toward the polymer. **Roles:** - **(i) NaOH:** Deprotonates BPA → more nucleophilic bisphenolate; neutralizes HCl byproduct. - **(ii) Organic solvent ($\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$):** Dissolves phosgene and the growing PC; prevents water-side hydrolysis of phosgene. - **(iii) Phase-transfer catalyst:** Solubilizes the ionic bisphenolate in the organic phase, accelerates the interphase transport, and enables high $\overline{M_w}$ at moderate temperatures. --- #### Solution to Question A (b) — Property Comparison and Bullet-Resistance Justification | Property | **PC (BPA)** | **PMMA** | **PS** | |---|---|---|---| | $T_g$ ($^\circ\text{C}$) | **147** | 105 | 100 | | Notched Izod (J/m) | **600–850** | 16–20 | 21 | | Light Transmittance (%) | **$\geq 90$** | 92 | 88 | **Justification for bullet-resistant glazing (3 marks):** - The **bisphenol-A moiety** contains two rigid aromatic phenyl rings connected by a quaternary carbon with two methyl groups. This bulky architecture provides exceptional **stiffness** and **energy-dissipation capacity** when impacted — the rings resist deformation, while the carbonate linkages provide limited rotational freedom to absorb shock without cracking. - The **high $T_g$ (147 °C)** ensures dimensional stability across extreme temperature ranges (e.g., $-40\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $+120\,^\circ\text{C}$ on aircraft canopies). - The **amorphous morphology** ensures optical transparency — no crystalline domains to scatter light. - The result is a unique combination: **toughness of an engineering plastic** + **clarity of glass**, ideal for ballistic-rated transparent armor in aircraft cockpits, armored vehicles, and riot shields. --- #### Solution to Question B (a) — Melt Transesterification Route **Description (4 marks):** Melt transesterification is a *non-phosgene* route. Bisphenol-A is reacted with **diphenyl carbonate** $(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O})_2\text{CO}$ in the molten state at $180-300\,^\circ\text{C}$ in the presence of a **transesterification catalyst** (e.g., lithium acetylacetonate or sodium hydroxide at 10–100 ppm). The byproduct, **phenol**, is removed under progressively increasing vacuum to shift the equilibrium toward high molecular weight. **Differences from interfacial route (3 marks):** | Aspect | Interfacial Route | Melt Transesterification | |---|---|---| | (i) Monomers | BPA + phosgene (toxic gas) | BPA + diphenyl carbonate (solid, safer) | | (ii) Byproducts | NaCl (salt) + H₂O | Phenol (recoverable, valuable) | | (iii) Environmental | Phosgene toxicity concerns; large brine waste | No phosgene; lower waste; "green chemistry" | | (iv) MW control | Easier (interfacial PTC accelerates) | Harder (requires high-vacuum phenol stripping + very pure monomers) | --- #### Solution to Question B (b) — Carothers Derivation and Calculation **Derivation (4 marks):** [See **Section 3.1** above for the full 7-step derivation. The student must show all algebraic transitions and end at $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$.] **Calculation (3 marks):** Given: $N_B = 1.00\;\text{mol}$ (BPA), $N_A = 1.01\;\text{mol}$ (phosgene), $p = 0.98$, $M_0 = 254.27\;\text{g/mol}$. **Step 1:** Compute $r$. $$r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} = \frac{1.01}{1.00} = 1.01$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into Carothers' equation. $$\begin{aligned} \overline{DP}_n &= \frac{1 + 1.01}{1 + 1.01 - 2(1.01)(0.98)} \\ &= \frac{2.01}{2.01 - 1.9796} \\ &= \frac{2.01}{0.0304} \\ &\approx 66.1 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $\overline{M}_n$. $$\overline{M}_n = \overline{DP}_n \times M_0 = 66.1 \times 254.27 \approx 16{,}807\;\text{g/mol} \approx 1.68 \times 10^4\;\text{g/mol}$$ **Interpretation:** A 1% stoichiometric imbalance at $p = 0.98$ limits $\overline{M}_n$ to ~16,800 g/mol. To reach commercial PC grade ($\overline{M}_n \approx 30{,}000$ g/mol), either $p$ must be pushed to $\geq 0.99$ *with* $r$ very close to 1.000, or a chain-coupling agent (e.g., phenyl chloroformate end-capper) is used. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting stoichiometric factor of 2 in Carothers' denominator.** Many students write $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-rp}$ — this is the A–B (homopolymerization) form, not A–A + B–B. **Lose 2 marks.** > 2. **Mixing up phosgene ($\text{COCl}_2$, MW 98.92) with carbonyl chloride notation or chloroformate intermediates.** Draw the *full* structures in the mechanism — don't just write "phosgene attacks phenolate." > 3. **Confusing $T_g$ and $T_m$.** Polycarbonate is **amorphous** — it has *no* $T_m$. Students who state "$T_m$ of PC is 230 °C" lose marks. > 4. **Ignoring the role of the phase-transfer catalyst** — it is *the* enabler of the interfacial route. Without it, polymerization stalls at oligomer levels. > 5. **In the Carothers problem, forgetting to take the *smaller* of $N_A/N_B$ or $N_B/N_A$ as $r \leq 1$.** Conventionally $r \leq 1$; otherwise flip the ratio. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Definition:** Polycarbonate is an engineering thermoplastic with the **carbonate group** $(-\text{O}-\text{CO}-\text{O}-)$ in its backbone. - [x] **Repeat unit of BPA-PC:** $-\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{CO}-$ with $M_0 = 254.27\;\text{g/mol}$. - [x] **Monomers:** Bisphenol-A (BPA) + Phosgene ($\text{COCl}_2$) *or* Diphenyl carbonate. - [x] **Commercial synthesis routes:** (1) **Interfacial polycondensation** (dominant, Schotten–Baumann), (2) **Melt transesterification** (non-phosgene, green), (3) Ring-opening (specialty). - [x] **Key operating conditions (interfacial):** $25-40\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2$ solvent, NaOH aqueous, triethylamine PTC. - [x] **Key operating conditions (melt):** $180 \to 300\,^\circ\text{C}$ gradient, lithium acetylacetonate catalyst, vacuum phenol stripping. - [x] **$T_g$ of BPA-PC = $147\,^\circ\text{C}$** — highest among transparent thermoplastics. - [x] **Amorphous polymer** — no $T_m$; transparent due to lack of crystalline scattering. - [x] **Carothers equation (A–A + B–B):** $\overline{DP}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$. - [x] **Fox–Flory:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/\overline{M}_n$; plateaus once $\overline{M}_n > M_c \approx 4{,}800$. - [x] **Impact strength: 600–850 J/m** — ~30× higher than PMMA. - [x] **Light transmittance $\geq 90\%$, refractive index 1.586.** - [x] **Limiting Oxygen Index $\approx 25\%$** — self-extinguishing. - [x] **Trade names:** Lexan® (SABIC), Makrolon® (Covestro), Calibre® (Trinseo), Panlite® (Teijin). - [x] **Top applications:** safety/ballistic glazing, optical data storage (CD/DVD/Blu-ray), automotive headlamps, medical devices, electronics housings, aircraft canopies. - [x] **Major weakness:** **Stress cracking** in ketones, aromatic hydrocarbons, chlorinated solvents — even at low concentrations. - [x] **Carothers pitfall:** For $r = 1.01,\;p = 0.98$ → $\overline{DP}_n \approx 66$; tiny stoichiometric imbalances severely limit $\overline{M}_n$. - [x] **BPA controversy:** Health concerns over BPA leaching into food/water → commercial shift to **BPA-free** polycarbonates using *bisphenol S, TMC, isosorbide* (advanced topics beyond Module 1). - [x] **Why amorphous?** The isopropylidene $\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2$ bridge introduces a chain *kink* that frustrates crystalline packing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Mantra:** "Structure dictates property, property dictates application, application dictates synthesis, synthesis dictates molecular weight, and molecular weight dictates *everything*." Memorize the loop.

topics.title: PMMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PMMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poly(methyl methacrylate)** (abbreviated as **PMMA**) is a synthetic, linear, addition-type thermoplastic polymer belonging to the family of **polyacrylates**. It is synthesized by the **free-radical chain-growth polymerisation** of the vinyl monomer **methyl methacrylate (MMA)**, whose IUPAC name is *methyl 2-methylprop-2-enoate*. The repeating unit of PMMA has the chemical formula **-(CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃))ₙ-**, and its average molecular weight typically lies in the range of **10⁵ to 10⁶ g/mol** for commercially processed grades.

PMMA is an **amorphous** polymer with a reported **glass transition temperature (T_g) of approximately 105 °C**, an atactic (random stereochemical) configuration, and outstanding optical transparency (≈ 92 % light transmittance), which is why it is commercially marketed as **"acrylic glass"** under trade names such as *Plexiglas*, *Lucite*, *Perspex*, and *Acrylite*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** PMMA is studied as the *prototypical vinyl addition polymer* — a textbook example to understand monomer–repeat unit–polymer relationships, tacticity, free-radical kinetics, and the dependence of polymer properties on pendant side groups.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of PMMA as a **long freight train made entirely of identical boxcars**. Each boxcar is a methyl methacrylate unit, and the entire train (the polymer chain) is built by coupling thousands of these boxcars end-to-end. The "side mirror" hanging from every boxcar is the bulky ester side group **–COOCH₃**, which prevents the chains from sliding past one another easily. This **side-group crowding** is precisely what gives PMMA its characteristic rigidity, glassy transparency, and high strength compared to its parent polymer, polyethylene, which is essentially the same train but with *no side mirrors*.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Monomer:** the single boxcar (MMA, a liquid)
> - **Repeat Unit:** the structural unit (–CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃)–) that repeats in the chain
> - **Degree of Polymerisation (DP):** the number of boxcars (n) coupled together
> - **Molecular Weight (M):** M = DP × M₀, where M₀ = 100.12 g/mol is the repeat unit molar mass

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Property | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit formula | –[CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃)]– | Vinyl addition backbone |
| Monomer molar mass (M₀) | **100.12 g/mol** | Basis for MW calculation |
| Glass transition temp (T_g) | **≈ 105 °C** | Defines upper use limit (amorphous) |
| Density (ρ) | **1.17 – 1.20 g/cm³** | Lighter than ordinary silicate glass (2.5) |
| Light transmittance | **≈ 92 %** | Higher than glass (~ 83 %) |
| Refractive index (n_D) | **1.49** | Excellent optical clarity |
| Tensile strength | **65 – 75 MPa** | Mechanically tough |
| Charpy impact (unnotched) | **10 – 15 kJ/m²** | More brittle than PC, tougher than PS |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Monomer — Methyl Methacrylate (MMA)

MMA is a **vinyl monomer** of the general form **CH₂=CHX** where the substituent **X = –COOCH₃** is an electron-withdrawing ester group. The α-carbon also bears a **methyl group (–CH₃)**, which classifies MMA as a *1,1-disubstituted ethylene*. The conjugation between the C=C double bond and the carbonyl (C=O) of the ester group stabilises the propagating radical, making MMA highly susceptible to free-radical polymerisation.

**Structure of MMA:**

$$
\text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\text{–COOCH}_3
$$

### 2.2 Free-Radical Polymerisation Mechanism — Step-by-Step

Free-radical polymerisation proceeds via three classical stages. The mechanism is **chain-growth** (not step-growth) and obeys the **steady-state radical approximation** in its kinetics.

#### 2.2.1 Initiation (two-step)

A thermal initiator (commonly **AIBN — azobisisobutyronitrile**, or **BPO — benzoyl peroxide**) decomposes homolytically to produce free radicals **R•**.

$$
\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C(CN)–N=N–C(CN)(CH}_3\text{)}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\ (\text{CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C}^{\bullet}\text{CN} + \text{N}_2
$$

The primary radical then adds to the C=C of MMA to form the chain-initiating species **M₁•**.

$$
\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)\text{–COOCH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{R–CH}_2\text{–C}^{\bullet}(\text{CH}_3)\text{–COOCH}_3
$$

#### 2.2.2 Propagation

The active centre successively adds monomer units, with each addition regenerating the radical at the chain end.

$$
\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M} \xrightarrow{k_p} \text{M}_{n+1}^{\bullet}
$$

The rate of propagation is the rate-determining step in the chain-growth cycle.

#### 2.2.3 Termination

Two growing chains combine or disproportionate, ending chain growth.

- **Combination:**  $\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{M}_{n+m}$
- **Disproportionation:**  $\text{M}_n^{\bullet} + \text{M}_m^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{M}_n\text{(saturated)} + \text{M}_m\text{(unsaturated)}$

For MMA, **disproportionation** is the dominant termination mode.

### 2.3 Tacticity of PMMA

Because the polymer backbone contains a **quaternary carbon** (the α-carbon in the repeat unit bears two different substituents: –CH₃ and –COOCH₃, in addition to H and the chain), PMMA exhibits **tacticity**. Commercial free-radical PMMA is predominantly **atactic** (≈ 70–80 % syndio, ≈ 20–30 % atactic content), but stereospecific catalysts (e.g., Ziegler–Natta) can yield **isotactic** or **syndiotactic** grades.

- **Isotactic PMMA:** all –COOCH₃ groups on the same side of the backbone → semi-crystalline, T_g ≈ 45 °C
- **Syndiotactic PMMA:** –COOCH₃ groups alternate sides → crystalline tendency, T_g ≈ 115 °C
- **Atactic PMMA:** random placement → fully amorphous, T_g ≈ 105 °C

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is commercial PMMA amorphous?** Because free-radical polymerisation is non-stereospecific, the pendant groups are placed randomly, preventing the chains from packing into an ordered crystalline lattice.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_n = \overline{M_0} \times \overline{DP_n}$ | Number-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 2 | $M_w = \overline{M_0} \times \overline{DP_w}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 3 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Polydispersity index (≈ 1.5–2.0 for FRP) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | Steady-state rate of polymerisation | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{[M]}{2 f k_d [I] \, t} \cdot \text{const}$ | Kinetic chain length | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_g(\text{PMMA}) \approx 105\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Glass transition (DSC mid-point) | °C |
| 7 | $T_m(\text{isotactic PMMA}) \approx 160\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Melting (only for crystalline grades) | °C |
| 8 | $\rho_{PMMA} = 1.18\ \text{g/cm}^3$ | Standard density (cast sheet) | g/cm³ |
| 9 | $\eta_{sp} / c = [\eta] + k' [\eta]^2 c$ | Huggins equation for intrinsic viscosity | dL/g |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Reminder:** In the above table, $M_n$ and $M_w$ are *number-average* and *weight-average* molecular weights, NOT the monomer mass $M_0$ (which equals 100.12 g/mol for MMA). Confusing these is a classic KTU valuation trap — examiners specifically test this distinction.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of PMMA

- **Aerospace canopies and aircraft windows** — half the weight of glass, vastly better impact resistance.
- **Automotive taillights and instrument clusters** — mouldable into complex shapes with optical clarity.
- **Biomedical bone cement** — PMMA is the *gold standard* filler in orthopaedic surgery for fixing prosthetics.
- **Optical fibres (POF)** — PMMA-based plastic optical fibres are used in short-distance data links and decorative lighting.
- **LCD backlights and light guides** — exploits total internal reflection.
- **Dentures and prosthetic devices** — biocompatible, easily pigmented.
- **Aquarium viewing panels** — historically the largest cast PMMA panels in the world.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Molecular Weight of PMMA from Degree of Polymerisation

The molecular weight of any addition polymer is the product of the molecular weight of its repeat unit and the degree of polymerisation.

**Step 1 — Identify the repeat unit of PMMA**

The repeat unit of PMMA, after opening the C=C double bond of MMA, is:

$$
-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3)-
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the molar mass of one repeat unit (M₀)**

The repeat unit contains the following atoms:

- Carbon: $1 (\text{from CH}_2) + 1 (\text{quaternary C}) + 1 (\text{CH}_3) + 1 (\text{C=O}) + 1 (\text{OCH}_3) = 5$ C atoms
- Hydrogen: $2 (\text{CH}_2) + 3 (\text{CH}_3) + 3 (\text{OCH}_3) = 8$ H atoms
- Oxygen: 2 O atoms

Atomic masses used: C = 12, H = 1, O = 16.

$$
M_0 = (5 \times 12) + (8 \times 1) + (2 \times 16)
$$

$$
M_0 = 60 + 8 + 32
$$

$$
\boxed{M_0 = 100\ \text{g/mol}}
$$

> This matches the molar mass of the MMA monomer (C₅H₈O₂ = 100.12 g/mol) because in vinyl addition polymerisation no small molecule is eliminated; the empirical formula of the repeat unit is identical to that of the monomer.

**Step 3 — Apply the molecular-weight relation**

For a commercial PMMA grade with $\overline{DP_n} = 5000$ (typical for cast sheet):

$$
M_n = M_0 \times \overline{DP_n}
$$

$$
M_n = 100 \times 5000
$$

$$
\boxed{M_n = 5.0 \times 10^{5}\ \text{g/mol}}
$$

This corresponds to a *medium-grade* commercial PMMA. Injection-moulding grades typically have $M_n$ in the range $8 \times 10^4$ to $1.5 \times 10^5$ g/mol, while cast sheet grades can exceed $10^6$ g/mol.

### 3.2 Derivation: Kinetic Chain Length ($\overline{X_n}$) for PMMA Synthesis

The kinetic chain length is the average number of monomer molecules added per active radical before termination.

**Step 1 — Rate of propagation ($R_p$)**

By the steady-state assumption, the rate of radical formation equals the rate of radical termination:

$$
R_i = R_t
$$

$$
2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [\text{M}^{\bullet}]^2
$$

Solving for radical concentration:

$$
[\text{M}^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
$$

The rate of polymerisation is:

$$
R_p = k_p [\text{M}] [\text{M}^{\bullet}] = k_p [\text{M}] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of kinetic chain length**

$$
\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{\text{rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{rate of radical initiation}} = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the rates**

$$
\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{k_p [\text{M}] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}}{2 f k_d [I]}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{k_p [\text{M}]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d [I] \, k_t}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for a typical bulk polymerisation of MMA**

Using literature values at 60 °C:
- $k_p \approx 515\ \text{L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$
- $k_t \approx 1.5 \times 10^{7}\ \text{L mol}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$
- $f k_d \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ (for AIBN at 60 °C, $f \approx 0.6$)
- $[\text{M}] \approx 9.4\ \text{mol/L}$ (bulk MMA at 25 °C)

$$
\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{515 \times 9.4}{2 \sqrt{(2.5 \times 10^{-6})(1.5 \times 10^{7})}}
$$

First compute the denominator's inner term:

$$
f k_d \cdot k_t = (2.5 \times 10^{-6})(1.5 \times 10^{7}) = 37.5
$$

Then:

$$
\sqrt{37.5} \approx 6.12
$$

Denominator: $2 \times 6.12 = 12.24$.

Numerator: $515 \times 9.4 = 4841$.

$$
\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{4841}{12.24} \approx 395
$$

Hence, each growing radical adds roughly **395 monomer units** before termination — a value consistent with experimental data for bulk MMA polymerisation at 60 °C.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Structural Feature on PMMA Property

| Structural Feature | Consequence | Property Affected | Quantitative Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulky –COOCH₃ side group | Steric hindrance, restricted chain rotation | High T_g | T_g ≈ 105 °C (vs 20 °C for polyethylene) |
| Quaternary α-carbon with –CH₃ | Tacticity, prevents close packing | Amorphous morphology | Density 1.18 g/cm³ (lower than crystalline 1.23) |
| Carbonyl (C=O) in side group | Polar interactions, dipole-dipole | Solubility in polar solvents | Dissolves in acetone, chloroform, toluene |
| Absence of –OH / –NH groups | No hydrogen bonding between chains | Moderate mechanical strength | Tensile strength 65–75 MPa |
| Vinyl backbone (C–C single bond) | High bond energy | Thermal stability onset > 200 °C | Decomposes (not melts) above 280 °C |
| Atactic placement | Disrupted crystallisation | Transparency | 92 % transmittance (vs 80–85 % for PS) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Monomer-to-Polymer Architectural Flow of PMMA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Raw Materials Acetone HCN Methanol Sulphuric Acid"] --> A1["Methacrylamide Sulphate Intermediate"]
    A1 --> A2["Methyl Methacrylate Monomer CH2 C CH3 COOCH3"]
    A2 --> A3["Thermal Initiator AIBN or BPO Decomposes to R dot"]
    A3 --> A4["Initiation Step R dot adds to C double bond C of MMA"]
    A4 --> A5["Propagation Step M n dot plus M yields M n+1 dot Repeats n times"]
    A5 --> A6["PMMA Macromolecule Backbone CH2 C CH3 COOCH3 repeating n units"]
    A5 --> A7["Termination by Disproportionation Dominant for MMA"]
    A5 --> A8["Termination by Combination Secondary for MMA"]
    A6 --> A9["Atactic Commercial PMMA Tacticity Random Amorphous Tg 105 C"]
    A6 --> A10["Syndiotactic PMMA Tacticity Alternating Higher Tg Semi Crystalline"]
    A6 --> A11["Isotactic PMMA Tacticity Same Side Crystalline Tg 45 C"]
    A9 --> B1["Optical Sheet Aircraft Canopy Acrylic Glass"]
    A9 --> B2["Biomedical Bone Cement Orthopaedic Fixation"]
    A9 --> B3["Automotive Lenses LED Light Guides"]
    A9 --> B4["Plastic Optical Fibres Short Distance Data Links"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Free-Radical Polymerisation Stages of PMMA

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator AIBN
    participant M as MMA Monomer
    participant R as Radical R dot
    participant Pn as Active Chain Mn dot
    participant P as PMMA Dead Polymer

    I->>R: Thermal homolysis at 60 C produces 2 R dot plus N2
    R->>Pn: R dot attacks C double bond C of MMA forms M1 dot
    loop Propagation n-1 times
        Pn->>Pn: Mn dot plus M yields M n+1 dot via kp
    end
    Pn->>P: Two Mn dot terminate by disproportionation yielding saturated and unsaturated dead polymer
    Note over P: M_n saturation and unsaturation marks end of chain growth
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Property-Application Mapping of PMMA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Optical Transparency 92 percent"] --> U1["Aircraft Windows"]
    P1 --> U2["LED Light Guides"]
    P1 --> U3["Lens Arrays"]
    P2["Biocompatibility ISO 10993"] --> U4["Bone Cement"]
    P2 --> U5["Dental Prosthetics"]
    P3["Weatherability UV Stable"] --> U6["Outdoor Signage"]
    P3 --> U7["Aquarium Panels"]
    P4["Ease of Thermoforming"] --> U8["Sanitary Ware"]
    P4 --> U9["Automotive Lenses"]
    P5["Density 1.18 g per cm cube Lighter than Glass"] --> U10["Aerospace Glazing"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Bank, 3 marks]**
*Define the term "repeat unit" of a polymer. Write the repeat unit of PMMA and calculate the molar mass of one repeat unit.*

**Model Answer:**
A **repeat unit** (or *mer*) is the smallest structural unit whose repeated linkage, end-to-end, generates the complete polymer chain. For addition polymers it is identical in empirical formula to the monomer (since no small molecule is eliminated during polymerisation).

**Repeat unit of PMMA:**

$$
-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)(\text{COOCH}_3)-
$$

**Molar mass calculation:**

$$
M_0 = 5(\text{C}) + 8(\text{H}) + 2(\text{O}) = (5 \times 12) + (8 \times 1) + (2 \times 16) = 100\ \text{g/mol}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Writing **C₅H₈O₂** (monomer formula) instead of correctly identifying the **repeat unit** structure. KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark if you do not draw the polymer-chain fragment — the empirical formula alone is *not* the repeat unit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, 3 marks]**
*Why is commercial PMMA amorphous and transparent, while polyethylene prepared under similar conditions is partially crystalline and opaque?*

**Model Answer (3 key points):**
1. **Tacticity:** PMMA has a quaternary α-carbon with two different substituents (–CH₃ and –COOCH₃). Free-radical polymerisation gives a *random* (atactic) placement of the bulky ester groups, preventing the chains from folding into ordered crystalline regions.
2. **Steric hindrance:** The bulky –COOCH₃ side groups physically block close chain packing, ensuring an amorphous morphology with light transmittance of ≈ 92 %.
3. **Contrast with polyethylene:** PE has no bulky side group; its chains can fold into lamellae, producing crystallites that scatter light and yield an opaque, waxy solid.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, 14 marks, CO1 / CO2]**

**(a)** Discuss the free-radical polymerisation mechanism of methyl methacrylate (MMA) leading to PMMA, clearly indicating the **initiation, propagation, and termination** steps. Mention the role of the initiator and the dominant mode of termination for this system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the terms **degree of polymerisation (DP)**, **number-average molecular weight (M_n)**, and **polydispersity index (PDI)**. For a PMMA sample with $\overline{DP_n} = 8500$ and $\overline{DP_w} = 14450$, calculate the PDI and comment on the synthesis route that would yield such a distribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**
- **Initiation (2 marks):** AIBN/BPO undergoes homolytic cleavage on heating to yield primary radicals R•. R• adds to the C=C bond of MMA forming the chain-initiating radical M₁•. Show both reactions with proper arrow-pushing notation.
- **Propagation (3 marks):** The active chain-end radical M_n• successively adds monomer M via $k_p$, regenerating the radical at each step. Write the general propagation step. Note that MMA follows a head-to-tail addition predominantly because of radical stability on the substituted carbon.
- **Termination (2 marks):** For MMA, **disproportionation dominates** (≈ 80 %) over combination because the tertiary radical intermediate is sterically crowded. Show both disproportionation and combination reactions.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Degree of Polymerisation (DP):** the average number of repeat units per polymer chain.
- **Number-average molecular weight:** $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$, sensitive to the number of chains.
- **PDI:** $\dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$; for a perfectly uniform polymer PDI = 1; for free-radical polymerisation PDI is typically 1.5–2.0.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Assuming $M_0 = 100$ g/mol for PMMA:

$$
M_n = \overline{DP_n} \times M_0 = 8500 \times 100 = 8.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{g/mol}
$$

$$
M_w = \overline{DP_w} \times M_0 = 14450 \times 100 = 1.445 \times 10^{6}\ \text{g/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n} = \dfrac{1.445 \times 10^{6}}{8.5 \times 10^{5}} = 1.7}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** A PDI of 1.7 is characteristic of **free-radical polymerisation (FRP)**. Anionic or living polymerisation would yield PDI closer to 1.0–1.1. Therefore, the sample was almost certainly produced by FRP.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 marks, CO2 / CO3]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **tacticity** in PMMA. Compare the properties (T_g, crystallinity, optical behaviour) of *isotactic*, *syndiotactic*, and *atactic* PMMA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe any **five important engineering applications** of PMMA, explaining the specific property that makes the polymer suitable for each application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

- **Definition of tacticity (2 marks):** Tacticity refers to the stereoregular arrangement of pendant side groups along the polymer backbone. Because the α-carbon of PMMA is a stereocentre, the –COOCH₃ group can be placed on either side of the backbone.
- **Isotactic PMMA (2 marks):** All –COOCH₃ groups lie on the same side of the chain. Chains pack in helices, producing a *semicrystalline* morphology. **T_g ≈ 45 °C**, melting point ≈ 160 °C. Optically translucent to opaque.
- **Syndiotactic PMMA (2 marks):** –COOCH₃ groups alternate regularly. Best chain packing, **T_g ≈ 115 °C**, highest melting tendency. Produced only by stereospecific catalysts.
- **Atactic PMMA (1 mark):** Random placement. Fully **amorphous**, T_g ≈ 105 °C, optically transparent (92 % transmittance). This is the commercial grade.

**Model Solution to (b):** Any five of the following, each carrying roughly 1.4 marks.

| # | Application | Property Exploited |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aircraft windows & canopies | 92 % transparency + half the weight of glass |
| 2 | Orthopaedic bone cement | Biocompatibility + ease of moulding in situ |
| 3 | LED light guides & backlights | High refractive index + total internal reflection |
| 4 | Automotive taillights & headlamps | UV stability + impact resistance |
| 5 | Aquarium viewing panels | High tensile strength + clarity in thick sections |
| 6 | Plastic optical fibres (POF) | Attenuation < 0.2 dB/m at 650 nm |
| 7 | Dentures and prosthetic devices | Biocompatibility + ease of pigmentation |
| 8 | Outdoor signage and canopy roofing | Weatherability + 10-year outdoor life |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Mark Deduction:**
> 1. **Drawing the MMA monomer (CH₂=C(CH₃)COOCH₃) instead of the repeat unit** of PMMA in repeat-unit questions. The repeat unit *must* show the opened C=C as a single bond: –CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃)–.
> 2. **Confusing M_n (number-average) with M_0 (monomer molar mass)**. M_n is for the *polymer*, M_0 is for the *repeat unit* (= 100 g/mol for PMMA).
> 3. **Stating the wrong T_g for PMMA.** It is **≈ 105 °C**, NOT 100 °C and NOT 80 °C (PS) and NOT 150 °C (PC). KTU examiners specifically test this number.
> 4. **Calling PMMA a condensation polymer.** It is an **addition (chain-growth)** polymer; no by-product is eliminated.
> 5. **Forgetting to write the inhibitor** (e.g., MEHQ = monomethyl ether of hydroquinone) when discussing commercial MMA storage. Industrial MMA is shipped with ~ 50 ppm MEHQ to prevent premature polymerisation.
> 6. **Wrong termination mode:** writing "combination" as dominant for MMA. The correct answer is **disproportionation**, due to the bulky tertiary radical intermediate.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PMMA = poly(methyl methacrylate)**; monomer = **methyl methacrylate (MMA)**; empirical repeat-unit mass **M₀ = 100 g/mol**.
- PMMA is a **vinyl addition (chain-growth) polymer** formed by **free-radical polymerisation** of MMA, typically initiated by **AIBN** or **BPO**.
- The three stages are **initiation, propagation, termination**; for MMA, **disproportionation is the dominant termination mode**.
- The bulky **–COOCH₃ side group** raises the **T_g to ~ 105 °C** and prevents crystallisation in the atactic form, giving **amorphous, transparent** (92 % transmittance) material.
- **Tacticity** matters: atactic (amorphous, commercial) vs isotactic (crystalline, T_g ≈ 45 °C) vs syndiotactic (highest T_g ≈ 115 °C).
- Key properties to memorise: density 1.18 g/cm³, refractive index 1.49, tensile strength 65–75 MPa, decomposition onset > 200 °C.
- **Degree of polymerisation:** $\overline{DP_n} = M_n / M_0$.
- **Polydispersity index (PDI)** for FRP-synthesised PMMA ≈ **1.5–2.0**; for living/anionic PMMA, PDI → 1.0.
- Commercial MMA is stored with ≈ 50 ppm **MEHQ** inhibitor to prevent unintended polymerisation.
- **Engineering uses** to remember: aircraft glazing, orthopaedic bone cement, automotive lenses, LED light guides, plastic optical fibres, dentures, signage.
- PMMA is marketed as **Plexiglas, Lucite, Perspex, Acrylite**.
- Remember the **kinetic chain length** formula: $\overline{X_n} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d [I] \, k_t}}$ — this is a high-yield KTU derivation.
- PMMA **does not crystallise** under normal free-radical synthesis; it softens at T_g rather than melting sharply.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PF Resin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** PF Resin # 🧪 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Phenol-Formaldehyde (PF) Resin** is a synthetic thermosetting polymer produced by the polycondensation reaction between **phenol (C₆H₅OH)** and **formaldehyde (HCHO)**, first commercially developed by **Leo Hendrik Baekeland** in **1907** under the trade name **"Bakelite"** — earning it the title of the **world's first fully synthetic plastic**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > PF resins belong to the class of *phenol-aralkyl resins* and are produced via **step-growth (condensation) polymerization** with the elimination of a small molecule byproduct (water). They exhibit a **3-D crosslinked network** on curing and are **irreversibly hardenable** — i.e., true **thermosets**. The reaction yields two industrially important variants based on the **catalyst system** and **molar ratio** of monomers: | Type | Catalyst | F/P Molar Ratio | Stage | Curing | |------|----------|------------------|-------|--------| | **Novolac** | Acid (HCl, H₂SO₄, Oxalic acid) | F/P < 1 (≈ 0.75 – 0.85) | **Two-stage** resin | Requires hardener (Hexa) | | **Resol** | Base (NaOH, NH₄OH) | F/P > 1 (≈ 1.2 – 1.5) | **One-stage** resin | Self-curing on heating | > [!NOTE] > **Monomer Functionalities:** Phenol = **3** (two *ortho* + one *para* positions); Formaldehyde = **2**. Since both are multifunctional, the system readily forms a **crosslinked network** — the foundation of thermoset behavior. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are building a **Lego castle** 🏰: - **Phenol molecules** are like the **corner blocks** (3 connection points). - **Formaldehyde molecules** are like the **short linker bricks** (2 connection points). - When you join them, they form chains, branches, and ultimately an **infinitely connected 3-D network** — once set, you cannot melt and reshape them (unlike thermoplastics like Lego pieces you can rebuild). ### 🍳 A Kitchen Analogy Think of baking a **cake** (Resol/Novolac after adding hardener): - The **batter** (linear/slightly branched resin) is pourable. - Once heated in the **oven** (curing), it becomes a solid, hard, infusible cake. - You can never reverse the cake back to batter — this is the **thermoset** character. > [!TIP] > **Why PF Resin is Irreversibly Hard:** > Crosslinking consumes all available reactive sites. Reheating cannot flow the material because covalent C–C and C–O–C bonds lock every chain in a giant macromolecular web. Attempts to heat a fully cured PF resin cause **thermal decomposition**, not melting. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics (for KTU) - **Density of cured PF resin:** ≈ **1.24 – 1.32 g/cm³** - **Heat of polymerization (ΔHₚ):** ≈ **–580 J/g** (exothermic) - **Curing temperature range:** **120 °C – 180 °C** - **Moulding pressure:** **15 – 35 MPa** - **Glass transition (post-cure):** **> 250 °C** (decomposes before Tg is observed) - **Service temperature:** up to **~ 200 °C continuous** - **Char yield at 800 °C:** ≈ **50 – 60 %** (excellent flame retardance) - **Dielectric strength:** ≈ **10 – 16 kV/mm** > [!IMPORTANT] > These values are the **memorizable numerical set** examiners often quote in Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize the *order of magnitude* rather than exact digits. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemistry of Phenol–Formaldehyde Reaction The reaction is fundamentally an **electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS)** on the activated phenol ring, followed by **condensation polymerization**. ### Stage A — Methylolation (Addition Reaction) Formaldehyde (or its protonated/activated form) attacks the *ortho* and *para* positions of phenol to form **methylol (–CH₂OH) intermediates**. $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH}$$ Multiple methylolations can occur (up to 3 per ring), giving: - 2-hydroxymethyl phenol (saligenin) - 2,4-bis(hydroxymethyl) phenol - 2,4,6-tris(hydroxymethyl) phenol ### Stage B — Condensation (Bridge Formation) Methylol groups condense with each other or with active phenol positions to form: - **Methylene bridges:** `–CH₂–` - **Dimethylene ether bridges:** `–CH₂–O–CH₂–` (less thermally stable; converts to –CH₂– on further heating) $$\text{2 HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} + \text{CH}_2\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ $$\text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{H–C}_6\text{H}_3\text{(OH)–} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_3\text{(OH)–} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ --- ## 2.2 Mechanism of Acid vs. Base Catalysis ### 🔬 Acid-Catalyzed (NOVOLAC) — Electrophilic Attack on Phenol 1. Protonation of HCHO: $\text{HCHO} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow {}^+\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ 2. Electrophilic attack on phenol ring at *o/p* positions. 3. Methylol formed, water eliminated → *o/p*-quinone methide intermediate. 4. Quinone methide attacks another phenol → **methylene bridge**. > Net result: predominance of **–CH₂–** bridges (no free methylols in final resin). ### 🔬 Base-Catalyzed (RESOL) — Phenoxide Attack on Formaldehyde 1. NaOH deprotonates phenol: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- + \text{H}^+$ 2. Phenoxide (nucleophile) attacks C of HCHO. 3. Forms methylol intermediates, which are stable under alkaline conditions. 4. On heating, methylols condense → methylene/ether bridges. > Net result: resin contains both **methylol groups and ether bridges** — making it self-curing. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE/ISE for Module 1. Avoid using the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|-------------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $f_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Average functionality of monomer mixture | Dimensionless | | 2 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}}$ | Carothers equation (step-growth) | $\bar{X}_n$ = number-average DP | | 3 | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{f_{avg}}$ | **Gel point** (Carothers) for A–B system | Critical extent of reaction | | 4 | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{f}$ | Gel point for homopolymer of A_f | For A_f monomer | | 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_i (f_i - 2) \, p^2 \, N_i$ | Fraction of all bonds leading to crosslinks (Stockmayer) | $\tau$ = crosslink density | | 6 | $\Delta H_p = \Delta h \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{M_0}$ | Volumetric heat of polymerization | J/g or J/cm³ | | 7 | $M_c = \dfrac{\rho}{\nu_e}$ | Molecular weight between crosslinks | g/mol | | 8 | $\eta = \eta_0 \, \exp\left(\dfrac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)$ | Viscosity-temperature relation (curing) | Pa·s | > [!IMPORTANT] > For PF resin, $f_{avg}$ at gel point must be calculated considering only the *effective* functionality of phenol (since formaldehyde is the bifunctional difunctional linker, $f = 2$). Use $p_c = 2/f$ for a system where **only phenol is the crosslinker**. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Sector | Application | Reason | |--------|-------------|--------| | **Electrical** | Switchboards, plugs, insulators | High dielectric strength, flame retardance | | **Automotive** | Brake pads, clutch plates | High heat resistance, char yield | | **Adhesives** | Plywood laminates (Resol) | Strong wood bonding, water resistance | | **Foundry** | Sand binders | Cures under heat to bind sand grains | | **Aerospace** | Ablative heat shields | High char yield protects substrate | | **Friction materials** | Brake linings | Maintains friction at high temperatures | > [!TIP] > In your KTU answer, always quote **two real applications** when asked to "discuss the engineering significance" — the examiner awards the *application mark* generously. --- ## 2.5 Comparative Logic Flow — Resol vs. Novolac **Step 1:** Determine molar ratio (F/P). **Step 2:** Choose catalyst type. **Step 3:** Below 1 ratio + acid → Novolac (linear, fusible, thermoplastic-like *before cure*). **Step 4:** Above 1 ratio + base → Resol (branched, contains free methylols, *one-stage thermoset*). **Step 5:** Novolac needs **hexamethylenetetramine (Hexa)** as hardener (~ 8 – 15 phr). **Step 6:** Hexa decomposes at 120 °C to give NH₃ + HCHO, providing methylene bridges. > The "Why" and "How": > - **Why** is Novolac stable without hardener? Because acid catalysis gives *only* methylene bridges and **no free methylol groups** remain. > - **How** does Hexa cure Novolac? It supplies *additional* formaldehyde and ammonia-derived crosslink points (–CH₂–NH–CH₂– and triazine rings). # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Gel Point of Phenol-Formaldehyde System Consider a **Novolac** system with acid catalyst. Phenol (functionality 3) and formaldehyde (functionality 2) react. ### Step 1 — Identify Reactive Functionalities - Phenol contributes **3 reactive H positions** (2 *o*, 1 *p*) — thus $f_{phenol} = 3$. - Formaldehyde contributes **2 reactive C–H bonds** — thus $f_{HCHO} = 2$. ### Step 2 — Stoichiometric Mole Balance For a feed with $N_P$ moles of phenol and $N_F$ moles of formaldehyde: - Total reactive sites from phenol: $N_P \cdot 3$ - Total reactive sites from formaldehyde: $N_F \cdot 2$ ### Step 3 — Average Functionality $$ \begin{aligned} f_{avg} &= \frac{N_P \cdot 3 + N_F \cdot 2}{N_P + N_F} \\ &= \frac{3 N_P + 2 N_F}{N_P + N_F} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion logic:** This is simply the *weighted arithmetic mean* of the functionalities, weighted by their mole fractions. The denominator normalizes by total mole count. ### Step 4 — Identify Limiting Reactant In Novolac, formaldehyde is **limiting** (F/P < 1). The extent of reaction $p$ is measured against the *formaldehyde* (difunctional) limit. Hence, for the **Carothers gel-point formula** in an **A–B** system where B is difunctional: $$ p_c = \frac{2}{f_{phenol}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 $$ > **Conversion logic:** For a step-growth system where *only one monomer is multifunctional*, gel forms when the *multifunctional monomer* has reacted to the extent required to branch the system infinitely. Carothers derived this as the critical point where $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$. ### Step 5 — Use of Stockmayer (Statistical) Gel Point A more accurate treatment (Stockmayer, 1952) gives: $$ p_c = \frac{1}{(f_{phenol} - 1) \cdot r} \quad \text{where } r = \frac{N_F}{N_P} \cdot \frac{2}{3} $$ > **Conversion logic:** Stockmayer considered the **probability of branching** rather than average. The factor $r$ normalizes the actual formaldehyde–phenol ratio to its stoichiometric ideal. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A Novolac resin is prepared using **1.0 mol phenol** and **0.8 mol formaldehyde** with HCl catalyst. Calculate (a) the average functionality, (b) the gel point extent of reaction. ### Solution: **(a) Average functionality** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{avg} &= \frac{N_P \cdot f_P + N_F \cdot f_F}{N_P + N_F} \\ &= \frac{(1.0)(3) + (0.8)(2)}{1.0 + 0.8} \\ &= \frac{3.0 + 1.6}{1.8} \\ &= \frac{4.6}{1.8} \\ &\approx 2.556 \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Gel point (Carothers) using multifunctional-monomer-limited case** Since HCHO is the limiting reagent ($N_F < N_P$, F/P = 0.8 < 1), gel point with respect to formaldehyde conversion is: $$ p_c = \frac{2}{f_{phenol}} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.667 \quad \text{(or 66.7 \%)} $$ If we instead use the **Stockmayer** approach: $$ \begin{aligned} r &= \frac{N_F}{N_P} \cdot \frac{f_F}{f_P} = (0.8) \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 0.5333 \\ p_c &= \frac{1}{(f_P - 1)\, r} = \frac{1}{(3-1)(0.5333)} = \frac{1}{1.0666} \approx 0.9375 \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The Stockmayer value is more conservative — gel forms only when 93.75 % of phenol H's have reacted. This is the *realistic* KTU-correct answer. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Conditions → Product Type | Reaction Condition | Product Family | Branching | Curing Behavior | |--------------------|----------------|-----------|-----------------| | F/P < 1, **acid** | **Novolac** | Linear/lightly branched | Needs Hexa hardener | | F/P > 1, **base** | **Resol** | Branched (free methylols) | Self-curing on heat | | F/P = 1, base | **Resitol** (intermediate) | Crosslinked but not infusible | Rubber-like B-stage | | Heat-cured Resol | **Resite (C-stage)** | Fully crosslinked | Infusible, insoluble thermoset | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Crosslink Density and Modulus The **crosslink density** $\nu_e$ (mol/cm³ of network chains per cm³) relates to molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$: $$ \nu_e = \frac{\rho}{M_c} - \frac{2}{M_0} $$ > For highly crosslinked PF resin, the second term is negligible, so $\nu_e \approx \rho / M_c$. The **equilibrium elastic modulus** (rubber elasticity theory applied above Tg) is: $$ E = 3 \, \nu_e \, R T = 3 \, \dfrac{\rho \, R T}{M_c} $$ > **Conversion logic:** This is the classical **kinetic theory of rubber elasticity** of Flory. For PF resin, since Tg is higher than the decomposition temperature, this equation is *not* directly measurable, but conceptually important for the structure-property relationship examiners love to test. --- ## 3.5 Summary of Logical Steps in the KTU Answer For any PF-resin question, structure your derivation as: 1. **Write the reaction** (with structural formulas). 2. **Identify the catalyst system** (acid/base). 3. **State the F/P ratio** and identify **limiting reactant**. 4. **Compute $f_{avg}$** using mole balance. 5. **Apply** $p_c = 2/f$ (Carothers) **or Stockmayer** formula. 6. **Interpret**: $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$ at gel point → **infinite network** forms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Logic Trap:** A common KTU mistake is to use $\bar{X}_n = 2/(2 - p \cdot f_{avg})$ and set $\bar{X}_n = \infty$ to get $p_c = 2/f_{avg}$. This is **valid only for homopolymerization of A_f monomers**, not for A–B systems. *Always specify the system before applying the formula.* # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — PF Resin Synthesis The diagram below maps the **synthesis pathway**, branching based on the **catalyst and F/P ratio**, to the final product family. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phenol C6H5OH] --> B{Catalyst Selection} C[Formaldehyde HCHO] --> B B -->|Acid Catalyst
HCl, H2SO4, Oxalic| D[Novolac Synthesis Path] B -->|Base Catalyst
NaOH, NH4OH| E[Resol Synthesis Path] D -->|F to P ratio less than 1
F less than P| D1[Methylolation Stage] D1 --> D2[Condensation Stage] D2 --> D3[Linear Novolac Resin
Fusible and Soluble] D3 --> D4[Add Hexa Hardener
8 to 15 phr] D4 --> D5[Heat at 120 to 180 C] D5 --> D6[Crosslinked Cured PF Resin
Thermoset Final Product] E -->|F to P ratio greater than 1
F greater than P| E1[Methylolation Stage
Base Catalyzed] E1 --> E2[Branched Resol Resin
Contains Free Methylols] E2 --> E3[Direct Heat Cure
120 to 180 C] E3 --> E6[Crosslinked Cured PF Resin
Thermoset Final Product] D6 --> F[Applications:
Switches, Brake Pads,
Plywood Adhesive] E6 --> F ``` ## 4.2 Reaction Chemistry — Block Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A[Stage 1 Methylolation] A1[Phenol Ring] --> A2[Ortho or Para Attack] A2 --> A3[Methylol Intermediate
CH2OH on Ring] end subgraph B[Stage 2 Condensation] A3 --> B1[Methylol Plus Methylol] A3 --> B2[Methylol Plus Phenol H] B1 --> B3[Methylene Bridge CH2] B1 --> B4[Dimethylene Ether Bridge CH2 O CH2] B2 --> B3 end subgraph C[Stage 3 Network] B3 --> C1[Branched Network] B4 --> C1 C1 --> C2[Crosslinked 3D Thermoset] end ``` ## 4.3 Resol vs. Novolac — Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Start: Select PF Resin Type] --> Q{Is curing agent
available on site?} Q -->|No, want one-pack| R1[Choose Resol] Q -->|Yes, Hexa acceptable| R2[Choose Novolac] R1 --> S1[Base catalyst NaOH or NH4OH] R1 --> S2[F to P ratio 1.2 to 1.5] S1 --> T1[Self curing at 120 to 180 C] S2 --> T1 R2 --> U1[Acid catalyst HCl or Oxalic] R2 --> U2[F to P ratio 0.75 to 0.85] U1 --> V1[Add Hexa 8 to 15 phr] U2 --> V1 V1 --> W1[Heat cure at 120 to 180 C] T1 --> X[Final Cured PF Thermoset] W1 --> X ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** When asked "**Differentiate between Resol and Novolac**," these three flowcharts (synthesis, mechanism, and decision matrix) together form a *complete picture* — KTU examiners award full marks when the answer integrates *ratio + catalyst + structure + curing*. ## 4.4 Schemata — Bridge Formation Detail ``` ORTHO-METHYLENE BRIDGE (Stable, Acid Catalysis) ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ C₆H₄—OH │ │ C₆H₄—OH │ │ \ │ │ / │ │ CH₂ │ ←→ │ CH₂ (bridge) │ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ o-connection o-connection DIMETHYLENE ETHER BRIDGE (Intermediate, Base Catalysis) ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ C₆H₄—OH │ │ C₆H₄—OH │ │ \ │ │ / │ │ CH₂—O—CH₂ │ ←→ │ │ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🔹 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) > **Instructions (per KTU 2024):** Answer in **2 – 3 sentences**, with a labeled diagram if applicable. Each question carries **3 marks**. --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Define Phenol-Formaldehyde resin. List two major applications.** **Model Answer:** Phenol-Formaldehyde (PF) resin is a **thermosetting polymer** synthesized by the **step-growth condensation** of phenol (C₆H₅OH) and formaldehyde (HCHO), with the elimination of water. It was the **world's first fully synthetic plastic**, commercialized in 1907 by **Leo Baekeland** under the trade name *Bakelite*. **Two major applications:** 1. **Electrical switchboards and insulators** (high dielectric strength and flame retardance). 2. **Plywood adhesives and brake linings** (excellent heat resistance and bonding strength). *Examiner allocates: 1 mark definition, 1 mark chemical nature, 1 mark applications.* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **What is the difference between Resol and Novolac resins?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | **Resol** | **Novolac** | |---------|-----------|-------------| | Catalyst | **Base** (NaOH / NH₄OH) | **Acid** (HCl / Oxalic acid) | | F/P ratio | **> 1** (excess HCHO) | **< 1** (excess phenol) | | Stage | **One-stage** (self-curing) | **Two-stage** (needs Hexa) | | Free methylol groups | **Present** | Absent | | Bridge type | –CH₂– and –CH₂–O–CH₂– | Mainly –CH₂– | *Examiner allocates: 1.5 marks comparison + 1.5 marks correct technical points.* --- ## 🔹 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Each Part-B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question out of two** internal choices. --- ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** With a neat reaction scheme, explain the **synthesis of Novolac resin**. Discuss the role of the catalyst and the effect of the F/P molar ratio on the structure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **average functionality**, the **Carothers gel point**, and the **Stockmayer gel point** for a Novolac system prepared with **1.0 mol phenol** and **0.7 mol formaldehyde** under acid catalysis. Comment on which value is more realistic. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) **Synthesis of Novolac Resin — Step-Growth Acid Catalysis** **Step 1: Methylolation** In the presence of an acid catalyst (e.g., HCl), formaldehyde is protonated to form the electrophile $\,^+\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$, which attacks the *ortho* and *para* positions of the phenol ring. $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+, \, 90\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} $$ (2- and 4-hydroxymethyl phenols are formed.) **Step 2: Condensation** Methylol groups condense with another phenol ring (eliminating water) or with another methylol (eliminating HCHO + H₂O), forming **methylene (–CH₂–) bridges**. $$ \text{2 HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–OH} + \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 3: Chain Growth and Branching** Continued reaction yields linear and lightly branched **Novolac chains** with $M_n \approx 500$ – $5000$ g/mol. The product is **fusible** and **soluble** in organic solvents. **Role of Catalyst:** Acid protonates formaldehyde, activating it as an electrophile — hence the reaction is *electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS)*. **Effect of F/P Ratio:** - **F/P < 1** → linear/branched chains (excess phenol caps chain ends; **no free methylols**). - **F/P = 1** → maximum crosslinking potential (theoretically full network). - **F/P > 1** → Resol-type (excess HCHO) — switches to base catalysis regime. > **Mark allocation (7 marks):** 2 marks reaction scheme, 2 marks catalyst role, 1 mark F/P effect, 1 mark structural description, 1 mark for flow/coherence. --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) **Given:** $N_P = 1.0$ mol, $N_F = 0.7$ mol, $f_P = 3$, $f_F = 2$. **(i) Average Functionality** $$ \begin{aligned} f_{avg} &= \frac{N_P \cdot f_P + N_F \cdot f_F}{N_P + N_F} \\ &= \frac{(1.0)(3) + (0.7)(2)}{1.0 + 0.7} \\ &= \frac{3.0 + 1.4}{1.7} = \frac{4.4}{1.7} \\ &\approx 2.588 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Carothers Gel Point** Since the system has *one multifunctional monomer* (phenol, $f = 3$) and HCHO is difunctional linker, the Carothers gel point with respect to formaldehyde consumption is: $$ p_c^{\text{Carothers}} = \frac{2}{f_{phenol}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 $$ **(iii) Stockmayer Gel Point** $$ \begin{aligned} r &= \frac{N_F}{N_P} \cdot \frac{f_F}{f_P} = (0.7) \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 0.4667 \\ p_c^{\text{Stockmayer}} &= \frac{1}{(f_P - 1) \cdot r} = \frac{1}{(3 - 1)(0.4667)} = \frac{1}{0.9333} \approx 1.071 \end{aligned} $$ > Since $p_c$ cannot exceed 1 in practice, this means **gel does not form** in this Novolac system with $r = 0.7$ — confirming the **two-stage** nature (no gelation without Hexa hardener). **Comment:** The **Stockmayer value is more realistic** because it accounts for the actual F/P stoichiometric ratio $r$, while the Carothers formula assumes an idealized homopolymerization of A_f. In practice, Novolac resins do **not** gel on their own (since $p_c^{\text{Stock}} > 1$), which is why they require a hardener like Hexa to form the final thermoset network. > **Mark allocation (7 marks):** 2 marks $f_{avg}$ calculation, 1.5 marks Carothers, 1.5 marks Stockmayer, 2 marks interpretation. --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** With a clear mechanism, discuss the **base-catalyzed synthesis of Resol resin**. Explain why the Resol is a **one-stage** resin and can cure on heating without any external hardener. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **function of Hexamethylenetetramine (Hexa)** as a curing agent for Novolac. With suitable chemical equations, show how Hexa supplies the methylene and amine bridges required to fully crosslink the Novolac network. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) **Mechanism of Base-Catalyzed Resol Synthesis** **Step 1: Phenoxide Formation** Sodium hydroxide (or NH₄OH) deprotonates phenol to form the resonance-stabilized **phenoxide ion**, a strong nucleophile: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 2: Nucleophilic Addition to Formaldehyde** The phenoxide oxygen (or carbon) attacks the carbonyl carbon of HCHO in a **Cannizzaro-type** addition: $$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{O}^- $$ On protonation: $$ \text{HO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{OH} $$ Multiple methylolations occur because the *o/p* positions of phenol are highly activated. With F/P > 1, the ring can carry up to **3 methylol groups** (2,4,6-tris(hydroxymethyl) phenol). **Step 3: Resol Formation** Methylol groups condense with each other or with phenolic H's to form **–CH₂–** and **–CH₂–O–CH₂–** bridges: $$ \text{Ar–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{H–Ar} \longrightarrow \text{Ar–CH}_2\text{–Ar} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Why One-Stage?** Because the resin **already contains free methylol groups** (–CH₂OH), which are reactive sites for further crosslinking. On heating (120 – 180 °C), these methylols self-condense, eliminating water to give a fully crosslinked 3-D network. **No external hardener is needed**, hence "**single-stage**" or "**one-pot**" thermoset. > **Mark allocation (7 marks):** 2 marks mechanism, 2 marks structural detail (methylol retention), 2 marks one-stage reasoning, 1 mark conclusion. --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) **Role of Hexamethylenetetramine (Hexa) in Curing Novolac** Hexa has the formula **(CH₂)₆N₄** — a cage-like molecule with 4 nitrogen atoms bridged by 6 methylene groups. It is added at **8 – 15 phr** to Novolac. **Step 1: Thermal Decomposition of Hexa** On heating above ~ 120 °C, Hexa decomposes to supply formaldehyde, ammonia, and amine intermediates: $$ (\text{CH}_2)_6\text{N}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{HCHO} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{Amine fragments} $$ **Step 2: Methylene Bridge Formation** The released HCHO reacts with Novolac phenol rings (similar to synthesis stage) to create new **–CH₂– bridges**. $$ \text{Ar–H} + \text{HCHO} + \text{H–Ar} \longrightarrow \text{Ar–CH}_2\text{–Ar} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 3: Amine Bridge Formation** The released NH₃ and amine fragments react with methylols to form **benzyl amine bridges** (–CH₂–NH–CH₂–) and **dibenzyl amine** linkages. $$ \text{Ar–CH}_2\text{OH} + \text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Ar–CH}_2\text{–NH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{Ar–CH}_2\text{–NH}_2 + \text{HOCH}_2\text{–Ar} \longrightarrow \text{Ar–CH}_2\text{–NH–CH}_2\text{–Ar} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 4: Crosslinking Network** A combination of methylene (–CH₂–) and amine (–CH₂–NH–CH₂–) bridges converts the linear Novolac into a **3-D infusible thermoset network**. > **Why Hexa is the chosen hardener?** > - Single solid material → easy to mix. > - Releases HCHO + NH₃ → provides BOTH methylene and amine bridges. > - Cures in a narrow temperature window (120 – 180 °C). > - No volatile byproducts at moulding pressure. > **Mark allocation (7 marks):** 2 marks Hexa structure, 2 marks decomposition, 2 marks bridge formation equations, 1 mark network explanation. --- > [!WARNING] > ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Conflating "Resol" and "Resite":** Resol is the *uncured* one-stage resin; **Resite (C-stage)** is the *fully cured* thermoset. Resitol is the *intermediate B-stage*. Many students mix these up — examiners *deduct 1 mark* per misuse. > 2. **Forgetting the catalyst role in mechanism:** When writing mechanism, students often skip the *acid protonation of HCHO* or *base deprotonation of phenol*.
