This is a **2-mark deduction** in the mechanism part. > 3. **Using $p_c = 2/f_{avg}$ blindly:** This is only valid for **homopolymerization of A_f**. For A–B systems, the *limiting reactant's* stoichiometry must be checked first. > 4. **No diagram in synthesis question:** A 14-mark synthesis question **always expects a reaction scheme or block diagram**. Without it, expect a **1 – 2 mark** deduction. > 5. **Quoting raw textbook numbers** (e.g., "heat of polymerization = 539 J/g") — better to say "approximately 580 J/g" or quote a range; KTU 2024 expects *approximate engineering* values. --- ## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > **Last 60-Second Revision for KTU Exam** - ✅ **PF Resin** = Phenol + Formaldehyde, condensation polymer, **first synthetic plastic (Bakelite, 1907)**. - ✅ **Phenol functionality = 3** (2 *o*, 1 *p*); **Formaldehyde functionality = 2**. - ✅ **Two types:** **Novolac** (acid, F/P < 1, two-stage, needs Hexa) and **Resol** (base, F/P > 1, one-stage, self-curing). - ✅ **Mechanism:** Acid catalysis → EAS on phenol ring; Base catalysis → phenoxide nucleophilic attack on HCHO. - ✅ **Bridges:** –CH₂– (methylene, stable) and –CH₂–O–CH₂– (dimethylene ether, intermediate, converts to –CH₂– on heating). - ✅ **Carothers Gel Point:** $p_c = 2/f$ (multifunctional monomer). - ✅ **Stockmayer Gel Point:** $p_c = 1/[(f-1)r]$ — more realistic for A–B systems. - ✅ **Hexa hardener** = (CH₂)₆N₄; supplies HCHO + NH₃; gives methylene and amine bridges. - ✅ **Stages of cure:** A-stage (resol, fusible) → B-stage (resitol, rubbery) → C-stage (resite, infusible thermoset). - ✅ **Properties:** High heat resistance, char yield 50 – 60 %, dielectric strength 10 – 16 kV/mm, **irreversibly hard**, decomposes before Tg. - ✅ **Applications:** Switchboards, brake pads, plywood adhesive, foundry sand binder, ablative shields. - ✅ **Key numeric to remember:** ΔHₚ ≈ **–580 J/g**; cure T ≈ **120 – 180 °C**; service T up to **200 °C**. - ✅ **Distinguish from UF and MF resins:** PF has *phenol ring* (heat resistant); UF/MF have *amino* (lower heat resistance). > **🎓 Final Exam Mantra:** "Catalyst decides the family; ratio decides the structure; heat decides the cure."

topics.title: MF Resin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** MF Resin # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of MF Resin ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF) Resin** is a *thermosetting* **aminoplastic** synthesised by the **step-growth condensation polymerisation** of **melamine (1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triamine, C₃H₆N₆)** with **formaldehyde (CH₂O)** in an aqueous medium, typically under mildly alkaline or neutral conditions. The reaction proceeds in two principal stages: 1. **Methylolation (Addition Stage):** Formaldehyde adds across the –NH₂ groups of melamine to form methylol (–CH₂OH) derivatives, predominantly **trimethylolmelamine** and **hexamethylolmelamine**. 2. **Condensation Stage:** The methylol groups condense with each other (or with active hydrogens) to form **methylene bridges (–CH₂–)** and **dimethylene ether bridges (–CH₂–O–CH₂–)**, releasing water as a byproduct. The fully cured network is a **highly cross-linked, three-dimensional structure** in which the triazine rings are interconnected through these methylene/ether linkages, giving rise to exceptional hardness, thermal stability, and water resistance. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** MF resin belongs to the broader family of **amino resins** (alongside urea-formaldehyde, UF). The presence of the *triazine ring* imparts superior properties compared to UF, justifying its use in high-end laminates, dinnerware (e.g., melamine crockery), and flame-retardant coatings. ## 1.2 Monomer Chemistry — Building Blocks of MF Resin ### 1.2.1 Melamine (C₃H₆N₆) - **IUPAC Name:** 1,3,5-Triazine-2,4,6-triamine - **Molecular Weight:** 126.12 g/mol - **Structure:** A central six-membered *1,3,5-triazine ring* (alternating C and N) bearing three exocyclic **–NH₂** amine groups in a 1,3,5 (symmetrical) arrangement. - **Functionality (f):** 6 active hydrogen sites (2 per –NH₂ group × 3 amine groups), making melamine a **hexafunctional monomer** (f = 6). This high functionality is the *primary driver* of dense cross-linking. - **Physical state:** White crystalline solid, melting point ≈ **354 °C**, sparingly soluble in water. ### 1.2.2 Formaldehyde (CH₂O) - **IUPAC Name:** Methanal - **Molecular Weight:** 30.03 g/mol - **Form supplied:** Typically used as **aqueous formalin (37–40% w/w)** or as **paraformaldehyde** (solid oligomer). - **Functionality (f):** 2 (bifunctional), enabling chain extension and cross-linking. - **Reactive form in solution:** Methylene glycol HOCH₂OH (formalin exists predominantly as methylene glycol in water). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Functionality Principle:** When an A_f monomer (here, melamine, f = 6) reacts with a B₂ monomer (here, formaldehyde acts as a 2-functional bridging unit), a **branched and ultimately cross-linked network** is inevitable once conversion exceeds a critical threshold (the *gel point*). The high functionality of melamine (6) drives a very low gel-point conversion and a highly cross-linked final product. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Three-Armed Hub and Connectors" Imagine a **wooden hub with six attachment pegs** (this is melamine — the triazine core with six active H's). Each peg can grab a "connector" (a formaldehyde unit, –CH₂–). When many such hubs are scattered in a beaker, the connectors start zipping hubs together: - **Stage 1 — Attaching the connectors:** Each peg is "capped" with a connector that has a sticky end (methylol formation, –CH₂OH). Now every hub has up to six "sticky arms" sticking out. - **Stage 2 — Joining the arms:** The sticky ends of one hub react with sticky ends (or unused pegs) on neighbouring hubs, forming rigid bridges between hubs. - **End result:** A single giant "molecular sponge" — a **3D network** so interconnected that it cannot melt or dissolve. This is your cured MF resin. > [!TIP] > **Why does MF outperform UF (urea-formaldehyde)?** Urea has only 4 active hydrogens (functionality 4) and a less stable molecular core. Melamine's *triazine ring* is more chemically and thermally stable, plus its higher functionality yields a *denser* cross-link network. That is why a melamine dinnerware plate survives boiling water and a UF-bonded particleboard does not. ## 1.4 Classification Snapshot | Parameter | Value / Description | |---|---| | Polymer class | Thermosetting aminoplastic | | Polymerisation type | Step-growth condensation | | Molar ratio (F : M) typically | 2 : 1 to 3 : 1 | | Catalyst | Alkaline (NaOH, Na₂CO₃) for addition; Acid for curing | | Cured state | Infusible, insoluble, hard, transparent | | Key linkage types | Methylene (–CH₂–) and Dimethylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–) | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reaction Mechanism — The Two-Stage Condensation The synthesis of MF resin is described in the classical **Hunt–Dunn–Megson** framework, which divides the overall process into two discrete kinetic stages. ### Stage A — Methylolation (Addition, pH ≈ 7.5–9.0) At alkaline pH, formaldehyde exists predominantly as the **formaldehyde anion** (H₂C=O + OH⁻ → ⁻CH₂O⁻, the methylene glycolate anion), which is a strong nucleophile. This anion attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of another formaldehyde, and the resulting species attacks the nitrogen of melamine's –NH₂ group. **Overall stoichiometry of full methylolation:** $$ \text{C}_{3}\text{N}_{3}(\text{NH}_{2})_{3} + 6\,\text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{3}\text{N}_{3}(\text{NHCH}_{2}\text{OH})_{6} $$ This product, **hexamethylolmelamine (HMM)**, is the most methylolated species. In practice, commercial resins are mixtures of *mono-* through *hexa-* methylolmelamines, with the average substitution level controlled by the **formaldehyde-to-melamine molar ratio (F/M)** and reaction conditions. **Generalised intermediate:** $$ \text{Mel–(NH}_{2}\text{)}_{3} + n\,\text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{Mel–(NHCH}_{2}\text{OH)}_{n}\text{–(NH}_{2}\text{)}_{3-n} + (3-n)\,\text{H}_{2}\text{O} $$ where *n* ranges from 1 to 6. ### Stage B — Condensation (Cross-linking, pH ≈ 4.5–6.0 or with heat) Methylol groups condense with each other (or with residual –NH– or –NH₂ groups) via two parallel pathways: **Pathway 1 — Methylene Bridge Formation (–CH₂–):** $$ \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{OH} + \text{H–NH–} \longrightarrow \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $$ **Pathway 2 — Dimethylene Ether Bridge Formation (–CH₂–O–CH₂–):** $$ \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{OH} + \text{HOCH}_{2}\text{–NH–} \longrightarrow \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{–O–CH}_{2}\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > At high curing temperatures (>130 °C), the **ether bridges convert to methylene bridges** with loss of formaldehyde: > > –NH–CH₂–O–CH₂–NH– → –NH–CH₂–NH– + HCHO > This is why post-cure emissions of formaldehyde must be controlled in commercial MF laminates. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Data Sheet The following table consolidates all the quantitative relationships required to solve KTU 2024 problems on MF resin. | Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Relationship | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Monomer functionality of melamine, $f_{\text{mel}}$ | $f_{\text{mel}} = 3 \times 2 = 6$ | 3 amine groups × 2 active H's per group | | Monomer functionality of formaldehyde, $f_{\text{F}}$ | $f_{\text{F}} = 2$ | Bifunctional bridging unit | | Average functionality, $\bar{f}$ | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{\sum N_{i} f_{i}}{\sum N_{i}}$ | $N_{i}$ = moles of monomer *i* | | Carothers' extent of reaction, $p$ | $p = \dfrac{N_{0} - N}{N_{0}}$ | $N_{0}$ = initial functional groups; $N$ = unreacted | | Number-average DP, $\overline{X}_{n}$ (for $f$-functional system) | $\overline{X}_{n} = \dfrac{2}{2 - p\,\bar{f}}$ | Valid up to gel point | | Gel-point extent, $p_{c}$ | $p_{c} = \dfrac{2}{\bar{f}}$ | For $A_{f} + B_{2}$ systems | | Stoichiometric imbalance, $r$ | $r = \dfrac{N_{A}}{N_{B}} \le 1$ | $N_{A}$ = total A functional groups | | $\overline{X}_{n}$ with stoichiometric imbalance | $\overline{X}_{n} = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2\,r\,p}$ | Carothers' modified form | | F : M molar ratio | $\dfrac{n_{\text{HCHO}}}{n_{\text{Mel}}}$ | Typically 2 : 1 to 3 : 1 | | Theoretical water released (full cure) | $n_{\text{H}_{2}\text{O}} = 6\,n_{\text{Mel}}$ | Per mole of melamine (max) | | Molecular weight of melamine, $M_{\text{Mel}}$ | $126.12 \,\text{g/mol}$ | C₃H₆N₆ | | Molecular weight of formaldehyde, $M_{\text{F}}$ | $30.03 \,\text{g/mol}$ | CH₂O | | Molar mass of repeat unit (approx. methylene bridged) | $M_{0} \approx 126 + 3 \times 30 = 216 \,\text{g/mol}$ | For 3 : 1 F : M linear model | > [!WARNING] > **Table syntax safeguard:** All absolute-value and "such that" notations inside this table use the LaTeX commands `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid corrupting the markdown pipe-delimited columns. Never write `|x|` inside a table cell. ## 2.3 Why MF Resin Behaves the Way It Does — The "Why" Behind Each Property | Property | Molecular Origin | |---|---| | High hardness & scratch resistance | Dense 3D cross-link network restricts chain mobility | | Excellent water resistance | Triazine ring is hydrophobic; lower free –NH– groups than UF | | Thermal stability (up to ~150–200 °C continuous) | Aromatic-like triazine ring + strong C–N covalent network | | Transparency & colour stability | Pure white solid; no chromophoric impurities; UV-resistant compared to phenolics | | Flame retardancy | Triazine ring releases nitrogen gas on decomposition, diluting combustion gases | | Brittleness | Very high cross-link density; no yielding mechanism | | Irreversibility | Covalent C–N and C–O–C bonds; decomposition rather than melting on heating | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of MF Resin - **Decorative laminates (Formica-type):** Impregnated paper + MF resin binder; surface layer of MF over phenolic-bonded kraft paper core. - **Tableware:** "Melamine" plates, cups, bowls (food-grade MF, mandated to be cured below 70 °C service). - **Adhesives:** Superior water resistance vs. UF → used in exterior-grade plywood and marine boards. - **Coatings:** Cross-linker for alkyd, acrylic, and polyester coatings (HMM-based) — improves hardness, chemical resistance, and gloss. - **Moulding compounds:** Filled MF (with α-cellulose, glass fibre) for electrical fittings, switchboards, and ash trays (legacy use). - **Concrete superplasticisers:** Sulphonated MF condensates act as water-reducing admixtures. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Functionality $\bar{f}$ for a Practical F : M = 3 : 1 Mixture **Problem:** Determine the average functionality $\bar{f}$ of the system when 1 mol of melamine is reacted with 3 mol of formaldehyde. Use the result to estimate the **Carothers' gel point** $p_{c}$. ### Step 1 — Identify the monomer mixture We have two monomers in the system: - Melamine: $N_{\text{Mel}} = 1$ mol, functionality $f_{\text{Mel}} = 6$. - Formaldehyde: $N_{\text{F}} = 3$ mol, functionality $f_{\text{F}} = 2$. ### Step 2 — Apply the average-functionality definition $$ \bar{f} = \frac{\sum N_{i} f_{i}}{\sum N_{i}} $$ Substituting values: $$ \bar{f} = \frac{(1)(6) + (3)(2)}{1 + 3} $$ $$ \bar{f} = \frac{6 + 6}{4} = \frac{12}{4} = 3 $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Carothers gel-point condition For step-growth polymerisation of a multi-functional monomer system, the gel point occurs when $\bar{f} \cdot p_{c} = 2$: $$ p_{c} = \frac{2}{\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 $$ ### Step 4 — Interpret At approximately **66.7 % conversion of functional groups**, the MF system gels into an infinite 3D network. In industrial practice, the resin is therefore *stabilised* at the A-stage (B-stage syrup, ~30–50 % conversion) and shipped to be cured at the user's site. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for industry:** The resin maker stops the reaction before the gel point, dissolves the oligomer in water, and supplies a stable syrup. The moulder/laminator then completes the condensation with heat and acid catalyst to drive $p$ past $p_{c} = 0.667$ and "lock in" the cross-linked network. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation $\overline{X}_{n}$ Just Before Gelation Using the multi-functional Carothers equation: $$ \overline{X}_{n} = \frac{2}{2 - p\,\bar{f}} $$ Substitute $p = 0.66$ (just below gel) and $\bar{f} = 3$: $$ \overline{X}_{n} = \frac{2}{2 - (0.66)(3)} = \frac{2}{2 - 1.98} = \frac{2}{0.02} = 100 $$ Thus, the oligomers grow to an average size of ~100 monomer units *just before* gelation. Beyond the gel point, the finite molecules co-exist with an infinite network. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stoichiometric Mass Balance for an F : M = 2 : 1 Resin **Question:** A lab technician wants to prepare 500 g of MF resin syrup at a F : M molar ratio of 2 : 1 using formalin (37 % w/w HCHO, density ≈ 1.09 g/mL). How many grams of melamine and how many mL of formalin are required? ### Step 1 — Set up the mass balance Let the moles of melamine be $n_{\text{Mel}}$. Then $n_{\text{F}} = 2\,n_{\text{Mel}}$. Theoretical mass of monomers: $$ m_{\text{theor}} = n_{\text{Mel}}\,M_{\text{Mel}} + 2\,n_{\text{Mel}}\,M_{\text{F}} $$ $$ m_{\text{theor}} = n_{\text{Mel}}(126.12) + 2\,n_{\text{Mel}}(30.03) = n_{\text{Mel}}(186.18) \;\text{g} $$ ### Step 2 — Solve for $n_{\text{Mel}}$ (assuming 100 % conversion and 100 % yield for sizing) $$ n_{\text{Mel}} = \frac{500}{186.18} \approx 2.686 \;\text{mol} $$ ### Step 3 — Mass of melamine $$ m_{\text{Mel}} = 2.686 \times 126.12 \approx 338.8 \;\text{g} $$ ### Step 4 — Mass and volume of formalin $$ m_{\text{F,total}} = 2 \times 2.686 \times 30.03 \approx 161.3 \;\text{g of pure HCHO} $$ Since formalin is 37 % w/w: $$ m_{\text{formalin}} = \frac{161.3}{0.37} \approx 436.0 \;\text{g} $$ $$ V_{\text{formalin}} = \frac{436.0}{1.09} \approx 400.0 \;\text{mL} $$ ### Step 5 — Sanity check Total mass input: $338.8 + 436.0 = 774.8$ g. The difference between this input and 500 g of "resin" reflects the water introduced by the formalin (63 % × 436 = 274.7 g water) and water released by condensation (theoretically ~2 mol per mol melamine = 96.4 g). The syrup therefore contains MF oligomers dissolved in water — exactly the commercial form. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Position of MF Resin | Attribute | Urea-Formaldehyde (UF) | Melamine-Formaldehyde (MF) | Phenol-Formaldehyde (PF) | |---|---|---|---| | Co-monomer functionality | 4 | 6 | 3 (on P) + 2 (on F) | | Water resistance | Poor | Excellent | Good | | Heat resistance | Moderate | High | High | | Colour stability | Yellows | Excellent (white) | Darkens (reddish-brown) | | Cost | Lowest | Highest of the three | Moderate | | Typical use | Particle board, indoor | Laminates, dinnerware, coatings | Brake linings, Bakelite | | Cross-link bridge | Methylene | Methylene + Ether | Methylene + Ether | --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Calculating Released Water During Curing For a 1 mol melamine / 3 mol formaldehyde system undergoing **full cure** (every methylol converted to a methylene bridge with another nitrogen): **Per melamine unit:** 3 methylols (assuming only 3 F reacted) condense with 3 –NH– sites, releasing 3 H₂O molecules. **Generalised maximum water release:** $$ n_{\text{H}_{2}\text{O}}^{\max} = 6\,n_{\text{Mel}} \quad \text{(for hexamethylolmelamine full condensation)} $$ For partial substitution with $n$ methylols per melamine molecule: $$ n_{\text{H}_{2}\text{O}} = n_{\text{Mel}}\,(n - 1) \quad \text{(if all methylols form methylene bridges)} $$ **Numerical illustration:** If $n_{\text{Mel}} = 2.686$ mol (from §3.3) and the average substitution is trimethylol ($n = 3$): $$ n_{\text{H}_{2}\text{O}} = 2.686 \times 2 = 5.37 \;\text{mol} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{\text{H}_{2}\text{O}} = 5.37 \times 18.02 \approx 96.8 \;\text{g} $$ This 96.8 g of water must be driven off (or sealed under pressure) during the lamination hot-press cycle to obtain a void-free, fully cured laminate. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Monomer & Repeat-Unit Structural Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram) The diagram below maps the molecular architecture of the MF system: melamine's triazine core, formaldehyde's bridging role, methylol intermediates, and the final cross-linked network. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["MELAMINE CORE

In reality, the cross-linked network is *infinite* and *aperiodic*; the diagram is a *schematic*, not a literal atom map. The bridge labels (`CH2 BRIDGE`, `CH2 O CH2`) identify the chemical linkage type. ## 4.4 Curing Stages — A-Stages of MF Resin The phenolic/amino-resin literature classically describes the resin's progress through three *A-stages*: | Stage | Name | State | Solubility | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | Resol / A-stage | Low MW liquid/syrup | Water-soluble | Impregnation of paper/fabric | | B | Resitol / B-stage | Soft solid | Swells only | Intermediate handling | | C | Resite / C-stage | Hard, infusible | Insoluble | Final cured product | > [!IMPORTANT] > The A → B transition is achieved by **modest heating**; the B → C transition requires **heat + (often) acid catalyst** and is **irreversible**. Once at C-stage, the resin cannot be reprocessed — it must be machined to shape before final cure. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme] **Define MF resin. List any two of its distinguishing properties and one major application.** **Model Answer:** MF resin is the thermosetting condensation polymer of **melamine (1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triamine)** and **formaldehyde**, having a 3D cross-linked network held together by methylene (–CH₂–) and dimethylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–) bridges. **Distinguishing properties (any two):** - Excellent **water resistance** (superior to UF resin). - High **thermal stability** up to ~150–200 °C continuous service. - Outstanding **hardness and scratch resistance**. - **Colour stability** and **transparency** (does not yellow like UF). - Inherent **flame retardancy** (N₂ release from triazine ring on pyrolysis). **Major application:** Decorative surface laminates (Formica-type sheets) for countertops and furniture, or unbreakable melamine dinnerware (cups, plates, bowls). *(Word count guideline: ~80–100 words; allocate 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for properties, 1 mark for application.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme] **Differentiate between the A-stage and C-stage of an MF resin. Why is the resin shipped in A-stage?** **Model Answer:** - **A-stage:** Low-molecular-weight, water-soluble liquid syrup (resol). The polymer chains are short, linear, and have not yet formed the cross-linked network. The resin is fusible and soluble. - **C-stage:** Fully cured, highly cross-linked, infusible, insoluble hard solid (resite). The 3D network is locked in by methylene and ether bridges. **Why shipped in A-stage?** Because A-stage resin is still fusible, soluble, and impregnable — it can be soaked into paper, fabric, or wood fillers, and can then be moulded/laminated by the end user with heat and pressure. If shipped in C-stage, it would be a hard, infusible solid that cannot be shaped or used as a binder. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme ESE, Module 1]** **(a)** With the help of a balanced chemical equation, explain the **methylolation reaction** in the synthesis of melamine-formaldehyde resin. Discuss the role of **pH** in determining the dominant product. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **condensation stage** of MF resin synthesis. Compare the **methylene bridge** and the **dimethylene ether bridge** with respect to their formation conditions, stability, and interconversion. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-A (a) **1. Definition and stoichiometry (2 marks)** Methylolation is the **addition** of formaldehyde to the amine (–NH₂) groups of melamine to form methylol (hydroxymethyl) intermediates. The general balanced equation for *trimethylolmelamine* formation is: $$ \text{C}_{3}\text{N}_{3}(\text{NH}_{2})_{3} + 3\,\text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{3}\text{N}_{3}(\text{NHCH}_{2}\text{OH})_{3} $$ For *hexamethylolmelamine* (the maximum substitution product): $$ \text{C}_{3}\text{N}_{3}(\text{NH}_{2})_{3} + 6\,\text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{3}\text{N}_{3}(\text{NHCH}_{2}\text{OH})_{6} $$ **2. Mechanism in 3 steps (3 marks)** - **Step i:** In alkaline medium, formaldehyde is converted to the methylene glycolate anion, ⁻CH₂O⁻, a strong nucleophile: $$ \text{HCHO} + \text{OH}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \,^{-}\text{CH}_{2}\text{O}^{-} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $$ - **Step ii:** The nitrogen lone pair of melamine's –NH₂ attacks the carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde (or the carbanion above), forming a C–N bond: $$ \text{–NH}_{2} + \text{HCHO} \longrightarrow \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{OH} $$ - **Step iii:** With excess formaldehyde, the same nitrogen can react a second time, producing a *di-methylol* amine, eventually leading to trimethylol- and hexamethylol-melamine. **3. Role of pH (2 marks)** - **Alkaline pH (7.5–9.0):** Favours methylolation. The dominant species are the methylol derivatives; the reaction stops at the addition product. Industrially, methylolation is therefore conducted with **NaOH or Na₂CO₃** as catalyst. - **Acidic pH (<6):** Favours the *condensation* of methylols to form methylene/ether bridges; methylolation is suppressed because methylols are immediately consumed in condensation. - **Neutral pH:** Slow methylolation; reaction mixture is metastable — used to *store* the A-stage resin for limited time. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A-a):** Students commonly write "formaldehyde attacks melamine" without specifying the **nucleophilic role of the amine nitrogen** and the **electrophilic carbonyl carbon**. Always state the *direction of electron flow* and the role of the alkaline catalyst in generating the methylene glycolate nucleophile. Marks are lost for hand-wavy mechanism statements. #### Model Solution to Q-A (b) **1. The condensation stage — definition and general scheme (2 marks)** Once the methylol intermediates are formed, the reaction is switched to acidic pH (≈ 4.5–6.0) and the temperature is raised (90–150 °C). Under these conditions, methylol groups condense with each other or with available –NH– groups, eliminating water. **2. Methylene bridge formation (2.5 marks)** $$ \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{OH} + \text{H–NH–} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^{+}, \Delta} \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $$ - **Conditions:** Strongly favoured under **acid catalysis, high temperature, and low formaldehyde excess**. - **Properties:** Thermodynamically the **most stable** linkage; it is the dominant bridge in fully cured MF networks. - **Formed preferentially** when curing temperature is high (>130 °C). **3. Dimethylene ether bridge formation (2.5 marks)** $$ \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{OH} + \text{HOCH}_{2}\text{–NH–} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^{+}, \Delta} \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{–O–CH}_{2}\text{–NH–} + \text{H}_{2}\text{O} $$ - **Conditions:** Favoured at **lower temperatures and higher formaldehyde excess**. - **Properties:** Less thermally stable; reverts to methylene bridge on further heating. **4. Interconversion (2 marks)** At elevated temperatures (>130 °C), the ether bridge decomposes to a methylene bridge with **loss of one formaldehyde molecule**: $$ \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{–O–CH}_{2}\text{–NH–} \xrightarrow{\Delta > 130\,^{\circ}\text{C}} \text{–NH–CH}_{2}\text{–NH–} + \text{HCHO} $$ This is why post-curing MF laminates can release small quantities of formaldehyde — a critical concern for **indoor air quality** and **food-contact applications** (e.g., melamine dinnerware). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A-b):** Do *not* state that "ether bridges are stronger than methylene bridges" — this is incorrect. The methylene bridge is the more stable linkage. Also, do not omit the *formaldehyde release* from the ether-to-methylene conversion; this is the most-tested concept in ESE answers. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme ESE, Module 1]** **(a)** Compute the **average functionality** $\bar{f}$ and the **Carothers' gel-point extent** $p_{c}$ for a melamine-formaldehyde system with a **F : M molar ratio of 2 : 1**. Estimate the **number-average degree of polymerisation** $\overline{X}_{n}$ at $p = 0.65$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List the major **industrial applications** of MF resin. Explain why MF resin is preferred over UF resin for **kitchen laminates and dinnerware**, citing at least four property-based reasons. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-B (a) **Step 1 — Average functionality (3 marks)** Given $N_{\text{Mel}} = 1$ mol with $f_{\text{Mel}} = 6$ and $N_{\text{F}} = 2$ mol with $f_{\text{F}} = 2$: $$ \bar{f} = \frac{(1)(6) + (2)(2)}{1 + 2} = \frac{6 + 4}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33 $$ **Step 2 — Carothers' gel-point extent (2 marks)** $$ p_{c} = \frac{2}{\bar{f}} = \frac{2}{3.33} \approx 0.60 $$ So the system gels when **~60 % of all functional groups have reacted**. **Step 3 — Number-average DP at $p = 0.65$ (2 marks)** Since $p = 0.65 > p_{c} = 0.60$, the system is *just past* the gel point. The Carothers expression $\overline{X}_{n} = \frac{2}{2 - p\bar{f}}$ is technically valid only for the **sol fraction** (the finite molecules coexisting with the gel). The denominator becomes: $$ 2 - p\bar{f} = 2 - (0.65)(3.33) = 2 - 2.165 = -0.165 $$ A **negative denominator** indicates that the *finite sol fraction* is shrinking as more material is absorbed into the gel; the *average size of the sol molecules* still grows, but the macroscopic system is now an infinite network. A physically meaningful statement is: > *"At $p = 0.65$, which is above the calculated gel point of $p_{c} \approx 0.60$, the system has gelled. The number-average DP of the finite sol is no longer meaningful in the standard Carothers' sense; the polymer now exists as a 3D infinite network coexisting with finite oligomers."* For **pedagogy**, the examiner typically expects the student to (i) compute $\bar{f}$, (ii) state $p_{c}$, and (iii) recognise the regime — not to manipulate the negative denominator. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B-a):** Many students mechanically compute $\overline{X}_{n}$ even after the gel point and report a *negative* number. Always *check* whether $p$ is below or above $p_{c}$ before plugging into the Carothers' formula. State the physical interpretation explicitly. #### Model Solution to Q-B (b) **Major industrial applications (3.5 marks):** 1. **Decorative surface laminates** (Formica-type) for kitchen countertops, furniture, flooring. 2. **Melamine dinnerware** (unbreakable plates, cups, bowls for institutional catering). 3. **Adhesives** for exterior-grade plywood, marine plywood, and particleboard (waterproof bonding). 4. **Coatings and paints** — HMM as a cross-linker for alkyd, acrylic, and polyester coatings. 5. **Moulding compounds** — α-cellulose-filled MF for electrical fittings, switchboards. 6. **Concrete admixtures** — sulphonated MF as superplasticisers. 7. **Textile finishing** — wrinkle-resistant, flame-retardant finishes on cotton. **Why MF is preferred over UF for kitchen laminates and dinnerware (3.5 marks — at least four reasons):** | # | Property | MF Resin | UF Resin | Why it matters for kitchen use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Water resistance | Excellent | Poor | Plates are washed daily; laminates near sinks | | 2 | Heat resistance | Up to 150–200 °C | Up to ~80 °C | Hot vessels placed on dinnerware/counter | | 3 | Colour stability | Does not yellow | Yellows with age | Aesthetics of white kitchen surfaces | | 4 | Hardness / scratch resistance | Very high | Moderate | Daily utensil use, cleaning | | 5 | Chemical resistance | Good | Marginal | Detergents, food acids (lemon, vinegar) | | 6 | Flame retardancy | Inherent (triazine-N₂) | Poor | Kitchen-safety consideration | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B-b):** Do not list *only* applications without justifying the comparison. Marks are split 50/50 between the application list and the property-based comparison. State at least four distinct properties *and* link each to a kitchen use scenario. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for MF Resin (Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme):** - **MF resin = Melamine + Formaldehyde** (condensation polymerisation, thermosetting aminoplastic). - **Monomers and functionalities:** Melamine ($f = 6$, triazine ring with 3 –NH₂ groups); Formaldehyde ($f = 2$, methylene glycolate anion is the active species in alkaline medium). - **Two reaction stages:** **Methylolation** (alkaline, addition, forms –CH₂OH on N) → **Condensation** (acid + heat, forms –CH₂– and –CH₂–O–CH₂– bridges with loss of H₂O). - **Bridges:** - Methylene (–CH₂–): More stable, formed at high temperature. - Dimethylene ether (–CH₂–O–CH₂–): Less stable, formed at lower T and high F excess; reverts to methylene with HCHO release > 130 °C. - **Average functionality formula:** $\bar{f} = \frac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$. - **Carothers' gel point:** $p_{c} = 2 / \bar{f}$. - **Modified $\overline{X}_{n}$:** $\overline{X}_{n} = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$, valid for $p < p_{c}$. - **Typical F : M ratio:** 2 : 1 to 3 : 1 in commercial resins. - **A-stage:** Water-soluble syrup (resol); B-stage: swells; C-stage: infusible, fully cross-linked (resite). Resin is shipped in **A-stage**. - **Cured MF properties:** Hard, scratch-resistant, transparent, water-resistant, thermally stable (up to ~150–200 °C), inherently flame-retardant (N₂ release), *brittle* (no yield), insoluble in all solvents. - **Key applications:** Decorative laminates, melamine dinnerware, exterior adhesives, coatings cross-linker, electrical mouldings, concrete superplasticisers. - **MF vs UF comparison:** MF has higher functionality (6 vs 4), superior water/heat resistance, no yellowing, and inherent flame retardancy — hence the higher cost and preference for high-end uses. - **Engineering concerns to remember:** Formaldehyde release during cure and service → drives regulations on F-emission standards (E0, E1, E2 grades for wood panels). - **Catalyst memory aid:** **"Add at Alkaline, Cure at Acidic"** — methylolation needs OH⁻, condensation needs H⁺.

topics.title: Nylon 6
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Nylon 6 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Nylon 6** (chemically known as **polycaprolactam** or **polyamide 6 (PA 6)**) is a **semi-crystalline thermoplastic linear polyamide** prepared by the **ring-opening polymerisation (ROP)** of the cyclic lactam monomer **ε-caprolactam** (a 7-membered cyclic amide). The repeating structural unit contains **six methylene (–CH₂–) groups** followed by **one amide linkage (–NH–CO–)**, giving the characteristic empirical repeat unit **–[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]–**. The IUPAC systematic name of the repeat unit is **poly[imino(1-oxohexane-1,6-diyl)]** and the CAS Registry Number for the polymer is **25038-54-4**. The degree of crystallinity in commercially produced Nylon 6 typically ranges between **30–40%**, with an equilibrium melting temperature of approximately **Tₘ ≈ 225 °C** and a glass transition temperature of **Tₘ ≈ 225 °C** and **T_g ≈ 50 °C**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Nylon 6 belongs to the family of **step-growth/lactam polymerisation polymers** and is a *mandatory* topic under Module 1 ("Polymerisation Mechanisms and Industrial Polymers"). Students must be able to draw the mechanism, write the repeat unit, and compare it with Nylon 6,6. > [!NOTE] > **Key Identity Markers of Nylon 6** > - Repeat unit: **–[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]–** > - Monomer: **ε-caprolactam** (C₆H₁₁NO, M = 113.16 g/mol) > - Average repeat unit molecular weight: **M₀ = 113.16 g/mol** > - Melting point: **≈ 220–225 °C** > - Density: **1.13 g/cm³** (amorphous 1.09; crystalline 1.23) > - Moisture regain: **≈ 3.5–4.5%** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine **ε-caprolactam** as a single, tightly locked **padlock** (a closed ring) — it cannot link to anything while the ring is closed. The polymerisation process is like **snapping open each padlock and threading the broken ends into a long chain of interlocked rings**. Once enough rings snap and link, the resulting chain is the polymer — **Nylon 6** — and the chain has such strong inter-chain "handshakes" (the amide *H-bonds*) that it becomes a tough, flexible, and durable plastic used in everything from toothbrush bristles to automotive gears. Another useful analogy: think of caprolactam molecules as **individual paper clips**, each closed into a ring. Heating with a little water (or a strong base) is like using a "ring-opener tool" that not only opens each clip but also welds the open ends to neighbouring clips. The result: a continuous chain of welded clips — the Nylon 6 chain. ### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context Nylon 6 was first synthesised by **Paul Schlack** at IG Farbenindustrie (Germany) in **1938**, inspired by the earlier Wallace Carothers work on Nylon 6,6 at DuPont. Because the monomer ε-caprolactam is cheaply obtained from **cyclohexanone–oxime** (via the Beckmann rearrangement), Nylon 6 became Europe's preferred polyamide and is now globally the **second-largest volume polyamide** after Nylon 6,6, with global production exceeding **5 million metric tonnes per year**. > [!NOTE] > **Trade Names of Nylon 6 (Industry Recognition)** > - **Perlon** (historical, Germany) > - **Akulon** (DSM, Netherlands) > - **Ultramid B** (BASF, Germany) > - **Durethan B** (Lanxess, Germany) > - **Capron** (Allied Signal, USA) > - **Nylatron**, **Zytel**, **Grilon** — common engineering grades. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Monomer — ε-Caprolactam ε-Caprolactam is a **7-membered cyclic amide (lactam)** whose structure contains a strained amide bond. This ring strain (and the stability of the opened linear amide) is the **thermodynamic driving force** for ring-opening polymerisation. **Physical & chemical constants of ε-caprolactam:** | Property | Value | Units | |----------|-------|-------| | Molecular formula | C₆H₁₁NO | — | | Molar mass (M_m) | **113.16** | g/mol | | Melting point | **69.2** | °C | | Boiling point | **268–270** | °C | | Density (melt) | **1.02** | g/cm³ | | Ring strain energy | ~**25–30** | kJ/mol | | Solubility in water | Freely soluble | — | ### 2.2 Three Industrial Routes to Nylon 6 1. **Hydrolytic polymerisation** (cationic / water-initiated) — *the most common industrial route*; uses **0.5–5% water** as initiator at **250–270 °C** under nitrogen at slightly elevated pressure. 2. **Anionic polymerisation** (activated anionic / "AA-ROP") — used for *cast Nylon 6* (monomer casting); uses strong bases (NaH, Na, KOH) with **N-acetyl caprolactam** as promoter. Extremely fast — completes in minutes in moulds. 3. **Cationic polymerisation** — initiated by protic acids (HCl, H₂SO₄); less important industrially. ### 2.3 Hydrolytic Polymerisation — The Industrial Workhorse The mechanism proceeds in **three reversible steps**: **Step 1 — Initiation (ring opening by water):** Water hydrolyses the caprolactam ring to form **6-aminocaproic acid (ACA)**, the linear "open-ring" species that bears both –NH₂ and –COOH end groups. **Step 2 — Propagation (step-growth polycondensation):** The –NH₂ group of one ACA molecule attacks the –COOH group of another (or the cyclic amide of another caprolactam) forming an amide bond and releasing water. The chain grows step-by-step with **water as the by-product**. **Step 3 — Equilibrium & Cyclic Oligomer Formation:** The reaction is **reversible**; at high temperature, water hydrolyses amide bonds, regenerating caprolactam. Removing water shifts the equilibrium toward high molecular weight. Cyclic oligomers (mainly cyclic dimer) are also formed, lowering yield. ### 2.4 Anionic ("Activated Anionic") Ring-Opening Polymerisation — Mechanism This is the route used in **monomer-cast Nylon 6 (MC Nylon 6)** for large industrial parts. The mechanism is as follows: 1. **Catalyst (e.g., NaH)** deprotonates caprolactam to give the **lactam anion** (a resonance-stabilised amide anion). 2. **Promoter (N-acetyl caprolactam)** reacts with the lactam anion to form the highly reactive **N-acyl lactam (imidate) intermediate**, which is the *true* active centre. 3. The imidate nitrogen attacks another caprolactam carbonyl, opening the ring and regenerating an N-acyl lactam at the new chain end. 4. Chain propagation continues **without loss of any small molecule** — this is **not a polycondensation** but a true **chain-growth addition polymerisation** of a cyclic monomer. The polymerisation enthalpy is **ΔHₚ ≈ –84 kJ/mol of repeat unit** (exothermic). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Distinction Point:** Nylon 6 is *uniquely* synthesised by **ring-opening polymerisation**, NOT by a conventional polycondensation of a diamine + diacid. This contrasts with Nylon 6,6 (HMDA + adipic acid) and is a high-value examination point. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes | |---|----------------------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | Repeat unit MW (M₀) | $M_0 = 113.16$ | g/mol (C₆H₁₁NO) | | 2 | Carothers number-average DP | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = conversion of functional groups | | 3 | Carothers weight-average DP | $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | for A–B step-growth | | 4 | Equilibrium constant (hydrolytic) | $K = \dfrac{[ACA]}{[\text{Caprolactam}][H_2O]}$ | typically ~ 300–800 at 250 °C | | 5 | Polymerisation enthalpy (anionic) | $\Delta H_p \approx -84$ | kJ/mol repeat unit | | 6 | Flory ring-chain equilibrium | $\dfrac{[Cyc]}{[Lin]} = \dfrac{1}{DP}$ | explains cyclic oligomer content | | 7 | Mooney viscosity–MW relation | $\ln \eta = A + B \, M_w^{0.5}$ | empirical, for QC | | 8 | Crystallinity (DSC) | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \times 100\%$ | $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(PA6) = 230$ J/g | | 9 | Glass transition (Fox) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | used for moisture/ blends | | 10 | Moisture-induced Tg depression | $T_g \approx 50 - k \cdot (\%H_2O)$ | $k \approx 5$ °C per % H₂O | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility - **Automotive:** Engine covers, air-intake manifolds, radiator tanks — heat resistance to 180 °C continuous. - **Textiles:** Carpets, parachutes, tyre cord (replacing cotton/rayon) — high tensile strength and abrasion resistance. - **Electrical:** Connectors, switch housings, cable ties — excellent dielectric and arc-tracking resistance. - **Industrial:** Gears, bushings, bearings — low friction, self-lubricating, high impact strength. - **Films:** BOPA (biaxially oriented polyamide 6) films for food packaging — outstanding oxygen barrier. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation (Carothers Equation for Hydrolytic Route) In the **hydrolytic polymerisation** of caprolactam, the active functional groups are the **–NH₂** and **–COOH** ends of the linear species. Water initially opens the ring giving 6-aminocaproic acid (ACA), and further condensation proceeds as a typical **A–B type step-growth polycondensation** (the amine of one chain attacks the acid of another, with loss of water). Let: - $N_0$ = total number of monomer (or ACA) molecules initially present (after water initiation). - $N$ = number of molecules remaining at time $t$ (chains of any size). - $p$ = fraction of functional groups (–NH₂ or –COOH, equal in number) that have reacted. - $\bar{X}_n$ = number-average degree of polymerisation. Each condensation step links **2 molecules into 1**, releasing 1 water molecule. The total number of condensation events that have occurred is: $$ N_0 - N $$ Since each condensation consumes **2 functional groups** (one –NH₂ and one –COOH), the total number of functional groups reacted is: $$ 2(N_0 - N) $$ The initial number of functional groups (each molecule contributes exactly one –NH₂ and one –COOH) is: $$ 2 N_0 $$ Therefore the **fraction of functional groups reacted** is: $$ p = \dfrac{2(N_0 - N)}{2 N_0} = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0} $$ Solving for $N$: $$ N = N_0 (1 - p) $$ The **number-average degree of polymerisation** is the average number of repeat units per chain. Initially there were $N_0$ single monomers. After reaction, those monomers are distributed into $N$ chains. The average chain length is therefore the ratio of initial molecules to remaining molecules: $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{N_0}{N_0 (1 - p)} = \dfrac{1}{1 - p} $$ $$ \boxed{\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1 - p}} $$ > **Conversion logic:** This is the **Carothers equation for A–B step-growth polymerisation**. It is a *mandatory* KTU derivation; appearing frequently in Part B (14-mark) questions. ### 3.2 Derivation II — Weight-Average Degree of Polymerisation (Most-Probable Distribution) For a linear step-growth polymerisation, the fraction of molecules having exactly $x$ repeat units follows the **Flory most-probable distribution**: $$ N_x = N \, (1 - p) \, p^{\,x - 1} $$ where $N_x$ is the number of chains of length $x$. The total mass in chains of length $x$ is $x \, M_0 \, N_x$ and the total mass in the system is $M_0 \, N_0 = M_0 \, N/(1-p)$. The **weight fraction** $w_x$ of chains of length $x$ is: $$ w_x = \dfrac{x \, N_x}{N_0} = \dfrac{x \, N (1-p) p^{x-1}}{N_0} = x \, (1-p)^2 \, p^{\,x-1} $$ The **weight-average DP** is defined by: $$ \bar{X}_w = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \, w_x $$ Substituting: $$ \bar{X}_w = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot x (1-p)^2 p^{x-1} = (1-p)^2 \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 p^{x-1} $$ We use the standard identity: $$ \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 p^{x-1} = \dfrac{1 + p}{(1-p)^3} $$ Therefore: $$ \bar{X}_w = (1-p)^2 \cdot \dfrac{1 + p}{(1-p)^3} = \dfrac{1 + p}{1 - p} $$ $$ \boxed{\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1 + p}{1 - p}} $$ The **polydispersity index** is therefore: $$ \text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1 + p $$ As $p \to 1$, PDI $\to 2$, the classical signature of step-growth polymerisation — a frequently tested KTU result. ### 3.3 Derivation III — Molecular Weight from End-Group Analysis A 14-mark problem may require determining $\bar{M}_n$ of Nylon 6 by titrating the **amine end-groups (–NH₂)** with perchloric acid in phenol/methanol. Let: - $V$ = volume of HClO₄ used (L) - $N$ = normality of HClO₄ (eq/L) - $w$ = mass of Nylon 6 sample (g) Each chain has **one –NH₂** end (assuming equimolar bifunctional growth), so equivalents of –NH₂ = equivalents of acid used: $$ \text{Equivalents of } -NH_2 = V \cdot N $$ Number of molecules (chains) in the sample: $$ \text{No. of molecules} = V \cdot N \cdot N_A $$ The number-average molecular weight is mass per molecule: $$ \bar{M}_n = \dfrac{w}{V \cdot N \cdot N_A} \times N_A = \dfrac{w}{V \cdot N} $$ But since we work in SI-friendly units using moles directly: $$ \bar{M}_n = \dfrac{w \ (\text{g})}{V \cdot N \ (\text{mol})} $$ $$ \boxed{\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{w}{V \cdot N}} $$ > **Sample numerical:** A 2.0 g sample of Nylon 6 required 12.5 mL of 0.02 N HClO₄. Then $\bar{M}_n = 2.0 / (0.0125 \times 0.02) = 8000$ g/mol. ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Equilibrium Constant and Effect of Water on $\bar{X}_n$ For the hydrolytic system, the equilibrium is: $$ \text{Caprolactam} + H_2O \rightleftharpoons \text{ACA (open chain)} $$ The concentration of **water** in the melt must be **removed** (e.g., by a bleed stream or by a final vacuum stage) to drive equilibrium to high molecular weight. If [W] denotes equilibrium water concentration: $$ K = \dfrac{[ACA]}{[\text{Cap}] \, [W]} $$ Since each chain end corresponds to one open unit, the **number of chains per unit volume** is proportional to [ACA] (when all chain ends are linear): $$ N = [\text{ACA}] = K \, [\text{Cap}] \, [W] $$ The DP is then: $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{[\text{Cap}]_0}{N} = \dfrac{[\text{Cap}]_0}{K \, [\text{Cap}] \, [W]} $$ As $[W] \to 0$, $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$ — a key industrial insight explaining why **Nylon 6 melt spinning lines always finish with a vacuum vent extruder**. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Nylon 6 vs Nylon 6,6 | Property / Parameter | **Nylon 6 (PA 6)** | **Nylon 6,6 (PA 66)** | |----------------------|--------------------|------------------------| | Monomer | ε-Caprolactam (single) | HMDA + Adipic acid (two) | | Synthesis | Ring-opening polymerisation (ROP) | Step-growth polycondensation | | Repeat unit | –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– | –[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]– | | M₀ (g/mol) | 113.16 | 226.32 | | Tₘ (°C) | 220–225 | 255–265 | | T_g (°C) | 50 (dry) | 50–60 (dry) | | Density (g/cm³) | 1.13 | 1.14 | | Moisture regain (%) | 3.5–4.5 | 3.5–4.5 | | Cyclic oligomer content | ~ 1–2% (cyclic dimer) | negligible | | Process route | Continuous VK-tube / monomer casting | Autoclave / continuous | | Hydrogen bonding | Every 6th carbon (asymmetric) | Every 6th carbon (regular) | | Impact strength | Higher | Lower | | Surface finish | Better | Acceptable | | Dye affinity | Higher (asymmetric amide) | Lower | | Cost (relative) | Lower | Higher | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Monomer-to-Polymer Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[cyclohexanone] --> B[cyclohexanone oxime] B --> C[epsilon caprolactam] C --> D[caprolactam melt with 0.5 to 5 percent water] D --> E{polymerisation reactor
250 to 270 deg C
N2 pressure} E --> F[Nylon 6 melt
Mn 15000 to 25000] F --> G[extruder with vacuum vent] G --> H[final Nylon 6 chip
Mn greater than 18000] H --> I[spinning fibres] H --> J[injection moulding] H --> K[BOPA film extrusion] ``` ### 4.2 Reaction Pathway — Hydrolytic ROP ```mermaid flowchart TB stepA[epsilon caprolactam 7 membered ring] -->|hydrolysis with H2O at 250 C| stepB[6 aminocaproic acid ACA linear open chain] stepB -->|step growth amide bond formation
releases H2O| stepC[Nylon 6 oligomer DP 3 to 10] stepC -->|repeated condensation
water removal by N2 bleed| stepD[Nylon 6 polymer DP greater than 150] stepD -->|equilibrium
cyclic dimer formation| stepE[cyclic oligomers side product] stepD -->|final vacuum stage| stepF[high MW Nylon 6 chip] ``` ### 4.3 Mechanism of Anionic ROP ```mermaid flowchart LR m1[caprolactam] -->|NaH deprotonation| m2[lactam anion] m2 -->|reaction with N acetyl caprolactam promoter| m3[N acyl lactam imidate active centre] m3 -->|nucleophilic attack on caprolactam carbonyl| m4[ring opened acyl lactam with extended chain] m4 -->|chain propagation
no small molecule release| m5[Nylon 6 chain growing at active end] m5 -->|termination by impurity or proton source| m6[dead Nylon 6 chain] ``` ### 4.4 Repeat-Unit Block Schematic (H-Bonding View) ```mermaid flowchart TB chainA[Nylon 6 chain A] -->|amide C=O| bond1[hydrogen bond] bond1 -->|amide N-H| chainB[Nylon 6 chain B adjacent] chainB -->|amide C=O| bond2[hydrogen bond] bond2 -->|amide N-H| chainC[Nylon 6 chain C next adjacent] chainA --- note1[note every 6th carbon in backbone carries an amide linkage] chainB --- note1 chainC --- note1 ``` > **Engineering interpretation of the schematic:** The H-bond network is **directional** (N–H···O=C), giving Nylon 6 a **pseudo-crosslinked** behaviour in the solid state, which is why it has high strength and crystallinity despite being a thermoplastic. Moisture disrupts these H-bonds, plasticising the polymer and lowering Tg — the reason "dry-as-moulded" Nylon 6 must be **conditioned** (2.5% moisture regain) before use in engineering parts. ### 4.5 VK-Tube Industrial Reactor (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR feed[caprolactam + water feed] --> preheat[preheater 80 C] preheat --> VK1[top section VK tube
temperature 250 C
pressure 5 atm] VK1 --> VK2[middle section VK tube
temperature 260 C
atmospheric pressure water bleed] VK2 --> VK3[finishing section
temperature 250 C
vacuum 50 mbar] VK3 --> pump[melt pump] pump --> die[spinneret or chip cutter] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"Write the repeat unit structure of Nylon 6 and name the monomer used for its preparation."** *(CO1, Remember)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Monomer:** ε-Caprolactam (C₆H₁₁NO, 7-membered cyclic amide). - **Repeat unit:** –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– - **Polymerisation route:** Ring-opening polymerisation (ROP) of caprolactam; industrially by the **hydrolytic route** with 0.5–5% water at 250–270 °C. - **Polymer class:** Aliphatic polyamide (polyamide 6 / PA 6). **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **"Differentiate between hydrolytic and anionic ring-opening polymerisation of caprolactam."** *(CO1, Understand)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Hydrolytic ROP | Anionic ROP | |---------|----------------|--------------| | Initiator | Water (0.5–5%) | NaH / Na / KOH (strong base) | | Promoter | None | N-acetyl caprolactam | | Mechanism | Step-growth condensation (water released) | Chain-growth via N-acyl lactam | | Time | Several hours | Minutes | | Molecular weight | High (controllable) | Moderate | | Use | Fibres, films, moulding | Monomer-cast (MC) Nylon 6, large parts | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]* **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **hydrolytic ring-opening polymerisation of ε-caprolactam** to produce Nylon 6. Include the mechanism, role of water, conditions used, and explain how molecular weight is controlled. **(b) [7 marks]** A 2.50 g sample of Nylon 6 was dissolved in phenol/methanol and titrated against 0.020 N HClO₄. The end-point was reached at 15.0 mL. Calculate the **number-average molecular weight** ($\bar{M}_n$) of the polymer. Given $M_0 = 113$ g/mol, also find the **number-average degree of polymerisation** ($\bar{X}_n$). **Model Solution:** **(a) Hydrolytic ROP of ε-caprolactam (7 marks):** - **Step 1 — Initiation:** Water (catalyst, 0.5–5%) hydrolyses the caprolactam ring at 250 °C to form **6-aminocaproic acid (ACA)**, the linear "open-ring" species with –NH₂ and –COOH groups. $$\text{Caprolactam} + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2N-(CH_2)_5-COOH$$ - **Step 2 — Propagation (step-growth):** The –NH₂ of one ACA molecule attacks the –COOH of another, forming an amide bond and releasing water. The chain grows by **step-growth condensation**. $$H_2N-(CH_2)_5-COOH + H_2N-(CH_2)_5-COOH \rightarrow H_2N-(CH_2)_5-CO-NH-(CH_2)_5-COOH + H_2O$$ - **Step 3 — Equilibrium:** The reaction is reversible; water hydrolyses amide bonds. **Water must be removed** (N₂ bleed, final vacuum) to drive equilibrium to high MW. - **Conditions:** 250–270 °C, N₂ atmosphere, slightly elevated pressure initially then atmospheric/vacuum. - **MW control:** By **(i)** water concentration (lower [H₂O] → higher MW), **(ii)** reaction time, **(iii)** temperature, and **(iv)** chain terminators (mono-functional acids like acetic acid). - **By-products:** Cyclic dimer (~ 1–2%) — must be removed by hot-water extraction. - **Reactor:** VK-tube (Vereinfacht Kontinuierlich = simplified continuous) for industrial production. **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** Given: - $w = 2.50$ g, $V = 15.0$ mL $= 0.0150$ L, $N = 0.020$ N (eq/L), $M_0 = 113$ g/mol. **Step 1 — Moles of –NH₂ (and hence number of chains):** $$ n = V \times N = 0.0150 \times 0.020 = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol} $$ **Step 2 — Number-average molecular weight:** $$ \bar{M}_n = \dfrac{w}{n} = \dfrac{2.50}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 8333 \text{ g/mol} \approx 8330 \text{ g/mol} $$ **Step 3 — Number-average degree of polymerisation:** $$ \bar{X}_n = \dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} = \dfrac{8333}{113} \approx 73.7 \approx 74 $$ **Final Answers:** $\bar{M}_n \approx 8330$ g/mol; $\bar{X}_n \approx 74$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]* **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **anionic (activated anionic) ring-opening polymerisation** of caprolactam. Highlight the role of the catalyst and promoter, and explain why the polymerisation is so much faster than the hydrolytic route. **(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **structure, properties, and applications** of Nylon 6 with Nylon 6,6. Why is Nylon 6 called a "pseudo-step-growth" polymer? **Model Solution:** **(a) Anionic ROP of caprolactam (7 marks):** - **Catalyst:** Strong base such as NaH, metallic Na, or KOH deprotonates caprolactam at the N–H to form a resonance-stabilised **lactam anion**. - **Promoter (co-catalyst):** **N-acetyl caprolactam** reacts with the lactam anion to form the highly reactive **N-acyl lactam (cyclic imidate)** — the actual active centre. - **Propagation:** The imidate nitrogen (nucleophile) attacks the carbonyl carbon of a fresh caprolactam molecule, opening its ring and re-generating a new N-acyl lactam end. **No small molecule is released**; the polymerisation is therefore an *addition* polymerisation in the strict sense. - **Rate:** Because the active species is a strong nucleophile and the ring strain is relieved on opening, propagation is extremely fast — **monomer-to-polymer in 5–30 minutes**, vs. several hours for hydrolytic ROP. - **Industrial use:** **Monomer-cast (MC) Nylon 6** — molten caprolactam + catalyst/promoter is poured into a heated mould where polymerisation occurs *in situ* into large, near-net-shape parts (gears, liners, pulleys). - **Drawback:** Sensitivity to moisture and impurities; the active centres are quenched by proton donors. **(b) Comparison of Nylon 6 vs Nylon 6,6 (7 marks):** | Aspect | Nylon 6 | Nylon 6,6 | |--------|---------|-----------| | **Monomers** | One (caprolactam) | Two (HMDA + adipic acid) | | **Reaction** | Ring-opening | Step-growth polycondensation | | **Repeat unit** | –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– (asymmetric) | –[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]– (symmetric) | | **M₀ (g/mol)** | 113 | 226 | | **Tₘ (°C)** | 220–225 | 255–265 | | **Crystallisation** | Faster, narrower processing window | Slower, broader window | | **Impact strength** | Higher | Lower | | **Dye uptake** | Better (asymmetric amide) | Poorer | | **Cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Cyclic oligomers** | Present (~ 1–2%) | Negligible | | **Applications** | Carpets, fishing net, MC Nylon gears | Tyre cord, engineering plastics | **Why "pseudo-step-growth"?** Although Nylon 6 is synthesised industrially by a *step-growth condensation* (hydrolytic route releasing water), the molecular-weight build-up and Flory statistics ($\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$, PDI = $1+p$) follow step-growth kinetics. Hence Nylon 6 from the hydrolytic route is best classified as **"pseudo-step-growth"** — a nomenclature often tested in KTU examinations. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Points on Nylon 6 Questions (KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Confusing the repeat unit:** Students often write –[NH–(CH₂)₄–CO]– (Nylon 4) or –[NH–(CH₂)₆–CO]– (Nylon 7). For Nylon 6, the *correct* repeat unit is **–[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]–** — exactly **five CH₂ groups** between the amide nitrogens. *Loss: 2–3 marks.* > 2. **Wrong polymerisation type:** Writing "polycondensation of diamine + diacid" instead of **ring-opening polymerisation of caprolactam**. *Loss: 2 marks.* > 3. **Missing cyclic dimer:** Forgetting that ~ 1–2 wt% of **cyclic oligomer (caprolactam dimer)** is always present in Nylon 6 melt and must be removed by hot-water leaching for fibre applications. *Loss: 1–2 marks.* > 4. **Forgetting to remove water in hydrolytic route:** The hydrolytic ROP is *reversible*; without water removal, DP saturates at low values (DP ≈ 50–80). State explicitly that **N₂ bleed + final vacuum stage** is mandatory. *Loss: 2 marks.* > 5. **Numerical end-group analysis:** Mixing up normality with molarity, or forgetting to convert mL → L. *Loss: 1 mark per mistake.* ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Nylon 6 = polycaprolactam = polyamide 6 = PA 6**, prepared by **ring-opening polymerisation** of **ε-caprolactam**. - ✅ **Repeat unit:** –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– ; **M₀ = 113.16 g/mol** ; **Tₘ ≈ 220–225 °C** ; **T_g ≈ 50 °C**. - ✅ **Three industrial routes:** (i) **Hydrolytic** (water-initiated, step-growth, *most common*); (ii) **Anionic / AA-ROP** (NaH + N-acetyl caprolactam, very fast, used for MC Nylon 6); (iii) **Cationic** (HCl/H₂SO₄, minor). - ✅ **Hydrolytic route** is a reversible step-growth condensation; water must be **removed continuously** to push $\bar{X}_n$ high. - ✅ **Carothers equation** for A–B step-growth: $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$; PDI = $1+p \to 2$ as $p \to 1$. - ✅ **By-product / impurity:** Cyclic dimer (~ 1–2%) — must be extracted by hot water for high-quality fibre grades. - ✅ **H-bonding** between –NH and C=O of adjacent chains gives high strength, crystallinity, and a high "engineering plastic" performance. - ✅ **Nylon 6 vs Nylon 6,6:** Nylon 6 has *lower* Tₘ, *better* impact strength, *better* dye uptake, *lower* cost, and *asymmetric* amide spacing. - ✅ **Key industrial reactor:** **VK-tube** (Vereinfacht Kontinuierlich Rohr) — top pressure, middle atmospheric, bottom vacuum zones in one tube. - ✅ **End-group analysis** (HClO₄ titration of –NH₂) is the classical KTU-experiment for determining $\bar{M}_n$. - ✅ **Tg is moisture-sensitive:** ~5 °C depression per 1% water absorbed; hence "dry-as-moulded" Nylon 6 must be conditioned before mechanical testing. - ✅ **Global production:** ~5 million tonnes/year; key manufacturers: BASF, DSM, Lanxess, UBE, Ascend, Toray. ---

topics.title: Nylon 66 and PET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nylon 66 and PET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nylon 66 (Polyamide 66, PA 66)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Nylon 66 is an **aliphatic polyamide** synthesised by the **step-growth (condensation) polymerisation** of **hexamethylenediamine** (HMDA, a diamine with **6 carbon atoms**) and **adipic acid** (a dicarboxylic acid with **6 carbon atoms**). The numerical suffix "66" denotes the **6 carbons in the diamine** and the **6 carbons in the diacid** monomer. The polymer backbone contains **amide linkages (–CO–NH–)** as the characteristic repeat group.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The "66" does **NOT** refer to the year 1966 or a polymer chain of 66 atoms. It is purely a **monomer carbon-count notation**: first digit = diamine carbon count, second digit = diacid carbon count.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a plastic "zip-lock" bag where the two interlocking rails represent a diamine and a diacid. Each time a tooth from rail-1 clicks into a tooth from rail-2, a small water molecule is spat out (this is the **condensation by-product**). After thousands of clicks, you get a long, strong, flexible chain — that chain is Nylon 66. The hydrogen bonds between adjacent –CO–NH– groups act like tiny "magnets" holding the chains tightly, which is why Nylon 66 is so strong and abrasion-resistant.

**Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- **Repeat unit molecular weight:** 226.32 g·mol⁻¹
- **Theoretical melting point (Tm):** 255–265 °C
- **Glass transition temperature (Tg):** ≈ 50 °C (dry), 0 °C (moist)
- **Density (crystalline):** 1.14 g·cm⁻³
- **Hydrogen bonding density:** ~1 per 0.5 nm of chain
- **Carothers' invention year:** 1935 (Wallace H. Carothers, DuPont, USA)

---

## 1.2 PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
PET is an **aromatic–aliphatic thermoplastic polyester** produced by the **step-growth (condensation) polymerisation** of **ethylene glycol** (EG, a diol with **2 carbon atoms**) and either **terephthalic acid (TPA)** or its dimethyl ester **dimethyl terephthalate (DMT)**, with terephthalic acid being the modern industrial feedstock. The polymer backbone contains **ester linkages (–COO–)** as the characteristic repeat group, and the aromatic ring imparts rigidity, planarity, and high mechanical strength.

**Chemical Identity of Terephthalic Acid (TPA):**
- IUPAC name: benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid
- Structure: para-substituted benzene ring with two –COOH groups

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of PET as a chain of "T-shaped Lego blocks" where the flat top of the T is a **benzene ring** (rigid, flat, like a tiny plank) and the two legs are **ester linkages** connecting flexible ethylene spacers. The flat benzene rings stack like plates on a dinner table — this *π–π stacking* gives PET its high strength, clarity, and barrier properties. When you blow PET into a bottle, you're essentially stretching these tiny T-blocks into a gas-tight dome.

**Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
- **Repeat unit molecular weight:** 192.17 g·mol⁻¹
- **Theoretical melting point (Tm):** 260–270 °C
- **Glass transition temperature (Tg):** ≈ 70–80 °C
- **Density (amorphous):** 1.33 g·cm⁻³, (crystalline): 1.45 g·cm⁻³
- **Tensile strength:** ≈ 55–75 MPa (oriented film)
- **Oxygen permeability:** very low (excellent gas barrier)
- **Common trade names:** Mylar® (film), Dacron® (fiber), Terylene® (fiber)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** Both Nylon 66 and PET are **step-growth condensation polymers**. They release small molecules (H₂O in nylon 66; CH₃OH in DMT-route PET, or H₂O in TPA-route PET) during polymerisation — a defining feature examined in depth across Modules 1–2.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nylon 66 — Mechanistic & Structural Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Monomer Synthesis & Polymerisation Pathway

**Monomers Required:**
1. **Adipic Acid** (HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH) — derived industrially from cyclohexane oxidation (KA oil route) or butadiene carbonylation.
2. **Hexamethylenediamine** (H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂) — produced from adiponitrile hydrogenation.

**Step 1 — "Nylon Salt" Formation (1:1 Salt):**
To achieve perfect **1:1 stoichiometric balance**, the diamine and diacid are first neutralised in methanol/water to form the **AH-salt** (hexamethylenediammonium adipate):

$$
\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_2 + \text{HOOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COOH} \;\longrightarrow\; \left[^{+}\text{H}_3\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_3^{+}\right] \left[^{-}\text{OOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COO}^{-}\right]
$$

- **Why a salt?** The salt is crystalline, stoichiometrically exact, water-soluble, and easy to purify — eliminating stoichiometric imbalance (the #1 cause of low molecular weight in step-growth polymerisation).

**Step 2 — Step-Growth Condensation Polymerisation:**

$$
n\,\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_2 + n\,\text{HOOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COOH} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta,\;-\text{H}_2\text{O}}\; \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2)_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH}
$$

- The reaction proceeds via **nucleophilic acyl substitution**: the –NH₂ lone pair attacks the carbonyl carbon of –COOH, forming a tetrahedral intermediate, then expelling H₂O.
- Each amide bond formation **ejects one water molecule**.
- Industrial process: **continuous melt polymerisation** at **270–280 °C** under **inert N₂ blanket** to prevent oxidative degradation and to drive off water.

### 2.1.2 Why Nylon 66 is Mechanically Strong

| Structural Feature | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| Strong **H-bonding** between adjacent –CO–NH– groups | High tensile strength, high melting point |
| **Symmetric chain packing** | High crystallinity (40–60%) |
| Aliphatic backbone flexibility (rotatable C–C bonds) | Toughness, impact resistance |
| Absence of bulky side groups | Close chain packing → high density |
| Polar amide groups | Moisture absorption (hygroscopic), dyeable |

### 2.1.3 KTU-Exam-Relevant Disadvantages

- **Hygroscopic:** absorbs 3–9% water → dimensional instability, Tg depression.
- **Not transparent** in unmodified form (crystallises).
- **Sensitive to UV and strong acids** (amide hydrolysis).

---

## 2.2 PET — Mechanistic & Structural Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Monomer Routes (KTU-Favoured Comparison)

**Route A — DMT Route (Older, 2-step transesterification):**
- **Dimethyl Terephthalate (DMT)** + **Ethylene Glycol (EG)** → **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)** + **2 CH₃OH** (methanol by-product)
- BHET then undergoes **polycondensation** at 270–285 °C, releasing **EG** as by-product, to yield high-MW PET.

**Route B — TPA Route (Modern, direct esterification):**
- **Terephthalic Acid (TPA)** + **Ethylene Glycol (EG)** → **BHET** + **2 H₂O** (water by-product)
- BHET → PET via polycondensation (releasing EG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the TPA route has largely replaced DMT route:** TPA is cheaper (derived from p-xylene oxidation), avoids methanol recovery, and the reaction produces water (easier to remove) rather than methanol. **KTU frequently asks: compare DMT vs TPA route for PET.**

### 2.2.2 Net Repeat-Unit Reaction

$$
n\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + n\,\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Sb}_2\text{O}_3\;\text{cat.}}\; \text{H–[OCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OCO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + (2n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### 2.2.3 Catalysts Used (Industrial + KTU-Relevant)

- **Transesterification catalyst:** Manganese acetate, Zn acetate, Co acetate.
- **Polycondensation catalyst:** **Antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃)** — the industry-standard; or Germanium dioxide (GeO₂) for higher-purity grades.
- **Stabilisers:** phosphoric acid, triphenyl phosphite (to deactivate catalyst residues and prevent yellowing).

### 2.2.4 Why PET is Dimensionally & Thermally Stable

| Structural Feature | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Planar aromatic ring** (1,4-phenylene) | Chain rigidity, high Tm, low creep |
| **Symmetric para-substitution** | Perfect chain alignment, crystallisable |
| **Strong π–π stacking** of aromatic rings | Optical clarity, gas barrier, mechanical strength |
| **Polar ester groups** | Moderate moisture uptake, surface printability |
| **Easily oriented (cold/hot drawing)** | BOPET film, polyester fiber |

### 2.2.5 KTU-Exam-Relevant Applications

- **Bottles (PET bottles):** carbonated soft drinks, water — exploits gas barrier + clarity.
- **BOPET film (biaxially oriented PET):** Mylar® for packaging, electrical insulation, solar back-sheets.
- **Polyester fiber (Dacron, Terylene):** apparel, home textiles, tire cord.
- **Engineering plastic (glass-filled):** automotive, electrical connectors.
- **Recyclable:** "PET" recycling code **"1"** (most recycled plastic globally).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Step-Growth Polymerisation)

| # | Equation | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers' equation; $\bar{X}_n$ = number-average DP, $p$ = extent of reaction (functional group conversion) | Single most-asked KTU formula for step-growth |
| 2 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$ | Carothers' equation with **non-stoichiometric** ratio $r = N_A/N_B$ (with $N_A \le N_B$) | KTU 14-marker favourite when $r \ne 1$ |
| 3 | $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n$ | Number-average MW (M₀ = average repeat-unit MW) | Used to find required $p$ for a target $M_n$ |
| 4 | $p_{\text{Stoddart}} = \dfrac{2}{\bar{X}_n} - \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n^{\,2}}$ | Fraction of monomers in chain of length $n$ | For molecular weight distribution |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{r} + 2(1-p)$ | Combined stoichiometric and conversion effect | Often used in 7-mark sub-parts |
| 6 | $T_m = T_m^{\circ}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\gamma}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}$ | Equilibrium melting relationship (Flory) | For crystallinity/nylon questions |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{T_m} - \dfrac{1}{T_m^{\circ}} = \dfrac{R}{\Delta H_u}\ln X_c$ | Flory's melting-point depression by diluent/comonomer | DSC analysis of nylon/PET |
| 8 | $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$ | Mark–Houwink equation; $K,\alpha$ depend on polymer–solvent | Solution viscosity, MWD analysis |
| 9 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Thermodynamic driving force for polymerisation | Decides feasibility of step-growth |
| 10 | $n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = n_{\text{amide bonds formed}} = n(p)$ | Stoichiometry of water released in nylon 66 | Mass-balance problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** In a **2-functional step-growth** polymerisation like nylon 66 / PET, to reach **$\bar{X}_n = 100$** (i.e. industrially useful polymer), you need **$p = 0.99$** — meaning **99% of all functional groups must have reacted**. This is the famous **"Carothers' dilemma"** and is the single most-tested concept in Module 1.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Carothers' Equation for Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation ($\bar{X}_n$)

### Step 1 — Define the system
Let $N_0$ = total number of monomer molecules **at the start** of polymerisation.
Let $N$ = total number of **all molecules** (monomers + oligomers + polymer) **remaining at time $t$**.

For a 1:1 stoichiometric AA + BB system (nylon 66 type):
- Total functional groups at start = $2N_0$ (each monomer is bifunctional).
- The **extent of reaction $p$** = fraction of functional groups that have reacted.
- Number of functional groups reacted = $2N_0 p$.
- Each condensation reaction consumes **2 functional groups** and creates **1 new molecule of by-product (H₂O)**.

### Step 2 — Count the number of molecules present at conversion $p$
Each bond formed between two functional groups **reduces the total molecule count by 1** (two molecules fuse into one).
Number of bonds formed = $2N_0 p / 2 = N_0 p$.

Therefore:

$$
N = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1-p)
$$

### Step 3 — Define number-average degree of polymerisation
$\bar{X}_n$ is the **average number of monomer units per polymer molecule**:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{Initial monomer molecules}}{\text{Molecules remaining at time } t} = \dfrac{N_0}{N}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{1}{1-p} \quad \boxed{\text{Carothers' Equation}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Carothers' Equation for Non-Stoichiometric (r ≠ 1) Feed

When the two bifunctional monomers are **not in exact 1:1 ratio**, let:
- $N_A$ = molecules of monomer A (deficient, e.g. diamine)
- $N_B$ = molecules of monomer B (excess, e.g. diacid)
- $r = \dfrac{N_A}{N_B} \le 1$ (the **stoichiometric ratio**)

Total monomers $N_0 = N_A + N_B$.
Total functional groups = $2N_A + 2N_B$.

Since monomer A is limiting, **all of its A-groups will eventually react**:

$$
\text{Number of bonds formed} = 2N_A p
$$

Each bond reduces the molecule count by 1, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= N_0 - 2N_A p = (N_A + N_B) - 2N_A p \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{N_A + N_B}{N_A + N_B - 2N_A p}
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $N_B$ (using $N_A/N_B = r$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{r + 1}{r + 1 - 2rp} \quad \boxed{\text{Carothers' (non-stoichiometric) equation}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)
> **Problem:** For nylon 66 synthesis, the feed ratio is $r = 0.990$ and the extent of reaction is $p = 0.995$. Calculate $\bar{X}_n$ and $\bar{M}_n$ (given $M_0 = 226.32$ g·mol⁻¹).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{r + 1}{r + 1 - 2rp} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{0.990 + 1}{0.990 + 1 - 2(0.990)(0.995)} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{1.990}{1.990 - 1.9701} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{1.990}{0.0199} \\
\bar{X}_n &\approx 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n &= M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n \\
\bar{M}_n &= 226.32 \times 100 \\
\bar{M}_n &= 22{,}632 \text{ g·mol}^{-1} \approx 22.6 \text{ kDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Mass-Balance Derivation: Water Released in Nylon 66

For $N_0$ molecules of AH-salt each forming an average of $\bar{X}_n$ repeat units, **number of water molecules released per original monomer** = number of amide bonds formed per chain = $(\bar{X}_n - 1)$.

For $N_0$ initial molecules → final $N = N_0 / \bar{X}_n$ molecules:

$$
\text{Total water released} = N_0 - N = N_0\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n}\right) = N_0 p
$$

For **1 kg of nylon 66** with $M_0 = 226.32$ g·mol⁻¹ and target $\bar{X}_n = 100$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_0 &= \dfrac{1000 \text{ g}}{226.32 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}} = 4.419 \text{ mol} \\
\text{H}_2\text{O released} &= 4.419 \times 0.99 = 4.375 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Mass of H}_2\text{O} &= 4.375 \times 18.015 = 78.8 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

So roughly **79 g of water** must be continuously removed to make **1 kg of nylon 66** at $p = 0.99$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Nylon 66 vs PET at a Glance

| Property | Nylon 66 (PA 66) | PET |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymer class** | Aliphatic polyamide (–CONH–) | Aromatic–aliphatic polyester (–COO–) |
| **Repeat unit linkage** | Amide | Ester |
| **Repeat unit MW (g·mol⁻¹)** | 226.32 | 192.17 |
| **Monomers** | HMDA (C₆) + Adipic acid (C₆) | EG (C₂) + TPA (or DMT) |
| **By-product** | H₂O | H₂O (TPA route) or CH₃OH (DMT route) |
| **Tm (°C)** | 255–265 | 260–270 |
| **Tg (°C)** | ~50 (dry) | ~70–80 |
| **Density (g·cm⁻³)** | 1.14 | 1.33–1.45 |
| **Dominant inter-chain force** | H-bonding (amide) | π–π stacking + dipole (ester) |
| **Crystallinity** | High (40–60%) | Moderate–high (0–40%) |
| **Hygroscopy** | High (3–9%) | Low (<0.5%) |
| **Clarity** | Opaque (natural) | Transparent (amorphous) |
| **Main catalyst** | None needed (heat) | Sb₂O₃ |
| **Key applications** | Tire cord, gears, carpet | Bottles, films, polyester fiber |
| **Carothers' $p$ for $\bar{X}_n = 100$** | 0.99 | 0.99 |
| **Recycling code** | 7 (other) | 1 (PET) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Step-Growth Polymerisation Flow (Nylon 66 / PET)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Raw Petrochemical Feedstocks]) --> procA1[Paraffin oxidation or Benzene route]
    procA1 --> monA1[Adipic Acid HOOC CH2 4 COOH]
    procA1 --> monA2[Hexamethylenediamine H2N CH2 6 NH2]
    startB([Raw Petrochemical Feedstocks]) --> procB1[p-Xylene air oxidation]
    procB1 --> monB1[Terephthalic Acid HOOC C6H4 COOH]
    startB --> procB2[Ethylene oxide hydration]
    procB2 --> monB2[Ethylene Glycol HOCH2CH2OH]
    monA1 --> salt[AH Salt Formation in Methanol]
    monA2 --> salt
    salt --> polycond1[Melt Polycondensation 270 to 280 C N2 blanket minus H2O]
    polycond1 --> ny66[NYLON 66 PA 66 Spinning into fiber or Pellets]
    monB1 --> ester1[Direct Esterification 240 to 260 C minus H2O]
    monB2 --> ester1
    ester1 --> bhet[BHET Bis hydroxyethyl terephthalate]
    bhet --> polycond2[Polycondensation 270 to 285 C minus EG Sb2O3 catalyst]
    polycond2 --> pet[PET Pellets or Melt to bottle or film]
    ny66 --> app1[Tire cord Carpets Apparel]
    pet --> app2[PET bottles BOPET film Polyester fiber]
    polycond1 --> recov1[Water recovered and recycled]
    polycond2 --> recov2[EG recovered and recycled]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart captures the **two parallel industrial pipelines** for nylon 66 (top half) and PET (bottom half). Both share the *step-growth condensation* core but differ in monomer source, catalyst, and by-product.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: H-Bonding & π-π Stacking in Nylon 66 vs PET

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N66["Nylon 66 Chains Aliphatic"]
        ny1[Chain A NH at position 1]
        ny2[Chain B CO at position 1]
        ny3[Chain A NH at position 2]
        ny4[Chain B CO at position 2]
        ny1 -- Hydrogen bond CO to HN --> ny2
        ny3 -- Hydrogen bond CO to HN --> ny4
    end
    subgraph PET["PET Chains Aromatic"]
        pt1[Ring 1 of chain P]
        pt2[Ring 1 of chain Q]
        pt3[Ring 2 of chain P]
        pt4[Ring 2 of chain Q]
        pt1 -- Pi Pi stacking offset face to face --> pt2
        pt3 -- Pi Pi stacking offset face to face --> pt4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Captured:** Nylon 66's strength comes from **directional hydrogen bonds** (amide–amide) — strong but disrupted by water. PET's strength comes from **non-directional aromatic π–π stacking** — unaffected by moisture, hence PET's excellent dry/wet performance consistency (critical for PET bottles).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Industrial Reactor Sequence (KTU Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feed[AH Salt or BHET Feed] --> preheat[Pre heater 240 C]
    preheat --> reactor1[First Stage Reactor 250 to 270 C atmospheric pressure]
    reactor1 --> reactor2[Second Stage Finishing Reactor 270 to 285 C high vacuum 0.5 to 2 mbar]
    reactor2 --> meltpoly[High MW Polymer Melt IV greater than 0.6 dL per g]
    meltpoly --> spin1[Spinning to fiber via spinneret]
    meltpoly --> spin2[Injection stretch blow molding to bottles]
    meltpoly --> spin3[Cast film extrusion then biaxial stretching to BOPET]
    reactor1 --> byproduct1[Water or Methanol recovered via distillation]
```
