```mermaid
    reactor2 --> byproduct2[EG recovered via vacuum and condensation]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**"Explain the formation of 'AH-salt' in nylon 66 synthesis. Why is it prepared before polymerisation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** AH-salt (adipic acid hexamethylenediamine salt) is the 1:1 crystalline salt formed by neutralisation of the –NH₂ groups of hexamethylenediamine with the –COOH groups of adipic acid, in methanol or water:
   $\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_2 + \text{HOOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COOH} \rightarrow {}^{+}\text{H}_3\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_3^{+}\,{}^{-}\text{OOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COO}^{-}$
2. **Reason for preparation (2 marks):** (a) ensures **exact 1:1 stoichiometry** between the two functional groups, eliminating imbalance that would otherwise cap molecular weight; (b) AH-salt is a **crystalline, easily purified, water-soluble** intermediate, free from impurities that catalyse side reactions; (c) permits **accurate weighing and storage** of monomers before polymerisation.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**"Compare DMT route and TPA route for PET synthesis."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | DMT Route | TPA Route |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer** | Dimethyl terephthalate (ester) | Terephthalic acid (acid) |
| **Initial step** | Transesterification | Direct esterification |
| **By-product** | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Water (H₂O) |
| **Catalyst for 1st step** | Mn, Zn, Co acetates | None (autocatalysed by –COOH) |
| **Industrial preference** | Older, mostly obsolete | **Modern, preferred** (cheaper, greener) |
| **Monomer solubility** | Soluble in EG | Insoluble → needs higher T/P |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** TPA route is industrially preferred today due to lower cost, avoidance of methanol, and water by-product (easier separation).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the step-growth polymerisation of nylon 66 from adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine. Discuss the role of the AH-salt and the molecular-weight limitations of step-growth polymerisation.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a nylon 66 synthesis, $r = 0.995$ and $p = 0.98$. Calculate (i) $\bar{X}_n$, (ii) $\bar{M}_n$, (iii) the mass of water released per kg of polymer. ($M_0 = 226.32$ g·mol⁻¹ for nylon 66 repeat unit.)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-growth polymerisation of Nylon 66 (7 marks):**

1. **Monomer identification (1 mark):**
   - Adipic acid: HOOC–(CH₂)₄–COOH
   - Hexamethylenediamine (HMDA): H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂

2. **AH-salt formation (1.5 marks):** The acid and amine are first dissolved in hot methanol and neutralised to form the **AH-salt**:
   $\text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2 + \text{HOOC–R'–COOH} \rightarrow \text{[\,}^{+}\text{H}_3\text{N–R–NH}_3^{+}\text{][\,-OOC–R'–COO}^{-}\text{]}$
   This guarantees **exact 1:1 stoichiometry** of –NH₂ and –COOH groups, the critical prerequisite for high molecular weight in step-growth polymerisation.

3. **Condensation polymerisation (2 marks):** The salt is heated to **270–280 °C** under **N₂ blanket**:
   $n\,\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH}_2 + n\,\text{HOOC–(CH}_2)_4\text{–COOH} \xrightarrow{\Delta,-\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2)_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2)_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH}$
   Each amide bond formation releases one H₂O molecule. The reaction follows **nucleophilic acyl substitution** mechanism.

4. **Molecular-weight limitations (1.5 marks):** According to **Carothers' equation**:
   $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$
   To achieve a useful $\bar{X}_n$ (≥ 100), we need $p \ge 0.99$. Even 1% unreacted groups halves the chain length. Hence **purity, stoichiometric balance, and complete removal of H₂O** are critical engineering challenges.

5. **Industrial finishing (1 mark):** Final stage uses **high vacuum** (1–2 mbar) to drive the equilibrium toward higher MW by removing trace water.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 0.995$, $p = 0.98$, $M_0 = 226.32$ g·mol⁻¹.

**(i) Calculation of $\bar{X}_n$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{r + 1}{r + 1 - 2rp} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{0.995 + 1}{0.995 + 1 - 2(0.995)(0.98)} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{1.995}{1.995 - 1.9502} \\
\bar{X}_n &= \dfrac{1.995}{0.0448} \\
\bar{X}_n &\approx 44.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Calculation of $\bar{M}_n$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{M}_n &= M_0 \times \bar{X}_n \\
\bar{M}_n &= 226.32 \times 44.5 \\
\bar{M}_n &\approx 10{,}071 \text{ g·mol}^{-1} \approx 10.07 \text{ kDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mass of water released per kg of polymer (2 marks):**
Moles of repeat unit per kg = $1000 / 226.32 = 4.419$ mol.
Moles of H₂O released per repeat unit = number of amide bonds per repeat unit = **1** (since each repeat unit formation involves one H₂O release). However, water released per chain = $(\bar{X}_n - 1)$, so per mole of repeat units, water released = $(1 - 1/\bar{X}_n) = (1 - 1/44.5) = 0.9775$ mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2\text{O moles} &= 4.419 \times 0.9775 = 4.320 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Mass of H}_2\text{O} &= 4.320 \times 18.015 = 77.82 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answer:** $\bar{X}_n \approx 44.5$, $\bar{M}_n \approx 10.07$ kDa, water released ≈ **77.8 g per kg of nylon 66**.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **TPA route** for the manufacture of PET. Identify the catalysts used at each stage and justify the choice of Sb₂O₃ as the polycondensation catalyst.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the structure-property relationships of **nylon 66** and **PET**. Why does PET retain transparency and dimensional stability in humid conditions while nylon 66 does not?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TPA route for PET manufacture (7 marks):**

1. **Stage 1 — Direct Esterification (2 marks):** Terephthalic acid (TPA) is reacted with ethylene glycol (EG) at **240–260 °C** and **4–6 bar** in a stirred autoclave. No external catalyst is required (autocatalysed by –COOH). The reaction is:
   $\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + 2\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OCO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$
   Product is **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)**.

2. **Stage 2 — Polycondensation (2.5 marks):** BHET is transferred to a second reactor operating at **270–285 °C** and **high vacuum (0.5–2 mbar)**, catalysed by **antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃, 100–300 ppm)**:
   $n\,\text{BHET} \xrightarrow{\text{Sb}_2\text{O}_3,\,-\text{EG}} \text{PET} + (n-1)\,\text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$
   The vacuum continuously removes EG, driving the equilibrium forward.

3. **Why Sb₂O₃ (1.5 marks):**
   - **High catalytic activity** for esterification/polycondensation at 270–290 °C.
   - **Thermal stability** at PET processing temperatures (no decomposition).
   - **Food-contact approval** by FDA/EU (Sb leaching < 0.05% w/w).
   - **Low cost** compared to GeO₂ and Ti(OBu)₄.
   - Produces **low-colour, high-clarity** PET (critical for bottles/film).

4. **Catalyst summary table (1 mark):**

| Stage | Catalyst | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Direct esterification | None (autocatalysed) | Forms BHET |
| Polycondensation | **Sb₂O₃** | Builds high MW PET |

---

**(b) Structure–Property Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | Nylon 66 | PET | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inter-chain force** | Strong H-bonding (amide) | π–π stacking + dipole (ester) | H-bonds in nylon 66 break with water → nylon 66 absorbs water and loses stiffness. PET's π-stacking is **unaffected by water** → PET is dimensionally stable in humidity. |
| **Backbone** | Aliphatic (–CH₂–)₆ + (–CH₂–)₄ — flexible | Aromatic + (–CH₂–CH₂–) — semi-rigid | PET's aromatic ring raises **Tg (70–80 °C)** vs nylon 66's **Tg (50 °C dry)**, giving PET better heat resistance for hot-fill bottles. |
| **Crystallinity** | High (40–60%) → opaque | Tuneable (amorphous: clear; crystalline: opaque) | Amorphous PET (rapidly quenched) is **transparent** → ideal for water bottles. Nylon 66 always crystallises → always opaque. |
| **Hygroscopy** | High (3–9% water uptake) | Very low (<0.5%) | Nylon 66 swells and changes dimensions in humid climates; PET does not. |
| **Tg depression by water** | Severe (Tg drops to ~0 °C when wet) | Negligible | Nylon 66 becomes rubbery when wet; PET retains rigidity. |
| **Gas barrier** | Moderate | Excellent (especially for O₂ and CO₂) | PET is the global standard for carbonated-beverage bottles; nylon 66 is not. |
| **Optical clarity** | Opaque (natural) | Transparent (amorphous) | PET is the only choice for clear packaging. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** PET's combination of (i) aromatic rigidity, (ii) π-stacking-dominated inter-chain forces (insensitive to water), and (iii) controllable crystallinity makes it the superior choice for **transparent, dimensionally stable, moisture-resistant** applications. Nylon 66's hydrogen-bond-dominated structure, while mechanically strong, is inherently **water-sensitive**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Nylon 66 / PET Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "66" with year 1966 or 66 atoms** — it is the *carbon count of the two monomers*. Costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing Carothers' equation for non-stoichiometric case as $1/(1-p)$** — full marks only for the **$(1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$** form when $r$ is given.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit**.
> 4. **Mixing up DMT and TPA routes** — DMT releases **methanol**; TPA releases **water**. Stating the wrong by-product costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning the role of vacuum in the final polymerisation stage** — without the vacuum to remove H₂O (nylon 66) or EG (PET), the equilibrium will not shift forward and MW will plateau. Worth 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting to justify the choice of Sb₂O₃ catalyst** — "it works" is not enough; mention FDA approval, thermal stability, and low colour.
> 7. **Confusing Tg and Tm** — Tm is the melting point (ordered crystals melt); Tg is the glass transition (amorphous regions soften). Often asked as a trick question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nylon 66 = Polyamide 66 = PA 66** = step-growth condensation of **HMDA (C₆) + Adipic acid (C₆)** → releases **H₂O** → **amide (–CONH–)** backbone.
- **PET = Polyethylene Terephthalate** = step-growth condensation of **EG (C₂) + TPA** (or DMT) → releases **H₂O** (TPA route) or **CH₃OH** (DMT route) → **ester (–COO–)** backbone.
- **"66" and "PET" naming** are *not* year-based or atom-count based for the polymer — they are **monomer carbon counts** (nylon 66) or **monomer acronyms** (PET = PolyEthylene Terephthalate).
- **AH-salt** formation is *mandatory* for high-MW nylon 66 to lock in 1:1 stoichiometry.
- **Carothers' equation:** $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ (stoichiometric) or $(1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ (non-stoichiometric).
- **Rule of thumb:** $\bar{X}_n = 100 \Rightarrow p = 0.99$ — only 1% unreacted groups cut chain length in half.
- **Catalyst for PET polycondensation:** **Sb₂O₃** (industrial standard), with **Mn/Zn/Co acetates** for the transesterification step (DMT route only).
- **By-products must be removed continuously** to drive equilibrium forward — water for nylon 66, EG for PET, under high vacuum in the finishing stage.
- **Tg hierarchy:** PET (~75 °C) > Nylon 66 dry (~50 °C) > Nylon 66 wet (~0 °C). PET retains stiffness in humidity; nylon 66 doesn't.
- **Recycling code 1 = PET** (most recycled plastic); **Nylon 66 = recycling code 7** ("other").
- **Key applications:** Nylon 66 → tire cord, carpets, engineering plastic. PET → bottles, BOPET film, polyester fiber.
- **Mechanical strength origin:** Nylon 66 = directional H-bonds; PET = non-directional π–π stacking (insensitive to moisture).
- **Mark–Houwink parameters** for nylon 66 in *m-cresol*: $K = 3.20 \times 10^{-4}$ dL·g⁻¹, $\alpha = 0.74$; for PET in *phenol/TCE*: $K = 7.55 \times 10^{-4}$ dL·g⁻¹, $\alpha = 0.685$ (commonly asked in viscosity-MW problems).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Epoxy Polymer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Epoxy Polymer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epoxy Polymer (Epoxy Resin):** A class of **thermosetting polymers** containing one or more reactive **epoxide groups** (also called **oxirane rings** or **glycidyl groups**) — a three-membered cyclic ether ring in which one oxygen atom is bonded to two adjacent carbon atoms. These resins undergo irreversible **crosslinking (curing)** reactions with suitable **hardeners** (curing agents) to form a rigid, infusible, three-dimensional network with excellent mechanical strength, chemical resistance, and adhesive properties.

The most commercially significant representative is the **Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A (DGEBA)**, formed by the condensation reaction between **Epichlorohydrin (ECH)** and **Bisphenol-A (BPA)**.

## 1.2 The Epoxide Group — The Heart of the Chemistry

> [!NOTE]
> **The Oxirane (Epoxide) Ring:**
> A three-membered ring consisting of **two carbon atoms** and **one oxygen atom**. The ring angle is forced to ~60° (vs. the normal tetrahedral angle of 109.5°), creating **high angle strain (~27 kcal/mol) and ring tension**.

$$\text{Oxirane Ring Skeleton:} \quad \begin{array}{c} \text{C} \\ \diagup \,\, \text{O} \,\, \diagdown \\ \text{C} \end{array}$$

Because of this severe strain, the ring is **highly reactive** toward nucleophiles (amines, alcohols, anhydrides, thiols) — this reactivity is the foundation of epoxy curing chemistry.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Molecular Trap" Concept

Think of the epoxide ring as a **stretched rubber band pulled into a triangular loop**. It is *desperate* to snap back to a relaxed linear arrangement. When a hardener (amine or anhydride) approaches and "pokes" one of the carbons (a nucleophilic attack), the ring **snaps open**, releasing its strain energy and forming a strong covalent bond. This happens millions of times across the resin to form a **rigid 3D molecular cage** — the cured epoxy network. The polymer can no longer melt or flow: it is set permanently.

## 1.4 Key Classification Markers

| Feature | Epoxy Resin Characteristic | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymer Class** | Thermoset (irreversibly hardened) | — |
| **Reactive Group** | Oxirane / Epoxide / Glycidyl | Ring strain ~27 kcal/mol |
| **Common Backbone** | Bisphenol-A (DGEBA-type) | — |
| **Curing Type** | Step-growth crosslinking | No by-products (with amines) |
| **EEW (Epoxide Eq. Wt.)** | Grams of resin per mole of oxirane oxygen | g/eq |
| **Typical Viscosity** | Low to high (liquid → solid) | 10 – 10,000 mPa·s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Identity Markers for Epoxy Resins:**
> - **Epoxide Equivalent Weight (EEW):** grams of resin containing **one mole of epoxide groups**
> - **Epoxy Value:** number of **epoxide equivalents per 100 g** of resin (reciprocal of EEW × 100)
> - **Hydroxyl Value:** measures pendant –OH groups formed during resin synthesis

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of the Epoxide Group

The three-membered oxirane ring has two polarised C–O bonds. The carbon atoms carry a partial positive charge ($\delta^+$) and the oxygen carries a partial negative charge ($\delta^-$).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Polarisation:} \quad & \text{C}^{\delta^+} - \text{O}^{\delta^-} - \text{C}^{\delta^+}
\end{aligned}$$

This makes both carbons susceptible to **nucleophilic attack (SN2 mechanism)**. The strained ring opens and the nucleophile attaches to one carbon while the oxygen becomes a hydroxyl (–OH) or ether (–O–) linkage.

## 2.2 Step-Growth Synthesis of DGEBA — The Taffy Process

DGEBA is synthesised via the **condensation of Epichlorohydrin (ECH) and Bisphenol-A (BPA)** in the presence of NaOH (a two-step addition–elimination sequence).

### Step 1 — Addition (Ring Opening of ECH by BPA)

The phenolate ion of BPA attacks the epoxide ring of epichlorohydrin, opening it and forming a chlorohydrin intermediate.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BPA-O}^- + \text{CH}_2-\text{CH(O)}-\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{BPA-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{Cl}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Elimination (Ring Closure with NaOH)

Sodium hydroxide deprotonates the –OH and eliminates HCl, **regenerating a new epoxide ring** at the end of the chain.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BPA-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{BPA-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(O)-CH}_2 + \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

The product is the **monoglycidyl ether of BPA**; further reaction builds the oligomer.

### Generalised DGEBA Repeat Unit

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{H}-\left[\text{O}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{O}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{O})-\text{CH}_2\right]_n-\text{OH}
\end{aligned}$$

When $n = 0$, the resin is **pure DGEBA** (the standard liquid epoxy resin); higher $n$ values give solid resins with higher molecular weight.

## 2.3 Curing Mechanisms — Crosslinking with Hardeners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Curing = Crosslinking.** A hardener reacts with the epoxide rings, creating covalent bridges between chains and converting the linear/branched prepolymer into a 3D network. Epoxy resins are **always supplied as two-part systems**: **Resin + Hardener**.

### (a) Cure with Primary Amines (Aliphatic — e.g., Ethylenediamine, DETA)

A primary amine ($R-NH_2$) has **two active hydrogens**, each capable of opening an epoxide ring.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R-NH}_2 + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'} \quad \text{(secondary amine forms)}
\end{aligned}$$

The newly formed secondary amine further reacts with another epoxide, producing a tertiary amine and a new hydroxyl.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'} + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R-N(CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R')}_2
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) Cure with Anhydrides (e.g., Phthalic Anhydride, Maleic Anhydride)

Anhydrides first react with hydroxyl groups to form a monoester and a carboxylate; the carboxylate then opens an epoxide ring, creating a secondary –OH; this –OH re-opens another anhydride, propagating the network.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Anhydride} + \text{R-OH} \rightarrow \text{Monoester} + \text{R-COOH}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{R-COOH} + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R-COO-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'}
\end{aligned}$$

### (c) Cure with Polyamides, Dicyandiamide (DICY), and Latent Hardeners

Used for one-pack systems, powder coatings, and electronic-grade encapsulants.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Epoxy Calculations (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU exam problem on epoxies uses these formulae. Memorise the EEW–HEW pair and the stoichiometric ratio.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Epoxide Equivalent Weight (EEW)** | $\text{EEW} = \dfrac{100}{\text{Epoxy Value}}$ | mass containing 1 mole oxirane | g/eq |
| 2 | **Epoxy Value** | $\text{Epoxy Value} = \dfrac{\text{Moles of Epoxide}}{\text{Mass of Resin}} \times 100$ | — | mol/100 g |
| 3 | **Molecular Weight of DGEBA Oligomer** | $\text{M}_n = 340 + 284\,n$ | $n$ = number of repeat units; 340 = DGEBA unit; 284 = repeat increment | g/mol |
| 4 | **EEW of DGEBA (n=0)** | $\text{EEW} = \dfrac{340}{2} = 170$ | 2 epoxide groups per molecule | g/eq |
| 5 | **Amine Hydrogen Equivalent Weight (AHEW)** | $\text{AHEW} = \dfrac{\text{M.W. of Amine}}{\text{No. of Active H Atoms}}$ | mass per active H | g/eq |
| 6 | **Anhydride Equivalent Weight (AEW)** | $\text{AEW} = \dfrac{\text{M.W. of Anhydride}}{\text{No. of Anhydride Groups}}$ | mass per anhydride | g/eq |
| 7 | **Stoichiometric Mix Ratio (by mass)** | $\dfrac{\text{Mass of Hardener}}{\text{Mass of Resin}} = \dfrac{\text{AHEW}}{\text{EEW}}$ | ratio for ideal cure | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Phr (parts per hundred resin)** | $\text{Phr} = \dfrac{\text{Weight of Hardener}}{\text{Weight of Resin}} \times 100$ | practical dosage form | phr |
| 9 | **Number of Epoxide Groups (f)** | For BPA-based epoxy: $f = 2$ (terminal) | chain-end epoxides | — |
| 10 | **Crosslink Density (Ve)** | $\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho}{M_c}$ | $\rho$ = density; $M_c$ = mol. wt. between crosslinks | mol/m³ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Adhesives:** Aerospace, automotive bonding (epoxy's polar –OH groups bond to metals, glass, ceramics).
- **Coatings & Paints:** Anti-corrosion marine coatings, food-can linings.
- **Composites:** Carbon-fibre reinforced epoxy (CFRP) for aircraft structures, wind turbine blades.
- **Electronics:** PCB laminates (FR-4), semiconductor encapsulation (low shrinkage, high insulation).
- **Civil Engineering:** Floorings, concrete repair mortars, bridge crack injection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Molecular Weight Formula for DGEBA Oligomer

### Starting Molecule: Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A (DGEBA, n = 0)

We derive the molecular weight by **atom-counting** the structure:

$$\text{DGEBA Structure:}\quad \text{CH}_2(\text{O})\text{CH-CH}_2\text{-O-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-C}(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(O)CH}_2$$

**Atom-by-Atom Mass Inventory:**

| Structural Fragment | Composition | Mass Contribution (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| Two terminal glycidyl ether ends | $2 \times \text{CH}_2\text{-CH(O)-CH}_2\text{-O}$ | $\approx 170$ |
| Central Bisphenol-A residue | $-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-C}(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-}$ | $\approx 170$ |
| **Total for DGEBA (n = 0)** | | **340 g/mol** |

### Repeat Unit Increment (n → n+1)

Each additional repeat unit adds **one BPA residue (170)** + **one glycidyl ether bridge (114)** = **284 g/mol**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass increment per repeat} &= 170 + 114 \\
&= 284 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

### Generalised Formula

$$\boxed{\text{M}_n = 340 + 284\,n \quad \text{(g/mol)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Epoxide Equivalent Weight (EEW)

By definition, **EEW = grams of resin containing exactly one mole of epoxide oxygen**.

For DGEBA (n = 0):
- Molecular weight = **340 g/mol**
- Number of epoxide rings per molecule = **2** (one at each terminal end)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EEW} &= \dfrac{\text{Molecular Weight}}{\text{Number of Epoxide Groups}} \\
&= \dfrac{340}{2} \\
&= 170 \text{ g/eq}
\end{aligned}$$

For DGEBA with n = 1 (one repeat unit):
- Molecular weight = $340 + 284 = 624$ g/mol
- Epoxide groups still = **2** (terminal only)
- EEW = $624 / 2 = 312$ g/eq

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** As $n$ increases (higher MW resin), the EEW increases because there are still only **two chain-end epoxides per molecule**, but the molecule is heavier.

## 3.3 Derivation — Stoichiometric Hardener Loading

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the mass of **triethylenetetramine (TETA)** required to cure **100 g of DGEBA** having EEW = 170 g/eq.

**Given:**
- EEW (resin) = 170 g/eq
- TETA formula = $\text{H}_2\text{N-(CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-NH)}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-NH}_2$
- Active H atoms in TETA = **6** (two primary + two secondary — wait, count again: TETA has 4 NH groups? No: 2 primary + 2 secondary = total **6 active H atoms**)
- M.W. of TETA = **146.24 g/mol**

### Step 1 — Calculate Amine Hydrogen Equivalent Weight (AHEW)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AHEW} &= \dfrac{\text{Molecular Weight of Amine}}{\text{Number of Active Hydrogens}} \\
&= \dfrac{146.24}{6} \\
&= 24.37 \text{ g/eq}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Stoichiometric Mass Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\text{Mass of Hardener}}{\text{Mass of Resin}} &= \dfrac{\text{AHEW}}{\text{EEW}} \\
&= \dfrac{24.37}{170} \\
&= 0.1434
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Required Hardener Mass for 100 g Resin

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of TETA} &= 0.1434 \times 100 \text{ g} \\
&= 14.34 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Express in Phr

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Phr} &= \dfrac{14.34}{100} \times 100 = 14.34 \text{ phr}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Rule of Thumb:** For liquid DGEBA (EEW ≈ 170–190) cured with TETA, the typical mix ratio is **~10–15 phr of TETA**.

## 3.4 Derivation — Epoxy Value from EEW

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Epoxy Value} &= \dfrac{\text{Moles of Epoxide Oxygen}}{\text{100 g of Resin}} \\
&= \dfrac{1 \text{ mol/eq}}{\text{EEW (g/eq)}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{100}{\text{EEW}}
\end{aligned}$$

For DGEBA with EEW = 170:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Epoxy Value} &= \dfrac{100}{170} \\
&= 0.588 \text{ mol/100 g}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Variables on Cured Epoxy Properties

| Variable Increased | $T_g$ (Glass Transition) | Crosslink Density | Tensile Strength | Flexibility | Chemical Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EEW ↑ (higher n)** | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ initially | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Amine functionality ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Anhydride stoichiometry** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Cure temperature ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Excess hardener** | Variable | Plateaus then ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "epoxide equivalent weight (EEW)". How is the EEW of a DGEBA resin of n = 0 calculated?**

**Model Answer:**
Epoxide equivalent weight (EEW) is defined as the **mass (in grams) of epoxy resin that contains exactly one mole of epoxide (oxirane) groups**. It is a measure of the concentration of reactive epoxy functionality in the resin.

For DGEBA with n = 0:
- Molecular weight = **340 g/mol**
- Number of epoxide groups per molecule = **2**

$$\text{EEW} = \dfrac{340}{2} = 170 \text{ g/eq}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Briefly explain the role of a "hardener" in the curing of epoxy resins. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A **hardener (curing agent)** is a low-molecular-weight polyfunctional compound that reacts with the epoxide groups of the resin to form a **three-dimensional crosslinked network**. The hardener itself becomes part of the polymer backbone through covalent bond formation.

Examples:
1. **Ethylenediamine (EDA)** — a primary aliphatic diamine (4 active hydrogens).
2. **Phthalic anhydride** — an aromatic anhydride that cures via ester formation with hydroxyl groups and ring opening of epoxides.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **hardener with a catalyst**. A hardener becomes *chemically incorporated* into the network; a true catalyst (e.g., tertiary amines like BDMA, imidazoles) only accelerates the reaction without being consumed stoichiometrically.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) Describe the synthesis of DGEBA epoxy resin from Bisphenol-A and Epichlorohydrin. Include the reaction mechanism and the structure of the repeat unit. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Formation of BPA phenolate:**
Bisphenol-A (BPA) reacts with sodium hydroxide to form the **sodium phenolate salt**.

$$\text{BPA-OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{BPA-O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Nucleophilic addition to epichlorohydrin:**
The phenolate oxygen attacks the **less-hindered primary carbon** of the epoxide ring of epichlorohydrin, opening it to form a **chlorohydrin intermediate**.

$$\text{BPA-O}^- + \text{CH}_2\text{-CH(O)-CH}_2\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{BPA-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{Cl}$$

**Step 3 — Intramolecular dehydrohalogenation:**
NaOH abstracts the –OH proton; the resulting alkoxide displaces chloride intramolecularly, **regenerating a new epoxide ring** at the chain end.

$$\text{BPA-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{BPA-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(O)-CH}_2 + \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 4 — Chain propagation:**
The newly formed terminal epoxide can react with another BPA-phenolate, building the oligomer chain with general formula:

$$\text{M}_n = 340 + 284\,n \quad \text{(g/mol)}$$

The repeat unit structure:

$$-\text{O-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-C(CH}_3)_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2-$$

with terminal **glycidyl ether groups** at both chain ends.

---

**(b) Discuss the curing of epoxy resin with primary amines. Derive an expression to calculate the stoichiometric amount of triethylenetetramine (TETA) needed to cure 100 g of DGEBA resin (EEW = 185 g/eq). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Curing Mechanism:**
A primary amine ($R-NH_2$) **opens an epoxide ring** via nucleophilic attack. Each active amine hydrogen reacts with one epoxide group.

$$\text{R-NH}_2 + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'}$$

The resulting **secondary amine** has one active H and can open another epoxide:

$$\text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R'} + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R-N(CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R')}_2$$

**Calculation of TETA Loading:**

**Given:**
- EEW of DGEBA = 185 g/eq
- Molecular weight of TETA = 146.24 g/mol
- Active H atoms in TETA = 6

**Step 1 — AHEW of TETA:**

$$\text{AHEW} = \dfrac{146.24}{6} = 24.37 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric ratio:**

$$\dfrac{\text{Mass of Hardener}}{\text{Mass of Resin}} = \dfrac{24.37}{185} = 0.1317$$

**Step 3 — TETA required for 100 g resin:**

$$\text{Mass of TETA} = 0.1317 \times 100 = 13.17 \text{ g}$$

**Result:** Approximately **13.2 g of TETA per 100 g of DGEBA resin** (≈ 13.2 phr).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) Explain the structure and properties of the epoxide (oxirane) group. Why is it highly reactive? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Structure of the Epoxide Group:**
The epoxide (oxirane) group is a **three-membered cyclic ether** containing **two carbon atoms and one oxygen atom**. The C–O–C bond angle is compressed to approximately **60°**, far below the tetrahedral angle of 109.5° preferred by $sp^3$ hybridised carbons.

**Why is it highly reactive?**

1. **Angle Strain (~27 kcal/mol):** The forced 60° bond angles create significant **Baeyer strain**, destabilising the ring.
2. **Torsional Strain (Eclipsing):** The three C–H bonds are forced into **eclipsed conformations**, similar to cyclopropane.
3. **Bond Polarisation:** Oxygen's electronegativity polarises both C–O bonds, making the carbons electrophilic ($\delta^+$).
4. **Ring Strain Relief:** Upon ring opening, the strain energy is released, providing a strong thermodynamic driving force.

**Resulting Properties of the Epoxide Group:**
- **High electrophilicity** of ring carbons.
- **Susceptibility to nucleophilic attack (SN2-like mechanism).**
- **Reacts readily** with amines, alcohols, anhydrides, carboxylic acids, thiols, and water.
- Forms strong **covalent C–X bonds** after opening, creating crosslinked networks.

The strained oxirane ring is the **sole structural feature** that makes epoxy resins curable at room temperature — distinguishing them from polyesters or polyethers which require high-temperature catalysis.

---

**(b) Compare the curing of epoxy resins with amines versus anhydrides. List the advantages and limitations of each system. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | **Amine Cure** | **Anhydride Cure** |
|---|---|---|
| **Reaction type** | Ring opening of epoxide by N–H | Ring opening of epoxide by –COOH (formed first from OH + anhydride) |
| **Cure temperature** | Room temperature possible | Requires heat (~80–200 °C) |
| **By-products** | None | None (but sometimes water if esterification is incomplete) |
| **Exotherm** | High (large pot exotherm in thick castings) | Low |
| **Pot life** | Short (minutes to hours) | Longer |
| **Glass transition ($T_g$)** | Moderate (100–150 °C) | Higher (150–200 °C) |
| **Mechanical properties** | Tough, flexible | Higher heat resistance, brittle |
| **Chemical resistance** | Good (alkali resistant) | Excellent (acid resistant) |
| **Common uses** | Adhesives, coatings, civil repair | Electronics, composites, laminates |
| **Skin/Health hazard** | Amine blush, corrosive, irritant | Lower volatility, safer |
| **Shrinkage on cure** | Low (~1–2%) | Very low |

**Advantages of Amine Cure:**
- Room-temperature curing possible.
- Fast reactivity, no heating equipment needed.
- Good adhesion to damp or unprepared surfaces.

**Limitations of Amine Cure:**
- Short pot life.
- Toxic, corrosive handling.
- Lower $T_g$ limits high-temperature use.

**Advantages of Anhydride Cure:**
- Very low exotherm → suitable for thick castings and electrical insulation.
- Higher $T_g$ and excellent dielectric properties.
- Lower toxicity, longer pot life.

**Limitations of Anhydride Cure:**
- Requires elevated temperature for cure.
- Sensitive to stoichiometry; excess anhydride degrades properties.
- Generally slower, energy-intensive.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Epoxy Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting active hydrogen count** — TETA has **6 active H**, not 4. Always count primary + secondary + tertiary N–H bonds.
> 2. **Confusing EEW with molecular weight** — EEW is mass per **epoxide group**, not mass per molecule. Dividing M by number of epoxides is mandatory.
> 3. **Writing "epoxide" without explaining ring strain** — KTU examiners specifically ask *why* the ring is reactive; mentioning **angle strain (~60°), torsional strain, and bond polarisation** is essential.
> 4. **Skipping the role of NaOH** — In the Taffy process, NaOH does *two* jobs: (i) deprotonates BPA, and (ii) eliminates HCl to regenerate the epoxide ring. Students often mention only one.
> 5. **Mis-identifying hardeners vs. catalysts** — Tertiary amines (BDMA, DMP-30) and imidazoles are *catalysts* (not consumed); amines like TETA are *hardeners* (consumed stoichiometrically).
> 6. **Calculation errors in phr** — Phr = mass of hardener per 100 g of resin. Many students compute per 1 g and forget to multiply.
> 7. **No mention of hydroxyl groups in DGEBA** — The backbone –OH groups are crucial for adhesion (hydrogen bonding to substrates) and for initiating anhydride cures.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Epoxy resin = thermosetting prepolymer with strained 3-membered oxirane (epoxide) rings.**
- ⭐ The **oxirane ring** has ~**27 kcal/mol angle strain** → drives ring-opening cure reactions.
- ⭐ **DGEBA** (Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A) is the workhorse commercial epoxy, synthesised by the **Taffy Process** (BPA + ECH + NaOH).
- ⭐ **General molecular weight formula:** $\text{M}_n = 340 + 284\,n$ (g/mol); $n = 0$ gives pure DGEBA, MW = 340.
- ⭐ **EEW = M / (number of epoxide groups)** = 170 g/eq for DGEBA (n = 0).
- ⭐ **Epoxy Value = 100 / EEW** (mol/100 g).
- ⭐ **AHEW** = MW of amine / number of active H atoms (TETA = 146.24/6 = 24.37 g/eq).
- ⭐ **AEW** = MW of anhydride / number of anhydride groups.
- ⭐ **Stoichiometric mix ratio by mass** = AHEW / EEW; in **phr** multiply by 100.
- ⭐ **Curing with amines:** NH₂ opens epoxide → secondary amine → tertiary amine (each N–H consumes one epoxide).
- ⭐ **Curing with anhydrides:** Anhydride first reacts with –OH → carboxylate → ring opening → ester network.
- ⭐ **Hardener ≠ Catalyst.** Hardeners are stoichiometrically consumed; catalysts (BDMA, imidazoles) are not.
- ⭐ **Pendant –OH groups** in DGEBA are responsible for **adhesion** (hydrogen bonding) and **anhydride cure initiation**.
- ⭐ Cured epoxy applications: **adhesives, coatings, CFRP composites, PCB laminates (FR-4), civil repair mortars, electronic encapsulation.**
- ⭐ Higher functionality hardener → higher $T_g$ → higher crosslink density → more brittle but more chemically resistant network.
- ⭐ Anhydride cure gives **lower exotherm** and **higher $T_g$** than amine cure — preferred for electrical insulation and thick castings.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kevlar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kevlar

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kevlar** is the registered trademark of a high-performance para-aramid synthetic fiber manufactured by **DuPont (E. I. du Pont de Nemours and Company)**. Chemically, it is **poly-para-phenylene terephthalamide (PPTA)**, a wholly aromatic polyamide in which both the diamine and the diacid components contain para-substituted benzene rings linked through amide (–CO–NH–) linkages.

The IUPAC systematic name is *poly(iminoterephthaloylimino-1,4-phenylene)*. The repeating unit is:

$$\left[ - \text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}- \right]_n$$

where the para-phenylene diamine residue ($-\text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-$) alternates with the terephthaloyl residue ($-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Specialty Polymers):** Kevlar is the canonical *high-performance engineering thermoplastic* example that examiners use to test student knowledge of **structure–property relationships**, **liquid crystalline polymer behaviour**, and **condensation polymerisation mechanisms**. Memorise its chemical structure, repeat unit, monomer pair, and the role of hydrogen bonding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (Board-Exam Favourite):** Kevlar was serendipitously discovered in **1965** by **Stephanie Louise Kwolek**, an American polymer chemist of Polish origin working at DuPont's Pioneering Research Laboratory in Wilmington, Delaware. She observed that certain polyamide solutions formed *liquid crystalline phases*, which led to the spinning of ultra-strong fibers. This is a frequently asked two-mark question in KTU examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have thousands of **microscopic, perfectly straight spaghetti sticks** made of rigid aromatic rings. Now imagine you stack them inside a long, narrow tube so that every stick is *perfectly parallel* to its neighbour, and you also lightly "glue" each stick to its neighbour using tiny **hydrogen bonds** (think of them as weak molecular Velcro strips). When you try to pull the bundle from either end, every stick has to break at once because the load is shared uniformly.

That, in essence, is **Kevlar**:

- The **straight aromatic rings** = the spaghetti sticks (they cannot bend or fold because of the rigid para-phenylene backbone).
- The **amide N–H · · · O=C hydrogen bonds** = the Velcro strips gluing adjacent chains together laterally.
- The **parallel alignment** = achieved during the wet-spinning process from a lyotropic liquid-crystalline solution in concentrated sulfuric acid.

This is why Kevlar has a **specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio) roughly **5 to 10 times that of steel** on an equal-weight basis, while its density is only about **1.44 g/cm³** — less than half that of steel (**7.85 g/cm³**).

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic — "Kevlar = PPDA + TPC"**: **K**evlar is made from **P**ara-**P**henylene **D**i**A**mine (PPDA) + **T**ere**P**hthaloyl **C**hloride (TPC). Both are *para-substituted*; the *meta* isomer gives the much weaker **Nomex**.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Identity Markers (Bold Constants)

| Physical Metric | Kevlar 49 (Standard) Value |
|---|---|
| Density (ρ) | **1.44 g/cm³** |
| Tensile Strength | **3.6 GPa** (≈ 525,000 psi) |
| Young's Modulus (E) | **130 GPa** (Kevlar 49) |
| Elongation at Break | ≈ **2.4 – 3.6 %** |
| Decomposition Temperature | **≥ 500 °C** |
| LOI (Limiting Oxygen Index) | ≈ **29** (self-extinguishing) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Kevlar Within Polymer Science

Kevlar is simultaneously classified in **three orthogonal taxonomies** that examiners love to test:

1. **By chain architecture:** *Wholly aromatic, rigid-rod, linear polymer* (no aliphatic –CH₂– spacers in the main chain).
2. **By inter-chain interaction:** *Hydrogen-bonded polymer* (the amide linkage gives strong secondary bonding).
3. **By physical state during processing:** *Lyotropic liquid-crystalline polymer* (forms ordered anisotropic solutions above a critical concentration in strong acids).
4. **By polymerisation mechanism:** *Step-growth (condensation) polymerisation* with elimination of **HCl**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "lyotropic"?** *Lyo-* (Greek: *lyein* = to dissolve) implies the mesophase forms in solution, as opposed to *thermotropic* polymers (e.g., Vectra) which form liquid crystals in the melt. The critical concentration in 100 % H₂SO₄ is roughly **8 – 10 wt %**.

### 2.2 Monomer Pair and Polymerisation Chemistry

The two complementary A–B type monomers used in industrial Kevlar synthesis are:

**Monomer 1 — 1,4-Phenylene diamine (PPD):**

$$\text{H}_2\text{N}-\underset{\text{para-phenylene}}{=}\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}_2$$

**Monomer 2 — Terephthaloyl chloride (TPC):**

$$\text{Cl}-\text{CO}-\underset{\text{para-phenylene}}{=}\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-\text{Cl}$$

The polymerisation is **interfacial or low-temperature solution polycondensation** in *N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone* (NMP) with dissolved **CaCl₂** (or in *hexamethylphosphoramide*). The by-product is **HCl**, which is neutralised by an acid acceptor (e.g., Ca(OH)₂ or pyridine).

### 2.3 The Step-Growth Polymerisation — Why High Molar Mass is Hard

Because Kevlar is made by step-growth polymerisation, it obeys the **Carothers equation**. To reach a usable engineering polymer (DPₙ ≥ 100, more practically DPₙ ≥ 200 for fibers), the reaction conversion **p** must exceed:

$$p \geq 1 - \frac{1}{\text{DP}_n}$$

For DPₙ = 200, we need **p ≥ 0.995**, i.e., **99.5 %** conversion. This is the reason DuPont had to invent extremely pure, rigorously stoichiometric monomers and aggressive mixing — even a 0.5 % imbalance caps the molecular weight.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum mathematical toolkit** the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce for any Kevlar-related numerical. Pipes are deliberately written as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers equation; DPₙ = number-average degree of polymerisation, *p* = fractional conversion | Compute required conversion for a target DPₙ |
| 2 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Alternative notation for DPₙ | Same as above |
| 3 | $\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0$ | Number-average molar mass; M₀ = molar mass of repeat unit | Compute $\overline{M}_n$ from DPₙ |
| 4 | $\text{Repeat unit mass } M_0 = 238.24 \text{ g/mol}$ | Molar mass of the PPTA repeat unit ($-\text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-$) | Used in all Kevlar molar-mass problems |
| 5 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Tensile stress; F = force, A₀ = initial cross-section | Compare Kevlar vs steel |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \cdot 100\,\%$ | Engineering strain | Ductility comparison |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's modulus | Stiffness comparison |
| 8 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\rho}$ | Strength-to-weight ratio (kN·m/kg) | Bullet-proof vest design |
| 9 | $\text{Intrinsic viscosity } \vert \eta \vert = K M^a$ | Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation | Determining M from viscosity in H₂SO₄ |
| 10 | $T_m \geq 500\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (with decomposition) | No clean melt — Kevlar **decomposes** before melting | Why it is dry-jet wet-spun, not melt-spun |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

Kevlar is a **multi-domain engineering material** with quantifiable performance benefits:

- **Ballistic Protection:** Used in *soft body armour* (NIJ Level II–IIIA). Energy absorption occurs through *fiber pull-out*, *chain scission*, and *crazing* mechanisms.
- **Aerospace:** Asbestos replacement in brake pads; reinforcement in aircraft composite skins.
- **Automotive:** Tire cord reinforcement (Kevlar-belted radial tires) for high-speed stability and puncture resistance.
- **Marine & Rope:** High-modulus mooring lines, deep-sea umbilicals.
- **Civil:** Concrete and asphalt reinforcement; earthquake-resistant column wraps.
- **Sports:** Canoe hulls, racing sails, climbing rope cores, bicycle tires.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's heuristic:** Every time a KTU question describes a polymer used in "bullet-proof vests" or "high-strength composites," the answer is almost always *Kevlar* (para-aramid). *Spectra* (UHMWPE) and *Zylon* (PBO) are alternatives, but Kevlar is the default textbook answer.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Repeat-Unit Molar Mass (M₀) of Kevlar

**Step 1 — Write the repeat unit explicitly.**

The repeat unit of PPTA is:

$$-\text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}-$$

**Step 2 — Identify the atomic inventory of one repeat unit.**

The structure contains:

- Two **para-phenylene rings** (each ring = C₆H₄)
- Two **amide linkages** (each linkage = –NH–CO–)

Counting atoms per repeat unit:

- **Carbon atoms:** $6 + 6 + 1 + 1 = 14$  (12 from the two rings + 2 from the two carbonyl carbons)
- **Hydrogen atoms:** $4 + 4 + 1 + 1 = 10$  (8 aromatic H + 2 amide N–H)
- **Nitrogen atoms:** 2 (both from the amide groups)
- **Oxygen atoms:** 2 (both from the carbonyl groups)

**Step 3 — Compute the molecular formula.**

$$\text{Molecular formula per repeat unit} = \text{C}_{14}\text{H}_{10}\text{N}_2\text{O}_2$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by atomic weights (g/mol).**

| Atom | Count | Atomic Mass (g/mol) | Sub-Total (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| C | 14 | 12.011 | 168.154 |
| H | 10 | 1.008 | 10.080 |
| N | 2 | 14.007 | 28.014 |
| O | 2 | 15.999 | 31.998 |

**Step 5 — Sum the sub-totals.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= 168.154 + 10.080 + 28.014 + 31.998 \\
M_0 &= 238.246 \text{ g/mol} \\
M_0 &\approx 238.24 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final boxed value used in all subsequent problems.**

$$\boxed{M_0 = 238.24 \text{ g/mol}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Carothers Equation Applied to Kevlar

**Step 1 — Define fractional conversion p.**

In a step-growth polymerisation of an A–B type system with perfect stoichiometry, let **p** be the fraction of functional groups (–NH₂ or –COCl) that have reacted at time *t*.

**Step 2 — Express the number-average degree of polymerisation.**

By definition:

$$\text{DP}_n = \overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{Total number of monomer units originally present}}{\text{Total number of molecules remaining at time } t}$$

If we start with **N₀** monomer molecules and the fraction reacted is *p*, the number of unreacted ends corresponds to **N₀(1 – p)** molecules remaining on average. Hence:

$$\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$$

**Step 3 — Carothers equation (final form).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DP}_n &= \dfrac{1}{1-p} \\
\text{or equivalently} \quad p &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{\text{DP}_n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the conversion required for a typical Kevlar grade.**

For Kevlar 49 fibers, the typical number-average molecular weight is roughly $\overline{M}_n \approx 40{,}000$ g/mol. Hence:

$$\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{238.24} \approx 167.9$$

Required conversion:

$$\begin{aligned}
p &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{\text{DP}_n} \\
p &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{167.9} \\
p &= 1 - 0.005955 \\
p &= 0.99405 \\
p &\approx 99.405\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Take-away:** A 0.6 % shortfall in monomer purity (e.g., monofunctional impurities) or stoichiometry will halve the achievable molecular weight. DuPont specifies monomers with **purity ≥ 99.8 %** and uses *on-line FTIR* to verify stoichiometry in real time.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Specific Strength — Kevlar vs Steel (Comparative Numerical)

This is a **standard KTU numerical** type. We will derive the specific strength of both materials quantitatively.

**Given Data:**

| Material | Density ρ (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength σ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Kevlar 49 | 1.44 | 3600 |
| Mild Steel | 7.85 | 500 |

**Step 1 — Convert densities to SI (kg/m³) for consistency.**

$$\rho_{\text{Kevlar}} = 1.44 \times 1000 = 1440 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho_{\text{Steel}} = 7.85 \times 1000 = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Convert tensile strengths to Pa (SI).**

$$\sigma_{\text{Kevlar}} = 3600 \text{ MPa} = 3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{Steel}} = 500 \text{ MPa} = 5.0 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute specific strength = σ / ρ.**

For Kevlar 49:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SS}_{\text{Kevlar}} &= \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^9}{1440} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m/kg} \\
&= 2500 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

For Mild Steel:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SS}_{\text{Steel}} &= \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^8}{7850} \\
&= 6.37 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m/kg} \\
&= 63.7 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of specific strengths.**

$$\dfrac{\text{SS}_{\text{Kevlar}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{Steel}}} = \dfrac{2500}{63.7} \approx 39.2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** On a *per-unit-mass* basis, Kevlar 49 is approximately **39 times stronger** than mild steel, even though on a per-unit-volume basis the ratio is only $\dfrac{3600}{500} = 7.2$. This is the fundamental reason Kevlar dominates weight-critical engineering applications (ballistic armor, aerospace, racing yachts).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Carothers / Kevlar Molar Mass Problem

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]:** Kevlar is synthesised from 1,4-phenylene diamine and terephthaloyl chloride in equimolar ratio. If the final conversion is **99.0 %**, compute (a) the number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{X}_n$, and (b) the number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n$.

**Solution.**

**Part (a):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1}{1-p} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1-0.990} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.010} \\
&= 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{M}_n &= \overline{X}_n \cdot M_0 \\
&= 100 \times 238.24 \\
&= 23{,}824 \text{ g/mol} \\
&\approx 23.8 \text{ kg/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check:** Industrial Kevlar grades have $\overline{M}_n$ in the range **20,000 – 40,000 g/mol**, so a conversion of 99.0 % is just at the threshold of useful fiber properties. Higher grades (Kevlar 149) require conversion ≥ 99.5 %.
