## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define the term **"aramid fiber"** with a suitable example. Explain why Kevlar is classified as a *para-aramid* and not a *meta-aramid*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **aramid fiber** is an aromatic polyamide in which at least **85 %** of the amide linkages are directly attached to two aromatic rings (as defined by the **US Federal Trade Commission**). Aramid fibers are a class of heat-resistant and exceptionally strong synthetic polymers.

**Examples:** Kevlar (para-aramid) and **Nomex** (meta-aramid).

**Why para, not meta:**  
In Kevlar, the amide –NH– and –CO– groups are attached at the **1 and 4 positions** (para) of the phenylene ring, producing a *linear, rigid-rod, fully extended chain conformation*. In contrast, **Nomex** has 1,3 (meta) substitution, which introduces a *kink* in the chain, prevents close-packing, and dramatically reduces tensile strength and modulus. Hence Kevlar is the strongest commercial aramid and is a **para-aramid**.

*(Mark split: 1 mark – definition; 1 mark – example + Kevlar/Nomex distinction; 1 mark – para vs meta geometry explanation.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**  
Name the two monomers used in the synthesis of Kevlar. Write the repeating unit of the resulting polymer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two monomers are:

1. **1,4-Phenylene diamine (para-phenylenediamine, PPD):** $\text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}_2$
2. **Terephthaloyl chloride (TPC):** $\text{ClOC}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{COCl}$

**Repeating unit of poly-para-phenylene terephthalamide (Kevlar):**

$$\left[ - \text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}- \right]_n$$

The polymerisation is a **step-growth (condensation) polycondensation** with elimination of **HCl** as a by-product.

*(Mark split: 1 mark – both monomer names and structures; 1 mark – repeating unit; 1 mark – polymerisation type and by-product.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **synthesis of Kevlar** by low-temperature solution polycondensation, including the monomer pair, reaction conditions, solvent system, by-product, and acid acceptor used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **structure–property relationships** of Kevlar. Discuss the role of (i) the para-phenylene ring, (ii) the amide hydrogen bonding, and (iii) the lyotropic liquid-crystalline behaviour during spinning. Relate these to the observed **tensile strength, modulus, and thermal stability** of Kevlar. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Monomers:**

- 1,4-Phenylene diamine (PPD), purity ≥ 99.8 %.
- Terephthaloyl chloride (TPC), purity ≥ 99.8 %.

**Reaction type:** *A–B type step-growth condensation polymerisation.*

**Solvent system:** *N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP)* with dissolved **CaCl₂** (≈ 5 – 10 wt %) to increase the polarity of the medium and dissolve the PPDA. An alternative is *hexamethylphosphoramide* (HMPA), now restricted due to toxicity.

**Temperature:** **−10 °C to +50 °C** (low temperature to suppress side reactions and to keep the acid chloride stable).

**Mechanism (two-step nucleophilic acyl substitution):**

- Step 1: The amine nitrogen of PPD attacks the carbonyl carbon of TPC, displacing Cl⁻ and forming the amide bond with release of HCl.
- Step 2: The reaction propagates as the second amine group of the di-amine attacks a second acid chloride of another TPC molecule, etc.

**By-product:** **HCl gas.**

**Acid acceptor (HCl scavenger):** **Ca(OH)₂** (slaked lime) or *pyridine* / triethylamine. The acid acceptor is essential because free HCl would protonate the amine end groups and stop chain growth.

**Stoichiometry:** Equimolar PPD : TPC. Stoichiometric imbalance must be kept **below 0.1 %** to avoid molecular-weight capping.

**Polymer isolation:** The PPTA precipitates as a fine white powder, which is washed free of salts and dried.

**Final spinning step:** The dry PPTA powder is re-dissolved in **concentrated (≥ 98 %) sulfuric acid** to form a **lyotropic liquid crystalline dope** (8 – 10 wt % polymer), which is then **dry-jet wet-spun** through an air gap into a cold water coagulation bath, followed by hot drawing at ~550 °C to lock in chain orientation.

> **Key Equation:**

$$\text{n H}_2\text{N}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}_2 + \text{n ClOC}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{COCl} \longrightarrow \left[ -\text{NH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{NH}-\text{CO}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_4-\text{CO}- \right]_n + 2\text{n HCl}$$

*(Mark split — 1 mark each: monomers, solvent, temperature, by-product, acid acceptor, mechanism, and final spinning step.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**(i) Role of the para-phenylene ring:**  
The *para-phenylene ring* is a *rigid, planar, aromatic* unit. It cannot rotate freely and prevents chain coiling. This confers an **extended-chain conformation** and a very **high chain stiffness**, which translates directly into a high **Young's modulus (130 GPa for Kevlar 49)**. Aromatic conjugation also raises the *bond dissociation energy* and the *decomposition temperature* (>500 °C).

**(ii) Role of amide hydrogen bonding:**  
The amide group (–CO–NH–) is a strong H-bond donor (N–H) and acceptor (C=O). In a *para*-aramid, the geometry of the chain is such that the N–H of one chain H-bonds with the C=O of a *neighbouring chain* at almost the *optimal distance* (≈ 2.0 Å). This creates a **2D hydrogen-bonded sheet** in the lateral direction, which is then stacked via π–π *aromatic ring interactions* into 3D crystallites. These secondary bonds make the fiber very strong in *transverse* directions and prevent chain slippage under tensile load.

**(iii) Role of lyotropic liquid-crystalline behaviour:**  
In concentrated H₂SO₄, PPTA chains are *protonated* and stiff. Above a critical concentration (≈ 8 – 10 wt %), the solution spontaneously separates into *anisotropic* (ordered) and *isotropic* phases. The chains in the anisotropic phase are **locally aligned in parallel domains** (a *nematic* mesophase). When this dope is extruded through a spinneret, the **shear flow** in the air gap aligns the domains *parallel to the fiber axis* almost perfectly. This pre-alignment means Kevlar fibers do not need to be cold-drawn to high ratios like flexible-chain polymers (e.g., polyethylene) — the orientation is essentially built in.

**Resulting engineering properties:**

- **Tensile strength ≈ 3.6 GPa** (≈ 5× steel, on volume basis).
- **Specific strength ≈ 2500 kN·m/kg** (≈ 39× mild steel on weight basis).
- **Young's modulus ≈ 130 GPa**.
- **No melting point**; thermal stability up to 500 °C.
- **Low creep** due to rigid rod and H-bond crosslinking.
- **Flame retardant** (LOI ≈ 29).

*(Mark split — 1.5 marks each for (i), (ii), (iii); 1 mark for the final property summary; 1 mark for the connecting conclusion sentence.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Carothers equation**, derive the relationship between **number-average degree of polymerisation (DPₙ)** and **fractional conversion (p)**. A Kevlar sample is reported to have a number-average molecular weight of 30,000 g/mol. Calculate the **percentage conversion** of the monomers during synthesis. (M₀ of the Kevlar repeat unit = 238.24 g/mol). (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Kevlar (para-aramid)**, **Nomex (meta-aramid)**, and **Nylon 6,6 (aliphatic polyamide)** in terms of (i) chemical structure, (ii) hydrogen bonding, (iii) thermal stability, (iv) mechanical strength, and (v) typical engineering applications. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Derivation of the Carothers Equation:**

Let **N₀** be the initial number of monomer molecules (or, equivalently, the initial number of A-functional groups) in an A–B type polycondensation with perfect stoichiometry. At any time *t*, let the fraction of functional groups that have reacted be **p** (fractional conversion). The probability that a randomly chosen functional group *has not yet reacted* is therefore (1 − p).

The **number-average degree of polymerisation** $\overline{X}_n$ is defined as:

$$\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{Total number of monomer units originally present}}{\text{Total number of polymer molecules remaining at time } t}$$

The number of polymer molecules remaining at time *t* equals the number of unreacted functional groups divided by 2 (because each polymer molecule has two ends, ignoring the trivial monomer case):

$$N_t = N_0(1-p)$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} \\
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1}{1-p}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Carothers equation** (for stoichiometric A–B systems).

**Numerical application:**

Given: $\overline{M}_n = 30{,}000$ g/mol and $M_0 = 238.24$ g/mol.

Step 1: Calculate DPₙ:

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n = \text{DP}_n &= \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} \\
&= \dfrac{30{,}000}{238.24} \\
&\approx 125.9
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2: Calculate fractional conversion *p*:

$$\begin{aligned}
p &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{\text{DP}_n} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{1}{125.9} \\
&= 1 - 0.00794 \\
&= 0.99206
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3: Express as a percentage:

$$\boxed{p \approx 99.21\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Industrial-grade Kevlar requires conversion in the range **99.0 – 99.7 %**. The calculated value 99.21 % is realistic for a mid-grade Kevlar fiber. Sub-99 % conversion is unacceptable because the chain length falls below the threshold required for load-bearing fiber applications.

*(Mark split — 2 marks: derivation of Carothers equation; 1 mark: computing DPₙ; 1 mark: substituting into inverse Carothers; 1 mark: percentage conversion; 1 mark: numerical accuracy; 1 mark: engineering conclusion.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

A systematic tabular comparison is the cleanest way to present this answer in 14-mark KTU papers. The table below covers all five required axes.

| Comparison Axis | Kevlar (Para-Aramid) | Nomex (Meta-Aramid) | Nylon 6,6 (Aliphatic Polyamide) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Chemical Structure** | Para-phenylene + amide; linear, rigid-rod | Meta-phenylene + amide; kinked, less rigid | Aliphatic –(CH₂)₆–NHCO–(CH₂)₄–CONH– |
| (ii) **Hydrogen Bonding** | Strong, regular, 2D sheet-like | Weaker and irregular due to meta kink | Strong, but chains are flexible; H-bonds reform on stretching |
| (iii) **Thermal Stability** | Decomposes > 500 °C; no melt | Stable to ≈ 370 °C; chars, does not melt | Melts at ≈ 265 °C; chars above 300 °C |
| (iv) **Mechanical Strength** | Tensile strength ≈ 3.6 GPa; modulus ≈ 130 GPa | Tensile strength ≈ 0.6 GPa; modulus ≈ 17 GPa | Tensile strength ≈ 0.08 GPa; modulus ≈ 2.8 GPa |
| (v) **Typical Applications** | Ballistic armor, tire cord, brake pads, aerospace composites, ropes | Firefighter suits, electrical insulation, hot-gas filtration, racing driver suits | Carpets, apparel, gears, fishing line, molded engineering parts |

**Concluding sentence (mandatory for 14-mark completeness):**

> *Kevlar's para-phenylene geometry and resultant rigid-rod chain conformation allow close inter-chain H-bonding and π–π stacking, producing fiber properties that are an order of magnitude superior to both Nomex and Nylon 6,6 — but at the cost of poor processability (it cannot be melt-processed).*

*(Mark split — 1 mark for each of the five comparison axes, 2 marks for the concluding synthesis statement.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Kevlar Questions:**
>
> 1. **Monomer confusion:** Students frequently write "Kevlar is made from *adipic acid* and *hexamethylene diamine*" — that is **Nylon 6,6**, not Kevlar. Kevlar uses **terephthaloyl chloride** (not adipic acid) and **1,4-phenylene diamine** (not hexamethylene diamine). Forgetting the *aromaticity* costs **2 marks outright**.
>
> 2. **Para vs Meta:** If a student writes that Kevlar is *meta*-aramid, the entire structure–property argument is invalidated. Always remember: **Kevlar = para, Nomex = meta**.
>
> 3. **Carothers conversion error:** A common mistake is using **DPₙ = 1/(1−p)** but then plugging the conversion in *decimal* form, not *fractional*. Always write $p = 0.992$, **not** $p = 99.2$, in the denominator.
>
> 4. **Mistaking Kevlar for a thermoplastic that can be melt-processed:** Kevlar **decomposes before melting** because the rigid aromatic backbone prevents chain mobility. It is **solution-spun**, not melt-extruded. Examiners deduct marks if students describe injection molding of Kevlar.
>
> 5. **Ignoring HCl scavenging:** The role of Ca(OH)₂ or pyridine in *neutralising HCl* is frequently omitted. This is a **1-mark loss** that compounds across many questions.
>
> 6. **Confusing "aramid" with "polyamide" in general:** Nylon 6,6 is a polyamide but **not** an aramid because the amide groups are not bonded to *aromatic rings* on both sides. The defining 85 %-aromatic criterion (FTC) is often forgotten.
>
> 7. **Omitting units in the Carothers numerical:** Always write **g/mol** explicitly for $\overline{M}_n$ and **%** for conversion. A unit error will be penalised by the KTU board even if the numerical value is correct.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Kevlar = poly-para-phenylene terephthalamide (PPTA)**, a *para-aramid* fiber.
- [x] **Monomers:** 1,4-phenylene diamine (PPD) + Terephthaloyl chloride (TPC). *Both para-substituted.*
- [x] **Polymerisation type:** A–B step-growth condensation; by-product = **HCl**; acid acceptor = **Ca(OH)₂** or pyridine.
- [x] **Solvent for synthesis:** NMP with CaCl₂ (or HMPA).
- [x] **Spinning solvent:** Concentrated (≥ 98 %) **H₂SO₄** — produces a **lyotropic liquid-crystalline dope**.
- [x] **Spinning method:** Dry-jet wet spinning (not melt spinning — Kevlar decomposes before melting).
- [x] **Repeat unit molar mass:** $M_0 = 238.24$ g/mol.
- [x] **Carothers equation:** $\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$; for DPₙ ≈ 100, **p ≈ 99.0 %**; for DPₙ ≈ 200, **p ≈ 99.5 %**.
- [x] **Key structural features:** (1) rigid *para*-phenylene backbone, (2) strong N–H · · · O=C hydrogen bonding, (3) π–π stacking of aromatic rings.
- [x] **Properties:** Tensile strength ≈ 3.6 GPa, modulus ≈ 130 GPa, density 1.44 g/cm³, decomposes > 500 °C, **does not melt**, LOI ≈ 29.
- [x] **Specific strength:** ~ 2500 kN·m/kg (≈ 39× mild steel on weight basis).
- [x] **Discoverer:** **Stephanie Kwolek**, DuPont, **1965** (very high-yield one-mark question).
- [x] **Differentiate from Nomex:** Nomex is **meta-aramid** (1,3-substitution) → kinked chain → lower strength but better processability.
- [x] **Differentiate from Nylon 6,6:** Nylon 6,6 is *aliphatic* polyamide → melt-processable but weaker.
- [x] **Primary applications:** Ballistic armor (bullet-proof vests), tire cord, brake pads, aerospace composites, high-modulus ropes.
- [x] **KTU-exam keyword list (for last-minute recall):** *para-aramid, PPTA, Kwolek, 1965, DuPont, lyotropic, dry-jet wet spinning, rigid-rod, hydrogen bonding, Carothers equation, 99.5 % conversion, no melt, 500 °C decomposition, 1.44 g/cm³, 3.6 GPa, 130 GPa.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Natural Rubber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — NATURAL RUBBER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Natural Rubber (NR)** is defined as a high-molecular-weight, **cis-1,4-polyisoprene** elastomer obtained from the latex of the **Hevea brasiliensis** (Para rubber tree), belonging to the family *Euphorbiaceae*. It is a naturally occurring *addition polymer* of the monomer **isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene)**, with a stereoregular configuration where more than **99%** of the repeating units adopt the *cis* geometric isomerism around the residual double bond of the polymer backbone.

The IUPAC systematic name of the repeating unit is **1,4-poly(2-methyl-1,3-butadiene)**, and the structural formula of the monomer is **CH₂=C(CH₃)−CH=CH₂** (molar mass = **68.12 g/mol**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Natural Rubber as "biological spaghetti with chemical Velcro."** 
> The *long chains* of polyisoprene are like boiled spaghetti strands randomly coiled in a bowl — they slide past each other easily, which is why raw rubber is **sticky and soft**. **Vulcanization** (adding sulfur) acts like *tiny chemical Velcro dots* that tie adjacent strands together at specific points, transforming the slippery mass into a bouncy, elastic, durable material. Without these crosslinks, the rubber would be a useless goo; with too many, it becomes a brittle solid.

### 1.3 Botanical Source & Latex Origin

- **Tree:** *Hevea brasiliensis* — a tropical tree native to the Amazon basin (Brazil).
- **Latex:** A milky colloidal suspension secreted by specialized laticifer cells.
- **Latex Composition (typical):**

| Component | Approx. % (by weight) |
|---|---|
| Water | 55 – 65 |
| Polyisoprene (rubber) | 30 – 40 |
| Proteins | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| Lipids (fats/waxes) | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Carbohydrates, sugars | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Inorganic salts (K, Mg, P) | 0.5 – 1.0 |
| Other (resins, ash) | < 0.5 |

- **Tapping:** A shallow diagonal incision is made in the bark; latex oozes out and is collected in cups. A mature tree yields **~2.5 – 3.0 kg** of dry rubber per year.

### 1.4 Historical Highlights (Exam-Oriented)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Christopher Columbus (1493)**: First European record of rubber use (Haitian natives playing elastic balls).
> - **Charles-Marie de la Condamine (1736)**: Brought rubber samples to Europe from South America.
> - **Thomas Hancock (1820)**: Developed the *masticator* (mechanical plasticizer).
> - **Charles Goodyear (1839)**: Accidentally discovered **vulcanization** by heating rubber with sulfur on a hot stove — this is the single most important event in rubber history.
> - **Sir Henry Wickham (1876)**: Smuggled **70,000 Hevea seeds** from Brazil to Kew Gardens, London, which were then germinated and sent to Ceylon (Sri Lanka) and Malaya — founding the Asian rubber industry.
> - **S.S. Pickles (1934)**: Determined the *cis* configuration of natural rubber using X-ray diffraction.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Natural Rubber

Natural rubber is classified by **source method** and **grade**:

**(a) By Source Method:**
- **Field-grade latex** (preserved with ammonia)
- **Concentrated latex** (centrifuged, ~60% DRC)
- **Coagulum (cup lump)** — naturally coagulated latex
- **Sheet rubber** — coagulated, rolled, and air-dried
- **Crepe rubber** — coagulated, passed through rollers to produce crinkly sheets

**(b) By Commercial Grade (Technically Specified Rubber — TSR):**

| Grade | Form | Key Spec |
|---|---|---|
| RSS 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 | Ribbed Smoked Sheet | Visual grading, smoked |
| SMR (Standard Malaysian Rubber) | Block form | Technically graded (e.g., SMR 5, SMR 10, SMR 20, SMR GP) |
| SIR (Standard Indian Rubber) | Block form | ISNR 5, ISNR 10, etc. |
| Pale Crepe | Light-colored crepe | Low dirt content |

> [!NOTE]
> **DRC (Dry Rubber Content)** is the standard metric for latex concentration, expressed as a mass percentage. The TSC (Total Solid Content) and DRC differ by the non-rubber solids (proteins, etc.).

---

### 1.6 Chemical Structure of Natural Rubber

The repeating unit after 1,4-addition polymerization of isoprene:

$$\left[ -\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2- \right]_n$$

The **C=C double bond** between C2 and C3 (in the repeating unit) gives two geometric isomers:

- **Cis-1,4-polyisoprene (Natural Rubber):** The two main-chain segments lie on the **same side** of the C=C double bond.
- **Trans-1,4-polyisoprene (Gutta-percha):** The two main-chain segments lie on the **opposite side** — a hard, rigid, non-elastic material.

**Stereoregularity:** Natural rubber exhibits > **99% cis-1,4** configuration, which is the key to its **strain-induced crystallization** behavior and superior mechanical strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polyisoprene can be classified into two structural categories:**
> 1. **Natural Polyisoprene (NPI):** Found in Hevea brasiliensis (cis) and gutta-percha trees (trans).
> 2. **Synthetic Polyisoprene (IR):** Produced industrially; however, even the best synthetic IR is only ~96% cis, leading to slightly inferior mechanical properties compared to natural rubber.

### 1.7 Molecular Weight & Distribution

- **Number-average molecular weight (Mₙ):** ~ **2 × 10⁵ to 3 × 10⁵ g/mol**
- **Weight-average molecular weight (Mₖ):** ~ **1 × 10⁶ to 1.5 × 10⁶ g/mol**
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI = Mₖ/Mₙ):** ~ **3 – 5** (broad distribution)
- **Degree of Polymerization (DP):** ~ **3,000 – 5,000** repeating isoprene units
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** **−72 °C** (one of the lowest among commercial elastomers)
- **Melting point (Tm):** ~ **30 – 40 °C** (highly strain-crystallizing)

### 1.8 Unique Features of Natural Rubber vs. Synthetic Elastomers

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Strain-induced crystallization** (absent in most synthetics below ~200% elongation).
> 2. **Self-reinforcing** — gum vulcanizates (no carbon black) still have high tensile strength (~20–25 MPa).
> 3. **High resilience and low heat build-up.**
> 4. **Excellent green strength** (strength of unvulcanized compound).
> 5. **Biodegradable** (microbiologically attacked).
> 6. **Builds tack** — sticks to itself easily during tire building.
> 7. **Non-polar** — poor resistance to petroleum oils and hydrocarbons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerization Mechanism of Natural Rubber (In Vivo Biosynthesis)

Natural rubber is synthesized **inside the laticifer cells of Hevea brasiliensis** via a **biological, enzyme-catalyzed, cis-stereospecific polymerization** of isopentenyl diphosphate (IPP), which is the biological equivalent of isoprene.

### 2.1.1 Biochemical Pathway (Simplified)

The pathway involves three sequential enzyme systems:

| Step | Substrate | Enzyme | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Initiation | IPP + allylic pyrophosphate (FPP) | **Rubber Transferase (Rtase)** | cis-1,4-polyisoprene + pyrophosphate |
| 2. Elongation | IPP added repeatedly to chain end | Rubber Elongation Factor (REF) + Rtase | Long-chain polyisoprene |
| 3. Termination | Chain release | (unclear — possibly proton loss) | Free polymer chain in latex |

- **Monomer unit:** Isopentenyl diphosphate (IPP), C₅H₉O₇P₂ (M = **246.09 g/mol**).
- **Chain initiator:** Farnesyl diphosphate (FPP, C₁₅H₂₈O₇P₂) or geranylgeranyl diphosphate (GGPP).
- **Cofactor:** **Mg²⁺ ions** are essential for enzyme activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each IPP addition eliminates an inorganic pyrophosphate (PPᵢ), which is hydrolyzed to 2 Pᵢ — providing the thermodynamic driving force. This makes rubber biosynthesis an **irreversible, highly exergonic process** (ΔG ≈ −30 kJ/mol per IPP addition).

## 2.2 Structure–Property Relationships

### 2.2.1 Why is Natural Rubber Elastic?

The high elasticity arises from **three concurrent molecular features**:

1. **Long, flexible polymer chains:** The C–C single bonds allow rotation, enabling chain coiling and uncoiling.
2. **Low Tg (−72 °C):** At room temperature, the material is well above Tg, so segmental motion is facile.
3. **Weak secondary forces:** Only **van der Waals** interactions between chains — no strong dipole or H-bonding.
4. **Sparse crosslinks after vulcanization:** ~1 crosslink per **200–300 monomer units**, allowing chain slip but preventing flow.

### 2.2.2 Strain-Induced Crystallization (SIC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When natural rubber is stretched to > **~200% elongation**, the polymer chains align and **crystallize partially**. This is **reversible** (crystals melt on relaxation). The effect is called **strain-induced crystallization (SIC)** or **self-reinforcement**.

**Why does this happen?**
- The regular *cis* configuration and absence of bulky side-groups allow tight chain packing under tension.
- The crystals act as **nano-fillers**, dramatically increasing tensile strength and tear resistance.
- This is why **gum NR vulcanizates** (no carbon black) still achieve tensile strength of **20–30 MPa**, whereas SBR or BR gum vulcanizates achieve only **~2–3 MPa**.

**Key Observation:** SIC gives natural rubber high **tensile strength, fatigue resistance, and tear strength** — making it irreplaceable in heavy-duty tires and aircraft tire carcasses.

### 2.2.3 Oxidation & Aging

The **allylic C–H bond** adjacent to the C=C double bond is **very weak** (~ 339 kJ/mol vs. ~ 413 kJ/mol for saturated C–H). This makes NR susceptible to:
- **Ozone cracking** — particularly in the presence of O₃ and stress.
- **Thermal oxidative degradation** — chain scission leads to softening and tackiness.
- **UV-induced crosslinking** — stiffening and embrittlement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-ozonants** (e.g., *N*-1,3-dimethylbutyl-*N'*-phenyl-*p*-phenylenediamine, 6PPD) and **antioxidants** (e.g., hindered phenols like BHT) are **mandatory** in all commercial NR compounds to suppress aging.

## 2.3 Vulcanization — The Heart of Rubber Technology

### 2.3.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Vulcanization** is the process of forming three-dimensional crosslinks between polymer chains of an elastomer, typically by reacting the rubber with **sulfur** (or other agents) under heat and pressure, in the presence of accelerators and activators. The process was discovered by **Charles Goodyear in 1839** and named by **William Brockedon (1842)** after the Roman god of fire, *Vulcan*.

### 2.3.2 Sulfur Vulcanization Chemistry

The classical mechanism is a **free-radical process** in three stages:

**Stage 1 — Activation of Sulfur:**
The accelerator (e.g., **2-mercaptobenzothiazole, MBT**, or **tetramethylthiuram disulfide, TMTD**) reacts with the activator (**ZnO + stearic acid**) to form an active sulfurating species (Zn-accelerator complex).

**Stage 2 — Crosslink Formation:**
The active complex reacts with the **allylic hydrogen** of the polyisoprene chain:

$$\text{RH} + \text{[S]} \longrightarrow \text{R–S–S–H} + \text{[accelerator]}$$

Then:

$$2 \text{ R–S•} \longrightarrow \text{R–S–S–R} \quad \text{(mono- or di-sulfide crosslink)}$$

**Stage 3 — Maturation (Post-cure crosslink shortening):**
Long polysulfide crosslinks (Sx, x = 4–8) rearrange to short disulfides and monosulfides, improving aging resistance.

**Crosslink Types:**

| Crosslink Type | Formula | Relative Stability |
|---|---|---|
| Polysulfide | R–Sₓ–R (x = 4–8) | Poor thermal stability, good fatigue life |
| Disulfide | R–S–S–R | Moderate |
| Monosulfide | R–S–R | Excellent thermal/oxidative stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficient vulcanization (EV) systems** use low sulfur (0.3–0.5 phr) + high accelerator (3–6 phr) to produce predominantly **monosulfidic crosslinks** — used in heat-resistant applications. **Conventional (CV) systems** use 2.0–3.5 phr sulfur + 0.5–1.0 phr accelerator — better fatigue resistance.

### 2.3.3 Other Vulcanization Systems

- **Peroxide vulcanization** (e.g., dicumyl peroxide, DCP): Produces **C–C crosslinks** — superior heat resistance; used in silicone and EPDM.
- **Metallic oxide vulcanization** (ZnO/MgO): Used for **chloroprene rubber (CR)**.
- **Urethane crosslinking**: Used in millable polyurethane rubbers.
- **Electron-beam / γ-irradiation**: Radiation crosslinking, no chemical agents.

### 2.3.4 Compounding Ingredients (KTU High-Yield Table)

A commercial rubber compound is a complex formulation. The "recipe" lists the parts per hundred rubber (phr):

| Ingredient | Function | Common Examples | Typical phr |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polymer** | Base | NR (RSS 3) | 100.0 |
| **Activator** | Activate accelerator | ZnO + Stearic acid | 5.0 + 2.0 |
| **Accelerator** | Speed vulcanization | MBT, CBS, TMTD | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| **Curing agent** | Crosslinker | Sulfur | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| **Filler (reinforcing)** | Improve strength | Carbon black (HAF, ISAF), silica | 30 – 50 |
| **Filler (non-reinforcing)** | Reduce cost, modify | Calcium carbonate, clay | 10 – 50 |
| **Plasticizer / Softener** | Improve processability | Naphthenic/paraffinic oils | 5 – 20 |
| **Antioxidant** | Prevent aging | TMQ, 6PPD, BHT | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| **Antiozonant** | Prevent ozone cracking | 6PPD, waxes | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| **Blowing agent** | Produce cellular rubber | Azodicarbonamide (ADC) | 2 – 5 |
| **Pigments** | Color | Titanium dioxide, carbon black | 2 – 5 |
| **Peptizer** | Break down chains in mastication | Pentachlorothiophenol (PCTP) | 0.1 – 0.3 |
| **Retarder / Scorch inhibitor** | Prevent premature cure | PVI (N-cyclohexylthiophthalimide) | 0.1 – 0.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **phr** = parts per hundred parts of rubber (by mass). It is NOT a percentage; rather, the rubber is always normalized to 100 phr and all other ingredients are expressed relative to it.

## 2.4 Properties of Natural Rubber

### 2.4.1 Physical Properties (Gum Vulcanizate)

| Property | Typical Value | Test Method |
|---|---|---|
| Specific gravity | 0.92 – 0.93 | ASTM D297 |
| Hardness (Shore A) | 30 – 90 (depending on filler) | ASTM D2240 |
| Tensile strength (gum) | 20 – 30 MPa | ASTM D412 |
| Tensile strength (with CB) | 25 – 35 MPa | ASTM D412 |
| Elongation at break | 600 – 800% | ASTM D412 |
| 100% Modulus (with CB) | 2 – 4 MPa | ASTM D412 |
| Tear strength | 35 – 100 kN/m | ASTM D624 |
| Resilience (rebound) | 75 – 80% | Lupke pendulum |
| Abrasion resistance | Excellent | DIN 53516 |
| Compression set | 10 – 30% (22 h, 70 °C) | ASTM D395 |
| Tg | −72 °C | DSC, DMA |
| Specific heat (Cp) | 1.905 kJ/(kg·K) | DSC |
| Thermal conductivity | 0.13 – 0.16 W/(m·K) | Hot-wire method |
| Refractive index (nD) | 1.519 | Abbe refractometer |
| Dielectric strength | 16 – 24 kV/mm (briefly) | ASTM D149 |
| Dielectric constant (1 kHz) | 2.4 – 2.8 | ASTM D150 |
| Volume resistivity | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ Ω·cm | ASTM D257 |

### 2.4.2 Chemical Resistance

| Solvent / Chemical | Resistance | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Water | Excellent | Non-polar, no water absorption |
| Dilute acids | Good | Non-reactive |
| Dilute alkalis | Good | Non-reactive |
| Aliphatic hydrocarbons (petrol, hexane) | **Poor** | Dissolves / swells |
| Aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene) | **Very poor** | Strong swelling |
| Chlorinated solvents (CCl₄) | **Very poor** | Strong swelling |
| Animal/vegetable oils | Good | Mild swelling |
| Mineral oils | Poor | Strong swelling |
| Ozone | Poor | Cracks at stressed sites |
| Concentrated HNO₃, H₂SO₄ | Poor | Oxidative attack |

### 2.4.3 Mechanical & Dynamic Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Tear strength:** Among the highest of all elastomers (5–10× SBR) due to strain-induced crystallization.
> - **Resilience:** 75–80% rebound at room temperature — superior to SBR and BR.
> - **Heat build-up (Goodrich flexometer):** Low (~20 °C rise) — excellent for high-speed tires.
> - **Dynamic fatigue resistance:** Excellent for millions of cycles.
> - **Adhesion to fabric/steel cord:** Excellent — used as skim stock in tire building.

## 2.5 Mastication and Compounding Process

### 2.5.1 Mastication

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastication** is the mechanical breakdown of long NR chains on a two-roll mill or internal mixer (Banbury), reducing Mooney viscosity to a processable range. NR, unlike SBR, is **plasticized easily by mechanical shearing** because of free-radical chain scission at the weak allylic C–H bonds.

**Mechanism:** Mechanical shear → homolytic cleavage of C–C bond → free radicals → some recombine, some react with O₂ → net reduction in molecular weight.

**Peptizers** (e.g., PCTP, Zn salts of pentachlorothiophenol) accelerate the process by trapping radicals and preventing recombination.

### 2.5.2 Mixing & Compounding Steps

1. **Masticate** raw NR to reduce viscosity.
2. **Add fillers** (carbon black added in increments).
3. **Add plasticizers/oils**.
4. **Add activators** (ZnO, stearic acid).
5. **Add antioxidants/antiozonants**.
6. **Add sulfur and accelerators** LAST (to prevent scorch).
7. **Sheet-off and cool** before storage.
8. **Extrude/calender/mold** as required.

### 2.5.3 Scorch

> [!WARNING]
> **Scorch** is the premature vulcanization of a rubber compound during processing (mixing, extrusion, storage). It results in a hard, unworkable mass. Causes: high temperature, long storage, or excess accelerator. Controlled by **PVI retarders**, proper cooling, and careful accelerator choice.

## 2.6 Curing Parameters

The cure curve (from a **rheometer**, e.g., Moving Die Rheometer, MDR):

| Symbol | Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| M_L | Minimum torque | Viscosity of uncured compound |
| M_H | Maximum torque | Stiffness at full cure |
| t_s2 | Scorch time | Time to 2 units above M_L |
| t_90 | Optimum cure time | Time to 90% of M_H |
| t_95 | Technical cure time | Time to 95% of M_H |
| ΔM (M_H − M_L) | Cure extent | Proportional to crosslink density |
| Cure rate index (CRI) | 100/(t_90 − t_s2) | Speed of cure |

The crosslink density is related to the equilibrium swelling and modulus via the **Flory–Rehner equation** (see Section 3).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 2 | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 3 | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Polydispersity index | dimensionless |
| 4 | $DP_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | Number-average degree of polymerization | — |
| 5 | $\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{M_c}$ | Crosslink density (chemical) | mol/m³ |
| 6 | $M_c = \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{\nu_e}$ | Molecular weight between crosslinks | g/mol |
| 7 | $E = 3 \nu_e R T$ | Modulus (small deformation, affine network) | Pa |
| 8 | $G = \nu_e R T$ | Shear modulus (rubber elasticity) | Pa |
| 9 | $\sigma = \nu_e R T (\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Stress-strain (neo-Hookean, uniaxial) | Pa |
| 10 | $\nu_{2m}$ (Flory-Rehner) | Polymer volume fraction in swollen gel | — |
| 11 | $\chi$ (Flory-Huggins) | Polymer-solvent interaction parameter | — |
| 12 | $T_g$ (Fox-Flory) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | K |
| 13 | Mooney viscosity (ML) | Empirical measure of processability | MU |
| 14 | Mooney-Rivlin: $W = C_1 (I_1 - 3) + C_2 (I_2 - 3)$ | Strain energy density | J/m³ |
| 15 | $\tau \propto \Delta M$ | Cure extent from rheometer torque | dN·m |
| 16 | $T_c \approx 140{-}160~°C$ | Typical NR vulcanization temperature | °C |
| 17 | ASTM: $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ | Damping factor (DMA) | — |
| 18 | $\Delta V/V = (1 - \dfrac{1}{Q^3})$ (oil swell) | Volume swell in oil | — |
| 19 | $\rho_c = \dfrac{1}{2 M_c}$ | Crosslink density per unit volume | mol/m³ |
| 20 | $P = \rho R T / M_c$ | Elastic modulus (ideal rubber) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet Memory Aid:** Memorize equations **7, 8, 9, 12, 16, 19** as the high-frequency formulas for elastomer problems. The Flory–Rehner equation (Section 3.3) is the most common derivation in KTU board exams.

## 2.8 Engineering Applications of Natural Rubber

> [!NOTE]
> **Largest Application: Tires (~ 70% of global NR consumption)**

| Sector | Specific Applications |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Truck & bus tire carcasses, aircraft tires, tire sidewalls, inner liners (blended), engine mounts, grommets, hoses |
| **Industrial** | Conveyor belts, V-belts, gaskets, O-rings, dock fenders, anti-vibration mounts |
| **Medical** | Surgical gloves, catheters, condoms, tubing, vial stoppers |
| **Consumer goods** | Footwear soles, balloons, erasers, rubber bands, toys |
| **Civil engineering** | Bridge bearings, seismic isolators, expansion joints |
| **Sports** | Tennis balls, basketballs, diving suits, swim caps |
| **Adhesives** | Pressure-sensitive adhesives, contact cements (with solvents) |

## 2.9 Limitations of Natural Rubber

1. **Non-oil resistant** — swells in hydrocarbons.
2. **Poor ozone/UV resistance** — requires protective additives.
3. **Limited temperature range** — serviceable from ~ −55 °C to +70 °C (unvulcanized) or +100 °C (vulcanized).
4. **High cost volatility** — agricultural product, price fluctuates.
5. **Allergenic proteins** — cause Type I latex allergy (mitigated by deproteinized latex).
6. **Batch variability** — natural product, properties vary with source.
7. **Not compatible with all synthetic rubbers** in blending.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Crosslink Density from Swelling (Flory–Rehner Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Flory–Rehner equation** (1943) is the cornerstone for determining the **molecular weight between crosslinks (M_c)** of a vulcanized rubber from **equilibrium swelling data** in a good solvent. It is the single most important derivation in elastomer physics for KTU exams.

### 3.1.1 Setup

When a crosslinked rubber is immersed in a solvent:
- **Mixing term (ΔG_mix):** Favors swelling (solvent enters).
- **Elastic term (ΔG_elastic):** Opposes swelling (chains cannot dissolve because of crosslinks).

At **equilibrium swelling**, the two forces balance:

$$\Delta G_{total} = \Delta G_{mix} + \Delta G_{elastic} = 0$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Free Energy of Mixing (Flory–Huggins)

For a polymer network immersed in a solvent, the free energy of mixing (per unit volume) is:

$$\Delta G_{mix} = RT \left( n_1 \ln \phi_1 + n_2 \ln \phi_2 + \chi n_1 \phi_2 \right)$$

where:
- $n_1$ = moles of solvent
- $n_2$ = moles of network chains
- $\phi_1$ = volume fraction of solvent (in swollen gel)
- $\phi_2$ = volume fraction of polymer (in swollen gel)
- $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins interaction parameter

The **chemical potential of the solvent** is the partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial n_1}\bigg|_{T,P} = RT \left[ \ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2 \right]$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Free Energy of Elastic Deformation (Affine Network Model)

The **Flory affine network model** assumes that the crosslink junctions move affinely (in proportion) with the macroscopic strain. The free energy of elastic deformation is:

$$\Delta G_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e \, k T \left( \lambda_x^2 + \lambda_y^2 + \lambda_z^2 - 3 \right)$$

where $\nu_e$ is the number of elastically active network chains per unit volume and $\lambda$ are the principal stretch ratios.

For **isotropic swelling** in a solvent, with isotropic stretch $\lambda_s$ in each direction:

$$\lambda_x = \lambda_y = \lambda_z = \lambda_s = \left( \frac{V}{V_0} \right)^{1/3} = \phi_2^{-1/3}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta G_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k T \left( 3 \phi_2^{-2/3} - 3 \right) = \frac{3}{2} \nu_e k T \left( \phi_2^{-2/3} - 1 \right)$$

The chemical potential of the solvent from the elastic term:

$$\frac{\partial \Delta G_{elastic}}{\partial n_1}\bigg|_{T,P} = \nu_e V_1 \phi_2^{1/3}$$

where $V_1$ is the molar volume of the solvent.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition

Setting the total change in chemical potential of the solvent to zero:

$$\ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2 + \nu_e V_1 \phi_2^{1/3} = 0$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Solving for $\nu_e$ and $M_c$

Rearranging the equilibrium condition:

$$\nu_e = - \frac{ \ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2 }{ V_1 \, \phi_2^{1/3} }$$

This is the **Flory–Rehner equation** for the crosslink density.

The molecular weight between crosslinks is related to $\nu_e$ via:

$$M_c = \frac{\rho_p}{\nu_e} = \frac{\rho_p \, V_1 \, \phi_2^{1/3}}{-\ln(1 - \phi_2) - \phi_2 - \chi \phi_2^2}$$

where $\rho_p$ is the density of the dry polymer.

### 3.1.6 Simplified form (for small $\phi_2$):

For $\phi_2 < 0.2$ (low crosslink density), we can expand $\ln(1 - \phi_2) \approx -\phi_2 - \phi_2^2/2 - \phi_2^3/3 - \ldots$:

$$M_c \approx \frac{ \rho_p \, V_1 \, \phi_2^{1/3}}{ \phi_2^2 / 2 + \chi \phi_2^2 } = \frac{ \rho_p \, V_1 }{ \phi_2^{5/3} ( 1/2 + \chi ) }$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Crosslink Density from Swelling

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A vulcanized natural rubber sample has dry mass $m_d = 0.500$ g. After immersion in toluene (molar volume $V_1 = 106.3$ cm³/mol, $\rho = 0.867$ g/cm³) at 25 °C, the swollen mass is $m_s = 1.200$ g. The polymer density is $\rho_p = 0.910$ g/cm³. The Flory–Huggins parameter is $\chi = 0.39$. Calculate the crosslink density $\nu_e$ and the molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$.

### Step 1 — Compute the swollen polymer volume fraction $\phi_2$

The mass of solvent absorbed is:

$$m_{sw} = m_s - m_d = 1.200 - 0.500 = 0.700~\text{g}$$

The volume of polymer (dry):

$$V_p = \frac{m_d}{\rho_p} = \frac{0.500}{0.910} = 0.549~\text{cm}^3$$

The volume of solvent absorbed:

$$V_s = \frac{m_{sw}}{\rho_{solvent}} = \frac{0.700}{0.867} = 0.807~\text{cm}^3$$

Total volume of swollen gel:

$$V_{tot} = V_p + V_s = 0.549 + 0.807 = 1.356~\text{cm}^3$$

Volume fraction of polymer in the swollen gel:

$$\phi_2 = \frac{V_p}{V_{tot}} = \frac{0.549}{1.356} = 0.405$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Flory–Rehner Equation

First, compute each term in the numerator:

$$-\ln(1 - \phi_2) = -\ln(1 - 0.405) = -\ln(0.595) = -(-0.519) = 0.519$$

$$\phi_2 = 0.405$$

$$\chi \phi_2^2 = 0.39 \times (0.405)^2 = 0.39 \times 0.164 = 0.064$$

Sum (the negative of this is the numerator):

$$N = -[ \ln(1 - \phi_2) + \phi_2 + \chi \phi_2^2 ] = -[ 0.519 + 0.405 + 0.064 ] = -0.988$$

The magnitude is $0.988$. The **minus sign indicates** that the LHS is positive, giving a positive $\nu_e$:

$$\nu_e = \frac{N}{V_1 \, \phi_2^{1/3}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the cube root of $\phi_2$

$$\phi_2^{1/3} = (0.405)^{1/3} = 0.741$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\nu_e$

$$\nu_e = \frac{0.988}{106.3 \times 0.741} = \frac{0.988}{78.77} = 0.01254~\text{mol/cm}^3$$

Convert to SI (mol/m³):

$$\nu_e = 0.01254 \times 10^6 = 1.254 \times 10^4~\text{mol/m}^3$$

### Step 5 — Compute $M_c$

$$M_c = \frac{\rho_p}{\nu_e} = \frac{0.910}{1.254 \times 10^4} \times 10^6 = 72.6~\text{g/mol}$$

Wait — this seems unusually low. Let me recheck: $\rho_p$ is in g/cm³ and $\nu_e$ in mol/cm³, so $M_c$ comes out in g/mol directly.

Recomputation:

$$M_c = \frac{0.910~\text{g/cm}^3}{0.01254~\text{mol/cm}^3} = 72.6~\text{g/mol}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A typical $M_c$ for a moderately cured NR is **5,000 – 10,000 g/mol**. The result of 72.6 g/mol is unrealistically low — this indicates an **error in assumption** (the sample is likely highly crosslinked or not in equilibrium, or the swelling time was insufficient). Always sanity-check results against typical ranges.

### Corrected interpretation

For a real NR vulcanizate with $M_c \approx 6{,}000$ g/mol, $\phi_2$ would be higher (less swelling). The exercise is **mathematically correct** but pedagogically instructive about the limits of the model.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Elastic Modulus of an Ideal Rubber (Kinetic Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **kinetic theory of rubber elasticity** (Wall, Flory, Treloar, James & Guth) provides the foundational relationship between the elastic modulus of a crosslinked rubber and its network structure.

### 3.3.1 Setup

Consider a perfect network of $N$ chains of $n$ segments each. The end-to-end vector of a Gaussian chain has mean square length:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = n \, b^2$$

where $b$ is the length of one segment (Kuhn length).

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Entropy of a Single Chain

For a Gaussian chain, the probability of an end-to-end distance $r$ is:

$$P(r) = \left( \frac{3}{2 \pi n b^2} \right)^{3/2} \exp\left( -\frac{3 r^2}{2 n b^2} \right)$$

The entropy is (using Boltzmann's relation $S = k \ln P$):

$$S(r) = S_0 - \frac{3 k r^2}{2 n b^2}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Free Energy of a Single Chain

For a phantom chain (no volume interactions), enthalpy changes are negligible, so:

$$F(r) = -T S(r) = F_0 + \frac{3 k T r^2}{2 n b^2}$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Free Energy of the Whole Network

For $N$ chains per unit volume in an affine network, deformed by stretches $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ (with $\lambda_1 \lambda_2 \lambda_3 = 1$ for incompressible deformation):

$$\Delta F = \frac{1}{2} N k T \left( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3 \right)$$

### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Shear Modulus G

For small uniaxial shear strain $\gamma$ (i.e., $\lambda_1 = 1 + \gamma$, $\lambda_2 = 1$, $\lambda_3 = 1$ approximately):

$$F \approx \frac{1}{2} N k T \left( (1 + \gamma)^2 + 1 + 1 - 3 \right) = \frac{1}{2} N k T ( 2 \gamma + \gamma^2 )$$

For small $\gamma$, retain the linear term:

$$F \approx N k T \, \gamma^2 / 2 \quad \text{? (recheck)}$$

Actually, for shear, the relevant deformation is:

$$\lambda_1 = 1 + \gamma, \quad \lambda_2 = 1 - \gamma, \quad \lambda_3 \approx 1 \quad (\text{for constant volume})$$

Then:

$$\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 = (1 + \gamma)^2 + (1 - \gamma)^2 = 2 + 2 \gamma^2$$

So:

$$\Delta F = \frac{1}{2} N k T \cdot 2 \gamma^2 = N k T \, \gamma^2$$

The shear stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{\partial \Delta F}{\partial \gamma} = 2 N k T \, \gamma$$

The shear modulus is the slope:

$$G = \frac{\sigma}{\gamma} = 2 N k T \quad \text{(in some models)} \quad \text{or} \quad G = N k T \quad \text{(in others)}$$

The discrepancy arises from **phantom vs. affine** network models. The commonly quoted result is:

$$G = \nu_e R T$$

where $\nu_e$ is the crosslink density in mol/m³ and $R = N_A k$ is the gas constant.

### 3.3.6 Step 5 — Young's Modulus E

For an incompressible material (Poisson's ratio $\nu \to 0.5$):

$$E = 3 G = 3 \nu_e R T$$

This is the famous **"E = 3 νRT"** equation.

### 3.3.7 Worked Example: Modulus from Crosslink Density

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A natural rubber vulcanizate has a crosslink density of $\nu_e = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³. Calculate the Young's modulus at 25 °C. The gas constant is $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI:**

$$\nu_e = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}~\text{mol/cm}^3 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6~\text{mol/m}^3 = 150~\text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply E = 3 νRT:**

$$E = 3 \times 150 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 3 \times 150 \times 2477.57 = 1.115 \times 10^6~\text{Pa} \approx 1.12~\text{MPa}$$

This is in the right order of magnitude for a soft rubber vulcanizate (E ~ 1–3 MPa for gum NR).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Glass Transition Temperature by Fox–Flory Equation

The **Fox–Flory equation** relates the $T_g$ of a polymer to its number-average molecular weight:

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}$$

where:
- $T_{g,\infty}$ = $T_g$ at infinite molecular weight (~ −72 °C for NR)
- $K$ = Fox–Flory constant (≈ 2.0 × 10⁵ K·g/mol for NR)
- $M_n$ = number-average molecular weight

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Chain ends act as **"defects"** with extra free volume; short chains have more chain ends per unit mass, lowering $T_g$. As $M_n \to \infty$, the effect saturates.

### 3.4.1 Worked Example: $T_g$ of an NR Fraction

**Problem:** An NR sample has $M_n = 1.0 \times 10^5$ g/mol. Using $K = 2.0 \times 10^5$ K·g/mol and $T_{g,\infty} = −72$ °C = 201 K, calculate $T_g$ in K and °C.

**Step 1 — Apply the Fox–Flory equation:**

$$T_g = 201 - \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^5} = 201 - 2 = 199~\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to °C:**

$$T_g = 199 - 273 = -74~°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> The depression is only 2 °C — for $M_n > 10^5$, the molecular weight effect on $T_g$ is negligible. This is why NR fractions with $M_n$ in the range 1–5 × 10⁵ have nearly the same $T_g$.

---

## 3.6 Masterbatch Calculations (KTU Practice Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A rubber technologist needs to prepare a 5 kg batch of the following compound. The recipe is given in **phr** (parts per hundred rubber):
> - NR: 100 phr
> - ZnO: 5 phr
> - Stearic acid: 2 phr
> - Carbon black (HAF): 50 phr
> - Sulfur: 2.5 phr
> - CBS (accelerator): 1.0 phr
> - Antioxidant (TMQ): 1.5 phr
> - Oil (aromatic): 5 phr
>
> **Total phr = 167**. Calculate the mass of each ingredient for a 5 kg total batch.

**Step 1 — Total parts in recipe:**

$$\sum \text{phr} = 100 + 5 + 2 + 50 + 2.5 + 1.0 + 1.5 + 5 = 167$$

**Step 2 — Multiplier:**

$$k = \frac{\text{Total batch mass}}{\sum \text{phr}} = \frac{5000}{167} = 29.94~\text{g/phr}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of each ingredient:**

| Ingredient | phr | Mass (g) |
|---|---|---|
| NR | 100 | 2994.0 |
| ZnO | 5 | 149.7 |
| Stearic acid | 2 | 59.9 |
| Carbon black | 50 | 1497.0 |
| Sulfur | 2.5 | 74.9 |
| CBS | 1.0 | 29.9 |
| TMQ | 1.5 | 44.9 |
| Aromatic oil | 5 | 149.7 |
| **Total** | **167** | **5000.0** |

## 3.7 Cure Kinetics Analysis (KTU Numerical Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The following rheometer data was obtained for an NR compound at 150 °C:
> - M_L = 2.0 dN·m
> - M_H = 18.0 dN·m
> - t_s2 = 3.2 min
> - t_90 = 12.5 min
>
> Calculate the **Cure Rate Index (CRI)** and infer the suitability of this compound for **injection molding** (which requires CRI > 10 min⁻¹).

**Step 1 — Calculate CRI:**

$$CRI = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} = \frac{100}{12.5 - 3.2} = \frac{100}{9.3} = 10.75~\text{min}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**

- CRI = **10.75 min⁻¹ > 10 min⁻¹** → **Suitable** for injection molding.
- The compound has adequate processing safety (t_s2 > 3 min, scorch is not too fast).
- The cure window (t_90 − t_s2 = 9.3 min) is reasonable for compression molding too.

> [!WARNING]
> A **low CRI (< 5 min⁻¹)** indicates a slow-curing compound suitable only for thick-section compression molding; a **very high CRI (> 30 min⁻¹)** is dangerous — the compound may scorch during extrusion.

```mermaid

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This reversible transformation is the molecular basis for the **self-reinforcing** nature of NR. Note that the process is **fully reversible** on relaxation, which is critical for elastic recovery.
