# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
Define **Natural Rubber**. Write the chemical structure of its repeating unit and explain why it is called **cis-1,4-polyisoprene**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Natural rubber is a high-molecular-weight elastomer obtained from the latex of *Hevea brasiliensis*, chemically identified as **cis-1,4-polyisoprene**. The repeating unit, formed by 1,4-addition polymerization of isoprene, is:

$$-\left[ \text{CH}_2 - \text{C}(\text{CH}_3) = \text{CH} - \text{CH}_2 \right]-_n$$

The "1,4" refers to the carbon atoms of the original isoprene that are linked in the polymer chain. The "cis" designation means the two main-chain segments ($\text{-CH}_2$ groups) lie on the **same side** of the C=C double bond, giving the chain a regular, kink-free structure that enables strain-induced crystallization.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module Test]**
What is **vulcanization**? Name its discoverer and state two effects of vulcanization on natural rubber.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Vulcanization is the process of forming three-dimensional crosslinks between polymer chains of rubber, typically using **sulfur** at elevated temperature in the presence of accelerators and activators. The process was discovered accidentally by **Charles Goodyear in 1839**. Two effects of vulcanization:

1. **Improved mechanical strength** — tensile strength, modulus, and tear strength increase dramatically.
2. **Reduced plasticity and tackiness** — the raw rubber no longer flows or sticks to itself.
3. (Bonus) Enhanced elasticity over a wider temperature range and better aging resistance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 marks total)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester]**
**(a)** Discuss the **occurrence, isolation, and processing of natural rubber latex** from *Hevea brasiliensis* into commercially usable forms. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **chemistry of sulfur vulcanization** of natural rubber. Discuss the role of **activators and accelerators** in the process. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Occurrence, Isolation, and Processing (7 marks):**

**Occurrence (1 mark):** Natural rubber occurs as a milky colloidal fluid called **latex**, contained in specialized cells called **laticifers** in the bark of the *Hevea brasiliensis* tree. The latex is a colloidal dispersion of polyisoprene particles (200 nm–3 μm) in an aqueous serum.

**Tapping (2 marks):** Tapping is the controlled wounding of the bark. A spiral or V-shaped cut is made with a tapping knife at an angle, severing the laticifers. The exuded latex is collected in a small cup attached to the tree. Tapping is typically done every 2–3 days, alternating panels to allow regeneration. Yield: ~2.5 kg dry rubber/year/tree.

**Preservation (1 mark):** Latex is perishable due to bacterial and enzymatic action. **Ammonia (0.5–1.0%)** is added as a preservative and to prevent premature coagulation. Tetramethylthiuram disulfide (TMTD) may also be added as a biostat.

**Concentration (1 mark):** Field latex (30–40% DRC) is concentrated to ~60% DRC by **centrifugation** (preferred) or **creaming** (using ammonium alginate), or **evaporation** (to 70% DRC for specialty grades). The concentrated latex is the form used for dipped products (gloves, balloons, condoms).

**Coagulation and Drying (1 mark):** For solid rubber, the latex is coagulated by adding **formic acid or acetic acid** to bring the pH to ~4.5–5.0. The coagulum is passed through rollers, washed, and dried. **Ribbed Smoked Sheets (RSS)** are made by air-drying and smoking; **Pale Crepe** is made by passing coagulum through multiple roller sets without smoking.

**Grading (1 mark):** Solid rubber is graded either **visually** (RSS 1–5) or **technically** (SMR, SIR, TSR — based on dirt content, ash, nitrogen, volatile matter, plasticity retention index, Mooney viscosity).

**(b) Sulfur Vulcanization Chemistry (7 marks):**

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NR Chain Allylic H] --> B[Accelerator MBT]
    B --> C[Activator ZnO Stearic Acid]
    C --> D[Active Sulfurating Complex Zn-MBT-Sx]
    D --> E[Abstract Allylic H]
    E --> F[Polyisoprenyl Sulfenyl Radical]
    F --> G[Combine with Another Radical]
    G --> H[Polysulfide Crosslink R-Sx-R]
    H --> I[Maturation Shortening]
    I --> J[Di or Monosulfide Crosslink]
```

**Chemistry (4 marks):**
The classical sulfur vulcanization proceeds in three stages:
1. **Initiation:** The accelerator (e.g., 2-mercaptobenzothiazole, MBT) reacts with sulfur to form an active accelerator polysulfide. The activator system (ZnO + stearic acid) converts this to a **zinc-accelerator complex**, which is the true sulfurating species.
2. **Crosslink formation:** The complex abstracts the **allylic hydrogen** from the polyisoprene chain (weakened by the adjacent C=C). A polyisoprenyl sulfenyl radical is generated, which attacks another chain's allylic site to form a **polysulfide crosslink (R–Sₓ–R)**, with x = 4–8 initially.
3. **Maturation (post-cure):** With continued heating, the polysulfide crosslinks **shorten** to disulfides and monosulfides, improving thermal and oxidative stability.

**Role of Activators (1 mark):** ZnO and stearic acid form zinc stearate, which solubilizes the accelerator and forms the active zinc-accelerator complex. They also neutralize any acidic byproducts and improve the rate of vulcanization.

**Role of Accelerators (1 mark):** Accelerators (e.g., MBT, CBS, TMTD) reduce the vulcanization time from hours (with sulfur alone) to **minutes**, and lower the required sulfur level. They also permit vulcanization at lower temperatures. Different accelerator classes (thiazoles, sulfenamides, thiurams) give different cure rates and scorch safety.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 marks total)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **strain-induced crystallization (SIC)** in natural rubber. How does it contribute to the superior mechanical properties of NR compared to other synthetic elastomers? (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **compounding of natural rubber** with various ingredients, with specific emphasis on the role of each ingredient in the final vulcanizate properties. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Strain-Induced Crystallization (SIC) (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Strain-induced crystallization is the phenomenon in which a normally amorphous elastomer **partially crystallizes** when subjected to mechanical deformation, with the crystals disappearing upon release of the strain.

**Mechanism (3 marks):** The cis-1,4 configuration of natural polyisoprene gives a highly **stereoregular** chain backbone with no bulky side groups. At rest, the chains are in a random coil. When stretched, the chains align and pack into an **ordered crystalline lattice** (orthorhombic, with chain axis along the stretch direction). Critical elongation for SIC onset in NR is around **200–250%**. The crystallites are very small (10–50 nm) and act as physical crosslinks and nano-fillers.

**Contribution to properties (3 marks):**
1. **Tensile strength:** SIC dramatically increases tensile strength — a gum NR vulcanizate (no carbon black) can reach 20–30 MPa, while gum SBR is only 2–3 MPa. Crystallites bear load and prevent chain slippage.
2. **Tear strength:** Crystallites at the crack tip resist crack propagation, giving NR 5–10× the tear strength of SBR.
3. **Fatigue resistance:** Repeated deformation cycles cause repeated crystallization–melting, dissipating energy without permanent damage.
4. **Self-reinforcement:** Eliminates the need for high filler loadings in some applications, keeping the rubber soft and resilient.

**Comparison with synthetics (1 mark):** Synthetic polyisoprene (IR) achieves only ~96% cis content and shows **reduced** SIC. Butadiene rubber (BR) and SBR lack stereoregular cis-1,4 structures and do not exhibit SIC at practical elongations. This is why NR is **uniquely suited for heavy-duty tires and dynamic applications.**

**(b) Compounding of Natural Rubber (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Compounding is the art and science of mixing various ingredients with the raw polymer to obtain a homogeneous mass with the desired processing, mechanical, and aging properties.

**Ingredients and their roles (6 marks — 1/2 mark each, total 6):**

| Ingredient | Role in Final Vulcanizate |
|---|---|
| **Polymer (NR)** | Provides elasticity, strength, fatigue resistance. |
| **Activator (ZnO + stearic acid)** | Forms Zn-stearate; activates accelerator; improves crosslink density. |
| **Accelerator (MBT, CBS, TMTD)** | Speeds vulcanization; reduces sulfur requirement; controls scorch time. |
| **Curing agent (Sulfur)** | Forms crosslinks between chains; controls crosslink density and type. |
| **Reinforcing filler (Carbon black, silica)** | Increases modulus, tensile strength, tear strength, abrasion resistance. |
| **Plasticizer (Aromatic/naphthenic oil)** | Reduces viscosity, improves filler dispersion, lowers hardness, improves low-T flexibility. |
| **Antioxidant (TMQ, 6PPD, BHT)** | Protects against thermal/oxidative aging; extends service life. |
| **Antiozonant (Wax, 6PPD)** | Migrates to surface; protects against ozone cracking. |
| **Peptizer (PCTP)** | Aids mastication by chain scission; reduces mix time and energy. |
| **Pigment (TiO₂, carbon black)** | Imparts color; carbon black also acts as UV absorber. |
| **Blowing agent (ADC)** | Decomposes during cure to produce N₂/CO₂; makes cellular (sponge) rubber. |
| **Retarder (PVI)** | Prevents scorch during processing; extends scorch time. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):** Students often list ingredients without specifying **which property each ingredient modifies**. Always state **one property per ingredient** to earn full marks. Also, do NOT confuse **antioxidant (against oxygen)** with **antiozonant (against ozone)** — they have different mechanisms and chemistries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Loss Areas in Natural Rubber Questions:**
> 1. **Drawing the wrong structure for isoprene.** Isoprene is 2-methyl-1,3-butadiene: $\text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2$. The methyl is on C2, not C3.
> 2. **Confusing NR with gutta-percha.** NR = cis-1,4-PIP (soft, elastic). Gutta-percha = trans-1,4-PIP (hard, rigid, crystalline).
> 3. **Forgetting to state the role of activator.** ZnO + stearic acid do NOT vulcanize rubber; they only **activate the accelerator**. This is a frequently missed 1-mark point.
> 4. **Stating "sulfur" alone as the curing agent in modern compounds.** Modern NR compounds use 0.3–3.5 phr sulfur + 0.5–6.0 phr accelerator; both are required.
> 5. **Skipping the units in Flory–Rehner problems.** Always state the units of $\nu_e$ (mol/m³ or mol/cm³) explicitly.
> 6. **Confusing "phr" with "percentage".** phr means "parts per hundred of rubber" — the polymer is always 100 phr.
> 7. **Stating "SBR is stronger than NR"** — incorrect. NR has higher gum tensile strength due to SIC; SBR requires heavy carbon black loading to reach comparable strength.
> 8. **Writing "TMTD is a vulcanizing agent"** — TMTD is an **ultra-accelerator**, not a vulcanizing agent. The vulcanizing agent is sulfur.
> 9. **Saying "vulcanization was discovered by Thomas Hancock"** — Incorrect. Hancock invented the masticator. Goodyear discovered vulcanization.
> 10. **Forgetting to mention the type of crosslink in EV vs. CV systems.** EV → mostly monosulfide; CV → polysulfide.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### NATURAL RUBBER — QUICK RECAP (Module 1)

- **Definition:** *Hevea brasiliensis* latex → **cis-1,4-polyisoprene** (> 99% cis).
- **Monomer:** Isoprene, CH₂=C(CH₃)−CH=CH₂.
- **Discovery of vulcanization:** Charles Goodyear, 1839.
- **Seeds smuggled to Asia:** Sir Henry Wickham, 1876.
- **Latex composition:** ~60% water, ~35% rubber, ~5% non-rubbers.
- **Tg:** −72 °C (lowest among major commercial elastomers except silicone).
- **Tm:** ~ 30–40 °C.
- **Mₙ:** 2–3 × 10⁵ g/mol; **Mₖ:** 1–1.5 × 10⁶ g/mol; **PDI:** 3–5.
- **Strain-induced crystallization (SIC):** Self-reinforcement at > 200% elongation.
- **Cure temperature:** 140–160 °C.
- **Cure agent:** Sulfur (1.5–3.5 phr CV, 0.3–0.5 phr EV).
- **Activator:** ZnO (5 phr) + stearic acid (2 phr).
- **Common accelerators:** MBT, CBS, TMTD, MBTS, DPG.
- **Antioxidant examples:** TMQ (2,2,4-trimethyl-1,2-dihydroquinoline), 6PPD.
- **Reinforcing filler:** HAF carbon black (N330), ISAF (N220), silica.
- **Oil resistance:** Poor (non-polar rubber).
- **Ozone resistance:** Poor (requires antiozonant + wax).
- **Tear strength:** Among the highest of all elastomers.
- **Largest application:** Tires (~70% of global NR consumption).
- **Limitations:** Allergenic proteins, batch variability, oil sensitivity, aging.
- **Comparison with gutta-percha:** NR = cis (elastic) vs. gutta-percha = trans (rigid).
- **E = 3 νRT** (kinetic theory of rubber elasticity).
- **Flory–Rehner equation** (crosslink density from swelling).
- **Fox–Flory equation** (Tg vs. Mₙ).
- **Masticator (1820, Hancock)** — distinct from vulcanization discovery.
- **Synthetic polyisoprene (IR):** ~96% cis, inferior to NR.
- **Latex allergy:** Type I hypersensitivity to residual proteins.
- **Deproteinized natural rubber (DPNR):** Low-protein variant for medical use.
- **epoxidized natural rubber (ENR):** Improved oil/air permeability resistance.
- **Depolymerized NR (DNR):** Lower molecular weight for specialty adhesives.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicone Rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Silicone Rubber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Silicone Rubber

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicone Rubber (Silicone Elastomer, MQ/VMQ/FVMQ/PMQ/FMQ systems):**
> Silicone rubber is a synthetic, semi-inorganic, thermosetting **elastomer** whose polymer backbone is composed of alternating **silicon (Si)** and **oxygen (O)** atoms (the **siloxane linkage, –Si–O–Si–O–**), with organic side groups (most commonly **methyl, –CH₃**; but also vinyl, phenyl, fluoroalkyl, or trifluoropropyl) attached to the silicon atoms. It is produced by the hydrolysis and polycondensation of chlorosilanes (e.g., dimethyldichlorosilane, methyltrichlorosilane) followed by controlled crosslinking (vulcanization) of the resulting linear polysiloxane chains.

The general structural repeat unit is:

$$[R_{2}SiO]_{n}$$

where **R** is an organic substituent (typically **–CH₃**). The most industrially significant member is **Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)**, with repeat unit **[(CH₃)₂SiO]ₙ**.

## 1.2 Nomenclature & Classification Symbols

| Symbol | Side Group | Polymer Type |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **MQ** | Methyl (M) on Quaternary Si | Dimethyl silicone |
| **VMQ** | Methyl + Vinyl | Most common general purpose |
| **PMQ** | Methyl + Phenyl | Low temperature flexibility |
| **FVMQ** | Fluorovinyl + Methyl | Solvent/fuel resistance |
| **FE** | Fluoro + Ethyl substituent | Fluorosilicone |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Flexible Quartz Skeleton":**
> Imagine **window glass (quartz, SiO₂)** — it is incredibly strong and heat resistant, but if you hit it, it shatters. Now imagine taking that same inorganic **Si–O–Si backbone** but hanging soft, organic **methyl (-CH₃) "fringes"** like a hedgehog's quills along the silicon atoms. The inorganic skeleton gives it **heat resistance and weather-ability** (because it is essentially "pre-oxidized"), while the organic methyl groups make it **soft, flexible, and water-repellent** (hydrophobic). That hybrid "quartz hedgehog" structure is exactly what silicone rubber is. Unlike organic rubbers (natural rubber, EPDM) which are entirely carbon-based and get destroyed by ozone/UV/heat, silicone rubber keeps working from **-60 °C to +230 °C** — like having a rubber band that does not mind being left in an oven or a freezer.

## 1.4 Historical Context for KTU

- **1940s**: First commercial silicone rubber developed at **Corning Glass Works** and **Dow Chemical** during WWII for military high-temperature applications.
- **Fredrick Kipping** (UK) coined the term "silicone" in 1901.
- Today: global market > **USD 8 billion** (industrial-grade alone), growing at ~6% CAGR.
- Key Indian players: **Momentive Performance Materials, Dow India, Wacker Metroark, Elkem, NuSil, Shin-Etsu**.

## 1.5 Why Silicone Rubber is a "Specialty" Elastomer

| Property | Natural Rubber | Silicone Rubber |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Backbone | C–C (organic) | **Si–O (inorganic)** |
| Max service temp | ~80 °C | **230 °C (continuous)** |
| Min service temp | –40 °C | **–60 °C to –100 °C** |
| UV/Ozone resistance | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Biocompatibility | Limited | **USP Class VI / FDA approved** |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | **5× – 10×** |

The combination of an **inorganic backbone with organic side groups** is what makes silicone rubber an engineering "problem-solver" rubber.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Siloxane Backbone — Why Si–O is Special

### 2.1.1 Bonding Characteristics

The **Si–O bond** is one of the strongest single bonds in polymer chemistry. Its high bond energy (≈ **452 kJ/mol**) and partial ionic character (~50%) give the siloxane linkage exceptional thermal and oxidative stability.

$$E_{Si-O} \approx 452 \text{ kJ/mol} \gg E_{C-C} \approx 348 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

| Property | Si–O Bond | C–C Bond (comparison) |
|---------|-----------|----------------------|
| Bond energy | **452 kJ/mol** | 348 kJ/mol |
| Bond length | **1.64 Å** | 1.54 Å |
| Bond angle | **~143°** (Si–O–Si) | 109.5° (C–C–C) |
| Rotation barrier | **low** (flexible) | low |
| Polarity | highly polar (Si δ⁺, O δ⁻) | essentially non-polar |

> [!NOTE]
> The wide **Si–O–Si bond angle (~143°)** means the siloxane backbone behaves like a "loose spring" — it can coil/uncoil easily. This explains the very **low glass transition temperature (T₉ ≈ –123 °C for PDMS)** and the extraordinary flexibility at low temperatures.

### 2.1.2 Reasons for High Flexibility

1. **Linear (no branching on backbone)**: each Si has only 2 O atoms → minimal steric hindrance.
2. **Large Si–O–Si angle**: the chain is "open" and rotatable.
3. **Partial ionic character**: low rotation barrier around Si–O.
4. **Methyl groups are small and symmetric**: minimal side-group interference.

## 2.2 Synthesis Route — The Rochow–Müller Direct Process to Silicone Rubber

The industrial synthesis proceeds in three broad stages:

### Step 1 — Direct Synthesis of Chlorosilanes (Rochow Process)

$$Si_{(s)} + 2 CH_{3}Cl_{(g)} \xrightarrow{Cu \text{ catalyst}, 250-300°C} (CH_3)_2SiCl_2 + \text{other methylchlorosilanes}$$

The principal product is **dimethyldichlorosilane (CH₃)₂SiCl₂**, plus by-products: (CH₃)₃SiCl (trimethyl), CH₃SiCl₃ (methyltrichloro), SiCl₄, etc.

### Step 2 — Hydrolysis of Chlorosilanes

$$n (CH_3)_2SiCl_2 + 2n H_2O \longrightarrow [(CH_3)_2Si(OH)]_n \text{ intermediates} + 2n HCl$$

This produces a mixture of **cyclic oligomers (D₃, D₄, D₅)** plus linear silanol-terminated oligomers. **D₄ (octamethylcyclotetrasiloxane)** is the preferred feed for ring-opening polymerization.

### Step 3 — Ring-Opening Polymerization (ROP) of D₄

$$n [(CH_3)_2SiO]_4 \xrightarrow{\text{acid or base catalyst, heat}} [(CH_3)_2SiO]_{4n}$$

Common catalysts: **KOH, n-BuLi, H₂SO₄, or strong acids (triflic acid)**. Molecular weight is controlled by the presence of a **monofunctional chain-stopper** such as hexamethyldisiloxane (MM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the ROP route?**
> Direct hydrolysis gives a broad MW distribution. ROP of purified D₄ with controlled catalyst and a chain-stopper allows **narrow polydispersity (Ð ≈ 1.05–1.2)** and defined end-groups, essential for medical-grade silicone.

## 2.3 Vulcanization (Curing) Mechanisms

Linear polysiloxane chains must be crosslinked to form the elastic network. Four principal mechanisms exist:

### 2.3.1 Peroxide Cure (used in HTV — High Temperature Vulcanizing)

$$R-Si-CH_3 + \text{R-O-O-R'} \xrightarrow{\Delta} R-Si-CH_2^\bullet + R'OH$$
$$\text{Two } -CH_2^\bullet \text{ radicals} \longrightarrow -CH_2-CH_2- \text{(crosslink)}$$

Common peroxides: **2,4-dichlorobenzoyl peroxide (DCBP), dicumyl peroxide (DCP), 2,5-dimethyl-2,5-di(t-butylperoxy)hexane (DBPH — "Varox")**.

### 2.3.2 Condensation Cure (RTV-1, Room Temperature)

$$-Si-OH + HO-Si- \xrightarrow{Sn \text{ or Ti catalyst}} -Si-O-Si- + H_2O$$

Used in **one-part RTV sealants** (acetic acid, oxime, or alcohol evolution).

### 2.3.3 Addition Cure (RTV-2 / LSR — Liquid Silicone Rubber)

$$-Si-Vinyl + H-Si- \xrightarrow{Pt catalyst (Karstedt)} -Si-CH_2-CH_2-Si-$$

This is the **hydrosilylation** reaction. By-product-free, no shrinkage → preferred for medical, optical, and food-contact applications.

### 2.3.4 Moisture-Cure (RTV-1 acetoxy)

$$-Si-OH + CH_3COOSi- \longrightarrow -Si-O-Si- + CH_3COOH$$

The familiar **vinegar smell** of household silicone sealant is acetic acid evolution.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Value / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Si–O bond energy, $E_{Si-O}$ | **≈ 452 kJ/mol** | responsible for thermal stability |
| 2 | Si–O bond length | **1.64 Å** | compared to 1.54 Å for C–C |
| 3 | Si–O–Si bond angle | **≈ 143°** | gives chain flexibility |
| 4 | T₉ of PDMS | **–123 °C** | lowest among common rubbers |
| 5 | Service temperature range | **–60 °C to +230 °C** | continuous (HTV) |
| 6 | Dielectric strength | **20–30 kV/mm** | electrical insulation |
| 7 | Dielectric constant (1 kHz) | **2.6 – 3.0** | low for HV insulation |
| 8 | Specific gravity | **1.10 – 1.50** | (compound-dependent) |
| 9 | Tensile strength (unfilled gum) | **≈ 0.3 – 0.7 MPa** | very low — must reinforce |
| 10 | Tensile strength (fumed silica filled) | **5 – 12 MPa** | typical HTV |
| 11 | Elongation at break | **100 – 700 %** | |
| 12 | Hardness, Shore A | **20 – 80** | |
| 13 | Compression set (24 h, 150 °C) | **5 – 25 %** | better than most organics |
| 14 | Crosslink density, $\nu$ | $\nu = \rho / M_c$ | mol/cm³ |
| 15 | Mooney–Rivlin parameter, $C_1$ | $C_1 = \nu RT$ | Pa |
| 16 | Ideal rubber elastic modulus, $E$ | $E = 3\nu RT$ | small-strain Young's modulus |
| 17 | Reinforcement factor (silica) | **10× – 30×** | tensile strength gain |
| 18 | Filler loading range | **20 – 60 phr** | phr = parts per hundred rubber |
| 19 | Thermal conductivity | **0.15 – 0.25 W/(m·K)** | insulating |
| 20 | Refractive index (PDMS) | **1.40 – 1.41** | used in optical devices |

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark KTU answers, ALWAYS quote at least 3 of these numerical values** — examiners reward quantified answers over hand-waving.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Silicone Rubber

1. **Aerospace**: Engine gaskets, thermal blankets, vibration dampers (sub-zero to +300 °C).
2. **Medical**: Catheters, tubing, breast implants, drug-eluting devices (USP Class VI, ISO 10993).
3. **Electronics**: Keypads, HV insulator boots, potting compounds, conformal coatings.
4. **Automotive**: Turbocharger hoses, O-rings, ignition cables.
5. **Food & Cookware**: Baking molds, spatulas (FDA 21 CFR 177.2600).
6. **Construction**: Structural glazing sealants, expansion joints.
7. **Renewable Energy**: PV panel encapsulation (EVA alternative in some architectures), wind-turbine blade coatings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Degree of Polymerization in Ring-Opening Polymerization with Chain-Stopper

The ROP of D₄ in the presence of a monofunctional chain-stopper (e.g., hexamethyldisiloxane, MM) follows equilibrium polymerization kinetics. The number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{DP}_n$ of the resulting linear PDMS is set by the **molar ratio of cyclic monomer to chain-stopper**.

### Step 3.1.1 — Define the Mole Balance

Let:
- $n_D$ = initial moles of cyclic D₄
- $n_M$ = initial moles of monofunctional chain-stopper (MM)
- Each linear polymer chain will have **2 chain-stopper units** (one at each end) for a fully difunctional chain, or **1 chain-stopper unit** at one end if the other end is capped differently.

For PDMS prepared by anionic ROP with MM as the sole chain-stopper:

$$\text{Number of linear chains formed, } N_{chains} = n_M$$

(The MM provides both chain ends, hence each MM molecule consumes the chain.)

### Step 3.1.2 — Compute Moles of Repeat Units Incorporated

Let the conversion of D₄ to linear polymer be $X$ (dimensionless fraction, $0 \le X \le 1$). The moles of D₄ units incorporated into the polymer is:

$$n_{incorporated} = X \cdot n_D$$

### Step 3.1.3 — Write the Degree of Polymerization Expression

By definition of the **number-average degree of polymerization**:

$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{\text{Total moles of repeat units in polymer}}{\text{Total moles of polymer molecules}}$$

Substituting the expressions derived in 3.1.1 and 3.1.2:

$$\overline{DP}_n = \frac{X \cdot n_D}{n_M}$$

For full conversion ($X \to 1$, which is approached at high temperature in base-catalyzed ROP):

$$\overline{DP}_n \approx \frac{n_D}{n_M}$$

### Step 3.1.4 — Final Closed-Form Working Equation

$$\boxed{\overline{DP}_n = \frac{n_D}{n_M} = \frac{[D_4]}{[MM]} \text{ at full conversion}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Suppose we want $\overline{DP}_n = 500$ for an LSR-grade PDMS. We have 0.50 mol of D₄.

$$n_M = \frac{n_D}{\overline{DP}_n} = \frac{0.50}{500} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol of MM}$$

Mass of MM = $1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 162.4$ g/mol = **0.162 g** (where 162.4 g/mol = MW of hexamethyldisiloxane, (CH₃)₃Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM TIP:** For KTU 14-mark questions, **always show the numerical calculation** for a specific DP or viscosity target — it carries 3-4 easy marks.

## 3.2 Derivation — Ideal Crosslink Density from Swelling Data (Flory–Rehner Equation)

The crosslink density of a silicone rubber network is experimentally determined by equilibrium swelling in a good solvent (e.g., toluene). The **Flory–Rehner equation** (1943) is the workhorse:

### Step 3.2.1 — Set Up the Thermodynamic Condition

At equilibrium swelling, the **chemical potential of mixing** balances the **chemical potential of elastic deformation**:

$$\Delta \mu_{mix} + \Delta \mu_{elastic} = 0$$

The Flory–Rehner expression for the crosslink density $\nu$ (mol of elastically active chains per unit volume) is:

$$\nu = \frac{-\left[\ln(1-\phi_p) + \phi_p + \chi \phi_p^2\right]}{V_1 \left[\phi_p^{1/3} - \frac{2 \phi_p}{f}\right]}$$

Where:
- $\phi_p$ = volume fraction of polymer in the swollen gel
- $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins polymer–solvent interaction parameter
- $V_1$ = molar volume of the solvent (e.g., toluene = 106.3 cm³/mol)
- $f$ = functionality of the crosslink (4 for tetrafunctional peroxide cure, but typically $f \to \infty$ simplification is used → denominator reduces to $\phi_p^{1/3} - 2\phi_p/f \approx \phi_p^{1/3}$)

### Step 3.2.2 — Simplify for Tetrafunctional Network

For $f = 4$:

$$\nu = \frac{-\left[\ln(1-\phi_p) + \phi_p + \chi \phi_p^2\right]}{V_1 \left[\phi_p^{1/3} - \frac{\phi_p}{2}\right]}$$

### Step 3.2.3 — Compute the Average Molecular Weight Between Crosslinks

$$M_c = \frac{\rho_p}{\nu}$$

Where $\rho_p$ is the density of the unswollen polymer (g/cm³).

### Step 3.2.4 — Numerical Worked Example

Given: a silicone rubber sample is swollen in toluene to equilibrium, $\phi_p = 0.25$, $\chi = 0.45$, $V_1 = 106.3$ cm³/mol, $\rho_p = 1.10$ g/cm³.

Compute the numerator:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= -\left[\ln(1-0.25) + 0.25 + 0.45 \times 0.25^2\right] \\
&= -\left[\ln(0.75) + 0.25 + 0.028125\right] \\
&= -\left[(-0.28768) + 0.25 + 0.028125\right] \\
&= -\left[-0.00955\right] \\
&= 0.00955
\end{aligned}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= V_1 \left[\phi_p^{1/3} - \frac{\phi_p}{2}\right] \\
&= 106.3 \times \left[0.25^{1/3} - 0.125\right] \\
&= 106.3 \times \left[0.62996 - 0.125\right] \\
&= 106.3 \times 0.50496 \\
&= 53.67 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}
\end{aligned}$$

Crosslink density:

$$\nu = \frac{0.00955}{53.67} = 1.78 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3$$

Molecular weight between crosslinks:

$$M_c = \frac{1.10}{1.78 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6180 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A typical HTV silicone rubber has $M_c$ in the range **5,000 – 25,000 g/mol**. Our value of 6,180 g/mol indicates a **moderately tight network** (good for high tensile, lower elongation — typical of medical-grade HTV).

## 3.3 Derivation — Vulcanization Kinetics (Peroxide Cure, First-Order Approximation)

The peroxide decomposition in HTV silicone is approximately first-order:

$$\frac{d[\text{ROOR}]}{dt} = -k_d [\text{ROOR}]$$

Integration gives:

$$[\text{ROOR}](t) = [\text{ROOR}]_0 \, e^{-k_d t}$$

Where the Arrhenius rate constant is:

$$k_d = A \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

For **2,5-dimethyl-2,5-di(t-butylperoxy)hexane (DBPH, "Varox")**:
- $E_a \approx 150$ kJ/mol
- $A \approx 1.0 \times 10^{17}$ min⁻¹
- Half-life of 1 hour at **177 °C** (i.e., $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_d = 60$ min at 450 K)

Therefore the typical HTV press cure: **10 min at 175 °C + post-cure 4 h at 200 °C**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Substituent Type

| Side Group (R) on Si | Effect on T₉ | Effect on Oil Resistance | Effect on Low-Temp Flexibility | Typical Use |
|---------------------|--------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------|
| –CH₃ (methyl) | –123 °C | Poor | Excellent | General purpose |
| –CH=CH₂ (vinyl, 0.1–0.5 mol%) | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | Easier cure (VMQ) |
| –C₆H₅ (phenyl, 5–10 mol%) | rises to –73 °C | improved | **improved at very low T** (retains flexibility) | Aerospace (PMQ) |
| –CH₂CH₂CF₃ (trifluoropropyl) | –65 °C | **Excellent** (jet fuel, solvents) | reduced | Fluorosilicone (FVMQ) seal in aircraft |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Silicone vs. Other Elastomers

| Property | NR | SBR | EPDM | NBR | **Silicone (VMQ)** | **FKM (Viton)** |
|----------|-----|-----|------|-----|--------------------|----------------|
| Tensile, MPa | 25 | 20 | 18 | 22 | **8** | 14 |
| Elongation, % | 600 | 500 | 500 | 500 | **400** | 250 |
| Max service T, °C | 80 | 100 | 150 | 110 | **230** | 220 |
| Min service T, °C | –50 | –40 | –50 | –30 | **–60 to –100** | –20 |
| Oil resistance | poor | poor | poor | **excellent** | fair | **excellent** |
| Weather/UV | poor | poor | excellent | poor | **excellent** | excellent |
| Cost (relative) | 1 | 1.2 | 1.5 | 2.5 | **8** | 20 |

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions, this matrix is gold.** Drop it in Table form, then write 4-5 lines of comparison paragraph — easy 10-12 marks secured.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Silicone Rubber Production Flowsheet (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Silicon Metal Si 98 percent] --> B[Rochow Muller Direct Process Cu catalyst 300 C CH3Cl gas]
    B --> C[Methylchlorosilane Mixture M2 D3 T Cl4 etc]
    C --> D[Distillation Column Separation]
    D --> E[Dimethyldichlorosilane CH3 2SiCl2 90 percent main]
    D --> F[Methyltrichlorosilane CH3SiCl3]
    D --> G[Trimethylchlorosilane CH3 3SiCl]
    E --> H[Hydrolysis with H2O]
    H --> I[Cyclic D4 Octamethylcyclotetrasiloxane]
    H --> J[HCl byproduct recovered]
    I --> K[Anionic or Cationic Ring Opening Polymerization]
    G --> L[Chain Stopper Hexamethyldisiloxane MM]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Linear PDMS Gum Vinylmethyl Siloxane Copolymer]
    M --> N[Compounding with Fumed Silica 20 to 60 phr]
    M --> O[Compounding with Peroxide DCBP DCP Varox]
    N --> P[HTV High Temperature Vulcanizing Rubber]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Molding Compression Transfer Injection 175 C 10 min]
    Q --> R[Post Cure Oven 200 C 4 h]
    R --> S[Finished Silicone Rubber Product]
```

```mermaid

    SR --> RTV
    SR --> LSR

    SR --> HV[High Consistency Silicone HCR]

    HTV[HTV High Temperature Vulcanizing Peroxide Cure 150 to 200 C] --> HTV1[Seals Gaskets O rings]
    HTV --> HTV2[Aerospace ducting]
    HTV --> HTV3[Cable insulation]

    RTV[RTV Room Temperature Vulcanizing] --> RTV1[RTV 1 One Part Moisture Cure Acetoxy or Oxime]
    RTV --> RTV2[RTV 2 Two Part Condensation or Addition Cure]

    LSR[LSR Liquid Silicone Rubber 2 Part Pt Catalyzed Addition Cure] --> LSR1[Medical implants]
    LSR --> LSR2[Baby bottle nipples]
    LSR --> LSR3[Optical lenses]
    LSR --> LSR4[Injection molding high volume]

    FC[FVMQ Fluorosilicone Fuel and Solvent Resistance] --> FC1[Aircraft fuel system seals]
    FC --> FC2[Automotive turbo hoses]

    HV[Fluids and Gels] --> HV1[Cable filling compounds]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> P[Peroxide Cure HTV]

    P1 --> P2[CH3 abstraction]
    P2 --> P3[Bimolecular radical recombination]
    P3 --> P4[CH2 CH2 ethylene bridge crosslink]

    B --> C[Condensation Cure RTV 2]

    C1 --> C2[Si O Si plus H2O]

    B --> AD[Addition Cure LSR]
    AD --> AD1[Si Vinyl plus Si H plus Pt Karstedt]
    AD1 --> AD2[Si CH2 CH2 Si no byproduct]

    B --> M[Moisture Cure RTV 1]
    M --> M1[Si OAc plus H2O ambient]
    M1 --> M2[Si OH condensation]
    M2 --> M3[Si O Si plus CH3COOH acetic acid smell]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Textual Representation of the PDMS Repeat Unit

For the KTU drawing question (frequent in 14-mark questions), the **textual schematic** to memorize is:

```
    CH3   CH3   CH3   CH3
    |     |     |     |
--Si--O--Si--O--Si--O--Si--O--    ...repeat...
    |     |     |     |
    CH3   CH3   CH3   CH3
```

Backbone is **Si–O–Si–O–Si–O** (inorganic, grey); side groups are **CH₃** (organic, flexible). Vinyl groups (CH=CH₂) appear randomly at 0.1–0.5 mol% of the Si atoms to provide cure sites for peroxide or Pt catalysis.

For **VMQ (vinyl-methyl silicone)**: at every ~200th Si atom, one CH₃ is replaced by CH=CH₂.

For **PMQ (phenyl-methyl silicone)**: 5–10 mol% of CH₃ groups are replaced by C₆H₅ rings.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Q: Define silicone rubber. Mention any two distinctive properties that distinguish it from natural rubber.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Silicone rubber is a synthetic elastomer whose polymer backbone is composed of alternating **silicon (Si)** and **oxygen (O)** atoms (siloxane linkage, –Si–O–Si–O–) with organic side groups (commonly methyl) attached to the silicon atoms. Its general structure is $[R_2SiO]_n$.
>
> **Two distinctive properties (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Wide service temperature range:** –60 °C to +230 °C, far exceeding natural rubber's –40 °C to +80 °C. This is due to the **high Si–O bond energy (≈ 452 kJ/mol)** and the wide Si–O–Si bond angle (≈ 143°).
> 2. **Excellent UV, ozone, and weathering resistance:** Because the backbone is already "pre-oxidized" (it is an Si–O–Si inorganic skeleton), there are no C=C double bonds in the main chain to be attacked by ozone, unlike natural rubber which cracks readily.
> *(Alternatively, biocompatibility or high dielectric strength may be cited.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Q: What is the role of vinyl groups (0.1–0.5 mol%) in silicone rubber? Why is fumed silica added?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Role of vinyl groups (1.5 marks):** Vinyl (–CH=CH₂) groups provide **reactive cure sites** for peroxide crosslinking and Pt-catalyzed hydrosilylation addition cure. They form more stable primary radicals under peroxide attack than methyl groups, enabling **faster, more efficient crosslinking** at lower temperatures. They also improve **compression set** properties.
>
> **Role of fumed silica (1.5 marks):** Unfilled PDMS gum has tensile strength of only ~0.3–0.7 MPa. **Fumed silica** (particle size 5–50 nm, surface area 150–400 m²/g) is added at 20–60 phr to provide **reinforcement** by forming a silica–silicone network via hydrogen bonding between silanol groups on the silica surface and the siloxane oxygen. This raises tensile strength by **10×–30×** to 5–12 MPa.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Silicone Rubber Questions:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "silicone" with "silicon"** — silicon is the element (Si), silicone is the polymer family containing Si–O–Si linkages. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Do NOT draw or write a "Si=O" double bond** — silicone is **NOT** analogous to a ketone (C=O). The Si–O linkage is single and is part of the repeating siloxane chain.
> 3. **Do NOT skip the synthesis stages** — a "describe the preparation" question without the Rochow process, hydrolysis, and ROP is incomplete and loses ~5 marks.
> 4. **Do NOT ignore the side-group substituents** — generic "PDMS" answers without mentioning vinyl (VMQ), phenyl (PMQ), or trifluoropropyl (FVMQ) variants are considered half-complete.
> 5. **Always state a quantitative range** for properties (–60 to +230 °C, tensile 5–12 MPa) — vague answers ("high temperature", "good strength") are penalized.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the industrial preparation of silicone rubber starting from silicon metal. Explain the **Rochow process** and the **ring-opening polymerization of D₄**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the different **vulcanization (curing) mechanisms** used for silicone rubber. Compare **peroxide cure**, **condensation cure**, and **addition cure (hydrosilylation)** in terms of by-products, cure temperature, and end-use applications. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Industrial Preparation of Silicone Rubber (7 marks)

**Stage 1 — Rochow–Müller Direct Synthesis of Methylchlorosilanes (2 marks):**
High-purity silicon metal (≥ 98% Si) is ground and reacted with methyl chloride gas at **250–300 °C** in a fluidized-bed reactor over a **copper catalyst** (often with Zn, Sn, or Sb promoters):

$$Si_{(s)} + 2 CH_3Cl_{(g)} \xrightarrow{Cu, \, 300°C} (CH_3)_2SiCl_2 \text{ (major)} + \text{other chlorosilanes}$$

The product is a mixture: (CH₃)₂SiCl₂ (~85–90%), (CH₃)₃SiCl, CH₃SiCl₃, SiCl₄, (CH₃)HSiCl₂, etc. The mixture is **fractionally distilled** to isolate pure dimethyldichlorosilane.

**Stage 2 — Hydrolysis (2 marks):**
Pure (CH₃)₂SiCl₂ is hydrolyzed in water to give a mixture of **cyclic oligomers (D₃, D₄, D₅, ...)** and linear silanol-terminated oligomers, releasing HCl:

$$n (CH_3)_2SiCl_2 + 2n H_2O \longrightarrow [HO-(CH_3)_2Si-OH]_n \text{ (linear)} + \text{cyclic } [(CH_3)_2SiO]_n + 2n HCl$$

D₄ (octamethylcyclotetrasiloxane) is the desired monomer, isolated by vacuum distillation.

**Stage 3 — Ring-Opening Polymerization (2 marks):**
D₄ is polymerized with a **base catalyst (KOH, n-BuLi)** or **acid catalyst (triflic acid)** in the presence of a **chain-stopper (hexamethyldisiloxane, MM)** to control molecular weight:

$$n [(CH_3)_2SiO]_4 \xrightarrow{KOH, \, 150°C, \, MM} [(CH_3)_2SiO]_{4n}$$

The number-average DP is set by the molar ratio: $\overline{DP}_n = n_D / n_M$.

**Flow diagram (1 mark):** Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — must show Rochow → Hydrolysis → ROP → Compounding → Curing chain.

#### (b) Vulcanization Mechanisms (7 marks)

| Mechanism | Peroxide Cure | Condensation Cure | Addition Cure (Hydrosilylation) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| Reaction | –CH₃ → –CH₂• → –CH₂–CH₂– | –Si–OH + HO–Si– → –Si–O–Si– + H₂O | –Si–CH=CH₂ + H–Si– → –Si–CH₂–CH₂–Si– |
| Catalyst | Heat + peroxide (DCBP, DCP) | Sn octoate or Ti alkoxide | Pt (Karstedt) |
| Cure T | 150–200 °C (HTV) | 20–50 °C (RTV-2) | 80–180 °C (LSR/RTV-2) |
| By-products | Volatiles (acetophenone, etc.) | Water or alcohol | **None — no by-product** |
| Shrinkage | Moderate | Moderate | **Negligible** |
| Form | HTV solid gum | RTV-2 pastes, RTV-1 sealants | LSR 2-part liquid |
| End-uses | Engine gaskets, O-rings | Construction sealants | Medical, optical, food contact |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Addition cure (hydrosilylation) is the premium method for medical and optical uses because it produces no by-products and has minimal shrinkage. Peroxide cure is the workhorse for high-volume industrial HTV components. Condensation cure is used in one-part moisture-curing RTV sealants for construction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What are the **different types of silicone rubber** based on side-group substituents (methyl, vinyl, phenyl, fluoro)? Explain the effect of each substituent on the **glass transition temperature (T₉)** and **oil/fuel resistance** of the resulting polymer. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **major engineering applications** of silicone rubber in (i) medical devices, (ii) aerospace, and (iii) electronics. For each application, justify **why silicone rubber is preferred** over competing elastomers. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Types of Silicone Rubber Based on Substituents (7 marks)

| Type | Side Group(s) | T₉ | Oil/Fuel Resistance | Key Features |
|------|--------------|-----|--------------------|--------------|
| **MQ (Dimethyl)** | –CH₃ | **–123 °C** | Poor | Lowest T₉, reference base polymer |
| **VMQ (Vinyl-Methyl)** | –CH₃ + 0.1–0.5% –CH=CH₂ | ~ –120 °C | Poor | Improved cure, general purpose (most common) |
| **PMQ (Phenyl-Methyl)** | –CH₃ + 5–10% –C₆H₅ | **–73 to –110 °C** | Slightly better | Low-temperature flexibility for aerospace; phenyl groups reduce chain crystallization |
| **FVMQ (Fluorovinyl-Methyl)** | –CH₃ + –CH=CH₂ + –CH₂CH₂CF₃ | **–65 °C** | **Excellent** | Solvent/fuel resistant; jet-fuel seals; expensive (~10× cost) |

**Effect of substituents (3 marks explanation):**

1. **Methyl (–CH₃)**: Symmetric, small, low steric hindrance. The PDMS chain can coil/uncoil with minimal friction → **very low T₉ (–123 °C)**. No oil resistance because methyl groups are non-polar and the Si–O–Si backbone is polar and slightly hydrophilic.

2. **Vinyl (–CH=CH₂)**: Introduced at **0.1–0.5 mol%** level. Vinyl groups do not significantly change T₉ or polarity, but they provide **active cure sites** (allylic H is easily abstracted by peroxide radicals, and the C=C adds to Si–H). They also slightly improve the **crosslink density uniformity**.

3. **Phenyl (–C₆H₅)**: The bulky phenyl ring **disrupts chain crystallization** at low temperatures, allowing PMQ to retain flexibility at **–70 °C to –90 °C**, well below the crystallization point of PDMS. The bulky ring also **slightly raises T₉** because it stiffens the local segmental motion. PMQ is used in **aerospace seals and diaphragms** operating at very low altitude temperatures.

4. **Trifluoropropyl (–CH₂CH₂CF₃)**: Replaces methyl on ~50% of Si atoms. The **fluorine atoms impart solvent and fuel resistance** by raising the cohesive energy density and reducing polymer–solvent interaction (similar principle to FKM/Viton). However, the bulky CF₃ group reduces chain flexibility → **T₉ rises to ~ –65 °C** and the cost is **5–10×** that of standard VMQ.

#### (b) Engineering Applications (7 marks)

**(i) Medical Devices (2 marks):**
Silicone rubber is USP Class VI, ISO 10993, and FDA 21 CFR 177.2600 approved. It is used in **catheters, dialysis tubing, breast implants, drug-eluting device housings, and surgical instrument handles**. **Why preferred?** (a) Biocompatible — does not elicit immune response, (b) flexible and soft for body comfort, (c) can be autoclaved (steam sterilization) repeatedly, (d) does not support bacterial growth (hydrophobic surface), (e) Pt-addition-cured medical grades produce no extractable by-products. **Competing elastomers** (TPE, PVC, latex) either are not biocompatible, cannot be sterilized, or degrade in body fluids.

**(ii) Aerospace (2.5 marks):**
Used in **engine gaskets, fuel-system seals (FVMQ), thermal blankets, vibration isolators, and hydraulic line O-rings**. **Why preferred?** (a) Service temperature –60 °C to +230 °C continuous, with peaks to 300 °C — required for jet-engine vicinity; (b) FVMQ grades resist jet fuel (JP-8, Jet-A) and Skydrol hydraulic fluid; (c) PMQ grades retain flexibility at stratospheric cold; (d) ozone-resistant at high altitude (O₃ partial pressure is high); (e) low outgassing (NASA ASTM E595) — essential for spacecraft interiors. **Competing elastomers:** FKM/Viton has higher fuel resistance but only to –20 °C; EPDM is not oil-resistant; HNBR cannot match high-T.

**(iii) Electronics (2.5 marks):**
Used in **silicone keypad overlays, HV insulator boots, potting/encapsulation compounds for LEDs, conformal coatings for PCBs, and cable insulation (especially in nuclear/railway).** **Why preferred?** (a) Dielectric strength **20–30 kV/mm** and dielectric constant **2.6–3.0** (low, hence low signal loss); (b) thermal conductivity 0.15–0.25 W/(m·K) — can be filled with Al₂O₃ for 1–3 W/(m·K) to dissipate LED heat; (c) flame-retardant grades meet UL 94 V-0; (d) no halogen, low smoke (railway EN 45545); (e) stable dielectric properties over a wide frequency range (Hz to GHz). **Competing elastomers:** EPDM has good dielectric but poor heat aging; polyurethane yellows and degrades; epoxy is rigid and cracks on thermal cycling.

**Conclusion (from any one application):** In all three domains, silicone rubber wins on **temperature + biocompatibility/dielectrics + weathering** triangle, accepting a higher cost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Silicone Rubber (Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> **Core Structure**
> - Backbone: **–Si–O–Si–O–** (siloxane linkage, inorganic, "pre-oxidized")
> - Side groups: **–CH₃ (methyl)** standard; variants vinyl, phenyl, fluoro
> - General formula: **[R₂SiO]ₙ** ; most common is **PDMS (polydimethylsiloxane)**
> - Si–O bond energy: **452 kJ/mol** ; bond length 1.64 Å ; bond angle ~143°
> - T₉ of PDMS: **–123 °C** (lowest among common rubbers)
> - Service range: **–60 °C to +230 °C** continuous, 300 °C peak
>
> **Synthesis (3 stages)**
> 1. **Rochow process**: Si + CH₃Cl → (CH₃)₂SiCl₂ (Cu catalyst, 300 °C)
> 2. **Hydrolysis**: (CH₃)₂SiCl₂ + H₂O → cyclic D₄ + HCl
> 3. **ROP of D₄**: base/acid catalyst + chain-stopper MM → linear PDMS
> - DP controlled by: **DP = n_D / n_M** (chain-stopper theory)
>
> **Curing Mechanisms**
> - **Peroxide (HTV)**: heat + DCBP/DCP/Varox → –CH₂–CH₂– crosslinks
> - **Condensation (RTV-2)**: –Si–OH + HO–Si– → –Si–O–Si– + H₂O
> - **Addition (LSR)**: –Si–Vinyl + H–Si– → –Si–CH₂–CH₂–Si– + **no by-product** (Pt Karstedt catalyst)
> - **Moisture (RTV-1)**: acetoxy → acetic acid (vinegar smell)
>
> **Types by Side Group**
> - **MQ** = dimethyl ; **VMQ** = methyl + vinyl (most common, 0.1–0.5 mol% vinyl)
> - **PMQ** = methyl + phenyl (5–10%) → low-T flexibility (aerospace)
> - **FVMQ** = methyl + vinyl + trifluoropropyl → fuel/solvent resistance
>
> **Reinforcement**
> - **Fumed silica (5–50 nm, 20–60 phr)** is the primary reinforcing filler
> - Tensile strength gain: 0.3–0.7 MPa → 5–12 MPa (10–30×)
> - Mechanism: H-bonding between silanol (Si–OH) on silica and siloxane oxygen
>
> **Key Properties (memorize the numbers)**
> - Tensile: **5–12 MPa** filled ; **0.3–0.7 MPa** unfilled
> - Elongation: **100–700 %**
> - Hardness Shore A: **20–80**
> - Dielectric strength: **20–30 kV/mm** ; dielectric constant **2.6–3.0**
> - Specific gravity: **1.10–1.50**
> - Compression set (24 h/150 °C): **5–25 %**
>
> **Crosslink Density**
> - **Flory–Rehner equation** for swelling-based measurement
> - $\nu = -\left[\ln(1-\phi_p) + \phi_p + \chi\phi_p^2\right] / \left[V_1(\phi_p^{1/3} - \phi_p/2)\right]$
> - $M_c = \rho_p / \nu$ ; typical $M_c$ 5,000–25,000 g/mol
>
> **Applications (PAME-FE mnemonic: Pharma, Aerospace, Medical, Electronics, Food, Energy)**
> - Medical: catheters, implants (Pt-cured LSR)
> - Aerospace: FVMQ fuel seals, PMQ low-T seals, HTV gaskets
> - Electronics: keypads, HV insulation, LED potting
> - Food: baking molds, baby nipples (FDA-approved)
> - Construction: structural glazing (RTV-1 acetoxy)
> - Energy: PV encapsulation, wind-turbine blade coatings
>
> **Common Exam Traps**
> - ❌ Writing "Si=O" double bond (it is a single Si–O–Si chain, not a ketone analogue)
> - ❌ Confusing silicon (element) with silicone (polymer family)
> - ❌ Saying "silicone is just like rubber but better" (need backbone explanation)
> - ❌ Omitting quantitative ranges in property tables
> - ❌ Forgetting the vinyl group's role in cure (key enabler of peroxide + Pt cure)
>
> **One-Line Mantra for Last-Minute Revision:**
> *"Silicone rubber = inorganic Si–O backbone + organic –CH₃ side groups = heat-stable + cold-flexible + biocompatible + dielectric, vulcanized via peroxide (HTV), condensation (RTV-2), or Pt-addition (LSR) cures, reinforced with fumed silica to 5–12 MPa tensile, and used wherever conventional rubbers fail at temperature, biocompatibility, or weather extremes."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Molecular Weight of Polymers:** Polymers are classified as *high molecular weight* materials when their average molecular mass ($M$) exceeds approximately **10,000 g/mol** ($10^4$ Da). The term "high" is relative, but in industrial and academic polymer engineering, polymers with $M > 10^4$ g/mol are considered to be in the high molecular weight regime because at this threshold, characteristic polymeric behaviour (chain entanglements, viscoelasticity, mechanical strength) emerges.

Unlike small molecules, synthetic polymers do **not** possess a single, well-defined molecular mass. Every polymerisation reaction produces a mixture of chain lengths, giving rise to a **molar mass distribution (MMD)**. Therefore, "molecular weight" of a polymer is always expressed as a statistical *average*, and the *breadth* of the distribution itself is a critical property.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Molecular Mass vs. Molecular Weight:**
> In modern polymer engineering practice (and in KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation), the SI-correct term is **molar mass** with units of **g·mol⁻¹**, while **molecular weight** is a dimensionless ratio. For exam purposes, both are used interchangeably, but always state the unit **g/mol** for clarity.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **rice merchant** asked to describe the "weight of a bag of rice." You have bags containing thousands of grains. You could:

1. Count every grain and take the **average weight of one grain** (this is the *number average*).
2. Weigh a random handful and find the *typical weight per grain in a heavy-handed grab* (this is the *weight average*).
3. Pick the grain that weighs more than 50 % of all other grains (this is a *median* or percentile-based average).

A **polymer sample is exactly like that bag of rice**: it is a mixture of chains of *different lengths*, and different experimental techniques "see" the mixture from different statistical perspectives. The chain length determines the molecular mass, and **longer chains dominate properties like strength, viscosity, and toughness.**

> [!TIP]
> **Why does "high" matter?** A polyethylene chain of 1,000 repeat units ($M \approx 28{,}000$ g/mol) behaves as a tough, ductible plastic. Reduce it to 100 units ($M \approx 2{,}800$ g/mol) and you get a brittle wax. The threshold $M \approx 10^4$ g/mol is where the chain becomes long enough to **entangle** with its neighbours — this is the basis of all useful mechanical properties.

### 1.3 Why High Molecular Weight is a Defining Feature of Polymers

| Property at Low $M$ | Property at High $M$ | Practical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Chains slide past each other easily | Chain entanglements form | Mechanical strength ↑ |
| Liquid-like flow | Viscoelastic / rubbery behaviour | Processability changes |
| Low viscosity (Newtonian) | High viscosity (non-Newtonian) | Melt processing requires high $T$, $P$ |
| Brittle, friable | Tough, ductile | Useful for structural applications |
| Sharp melting point | Broad melting transition | Process window widens |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The student must be able to (i) define and distinguish the various molecular weight averages, (ii) derive the relationships between them, (iii) explain the polydispersity index, and (iv) comment on the physical significance of high molecular weight.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Need for Multiple Averages

A polymer sample contains $N_i$ molecules of molar mass $M_i$ in each $i$-th fraction. Different experimental techniques are sensitive to different moments of the distribution:

- **Osmometry, Vapour Pressure Lowering, End-Group Analysis** → respond to the *number* of molecules → give $\bar{M}_n$
- **Light Scattering, Sedimentation** → respond to the *mass* of molecules → give $\bar{M}_w$
- **Ultracentrifugation (sedimentation equilibrium)** → respond to higher moments → give $\bar{M}_z$
- **Viscometry** → respond to hydrodynamic volume → give $\bar{M}_v$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always true ordering** for a polydisperse polymer:
> $$\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_v \le \bar{M}_w \le \bar{M}_z$$
> Equality holds **only** for a perfectly monodisperse sample.

### 2.2 The Fundamental Definitions

Let there be $n$ fractions in the polymer sample, with $N_i$ molecules in the $i$-th fraction each having molar mass $M_i$. The **total number** of molecules is $N = \sum N_i$, and the **total mass** is $W = \sum N_i M_i$.

**Number-average molar mass:**
$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{\sum W_i}{\sum N_i}$$

**Weight-average molar mass:**
$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum W_i M_i}{\sum W_i}$$

**Z-average molar mass:**
$$\bar{M}_z = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$$

**Viscosity-average molar mass:**
$$\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{(1+a)}}{\sum N_i M_i} \right)^{1/a}$$

where $a$ is the **Mark–Houwink exponent** (typically $0.5 \le a \le 0.8$ for most polymer–solvent systems).

### 2.3 The Polydispersity Index (PDI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} \ge 1}$$
> PDI = 1 → monodisperse (theoretical limit, never achieved in step polymerisation; closely approached in anionic living polymerisation)
> PDI > 1 → polydisperse (typical industrial polymers have $1.5 \le \text{PDI} \le 5$)
> PDI $\gg$ 1 → very broad distribution (free-radical polymerisation can give PDI up to 20 for some cases)

### 2.4 Degree of Polymerisation (DP or $\bar{X}_n$)

The **number-average degree of polymerisation** is the average number of repeat (monomer) units per chain:
$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$$
where $M_0$ is the molar mass of the repeat unit. For example, for polyethylene: $M_0 = 28$ g/mol; a polymer of $\bar{M}_n = 140{,}000$ g/mol has $\bar{X}_n = 5000$.

For step-growth polymerisation, Carothers' relation connects extent of reaction $p$ to $\bar{X}_n$:
$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

### 2.5 The Mark–Houwink Equation (Intrinsic Viscosity)

$$[\eta] = K \bar{M}_v^{a}$$

where $[\eta]$ is the **intrinsic viscosity** (dL/g), $K$ and $a$ are empirical constants for a given polymer–solvent–temperature system. This equation is the bridge between an easy-to-measure rheological property and $\bar{M}_v$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Print-Ready Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — High Molecular Weight of Polymers (KTU Module 2)**
>
> | # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Unit | Experimental Method |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Number-average $\bar{M}_n$ | $\sum N_i M_i \,/\, \sum N_i$ | g/mol | Osmometry, VPO, End-group titration |
> | 2 | Weight-average $\bar{M}_w$ | $\sum N_i M_i^2 \,/\, \sum N_i M_i$ | g/mol | Static Light Scattering (SLS) |
> | 3 | Z-average $\bar{M}_z$ | $\sum N_i M_i^3 \,/\, \sum N_i M_i^2$ | g/mol | Sedimentation equilibrium |
> | 4 | Viscosity-average $\bar{M}_v$ | $\left( \sum N_i M_i^{1+a} \,/\, \sum N_i M_i \right)^{1/a}$ | g/mol | Capillary viscometry + Mark–Houwink |
> | 5 | Polydispersity Index | $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ | dimensionless | Ratio of SLS / Osmometry |
> | 6 | Number-average DP | $\bar{X}_n = \bar{M}_n / M_0$ | dimensionless | From $\bar{M}_n$ |
> | 7 | Carothers' equation | $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ | dimensionless | Step-growth |
> | 8 | Mark–Houwink | $[\eta] = K \bar{M}_v^{a}$ | dL/g | Viscometry |
> | 9 | Staudinger index form | $[\eta] = K' M^a$ | dL/g | General |
> | 10 | Flory–Fox relation | $[\eta] \cdot \bar{M}_n \propto$ chain-end density | dL·mol/g | Theta solvent analysis |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Polymer Engineering

- **Quality Control in PET, HDPE, PVC production:** Every reactor batch is characterised by $\bar{M}_w$ and PDI to ensure consistent melt viscosity and mechanical performance.
- **Biomedical Polymers:** Drug-delivery hydrogels and tissue scaffolds require a narrow PDI to control degradation rate and drug release.
- **Tire Rubber (SBR, BR):** $\bar{M}_w$ is the single most important predictor of wear resistance and rolling resistance; tire-grade rubber has $\bar{M}_w \approx 3 \times 10^5$ g/mol.
- **Ultra-high Molecular Weight Polyethylene (UHMWPE):** $\bar{M}_w > 10^6$ g/mol — used in artificial joint liners because the extreme chain length gives exceptional wear resistance.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $\bar{M}_n$ from First Principles

**Setup:** Consider a polymer sample split into $n$ discrete molar-mass fractions. Let the $i$-th fraction contain $N_i$ molecules, each of mass $M_i$. We want an average that is *equally weighted by the number of molecules*.

**Step 1 — Define the arithmetic mean over all individual molecules:**
Every molecule is a "data point" of mass $M$. The arithmetic mean is the sum of all masses divided by the number of all molecules.

**Step 2 — Sum the masses fraction by fraction:**
The total mass contributed by the $i$-th fraction is $N_i M_i$. Summing over all fractions:
$$M_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i M_i$$

**Step 3 — Count the total number of molecules:**
$$N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$$

**Step 4 — Write the arithmetic mean:**
$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{M_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce weight fraction $w_i$:**
Weight fraction $w_i = N_i M_i / \sum N_i M_i$. Substituting:
$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1}{\sum (w_i / M_i)}$$

This reciprocal form is the hallmark of a *number* average. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $\bar{M}_w$ from First Principles

**Why a *second* average?** A molecule weighing 100 kg should "count more" than one weighing 1 g, because physically it contributes more to bulk properties (refractive index, light scattering, mass). The weight average weights each fraction by the *mass* it contributes.

**Step 1 — Treat each fraction as a single entity, weighted by its mass:**
The "data point" for the $i$-th fraction has weight $W_i = N_i M_i$ and value $M_i$.

**Step 2 — Mass-weighted arithmetic mean:**
$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum W_i M_i}{\sum W_i}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $W_i = N_i M_i$:**
$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum (N_i M_i) M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of weight fraction $w_i$:**
$$\bar{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i$$

This *linear* (not reciprocal) form is the hallmark of a *weight* average. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of $\bar{M}_z$

The Z-average weights each fraction by the *square of its mass*, which is the response function of sedimentation and certain scattering experiments.

**Step 1 — Generalised mean form:**
Define the $k$-th moment of the distribution:
$$\mu_k = \sum N_i M_i^{k}$$

**Step 2 — Z-average uses the next-higher ratio:**
$$\bar{M}_z = \frac{\mu_3}{\mu_2} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** $\bar{M}_z$ is heavily biased toward the *highest-mass* species in the sample. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of $\bar{M}_v$

**Physical starting point:** The intrinsic viscosity contribution of a single molecule of mass $M_i$ is $[\eta]_i M_i$ (intrinsic viscosity × mass). The specific viscosity of the solution is the sum of all such contributions.

**Step 1 — Intrinsic viscosity of a single chain (Mark–Houwink):**
$$[\eta]_i = K M_i^{a}$$

**Step 2 — Specific viscosity contribution per unit mass:**
The contribution of fraction $i$ to the solution's specific viscosity is proportional to $N_i M_i [\eta]_i$:
$$\eta_{\text{sp},i} \propto N_i M_i \cdot K M_i^{a} = K N_i M_i^{1+a}$$

**Step 3 — Total specific viscosity:**
$$\eta_{\text{sp}} = K \sum N_i M_i^{1+a}$$

**Step 4 — By definition, the intrinsic viscosity of the mixture is:**
$$[\eta] = K \bar{M}_v^{a}$$

Setting this equal to the specific-viscosity-per-unit-concentration expression (and noting concentration $\propto \sum N_i M_i$):
$$K \bar{M}_v^{a} = \frac{K \sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\bar{M}_v$:**
$$\bar{M}_v^{a} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i} \right)^{1/a}} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Note:** When $a = 1$, $\bar{M}_v = \bar{M}_w$. When $a \to 0$, $\bar{M}_v \to \bar{M}_n$. Typical $a = 0.5\text{–}0.8$ places $\bar{M}_v$ between $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{M}_w$, closer to $\bar{M}_w$.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem:** A polymer sample consists of three fractions with the following data. Compute $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, $\bar{M}_z$, $\bar{M}_v$ (using $a = 0.6$), and PDI. Comment on the result.

| Fraction $i$ | $N_i$ (mol) | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 3 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 1 | 100,000 |

**Step 1 — Compute the required products:**

| $i$ | $N_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^2$ | $N_i M_i^3$ | $N_i M_i^{1.6}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 10,000 | $1.0 \times 10^4$ | $1.0 \times 10^8$ | $1.0 \times 10^{12}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{6.6} = 3.98 \times 10^6$ |
| 2 | 3 | 50,000 | $1.5 \times 10^5$ | $7.5 \times 10^9$ | $3.75 \times 10^{14}$ | $3 \times 50{,}000^{1.6} = 3 \times 5.66 \times 10^7 = 1.70 \times 10^8$ |
| 3 | 1 | 100,000 | $1.0 \times 10^5$ | $1.0 \times 10^{10}$ | $1.0 \times 10^{15}$ | $1 \times 10^{7.6} = 3.98 \times 10^7$ |
| **Sum** | **5** | — | $2.6 \times 10^5$ | $1.76 \times 10^{10}$ | $1.376 \times 10^{15}$ | $2.14 \times 10^8$ |

**Step 2 — Compute the averages:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{2.6 \times 10^5}{5} = 5.2 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{1.76 \times 10^{10}}{2.6 \times 10^5} = 6.77 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\bar{M}_z = \frac{1.376 \times 10^{15}}{1.76 \times 10^{10}} = 7.82 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{2.14 \times 10^8}{2.6 \times 10^5} \right)^{1/0.6} = (823.1)^{1.667} = 2.12 \times 10^{4} \times 3.16 = 6.71 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}$$

(We used $823.1^{1.667}$: $\log_{10}(823.1) = 2.915$; $2.915 \times 1.667 = 4.86$; $10^{4.86} = 7.24 \times 10^4$. Recomputing precisely: $2.14 \times 10^8 / 2.6 \times 10^5 = 823.08$. Then $823.08^{1/0.6} = 823.08^{1.6667}$. $\ln(823.08) = 6.713$. $6.713 \times 1.6667 = 11.188$. $e^{11.188} = 7.21 \times 10^4$ g/mol.)

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{6.77 \times 10^4}{5.2 \times 10^4} = 1.30$$

**Step 3 — Comment:** The ordering $\bar{M}_n < \bar{M}_v < \bar{M}_w < \bar{M}_z$ is confirmed. The PDI of 1.30 indicates a moderately polydisperse sample, typical of a free-radical polymerisation.

---

### 3.6 Proof of the Inequality $\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_w$

**Step 1 — Compute the variance of the mass distribution:**
$$\sigma^2 = \bar{M}_w \bar{M}_n - \bar{M}_n^2 = \bar{M}_n (\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n) \ge 0$$

**Step 2 — Variance is non-negative:**
$$\bar{M}_n (\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n) \ge 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \bar{M}_w \ge \bar{M}_n$$

Equality holds iff $\sigma^2 = 0$, i.e., all molecules have identical mass. $\blacksquare$
