## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A, Q1(a)]** (3 Marks)
> **Q1.** Define number-average ($\bar{M}_n$) and weight-average ($\bar{M}_w$) molecular weights of a polymer. Why does a polymer sample not have a single unique molecular weight?

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70–90 words):**
> A polymer sample is a mixture of chains of different lengths produced during polymerisation. The number-average $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ is the arithmetic mean weighted by the number of molecules; it is sensitive to small chains and is measured by osmometry. The weight-average $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ weights each species by its mass contribution and is measured by light scattering. Because chain length varies statistically, the sample has a *distribution* of masses, so multiple averages are required for full characterisation. **(3/3)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B, Q1(b)]** (3 Marks)
> **Q2.** What is the Polydispersity Index (PDI)? What does PDI = 1 and PDI = 2 signify? Comment on the relationship between PDI and the polymerisation mechanism.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70–90 words):**
> PDI $= \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ is the ratio of weight- to number-average molecular weight and measures the breadth of the molar mass distribution. PDI = 1 implies a *monodisperse* sample (all chains identical — approached in living anionic polymerisation). PDI = 2 implies a moderately broad distribution, typical of classical *step-growth* polymerisation. *Free-radical chain-growth* polymerisation gives PDI in the range 1.5–5, while *dispersion polymerisation* can yield PDI > 10. **(3/3)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, KTU Internal-Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, End-Semester, Module 2, Q12, Option A]** (14 Marks)

#### **Question A**

**(a)** Define the number-average, weight-average, and viscosity-average molecular weights. Derive the expression for $\bar{M}_v$ starting from the Mark–Houwink equation. Show that $\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_w$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A polymer sample has the following composition. Calculate $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, $\bar{M}_z$, and the PDI. Comment on the polydispersity.

| Fraction $i$ | $N_i$ (mol) | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 20,000 |
| 2 | 5 | 60,000 |
| 3 | 3 | 100,000 |
| 4 | 1 | 200,000 |

(7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Define the three averages (2 marks):**

Let $N_i$ be the number of molecules of mass $M_i$ in the $i$-th fraction of a polymer sample containing $k$ fractions.

- Number-average (colligative-property response):
$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i}$$

- Weight-average (light-scattering / sedimentation response):
$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}$$

- Viscosity-average (viscometry + Mark–Houwink):
$$\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i} \right)^{1/a}$$

**Step 2 — Derive $\bar{M}_v$ (3 marks):**

The intrinsic viscosity of a single chain of mass $M_i$ is, from the Mark–Houwink equation:
$$[\eta]_i = K M_i^{a}$$

The total specific viscosity of the solution is the sum of contributions from all fractions, each contribution being proportional to $N_i M_i [\eta]_i$:
$$\eta_{\text{sp,total}} \propto \sum N_i M_i \cdot K M_i^{a} = K \sum N_i M_i^{1+a}$$

Dividing by total concentration $c \propto \sum N_i M_i$ gives the intrinsic viscosity of the *mixture*:
$$[\eta]_{\text{mix}} = K \bar{M}_v^{a} = \frac{K \sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

Solving for $\bar{M}_v$:
$$\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i} \right)^{1/a}$$

**Step 3 — Prove $\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_w$ (2 marks):**

Compute the variance of mass over the distribution:
$$\sigma^2 = \bar{M}_w \bar{M}_n - \bar{M}_n^2 = \bar{M}_n (\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n)$$

Since variance is non-negative and $\bar{M}_n > 0$:
$$\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n \ge 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \bar{M}_w \ge \bar{M}_n$$

Equality holds only when $\sigma^2 = 0$, i.e., all chains have identical mass (monodisperse). **(7/7)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Build the calculation table (2 marks):**

| $i$ | $N_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^2$ | $N_i M_i^3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 20,000 | $4.00 \times 10^4$ | $8.00 \times 10^8$ | $1.60 \times 10^{13}$ |
| 2 | 5 | 60,000 | $3.00 \times 10^5$ | $1.80 \times 10^{10}$ | $1.08 \times 10^{15}$ |
| 3 | 3 | 100,000 | $3.00 \times 10^5$ | $3.00 \times 10^{10}$ | $3.00 \times 10^{15}$ |
| 4 | 1 | 200,000 | $2.00 \times 10^5$ | $4.00 \times 10^{10}$ | $8.00 \times 10^{15}$ |
| **Sum** | **11** | — | **$8.40 \times 10^5$** | **$1.16 \times 10^{11}$** | **$1.218 \times 10^{16}$** |

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, $\bar{M}_z$ (3 marks):**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{8.40 \times 10^5}{11} = 7.64 \times 10^4 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{1.16 \times 10^{11}}{8.40 \times 10^5} = 1.38 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\bar{M}_z = \frac{1.218 \times 10^{16}}{1.16 \times 10^{11}} = 1.05 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — PDI and comment (2 marks):**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{1.38 \times 10^5}{7.64 \times 10^4} = 1.81$$

**Comment:** A PDI of 1.81 indicates a moderately polydisperse polymer. The ordering $\bar{M}_n < \bar{M}_z < \bar{M}_w$ is verified, confirming a broad distribution biased toward higher masses. Such a PDI is typical of step-growth polymerisation at high conversion or of free-radical polymerisation with chain-transfer reactions. **(7/7)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End-Semester, Module 2, Q12, Option B]** (14 Marks)

#### **Question B**

**(a)** Discuss the various experimental methods used to determine the molecular weight of polymers. Indicate which average each method yields. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The intrinsic viscosity of a polystyrene sample in toluene at 25 °C was measured to be $[\eta] = 0.85$ dL/g. Using the Mark–Houwink constants $K = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g and $a = 0.69$, calculate the viscosity-average molecular weight $\bar{M}_v$. If this sample is also found to have $\bar{M}_n = 9.0 \times 10^4$ g/mol from osmometry, compute the PDI and state what the result implies about the distribution. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Introductory statement (1 mark):**
> No single experimental method measures the *full* molecular weight of a polymer, since polymers are polydisperse. Different techniques respond to different moments of the distribution, and hence yield different averages.

**Step 2 — Tabular exposition of methods (5 marks):**

| Method | Physical Property Measured | Absolute / Relative | Average Obtained | Typical $M$ Range (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vapour Pressure Osmometry (VPO) | $\Delta T$ of solvent vapour | Absolute | $\bar{M}_n$ | $10^3$ – $4 \times 10^4$ |
| Membrane Osmometry | Osmotic pressure $\pi$ | Absolute | $\bar{M}_n$ | $2 \times 10^4$ – $10^6$ |
| End-Group Analysis (titration, NMR) | Number of chain ends per unit mass | Absolute | $\bar{M}_n$ | $< 3 \times 10^4$ |
| Viscometry (capillary) | Flow time $\rightarrow [\eta]$ | Relative (needs calibration) | $\bar{M}_v$ | $10^3$ – $10^6$ |
| Static Light Scattering (SLS / Zimm plot) | Rayleigh ratio $R_\theta$ vs. angle | Absolute | $\bar{M}_w$ | $10^4$ – $10^7$ |
| Sedimentation Velocity | Sedimentation coefficient $s$ | Absolute | Approx. $\bar{M}_w$ | $10^4$ – $10^7$ |
| Sedimentation Equilibrium | Concentration gradient at equilibrium | Absolute | $\bar{M}_z$ (or $M_{z+1}$) | $10^4$ – $10^6$ |
| Size-Exclusion Chromatography (GPC / SEC) | Elution volume vs. calibration | Relative (or absolute with MALLS) | Full MMD → all averages | $10^2$ – $10^6$ |
| Mass Spectrometry (MALDI-TOF) | $m/z$ ratio of ions | Absolute | Full MMD | $10^3$ – $10^5$ (soft ionisation limit) |

**Step 3 — Closing summary (1 mark):**
> The choice of method depends on the molecular-weight range, whether the sample is in solution or melt, and the level of detail required (single average vs. full distribution). GPC with multi-angle laser light scattering (MALLS) is the most powerful single technique in modern polymer labs.

**(7/7)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Apply the Mark–Houwink equation (3 marks):**
$$[\eta] = K \bar{M}_v^{a}$$
$$0.85 = 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \bar{M}_v^{0.69}$$
$$\bar{M}_v^{0.69} = \frac{0.85}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000$$
$$\bar{M}_v = (5000)^{1/0.69} = (5000)^{1.4493}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the value (2 marks):**
$$\log_{10} \bar{M}_v = 1.4493 \times \log_{10}(5000) = 1.4493 \times 3.69897 = 5.3611$$
$$\bar{M}_v = 10^{5.3611} = 2.30 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — PDI calculation (1 mark):**
$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} \;\;\text{but we have}\;\; \bar{M}_v \text{ and } \bar{M}_n$$
Since $\bar{M}_v \approx \bar{M}_w$ (a good approximation because $a = 0.69$ is typical):
$$\text{PDI} \approx \frac{\bar{M}_v}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{2.30 \times 10^5}{9.0 \times 10^4} = 2.56$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
> A PDI of 2.56 indicates a broad molar-mass distribution, typical of free-radical polymerisation of styrene. The sample contains a significant fraction of low-molecular-weight chains ($\bar{M}_n = 9.0 \times 10^4$) alongside very long chains (the $\bar{M}_v$ value is $2.5 \times$ higher). Such a polymer would be suitable for general-purpose applications (e.g., packaging, disposable items) but not for demanding engineering uses where narrow distribution is critical.

**(7/7)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit omission:** Always write **g/mol** next to a molecular weight. A bare number will lose 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Confusing the order of operations in $\bar{M}_w$:** A common error is to write $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i^2 M_i / \sum N_i$. It is $\sum N_i M_i^2$, i.e., the *square is on $M_i$, not on $N_i$*. Memorise this.
> 3. **Forgetting the power on $M_i$ in $\bar{M}_z$:** Many students write $M_i^2$ instead of $M_i^3$ in the numerator and $M_i$ instead of $M_i^2$ in the denominator.
> 4. **Confusing $\bar{M}_v$ with $\bar{M}_w$:** Always show the Mark–Houwink derivation — it is the only way to prove the formula and secure full marks.
> 5. **PDI computed from $\bar{M}_v / \bar{M}_n$:** State explicitly that this is an *approximation* valid when $a \approx 0.7$. Do not present it as the definition.
> 6. **No comment on the answer:** For all numerical questions, KTU 2024 Scheme requires a concluding sentence interpreting the numerical value (e.g., "PDI of 1.81 indicates a moderately polydisperse polymer"). Skipping this costs ~1 mark per question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — High Molecular Weight of Polymers (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** A polymer is "high molecular weight" when $\bar{M}_n > 10^4$ g/mol; this threshold marks the onset of chain entanglements.
- **Polydispersity is the rule, not the exception:** Every synthetic polymer is a *mixture* of chain lengths, so a *distribution* is required, not a single number.
- **The four averages and their order:** $\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_v \le \bar{M}_w \le \bar{M}_z$. Always mention this in the answer.
- **Numerical expressions to memorise verbatim:**
  - $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ (number-weighted mean)
  - $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ (mass-weighted mean)
  - $\bar{M}_z = \sum N_i M_i^3 / \sum N_i M_i^2$ (Z-weighted mean)
  - $\bar{M}_v = \left( \sum N_i M_i^{1+a} / \sum N_i M_i \right)^{1/a}$
  - $\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n \ge 1$
- **Mark–Houwink equation:** $[\eta] = K \bar{M}_v^{a}$, where $K$ and $a$ are empirical constants for a given polymer–solvent–temperature system.
- **Carothers' relation** (for step-growth): $\bar{X}_n = 1 / (1 - p)$, where $p$ is the extent of reaction.
- **Technique → Average mapping (must know for viva and MCQs):**
  - Osmometry / VPO / End-group analysis → $\bar{M}_n$
  - Static Light Scattering / Sedimentation velocity → $\bar{M}_w$
  - Capillary viscometry → $\bar{M}_v$
  - Sedimentation equilibrium → $\bar{M}_z$
  - GPC / SEC → full molar mass distribution + all averages
- **PDI interpretation:** PDI $\approx 1$ (monodisperse, anionic), $\approx 2$ (step-growth, classical Flory), $1.5$–$5$ (free-radical chain-growth), $> 10$ (dispersion polymerisation).
- **Property–$\bar{M}_n$ relationship:** As $\bar{M}_n$ increases → tensile strength ↑, impact strength ↑, melt viscosity ↑, processing temperature ↑, crystallisation rate ↓.
- **UHMWPE:** A special class with $\bar{M}_w > 10^6$ g/mol, used in artificial-joint liners and high-strength fibres.
- **Always end numerical answers** with a *qualitative interpretation sentence* — this is a mandatory KTU 2024 evaluation criterion.
- **Forgetting units, omitting the variance proof, and confusing $M_i^k$ with $N_i^k$** are the three most common reasons for mark deductions in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Averaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Averaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Concept of Averaging" in Polymer Science?

In polymer engineering, **almost every synthetic polymer is a mixture of macromolecules of different chain lengths**. Unlike a small molecule (e.g., water, H₂O, with a fixed molecular weight of 18 g/mol), a polymer sample such as commercial polyethylene contains chains of 500 carbons, 5,000 carbons, 50,000 carbons, etc., all coexisting. Therefore, we cannot quote "the molecular weight" of polyethylene — we must quote an **average molecular weight**, and *how* we average it matters enormously because the resulting value depends on the averaging technique used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> The *Concept of Averaging* refers to the various statistical/mathematical methods used to compute representative average molecular weights of a polydisperse polymer sample. Different experimental techniques (osmometry, viscosity, light scattering, sedimentation) are sensitive to different molecular properties, giving rise to distinct averages: **Number Average (M̄ₙ)**, **Weight Average (M̄w)**, **Viscosity Average (M̄v)**, **Z-Average (M̄z)**, and higher moments. The order of these averages always obeys:
> $$\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_v \le \bar{M}_w \le \bar{M}_z$$

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Classroom Height Problem"

Imagine you walk into a classroom of 60 students and want to quote *"the average height of the class."*

- **Method 1 — Count every student equally (Number Average):** Add the heights of all 60 students and divide by 60. A short student and a tall student both contribute equally.
- **Method 2 — Weight by height (Weight Average):** Add up the *product* of (each student's height × their height) and divide by the sum of their heights. Here, a tall student contributes disproportionately more.
- **Method 3 — Weight by area (Z-Average):** Add up the product of (height × height × height) etc.

You will get **three different answers** from the same classroom. None is wrong — they answer different physical questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Polymers are exactly like that classroom. A short polymer chain contributes less to mass, less to viscosity, less to light scattering — but always *the same* to the **number**. Hence the value of the average shifts depending on what property your measuring instrument actually "sees."

## 1.3 Why Polymers Need Multiple Averages

The molecular weight distribution (MWD) of a polymer is typically represented as a histogram of **Nᵢ** molecules having molecular weight **Mᵢ** in the *i-th* bin. The moments of this distribution give the different averages. The general formula for the **q-th moment** is:

$$\bar{M}_q = \frac{\sum_i N_i \, M_i^{q}}{\sum_i N_i \, M_i^{q-1}}$$

This single equation, by varying **q**, gives us M̄ₙ (q=1), M̄w (q=2), M̄z (q=3), M̄z+1 (q=4), etc. — the entire ladder of averages.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value Range for Commercial Polymers |
|---|---|---|
| Number Average Molecular Weight | M̄ₙ | 10⁴ – 10⁵ g/mol |
| Weight Average Molecular Weight | M̄w | 10⁵ – 10⁶ g/mol |
| Polydispersity Index | PDI = M̄w/M̄n | 1.0 (monodisperse) to 20+ (highly polydisperse) |

For a perfectly monodisperse sample (all chains identical, like in proteins), **M̄ₙ = M̄w = M̄z = M**, and **PDI = 1.0**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting Up the Statistical Framework

Consider a polydisperse polymer sample. Let the sample be divided into *i = 1, 2, 3, ...* size fractions, where:

- **Nᵢ** = number of molecules in fraction *i*
- **Mᵢ** = molecular weight of molecules in fraction *i*
- **wᵢ** = weight (mass) of molecules in fraction *i* = Nᵢ · Mᵢ
- **W** = total weight of the sample = Σ wᵢ = Σ Nᵢ Mᵢ
- **N** = total number of molecules = Σ Nᵢ

The **weight fraction** of fraction *i* is defined as:

$$w_i^{\text{frac}} = \frac{w_i}{W} = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

## 2.2 The Four Primary Averages — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Number Average Molecular Weight (M̄ₙ)

**Definition:** Total mass of all molecules divided by the total number of molecules.

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\text{Total Mass}}{\text{Total Number of Molecules}} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$

**Physical Property It Depends On:** Number of molecules (colligative properties).
**Measurement Techniques:** Osmometry (vapor pressure, membrane, vapor-pressure), end-group analysis, cryoscopy, ebulliometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **M̄ₙ is "fair" — every chain counts as 1, regardless of size.** This is the lowest of all averages.

### 2.2.2 Weight Average Molecular Weight (M̄w)

**Definition:** Each molecule is weighted by its molecular weight (mass).

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} = \sum_i w_i^{\text{frac}} \, M_i$$

**Physical Property It Depends On:** Mass (size-dependent scattering, precipitation).
**Measurement Techniques:** Static Light Scattering (SLS), Sedimentation, Diffusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **M̄w is biased toward heavier chains** because a long chain contributes its *mass × mass* rather than just mass.

### 2.2.3 Viscosity Average Molecular Weight (M̄v)

**Definition:** Derived from intrinsic viscosity [η] via the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation**: [η] = K · Mᵃ. Substituting gives:

$$\bar{M}_v = \left[ \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \right]^{1/a}$$

where *a* is the Mark–Houwink exponent (typically 0.5 ≤ a ≤ 1.0).

**Physical Property It Depends On:** Hydrodynamic volume in dilute solution.
**Measurement Techniques:** Capillary viscometry (Ubbelohde viscometer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **M̄v lies between M̄ₙ and M̄w.** Specifically: M̄ₙ ≤ M̄v ≤ M̄w. If a = 1, M̄v → M̄w; if a = 0, M̄v → M̄ₙ.

### 2.2.4 Z-Average Molecular Weight (M̄z) and Higher

$$\bar{M}_z = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^3}{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}$$

**Measurement Techniques:** Sedimentation equilibrium, ultracentrifugation.
The Z+1 average: M̄z+1 = Σ NᵢMᵢ⁴ / Σ NᵢMᵢ³.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | M̄ₙ = Σ NᵢMᵢ / Σ Nᵢ | g·mol⁻¹ | Number average MW |
| 2 | M̄w = Σ NᵢMᵢ² / Σ NᵢMᵢ | g·mol⁻¹ | Weight average MW |
| 3 | M̄v = [ Σ NᵢMᵢ⁽¹⁺ᵃ⁾ / Σ NᵢMᵢ ]^(1/a) | g·mol⁻¹ | Viscosity average MW |
| 4 | M̄z = Σ NᵢMᵢ³ / Σ NᵢMᵢ² | g·mol⁻¹ | Z-average MW |
| 5 | PDI = M̄w / M̄n | dimensionless | Polydispersity Index |
| 6 | [η] = K · Mᵃ | dL·g⁻¹ | Mark–Houwink equation |
| 7 | wᵢ = NᵢMᵢ / Σ NᵢMᵢ | dimensionless | Weight fraction |
| 8 | M̄q = Σ NᵢMᵢᵍ / Σ NᵢMᵢᵍ⁻¹ | g·mol⁻¹ | General *q*-th moment |

**Boundary Conditions:**
- For monodisperse polymer: M̄ₙ = M̄w = M̄z = M → PDI = 1
- For polydisperse polymer: M̄ₙ < M̄w < M̄z → PDI > 1
- PDI ≥ 1 always (equality only for monodisperse)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **M̄ₙ** governs melt flow behavior (zero-shear viscosity η₀ ∝ M̄w³·⁴ for M̄w > Mc, but extrusion die swell depends on M̄ₙ distribution).
- **M̄w** governs mechanical strength, impact resistance, and tensile modulus in the solid state.
- **PDI** dictates processability: low PDI → narrow processing window; high PDI → broad, forgiving processing window.
- **M̄v** is the workhorse for routine QC in polymer industries because viscometry is fast, cheap, and requires only mg-scale samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Examiner Tip: Always state the *technique* alongside the average. Saying "M̄w is 200,000" without context loses marks. Say: "M̄w = 200,000 as determined by static light scattering."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of M̄ₙ from First Principles

**Given:** A polymer sample divided into fractions. Fraction *i* contains Nᵢ molecules each of molecular weight Mᵢ.

**Total mass of fraction *i*:**
$$w_i = N_i \cdot M_i$$

**Total mass of the entire sample:**
$$W = \sum_i w_i = \sum_i N_i M_i$$

**Total number of molecules in the entire sample:**
$$N = \sum_i N_i$$

**Definition of average mass per molecule (= number average):**
$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{W}{N} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$

✅ **Derivation complete.**

## 3.2 Derivation of M̄w from First Principles

**Conceptual Idea:** In weight averaging, each molecule contributes to the average in proportion to *its own mass*. We want an average of the form:

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i (\text{contribution of fraction } i)}{\text{sum of weighting factors}}$$

The "weight" assigned to each molecule in fraction *i* is itself Mᵢ (the molecular weight). So the contribution of fraction *i* is:

$$\text{Contribution}_i = N_i \cdot M_i \cdot M_i = N_i M_i^2$$

The sum of all weighting factors (= total mass):
$$\text{Total weight} = \sum_i N_i M_i$$

Therefore:
$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

**Alternative form using weight fraction wᵢfrac:**
Since $w_i^{\text{frac}} = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$, we have:

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum_i \left(\frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}\right) M_i}{1} = \sum_i w_i^{\text{frac}} \, M_i$$

✅ **Derivation complete.**

## 3.3 Derivation of M̄v from the Mark–Houwink Equation

**Given:** Intrinsic viscosity [η] is related to molecular weight by:

$$[\eta] = K M^a$$

For a polydisperse polymer, the intrinsic viscosity is the weight-fraction-weighted sum:

$$[\eta] = \sum_i w_i^{\text{frac}} \, [\eta]_i = \sum_i w_i^{\text{frac}} \cdot K M_i^a$$

**Substituting the Mark–Houwink equation for each fraction:**

$$[\eta] = K \sum_i w_i^{\text{frac}} M_i^a = K \sum_i \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j} M_i^a$$

$$[\eta] = K \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$$

**If we now identify [η]/K as a hypothetical molecular weight Mᵥᵃ:**

$$\frac{[\eta]}{K} = \bar{M}_v^{\,a} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

**Taking the (1/a)-th root of both sides:**

$$\bar{M}_v = \left[\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/a}$$

✅ **Derivation complete.**

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

A polymer sample contains three fractions:

| Fraction (i) | Number of molecules (Nᵢ) | Molecular weight (Mᵢ, g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 50 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 10 | 100,000 |

### Step 1: Compute M̄ₙ

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{(100)(10{,}000) + (50)(50{,}000) + (10)(100{,}000)}{100 + 50 + 10}$$

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000}{160} = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{160}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = 28{,}125 \text{ g/mol}}$$

### Step 2: Compute M̄w

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{(100)(10{,}000)^2 + (50)(50{,}000)^2 + (10)(100{,}000)^2}{4{,}500{,}000}$$

Numerator:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= 100 \times 10^8 + 50 \times 25 \times 10^8 + 10 \times 100 \times 10^8 \\
&= 10^{10} + 125 \times 10^{8} + 10^{10} \\
&= 1 \times 10^{10} + 1.25 \times 10^{10} + 1 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 3.25 \times 10^{10}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{3.25 \times 10^{10}}{4.5 \times 10^6} = 7{,}222.22 \approx 7{,}222 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 3: Compute M̄v (assume a = 0.6)

$$\bar{M}_v^{1.6} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1.6}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{1.6} = 100 \cdot (10{,}000)^{1.6} + 50 \cdot (50{,}000)^{1.6} + 10 \cdot (100{,}000)^{1.6}$$

Computing each term (using log arithmetic: 10000^1.6 = 10^4.8 = 6.31 × 10⁴):

$$\begin{aligned}
100 \cdot 6.31 \times 10^4 &= 6.31 \times 10^6 \\
50 \cdot (5 \times 10^4)^{1.6} &= 50 \cdot 5^{1.6} \times 10^{6.4} \\
&= 50 \cdot 12.62 \times 2.51 \times 10^6 \\
&\approx 50 \cdot 31.7 \times 10^6 = 1.585 \times 10^9 \\
10 \cdot (10^5)^{1.6} &= 10 \cdot 10^8 = 10^9 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{1.6} \approx 6.31 \times 10^6 + 1.585 \times 10^9 + 10^9 \approx 2.591 \times 10^9$$

$$\bar{M}_v^{1.6} = \frac{2.591 \times 10^9}{4.5 \times 10^6} = 575.8$$

$$\bar{M}_v = (575.8)^{1/1.6} = (575.8)^{0.625}$$

Computing: $\ln(575.8) = 6.356$, then $0.625 \times 6.356 = 3.973$, so $e^{3.973} \approx 53.1$

Wait — re-checking computation. Let me redo (10⁵)^1.6: 10^(5×1.6) = 10^8. Yes. So 10 × 10⁸ = 10⁹. And (5×10⁴)^1.6 = 5^1.6 × 10^6.4. 5^1.6 = exp(1.6 × ln 5) = exp(1.6 × 1.609) = exp(2.575) = 13.13. So 50 × 13.13 × 2.512 × 10⁶ = 50 × 32.98 × 10⁶ = 1.649 × 10⁹. And (10⁴)^1.6 = 10^6.4 = 2.512 × 10⁶, so 100 × 2.512 × 10⁶ = 2.512 × 10⁸.

$$\sum = 2.512 \times 10^8 + 1.649 \times 10^9 + 1.0 \times 10^9 = 1.9 \times 10^9$$

$$\bar{M}_v^{1.6} = \frac{1.9 \times 10^9}{4.5 \times 10^6} = 422.2$$

$$\bar{M}_v = 422.2^{0.625}$$

ln(422.2) = 6.045; 0.625 × 6.045 = 3.778; e^3.778 = 43.7. Hmm — but answer should be in range of M̄ₙ = 28,125. Let me reconsider — I made an arithmetic error. Let me carefully recompute.

Actually, (10,000)^1.6 — let me redo: 10,000 = 10^4; (10^4)^1.6 = 10^6.4 ≈ 2.51 × 10⁶. So 100 × 2.51 × 10⁶ = 2.51 × 10⁸.

(50,000)^1.6 = (5 × 10⁴)^1.6 = 5^1.6 × 10^6.4 = 13.13 × 2.51 × 10⁶ = 32.96 × 10⁶ = 3.30 × 10⁷. So 50 × 3.30 × 10⁷ = 1.65 × 10⁹.

(100,000)^1.6 = 10^8. So 10 × 10⁸ = 10⁹.

Sum = 0.251 × 10⁹ + 1.65 × 10⁹ + 1.0 × 10⁹ = 2.901 × 10⁹.

$$\bar{M}_v^{1.6} = \frac{2.901 \times 10^9}{4.5 \times 10^6} = 644.7$$

ln(644.7) = 6.469; 0.625 × 6.469 = 4.043; e^4.043 = 56.95.

So M̄v = 56.95 — but this is wrong by orders of magnitude. The issue is that my units of Nᵢ × Mᵢ^1.6 give a quantity with units g^1.6/mol^1.6 × (dimensionless), and dividing by ΣNᵢMᵢ which has units g/mol. So the ratio has units (g/mol)^0.6, then the 1/a root gives units (g/mol)^(0.6/1.6) = (g/mol)^0.375. That doesn't give g/mol.

I think my formulation is slightly off. Let me reconsider. The standard form should give Mᵥ in g/mol. The corrected definition is:

$$\bar{M}_v = \left(\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right)^{1/a}$$

When we divide (g/mol)^(1+a) / (g/mol), we get (g/mol)^a, and the 1/a root gives (g/mol). So mathematically it's consistent. But the numerical answer should be ~ 28,000–50,000 range.

Let me redo more carefully:
- NᵢMᵢ: (100)(10,000) = 10⁶; (50)(50,000) = 2.5×10⁶; (10)(100,000) = 10⁶. Sum = 4.5×10⁶.
- NᵢMᵢ^1.6: (100)(10⁴)^1.6 = (100)(10^6.4) = 100 × 2.5119 × 10⁶ = 2.512 × 10⁸
- (50)(5×10⁴)^1.6 = 50 × 5^1.6 × 10^6.4 = 50 × 13.13 × 2.5119 × 10⁶ = 1648.5 × 10⁶ = 1.649 × 10⁹
- (10)(10⁵)^1.6 = 10 × 10^8 = 10⁹
- Sum = 0.2512 × 10⁹ + 1.649 × 10⁹ + 1.0 × 10⁹ = 2.900 × 10⁹

$$\bar{M}_v^{1.6} = \frac{2.900 \times 10^9}{4.5 \times 10^6} = 644.4$$

So M̄v^1.6 = 644.4 g/mol. This has units of g/mol (since ΣNᵢMᵢ^(1+a) / ΣNᵢMᵢ = M^(1+a)/M = M^a, so units g/mol). Yes 644.4 has units g/mol. Then:

$$\bar{M}_v = (644.4)^{1/1.6} = (644.4)^{0.625}$$

Let me compute this properly:
log₁₀(644.4) = 2.809
0.625 × 2.809 = 1.756
10^1.756 = 57.0

So M̄v = 57 g/mol. That's WAY off. What's wrong?

Oh! The issue is the molecular weights need to be expressed in g/mol consistently, AND the formula assumes Mᵢ in consistent units. Let me check — Mᵢ = 10,000 g/mol = 10⁴ g/mol. Mᵢ^1.6 = (10⁴)^1.6 = 10^6.4 (g/mol)^1.6. The formula has this divided by Mᵢ = 10⁴ g/mol, giving (g/mol)^0.6. Then taking 1/a = 1/0.6 = 1.667 root... wait no, the formula is:

$$\bar{M}_v = \left[\frac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i}\right]^{1/a}$$

So inside bracket is (g/mol)^a = (g/mol)^0.6. Then 1/0.6 = 1.667 root... no wait, 1/a = 1/0.6 = 1.667. So we're taking (g/mol)^0.6 and raising to 1/a = 1/0.6. Hmm but the problem said a = 0.6. So 1/a = 1/0.6 = 1.667. Then [644.4 (g/mol)^0.6]^(1.667) = 644.4^1.667 × (g/mol).

log₁₀(644.4) = 2.809
1.667 × 2.809 = 4.694
10^4.694 = 49,400 g/mol.

That's more like it! So I had the reciprocal. The correct value: M̄v ≈ 49,400 g/mol. ✓

This lies between M̄ₙ = 28,125 and M̄w = 57,222 — perfect, consistent with M̄ₙ ≤ M̄v ≤ M̄w. ✅

### Step 4: Compute PDI

$$PDI = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{57{,}222}{28{,}125} = 2.03$$

This confirms the polymer is moderately polydisperse.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Value | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| M̄ₙ | 28,125 g/mol | Lowest |
| M̄v (a=0.6) | ~49,400 g/mol | Middle |
| M̄w | 57,222 g/mol | Highest |
| PDI | 2.03 | Polydisperse |

✅ **Validation:** 28,125 ≤ 49,400 ≤ 57,222 ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Polymer Molecular Weight Distribution and Averaging

The following diagram illustrates the entire "Concept of Averaging" workflow — from a polydisperse polymer sample, through its distribution function, to the derivation of different averages, and finally to their experimental measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["POLYMER SAMPLE\nPolydisperse Mixture"] --> B["FRACTIONATION\nSize Exclusion Chromatography"]
    B --> C["MOLECULAR WEIGHT DISTRIBUTION\nHistogram Ni vs Mi"]
    C --> D["STATISTICAL MOMENTS\nq-th Order Moment"]
    D --> E1["q = 1\nNUMBER AVERAGE Mn"]
    D --> E2["q = 2\nWEIGHT AVERAGE Mw"]
    D --> E3["q = 3\nZ AVERAGE Mz"]
    D --> E4["General q\nMq"]
    E1 --> F1["EXPERIMENTAL METHOD\nOsmometry, End Group Analysis"]
    E2 --> F2["EXPERIMENTAL METHOD\nStatic Light Scattering"]
    E3 --> F3["EXPERIMENTAL METHOD\nSedimentation Ultracentrifugation"]
    F1 --> G["VALIDATION\nMn less than Mw less than Mz"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["POLYDISPERSITY INDEX\nPDI equals Mw divided by Mn"]
    H --> I["MATERIAL PROPERTY\nMECHANICAL AND RHEOLOGICAL"]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Inter-Relationship of Averages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MO["Molecular Weight Distribution"]
        MWD["Ni molecules of mass Mi"]
    end
    subgraph AVG["Computed Averages"]
        MN["Mn equals Sum Ni Mi divided by Sum Ni"]
        MW["Mw equals Sum Ni Mi squared divided by Sum Ni Mi"]
        MZ["Mz equals Sum Ni Mi cubed divided by Sum Ni Mi squared"]
        MV["Mv from Mark Houwink Equation"]
    end
    subgraph TECH["Measurement Techniques"]
        T1["Osmometry"]
        T2["Light Scattering"]
        T3["Ultracentrifugation"]
        T4["Viscometry"]
    end
    MWD --> MN
    MWD --> MW
    MWD --> MZ
    MWD --> MV
    MN --> T1
    MW --> T2
    MZ --> T3
    MV --> T4
    T1 --> OUT["Polymer Characterization Report"]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Ordering of Molecular Weight Averages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Mn\nNumber Average\nLowest"] --> B["Mv\nViscosity Average\nBetween Mn and Mw"]
    B --> C["Mw\nWeight Average\nThird Moment"]
    C --> D["Mz\nZ Average\nHighest"]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style B fill:#e6f2ff
    style C fill:#99ccff
    style D fill:#6699cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As we move from Mn → Mw → Mz, the higher moments progressively amplify the contribution of long chains. The PDI (Mw/Mn) measures the *spread* of this distribution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Number Average Molecular Weight (M̄ₙ) and Weight Average Molecular Weight (M̄w). State ONE experimental technique to determine each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Number Average Molecular Weight (M̄ₙ):** It is defined as the total mass of all polymer molecules divided by the total number of molecules in the sample.
$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$
- **Weight Average Molecular Weight (M̄w):** It is defined as the weighted average in which each molecule contributes in proportion to its molecular mass.
$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$
- **M̄ₙ measurement:** Membrane osmometry / vapor pressure osmometry.
- **M̄w measurement:** Static light scattering.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the Polydispersity Index (PDI)? Mention its significance and the value for a monodisperse polymer.**

**Model Answer:**
PDI is defined as the ratio of weight-average to number-average molecular weight:
$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$$
- **Significance:** It quantifies the breadth of the molecular weight distribution. Higher PDI indicates a broader distribution and hence greater heterogeneity.
- **For a monodisperse polymer** (all chains identical, e.g., proteins): **PDI = 1.0**.
- For synthetic polymers, PDI typically ranges from 1.01 (anionic polymerization) to 20+ (free-radical polymerization).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Define and derive the expressions for Number Average (M̄ₙ) and Weight Average (M̄w) molecular weights for a polydisperse polymer. Mention the experimental techniques used to determine each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample consists of three fractions as given below. Calculate M̄ₙ, M̄w, and the Polydispersity Index. Comment on the polydispersity of the sample. **(7 marks)**

| Fraction | Nᵢ | Mᵢ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 20,000 |
| 2 | 80 | 60,000 |
| 3 | 20 | 1,00,000 |

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition of M̄ₙ:** It is the ratio of total mass of all polymer molecules to the total number of molecules.

**Derivation:**
Total mass = Σ NᵢMᵢ; Total number = Σ Nᵢ
$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$

**Definition of M̄w:** It is the average in which each molecule is weighted by its own mass.

**Derivation:** Weighting factor = Mᵢ; Contribution of fraction i = NᵢMᵢ · Mᵢ = NᵢMᵢ²
$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

**Techniques:**
- **M̄ₙ →** Membrane osmometry, vapor pressure osmometry, cryoscopy, end-group analysis.
- **M̄w →** Static light scattering, sedimentation-diffusion.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute ΣNᵢ**
$$\sum N_i = 200 + 80 + 20 = 300$$

**Step 2: Compute ΣNᵢMᵢ**

$$\sum N_i M_i = (200)(20{,}000) + (80)(60{,}000) + (20)(1{,}00{,}000)$$
$$= 4{,}000{,}000 + 4{,}800{,}000 + 2{,}000{,}000 = 1.08 \times 10^7$$

**Step 3: M̄ₙ**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1.08 \times 10^7}{300} = 36{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4: Compute ΣNᵢMᵢ²**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum N_i M_i^2 &= (200)(20{,}000)^2 + (80)(60{,}000)^2 + (20)(1{,}00{,}000)^2 \\
&= 200 \times 4 \times 10^8 + 80 \times 36 \times 10^8 + 20 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 8 \times 10^{10} + 28.8 \times 10^{10} + 2 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 38.8 \times 10^{10} = 3.88 \times 10^{11}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: M̄w**

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{3.88 \times 10^{11}}{1.08 \times 10^7} = 35{,}926 \text{ g/mol}$$

Wait — this gives M̄w < M̄n which is impossible. Let me recompute.

(200)(20,000)² = 200 × 400,000,000 = 80,000,000,000 = 8 × 10¹⁰ ✓
(80)(60,000)² = 80 × 3,600,000,000 = 288,000,000,000 = 2.88 × 10¹¹
(20)(1,00,000)² = 20 × 10,000,000,000 = 2 × 10¹¹

Sum = 0.8 × 10¹¹ + 2.88 × 10¹¹ + 2 × 10¹¹ = 5.68 × 10¹¹

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{5.68 \times 10^{11}}{1.08 \times 10^7} = 52{,}593 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 6: PDI**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{52{,}593}{36{,}000} = 1.46$$

**Comment:** PDI = 1.46 > 1 confirms the polymer is polydisperse. PDI is moderately low — consistent with a well-controlled polymerization (e.g., anionic polymerization gives PDI ~ 1.05–1.2; free-radical gives PDI ~ 1.5–2.0). This sample shows free-radical-type polydispersity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** of Mᵢ (must be consistent — g/mol throughout).
> 2. **Inverting the formula**: writing M̄ₙ = ΣNᵢ / ΣNᵢMᵢ. This is the most common error. KTU valuators deduct 2 marks per wrong formula.
> 3. **Arithmetic overflow** in ΣNᵢMᵢ² — always express in scientific notation.
> 4. **Not verifying M̄ₙ ≤ M̄w**: If you get M̄w < M̄ₙ, you've made an arithmetic error; re-check.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of Z-average and Viscosity-average molecular weights. Derive the expression for M̄v using the Mark–Houwink equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two monodisperse polymer samples, A and B, with molecular weights 50,000 and 200,000 g/mol respectively, are blended in the molar ratio 3:1. Calculate M̄ₙ and M̄w of the blend. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Z-Average Molecular Weight (M̄z):** It is the third-moment average, biased even more strongly toward high-molecular-weight chains than M̄w. It is defined as:
$$\bar{M}_z = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^3}{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}$$
- Determined experimentally by sedimentation equilibrium / ultracentrifugation.
- Useful for probing the high-MW tail of the distribution.

**Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (M̄v):** It is the molecular weight determined from intrinsic viscosity measurements using the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation: [η] = K · Mᵃ. It lies between M̄ₙ and M̄w.

**Derivation of M̄v:**
For a polydisperse sample, the intrinsic viscosity is the weight-fraction-weighted average of the intrinsic viscosities of individual fractions:
$$[\eta] = \sum_i w_i^{\text{frac}} [\eta]_i$$

Substituting [η]ᵢ = K · Mᵢᵃ:

$$[\eta] = K \sum_i w_i^{\text{frac}} M_i^a = K \cdot \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

Defining [η]/K = M̄vᵃ:

$$\bar{M}_v^a = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

Taking the (1/a)-th root:

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_v = \left[\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/a}}$$

This is the standard KTU form.

**Model Solution (b):**

Let sample A have M_A = 50,000; sample B have M_B = 200,000.
Molar ratio A : B = 3 : 1, so N_A = 3, N_B = 1 (in arbitrary units).

**Step 1: M̄ₙ calculation**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{N_A M_A + N_B M_B}{N_A + N_B} = \frac{(3)(50{,}000) + (1)(200{,}000)}{3 + 1}$$

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{150{,}000 + 200{,}000}{4} = \frac{350{,}000}{4} = 87{,}500 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2: M̄w calculation**

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{N_A M_A^2 + N_B M_B^2}{N_A M_A + N_B M_B}$$

Numerator:
$$(3)(50{,}000)^2 + (1)(200{,}000)^2 = 3 \times 2.5 \times 10^9 + 4 \times 10^{10}$$
$$= 7.5 \times 10^9 + 40 \times 10^9 = 47.5 \times 10^9$$

Denominator:
$$N_A M_A + N_B M_B = 350{,}000$$

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{47.5 \times 10^9}{3.5 \times 10^5} = 1.357 \times 10^5 = 135{,}714 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3: PDI**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{135{,}714}{87{,}500} = 1.55$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Question B:**
> 1. **Mixing up Nᵢ ratios**: When given "molar ratio 3:1," that means *number* of molecules, NOT weight. Many students incorrectly use mass ratios.
> 2. **Forgetting to derive M̄v**: Part (a) explicitly asks for derivation. Just stating the formula without derivation loses 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the exponent 1/a** in the final M̄v formula. Always show the root clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):**

- ✅ **M̄ₙ formula:** Σ NᵢMᵢ / Σ Nᵢ — measured by **osmometry** (colligative property).
- ✅ **M̄w formula:** Σ NᵢMᵢ² / Σ NᵢMᵢ — measured by **static light scattering**.
- ✅ **M̄z formula:** Σ NᵢMᵢ³ / Σ NᵢMᵢ² — measured by **ultracentrifugation**.
- ✅ **M̄v formula:** [Σ NᵢMᵢ⁽¹⁺ᵃ⁾ / Σ NᵢMᵢ]^(1/a) — measured by **viscometry** + Mark–Houwink.
- ✅ **Order:** M̄ₙ ≤ M̄v ≤ M̄w ≤ M̄z (always, for any polydisperse sample).
- ✅ **PDI = M̄w/M̄n.** PDI = 1 → monodisperse; PDI > 1 → polydisperse.
- ✅ **Mark–Houwink equation:** [η] = K · Mᵃ. K and *a* depend on polymer-solvent-temperature. Typical *a*: 0.5 (theta) to 1.0 (good solvent).
- ✅ **M̄w is biased toward heavy chains** (squared term); M̄ₙ is unbiased (linear term).
- ✅ **General q-th moment:** M̄q = Σ NᵢMᵢᵍ / Σ NᵢMᵢᵍ⁻¹.
- ✅ **Weight fraction:** wᵢ = NᵢMᵢ / Σ NᵢMᵢ.
- ✅ Always **state the technique** alongside the reported molecular weight.
- ✅ For the same sample, **viscometry gives M̄v only when Mark–Houwink parameters are known**; otherwise [η] is just a hydrodynamic quantity.
- ✅ **PDI ≈ 1.0** for anionic / living polymerizations; **PDI ≈ 1.5–2.0** for free-radical; **PDI ≈ 2.0+** for polycondensation (Flory distribution).
- ✅ **Higher moments amplify tail behavior**: small fractions of very long chains disproportionately raise M̄z.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** In numerical problems, *always* use scientific notation to handle NᵢMᵢ² terms — they grow rapidly and decimal-point errors are the #1 reason students lose marks despite knowing the formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Averages in Polymer Molecular Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Averages in Polymer Molecular Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In polymer science, a **synthetic polymer sample is never composed of macromolecules of a single, uniform molecular weight.** Unlike small molecules (e.g., water, ethanol) which have a fixed molar mass, a typical polymerisation reaction produces chains of varying lengths. Therefore, every polymer sample is characterised by a **molecular weight distribution (MWD)**, which describes the statistical spread of molar masses among the constituent chains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The *molecular weight averages* are a set of statistical mean values (specifically, moments of the distribution) that mathematically summarise the molecular weight distribution of a polydisperse polymer. The four primary averages are: **Number Average (Mn)**, **Weight Average (Mw)**, **Z-Average (Mz)**, and **Viscosity-Average (Mv)**.

The different averages are **not numerically equal** because each one weighs molecules according to a different physical property (number, mass, size, or hydrodynamic volume). For a perfectly monodisperse sample (all chains identical), all four averages would converge to a single value.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Classroom of Students"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Classroom Heights):**
> Imagine a classroom of **100 students** of varying heights between 150 cm and 185 cm.
> - The **Number Average Height** is what you get if you simply sum everyone's height and divide by 100. Every student counts *equally* (count = 1 each).
> - The **Weight Average Height** is what you get if you sum everyone's *weight × height* and divide by the total weight. Now a *heavier* student (say, a tall football player) contributes more to the average than a lighter student. The "weighting factor" makes tall, heavy students pull the average upward.
> - The **Z-Average Height** weights the contribution by an even higher power — analogous to the cube of the height — so the tallest few students dominate the result almost completely.
> - The **Viscosity Average Height** is a special, experimentally measured value (via solution viscosity) that lies between Mn and Mw, biased by the *effective volume* the student occupies in flowing through a narrow corridor.

In a polymer, the *students* are chains, the *heights* are molecular weights, the *weights* are molecular masses, and the *narrow corridor* is the viscometer capillary. The analogy makes clear why **Mw ≥ Mn ≥ Mv ≥ Mz ordering** is generally observed (with appropriate caveats).

## 1.3 Why Polymers Are Always Polydisperse

| Cause of Polydispersity | Mechanism |
|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Statistical (Step-Growth)** | Random coupling of monomers — Poisson-like spread of chain lengths |
| **Kinetic (Chain-Growth)** | Simultaneous chain initiation, propagation, and termination — broad MWD |
| **Chain Transfer Reactions** | Growing radical transfers to solvent/chain — broadens distribution |
| **Termination by Combination** | Couples two live chains — doubles molecular weight, broadens MWD |
| **Living Polymerisation** | Only narrow MWD polymers (e.g., anionic, RAFT) — but never truly monodisperse |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Bold

The two most reported single-value descriptors in any KTU question or technical datasheet are:

- **Mn — Number Average Molecular Weight (g/mol)**
- **Mw — Weight Average Molecular Weight (g/mol)**
- **PDI = Mw / Mn — Polydispersity Index (dimensionless)**
- **Đ (pronounced "D-stroke")** — modern IUPAC symbol for PDI (dimensionless)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Molecular Weight Averages — Mathematical Foundations

Let us define the polymer sample as having **Ni molecules of molar mass Mi**, where **i** runs from **1 to N** (number of distinct chain lengths).

### 2.1.1 Number Average Molecular Weight (Mn)

Mn is the **first moment** of the number-fraction distribution. It is obtained experimentally by techniques that count the *number* of molecules regardless of their size — such as **osmometry, end-group analysis, and vapour-phase osmometry**.

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \, M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i}
$$

**Intuition (Why "Number" Average?):** Every molecule — small or large — contributes *one vote*. The total mass of the sample is divided by the *total number of molecules*. Hence small molecules dominate because there are many more of them.

### 2.1.2 Weight Average Molecular Weight (Mw)

Mw is the **first moment of the weight-fraction distribution**, also expressed as the **second moment of the number-fraction distribution**. It is obtained experimentally by **static light scattering (SLS)**, which is sensitive to mass × concentration of solute.

$$
\overline{M}_w \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \, M_i^{2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \, M_i}
$$

**Intuition (Why "Weight" Average?):** Each molecule now contributes *Mi votes*. A chain of mass 100,000 contributes 100,000 times more than a chain of mass 1,000. Hence large molecules dominate.

### 2.1.3 Z-Average Molecular Weight (Mz)

Mz is the **second moment of the weight-fraction distribution** (or the third moment of the number distribution). It is obtained from **sedimentation equilibrium (ultracentrifugation)** measurements and is **extremely sensitive to high-molecular-weight tails**.

$$
\overline{M}_z \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \, M_i^{3}}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \, M_i^{2}}
$$

**Intuition (Why Z?):** Each molecule contributes *Mi²* votes. A chain 10× larger contributes 100× more. The Z-average is essentially "dominated by the heaviest chains in the sample."

### 2.1.4 Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (Mv)

Mv is determined from **intrinsic viscosity [η] measurements** using the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) equation**:

$$
[\eta] \;=\; K \, M_v^{a}
$$

where **K** and **a** are Mark–Houwink constants (dependent on polymer–solvent pair and temperature), and **a** typically lies between **0.5 and 0.8** for flexible coils in good solvents.

$$
\overline{M}_v \;=\; \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \, M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i \, M_i} \right)^{1/a}
$$

**Why this form?** Because intrinsic viscosity scales roughly as M^a, the experimentally determined "M" is really a weighted moment of order 1 + a. Since **0.5 ≤ a ≤ 1**, the Mv always lies between **Mn (a = 0 limit)** and **Mw (a = 1 limit)**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table for KTU Board Examination (2024 Scheme)**

| Average | Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Experimental Technique | Physical Property Measured |
|---------|--------|------------------------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Number Average | Mn | (Σ Ni Mi) / (Σ Ni) | Osmometry, VPO, End-group titration | Number of molecules |
| Weight Average | Mw | (Σ Ni Mi²) / (Σ Ni Mi) | Static Light Scattering (SLS) | Mass × concentration |
| Z-Average | Mz | (Σ Ni Mi³) / (Σ Ni Mi²) | Sedimentation (Ultracentrifuge) | Heaviest chain dominance |
| Viscosity Average | Mv | [ (Σ Ni Mi^(1+a)) / (Σ Ni Mi) ]^(1/a) | Capillary Viscometer | Hydrodynamic volume |

### 2.2.1 Polydispersity Index (PDI) — Master Equation

$$
\text{PDI} \;=\; \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} \;=\; Đ
$$

| PDI Value | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **PDI = 1.0** | Monodisperse (theoretical limit, approached by living polymerisation) |
| **1.0 < PDI < 1.5** | Narrow distribution (RAFT, ATRP, anionic) |
| **1.5 ≤ PDI ≤ 2.0** | Typical free-radical polymerisation |
| **PDI > 2.0** | Broad distribution (auto-acceleration, branching, step-growth at high conversion) |

### 2.2.2 Inequalities (Critical for KTU Proof Questions)

For any real, polydisperse sample:

$$
\overline{M}_z \;\geq\; \overline{M}_w \;\geq\; \overline{M}_v \;\geq\; \overline{M}_n
$$

This inequality is *strict* unless the sample is truly monodisperse (where all averages become identical).

### 2.2.3 Discrete Number-Fraction Form (Most Common in KTU Problems)

If **xi = Ni / Σ Ni** is the number fraction, then:

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \sum x_i M_i
$$

$$
\overline{M}_w \;=\; \sum w_i M_i \;=\; \frac{\sum x_i M_i^{2}}{\sum x_i M_i}
$$

where **wi = xi Mi / Σ xi Mi** is the weight fraction.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mn** controls **melt viscosity at low shear**, **tensile strength minima**, and **end-group concentration** (critical for reactive formulations, adhesives, and PU pre-polymers).
- **Mw** dominates **melt viscosity (zero-shear η₀)**, **tensile strength maxima**, and **impact resistance** (relevant in injection-moulding grade selection).
- **Mz** governs **shear-thinning behaviour, melt fracture onset, and die-swell** (critical for extrusion die design).
- **Mv** is the **quality-control average** in industrial polymerisation reactors — fast, cheap, capillary viscometer-based measurement.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number Average Molecular Weight (Mn) from a Counted Population

### Step 1: Define the Sample Composition

Consider a polymer sample containing **N₁ molecules of molar mass M₁**, **N₂ molecules of molar mass M₂**, …, **Ni molecules of molar mass Mi**. The total number of molecules is:

$$
N_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Total Mass of the Sample

The total mass of the polymer is the sum of the masses of all chains:

$$
m_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \, M_i
$$

*Conversion Logic:* Each of the **Ni** molecules of mass **Mi** contributes **Ni × Mi** to the total sample mass. Summing across all size populations gives the total mass.

### Step 3: Apply the Definition of the Number Average

The **number average molecular weight** is, by definition, the total mass divided by the total number of molecules:

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{total}}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Finalise

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \, M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* This is the **first moment** of the number distribution, weighted equally per molecule. Note that because **Mi** is in g/mol, the resulting Mn has units of g/mol.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weight Average Molecular Weight (Mw) from Light Scattering Principle

### Step 1: Recall Rayleigh's Light Scattering Law

For a dilute polymer solution, the **excess Rayleigh ratio (Rθ)** is related to the polymer concentration **c** and molecular weight by:

$$
\frac{Kc}{R_{\theta}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\overline{M}_w} \, P(\theta)^{-1} + 2 A_2 c
$$

where **K** is an optical constant, **P(θ)** is the form factor (angular dependence), and **A₂** is the second virial coefficient. In the limit **c → 0** and **θ → 0**, the equation reduces to:

$$
\left( \frac{Kc}{R_{\theta}} \right)_{c \to 0, \, \theta \to 0} \;=\; \frac{1}{\overline{M}_w}
$$

### Step 2: Why Mw, Not Mn, Emerges from Light Scattering

The Rayleigh scattering intensity of a single molecule is **proportional to the square of its mass** (because the polarisability scales with the number of scattering electrons, which scales with mass). Therefore, a chain of mass **Mi** contributes scattering proportional to **Mi²**.

The **total scattered intensity** from a sample is the sum of contributions from all molecules:

$$
I_{\text{total}} \;=\; k \sum_{i} N_i \, M_i^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Define the "Average" That Light Scattering Measures

The molecular weight obtained by light scattering is defined as:

$$
\overline{M}_{\text{LS}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i} N_i \, M_i^{2}}{\sum_{i} N_i \, M_i}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* The numerator is the *total scattering intensity* (proportional to Σ Ni Mi²). The denominator is the *total mass concentration* (proportional to Σ Ni Mi). The ratio gives the **second moment over the first moment** — i.e., **Mw**.

### Step 4: State the Result

$$
\boxed{\overline{M}_w \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \, M_i^{2}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \, M_i}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Mn ≤ Mw (Schwarz Inequality Proof)

This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU proof question**.

### Step 1: State the Goal

We wish to prove that for any distribution with at least two distinct values:

$$
\overline{M}_w \;\geq\; \overline{M}_n
$$

with equality **only if all Mi are equal** (monodisperse).

### Step 2: Define the Variance of the Distribution

The **variance** of the molar masses in the sample is:

$$
\sigma^{2} \;=\; \overline{M_n \bigl(M - \overline{M}_n \bigr)^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{N_{\text{tot}}}
$$

More explicitly, in terms of the number averages:

$$
\sigma^{2} \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i} \;-\; \left( \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} \right)^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Recognise the Two Terms

The first term in Step 2 is **Mw** (by definition, but missing the Mi in the denominator — let us rewrite more carefully):

The identity relating the second moment, the mean, and the variance is:

$$
\overline{M^{2}}_n \;=\; \overline{M}_n^{2} \;+\; \sigma^{2}
$$

where the **overbar with M²** denotes the *unweighted* second moment, which is **not** Mw. We must therefore proceed via the **Schwarz inequality** directly.

### Step 4: Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality

By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**, for any two real vectors **aᵢ = √(Ni Mi)** and **bᵢ = √(Ni Mi) × (Mi/√Mi)**:

$$
\left( \sum_i a_i b_i \right)^{2} \;\leq\; \left( \sum_i a_i^{2} \right) \left( \sum_i b_i^{2} \right)
$$

Choose **aᵢ = √(Ni Mi)** and **bᵢ = √(Ni) × Mi**. Then:

$$
\left( \sum_i N_i M_i \right)^{2} \;\leq\; \left( \sum_i N_i M_i \right) \left( \sum_i N_i M_i^{2} \right)
$$

### Step 5: Cancel the Common Factor

Dividing both sides by **(Σ Ni Mi)²**:

$$
1 \;\leq\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

### Step 6: Divide by Σ Ni to Recover Mn

Divide numerator and denominator on the right by **Σ Ni**:

$$
1 \;\leq\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2} / \sum_i N_i}{\sum_i N_i M_i / \sum_i N_i} \;=\; \frac{\overline{M}_w \cdot (\text{factor})}{\overline{M}_n \cdot (\text{factor})}
$$

A more direct rearrangement of Step 5 gives:

$$
\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \;\geq\; 1
$$

Multiply both sides by **Σ Ni Mi / Σ Ni**:

$$
\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i} \;\geq\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}
$$

### Step 7: Recognise the Final Result

$$
\boxed{\overline{M}_w \;\geq\; \overline{M}_n}
$$

**Equality condition:** Cauchy–Schwarz becomes equality **only when aᵢ ∝ bᵢ**, i.e., when all Mi are equal — meaning the sample is **monodisperse**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A polymer sample consists of three monodisperse fractions:
> - 5 g of polymer with M = 10,000 g/mol
> - 3 g of polymer with M = 50,000 g/mol
> - 2 g of polymer with M = 100,000 g/mol
>
> Calculate **Mn, Mw, and PDI**.

### Step 1: Convert Masses to Moles (Number of Molecules in Each Fraction)

$$
N_1 \;=\; \frac{5}{10{,}000} \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}
$$

$$
N_2 \;=\; \frac{3}{50{,}000} \;=\; 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
$$

$$
N_3 \;=\; \frac{2}{100{,}000} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Σ Ni and Σ Ni Mi

$$
\sum N_i \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{-4} + 6.0 \times 10^{-5} + 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 5.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}
$$

$$
\sum N_i M_i \;=\; (5.0 \times 10^{-4})(10{,}000) + (6.0 \times 10^{-5})(50{,}000) + (2.0 \times 10^{-5})(100{,}000)
$$

$$
\sum N_i M_i \;=\; 5.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 \;=\; 10.0 \text{ g}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Mn

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} \;=\; \frac{10.0 \text{ g}}{5.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}} \;=\; 17{,}241 \text{ g/mol}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Σ Ni Mi²

$$
\sum N_i M_i^{2} \;=\; (5.0 \times 10^{-4})(10{,}000)^{2} + (6.0 \times 10^{-5})(50{,}000)^{2} + (2.0 \times 10^{-5})(100{,}000)^{2}
$$

$$
\sum N_i M_i^{2} \;=\; 5.0 \times 10^{4} + 1.5 \times 10^{5} + 2.0 \times 10^{5} \;=\; 4.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ g}^{2}/\text{mol}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Mw

$$
\overline{M}_w \;=\; \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i} \;=\; \frac{4.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ g}^{2}/\text{mol}}{10.0 \text{ g}} \;=\; 40{,}000 \text{ g/mol}
$$

### Step 6: Compute PDI

$$
\text{PDI} \;=\; \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} \;=\; \frac{40{,}000}{17{,}241} \;=\; 2.32
$$

### Step 7: Interpretation

PDI = 2.32 indicates a **broad molecular weight distribution**, typical of step-growth polymerisation near the gel point or free-radical polymerisation with chain transfer. The Mw is ~2.3× higher than Mn because the small fraction of very high-M chains (100,000 g/mol) dominates Mw but barely affects Mn.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Measurement Techniques vs Averages

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the four molecular-weight averages to their principal experimental techniques and weighting physics. This is a **functional architecture view** rather than a physical drawing, ensuring Mermaid compatibility.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Sample Polydisperse MWD] --> B[Define Number Fractions Ni and Masses Mi]
    B --> C[Apply First Moment Number Average]
    B --> D[Apply Second Moment Weight Average]
    B --> E[Apply Third Moment Z Average]
    B --> F[Apply Exponent 1 plus a Moment Viscosity Average]
    C --> C1[Technique Osmometry VPO End Group Analysis]
    C --> C2[Property Measured Number of Molecules]
    C --> C3[Output Mn grams per mol]
    D --> D1[Technique Static Light Scattering SLS]
    D --> D2[Property Measured Mass times Concentration]
    D --> D3[Output Mw grams per mol]
    E --> E1[Technique Sedimentation Equilibrium Ultracentrifuge]
    E --> E2[Property Measured Heaviest Chain Dominance]
    E --> E3[Output Mz grams per mol]
    F --> F1[Technique Capillary Viscometer]
    F --> F2[Property Measured Hydrodynamic Volume]
    F --> F3[Output Mv grams per mol]
    C3 --> G[Compute PDI equals Mw over Mn]
    D3 --> G
    G --> H[Quality Control Single Value Descriptor]
    H --> I[Engineering Decision Moulding Grade Selection]
    H --> J[Engineering Decision Extrusion Die Design]
    H --> K[Engineering Decision Adhesive Formulation]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** A polydisperse polymer sample (A) is partitioned into populations (B). Each population is fed into one of four parallel mathematical moments (C, D, E, F). The moment is realised experimentally by a specific technique (C1, D1, E1, F1), which measures a specific physical property (C2, D2, E2, F2), yielding one of the four averages (C3, D3, E3, F3). Two of these (Mn and Mw) feed into the PDI calculation (G), which is the single-value descriptor (H) used in three major engineering decisions (I, J, K).

## 4.2 Order-of-Magnitude Flow — Inequalities Among Averages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Lowest Mn] --> N2[Next Mv]
    N2 --> N3[Next Mw]
    N3 --> N4[Highest Mz]
    N1 -. Equality only for monodisperse sample .-> N4
```

**Interpretation:** In a polydisperse sample, the averages form a strict ascending ladder: **Mn ≤ Mv ≤ Mw ≤ Mz**. The dotted arrow shows that the entire ladder collapses to a single point only for a perfectly monodisperse reference standard (e.g., a protein or a narrow polystyrene standard from anionic polymerisation).

## 4.3 Mark–Houwink–Sakurada Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Intrinsic Viscosity eta] --> P2[Mark Houwink Equation eta equals K Mv to the power a]
    P2 --> P3[Constants K and a depend on Polymer and Solvent Pair]
    P2 --> P4[Exponent a between 0.5 and 0.8 for flexible coils in good solvents]
    P2 --> P5[a equals 0 gives Mn limit]
    P2 --> P6[a equals 1 gives Mw limit]
    P3 --> P7[Reference Lookup Table in Polymer Handbook]
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P8[Selected a gives Mv value]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
```

**Interpretation:** The Mark–Houwink equation links the easily-measured intrinsic viscosity [η] to a molecular weight Mv that is bracketed between Mn and Mw depending on the exponent a. The exponent a itself encodes information about chain conformation in solution: a ≈ 0.5 for a Θ-solvent (ideal coil), a ≈ 0.8 for a good solvent (swollen coil), and a = 0 for a rigid rod.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]

**Q: Define the Number Average Molecular Weight (Mn) and Weight Average Molecular Weight (Mw) of a polymer. Mention one experimental technique used to determine each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Number Average Molecular Weight (Mn):** It is the total mass of all polymer molecules in a sample divided by the total number of molecules. Mathematically,

  $$
  \overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}
  $$

  It is determined by **membrane osmometry (or vapour pressure osmometry / end-group analysis)**. **[1 mark]**

- **Weight Average Molecular Weight (Mw):** It is the second-moment average, in which each molecule is weighted by its mass. Mathematically,

  $$
  \overline{M}_w \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
  $$

  It is determined by **static light scattering (SLS)**. **[1 mark]**

- **Distinction:** Mn counts every molecule equally, while Mw gives more weight to heavier molecules. Hence **Mw ≥ Mn** for any polydisperse sample, with equality only for monodisperse systems. **[1 mark]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

**Q: What is the Polydispersity Index (PDI)? What does PDI = 1 indicate and what does a PDI of 2 suggest about a polymer sample?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** The Polydispersity Index (PDI), also denoted **Đ**, is the ratio of the weight average to the number average molecular weight:

  $$
  \text{PDI} \;=\; Đ \;=\; \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}
  $$

  It is a **dimensionless** number that quantifies the breadth of the molecular weight distribution. **[1 mark]**

- **PDI = 1:** This indicates a **monodisperse** sample — all polymer chains have the **same** molecular weight. This is a theoretical limit approached only by **living polymerisations** (anionic, RAFT, ATRP) under ideal conditions. **[1 mark]**

- **PDI ≈ 2:** This suggests a **broad molecular weight distribution**, typical of conventional **free-radical polymerisation** or **step-growth polymerisation** at high conversion. The sample contains chains spanning a wide range of molar masses. **[1 mark]**

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]

**Q: (a)** Define Number Average (Mn), Weight Average (Mw), Z-Average (Mz), and Viscosity-Average (Mv) molecular weights. Write down their mathematical expressions and state the experimental technique used for each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A polymer sample contains three fractions: 0.40 g of M = 20,000 g/mol, 0.35 g of M = 50,000 g/mol, and 0.25 g of M = 80,000 g/mol. Calculate Mn, Mw, and the Polydispersity Index (PDI). **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**[1 mark per definition + 0.5 mark per technique, 0.5 mark per equation]**

**1. Number Average Molecular Weight (Mn):** It is the first moment of the number-fraction molecular weight distribution, giving equal statistical weight to every chain. It is measured by **osmometry** (or vapour pressure osmometry, or end-group analysis).

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}
$$

**2. Weight Average Molecular Weight (Mw):** It is the second moment of the number distribution (or first moment of the weight distribution), giving more statistical weight to heavier chains. It is measured by **static light scattering (SLS)**.

$$
\overline{M}_w \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

**3. Z-Average Molecular Weight (Mz):** It is the third moment of the number distribution, extremely sensitive to the highest-molecular-weight chains. It is measured by **sedimentation equilibrium in an analytical ultracentrifuge**.

$$
\overline{M}_z \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}
$$

**4. Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (Mv):** It is determined from intrinsic viscosity measurements via the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation** [η] = K Mᵥᵃ, where K and a are polymer–solvent–temperature dependent constants.

$$
\overline{M}_v \;=\; \left[ \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \right]^{1/a}
$$

The general inequality for any polydisperse sample is:

$$
\overline{M}_n \;\leq\; \overline{M}_v \;\leq\; \overline{M}_w \;\leq\; \overline{M}_z
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Calculate the number of moles (Ni) in each fraction**

$$
N_1 \;=\; \frac{0.40}{20{,}000} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
$$

$$
N_2 \;=\; \frac{0.35}{50{,}000} \;=\; 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol}
$$

$$
N_3 \;=\; \frac{0.25}{80{,}000} \;=\; 3.125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 2: Compute Σ Ni and Σ Ni Mi**

$$
\sum N_i \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-5} + 7.0 \times 10^{-6} + 3.125 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 3.0125 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
$$

$$
\sum N_i M_i \;=\; (2.0 \times 10^{-5})(20{,}000) + (7.0 \times 10^{-6})(50{,}000) + (3.125 \times 10^{-6})(80{,}000)
$$

$$
\sum N_i M_i \;=\; 0.400 + 0.350 + 0.250 \;=\; 1.000 \text{ g}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 3: Calculate Mn**

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{1.000 \text{ g}}{3.0125 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}} \;=\; 33{,}195 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 4: Compute Σ Ni Mi²**

$$
\sum N_i M_i^{2} \;=\; (2.0 \times 10^{-5})(20{,}000)^{2} + (7.0 \times 10^{-6})(50{,}000)^{2} + (3.125 \times 10^{-6})(80{,}000)^{2}
$$

$$
\sum N_i M_i^{2} \;=\; 8{,}000 + 17{,}500 + 20{,}000 \;=\; 45{,}500 \text{ g}^{2}/\text{mol}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 5: Calculate Mw**

$$
\overline{M}_w \;=\; \frac{45{,}500 \text{ g}^{2}/\text{mol}}{1.000 \text{ g}} \;=\; 45{,}500 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 6: Calculate PDI**

$$
\text{PDI} \;=\; \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n} \;=\; \frac{45{,}500}{33{,}195} \;=\; 1.371
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 7: Final answer with units and interpretation**

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; 33{,}195 \text{ g/mol}, \quad \overline{M}_w \;=\; 45{,}500 \text{ g/mol}, \quad \text{PDI} \;=\; 1.37
$$

The PDI of ~1.37 indicates a **moderately narrow molecular weight distribution**, possibly produced by a **controlled/"living" radical polymerisation** (e.g., RAFT or ATRP). **[1 mark]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

**Q: (a)** With the help of the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, prove that for any polydisperse polymer sample, **Mw ≥ Mn**. State the condition under which equality holds. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the **Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (Mv)**. Derive its expression from the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation** and discuss how its value compares to Mn and Mw. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Setup:** Let the sample contain **Ni** molecules of molar mass **Mi** for i = 1, 2, …, k. The two averages are:

$$
\overline{M}_n \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} \quad \text{and} \quad \overline{M}_w \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 2 — State Cauchy–Schwarz:** For any two real sequences {aᵢ} and {bᵢ}:

$$
\left( \sum_i a_i b_i \right)^{2} \;\leq\; \left( \sum_i a_i^{2} \right) \left( \sum_i b_i^{2} \right)
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Choose aᵢ and bᵢ:** Set **aᵢ = √(Ni Mi)** and **bᵢ = √(Ni) × Mi / √(Mi) × √(Mi) = √(Ni) Mi**. Equivalently, take **aᵢ = √(Ni Mi)** and **bᵢ = √(Ni Mi)·Mi**. Then:

- aᵢ bᵢ = Ni Mi
- aᵢ² = Ni Mi
- bᵢ² = Ni Mi² × (Mi/Mi) → ni Mi²

Substituting into Cauchy–Schwarz:

$$
\left( \sum_i N_i M_i \right)^{2} \;\leq\; \left( \sum_i N_i M_i \right) \left( \sum_i N_i M_i^{2} \right)
$$

**[2 marks]**

**Step 4 — Simplify:** Dividing both sides by (Σ Ni Mi)²:

$$
1 \;\leq\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

Multiplying both sides by Σ Ni Mi / Σ Ni:

$$
\overline{M}_n \;\leq\; \overline{M}_w
$$

**Hence proved.** **[1 mark]**

**Step 5 — Equality condition:** Cauchy–Schwarz becomes equality **only when aᵢ = λ bᵢ** for some constant λ. This requires **Mi = constant** for all i, i.e., the sample is **monodisperse**. **[1 mark]**

**Step 6 — Concluding remark:** Physically, Mn counts molecules equally (favours the abundant low-M chains) while Mw gives each molecule a weight proportional to its mass (favours the rare high-M chains). The inequality is therefore intuitive: the more a statistic emphasises heavy molecules, the larger the resulting average must be. **[1 mark]**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Concept:** The Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (Mv) is a *weighted* average obtained experimentally from the **intrinsic viscosity [η]** of a dilute polymer solution. It is *not* a true moment of the distribution but a Mark–Houwink-weighted moment lying between Mn and Mw. **[1 mark]**

**Step 2 — The Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) Equation:**

$$
[\eta] \;=\; K \, M_v^{a}
$$

where K and a are empirical constants that depend on the polymer, solvent, and temperature. The exponent a lies between **0.5** (Θ-solvent, ideal coil) and **0.8** (good solvent, swollen coil) for flexible polymers. **[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Intrinsic Viscosity for a Polydisperse Sample:** For a polydisperse sample, the measured intrinsic viscosity is a weight-average of the individual intrinsic viscosities:

$$
[\eta] \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i \, [\eta]_i}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

Substituting the MHS relation for each fraction:

$$
[\eta] \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i \cdot K M_i^{a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \;=\; K \, \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

**[2 marks]**

**Step 4 — Equate to the MHS-Form for the Whole Sample:** The same MHS equation applied to the *bulk* sample gives:

$$
[\eta] \;=\; K \, M_v^{a}
$$

Setting the two expressions equal:

$$
K \, M_v^{a} \;=\; K \, \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

Cancelling K:

$$
M_v^{a} \;=\; \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

Taking the (1/a)th power:

$$
\boxed{M_v \;=\; \left[ \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \right]^{1/a}}
$$

**[2 marks]**

**Step 5 — Comparison with Mn and Mw:** Since **0 ≤ a ≤ 1**, the exponent **(1 + a)** lies between **1 and 2**. Therefore Mv is a *moment of order between 1 and 2*:

$$
\overline{M}_n \;\leq\; \overline{M}_v \;\leq\; \overline{M}_w
$$

Specifically:
- If **a = 0** (rigid rod, no mass dependence), Mv → Mn.
- If **a = 1** (free-draining limit), Mv → Mw.

For typical flexible-coil polymers in good solvents (a ≈ 0.7), **Mv is close to Mw but still strictly less than it**. **[1 mark]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing the denominator in Mw:** Students often write Mw = (Σ Ni Mi²)/(Σ Ni) — this is WRONG and loses 2 marks. The correct denominator is **(Σ Ni Mi)**, not (Σ Ni). This is because light scattering is mass-sensitive, not count-sensitive.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Mn/Mw numericals:** Always state the answer with **g/mol**. Missing units = 0.5 mark deducted.
> 3. **Confusing Mv with Mn in the MHS equation:** Writing Mv = K M^a without showing the derivation from the polydisperse viscosity summation loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Inverting PDI definition:** PDI = Mw/Mn, **not** Mn/Mw. This is a 1-mark error per occurrence.
> 5. **Forgetting the equality condition in inequality proofs:** Cauchy–Schwarz is an equality **only** for monodisperse samples. Stating the inequality without the equality condition loses 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorise Before Exam**

- **Mn** = first moment of number distribution = total mass / total molecules = **osmometry**.
- **Mw** = second moment of number distribution = mass-weighted average = **static light scattering**.
- **Mz** = third moment of number distribution = ultra-heavy-chain-sensitive = **ultracentrifuge**.
- **Mv** = Mark–Houwink-weighted moment of order (1+a) = **capillary viscometer**.
- **PDI = Mw / Mn** = dimensionless descriptor of distribution breadth.
- **PDI = 1** → monodisperse (living polymerisation); **PDI > 1** → polydisperse.
- **Inequality chain:** **Mn ≤ Mv ≤ Mw ≤ Mz** for any real, polydisperse sample.
- **Proof technique for Mw ≥ Mn:** Cauchy–Schwarz inequality on vectors √(Ni Mi) and √(Ni)·Mi.
- **MHS equation:** [η] = K Mᵥᵃ with 0.5 ≤ a ≤ 0.8 for flexible coils.
- **Mv limits:** a = 0 → Mv → Mn; a = 1 → Mv → Mw.
- **Experimental choice mnemonic — "O.L.S.U.V" for M-n, M-w, M-z, M-v:**
  - **O**smometry → **M_n** (counting)
  - **L**ight Scattering → **M_w** (mass²-weighting)
  - **S**edimentation → **M_z** (mass³-weighting)
  - **V**iscosity → **M_v** (mass^1+a-weighting)
- **In numericals:** always convert mass-given data to **moles** (Ni) before applying the formulas; verify Σ Ni Mi = total sample mass as a sanity check.
- **KTU Hot Topics:** (1) Derivation of Mn ≤ Mw via Cauchy–Schwarz, (2) Mv derivation from MHS, (3) numerical problems with 2–3 fractions, (4) technique-to-average matching, (5) PDI interpretation in polymerisation mechanism identification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Number Average
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Number Average Molecular Weight (Mn)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$)** of a polymer is the **statistical mean molecular weight** of a polymer sample, calculated by summing the products of the number of molecules in each molecular weight fraction and their respective molecular weights, divided by the total number of polymer molecules in the sample. Mathematically, it is the **first moment of the number-distribution function** of molecular weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Number Average Molecular Weight is the total weight of all polymer molecules in a sample divided by the total number of polymer molecules present. It is sensitive to the number of molecules and is heavily influenced by the presence of low molecular weight species (oligomers, monomers).*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Height" Intuition

Imagine a classroom of **100 students** with varying heights. Suppose:
- **60 students** are 150 cm tall
- **30 students** are 165 cm tall
- **10 students** are 180 cm tall

The **Number Average Height** would be:

$$\bar{H}_n = \frac{(60 \times 150) + (30 \times 165) + (10 \times 180)}{60 + 30 + 10}$$

In the **same way**, for a polymer sample, each "molecule" is a "student", and each "molecular weight" is a "height". The number average gives **more weight (in terms of count) to the smaller molecules**, because there are typically *more* of them in a polymer distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> $\bar{M}_n$ treats every single polymer chain as **one unit** — whether it is a tiny trimer of 300 g/mol or a giant macromolecule of 300,000 g/mol. Each chain gets **equal statistical voting rights** (1 vote per molecule). This is the *fundamental conceptual difference* between $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{M}_w$ (Weight Average), which is the topic of the next section.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Unit:** $\text{g/mol}$ (grams per mole) or equivalently **Daltons (Da)**
- **Typical Range for Polymers:** $10^3$ to $10^7$ $\text{g/mol}$
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** 25 °C (for solution-based measurements)
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ (used in osmotic pressure equations)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Think **"N = Number" → Count-based average → Favors small chains.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The "Why" and "How" Behind Number Average Molecular Weight

#### Step 1 — Why Do We Need an Average at All?

Synthetic polymers are **polydisperse** — meaning a single sample contains polymer chains of **many different lengths** (and hence molecular weights). Unlike pure compounds (e.g., water = 18 g/mol exactly), a polymer sample has a *distribution* of molecular weights. We need a single representative value → hence the **average**.

#### Step 2 — The Statistical Foundation

Consider a polymer sample fractionated into $i$ discrete fractions:
- $N_1$ molecules have molecular weight $M_1$
- $N_2$ molecules have molecular weight $M_2$
- ... and so on up to $N_i$ molecules of weight $M_i$

The **total mass** of all molecules is:

$$W_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i M_i$$

The **total number of molecules** is:

$$N_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i$$

#### Step 3 — Defining $\bar{M}_n$

By definition (mass ÷ number of entities):

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{W_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$

#### Step 4 — Experimental Determination Methods

| S.No. | Method | Principle | Molecular Weight Range |
|:-----:|:-------|:----------|:----------------------:|
| 1 | **Vapor Pressure Osmometry (VPO)** | Measures ΔT due to vapor pressure lowering | $10^3$ – $4 \times 10^4$ g/mol |
| 2 | **Membrane Osmometry** | Measures osmotic pressure ($\pi$) | $2 \times 10^4$ – $10^6$ g/mol |
| 3 | **End-Group Analysis (Titration/Spectroscopy)** | Counts chemically distinct chain ends | $10^3$ – $5 \times 10^4$ g/mol |
| 4 | **Cryoscopy / Ebullioscopy** | Measures freezing point depression / boiling point elevation | $< 10^4$ g/mol (rare for polymers) |
| 5 | **Number-based mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF)** | Counts individual molecular ions | Up to $\sim 10^5$ g/mol |

#### Step 5 — Why $\bar{M}_n$ is the **Colligative Property Average**
