All methods that measure **colligative properties** (those depending on the *number* of particles) yield $\bar{M}_n$. This is because:
- Osmotic pressure depends on **moles of solute**, not their size
- Vapor pressure lowering depends on **mole fraction**
- Freezing point depression depends on **molality**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> *"Why is osmotic pressure measurement used to determine $\bar{M}_n$ and not $\bar{M}_w$?"*
> **Model Answer:** Osmotic pressure is a **colligative property** that depends on the **number of molecules** (moles) in solution, not on their mass. Hence, it yields the **number average** molecular weight, $\bar{M}_n$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|:-:|:------------------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$ | Fundamental definition of $\bar{M}_n$ | g/mol |
| 2 | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \left( \dfrac{N_i}{N_{total}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{M_i} \right)}$ | Reciprocal form using number fraction | g/mol |
| 3 | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum_i w_i}{\sum_i \left( w_i / M_i \right)}$ | Expressed in terms of weight fraction $w_i$ | g/mol |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 c$ | Osmotic pressure (van 't Hoff) equation | — |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{\bar{M}_n}\left[1 + \Gamma_1 c + \Gamma_2 c^2 + \cdots \right]$ | Virial form for osmotic pressure | — |
| 6 | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{RT \cdot c}{\pi}\bigg\vert_{c \to 0}$ | Limiting form as $c \to 0$ | g/mol |
| 7 | $DP_n = \dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$ | Number average degree of polymerization | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\sum N_i = N_{total}$ | Conservation of molecule count | — |
| 9 | $\sum x_i = 1$ | Number fractions must sum to unity | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_w$ | Fundamental inequality (monodisperse case: equality) | g/mol |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In Formula #4, the intercept at $c = 0$ is $\dfrac{RT}{\bar{M}_n}$, **NOT** $\dfrac{RT}{\bar{M}_w}$. A $\dfrac{\pi}{c}$ vs $c$ plot extrapolated to zero concentration gives $\bar{M}_n$ directly. KTU examiners *specifically* test this intercept interpretation.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control in Plastic Manufacturing:** $\bar{M}_n$ correlates with **melt flow index (MFI)**, which determines processability in injection molding and extrusion.
- **Adhesive & Coating Industry:** Lower $\bar{M}_n$ → better wetting, lower viscosity → optimized for spray/brush application.
- **Biomedical Polymers:** $\bar{M}_n$ affects **drug release kinetics** in polymer-drug conjugates and **renal clearance** thresholds.
- **Rubber Compounding:** $\bar{M}_n$ influences **green strength** (tensile strength of unvulcanized rubber).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 3.1 — From Fundamental Definition to Practical Form

**Starting Point:** A polymer sample consists of $N_{total}$ molecules, fractionated into $i$ discrete groups.

**Step 1 — Mass of fraction $i$:**

$$w_i = N_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 2 — Total mass of the entire sample:**

$$W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i$$

*Explanation:* Summing the mass of every fraction gives the total sample mass.

**Step 3 — Total number of molecules:**

$$N_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i$$

*Explanation:* Each fraction contributes its count to the total molecule pool.

**Step 4 — Applying the definition of "average molecular weight" (mass per molecule):**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{W_{total}}{N_{total}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i}$$

This is the **canonical, board-exam-ready form** of $\bar{M}_n$.

**Step 6 — Expressing in terms of number fraction $x_i = N_i / N_{total}$:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i M_i \quad (\text{since } \sum x_i = 1)$$

*Explanation:* Because number fractions sum to unity, the denominator becomes 1, giving a simple weighted average.

**Step 7 — Expressing in terms of weight fraction $w_i$:**

Since $w_i = N_i M_i / W_{total}$, it follows that $N_i = w_i W_{total} / M_i$. Substituting:

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i \left(\dfrac{w_i W_{total}}{M_i}\right) M_i}{\sum_i \left(\dfrac{w_i W_{total}}{M_i}\right)} = \frac{W_{total} \sum_i w_i}{W_{total} \sum_i \left(w_i / M_i\right)} = \frac{\sum_i w_i}{\sum_i \left(w_i / M_i\right)} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \left(w_i / M_i\right)}$$

(since $\sum w_i = 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The weight-fraction form is what is often used in lab analysis because **balances measure mass (weight), not number of molecules.**

---

### Worked Numerical Example 3.1 — Basic Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A polymer sample is separated into 3 fractions with the following data:

| Fraction | Number of molecules $N_i$ (mol) | Molecular weight $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|:--------:|:------------------------------:|:------------------------------:|
| 1 | 2 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 5 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 3 | 100,000 |

Calculate $\bar{M}_n$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $\sum N_i M_i$:**

$$N_1 M_1 = 2 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}000 \text{ g}$$

$$N_2 M_2 = 5 \times 50{,}000 = 250{,}000 \text{ g}$$

$$N_3 M_3 = 3 \times 100{,}000 = 300{,}000 \text{ g}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i = 20{,}000 + 250{,}000 + 300{,}000 = 570{,}000 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\sum N_i$:**

$$\sum N_i = 2 + 5 + 3 = 10 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{570{,}000 \text{ g}}{10 \text{ mol}}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = 57{,}000 \text{ g/mol}}$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example 3.2 — Osmometry-Based Determination

**Problem Statement:** The osmotic pressure of a polystyrene solution in toluene is measured at 27 °C (300 K). At concentration $c = 0.002$ g/cm³, $\pi = 0.024$ atm. At $c = 0.006$ g/cm³, $\pi = 0.080$ atm. Calculate $\bar{M}_n$ using the virial equation:

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 c$$

**Given:** $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K); $T = 300$ K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $\pi/c$ for both concentrations:**

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{c}\right)_1 = \frac{0.024}{0.002} = 12.0 \text{ atm·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{c}\right)_2 = \frac{0.080}{0.006} = 13.333 \text{ atm·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Set up two equations:**

For $c_1 = 0.002$:

$$12.0 = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 (0.002) \quad \cdots (1)$$

For $c_2 = 0.006$:

$$13.333 = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 (0.006) \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 3 — Subtract (1) from (2):**

$$13.333 - 12.0 = A_2 (0.006 - 0.002)$$

$$1.333 = A_2 (0.004)$$

$$A_2 = 333.33 \text{ atm·cm}^6/\text{g}^2$$

**Step 4 — Back-substitute into (1) to find $RT/\bar{M}_n$:**

$$12.0 = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + 333.33 \times 0.002$$

$$12.0 = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + 0.6667$$

$$\frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} = 11.333 \text{ atm·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $\bar{M}_n$:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{RT}{11.333} = \frac{0.0821 \times 300}{11.333}$$

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{24.63}{11.333}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n \approx 2.17 \text{ g/mol·cm}^3 \cdots \text{(unit check needed)}}$$

**Step 6 — Unit Reconciliation (critical KTU step):**

Since $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K) = 82.1 cm³·atm/(mol·K):

$$\frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} = 11.333 \text{ atm·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{82.1 \times 300}{11.333} = \frac{24{,}630}{11.333} \approx 2{,}173 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n \approx 2.17 \times 10^3 \text{ g/mol}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap:** The biggest mistake in osmometry problems is **ignoring unit conversion** between L and cm³. KTU examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks for this. Always state: *"1 L = 1000 cm³"* explicitly in your solution.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis

| Scenario | Effect on $\bar{M}_n$ | Reason |
|:---------|:----------------------|:-------|
| Add **many small molecules** (e.g., residual monomer) | **Decreases $\bar{M}_n$ drastically** | Small molecules inflate the count $\sum N_i$ without contributing much to mass |
| Add **few very large molecules** | **Barely changes $\bar{M}_n$** | They contribute large mass but only to $\sum N_i M_i$, and there are few of them |
| Monodisperse sample (all chains same length) | $\bar{M}_n = \bar{M}_w = M$ | All averages collapse to a single value |
| Bimodal distribution with equal peaks | Skewed toward lower peak | Number-average favors the lower-MW peak |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"$N$ is for Numbers — $\bar{M}_n$ sees the small guys first!"*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 4.1 — Functional Architecture of $\bar{M}_n$ Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Sample Polydisperse] --> B[Solution Preparation Dissolve in Solvent]
    B --> C{Selection of Method}
    C --> D1[Vapor Pressure Osmometry VPO]
    C --> D2[Membrane Osmometry]
    C --> D3[End-Group Analysis]
    D1 --> E[Measure Delta T]
    D2 --> F[Measure Osmotic Pressure pi]
    D3 --> G[Count Functional End Groups]
    E --> H[Apply Colligative Relation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Calculate Mn from Intercept]
    I --> J[Report Number Average Molecular Weight]
    J --> K[Quality Control and Processability Decision]
```

### Diagram 4.2 — Conceptual Comparison: $\bar{M}_n$ vs. $\bar{M}_w$ Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Polymer Sample]
        S1[Chain 1 MW 1000]
        S2[Chain 2 MW 10000]
        S3[Chain 3 MW 100000]
    end
    Input --> O1{Number Average Mn}
    Input --> O2{Weight Average Mw}
    O1 --> R1[Each Chain Counts 1 Vote Favors Small Chains]
    O2 --> R2[Each Chain's Mass Counts 1 Vote Favors Large Chains]
    R1 --> F1[Mn approx 3710 g/mol]
    R2 --> F2[Mw approx 90000 g/mol]
```

### Diagram 4.3 — Distribution Skewness Visualization (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Polydisperse Polymer Population] --> Q[High Count of Short Chains]
    P --> R[Low Count of Long Chains]
    Q --> S[Number Distribution N vs M Skewed Right]
    R --> S
    S --> T[Number Average Mn Shifted Left Lower Value]
    T --> U[Weight Average Mw Shifted Right Higher Value]
    U --> V[Polydispersity Index PDI Mw by Mn greater than 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams follow KTU's preferred **block-level functional architecture** convention. They map the *process flow* and *logical dependencies* rather than attempting physical drawings, which is the standard recommended approach in the KTU 2024 Scheme for engineering schematics in the absence of specialized drawing tools.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$). Mention any two methods for its determination.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$) is defined as the total weight of all polymer molecules in a sample divided by the total number of polymer molecules in the sample. Mathematically:
>
> $$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$
>
> where $N_i$ is the number of molecules having molecular weight $M_i$.

> **Methods (1 Mark — any two):**
> 1. **Vapor Pressure Osmometry (VPO)**
> 2. **Membrane Osmometry**
> 3. **End-Group Analysis**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Colligative property measurements yield $\bar{M}_n$ and not $\bar{M}_w$." Justify this statement in 2–3 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Colligative properties such as osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering, and boiling point elevation depend only on the **number of solute particles** (moles) in the solution, and not on their individual masses. Since $\bar{M}_n$ is the **number-based** statistical average, any method that counts particles (e.g., by measuring colligative effects) directly yields $\bar{M}_n$. In contrast, $\bar{M}_w$ is mass-based and requires techniques like light scattering.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Number Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$)** of a polydisperse polymer sample, starting from first principles. Express the result in terms of (i) number of molecules, and (ii) number fraction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample is fractionated into **four fractions** with the following data:

| Fraction | Mass $w_i$ (g) | Molecular Weight $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|:--------:|:--------------:|:------------------------------:|
| 1 | 10 | 5,000 |
| 2 | 25 | 20,000 |
| 3 | 40 | 50,000 |
| 4 | 25 | 1,00,000 |

Calculate:
(i) The Number Average Molecular Weight $\bar{M}_n$
(ii) The Number Average Degree of Polymerization $DP_n$ (given $M_0 = 100$ g/mol for the repeat unit) **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Consider a polydisperse polymer sample having $i$ distinct molecular weight fractions. Let the $i^{th}$ fraction contain $N_i$ molecules, each of molecular weight $M_i$.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** The total mass $W_{total}$ of all polymer molecules in the sample is:

$$W_{total} = \sum_{i} w_i = \sum_{i} N_i M_i$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** The total number of molecules is:

$$N_{total} = \sum_i N_i$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** By definition, the average molecular weight per molecule is:

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{W_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Defining the **number fraction** $x_i = \dfrac{N_i}{N_{total}}$, and since $\sum_i x_i = 1$:

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} = \sum_i x_i M_i$$

This is the expression in terms of **number fraction**.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Compute $N_i = w_i / M_i$ for each fraction:**

$$N_1 = \frac{10}{5{,}000} = 0.002 \text{ mol}$$

$$N_2 = \frac{25}{20{,}000} = 0.00125 \text{ mol}$$

$$N_3 = \frac{40}{50{,}000} = 0.0008 \text{ mol}$$

$$N_4 = \frac{25}{1{,}00{,}000} = 0.00025 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Sum up $N_i$:**

$$\sum_i N_i = 0.002 + 0.00125 + 0.0008 + 0.00025 = 0.0043 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Compute $\sum_i w_i$ (total mass):**

$$\sum_i w_i = 10 + 25 + 40 + 25 = 100 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Apply the weight-fraction form (since data is given in mass):**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_i w_i}{\sum_i (w_i / M_i)} = \frac{100}{0.002 + 0.00125 + 0.0008 + 0.00025} = \frac{100}{0.0043}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n \approx 23{,}256 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Compute $DP_n$:**

$$DP_n = \frac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0} = \frac{23{,}256}{100} = 232.56$$

$$\boxed{DP_n \approx 233}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified] — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the principle and procedure of **Membrane Osmometry** for the determination of $\bar{M}_n$. Derive the working equation and discuss the method of evaluating $\bar{M}_n$ graphically. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The osmotic pressures of a poly(vinyl chloride) solution in cyclohexanone at 30 °C are:

| Concentration $c$ (g/dL) | Osmotic Pressure $\pi$ (cm of solvent) |
|:------------------------:|:-------------------------------------:|
| 0.20 | 0.28 |
| 0.40 | 0.71 |
| 0.60 | 1.50 |
| 0.80 | 2.55 |

The density of cyclohexanone is 0.96 g/cm³. Calculate $\bar{M}_n$ using the reduced osmotic pressure equation:

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 c$$

Given: $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K), 1 atm = 76 cm Hg = $76 \times 13.6$ cm of solvent ≈ 1033.6 cm of solvent (since $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6$ g/cm³). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Membrane Osmometry Principle (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 Marks):** A semipermeable membrane allows **solvent molecules** to pass through but blocks **polymer molecules**. This creates an osmotic pressure $\pi$ on the polymer side, which is measured using a precision capillary. The relationship between $\pi$ and concentration gives $\bar{M}_n$ via the van 't Hoff equation.

**Working Equation Derivation (3 Marks):** For a polymer solution, the osmotic pressure is given by the virial expansion:

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = RT \left[ \frac{1}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 c + A_3 c^2 + \cdots \right]$$

For dilute solutions, higher terms are negligible:

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = \frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + RT A_2 c$$

**Graphical Evaluation (2 Marks):** A plot of $\dfrac{\pi}{c}$ (y-axis) versus $c$ (x-axis) yields a **straight line** with:
- **Intercept (at $c = 0$):** $\dfrac{RT}{\bar{M}_n}$ → gives $\bar{M}_n$ directly
- **Slope:** $RT A_2$ → gives the second virial coefficient $A_2$

> **Procedure Outline:** Dissolve polymer in solvent → assemble osmometer with semipermeable membrane → equilibrate → measure equilibrium capillary height difference → convert to $\pi$ → plot and extrapolate.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Convert $\pi$ from cm of solvent to atm:**

$$1 \text{ atm} = 76 \text{ cm Hg} \times 13.6 \text{ (density ratio)} = 1033.6 \text{ cm of solvent}$$

(assuming density correction with $\rho_{solvent}/\rho_{Hg} = 0.96/13.6$, then $1$ atm $= 76 \times 13.6 / 0.96 = 1077.3$ cm of solvent)

Using exact: $1 \text{ atm} = 76 \times 13.6 / 0.96 = 1077.33$ cm of cyclohexanone.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Convert all $\pi$ values to atm:**

$$\pi_1 = \frac{0.28}{1077.33} = 2.60 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm}$$

$$\pi_2 = \frac{0.71}{1077.33} = 6.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm}$$

$$\pi_3 = \frac{1.50}{1077.33} = 13.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm}$$

$$\pi_4 = \frac{2.55}{1077.33} = 23.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Calculate $\pi/c$ for each:**

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{c}\right)_1 = \frac{2.60 \times 10^{-4}}{0.20} = 13.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·dL/g}$$

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{c}\right)_2 = \frac{6.59 \times 10^{-4}}{0.40} = 16.48 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·dL/g}$$

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{c}\right)_3 = \frac{13.92 \times 10^{-4}}{0.60} = 23.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·dL/g}$$

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{c}\right)_4 = \frac{23.67 \times 10^{-4}}{0.80} = 29.59 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·dL/g}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Linear regression of $\pi/c$ vs $c$:**

Using least squares or two-point analysis (taking points 1 and 4):

$$\text{Slope } m = \frac{29.59 - 13.0}{0.80 - 0.20} \times 10^{-4} = \frac{16.59}{0.60} \times 10^{-4} = 27.65 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·dL}^2/\text{g}^2$$

$$\text{Intercept } b = 13.0 \times 10^{-4} - 27.65 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.20 = 13.0 \times 10^{-4} - 5.53 \times 10^{-4} = 7.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·dL/g}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Calculate $\bar{M}_n$ from intercept:**

$$\frac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} = 7.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm·dL/g}$$

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{RT}{7.47 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Using $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K), $T = 303$ K, and 1 L = 10 dL:

$$RT = 0.0821 \times 303 \times 10 = 248.76 \text{ atm·dL/mol}$$

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{248.76}{7.47 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.33 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n \approx 3.33 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (-2 marks):** Forgetting to convert cm of solvent → atm using density correction is the #1 reason students lose marks.
> 2. **Wrong Intercept Interpretation (-1 mark):** Plotting $c/\pi$ instead of $\pi/c$ reverses the meaning of slope and intercept. Always plot $\pi/c$ on the y-axis.
> 3. **No Density Correction (-1 mark):** Osmotic pressure in cm of solvent *must* be converted using solvent density relative to mercury.
> 4. **Skipping "First Principles" (-1 mark):** When the question says "derive from first principles," you **must** start with $W_{total} = \sum N_i M_i$, not just write the formula directly.
> 5. **Wrong use of $M_0$ in $DP_n$ (-1 mark):** $M_0$ is the **repeat unit molecular weight**, not the monomer molecular weight. For polymers like PVC, the monomer is vinyl chloride (62.5 g/mol) but the repeat unit is —CH₂—CHCl— (62.5 g/mol), so they're the same. For nylon-6, the monomer is caprolactam (113 g/mol) but the repeat unit is —(CH₂)₅—CONH— (113 g/mol). Always confirm.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **$\bar{M}_n$ Definition:** Total mass ÷ total number of molecules
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} = \sum_i x_i M_i$
- ✅ **Weight-fraction form:** $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_i / M_i)}$
- ✅ **All colligative property methods yield $\bar{M}_n$** (VPO, membrane osmometry, end-group analysis)
- ✅ **Van 't Hoff equation:** $\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{\bar{M}_n} + A_2 c$ — intercept at $c=0$ gives $\bar{M}_n$
- ✅ **$\bar{M}_n$ is sensitive to LOW molecular weight species** (small chains have high count)
- ✅ **$\bar{M}_n \le \bar{M}_w$** always (equality only for monodisperse samples)
- ✅ **$DP_n = \bar{M}_n / M_0$** (number-average degree of polymerization)
- ✅ **Unit conversion traps:** cm of solvent → atm (use density ratio with mercury)
- ✅ **Graph:** Plot $\pi/c$ vs $c$ — extrapolate to $c=0$ to find intercept
- ✅ **VPO range:** $10^3$ to $4 \times 10^4$ g/mol; **Membrane Osmometry range:** $2 \times 10^4$ to $10^6$ g/mol
- ✅ **Significance in industry:** Controls melt viscosity, mechanical strength, and processability

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Average
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weight Average

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_w}$)**
> The weight average molecular weight (also called the weight-mean molecular weight) is the statistical average molecular weight of a polymer sample in which each molecular species $i$ is weighted according to its **mass contribution** $w_i$ to the total mass of the sample. It is mathematically defined as the second moment of the molecular weight distribution divided by the first moment.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i \, M_i = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

where:
- $N_i$ = number of molecules (moles) of species with molecular weight $M_i$
- $w_i = \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$ = **weight fraction** of species $i$
- $M_i$ = molecular weight of species $i$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Molecular Weight Averages):**
> Weight average molecular weight is the average that correlates with **bulk, mass-dependent properties** of polymers such as melt viscosity, light scattering intensity, sedimentation coefficient in an ultracentrifuge, and mechanical strength.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom of **100 students** standing on a weighing scale. The class has the following composition:

| Student Group | Number ($N_i$) | Mass per student ($M_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| Lighter students (Class 6 kids) | 70 | 30 kg |
| Heavier students (Class 12 teens) | 30 | 60 kg |

- **Number Average Weight** = Simple arithmetic mean, treating every student equally → $\dfrac{(70 \times 30) + (30 \times 60)}{100} = 39$ kg.
- **Weight Average Weight** = Each student is weighted by *how much they themselves contribute to the total weight on the scale*. A heavy student contributes more to the *total mass* and is therefore "counted more times" → $\dfrac{(70 \times 30 \times 30) + (30 \times 60 \times 60)}{(70 \times 30) + (30 \times 60)} = \dfrac{171000}{3900} \approx 43.85$ kg.

The weight average is always **greater than or equal to** the number average. The **heavier the heavy molecules**, the larger this gap becomes — this gap is the essence of **polydispersity**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants / Metrics

- **Unit of $\overline{M_w}$** : **g·mol⁻¹** (or Da, Daltons).
- **Threshold Property** : $\overline{M_w}$ is the molecular weight that determines **colligative-mass properties** (light scattering, sedimentation).
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** = $\dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$; for a perfectly uniform polymer (monodisperse), PDI = **1.0** (ideal value).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

**Step 1 — Why do we need a "weight" average at all?**
A polymer sample is almost never composed of molecules of identical chain length. It contains chains of $N_1, N_2, N_3, \dots$ molecules with molar masses $M_1, M_2, M_3, \dots$. Many physical properties (viscosity, light scattering, tensile strength) depend not on *how many* molecules exist, but on *how much mass* each species carries. Therefore, the heavier chains must be given extra statistical weight.

**Step 2 — Defining the weight fraction $w_i$**
The total mass of the sample is $m_{tot} = \sum_i N_i M_i$. The fraction of that total mass belonging to species $i$ is:

$$w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$$

**Step 3 — Building the weighted mean**
The weighted average is the sum of (weight fraction × molecular weight):

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i M_i = \sum_i \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j} \cdot M_i = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

**Step 4 — Alternative expressions**
- In terms of mole fraction $x_i = \dfrac{N_i}{\sum_j N_j}$ :

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_i x_i M_i^2}{\sum_i x_i M_i}$$

- In terms of weight fraction directly:

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i M_i \quad \text{(if } \sum w_i = 1\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Relationship with $\overline{M_n}$ (Number Average)**
Recall $\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$. Hence:

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \geq \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} = \overline{M_n}$$

This inequality is strictly obeyed (proven via Cauchy–Schwarz in Step 3 of Section 3). Equality holds **only for monodisperse systems**.

**Step 6 — Physical methods that measure $\overline{M_w}$**
- **Static (Classical) Light Scattering** — based on Rayleigh–Debye–Zimm formalism.
- **Sedimentation Velocity** in analytical ultracentrifugation.
- **Small-Angle Neutron Scattering (SANS)**.
- **Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight (MALDI-TOF)** (single-chain level, but yields full distribution).

These techniques all measure the **second moment** of the molecular weight distribution, hence yield $\overline{M_w}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All vertical bars written as `\vert` to protect Markdown table syntax. Do not confuse with absolute value bars in LaTeX expressions.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$ | Primary definition (second moment $\div$ first moment) |
| 2 | $\overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i M_i$ | Equivalent form using weight fractions |
| 3 | $w_i = \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$ | Weight fraction of species $i$ |
| 4 | $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ | Always true; equality only for monodisperse |
| 5 | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$ | Polydispersity Index; PDI = 1 (ideal), > 1 (real) |
| 6 | $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum_i m_i M_i}{\sum_i m_i}$ | Mass-based working formula (where $m_i$ = mass of species $i$) |
| 7 | $\overline{M_w} = C \cdot \dfrac{K \cdot c}{R_\theta}$ (Zimm eq.) | Connection to Rayleigh ratio $R_\theta$ in light scattering |
| 8 | $[\eta] = K \cdot \overline{M_v}^{a}$ (Mark–Houwink) | Viscosity average $\overline{M_v}$ lies between $\overline{M_n}$ and $\overline{M_w}$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why $\overline{M_w}$ matters |
|---|---|
| **Packaging films (LDPE, LLDPE)** | Higher $\overline{M_w}$ → better melt strength → bubble stability in blown film extrusion |
| **Injection-moulded parts (PP, ABS)** | $\overline{M_w}$ governs impact toughness and modulus |
| **Tire rubber (SBR, BR)** | Wear resistance scales with $\overline{M_w}$; too low → poor abrasion |
| **Fibre spinning (PET, Nylon-6)** | Spinnability and draw-down ratio controlled by $\overline{M_w}$ |
| **Biomedical hydrogels** | $\overline{M_w}$ controls swelling, degradation kinetics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** A polymer chemist designing a UHMWPE fibre for bullet-proof vests optimises $\overline{M_w}$ in the range **3–6 million g·mol⁻¹**. Below this, energy absorption plummets; above this, melt processing becomes impossible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of $\overline{M_w}$ from First Principles

We begin with the working definition. Consider a polydisperse polymer consisting of $k$ distinct molecular species $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$. Let:

- $N_i$ = number of molecules (in moles) of species with molar mass $M_i$
- $m_i = N_i M_i$ = total mass of species $i$ in the sample
- $m_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i$ = total mass of the sample

**Step 1:** The **weight fraction** of species $i$ is, by definition:

$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{tot}} = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} N_j M_j}$$

*Conversion logic:* Divide the mass of one species by the total mass; this is a dimensionless ratio bounded in $[0, 1]$.

**Step 2:** A "weight average" arithmetic mean is constructed by multiplying each molecular weight by its weight fraction:

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i M_i$$

*Conversion logic:* Each species is "counted" $w_i$ times rather than $\frac{1}{k}$ times, because heavier molecules carry more mass.

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $w_i$ from Step 1:

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} N_j M_j} \right) M_i$$

*Conversion logic:* The denominator is a constant for the given sample and can be taken outside the summation:

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} N_j M_j} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i M_i}$$

*Final form obtained.* This is the canonical KTU-board form of the weight average molecular weight.

---

### 3.2 Proof that $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ (Mandatory for KTU)

We must rigorously show:

$$\frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \geq \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i}$$

**Step 1:** Cross-multiply (both denominators are positive):

$$\left( \sum_i N_i M_i^2 \right) \left( \sum_i N_i \right) \geq \left( \sum_i N_i M_i \right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Recognise the LHS as a Cauchy–Schwarz inequality form. Apply the standard Cauchy–Schwarz theorem for two vectors $\vec{a} = (\sqrt{N_i} M_i)$ and $\vec{b} = (\sqrt{N_i})$:

$$\left( \sum_i (\sqrt{N_i} M_i)(\sqrt{N_i}) \right)^2 \leq \left( \sum_i (\sqrt{N_i} M_i)^2 \right) \left( \sum_i (\sqrt{N_i})^2 \right)$$

$$\Rightarrow \left( \sum_i N_i M_i \right)^2 \leq \left( \sum_i N_i M_i^2 \right) \left( \sum_i N_i \right)$$

**Step 3:** This is exactly the inequality required. Equality holds iff $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are linearly dependent, i.e., $M_i = \text{constant}$ for all $i$ — which is the **monodisperse** case.

**Conclusion:** $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ for any polymer sample, with equality only at PDI = 1.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style calculation)

**Problem (Dec 2022 / Apr 2023 type):**
A polymer sample has the following molecular weight distribution. Compute $\overline{M_n}$, $\overline{M_w}$, and the PDI.

| Species $i$ | $N_i$ (mol) | $M_i$ (g·mol⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 10 000 |
| 2 | 5 | 50 000 |
| 3 | 3 | 100 000 |

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute $N_i M_i$ and $N_i M_i^2$ for each species:**

| Species | $N_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $1.0 \times 10^4$ | $2.0 \times 10^4$ | $2.0 \times 10^8$ |
| 2 | 5 | $5.0 \times 10^4$ | $2.5 \times 10^5$ | $1.25 \times 10^{10}$ |
| 3 | 3 | $1.0 \times 10^5$ | $3.0 \times 10^5$ | $3.0 \times 10^{10}$ |
| **Sum** | $\sum N_i = 10$ | — | $\sum N_i M_i = 5.7 \times 10^5$ | $\sum N_i M_i^2 = 4.55 \times 10^{10}$ |

**Step B — Compute $\overline{M_n}$:**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} = \frac{5.7 \times 10^5}{10} = 5.7 \times 10^4 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step C — Compute $\overline{M_w}$:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} = \frac{4.55 \times 10^{10}}{5.7 \times 10^5} = 7.982 \times 10^4 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step D — Compute PDI:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{7.982 \times 10^4}{5.7 \times 10^4} \approx 1.40$$

**Verification:** $\overline{M_w} > \overline{M_n}$ ✓, and PDI $> 1$ indicating a polydisperse (typical) polymer sample.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Average

| Property to be Predicted | Most Appropriate Average | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Freezing-point depression / Osmotic pressure | $\overline{M_n}$ | Colligative property → depends on *number* of molecules |
| Light scattering intensity | $\overline{M_w}$ | Scattering amplitude $\propto M_i^2$ → second moment |
| Sedimentation in ultracentrifuge | $\overline{M_w}$ | Sedimentation force $\propto m_i$ |
| Melt viscosity | $\overline{M_w}$ (or $\overline{M_v}$) | Viscous drag $\propto$ molecular volume |
| Intrinsic viscosity | $\overline{M_v}$ | Lies between $\overline{M_n}$ and $\overline{M_w}$ |
| Mechanical strength (fibres, films) | $\overline{M_w}$ | Entanglement density scales with mass |
| Vapour-phase osmometry | $\overline{M_n}$ | Depends on mole fraction |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How $\overline{M_w}$ is Determined by Light Scattering

The following Mermaid block describes the experimental pathway used to obtain $\overline{M_w}$ from a **static light-scattering (SLS)** measurement, which is a high-yield topic in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Solution of Concentration c] --> B[Incident Laser Beam lambda0]
    B --> C[Scattered Light at Angle theta]
    C --> D[Detector Measures Rayleigh Ratio Rtheta]
    D --> E[Zimm Plot Construction]
    E --> F[Extrapolation to theta = 0 and c = 0]
    F --> G[Intercept Gives K over M_w Inverse]
    G --> H[Calculate Weight Average Molecular Weight M_w Bar]
    H --> I[Second Virial Coefficient A2 from Slope]
    I --> J[Radius of Gyration Rg from Angular Dependence]
    H --> K[Report M_w in g per mol with PDI]
```

**Reading the flow:**
- The **polymer solution** is irradiated by a monochromatic laser.
- The **scattered intensity** at multiple angles and concentrations is fed into the **Zimm equation**:

$$\frac{K \cdot c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w}} + \frac{16 \pi^2}{3 \lambda_0^2} \cdot R_g^2 \cdot \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + 2 A_2 c$$

- A **Zimm plot** of $\dfrac{K c}{R_\theta}$ vs. $\sin^2(\theta/2) + k c$ yields a common extrapolated intercept at $\theta = 0, c = 0$ equal to $\dfrac{1}{\overline{M_w}}$.

---

### 4.2 Schematic — Comparison of Number, Weight, and Viscosity Averages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MN[Number Average M_n] -->|First Moment| COMPCOMP[Equal Weight per Molecule]
    MW[Weight Average M_w] -->|Second Moment| COMPCOMP
    MV[Viscosity Average M_v] -->|Intermediate| COMPCOMP
    MZ[Z Average M_z] -->|Third Moment| COMPCOMP
    COMPCOMP -->|PDI equals M_w over M_n| OUT[Monodisperse limit: PDI equals 1]
    COMPCOMP -->|PDI greater than 1| OUT2[Polydisperse real polymer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual mnemonic:** The sequence $\overline{M_n} \leq \overline{M_v} \leq \overline{M_w} \leq \overline{M_z}$ always holds for polydisperse systems. The further apart the averages, the broader the distribution.

### 4.3 Distribution Curve Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Narrow Distribution] --> A2[PDI near 1.05]
    A2 --> A3[M_n and M_w nearly equal]
    B1[Broad Distribution] --> B2[PDI between 2 and 5]
    B2 --> B3[M_w significantly greater than M_n]
    C1[Very Broad Free Radical Polymer] --> C2[PDI around 1.5 to 2.0]
    C2 --> C3[Long chain tail to high M]
    D1[Living Anionic Polymer] --> D2[PDI near 1.01 to 1.1]
    D2 --> D3[Narrow Poisson Distribution]
```

---

### 4.4 Engineering Decision Flowchart — Selecting $\overline{M_w}$ Target

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Polymer Application Selection] --> Q1{End Use Requirement}
    Q1 -->|High Mechanical Strength| PATH1[Target high M_w]
    Q1 -->|Easy Melt Processing| PATH2[Target moderate M_w]
    Q1 -->|Solution Viscosity Control| PATH3[Use M_v as guideline]
    PATH1 --> SPEC1[Example: UHMWPE 3 to 6 million g per mol]
    PATH2 --> SPEC2[Example: Injection grade PP 2 to 4 lakh g per mol]
    PATH3 --> SPEC3[Example: Coating resins 1 to 5 lakh g per mol]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **weight average molecular weight ($\overline{M_w}$)**. Write its mathematical expression and state two physical properties of polymers that depend on $\overline{M_w}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition:** Weight average molecular weight is the average molecular weight of a polymer in which each molecular species is weighted according to its mass contribution to the total mass of the sample.
>
> $$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$
>
> **Two properties depending on $\overline{M_w}$ (1 mark each):**
> 1. Light scattering intensity of polymer solutions.
> 2. Melt viscosity and mechanical strength of bulk polymers.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **number average molecular weight ($\overline{M_n}$)** and **weight average molecular weight ($\overline{M_w}$)** with respect to definition and physical significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $\overline{M_n}$ | $\overline{M_w}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ |
| Statistical moment | First moment | Second moment / First moment |
| Determines | Colligative properties (osmotic pressure, FP depression) | Mass-dependent properties (light scattering, melt viscosity) |
| Sensitivity to high $M$ species | Low | **High** |

> **Key point (1 mark):** $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$, with equality only for a monodisperse system.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam Model Question — Module 2]**

**Module Internal Choice — Attempt either Option A or Option B.**

---

#### ✅ Option A (14 Marks)

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:**
Define weight average molecular weight. Derive its mathematical expression. Show that $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ for any polymer sample.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition (2 marks):**
Weight average molecular weight is the mass-weighted statistical mean of the molecular weights of all species present in a polymer sample. Heavier molecules contribute more to this average because they carry more mass.

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i M_i, \quad \text{where } w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$$

**(ii) Derivation (3 marks):**

Starting from the weight-fraction definition:

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i M_i = \sum_i \left( \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j} \right) M_i = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

**(iii) Proof of $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ (2 marks):**

$$\overline{M_w} - \overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} - \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i} = \frac{\left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum_i N_i\right) - \left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2}{\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)\left(\sum_i N_i\right)}$$

By Cauchy–Schwarz, $\left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum_i N_i\right) \geq \left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2$, hence the numerator is $\geq 0$, giving $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$.

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:**
A polymer sample contains three molecular weight fractions as follows:

| Fraction | Mass (g) | Molecular Weight $M_i$ (g·mol⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 20 000 |
| 2 | 25 | 60 000 |
| 3 | 15 | 100 000 |

Calculate (i) $\overline{M_w}$, (ii) $\overline{M_n}$, and (iii) Polydispersity Index. Comment on the polydispersity.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $N_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}$ for each fraction:

| Fraction | $m_i$ (g) | $M_i$ (g·mol⁻¹) | $N_i = m_i / M_i$ (mol) | $N_i M_i^2$ (g²·mol⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 20 000 | $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | $2.0 \times 10^{8}$ |
| 2 | 25 | 60 000 | $4.167 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.5 \times 10^{9}$ |
| 3 | 15 | 100 000 | $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1.5 \times 10^{9}$ |
| **Sum** | **50 g** | — | $1.067 \times 10^{-3}$ mol | $3.2 \times 10^{9}$ |

**Step 2 — Compute $\overline{M_n}$:**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum m_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{50}{1.067 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 4.69 \times 10^4 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\overline{M_w}$:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^9}{50} = 6.4 \times 10^4 \text{ g·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Polydispersity Index:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^4}{4.69 \times 10^4} \approx 1.365$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** PDI > 1 indicates a **polydisperse** sample typical of step-growth or free-radical polymerisation products. The polymer has a moderate molecular weight distribution breadth.

---

#### ✅ Option B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:**
Explain the principle of **static light scattering** for determining $\overline{M_w}$. Write the relevant Zimm equation and explain how a Zimm plot yields the weight average molecular weight.

**Model Solution Outline (Examiner's Marking Scheme):**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Polymer solutions scatter light; scattering intensity depends on molecular weight and concentration. Excess Rayleigh ratio $R_\theta$ is measured as a function of angle $\theta$ and concentration $c$.
2. **Zimm Equation (3 marks):**

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w} P(\theta)} + 2 A_2 c + \ldots$$

where $P(\theta)$ is the form factor and $K$ is an optical constant.
3. **Zimm Plot Procedure (2 marks):** Plot $\dfrac{K c}{R_\theta}$ vs. $\sin^2(\theta/2) + k c$ at multiple angles and concentrations. Extrapolate both angle and concentration to zero. The common intercept equals $\dfrac{1}{\overline{M_w}}$.

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:**
Compare the **number average, weight average, and viscosity average** molecular weights. Mention the experimental methods used to determine each and the physical properties each average correlates with.

**Model Solution Outline:**

| Average | Formula | Experimental Method | Property Correlated |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\overline{M_n}$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Osmometry, VPO, End-group analysis | Colligative properties |
| $\overline{M_w}$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Light scattering, Sedimentation | Viscosity, mechanical strength |
| $\overline{M_v}$ | $\left( \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{a+1}}{\sum N_i M_i} \right)^{1/a}$ | Viscometry (Mark–Houwink) | Solution viscosity |

> **Inequality:** $\overline{M_n} \leq \overline{M_v} \leq \overline{M_w}$ always.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Weight-Average Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\overline{M_w}$ with $\overline{M_n}$ in the numerator.** Many students write $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$ (inverted). Remember: **square goes in the numerator** because weight average *up-weights* heavy molecules.
> 2. **Forgetting to square $M_i$.** The expression $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ contains $M_i^2$ in the numerator — losing this exponent is the single most common error.
> 3. **Skipping units.** Always express answers in **g·mol⁻¹**. Carrying kg·mol⁻¹ silently will lose the unit mark.
> 4. **Omitting the Cauchy–Schwarz justification.** Simply writing $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ without proof loses 1–2 marks. Always attach the inequality reasoning.
> 5. **Sign error in PDI:** PDI is $\dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$, not the reciprocal.
> 6. **Mixing up weight fraction $w_i$ with mole fraction $x_i$.** Weight fraction uses $N_i M_i$; mole fraction uses $N_i$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_w}$)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Mass-weighted mean of molecular weights in a polydisperse polymer.
- ✅ **Canonical Formula:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i}$$

- ✅ **Alternative Formula:** $\overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i M_i$ where $w_i$ is the weight fraction.
- ✅ **Always true:** $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ (Cauchy–Schwarz proof mandatory for full marks).
- ✅ **PDI** = $\dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$; PDI = 1 for monodisperse, PDI > 1 for polydisperse.
- ✅ **Determined experimentally by:** Light scattering (Rayleigh ratio $R_\theta$ via Zimm plot), sedimentation velocity ultracentrifugation, SANS.
- ✅ **Predicts mass-dependent properties:** melt viscosity, mechanical strength, light scattering intensity, sedimentation rate.
- ✅ **Does NOT predict:** colligative properties (use $\overline{M_n}$ instead).
- ✅ **Typical values:** Free-radical polymers PDI ≈ 1.5–2.0; living anionic polymers PDI ≈ 1.01–1.10; step-growth polymers PDI ≈ 2.0 (Carothers limit).
- ✅ **High-MW species dominate $\overline{M_w}$:** Doubling the mass of the heaviest fraction increases $\overline{M_w}$ dramatically, but barely shifts $\overline{M_n}$.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** UHMWPE fibres (3–6 million g·mol⁻¹); commodity polyolefins (10⁵–10⁶ g·mol⁻¹); coating resins (10⁴–10⁵ g·mol⁻¹).
- ✅ **Sequence mnemonic:** $\overline{M_n} \leq \overline{M_v} \leq \overline{M_w} \leq \overline{M_z} \leq \overline{M_{z+1}}$ for polydisperse systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscosity Average
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viscosity Average

# Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight ($M_v$ / $M_\eta$)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight ($M_v$ or $M_\eta$) is an empirically defined average molecular weight of a polymer that is determined experimentally by measuring the intrinsic viscosity of a dilute polymer solution. It is calculated using the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation and represents the molecular weight to which the viscosity of a polymer solution is most sensitive.*

It is denoted as $M_v$ (or $M_\eta$) and is calculated from the intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ using the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) equation**:

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\alpha}$$

where:
- $[\eta]$ = Intrinsic viscosity (dL/g)
- $K$ = Mark–Houwink constant (polymer–solvent–temperature dependent)
- $\alpha$ = Mark–Houwink exponent (typically between **0.5** and **0.8** for flexible linear polymers in good solvents, and approaches **0.5** in theta solvents)
- $M_v$ = Viscosity-average molecular weight (g/mol)

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍝

Imagine you have a bowl of noodles (polymer chains) mixed in soup (solvent). If you stir the soup, longer noodles tangle with each other more and make the soup thicker (more viscous) than shorter noodles do.

So by measuring how "thick" the soup becomes (viscosity), we can indirectly estimate the **average length of the noodles** — but it's a special kind of average that gives **more weight to the longer, thicker noodles** because they contribute disproportionately to the flow resistance.

This special "long-chain-biased" average is the **Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight ($M_v$)**.

---

### Position of $M_v$ among Molecular Weight Averages

> [!NOTE]
> $M_v$ always lies between the number-average and weight-average molecular weights:
> $$M_n \;\le\; M_v \;\le\; M_w$$
> - When $\alpha \rightarrow 1$, $M_v \rightarrow M_w$
> - When $\alpha \rightarrow 0$, $M_v \rightarrow M_n$

| Average | Symbol | Weighting Bias |
|---|---|---|
| Number-average | $M_n$ | Equal weight to every molecule |
| **Viscosity-average** | $M_v$ | **Bias toward larger chains** |
| Weight-average | $M_w$ | Bias toward heavier molecules |
| Z-average | $M_z$ | Strong bias toward largest chains |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Solution Viscosities Used

Before we get to $M_v$, we need to understand the experimental quantities used to calculate it:

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative Viscosity | $\eta_{rel}$ | Ratio of solution to solvent viscosity | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta}{\eta_0} = \dfrac{t}{t_0}$ |
| Specific Viscosity | $\eta_{sp}$ | Fractional increase in viscosity | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$ |
| Reduced Viscosity | $\eta_{red}$ | Specific viscosity per unit concentration | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ |
| Inherent Viscosity | $\eta_{inh}$ | Logarithmic viscosity per concentration | $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$ |
| **Intrinsic Viscosity** | $[\eta]$ | Limiting value at infinite dilution | $[\eta] = \displaystyle\lim_{c \to 0} \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = \displaystyle\lim_{c \to 0} \dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units of concentration ($c$):** Typically expressed in **g/dL** (grams per deciliter).
> **Units of $[\eta]$:** dL/g (deciliters per gram).

---

### 2.2 The Mark–Houwink–Sakurada Equation

This is the central equation of the topic.

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\alpha}$$

where:
- $K$ = Mark–Houwink constant (units depend on $[\eta]$ convention; typically mL/g if $M$ in g/mol and $c$ in g/mL)
- $\alpha$ = Exponent related to polymer–solvent interaction and chain conformation:
  - **0.5** → theta solvent (ideal random coil, unperturbed dimensions)
  - **0.5 – 0.8** → good solvent (expanded coil)
  - **0.8 – 1.0** → semi-rigid / rod-like polymers
  - **≈ 2.0** → rigid rods

---

### 2.3 Determination of Intrinsic Viscosity (Huggins & Kraemer Plots)

Intrinsic viscosity is obtained by extrapolation using two equations:

**Huggins Equation:**
$$\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 \cdot c$$

**Kraemer Equation:**
$$\dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} = [\eta] - k_K [\eta]^2 \cdot c$$

> Both lines, when extrapolated to $c = 0$, give the same intercept: $[\eta]$.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\alpha}$ | **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation** |
| 2 | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta}{\eta_0} = \dfrac{t}{t_0}$ | Relative viscosity (via flow time $t$) |
| 3 | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$ | Specific viscosity |
| 4 | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ | Reduced viscosity |
| 5 | $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$ | Inherent viscosity |
| 6 | $[\eta] = \displaystyle\lim_{c \to 0}\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ | Intrinsic viscosity (Huggins intercept) |
| 7 | $[\eta] = \displaystyle\lim_{c \to 0}\dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$ | Intrinsic viscosity (Kraemer intercept) |
| 8 | $M_n \le M_v \le M_w$ | Position of $M_v$ |
| 9 | $M_v = \left(\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum N_i M_i}\right)^{1/\alpha}$ | **Statistical definition** of $M_v$ |
| 10 | $k_H + k_K \approx 0.5$ | Huggins + Kraemer constant relation |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control in Polymer Manufacturing:** Used in PET, PVC, Nylon, and HDPE plants to monitor batch-to-batch consistency.
- **Polymer Characterization Labs:** Rapid, low-cost method (only requires a capillary viscometer like Ubbelohde).
- **Predicting Mechanical Properties:** $M_v$ correlates strongly with tensile strength, impact resistance, and melt viscosity — important for processing (extrusion, injection molding).
- **Pharmaceutical Polymers:** Excipient characterization in drug formulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $M_v$ from the Statistical Definition

The viscosity-average molecular weight is defined as:

$$M_v = \left[\dfrac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha} = \left[\dfrac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{\beta}}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha}$$

where $\beta = 1 + \alpha$ and $N_i$ is the number of molecules of species $i$ with molecular weight $M_i$.

**Derivation from the Mark–Houwink equation:**

Step 1 — Specific viscosity contribution from a single chain in dilute solution is given by an empirical relation (Staudinger–Kuhn type):

$$\eta_{sp,i} \propto [\eta]_i \cdot c_i$$

Step 2 — Intrinsic viscosity contribution of species $i$:

$$[\eta]_i = K M_i^{\alpha}$$

Step 3 — Total intrinsic viscosity for a polydisperse polymer is the **mass-weighted sum** (since viscosity depends on the volume each chain occupies):

$$[\eta]_{total} = \sum_i w_i [\eta]_i = \sum_i w_i K M_i^{\alpha}$$

Step 4 — Expressing in terms of number of molecules:

$$w_i = \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$$

Step 5 — Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\eta]_{total} &= K \cdot \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i \cdot M_i^{\alpha}}{\sum_j N_j M_j} \\[6pt]
&= K \cdot \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Compare with the MHS form $[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\alpha}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K M_v^{\alpha} &= K \cdot \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \\[6pt]
M_v^{\alpha} &= \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \\[6pt]
M_v &= \left[\dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ This is the **statistical definition** of the viscosity-average molecular weight.

---

### 3.2 Special Cases (Boundary Limits)

**Case 1 — Monodisperse polymer ($M_i$ same for all $i$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_v &= \left[\dfrac{N \cdot M^{1+\alpha}}{N \cdot M}\right]^{1/\alpha} \\[6pt]
&= \left[M^{\alpha}\right]^{1/\alpha} = M
\end{aligned}
$$

➡ For monodisperse samples, **all averages equal the true molecular weight**.

**Case 2 — Polydisperse sample, $\alpha = 1$:**

$$
M_v = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = M_w
$$

➡ When $\alpha = 1$, $M_v$ becomes the weight-average molecular weight.

**Case 3 — Polydisperse sample, $\alpha \to 0$:**

Using L'Hôpital's rule:

$$
M_v = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} \cdot \text{(limit)} = M_n
$$

➡ When $\alpha \to 0$, $M_v$ approaches the number-average molecular weight.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Numerical Determination of $M_v$

> **Problem:** A polymer sample contains three fractions. Using the data below and $\alpha = 0.6$, determine the viscosity-average molecular weight.

| Fraction | $N_i$ (mol) | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $10{,}000$ |
| 2 | 3 | $50{,}000$ |
| 3 | 5 | $100{,}000$ |

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\sum N_i M_i$:

$$
\sum N_i M_i = (2)(10{,}000) + (3)(50{,}000) + (5)(100{,}000) = 20{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 500{,}000 = 670{,}000
$$

Step 2 — Compute $M_i^{1+\alpha} = M_i^{1.6}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(10{,}000)^{1.6} &= 10^{4 \times 1.6} = 10^{6.4} = 2{,}511{,}886 \\
(50{,}000)^{1.6} &= (5 \times 10^4)^{1.6} = 5^{1.6} \times 10^{6.4} = 9.518 \times 10^{6.4} \approx 23{,}901{,}000 \\
(100{,}000)^{1.6} &= 10^{4 \times 1.6} = 10^{6.4} \times 10^{1.6} = 10^{8} = 100{,}000{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Compute $\sum N_i M_i^{1.6}$:

$$
\sum N_i M_i^{1.6} = (2)(2{,}511{,}886) + (3)(23{,}901{,}000) + (5)(100{,}000{,}000) \approx 5.024 \times 10^6 + 7.170 \times 10^7 + 5.000 \times 10^8
$$

$$
\sum N_i M_i^{1.6} \approx 5.772 \times 10^8
$$

Step 4 — Compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{1.6}}{\sum N_i M_i} = \dfrac{5.772 \times 10^8}{6.70 \times 10^5} \approx 861.5
$$

Step 5 — Take the $1/\alpha = 1/0.6 = 1.667$ power:

$$
M_v = (861.5)^{1.667}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(M_v) = 1.667 \times \log_{10}(861.5) = 1.667 \times 2.9351 = 4.913
$$

$$
\boxed{M_v \approx 81{,}800 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Finding $M_v$ from Intrinsic Viscosity

> **Problem:** A polymer in chloroform at $25^\circ C$ has $K = 0.00049$ dL/g and $\alpha = 0.62$. The intrinsic viscosity is measured as $[\eta] = 0.82$ dL/g. Find $M_v$.

**Solution:**

Rearranging $[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$:

$$
M_v = \left(\dfrac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/\alpha}
$$

$$
M_v = \left(\dfrac{0.82}{0.00049}\right)^{1/0.62}
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.82}{0.00049} = 1673.47
$$

$$
\ln(1673.47) = 7.422 \quad ; \quad \dfrac{7.422}{0.62} = 11.97
$$

$$
M_v = e^{11.97} \approx 1.58 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_v \approx 1.58 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Polymer Sample] --> B[Dissolve in Solvent]
    B --> C[Prepare Dilute Solutions at Various c]
    C --> D[Measure Flow Time t and Solvent Time t0 using Ubbelohde Viscometer]
    D --> E[Compute eta_rel = t / t0]
    E --> F[Compute eta_sp = eta_rel - 1]
    F --> G[Plot eta_sp / c vs c : Huggins Plot]
    F --> H[Plot ln eta_rel / c vs c : Kraemer Plot]
    G --> I[Extrapolate to c = 0]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Intrinsic Viscosity eta]
    J --> K[Look up K and alpha from Polymer-Solvent Tables]
    K --> L[Apply Mark Houwink Sakurada: eta = K times Mv to power alpha]
    L --> M[Compute Mv = eta by K to power 1 over alpha]
    M --> N[Final Report: Viscosity Average Molecular Weight Mv]
```

### 4.2 Position of $M_v$ in the Molecular Weight Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Lowest: Mn] -->|increases| B[Mv Viscosity Average]
    B -->|increases| C[Mw Weight Average]
    C -->|increases| D[Highest: Mz Z Average]
    style A fill:#d4f1f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ffe699,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style C fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f4b084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Huggins vs Kraemer Extrapolation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Concentration c g per dL] -->|x axis| B[Viscosity Function]
    B --> C[Huggins: eta_sp / c line slope = kH times eta squared]
    B --> D[Kraemer: ln eta_rel / c line slope negative kK times eta squared]
    C --> E[Both lines meet at intercept eta on y axis]
    D --> E
    style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Flow: Why $M_v$ is "Biased Toward Larger Chains"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Single Chain in Solvent] --> B[Chain Length L]
    B --> C[Hydrodynamic Volume Vh]
    C --> D[Vh proportional to M raised to power alpha times 1 plus alpha]
    D --> E[Viscosity contribution proportional to Vh]
    E --> F[Longer chains contribute disproportionately]
    F --> G[Therefore Mv is biased toward larger M]
    style G fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define viscosity-average molecular weight. Write the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation and explain the significance of $\alpha$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Viscosity-average molecular weight ($M_v$) is an empirical average molecular weight of a polymer determined from solution viscosity measurements, defined as:

$$M_v = \left[\dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha}$$

**MHS Equation (1 mark):**

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\alpha}$$

**Significance of $\alpha$ (1 mark):**
- $\alpha = 0.5$ → theta solvent (unperturbed chain)
- $0.5 \le \alpha \le 0.8$ → good solvent (expanded coil)
- $\alpha \to 1$ → semi-rigid chain; $M_v \to M_w$
- Higher $\alpha$ → more rigid conformation, stronger $M$ dependence

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why does $M_v$ always lie between $M_n$ and $M_w$? Justify with the relationship $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For $0 \le \alpha \le 1$, the function $M^\alpha$ lies between $M^0 = 1$ and $M^1 = M$. This means the weighting power $1+\alpha$ lies between 1 (which gives $M_n$) and 2 (which gives $M_w$).

Mathematically:

$$
\lim_{\alpha \to 0} M_v = \left[\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i}\right] = M_n \qquad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

$$
\lim_{\alpha \to 1} M_v = \left[\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}\right] = M_w \qquad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

For intermediate $\alpha$, $M_n < M_v < M_w$ (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the statistical expression for viscosity-average molecular weight $M_v$ starting from the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation. Show that $M_n \le M_v \le M_w$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The intrinsic viscosity of a polymer solution is 1.25 dL/g at $25^\circ$C. For the polymer–solvent system, $K = 4.3 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g and $\alpha = 0.65$. Calculate the viscosity-average molecular weight. If a fraction of this polymer has $M = 5 \times 10^5$ g/mol, what type of average ($M_n$, $M_v$, $M_w$) will this be most sensitive to? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For a polydisperse polymer, the total intrinsic viscosity is the mass-fraction-weighted sum:

$$[\eta] = \sum_i w_i [\eta]_i$$

For species $i$: $[\eta]_i = K M_i^{\alpha}$

Mass fraction $w_i = \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j}$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\eta] &= \sum_i \dfrac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j} \cdot K M_i^{\alpha} \\[6pt]
&= K \cdot \dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing with $[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$:

$$
M_v = \left[\dfrac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}\right]^{1/\alpha} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**Bounds (4 marks):**
- When $\alpha = 1$: $M_v = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = M_w$
- When $\alpha = 0$: $M_v = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} = M_n$
- Since $0 \le \alpha \le 1$, the exponent $1+\alpha$ lies between 1 and 2, giving $M_n \le M_v \le M_w$.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $[\eta] = 1.25$ dL/g, $K = 4.3 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g, $\alpha = 0.65$.

$$
M_v = \left(\dfrac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/\alpha} = \left(\dfrac{1.25}{4.3 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.65} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1.25}{4.3 \times 10^{-4}} = 2906.98
$$

$$
\log M_v = \dfrac{1}{0.65} \log(2906.98) = \dfrac{3.463}{0.65} = 5.328
$$

$$
M_v = 10^{5.328} = 2.13 \times 10^5 \text{ g/mol} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
$$

**Sensitivity (2 marks):** Since $\alpha = 0.65 < 1$, the weighting $M^{1.65}$ is closer to $M^1$ than $M^2$. Therefore $M_v$ is **more sensitive to $M_n$ than $M_w$** for this particular polymer–solvent system. The given fraction ($M = 5 \times 10^5$) contributes more heavily to $M_w$ than $M_v$, so it will affect $M_w$ most.

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Define intrinsic viscosity. Explain the Huggins and Kraemer equations. Describe how intrinsic viscosity is determined graphically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polystyrene sample has the following viscosity data in toluene at $25^\circ$C ($t_0 = 102.4$ s):

| $c$ (g/dL) | Flow time $t$ (s) |
|---|---|
| 0.000 | 102.4 |
| 0.001 | 115.6 |
| 0.002 | 130.1 |
| 0.003 | 146.0 |
| 0.004 | 163.2 |

Determine the intrinsic viscosity using the Huggins plot. Given $K = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g and $\alpha = 0.69$, calculate $M_v$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ is the limiting value of reduced viscosity or inherent viscosity as concentration $c$ approaches zero. It represents the contribution of isolated polymer molecules to the viscosity of the solution, free from polymer–polymer interactions.

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = \lim_{c \to 0} \dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}$$

**Huggins equation (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 \cdot c$$

A plot of $\eta_{sp}/c$ versus $c$ gives a straight line with intercept $[\eta]$ and slope $k_H[\eta]^2$.

**Kraemer equation (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} = [\eta] - k_K [\eta]^2 \cdot c$$

A plot of $\ln \eta_{rel}/c$ versus $c$ gives a straight line with intercept $[\eta]$ and slope $-k_K[\eta]^2$.

**Graphical determination (1 mark):** Both lines extrapolated to $c = 0$ give the same intercept $[\eta]$. The constants satisfy $k_H + k_K \approx 0.5$.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Compute $\eta_{rel} = t/t_0$ and $\eta_{sp}/c$:

| $c$ (g/dL) | $t$ (s) | $\eta_{rel}$ | $\eta_{sp}$ | $\eta_{sp}/c$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.000 | 102.4 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 | — |
| 0.001 | 115.6 | 1.1289 | 0.1289 | 128.9 |
| 0.002 | 130.1 | 1.2705 | 0.2705 | 135.3 |
| 0.003 | 146.0 | 1.4258 | 0.4258 | 141.9 |
| 0.004 | 163.2 | 1.5938 | 0.5938 | 148.4 |

**Linear fit for $\eta_{sp}/c$ vs $c$:** (3 marks)

Using least-squares fit on $(c, \eta_{sp}/c)$:
- Slope $\approx (148.4 - 128.9)/(0.004 - 0.001) = 19.5/0.003 = 6500$
- Intercept at $c = 0$:

$$
[\eta] \approx 128.9 - 6500 \times 0.001 = 128.9 - 6.5 = 122.4 \text{ dL/g (approximately)}
$$

More accurately, fitting a linear regression:

$[\eta] \approx 1.20 \times 10^{2}$ dL/g ≈ **120 dL/g** (2 marks)

**Calculation of $M_v$:**

$$
M_v = \left(\dfrac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/\alpha} = \left(\dfrac{120}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.69} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

$$
\dfrac{120}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.06 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\log M_v = \dfrac{1}{0.69} \log(7.06 \times 10^5) = \dfrac{5.849}{0.69} = 8.476
$$

$$
M_v = 10^{8.476} \approx 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ g/mol} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_v \approx 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units trap:** Forgetting to convert $K$ units. If $K$ is in mL/g, then $[\eta]$ is in mL/g; if $K$ is in dL/g, use dL/g consistently. Mismatch = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Algebra slip in $1/\alpha$:** Students often forget to take the inverse of $\alpha$, computing $M^{\alpha}$ instead of $M^{1/\alpha}$. This is a **−2 mark** deduction.
> 3. **Mixing averages:** Defining $M_v$ as $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum N_i M_i}$ (without the outer $1/\alpha$ power) is incomplete. Always show the full expression. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Time-vs-viscosity confusion:** $t/t_0 = \eta/\eta_0$ is valid only for **constant density and same capillary** — students often forget this assumption.
> 5. **Concentration units:** $c$ must be in g/dL when $[\eta]$ is in dL/g; mismatching gives **wrong numerical answer**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** $M_v$ is the molecular weight determined from solution viscosity via the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation $[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$.
- ✅ **Position:** Always satisfies $M_n \le M_v \le M_w$.
- ✅ **Statistical form:** $M_v = \left(\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+\alpha}}{\sum N_i M_i}\right)^{1/\alpha}$.
- ✅ **$\alpha$ range:** 0.5 (theta solvent) → 0.8 (good solvent); higher for rigid rods.
- ✅ **Experimental:** Intrinsic viscosity measured using Ubbelohde viscometer via Huggins + Kraemer extrapolation.
- ✅ **Formulas to memorize:**
  - $\eta_{rel} = t/t_0$
  - $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{rel} - 1$
  - Huggins: $\eta_{sp}/c = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c$
  - Kraemer: $\ln \eta_{rel}/c = [\eta] - k_K[\eta]^2 c$
  - MHS: $[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$
- ✅ **Sensitivity:** Higher $\alpha$ → $M_v$ approaches $M_w$; lower $\alpha$ → $M_v$ approaches $M_n$.
- ✅ **Engineering use:** Quality control in polymer production; correlates with mechanical and rheological properties.
- ✅ **Limiting cases:** $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow M_v = M_n$; $\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow M_v = M_w$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Z-Average
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Z-Average

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Z-Average Molecular Weight (M̄z):** The Z-average molecular weight is the **third moment** of the molecular weight distribution of a polymer. It is statistically defined as the ratio of the **sum of the cube of the molecular weights** of all molecules to the **sum of the squares of the molecular weights** of all molecules in the sample.

Mathematically, the Z-average molecular weight is expressed as:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i \, M_i^3}{\sum_{i} N_i \, M_i^2}$$

Where:
- $N_i$ = Number of molecules having molecular weight $M_i$
- $M_i$ = Molecular weight of the $i^{th}$ species
- $\overline{M_z}$ = Z-average molecular weight (g/mol)

In the continuous form (using the molar mass distribution function), it is written as:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} M^3 \, f(M) \, dM}{\int_{0}^{\infty} M^2 \, f(M) \, dM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Z-average molecular weight is the *highest-order* average commonly measured for polymers. It is more sensitive to the **high-molecular-weight tail** of the distribution than either the number-average ($\overline{M_n}$) or weight-average ($\overline{M_w}$) molecular weights.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Heavy Baggage" Analogy:**
> Imagine a baggage counter at an airport where you have 100 bags weighing 10 kg and only 5 bags weighing 100 kg.
> - **Number-Average ($\overline{M_n}$)**: Just counts bags — gives equal weight to the 100 light bags and 5 heavy ones.
> - **Weight-Average ($\overline{M_w}$)**: Counts each bag *as many times as its weight* — the heavy bags start to dominate.
> - **Z-Average ($\overline{M_z}$)**: Counts each heavy bag *even more aggressively* — it weighs each bag by the **square of its mass**. So the 5 bags of 100 kg each contribute $5 \times 100^2 = 500,000$, while the 100 bags of 10 kg each contribute only $100 \times 10^2 = 10,000$. The heavy bags dominate overwhelmingly!

**Key Insight:** Z-average is the most "high-mass-biased" of the three averages. Even a tiny fraction of ultra-high-molecular-weight chains can dramatically inflate the Z-average value.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Unit of $\overline{M_z}$:** **g/mol** (or **kg/kmol** in SI)
- **Typical magnitude:** $\overline{M_z} > \overline{M_w} > \overline{M_n}$ (always true for polydisperse polymers)
- **For a monodisperse polymer:** $\overline{M_z} = \overline{M_w} = \overline{M_n}$
- **Polydispersity Ratio (Z/Weight):** $\frac{\overline{M_z}}{\overline{M_w}}$ — a measure of distribution breadth
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Hierarchical Breakdown of Z-Average Molecular Weight

- **Step 1 — Origin of the "Z" Notation:**
  In sedimentation analysis using an **analytical ultracentrifuge**, the sedimentation coefficient $S$ is measured. The sedimentation coefficient depends on molecular weight. When the data is plotted and extrapolated, the variable **Z** is used to denote a weighting function proportional to $M^2$. Hence the name "Z-average."

- **Step 2 — Mathematical Structure (Power Series Weighting):**
  Each average is a moment of the molecular weight distribution. For Z-average:
  - Numerator uses $M_i^3$ → **third moment**
  - Denominator uses $M_i^2$ → **second moment**
  - Effectively, Z-average is $\frac{\text{Moment}_3}{\text{Moment}_2}$

- **Step 3 — Why Does It Emphasize Heavy Chains?**
  Since the contribution scales as $M^3$ in the numerator, a molecule that is 10× heavier contributes **1000×** more to the Z-average than a molecule 10× lighter. This makes it a powerful probe for **high-molecular-weight tails** and **chain entanglements**.

- **Step 4 — Experimental Determination:**
  Z-average is obtained from **sedimentation equilibrium experiments** in an analytical ultracentrifuge, where concentration gradients at equilibrium are interpreted using the relation:
  $$M_z = \frac{2RT}{(1-\overline{v}\rho)\omega^2} \cdot \frac{d\ln c}{dr^2}$$
  Here $\overline{v}$ = partial specific volume, $\rho$ = density, $\omega$ = angular velocity.

- **Step 5 — Engineering Relevance:**
  - Predicts **zero-shear viscosity** and **melt elasticity**
  - Correlates with **impact strength** and **toughness**
  - Critical in **fiber spinning** (high-MW tail affects spinnability)
  - Sensitive indicator of **chain branching** and **cross-linking**

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables / Units** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$ | Definition of Z-average (discrete) | $N_i$ = number, $M_i$ = g/mol |
| $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{\int M^3 f(M) dM}{\int M^2 f(M) dM}$ | Definition (continuous form) | $f(M)$ = distribution function |
| $\dfrac{\overline{M_z}}{\overline{M_w}} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^3 \cdot \sum N_i M_i}{\left(\sum N_i M_i^2\right)^2}$ | Z/W polydispersity index | Dimensionless ratio |
| $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{2RT}{(1-\overline{v}\rho)\omega^2} \cdot \dfrac{d\ln c}{dr^2}$ | Sedimentation equilibrium form | $R$ = J/mol·K, $\omega$ = rad/s |
| $\overline{M_z} \geq \overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ | Strict inequality for polydisperse | Inequality |
| $\overline{M_z} = \overline{M_w} = \overline{M_n}$ | Equality only for monodisperse | Equality condition |
| $\dfrac{d \ln c}{d r^2} = \dfrac{M_z \,\omega^2 (1-\overline{v}\rho)}{2RT}$ | Sedimentation equilibrium slope | Used in ultracentrifuge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The relationship $\overline{M_z} \geq \overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ is a **strict inequality** for any real (polydisperse) polymer. KTU questions often test whether you know the direction and the conditions under which equality holds.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Area** | **Why Z-Average Matters** |
|:---|:---|
| **Mechanical Properties** | High MW tails govern impact resistance |
| **Melt Rheology** | Zero-shear viscosity $\eta_0 \propto \overline{M_z}^{3.4}$ (for entangled melts) |
| **Fiber Spinning** | High MW fraction affects drawability and breakage |
| **Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC)** | Calibration curves use Mz for high-MW end verification |
| **Quality Control** | Detects presence of unwanted high-MW aggregates |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Z-Average from Basic Definition

Starting from the **general moment definition** of average molecular weight:

$$\overline{M}_{\alpha} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{\alpha + 1}}{\sum N_i M_i^{\alpha}}$$

For Z-average, the weighting parameter is $\alpha = 2$:

$$\overline{M}_z = \overline{M}_{\alpha=2} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{2+1}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}$$

This is the **fundamental discrete definition**.

### Derivation 2: Relation to Weight-Average and Polydispersity

The weight-average molecular weight is defined as:

$$\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

Multiplying $\overline{M}_w$ by itself (after rearrangement) and comparing with $\overline{M}_z$:

$$\overline{M}_w \cdot \overline{M}_w = \frac{\left(\sum N_i M_i^{2}\right)^2}{\left(\sum N_i M_i\right)^2}$$

Now forming the ratio $\dfrac{\overline{M}_z}{\overline{M}_w}$:

$$\frac{\overline{M}_z}{\overline{M}_w} = \frac{\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}}{\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i}} = \frac{\left(\sum N_i M_i^{3}\right)\left(\sum N_i M_i\right)}{\left(\sum N_i M_i^{2}\right)^2}$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
- If all molecules have identical mass (monodisperse), then $M_i = M$ for all $i$.
- The numerator becomes $\left(N M^3\right)\left(N M\right) = N^2 M^4$.
- The denominator becomes $\left(N M^2\right)^2 = N^2 M^4$.
- Therefore $\dfrac{\overline{M}_z}{\overline{M}_w} = 1$ ✓

### Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A polymer sample consists of three molecular weight fractions:

| Fraction | $N_i$ (molecules) | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 100 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 50 | 50,000 |
| 3 | 10 | 100,000 |

**Calculate $\overline{M_n}$, $\overline{M_w}$, and $\overline{M_z}$.**

**Step 1 — Number-Average ($\overline{M_n}$):**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\sum N_i M_i = (100)(10{,}000) + (50)(50{,}000) + (10)(100{,}000)$$

$$= 1{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}500{,}000$$

Compute denominator:

$$\sum N_i = 100 + 50 + 10 = 160$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{160} = 28{,}125 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Weight-Average ($\overline{M_w}$):**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\sum N_i M_i^{2} = (100)(10{,}000)^{2} + (50)(50{,}000)^{2} + (10)(100{,}000)^{2}$$

$$= (100)(1 \times 10^{8}) + (50)(2.5 \times 10^{9}) + (10)(1 \times 10^{10})$$

$$= 1 \times 10^{10} + 1.25 \times 10^{11} + 1 \times 10^{11}$$

$$= 1 \times 10^{10} + 12.5 \times 10^{10} + 10 \times 10^{10} = 23.5 \times 10^{10}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{2} = 2.35 \times 10^{11}$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{2.35 \times 10^{11}}{4.5 \times 10^{6}} \approx 52{,}222 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Z-Average ($\overline{M_z}$):**

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\sum N_i M_i^{3} = (100)(10{,}000)^{3} + (50)(50{,}000)^{3} + (10)(100{,}000)^{3}$$

$$= (100)(1 \times 10^{12}) + (50)(1.25 \times 10^{14}) + (10)(1 \times 10^{15})$$

$$= 1 \times 10^{14} + 6.25 \times 10^{15} + 1 \times 10^{16}$$

$$= 0.1 \times 10^{15} + 6.25 \times 10^{15} + 10 \times 10^{15} = 16.35 \times 10^{15}$$

$$\sum N_i M_i^{3} = 1.635 \times 10^{16}$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{1.635 \times 10^{16}}{2.35 \times 10^{11}} \approx 69{,}574 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of Inequality:**

$$\overline{M_n} = 28{,}125 < \overline{M_w} = 52{,}222 < \overline{M_z} = 69{,}574 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Polydispersity Ratios:**

$$\frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{52{,}222}{28{,}125} \approx 1.857$$

$$\frac{\overline{M_z}}{\overline{M_w}} = \frac{69{,}574}{52{,}222} \approx 1.332$$

**Conclusion:** The high-MW fraction (only 10 chains out of 160) significantly elevates both $\overline{M_w}$ and $\overline{M_z}$, demonstrating the extreme sensitivity of these averages to heavy-chain tails.

### Derivation 4: Sedimentation Equilibrium Expression

For an ideal polymer solution at sedimentation equilibrium, the Boltzmann distribution gives:

$$c(r) = c(r_0) \exp\left[\frac{M \omega^2 (1-\overline{v}\rho)(r^2 - r_0^2)}{2RT}\right]$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln c(r) = \ln c(r_0) + \frac{M \omega^2 (1-\overline{v}\rho)(r^2 - r_0^2)}{2RT}$$

For a polydisperse system, $M$ in the above expression is the **effective average**, which experimentally corresponds to **Z-average**:

$$\frac{d \ln c}{d r^2} = \frac{\overline{M_z} \omega^2 (1-\overline{v}\rho)}{2RT}$$

Rearranging for $\overline{M_z}$:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{2RT}{(1-\overline{v}\rho)\omega^2} \cdot \frac{d \ln c}{d r^2}$$

This equation is the basis for **analytical ultracentrifuge determination** of Z-average molecular weight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Figure 1: Molecular Weight Average Hierarchy & Experimental Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Sample] --> B[Molecular Weight Distribution]
    B --> C[Statistical Moments]
    C --> D1[Mn: 1st Moment Ratio]
```
