```mermaid
    C --> D2[Mw: 2nd Moment Ratio]
    C --> D3[Mz: 3rd Moment Ratio]
    D1 --> E1[Osmometry / Ebulliometry / End-group analysis]
    D2 --> E2[Light Scattering / Viscosity / GPC]
    D3 --> E3[Analytical Ultracentrifuge / Sedimentation Equilibrium]
    D1 --> F[Weighting: M power 1]
    D2 --> G[Weighting: M power 2]
    D3 --> H[Weighting: M power 3]
    H --> I[Most sensitive to heavy chains]
    G --> J[Sensitive to heavy chains]
    F --> K[Equal weight to all chains]
    E3 --> L[Z-Average Molecular Weight Result]
```

### Figure 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Z-Average Determination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Sample Input Stage]
        A1[Polymer Solution]
        A2[Known Concentration c0]
        A3[Solvent Density rho]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[Experimental Control Stage]
        B1[Analytical Ultracentrifuge]
        B2[Angular Velocity omega]
        B3[Cell Temperature T]
    end
    subgraph DETECTION[Measurement Stage]
        C1[Concentration Profile c r]
        C2[Optical Interferometry]
        C3[Slope d ln c over d r squared]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Calculation Stage]
        D1[Apply Z Average Equation]
        D2[Substitute Constants R, T, v bar]
        D3[Solve for Mz]
    end
    INPUT --> CONTROL
    CONTROL --> DETECTION
    DETECTION --> COMPUTE
    D3 --> E[Final Z Average Molecular Weight]
```

### Figure 3: Schematic of Molecular Weight Sensitivity Across Averages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Polymer Chain Population] --> Q1[100 Light Chains at 10000 g/mol]
    P --> Q2[10 Heavy Chains at 100000 g/mol]
    Q1 --> R1[Contribution to Mn: 100 times 10000]
    Q1 --> R2[Contribution to Mw: 100 times 100000000]
    Q1 --> R3[Contribution to Mz: 100 times 1000000000000]
    Q2 --> S1[Contribution to Mn: 10 times 100000]
    Q2 --> S2[Contribution to Mw: 10 times 10000000000]
    Q2 --> S3[Contribution to Mz: 10 times 1000000000000000]
    R3 --> T[Heavy Chain Dominates Mz by Factor 1000 times]
    S3 --> T
    T --> U[Z Average Strongly Biased Toward Heavy Chains]
```

### Figure 4: Comparative Sensitivity Diagram (Bar Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Chain Type] --> Y1[Light Chain 10 kg per mol]
    X --> Y2[Medium Chain 50 kg per mol]
    X --> Y3[Heavy Chain 100 kg per mol]
    Y1 --> Z1[Relative Contribution to Mn: 1.0]
    Y1 --> Z2[Relative Contribution to Mw: 1.0]
    Y1 --> Z3[Relative Contribution to Mz: 1.0]
    Y2 --> Z4[Relative Contribution to Mn: 5.0]
    Y2 --> Z5[Relative Contribution to Mw: 25.0]
    Y2 --> Z6[Relative Contribution to Mz: 125.0]
    Y3 --> Z7[Relative Contribution to Mn: 10.0]
    Y3 --> Z8[Relative Contribution to Mw: 100.0]
    Y3 --> Z9[Relative Contribution to Mz: 1000.0]
    Z9 --> W[Mz Exponentially Amplifies Heavy Chain Influence]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define Z-average molecular weight of a polymer. Mention the experimental technique used to determine it.

**Model Answer:**
The Z-average molecular weight ($\overline{M_z}$) is defined as the ratio of the sum of the cubes of the molecular weights to the sum of the squares of the molecular weights of all molecules in a polymer sample:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$$

It is the **third moment ratio** of the molecular weight distribution and emphasizes the **high-molecular-weight fractions** more strongly than $\overline{M_n}$ or $\overline{M_w}$. It is experimentally determined using **analytical ultracentrifugation**, specifically the **sedimentation equilibrium method**, where the equilibrium concentration gradient yields $\overline{M_z}$ directly via the relation:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{2RT}{(1-\overline{v}\rho)\omega^2} \cdot \frac{d \ln c}{dr^2}$$

**[3 Marks Breakdown]:** Definition — 1.5 marks, Formula — 0.5 marks, Experimental technique — 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** For any real polymer sample, the inequality $\overline{M_z} \geq \overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ always holds. Justify the inequality with respect to the **Z-average**.

**Model Answer:**
For a polydisperse polymer, molecules of different molecular weights coexist. The Z-average weights each molecule's contribution by the **square of its mass** ($M^2$), so a single heavy molecule contributes far more to $\overline{M_z}$ than to $\overline{M_w}$ (weighted by $M^1$) or $\overline{M_n}$ (weighted by $M^0$, i.e., unweighted count).

Since heavy molecules disproportionately elevate the Z-average, it is always **greater than or equal to** $\overline{M_w}$. Equality holds only when **all molecules have identical molecular weight** (monodisperse case), where the sample behaves as a single species.

Hence, by the **Jensen's inequality** applied to the convex function $f(x) = x^2$:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\sum w_i M_i^2}{\sum w_i M_i} \geq \overline{M_w}$$

**[3 Marks Breakdown]:** Conceptual justification — 1.5 marks, Mass-squared weighting argument — 1 mark, Equality condition — 0.5 mark.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for Z-average molecular weight from the general moment definition of average molecular weights. Discuss the significance of each term involved. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A polymer sample has the following composition:

| Fraction | $N_i$ | $M_i$ (g/mol) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 200 | 20,000 |
| 2 | 100 | 60,000 |
| 3 | 50 | 80,000 |
| 4 | 10 | 150,000 |

Calculate $\overline{M_n}$, $\overline{M_w}$, and $\overline{M_z}$. Comment on the polydispersity of the sample. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The general moment definition of average molecular weight is:

$$\overline{M}_{\alpha} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{\alpha+1}}{\sum N_i M_i^{\alpha}}$$

For Z-average, we choose $\alpha = 2$:

$$\overline{M}_z = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{2+1}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum N_i M_i^{2}}$$

**Significance of Terms:**
- $N_i M_i^3$: The **third moment** — contribution of each molecule weighted by its molecular weight cubed.
- $N_i M_i^2$: The **second moment** — total weight of molecules normalized.
- Ratio: Gives the **Z-average molecular weight**, biased toward heavier chains.

The Z-average arises naturally in **sedimentation equilibrium** because the concentration gradient depends on $M^2$ (squared mass weighting, hence "Z"). It is critical for understanding **viscoelastic behavior**, **zero-shear viscosity**, and **high-MW tail effects** in polymer processing.

**[7 Marks Breakdown]:** Moment definition — 2 marks, Substitution of $\alpha = 2$ — 1 mark, Final formula — 1 mark, Significance of each term — 2 marks, Applications — 1 mark.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Compute required terms:

| $i$ | $N_i$ | $M_i$ | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^{2}$ | $N_i M_i^{3}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 200 | 20,000 | $4 \times 10^{6}$ | $8 \times 10^{10}$ | $1.6 \times 10^{15}$ |
| 2 | 100 | 60,000 | $6 \times 10^{6}$ | $3.6 \times 10^{11}$ | $2.16 \times 10^{16}$ |
| 3 | 50 | 80,000 | $4 \times 10^{6}$ | $3.2 \times 10^{11}$ | $2.56 \times 10^{16}$ |
| 4 | 10 | 150,000 | $1.5 \times 10^{6}$ | $2.25 \times 10^{11}$ | $3.375 \times 10^{16}$ |
| **Sum** | **360** | — | **$1.55 \times 10^{7}$** | **$9.85 \times 10^{11}$** | **$8.351 \times 10^{16}$** |

**Number-Average:**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{1.55 \times 10^{7}}{360} = 43{,}056 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Weight-Average:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{9.85 \times 10^{11}}{1.55 \times 10^{7}} = 63{,}548 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Z-Average:**

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{8.351 \times 10^{16}}{9.85 \times 10^{11}} = 84{,}782 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Polydispersity Ratios:**

$$\frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{63{,}548}{43{,}056} \approx 1.476$$

$$\frac{\overline{M_z}}{\overline{M_w}} = \frac{84{,}782}{63{,}548} \approx 1.334$$

**Comment on Polydispersity:**
The ratios $> 1$ confirm the sample is **polydisperse**. The presence of fraction 4 (only 10 molecules at 150,000 g/mol) significantly inflates both $\overline{M_w}$ and $\overline{M_z}$, showing the **heavy-tail sensitivity**. The sample is **moderately polydisperse**, suitable for general-purpose applications but not for high-performance engineering.

**[7 Marks Breakdown]:** Tabular calculation — 3 marks, Three averages — 2 marks, Polydispersity ratios — 1 mark, Comment — 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain how Z-average molecular weight is determined experimentally using the **analytical ultracentrifuge**. Derive the relevant equation starting from the Boltzmann distribution law. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Why is Z-average more sensitive to high-molecular-weight fractions than weight-average? Support your answer with a numerical example involving two polymer fractions of molecular weights 10,000 g/mol and 100,000 g/mol. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Ultracentrifuge Method:**

In an analytical ultracentrifuge, a polymer solution is spun at high angular velocity $\omega$. At sedimentation equilibrium, the concentration gradient along the radius $r$ follows the Boltzmann distribution:

$$c(r) = c(r_0) \exp\left[\frac{M \omega^2 (1-\overline{v}\rho)(r^2 - r_0^2)}{2RT}\right]$$

Taking natural logarithm and differentiating with respect to $r^2$:

$$\frac{d \ln c}{d r^2} = \frac{M \omega^2 (1-\overline{v}\rho)}{2RT}$$

For a polydisperse system, the effective $M$ obtained from this slope is the **Z-average molecular weight**:

$$\boxed{\overline{M_z} = \frac{2RT}{(1-\overline{v}\rho)\omega^2} \cdot \frac{d \ln c}{dr^2}}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Fill sample cell with polymer solution of known concentration $c_0$.
2. Spin at constant $\omega$ until equilibrium is reached.
3. Measure concentration profile $c(r)$ via optical interferometry or Schlieren optics.
4. Plot $\ln c$ vs $r^2$; the slope gives $\overline{M_z}$.

**Variables:**
- $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\overline{v}$ = partial specific volume of polymer (m³/kg)
- $\rho$ = solvent density (kg/m³)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

**[7 Marks Breakdown]:** Boltzmann setup — 2 marks, Logarithmic manipulation — 1.5 marks, Final $\overline{M_z}$ equation — 1.5 marks, Experimental procedure — 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) — Sensitivity Comparison:**

**Numerical Example:**

Consider two fractions of equal **mass** (100 g each):
- Fraction A: 100 g of chains at 10,000 g/mol → $N_A = \dfrac{100}{10{,}000} \times N_A^{Av} = 6.022 \times 10^{18}$ molecules (per gram basis, use $N_i$ proportional to $\frac{1}{M_i}$).

For simplicity, take **proportional molecule counts**:
- Fraction A: $N_A = 100$ molecules at $M_A = 10{,}000$ g/mol
- Fraction B: $N_B = 10$ molecules at $M_B = 100{,}000$ g/mol

**Contribution to $\overline{M_w}$ numerator:**

$$N_A M_A^2 + N_B M_B^2 = (100)(10^8) + (10)(10^{10}) = 10^{10} + 10^{11} = 1.1 \times 10^{11}$$

Fraction B contributes $\dfrac{10^{11}}{1.1 \times 10^{11}} \approx 90.9\%$ to $\overline{M_w}$ numerator.

**Contribution to $\overline{M_z}$ numerator:**

$$N_A M_A^3 + N_B M_B^3 = (100)(10^{12}) + (10)(10^{15}) = 10^{14} + 10^{16} = 1.01 \times 10^{16}$$

Fraction B contributes $\dfrac{10^{16}}{1.01 \times 10^{16}} \approx 99.0\%$ to $\overline{M_z}$ numerator.

**Conclusion:**
Fraction B (heavy chains) contributes **99%** to $\overline{M_z}$ but only **90.9%** to $\overline{M_w}$, confirming that Z-average is **more biased** toward heavy chains due to its $M^3$ weighting versus $M^2$ in $\overline{M_w}$.

**[7 Marks Breakdown]:** Conceptual explanation — 2 marks, Numerical setup — 1 mark, $\overline{M_w}$ contribution calculation — 1.5 marks, $\overline{M_z}$ contribution calculation — 1.5 marks, Comparison and conclusion — 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\overline{M_z}$ with $\overline{M_w}$ formula:** Students often write $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$. The correct Z-average uses $M^3$ in the numerator and $M^2$ in the denominator.
> 2. **Reversing the inequality:** Always write $\overline{M_z} \geq \overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$, **never** the reverse.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** All averages must be quoted in **g/mol** or **kg/kmol**.
> 4. **Skipping the experimental context:** KTU ESE expects mention of the **analytical ultracentrifuge / sedimentation equilibrium** method. Students lose 1–2 marks by merely stating the formula without experimental relevance.
> 5. **Incorrect interpretation of polydispersity:** $\dfrac{\overline{M_z}}{\overline{M_w}} = 1$ does **not** always mean monodisperse — it only implies uniform mass weighting. The full condition for monodispersity is $\overline{M_z} = \overline{M_w} = \overline{M_n}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$ — third moment ratio.
- **Hierarchy:** $\overline{M_z} \geq \overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ (strict inequality for polydisperse samples).
- **Monodisperse case:** All three averages are equal: $\overline{M_z} = \overline{M_w} = \overline{M_n}$.
- **Experimental determination:** Analytical ultracentrifuge / sedimentation equilibrium method.
- **Key equation:** $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{2RT}{(1-\overline{v}\rho)\omega^2} \cdot \dfrac{d \ln c}{dr^2}$.
- **Sensitivity:** Z-average gives **1000× weight** to a chain that is 10× heavier — extreme bias toward heavy chains.
- **Polydispersity ratio (Z/W):** $\dfrac{\overline{M_z}}{\overline{M_w}}$ measures distribution breadth in the high-MW region.
- **Applications:** Zero-shear viscosity, melt elasticity, fiber spinnability, impact strength, mechanical toughness.
- **Distinguishing feature:** Z-average is the most "high-mass-biased" of all polymer molecular weight averages.
- **Practical significance:** Sensitive probe for chain branching, cross-linking, and the presence of unwanted high-MW aggregates in industrial polymers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polydispersity Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polydispersity Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polydispersity Index (PDI)**, also called the **Heterogeneity Index** or **Dispersity (Đ)**, is a measure of the **distribution of molecular weights** in a given polymer sample. Mathematically, it is defined as the **ratio of the weight-average molecular weight ($\bar{M}_w$) to the number-average molecular weight ($\bar{M}_n$)**:
> $$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$$
> A PDI value of **1.0** indicates a **monodisperse** polymer (theoretically possible only for proteins and certain living polymerizations such as anionic polymerization). Most synthetic polymers are **polydisperse**, with PDI values typically ranging from **1.5 to 2.0** for step-growth polymers, and **1.02 to 1.5** for controlled/living chain-growth polymers.

> [!NOTE]
> In the modern IUPAC recommended nomenclature, the symbol **Đ (D with stroke)** is preferred over "PDI" because the latter is a ratio, not a true index. However, KTU examination papers continue to widely use the term **PDI**, and students are expected to be fluent in both notations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a classroom of 100 students asked to stand in a line by **height**:

- **If every student is exactly the same height (e.g., 5'6")** — the line is "perfectly uniform." This is analogous to a **monodisperse** polymer where every chain has the *same* molecular weight. The PDI = 1.0.

- **If heights range from 5'0" to 6'2"** — there is a noticeable **spread** or inequality. This represents a typical **polydisperse** polymer. The PDI > 1.0.

- **If the range is wild (some 4'5", some 6'8")** — the spread is enormous, like a polymer made by an uncontrolled free-radical process, giving a high PDI (often 2.0–5.0).

The two students counting the average will get different answers:
- One who counts **each student equally** (number-average) sees the *typical* student.
- One who weighs by **height** (weight-average) is more influenced by the **tall students** (the heavy chains).

The bigger the gap between these two "averages," the **more polydisperse** the population.

## 1.3 Why PDI Matters in Polymer Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> - **Mechanical properties** (tensile strength, impact resistance) depend on the *weight-average* molecular weight because long chains bear most of the stress.
> - **Melt viscosity, flow behavior, and processability** depend on the *number-average* molecular weight.
> - **Crystallinity and optical clarity** of polymers are highly sensitive to PDI — narrower distributions give sharper, more predictable melting points and optical behavior.
> - **Biomedical and drug-delivery polymers** require very low PDI (≤ 1.1) to ensure uniform degradation, predictable pharmacokinetics, and reproducible performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundation: Molecular Weight Averages

Synthetic polymers are mixtures of chains of different lengths. We cannot assign a single molecular weight; we must use **statistical averages**. The two most important are:

### (a) Number-Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_n$)

This is the **simple arithmetic mean** of molecular weights, weighted by the **number of molecules** of each size. It is sensitive to the *presence of small molecules* (oligomers, unreacted monomer).

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i}$$

where:
- $N_i$ = number of molecules having molecular weight $M_i$
- $M_i$ = molecular weight of the $i$-th species

**Determined experimentally by:** Osmometry, Vapor Pressure Osmometry (VPO), End-Group Analysis, Membrane Osmometry, Freezing Point Depression.

### (b) Weight-Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_w$)

This is biased toward the **heavier (longer) molecules** because each molecule contributes to the average in proportion to *its own mass*. It is highly sensitive to the presence of long chains.

$$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i}$$

**Determined experimentally by:** Static Light Scattering (SLS), Sedimentation Velocity, Sedimentation Equilibrium.

### (c) Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_v$)

$$\bar{M}_v = \left[\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i}\right]^{1/a}$$

where $a$ is the **Mark–Houwink exponent** (typically 0.5 to 0.8). Note: $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_v \leq \bar{M}_w$.

### (d) Z-Average Molecular Weight ($\bar{M}_z$)

$$\bar{M}_z = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i^3}{\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_i M_i^2}$$

Used in ultracentrifugation and Size Exclusion Chromatography (SEC) with light scattering detection.

## 2.2 The Master Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Universal Inequality** (always true for any real polymer):
> $$\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_v \leq \bar{M}_w \leq \bar{M}_z$$
> Equality holds on all sides **only if and only if** the polymer is **monodisperse** (all chains identical in length).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Experimental Method | Typical Units |
|---|----------|--------|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Number-Avg MW | $\bar{M}_n$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Osmometry, VPO, End-Group Analysis | g/mol |
| 2 | Weight-Avg MW | $\bar{M}_w$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Static Light Scattering, Sedimentation | g/mol |
| 3 | Viscosity-Avg MW | $\bar{M}_v$ | $\left[\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum N_i M_i}\right]^{1/a}$ | Viscometry (intrinsic viscosity) | g/mol |
| 4 | Z-Average MW | $\bar{M}_z$ | $\dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$ | Ultracentrifugation, SEC-MALS | g/mol |
| 5 | Polydispersity Index | PDI / Đ | $\dfrac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Computed from 1 and 2 | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Most Probable Dist. (Flory) | $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ | $= 1 + \dfrac{1}{x_n}$ | Step-growth at high conversion | — |
| 7 | Degree of Polymerization | $x_n$ | $\dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$ | Computed | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The **number-fraction** of species $i$ is $n_i = N_i / \sum N_i$, and the **weight-fraction** is $w_i = N_i M_i / \sum N_i M_i$. The two averages can equivalently be written in terms of these fractions. Many KTU problems use **weight-fractions** $w_i$ with the corresponding midpoint $M_i$ of a molecular weight range — be alert to which basis the data is given in.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Processing & Rheology**: A polymer with high PDI flows poorly in injection molding because long chains cause **chain entanglements** while short chains act as lubricants. Knowing the PDI lets the engineer tune the processing window.
- **Predicting Mechanical Performance**: $\bar{M}_w$ governs tensile strength and impact resistance, while $\bar{M}_n$ governs brittleness and ease of failure. PDI informs the *balance*.
- **Quality Control**: In commercial polymer manufacturing, SEC traces are run routinely; the resulting $\bar{M}_w$, $\bar{M}_n$, and PDI are batch-release specifications.
- **Specialty Applications**: Drug-eluting stents, intraocular lenses, and hydrogels for tissue scaffolds demand **Đ ≤ 1.1**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof That $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$ (Always)

This is a high-yield derivation that KTU examiners love to test.

**Step 1 — Write the difference:**

$$\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} - \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

**Step 2 — Bring to a common denominator (the denominator becomes positive and non-zero, so the sign of the difference is determined by the numerator):**

$$\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n = \frac{\left(\sum N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum N_i\right) - \left(\sum N_i M_i\right)^2}{\left(\sum N_i M_i\right)\left(\sum N_i\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the numerator as the Cauchy–Schwarz (or variance-like) form. Define $a_i = \sqrt{N_i}\, M_i$ and $b_i = \sqrt{N_i}$. Then the numerator is:**

$$\left(\sum a_i b_i\right)^2 - \text{... actually let us expand explicitly:}$$

Let us expand both terms in the numerator.

$$\left(\sum N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum N_i\right) = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i M_i^2 \, N_j$$

$$\left(\sum N_i M_i\right)^2 = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i M_i \, N_j M_j = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i N_j M_i M_j$$

**Step 4 — Take the difference (the cross terms in the double sum):**

$$\text{Numerator} = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i N_j \left(M_i^2 - M_i M_j\right) = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i N_j M_i \left(M_i - M_j\right)$$

**Step 5 — Symmetrize the double sum (swap $i \leftrightarrow j$) and add to itself:**

$$\text{Numerator} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i N_j \left[M_i\left(M_i - M_j\right) + M_j\left(M_j - M_i\right)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i N_j \left(M_i - M_j\right)^2$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:**

$$\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n = \frac{\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i}\sum_{j} N_i N_j \left(M_i - M_j\right)^2}{\left(\sum N_i M_i\right)\left(\sum N_i\right)}$$

Every term on the right is **non-negative** ($N_i \geq 0$, $M_i \geq 0$, and the square is always $\geq 0$). The denominator is strictly positive. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n}$$

with equality **iff** $M_i = M_j$ for all $i,j$ with $N_i, N_j > 0$, i.e., the sample is **monodisperse**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the same algebra that proves the **variance of any distribution is non-negative**. The quantity $\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n$ is essentially a measure of the *squared spread* of molecular weights, weighted by the number of molecules.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (Numerical Estimation Type)

**Problem:** A polymer sample has the following molecular weight distribution obtained by SEC:

| Fraction $i$ | Midpoint $M_i$ (g/mol) | Mass fraction $w_i$ |
|:------------:|:-----------------------:|:-------------------:|
| 1 | 10 000 | 0.10 |
| 2 | 30 000 | 0.25 |
| 3 | 50 000 | 0.35 |
| 4 | 70 000 | 0.20 |
| 5 | 90 000 | 0.10 |

Calculate $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, and the PDI.

**Step 1 — Recognize the data is in weight-fraction form.** When data is given in $w_i$, we use:

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1}{\sum \frac{w_i}{M_i}} \quad ; \quad \bar{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum w_i M_i$ for $\bar{M}_w$:**

$$\bar{M}_w = (0.10)(10{,}000) + (0.25)(30{,}000) + (0.35)(50{,}000) + (0.20)(70{,}000) + (0.10)(90{,}000)$$

$$= 1{,}000 + 7{,}500 + 17{,}500 + 14{,}000 + 9{,}000 = 49{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sum (w_i / M_i)$ for $\bar{M}_n$:**

$$\sum \frac{w_i}{M_i} = \frac{0.10}{10{,}000} + \frac{0.25}{30{,}000} + \frac{0.35}{50{,}000} + \frac{0.20}{70{,}000} + \frac{0.10}{90{,}000}$$

$$= 1.0\times10^{-5} + 8.333\times10^{-6} + 7.0\times10^{-6} + 2.857\times10^{-6} + 1.111\times10^{-6}$$

$$= 2.930\times10^{-5} \text{ mol/g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\bar{M}_n$:**

$$\bar{M}_n = \frac{1}{2.930\times10^{-5}} = 34{,}130 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Compute PDI:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{M}_w}{\bar{M}_n} = \frac{49{,}000}{34{,}130} \approx 1.436$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result:** A PDI of 1.44 is consistent with a **moderately polydisperse polymer**, typical of an anionic or controlled radical polymerization (e.g., ATRP, RAFT). It indicates a relatively narrow distribution with some low-MW tail, but it is far narrower than a typical free-radical step-growth polymer (PDI ≈ 2.0).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | $\bar{M}_n$ | $\bar{M}_w$ | PDI |
|---------|:-----------:|:-----------:|:---:|
| Sensitivity to small chains | High | Low | Combination |
| Sensitivity to large chains | Low | High | Combination |
| Determines tensile strength? | No (minor) | Yes (dominant) | No directly |
| Determines melt viscosity? | Yes (primary) | Secondary | Modifies |
| Equals 1 for monodisperse? | Trivially | Trivially | Yes |
| Always $\geq$ its counterpart? | $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_w$ | $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Experimental technique | Osmometry | Light Scattering | Ratio |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Cue:** When a question provides a graph of $w(\log M)$ vs $\log M$ (typical SEC output), a **taller, narrower** peak represents a **lower PDI** (more uniform), and a **shorter, broader** peak represents a **higher PDI**. Always relate visual width to the numerical PDI for full marks in descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: Define Polydispersity Index. Explain its significance with at least two practical implications in polymer processing.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** is defined as the ratio of the **weight-average molecular weight ($\bar{M}_w$)** to the **number-average molecular weight ($\bar{M}_n$)**:
> $$\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w \,/\, \bar{M}_n$$
> It quantifies the **breadth of the molecular weight distribution** of a polymer sample. A PDI of **1.0** indicates a monodisperse polymer, while higher values indicate increasing heterogeneity.
>
> **Significance in processing:**
> 1. **Melt viscosity & flow:** A high PDI broadens the molecular weight distribution, causing poor flow and inconsistent die-swell in extrusion, because long chains dominate viscous response while short chains dominate diffusion.
> 2. **Mechanical performance:** A narrow PDI gives more reproducible tensile strength, impact resistance, and predictable failure modes — critical for engineering plastics and biomedical devices.
> 3. **Quality control:** Manufacturers specify PDI as a release criterion; e.g., ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) for joint replacements requires a narrow distribution to ensure uniform wear behavior.

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: Distinguish between $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{M}_w$ with respect to their mathematical definitions, experimental determination, and physical sensitivity.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | $\bar{M}_n$ | $\bar{M}_w$ |
> |--------|-------------|-------------|
> | **Definition** | $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ | $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ |
> | **Bias** | Sensitive to *small* chains (low MW) | Sensitive to *large* chains (high MW) |
> | **Experimental** | Osmometry, VPO, End-group analysis | Static Light Scattering (SLS), Sedimentation |
> | **Physical meaning** | Total mass divided by total number of chains | Mass-weighted average of chain sizes |
>
> For any polydisperse polymer, $\bar{M}_w > \bar{M}_n$, and the ratio $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ equals the PDI. Both averages are essential because they reflect different physical properties: $\bar{M}_n$ controls **colligative properties** and **melt flow**, while $\bar{M}_w$ controls **mechanical strength** and **light scattering behavior**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A [14 Marks] — Derivation + Calculation**

> [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2 Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the expressions for $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{M}_w$ in terms of the number-fractions $n_i$ of the molecular species. Show mathematically that $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$ for any polymer sample.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Number-fraction definition:** $n_i = N_i \,/\, N$, where $N = \sum N_i$ is the total number of molecules.

2. **Express $\bar{M}_n$ in terms of $n_i$:**

   $$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{\sum (N_i / N) M_i}{\sum (N_i / N)} = \frac{\sum n_i M_i}{1} = \sum n_i M_i$$

3. **Express $\bar{M}_w$ in terms of $n_i$:**

   $$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum (N_i / N) M_i^2}{\sum (N_i / N) M_i} = \frac{\sum n_i M_i^2}{\sum n_i M_i}$$

4. **Now prove $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$. Compute the difference:**

   $$\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum n_i M_i^2}{\sum n_i M_i} - \sum n_i M_i$$

5. **Common denominator:**

   $$\bar{M}_w - \bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum n_i M_i^2 - \left(\sum n_i M_i\right)^2}{\sum n_i M_i}$$

6. **Expand the square and apply the identity (variance is non-negative):**

   $$\sum n_i M_i^2 - \left(\sum n_i M_i\right)^2 = \sum_i n_i \left(M_i - \bar{M}_n\right)^2 \geq 0$$

7. **Conclusion:** Numerator $\geq 0$, denominator $> 0$, so $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$. Equality holds iff $M_i = \bar{M}_n$ for all $i$ with $n_i > 0$, i.e., the sample is monodisperse. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A polymer sample has the following composition by number of molecules:**

| Species $i$ | $M_i$ (g/mol) | Number of molecules $N_i$ |
|:-----------:|:-------------:|:------------------------:|
| 1 | 5 000 | 100 |
| 2 | 15 000 | 200 |
| 3 | 25 000 | 300 |
| 4 | 35 000 | 250 |
| 5 | 45 000 | 150 |

**Calculate $\bar{M}_n$, $\bar{M}_w$, and the PDI. Comment on the result.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Tabulate the required products:**

   | $M_i$ | $N_i$ | $N_i M_i$ | $N_i M_i^2$ |
   |:-----:|:-----:|:---------:|:-----------:|
   | 5 000 | 100 | 500 000 | $2.5\times10^9$ |
   | 15 000 | 200 | 3 000 000 | $4.5\times10^{10}$ |
   | 25 000 | 300 | 7 500 000 | $1.875\times10^{11}$ |
   | 35 000 | 250 | 8 750 000 | $3.0625\times10^{11}$ |
   | 45 000 | 150 | 6 750 000 | $3.0375\times10^{11}$ |
   | **Sum** | **1000** | **26 500 000** | **8.475×10¹¹** |

2. **Compute $\bar{M}_n$:**

   $$\bar{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{2.65\times10^7}{1000} = 26{,}500 \text{ g/mol}$$

3. **Compute $\bar{M}_w$:**

   $$\bar{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{8.475\times10^{11}}{2.65\times10^7} = 31{,}981 \text{ g/mol}$$

4. **Compute PDI:**

   $$\text{PDI} = \frac{31{,}981}{26{,}500} \approx 1.207$$

5. **Comment:** A PDI of **1.21** indicates a **moderately narrow** distribution, typical of a well-controlled chain-growth polymerization or a step-growth polymer at low conversion. The polymer is reasonably uniform but not perfectly monodisperse, suggesting it was synthesized by a method with some statistical spread in chain length.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] — Concept + Numerical**

> [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 2 Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss the factors that affect the polydispersity index of a polymer synthesized by step-growth polymerization. Explain how the PDI evolves as a function of conversion $p$ and derive the limiting value at $p \to 1$.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Definition of the Most-Probable (Flory) Distribution:** For ideal step-growth polymerization (A–A + B–B or A–B) with equal reactivity of functional groups, Flory showed that the number-fraction of $x$-mers is:

   $$n_x = (1-p)\, p^{x-1}$$

   where $p$ is the fraction of functional groups that have reacted.

2. **Number-average degree of polymerization:**

   $$\bar{x}_n = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \, n_x = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x (1-p) p^{x-1} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

3. **Weight-fraction of $x$-mers:**

   $$w_x = \frac{x n_x}{\sum x n_x} = x(1-p)^2 p^{x-1}$$

4. **Weight-average degree of polymerization:**

   $$\bar{x}_w = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \, w_x = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2 (1-p)^2 p^{x-1} = \frac{1+p}{1-p}$$

5. **Polydispersity index of step-growth polymers (the Flory–Schulz result):**

   $$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{x}_w}{\bar{x}_n} = \frac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1 + p$$

6. **Limiting value as $p \to 1$ (full conversion):**

   $$\boxed{\lim_{p \to 1} \text{PDI} = 2.0}$$

7. **Discussion of factors:**
   - **Stoichiometric imbalance:** If the ratio $r = N_A / N_B < 1$, the maximum achievable $\bar{x}_n$ is reduced, but the limiting PDI still approaches 2.
   - **Unequal reactivity** of functional groups: broadens the distribution; PDI can exceed 2.
   - **Side reactions (cyclization, branching):** increase heterogeneity → PDI > 2.
   - **Reaction mechanism:** Living polymerizations give PDI ≈ 1.0; free-radical gives PDI = 1.5–2.0; step-growth gives PDI ≈ 2.0 at high conversion.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] For a step-growth polymerization of adipic acid with ethylene glycol carried to 95% conversion, calculate (i) $\bar{x}_n$, (ii) $\bar{x}_w$, and (iii) the PDI. Also compute the same quantities at $p = 0.99$. Comment on the engineering implications.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**At $p = 0.95$:**

(i) $\bar{x}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20$

(ii) $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p} = \dfrac{1.95}{0.05} = 39$

(iii) $\text{PDI} = 1 + p = 1.95$

**At $p = 0.99$:**

(i) $\bar{x}_n = \dfrac{1}{0.01} = 100$

(ii) $\bar{x}_w = \dfrac{1.99}{0.01} = 199$

(iii) $\text{PDI} = 1.99$

**Comment / Engineering implications:**

- As conversion approaches 100%, $\bar{x}_n$ grows sharply (a doubling from 20 to 100 as $p$ moves from 0.95 to 0.99). This is the **Carothers' law** of step-growth polymerization: high molecular weight is *unlocked only at very high conversions*.
- The PDI **asymptotically approaches 2.0**, never falling below it, regardless of how carefully the stoichiometry is balanced. This is the **fingerprint of step-growth polymers** — even the "best" commercial polyester or polyamide has PDI ≈ 2.
- **Engineering implication:** A polyester at $p = 0.95$ has $\bar{M}_n$ too low for mechanical applications (brittle, low impact strength); the reactor must be driven to $p > 0.99$. However, reaching $p = 0.99$ requires extreme stoichiometric control, removal of byproducts (water), and prolonged reaction times — explaining why commercial polycondensation reactors run for many hours under vacuum.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $N_i$ and $w_i$ data:** If the problem gives *mass fractions* $w_i$ (as in SEC chromatograms), you **must** use $\bar{M}_n = 1/\sum(w_i/M_i)$ and $\bar{M}_w = \sum w_i M_i$. Using $w_i$ in place of $N_i$ in the $\bar{M}_n$ formula is the **#1 error** flagged in KTU valuation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units consistency:** Always quote molecular weights in **g/mol**. Mixing g/mol with kg/mol within the same calculation is an automatic ½ mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Assuming PDI < 1 is possible:** Some students confuse themselves with viscosity averages and write contradictory inequalities. Remember: $\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n \geq 1$ **always**, and equality is reserved for monodisperse samples.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the strict inequality $\bar{M}_w \geq \bar{M}_n$ in derivation parts:** The algebraic proof (using the variance identity) is a **full 4-mark** sub-question in many past papers. Skipping the "≥ 0" interpretation step loses 1 mark easily.
>
> 5. **Confusing the Flory limiting PDI of 2 with general PDI = 2:** The result $\text{PDI} \to 2$ as $p \to 1$ is **specific to ideal step-growth with equal reactivity**. Branching, cyclization, or non-equal reactivity push PDI above 2.
>
> 6. **Not writing the final sentence "engineering comment":** Many 7-mark and 14-mark questions explicitly ask "comment on the result." Even a single sentence relating the numerical answer to real processing behavior is worth **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Polydispersity Index**
>
> - **Definition:** $\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$. Always $\geq 1$.
> - **Monodisperse:** $\text{PDI} = 1.0$ (theoretical: proteins, anionic living polymers).
> - **Typical values:**
>   - Step-growth: $\text{PDI} \to 2.0$ as $p \to 1$.
>   - Free-radical chain-growth: $\text{PDI} \approx 1.5$–$2.0$.
>   - Controlled/living radical (RAFT, ATRP, NMP): $\text{PDI} \approx 1.05$–$1.3$.
>   - Natural polymers (proteins): $\text{PDI} = 1.0$.
> - **Key formulas to memorize:**
>   - $\bar{M}_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i = \sum n_i M_i = 1 / \sum (w_i/M_i)$.
>   - $\bar{M}_w = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i = \sum w_i M_i$.
>   - PDI step-growth: $1 + p$.
>   - PDI equals 1 + (1/$\bar{x}_n$) for ideal step-growth.
> - **Inequality chain:** $\bar{M}_n \leq \bar{M}_v \leq \bar{M}_w \leq \bar{M}_z$ (always).
> - **Methods to determine $\bar{M}_n$:** Osmometry, VPO, End-group analysis, Cryoscopy, Ebullioscopy.
> - **Methods to determine $\bar{M}_w$:** Static Light Scattering (SLS), Sedimentation (velocity / equilibrium).
> - **The ratio $\bar{M}_w/\bar{M}_n$ is the most common single number** used to characterize distribution breadth in industry and academia.
> - **IUPAC preferred notation:** Đ (D with stroke) = $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$.
> - **Engineering relevance:** Lower PDI → more uniform processing, sharper melting point, more predictable mechanical behavior, suitable for biomedical and optical applications.
> - **Higher PDI** → improved impact toughness (in some cases, the long-chain tail acts as a tie molecule), but worse flow and broader thermal transitions.

---

**End of Module 2 — Polydispersity Index | KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Osmotic Pressure Method and Viscometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Osmotic Pressure Method and Viscometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Osmotic Pressure Method — Formal Definition

**Osmotic Pressure (π)** is the hydrostatic pressure that must be applied to a polymer solution to prevent the inward flow of pure solvent across a **semipermeable membrane** (SPM) that is freely permeable to solvent molecules but completely impermeable to macromolecular solute chains. The technique yields the **Number-Average Molecular Weight (M̄ₙ)** of the polymer through the application of thermodynamic equilibrium and virial expansion of osmotic pressure data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The osmotic pressure method is one of the **absolute** (not requiring calibration with a known sample) methods for M̄ₙ determination, valid typically in the molecular weight range **2 × 10⁴ to 1.5 × 10⁶ g/mol**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a sealed box divided by a microscopic sieve. The sieve has holes just large enough for **water molecules** to slip through, but too small for the much larger **long polymer chains** (imagine trying to push a strand of cooked spaghetti through a window screen). When pure water is placed on one side and a sugar/polymer solution on the other, water naturally flows *into* the solution side trying to "dilute" it (a thermodynamic drive to equalize chemical potential). The pressure that builds up on the solution side as a consequence of this inflow is the **osmotic pressure**. By measuring how high a column of liquid it can support (or equivalently, the force per unit area), we can deduce how many polymer molecules were in the solution, and hence their average molecular weight.

### 1.2 The Viscometry Method — Formal Definition

**Viscometry** (also called **Solution Viscometry** or **Dilute Solution Viscometry**) is a relative method for determining the **Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (M̄ᵥ)** of a polymer by measuring the flow time of a polymer solution through a calibrated capillary and extracting the **Intrinsic Viscosity [η]**, which is then related to molecular weight through the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** M̄ᵥ obtained from viscometry lies between M̄ₙ and M̄_w (i.e., M̄ₙ ≤ M̄ᵥ ≤ M̄_w). The exponent α in the MHS equation controls where M̄ᵥ falls on this spectrum. When **α = 1**, M̄ᵥ = M̄_w; when **α → 0**, M̄ᵥ approaches M̄ₙ.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a polymer chain as a tangled ball of yarn swimming in honey. The bigger the yarn ball, the more it "drags" the honey and slows down the flow. If we time how long a fixed volume of honey-with-yarn takes to drain through a thin straw (compared to pure honey), we can estimate how big the yarn balls are. Long, floppy polymer chains entangle the solvent's flow lines (an effect captured mathematically as an increase in **viscosity**), so the longer the chains, the longer the flow time. Viscometry is the art of turning "how slow does it flow" into "how big are the molecules."

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Molar volume of water at 298 K:** **V₁⁰ = 18.07 cm³/mol**
- **Standard M̄ₙ working range:** **2 × 10⁴ to 1.5 × 10⁶ g/mol**
- **Standard M̄ᵥ working range (typical):** **10⁴ to 10⁷ g/mol**
- **Semipermeable membranes commonly used:** regenerated cellulose (Cellophane), cellulose acetate, gel cellophane, glass frit (dynamic osmometers)
- **Reference Temperature:** **T = 298 K (25 °C)** or **T = 303 K (30 °C)** (polymer-solvent specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When a question asks for the "molecular weight" without qualification, **osmotic pressure → M̄ₙ** and **viscometry → M̄ᵥ** by default. Never confuse these with M̄_w (light scattering / sedimentation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation of Osmotic Pressure

The driving force for osmosis is the **difference in chemical potential (μ)** of the solvent across the membrane. At equilibrium:

$$\mu_{1}^{0}(T, P) = \mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P + \pi)$$

Where subscript 1 denotes the solvent. Expanding the right-hand side via a Taylor series in pressure and rearranging gives the fundamental thermodynamic relationship:

$$\pi = -\frac{RT}{V_{1}^{0}} \ln a_{1} = -\frac{RT}{V_{1}^{0}} \ln(x_{1} \gamma_{1})$$

Where **V₁⁰** is the molar volume of the solvent, **a₁** is the activity, **x₁** is the mole fraction, and **γ₁** is the activity coefficient of the solvent.

For a polymer solution where the number of polymer molecules is much smaller than the number of solvent molecules, we can use the approximation:

$$\ln(1 - x_{2}) \approx -x_{2} - \frac{x_{2}^{2}}{2} - \frac{x_{2}^{3}}{3} - \cdots$$

Substituting and regrouping into a **virial expansion** in concentration:

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = \frac{RT}{M_{2}} + A_{2} RT\,c + A_{3} RT\,c^{2} + \cdots$$

Or, in a more compact standard form:

$$\frac{\pi}{c} = RT\left[\frac{1}{M_{2}} + \Gamma_{2} c + \Gamma_{3} c^{2} + \cdots \right]$$

The **second virial coefficient (A₂ or Γ₂)** is a sensitive indicator of polymer-solvent interactions:
- **A₂ > 0** → Good solvent (polymer coils are expanded)
- **A₂ = 0** → Theta (θ) solvent (polymer behaves ideally)
- **A₂ < 0** → Poor solvent (polymer coils are collapsed)

### 2.2 Operational Steps — Osmotic Pressure Method

1. **Membrane conditioning:** The SPM is conditioned to the working solvent to prevent asymmetric swelling.
2. **Solution preparation:** A series of polymer solutions of accurately known concentrations (typically 0.1 to 1 g/dL) is prepared.
3. **Equilibration:** The osmometer is filled and allowed to reach equilibrium at constant temperature **T**.
4. **Measurement:** The equilibrium height **h** of the capillary (or pressure differential) is recorded.
5. **Calculation of π:** π = ρgh (where ρ is the density difference between solution and pure solvent).
6. **Plotting:** A plot of (π/c) vs. c (or **reduced osmotic pressure**) is constructed.
7. **Extrapolation:** The best-fit straight line is extrapolated to **c = 0**.
8. **Molecular weight determination:** The y-intercept equals **RT / M̄ₙ**, hence:

$$M̄_{n} = \frac{RT}{\left(\dfrac{\pi}{c}\right)_{c \to 0}}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (No Vertical Pipes Used)

| **Equation** | **Formula** | **Application / Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| van't Hoff Law (ideal) | $\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{M_{2}}$ | Very dilute, ideal solutions | J·mol⁻¹ for R; g·mol⁻¹ for M |
| Virial expansion | $\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{M_{2}} + A_{2}RT\,c + A_{3}RT\,c^{2}$ | Real polymer solutions | c in g·cm⁻³ |
| Number-average MW | $M̄_{n} = \dfrac{RT}{(\pi/c)_{c \to 0}}$ | Osmotic pressure result | g·mol⁻¹ |
| Second virial coefficient | $A_{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(M_{2}^{2} / V_{1}^{0}\right) \chi$ (Flory-Huggins form) | Solvent quality indicator | cm³·mol·g⁻² |
| Relative viscosity | $\eta_{r} = \dfrac{\eta}{\eta_{0}} = \dfrac{t}{t_{0}}$ | From flow-time measurement | dimensionless |
| Specific viscosity | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{r} - 1$ | Excess viscosity due to polymer | dimensionless |
| Reduced viscosity | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ | Concentration-dependent | dL·g⁻¹ or cm³·g⁻¹ |
| Inherent viscosity | $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{\ln \eta_{r}}{c}$ | Alternative extrapolation | dL·g⁻¹ or cm³·g⁻¹ |
| Intrinsic viscosity | $[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0}\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = \lim_{c \to 0}\dfrac{\ln \eta_{r}}{c}$ | Single-molecular property | dL·g⁻¹ or cm³·g⁻¹ |
| Huggins equation | $\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_{H}[\eta]^{2} c$ | Linear extrapolation (above) | dL·g⁻¹ |
| Kraemer equation | $\dfrac{\ln \eta_{r}}{c} = [\eta] - k_{K}[\eta]^{2} c$ | Linear extrapolation (below) | dL·g⁻¹ |
| Mark–Houwink–Sakurada | $[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$ | Connects [η] to molecular weight | dL·g⁻¹ |
| Solvent-flow relation | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{r} - 1 \;\vert\; \eta \geq \eta_{0}$ | Viscosity inequality | dimensionless |
| Hagen-Poiseuille flow | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P\, t}{8 L V}$ | Capillary viscometer theory | Pa·s |
| Slope relation | $k_{H} + k_{K} \approx 0.5$ | Consistency check (Huggins-Kraemer) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Caution:** The intrinsic viscosity [η] is most commonly reported in **dL/g** (= 100 mL/g = 0.1 L/g) in polymer literature, but the SI-friendly form is **cm³·g⁻¹** (or m³·kg⁻¹). Forgetting the conversion by a factor of 100 is a classic KTU valuation error.

### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering Practice

- The **A₂ virial coefficient** is critical in selecting **good spinning solvents** for fiber spinning (e.g., wet spinning of cellulose, dry spinning of polyacrylonitrile).
- **[η]** is the workhorse for **routine quality control** in polymer manufacturing plants because viscometry is fast, cheap, and reliable.
- **M̄ₙ vs. M̄ᵥ**: Manufacturers of PVC, PS, and PMMA use M̄ₙ (osmometry) for **stoichiometric dosing** in condensation reactions, while M̄ᵥ (viscometry) is used for **mechanical performance** correlations (e.g., impact strength, melt viscosity).
- The **Mark–Houwink constants K and α** are tabulated for hundreds of polymer-solvent-temperature triples (the **Brandrup–Immergut Polymer Handbook**).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Osmotic Pressure Equation (van't Hoff to Virial Form)

**Starting Principle:** Thermodynamic equilibrium of chemical potential across the semipermeable membrane.

At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the pure solvent at pressure P must equal the chemical potential of the solvent in the polymer solution at pressure **P + π** (because the solution side must support an extra hydrostatic pressure π to stop the inflow of solvent).

$$\mu_{1}^{0}(T, P) = \mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P + \pi)$$

**Step 1: Expand the right-hand side in a Taylor series in pressure around P.**

$$\mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P + \pi) = \mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P) + \pi \left(\dfrac{\partial \mu_{1}^{soln}}{\partial P}\right)_{T} + \text{higher order terms}$$

**Step 2:** Use the thermodynamic identity $\left(\dfrac{\partial \mu_{1}}{\partial P}\right)_{T} = V_{1}$ (partial molar volume of the solvent).

$$\mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P + \pi) = \mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P) + \pi \bar{V}_{1}$$

**Step 3: Substitute back into the equilibrium condition.**

$$\mu_{1}^{0}(T, P) = \mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P) + \pi \bar{V}_{1}$$

Rearranging for π:

$$\pi = \dfrac{\mu_{1}^{0}(T, P) - \mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P)}{\bar{V}_{1}} = -\dfrac{\Delta \mu_{1}}{\bar{V}_{1}}$$

**Step 4: Express Δμ₁ using the activity a₁ of the solvent.**

For a non-ideal solution, $\mu_{1}^{soln} = \mu_{1}^{0} + RT \ln a_{1}$, hence:

$$\pi = -\dfrac{RT \ln a_{1}}{\bar{V}_{1}} = -\dfrac{RT \ln(x_{1} \gamma_{1})}{\bar{V}_{1}}$$

**Step 5: For very dilute solutions (polymer in solvent), x₂ ≪ 1.**

Expand $\ln x_{1} = \ln(1 - x_{2})$ as a Taylor series:

$$\ln(1 - x_{2}) = -x_{2} - \dfrac{x_{2}^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{x_{2}^{3}}{3} - \cdots$$

**Step 6: Express mole fraction x₂ in terms of mass concentration c and molecular weight M₂.**

For a solution of mass **m₂** of polymer in volume **V** of solution:

$$n_{2} = \dfrac{m_{2}}{M_{2}}, \quad n_{1} \approx \dfrac{\rho V}{M_{1}} \;\text{(if pure solvent, density ρ)}$$

$$x_{2} = \dfrac{n_{2}}{n_{1} + n_{2}} \approx \dfrac{n_{2}}{n_{1}} = \dfrac{m_{2} M_{1}}{\rho V M_{2}} = \dfrac{c M_{1}}{\rho M_{2}}$$

**Step 7: Substitute into the expansion and keep terms up to second order in c.**

$$-\ln a_{1} \approx x_{2} + \dfrac{x_{2}^{2}}{2} + \cdots = \dfrac{c M_{1}}{\rho M_{2}} + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{c M_{1}}{\rho M_{2}}\right)^{2} + \cdots$$

**Step 8: Insert into the expression for π.**

For a dilute solution, $\bar{V}_{1} \approx V_{1}^{0} = M_{1}/\rho$:

$$\pi = \dfrac{RT}{\bar{V}_{1}} \cdot \dfrac{c M_{1}}{\rho M_{2}} = \dfrac{RT c}{M_{2}}$$

This recovers the **van't Hoff law** for ideal dilute polymer solutions.

**Step 9: Include the second-order term to get the virial form.**

Including the x₂²/2 term introduces a c² contribution. Reorganizing as a polynomial in c (and absorbing higher-order terms into virial coefficients A₂, A₃, …):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{M_{2}} + A_{2} RT\, c + A_{3} RT\, c^{2} + \cdots}$$

**Step 10: Determine M̄ₙ by extrapolation.**

At c → 0, all higher-order terms vanish:

$$\left(\dfrac{\pi}{c}\right)_{c \to 0} = \dfrac{RT}{M_{2}}$$

For a polydisperse polymer, this M₂ is replaced by M̄ₙ:

$$\boxed{M̄_{n} = \dfrac{RT}{\left(\dfrac{\pi}{c}\right)_{c \to 0}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why M̄ₙ and not M̄_w?** Osmotic pressure is a **colligative property** that depends on the *number* of solute molecules per unit volume. Each chain contributes equally regardless of its size, so the result is the **number-average**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada Equation

**Starting Postulate:** Intrinsic viscosity [η] is a measure of the **hydrodynamic volume** of an isolated polymer coil in a flowing solvent. For a flexible Gaussian chain in a good solvent:

$$[\eta] \propto \dfrac{v_{h}}{M}$$

where **v_h** is the hydrodynamic volume of the coil.

**Step 1: Express the hydrodynamic volume using the radius of gyration R_g.**

For a polymer coil of radius of gyration R_g, the hydrodynamic volume scales as:

$$v_{h} \propto R_{g}^{3}$$

**Step 2: Use the scaling relation between R_g and M.**

From Flory's theory and reptation arguments:

$$R_{g} \propto M^{\nu}$$

where **ν** is the excluded-volume exponent:
- Ideal/θ solvent: ν = 0.5
- Good solvent: ν ≈ 0.6 (theoretical) ≈ 0.5–0.8 (experimental)

**Step 3: Substitute back into the expression for [η].**

$$[\eta] \propto \dfrac{R_{g}^{3}}{M} \propto \dfrac{M^{3\nu}}{M} = M^{3\nu - 1}$$

**Step 4: Define α ≡ 3ν − 1 and K as the proportionality constant.**

$$[\eta] = K M^{\alpha}$$

This is the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation**. The exponent α reflects chain stiffness and solvent quality:
- **α ≈ 0.5** → θ conditions (rigid rod chains in ideal solvent)
- **α = 0.5 to 0.8** → Flexible coils in good solvents
- **α = 0.8 to 2.0** → Stiff chains / semi-rigid rods
- **α = 2.0** → Theoretical upper limit (rigid rod)

**Step 5: Linearization for graphical determination of M.**

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln[\eta] = \ln K + \alpha \ln M$$

A plot of ln[η] vs ln M is linear with slope α and y-intercept ln K. This is the basis of the **universal calibration** method in Gel Permeation Chromatography.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Huggins Equation (Heuristic)

**Premise:** The specific viscosity η_sp is the fractional increase in viscosity due to the polymer. For a single isolated chain at infinite dilution:

$$\eta_{sp} \propto c[\eta]$$

**Step 2: For finite concentration, polymer coils interact.** Huggins proposed a virial-like expansion in concentration:

$$\eta_{sp} = [\eta] c + k_{H}[\eta]^{2} c^{2} + \cdots$$

Dividing by c:

$$\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_{H}[\eta]^{2} c + \cdots$$

**Step 3: Truncate at the linear term and identify the intercept.**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_{H}[\eta]^{2} c}$$

This is the **Huggins equation**, and plotting η_sp/c vs c yields a straight line with intercept **[η]** and slope **k_H[η]²**.

The **Huggins constant k_H** is typically **0.3 to 0.4** in good solvents and increases in poor solvents, providing diagnostic information on polymer-solvent interactions.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Kraemer Equation (Companion Form)

**Premise:** Take the natural log of the relative viscosity series and expand in concentration.

$$\ln \eta_{r} = \ln(1 + \eta_{sp}) = \eta_{sp} - \dfrac{\eta_{sp}^{2}}{2} + \dfrac{\eta_{sp}^{3}}{3} - \cdots$$

Substituting η_sp = [η]c + k_H[η]²c² + … and grouping terms:

$$\ln \eta_{r} = [\eta]c + \left(k_{H} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)[\eta]^{2} c^{2} + \cdots$$

Dividing by c and defining k_K ≡ ½ − k_H:

$$\dfrac{\ln \eta_{r}}{c} = [\eta] - k_{K}[\eta]^{2} c$$

This is the **Kraemer equation**, the natural companion to the Huggins equation. **The two extrapolations should give the same [η]** within experimental error, providing a powerful internal consistency check.

### 3.5 Combined Extrapolation (Huggins-Kraemer Plot)

A combined plot of **η_sp/c** (rising line, Huggins) and **ln η_r/c** (falling line, Kraemer) vs c gives two lines that converge to a common intercept [η] at c = 0. The Huggins slope is positive; the Kraemer slope is negative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consistency Check:** In a well-executed experiment, the Huggins intercept and Kraemer intercept must agree to within 1–2%. If they do not, suspect concentration measurement error or non-Newtonian flow effects.

### 3.6 The Hagen–Poiseuille Foundation for Capillary Viscometers

For laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a cylindrical capillary of radius r, length L, under a pressure head ΔP, the volumetric flow rate Q obeys:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

The time t for a fixed volume V to flow is $t = V/Q$, so:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P\, t}{8 L V}$$

For measurements of the same fluid in the same viscometer, the ratio of viscosities equals the ratio of flow times (with density correction):

$$\dfrac{\eta}{\eta_{0}} = \dfrac{\rho\, t}{\rho_{0}\, t_{0}} \approx \dfrac{t}{t_{0}} \quad \text{(for dilute solutions, } \rho \approx \rho_{0}\text{)}$$

This justifies the practical experimental identity $\eta_{r} = t / t_{0}$.
