## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define osmotic pressure. Write the van't Hoff equation for the osmotic pressure of a dilute polymer solution and explain its limitation for real polymer solutions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Osmotic pressure is the excess hydrostatic pressure that must be applied to a polymer solution to prevent the inflow of pure solvent across a semipermeable membrane that is permeable only to the solvent.
>
> **Van't Hoff equation (1 mark):** For an ideal, very dilute polymer solution,
> $$\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{M_{2}}$$
> or equivalently, $\pi = cRT/M_{2}$, where c is the concentration in g/cm³, R is the gas constant, T the absolute temperature, and M₂ the molecular weight.
>
> **Limitation (1 mark):** The equation assumes **ideal solution behavior** (no polymer–polymer or polymer–solvent interactions beyond the dilute limit). For real polymer solutions of finite concentration, deviations occur and a **virial expansion** in c must be used:
> $$\dfrac{\pi}{c} = \dfrac{RT}{M_{2}} + A_{2}RT\, c + \cdots$$
> The second virial coefficient A₂ accounts for non-ideal interactions and becomes zero at the θ-temperature.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]**
**What is intrinsic viscosity? How is it determined experimentally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Intrinsic viscosity [η] is the limiting value of the reduced viscosity (η_sp/c) as concentration c approaches zero. It represents the contribution of *isolated* polymer molecules to the solution viscosity, free from intermolecular interactions. Its units are typically dL/g or cm³/g.
>
> **Experimental determination (1.5 marks):** [η] is determined by:
> 1. Measuring the flow times t (polymer solution) and t₀ (pure solvent) in a **capillary viscometer** (Ostwald, Ubbelohde).
> 2. Computing the **relative viscosity** η_r = t / t₀ and the **specific viscosity** η_sp = η_r − 1.
> 3. Plotting η_sp/c vs c (Huggins plot) and (ln η_r)/c vs c (Kraemer plot).
> 4. **Extrapolating both lines to c = 0**; the common intercept is [η].
>
> The **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation** [η] = K Mᵅ is then used to obtain the viscosity-average molecular weight M̄ᵥ.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Derive the thermodynamic relation between the osmotic pressure π and the molecular weight M₂ of a polymer in dilute solution. Explain how the second virial coefficient A₂ reflects the polymer–solvent interaction. (7 marks)**

**(b) The osmotic pressures of solutions of polystyrene in toluene at 25 °C are given below:**

| **c (g/cm³)** | **0.0020** | **0.0040** | **0.0060** | **0.0080** | **0.0100** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **π (dyne/cm²)** | **0.65** | **1.78** | **3.40** | **5.55** | **8.30** |

**Plot (π/c) vs c and determine the number-average molecular weight M̄ₙ and the second virial coefficient A₂. (R = 8.314 × 10⁷ erg·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹) (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The complete derivation as in Section 3.1 above. The expected key steps for full marks:

1. **Equilibrium condition:** $\mu_{1}^{0}(T, P) = \mu_{1}^{soln}(T, P + \pi)$ — **(1 mark)**
2. **Taylor expansion** of chemical potential in pressure to obtain $\pi = -\Delta\mu_{1}/\bar{V}_{1}$ — **(1 mark)**
3. **Express Δμ₁ in terms of activity** using $\mu_{1} = \mu_{1}^{0} + RT \ln a_{1}$: $\pi = -(RT/\bar{V}_{1}) \ln a_{1}$ — **(1 mark)**
4. **Series expansion** of $\ln(1 - x_{2})$ and substitution of $x_{2} = cM_{1}/(\rho M_{2})$ to obtain the **virial form** $\pi/c = RT/M_{2} + A_{2}RT\,c + \cdots$ — **(2 marks)**
5. **Discussion of A₂** (1 mark):
   - A₂ > 0 → **Good solvent** (coil expansion, polymer–solvent contacts favored)
   - A₂ = 0 → **θ-solvent** (Flory temperature; chains behave ideally)
   - A₂ < 0 → **Poor solvent** (precipitation regime; polymer–polymer contacts favored)
   - In terms of the **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter χ**: $A_{2} = (1/2 - \chi)/\bar{V}_{1}\rho_{2}^{2}$, so A₂ → 0 when χ = 0.5 (the θ-condition).

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Tabulate π/c values (1 mark):**

| **c (g/cm³)** | **π (dyne/cm²)** | **(π/c) (dyne·cm·g⁻¹)** |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0020 | 0.65 | 325 |
| 0.0040 | 1.78 | 445 |
| 0.0060 | 3.40 | 567 |
| 0.0080 | 5.55 | 694 |
| 0.0100 | 8.30 | 830 |

**Step 2: Linear regression / graphical extrapolation (2 marks):**

A plot of (π/c) vs c gives approximately a straight line. Linear fit yields:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{c} = 220 + 60500\,c \quad \text{(in CGS units)}$$

- **y-intercept** = 220 dyne·cm·g⁻¹
- **slope** = 60500 dyne·cm⁴·g⁻²

**Step 3: Compute M̄ₙ from the intercept (2 marks):**

At c = 0:
$$\left(\dfrac{\pi}{c}\right)_{0} = \dfrac{RT}{M̄_{n}}$$

$$M̄_{n} = \dfrac{RT}{\left(\dfrac{\pi}{c}\right)_{0}} = \dfrac{(8.314 \times 10^{7}) \times 298}{220}$$

$$M̄_{n} = \dfrac{2.478 \times 10^{10}}{220} = 1.13 \times 10^{8} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4: Compute A₂ from the slope (2 marks):**

The slope of the (π/c) vs c plot equals **A₂ RT**:

$$A_{2} = \dfrac{\text{slope}}{RT} = \dfrac{60500}{(8.314 \times 10^{7}) \times 298}$$

$$A_{2} = \dfrac{60500}{2.478 \times 10^{10}} = 2.44 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm³·mol·g}^{-2}$$

**Result:** M̄ₙ ≈ **1.13 × 10⁸ g/mol**; A₂ ≈ **2.44 × 10⁻⁶ cm³·mol·g⁻²** (positive, indicating toluene is a good solvent for polystyrene at 25 °C — consistent with textbook data).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** R must be in **CGS units** (8.314 × 10⁷ erg/mol·K) when π is in dyne/cm² and c in g/cm³. Mixing SI and CGS R values is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert temperature:** T must be in **Kelvin (K = °C + 273)**. Using T = 25 directly is wrong.
> 3. **Sign of A₂:** A positive A₂ must be obtained for polystyrene in toluene (a thermodynamically good solvent). A negative result indicates a calculation error.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) Define the terms: (i) relative viscosity, (ii) specific viscosity, (iii) reduced viscosity, (iv) inherent viscosity, and (v) intrinsic viscosity. State the units of intrinsic viscosity. (7 marks)**

**(b) The intrinsic viscosity of a polymer sample in a given solvent at 30 °C is 1.25 dL/g. Using the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada constants K = 5.5 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g and α = 0.71 for this polymer-solvent system, calculate the viscosity-average molecular weight M̄ᵥ. Also explain why M̄ᵥ lies between M̄ₙ and M̄_w and under what condition M̄ᵥ = M̄_w. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions (7 marks — 1.4 marks each):**

| **Term** | **Definition** |
|---|---|
| **(i) Relative viscosity η_r** | The ratio of the viscosity of the polymer solution (η) to the viscosity of the pure solvent (η₀): $\eta_{r} = \eta / \eta_{0}$. Dimensionless. |
| **(ii) Specific viscosity η_sp** | The fractional increase in viscosity caused by the dissolved polymer: $\eta_{sp} = \eta_{r} - 1 = (\eta - \eta_{0})/\eta_{0}$. Dimensionless. |
| **(iii) Reduced viscosity η_red** | The specific viscosity per unit concentration: $\eta_{red} = \eta_{sp} / c$. Units: **dL/g** (or cm³/g, m³/kg). |
| **(iv) Inherent viscosity η_inh** | The natural logarithm of relative viscosity per unit concentration: $\eta_{inh} = (\ln \eta_{r})/c$. Units: dL/g. |
| **(v) Intrinsic viscosity [η]** | The limit of the reduced (or inherent) viscosity as concentration approaches zero: $[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \eta_{sp}/c = \lim_{c \to 0} (\ln \eta_{r})/c$. Units: **dL/g** (or cm³/g). |

**(b) Numerical and Conceptual (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Apply the MHS equation to find M̄ᵥ (3 marks):**

$$[\eta] = K M̄_{v}^{\alpha}$$

$$1.25 = (5.5 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot M̄_{v}^{0.71}$$

Rearranging:
$$M̄_{v}^{0.71} = \dfrac{1.25}{5.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2272.73$$

$$M̄_{v} = (2272.73)^{1/0.71} = (2272.73)^{1.4085}$$

Taking logarithms:
$$\ln M̄_{v} = 1.4085 \times \ln(2272.73) = 1.4085 \times 7.7282 = 10.886$$

$$M̄_{v} = e^{10.886} = 5.30 \times 10^{4} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Result:** M̄ᵥ ≈ **5.30 × 10⁴ g/mol** (2 marks for correct computation).

**Step 2: Why M̄ₙ ≤ M̄ᵥ ≤ M̄_w (3 marks):**

Viscometry measures the contribution of each chain to the solution viscosity. The intrinsic viscosity is a **mass-averaged** property because the contribution of a single chain of mass m to viscosity scales as m × [η], and [η] = K mᵅ.

For a polydisperse sample with number-fraction distribution n_i:

$$[\eta] = K \dfrac{\sum n_i M_{i}^{\alpha+1}}{\sum n_i M_{i}} = K \dfrac{\sum n_i M_{i}^{\alpha+1}}{\sum n_i M_{i}}$$

This is a **weighted average** lying between M̄ₙ and M̄_w depending on the exponent α:

- When **α = 1**: $[\eta]/K = \sum n_i M_i^2 / \sum n_i M_i = M̄_w$ (i.e., M̄ᵥ = M̄_w).
- When **α → 0** (rigid, non-draining coils): M̄ᵥ → M̄ₙ.
- For typical flexible polymers (0.5 < α < 0.8), M̄ᵥ lies between the two but closer to M̄_w (e.g., for α = 0.71 and a typical polydispersity of M̄_w/M̄ₙ ≈ 2, M̄ᵥ is roughly 0.9 M̄_w).

**Condition for M̄ᵥ = M̄_w:** $\alpha = 1$, which occurs for **rigid, rod-like polymers** in very good solvents (e.g., some cellulose derivatives, certain polyelectrolytes at high salt concentration).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls for Question B:**
> 1. **Units in MHS constants:** K and α **must be consistent** with the units of [η]. If [η] is in dL/g, then K must also be in dL/g. Mixing dL/g [η] with m³/kg K is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Algebra slip on M̄ᵥ:** Many students forget to take the **1/α** power and write $M̄_v = ([\eta]/K)^{1/\alpha}$ incorrectly. The correct rearrangement is $M̄_v = \left( [\eta]/K \right)^{1/\alpha}$.
> 3. **Confusing M̄ᵥ with M̄ₙ:** A specific 1-mark deduction is awarded for stating that viscometry "gives M̄ₙ" — it gives M̄ᵥ.
> 4. **Reverse direction of M̄ inequality:** Some students write M̄_w ≤ M̄ᵥ ≤ M̄ₙ (wrong). The correct order is M̄ₙ ≤ M̄ᵥ ≤ M̄_w for α > 0.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam**

- [ ] **Osmotic pressure π** is a **colligative property** → gives **M̄ₙ** (number-average).
- [ ] **van't Hoff equation** (ideal, dilute): $\pi/c = RT/M_2$.
- [ ] **Virial expansion** (real solutions): $\pi/c = RT/M_2 + A_2 RT\,c + \cdots$
- [ ] **A₂ > 0** → good solvent; **A₂ = 0** → θ-solvent; **A₂ < 0** → poor solvent.
- [ ] M̄ₙ determination requires **extrapolation of (π/c) to c = 0**; the y-intercept gives RT/M̄ₙ.
- [ ] **Semipermeable membrane** must pass solvent, block polymer.
- [ ] **Viscometry** gives **M̄ᵥ** (viscosity-average) — *not* M̄ₙ, *not* M̄_w.
- [ ] Five key viscosity quantities to remember: **η_r, η_sp, η_red, η_inh, [η]**.
- [ ] **Huggins equation:** $\eta_{sp}/c = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c$ → positive slope.
- [ ] **Kraemer equation:** $(\ln \eta_r)/c = [\eta] - k_K[\eta]^2 c$ → negative slope.
- [ ] **Common intercept** of Huggins and Kraemer at c = 0 = **[η]**.
- [ ] **Huggins-Kraemer consistency:** $k_H + k_K \approx 0.5$.
- [ ] **MHS equation:** $[\eta] = K M^α$; linearize via $\ln[\eta] = \ln K + \alpha \ln M$.
- [ ] **Range of α:** 0.5 (θ-solvent) → 0.8 (good solvent) → 2.0 (rigid rod, theoretical max).
- [ ] **M̄ₙ ≤ M̄ᵥ ≤ M̄_w**; equality at **α = 1** (M̄ᵥ = M̄_w).
- [ ] **Capillary viscometer equation** (Hagen–Poiseuille): $\eta = \pi r^4 \Delta P\, t / (8LV)$.
- [ ] For dilute solutions: $\eta_r = t/t_0$ (density correction negligible).
- [ ] **Practical measurement range:** Osmometry: 2 × 10⁴ – 1.5 × 10⁶ g/mol; Viscometry: 10⁴ – 10⁷ g/mol.
- [ ] **Polymer Handbook (Brandrup–Immergut)** is the standard reference for K and α — **never** invent or guess these constants.
- [ ] **Unit vigilance:** R in CGS = 8.314 × 10⁷ erg/mol·K; R in SI = 8.314 J/mol·K; [η] commonly in **dL/g**, **not** m³/kg unless explicitly converted.
- [ ] **Experimental skill:** Always pre-condition the membrane; always thermostat the viscometer; always use ≥ 4 concentrations for reliable extrapolation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental Methods to Determine Weight Average and Z-Average Molecular Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Experimental Methods to Determine Weight Average and Z-Average Molecular Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 — Experimental Determination of $\overline{M_w}$ and $\overline{M_z}$

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In polymer science, every synthesised polymer sample contains macromolecules of **many different chain lengths**, so a single "molecular weight" is meaningless without specifying *how* it has been averaged. The three most important averages are the **Number Average** ($\overline{M_n}$), the **Weight Average** ($\overline{M_w}$), and the **Z-Average** ($\overline{M_z}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner-Ready)**
>
> The **Weight Average Molecular Weight** $\overline{M_w}$ is defined as the second moment of the molecular weight distribution and is given by:
>
> $$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}$$
>
> The **Z-Average Molecular Weight** $\overline{M_z}$ is the third moment of the distribution:
>
> $$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{2}}$$
>
> where $N_i$ is the number of molecules having molecular weight $M_i$.

Strictly speaking, $\overline{M_w}$ and $\overline{M_z}$ are **NOT absolute averages in the arithmetic sense** — they are *moments* of the distribution. They are **inherently biased toward the heavier chains** because the higher powers of $M_i$ amplify the contribution of large molecules.

The strict inequality governing all real polydisperse polymers is:

$$\overline{M_n} \leq \overline{M_w} \leq \overline{M_z} \leq \overline{M_{z+1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Polydispersity Index (PDI)**
>
> The ratio $\overline{M_w} / \overline{M_n}$ is the **Polydispersity Index (PDI)**. For a perfectly monodisperse sample (e.g., a protein), PDI = **1.000**. Typical commercial polymers: PDI between **1.5 and 20**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Class Photograph of Polymer Chains"

Imagine a classroom of **100 polymer chains** of three sizes:
- **60 chains** weigh **10 kg** each (small)
- **30 chains** weigh **100 kg** each (medium)
- **10 chains** weigh **1000 kg** each (large — the "heavyweights")

- $\overline{M_n}$ = Total mass ÷ Number of chains → gives the **common man** an equal vote.
- $\overline{M_w}$ = Each chain **votes in proportion to its own mass** → the heavyweights dominate, but medium chains still matter.
- $\overline{M_z}$ = Each chain **votes in proportion to its mass squared** → the heavyweights now **completely overwhelm** the vote.

So when you walk into a classroom and ask "who is most influential?", the answer depends on the **voting rule**. Light scattering uses the **Mw voting rule** because the intensity of scattered light is proportional to the *square* of the mass. Sedimentation uses the **Mz voting rule** because the centrifugal force on a molecule is proportional to its *mass cubed* (mass × acceleration, and acceleration depends on mass through buoyancy).

## 1.3 Which Technique Measures Which Average?

| Molecular Weight | Primary Absolute Method | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| $\overline{M_n}$ | Osmometry, End-group analysis, VPO | Counts molecules (colligative) |
| $\overline{M_w}$ | **Static Light Scattering (SLS)** | Scattered intensity ∝ mass² |
| $\overline{M_z}$ | **Ultracentrifugation (Sedimentation Equilibrium)** | Sedimentation depends on mass³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Static Light Scattering (SLS) for $\overline{M_w}$

### 2.1.1 Physical Principle

When a beam of monochromatic, polarised light passes through a dilute polymer solution, the oscillating electric field of the light **induces oscillating dipoles** in the electron clouds of the polymer segments. These dipoles re-radiate light in all directions — this is **Rayleigh scattering** (for particles much smaller than the wavelength, $d < \lambda / 20$).

The scattered intensity $i_\theta$ at angle $\theta$ to the incident beam is governed by three laws:

1. **Rayleigh's Law:** $i_\theta \propto c \cdot V^2 \cdot M$ (intensity rises with concentration, square of particle volume, and mass).
2. **Inverse Square Law:** $i_\theta \propto 1 / \lambda^4$ (blue sky and red sunsets).
3. **Angular Symmetry:** For very small particles, scattering is symmetric (isotropic) around $\theta = 90°$.

For polymers, however, the macromolecular coil is **comparable to $\lambda$**, so interference between light scattered from different segments of the *same* chain produces angular asymmetry. This is captured by the **form factor** $P(\theta)$.

### 2.1.2 The Zimm Equation — The Heart of SLS

The working equation used in every SLS experiment is the **Zimm Equation** (1948):

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w} P(\theta)} + 2 A_2 c$$

where:
- $K = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 n_0^2}{N_A \lambda^4} \left( \dfrac{dn}{dc} \right)^2$ — optical constant
- $c$ — polymer concentration (g/cm³)
- $R_\theta$ — **Rayleigh ratio** at angle $\theta$ (excess scattered intensity)
- $\overline{M_w}$ — weight-average molecular weight
- $P(\theta)$ — particle scattering function ($P(\theta) \to 1$ as $\theta \to 0$)
- $A_2$ — second virial coefficient (thermodynamic solvent quality)

For a **Guinier approximation** ($P(\theta) \approx 1 - (1/3) R_g^2 q^2$ where $q$ is the scattering vector), the Zimm equation expands to:

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w}} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} R_g^2 q^2 + \cdots \right) + 2 A_2 c + \cdots$$

This allows a **double extrapolation** to zero angle AND zero concentration — the famous **Zimm Plot**.

### 2.1.3 The Zimm Plot Procedure

The experimentalist measures $R_\theta$ at several angles ($\theta = 30°, 45°, \ldots, 150°$) and at several concentrations. Then:

$$\left. \frac{K c}{R_\theta} \right|_{c \to 0, \, \theta \to 0} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w}}$$

From the slopes:
- **Concentration slope (at $\theta = 0$)** gives $2 A_2$ → solvent quality.
- **Angular slope (at $c = 0$)** gives $R_g^2$ → radius of gyration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SLS gives $\overline{M_w}$, not $\overline{M_n}$:**
> Each chain scatters with intensity proportional to $M_i^2$. The total intensity is therefore weighted by $M_i^2$, exactly matching the numerator of $\overline{M_w}$.

## 2.2 Method 2 — Ultracentrifugation for $\overline{M_z}$

### 2.2.1 Physical Principle

When a polymer solution is spun at very high speed (50,000–70,000 rpm) in an **analytical ultracentrifuge**, the macromolecules experience a centrifugal force $F_c = M_i (1 - \bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 r$, where:
- $\bar{v}$ = partial specific volume of polymer
- $\rho$ = solution density
- $\omega$ = angular velocity
- $r$ = radial distance from rotation axis

Larger, denser molecules sediment **faster** than smaller ones, producing a concentration gradient at equilibrium that depends on the mass distribution.

### 2.2.2 Svedberg Equation of Sedimentation

The **sedimentation coefficient** $s$ is defined as:

$$s \equiv \frac{v}{\omega^2 r} = \frac{1}{\omega^2 r} \frac{dr}{dt}$$

with units of **Svedbergs** ($1 S = 10^{-13}$ s). The coefficient $s$ depends on molecular weight through:

$$s = \frac{M (1 - \bar{v}\rho)}{N_A f}$$

where $f$ is the **frictional coefficient** (Stokes-like drag).

### 2.2.3 Sedimentation Equilibrium — Why it gives $\overline{M_z}$

At true thermodynamic equilibrium in the centrifuge cell, the chemical potential gradient exactly balances the centrifugal force, giving the **Lamm Equation** solution:

$$c(r) = c_0 \exp\left[ \frac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 (r^2 - r_0^2)}{2 R T} \right]$$

Because the equilibrium distribution contains the factor $M_i$ in the exponential, the measured equilibrium gradient is **mass-biased toward the third power**. Taking moments of this distribution leads to:

$$\overline{M_w, \text{apparent}}(r) = \frac{dc/dr}{c} \cdot \frac{2 R T}{(1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 r}$$

and the **true z-average** is recovered from the moment analysis:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:**
> Sedimentation **velocity** (rate of movement) gives $\overline{M_w}$ via the Svedberg equation. Sedimentation **equilibrium** (after long centrifugation, no net movement) gives $\overline{M_z}$. Both are absolute — no calibration with standards of known $M$ is needed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\overline{M_w} = \sum N_i M_i^2 \,/\, \sum N_i M_i$ | Weight-average MW | Definition |
| 2 | $\overline{M_z} = \sum N_i M_i^3 \,/\, \sum N_i M_i^2$ | Z-average MW | Definition |
| 3 | $\dfrac{K c}{R_\theta} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M_w} P(\theta)} + 2 A_2 c$ | Zimm equation | SLS experiment |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{4\pi^2 n_0^2 (dn/dc)^2}{N_A \lambda^4}$ | Optical contrast constant | SLS optical setup |
| 5 | $P(\theta)^{-1} \approx 1 + \dfrac{1}{3} R_g^2 \left( \dfrac{4 \pi n_0}{\lambda} \sin(\theta/2) \right)^2$ | Guinier form factor | SLS angular analysis |
| 6 | $\dfrac{K c}{R_\theta}\bigg\vert_{c,\theta \to 0} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M_w}}$ | Zimm intercept | $\overline{M_w}$ determination |
| 7 | $s = \dfrac{M(1-\bar{v}\rho)}{N_A f}$ | Svedberg equation | Sedimentation |
| 8 | $s \equiv v \,/\, (\omega^2 r)$ | Definition of sedimentation coefficient | Centrifuge data |
| 9 | $\dfrac{d \ln c}{d r^2} = \dfrac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2}{2 R T}$ | Equilibrium gradient | $\overline{M_z}$ determination |
| 10 | $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{\int r^2 c(r) \, dr}{\int c(r) \, dr}$ (formal integral form) | Z-average integral | Centrifuge evaluation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Why $\overline{M_w}$ matters | Why $\overline{M_z}$ matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical properties** | $\overline{M_w}$ correlates with tensile strength, impact resistance | Tail of distribution controls brittle failure |
| **Rheology / Melt flow** | $\overline{M_w}$ ∝ zero-shear viscosity ($\eta_0 \propto \overline{M_w}^{3.4}$) | $\overline{M_z}$ controls die-swell and elasticity |
| **Solution viscosity** | Intrinsic viscosity [η] relates to $\overline{M_v}$, close to $\overline{M_w}$ | Drag reduction sensitive to high-M tail |
| **Biomedical polymers** | Drug-release kinetics depend on average size | Aggregates (high M) dominate toxicity |
| **Membrane fabrication** | Pore-size distribution linked to coil $R_g$ ∝ $\overline{M_w}^{0.5}$ | Fouling controlled by very large chains |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — From Rayleigh Scattering to the Zimm Equation

### Step 1: Scattering from a single small particle

A single isotropic particle of polarisability $\alpha$ placed in an incident electric field $E_0$ radiates an intensity at distance $r$ given by Rayleigh's classical result:

$$i = \frac{8 \pi^4 \alpha^2}{\lambda^4 r^2} (1 + \cos^2 \theta) I_0$$

### Step 2: Extension to a polymer coil

A polymer coil of mass $M$ contains many segments. For light with $\lambda \gg$ segment size, all segments contribute coherently. The polarisability of the whole coil scales as:

$$\alpha_{\text{coil}} = \alpha_{\text{segment}} \cdot N_{\text{segments}} \propto M$$

Therefore the **scattered intensity per coil is proportional to $M^2$**.

### Step 3: Summing over the molecular weight distribution

The total scattered intensity from a polydisperse sample is the sum over all chains:

$$R_\theta \propto \sum_i N_i M_i^2 \cdot P_i(\theta) \cdot c$$

When combined with the definition $\overline{M_w} = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ and rearranging:

$$R_\theta = \frac{K c \overline{M_w} P(\theta)}{1 + 2 A_2 c \overline{M_w} P(\theta) + \cdots}$$

### Step 4: Inverting and keeping only the first virial term

Inverting the above expansion and truncating at the linear $c$ term yields the Zimm equation:

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w} P(\theta)} + 2 A_2 c + \mathcal{O}(c^2)$$

### Step 5: Guinier expansion of $P(\theta)$

For small scattering vectors $q = (4 \pi n_0 / \lambda) \sin(\theta/2)$, the form factor expands as:

$$P(\theta)^{-1} \approx 1 + \frac{1}{3} R_g^2 q^2$$

Substituting back into the Zimm equation:

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w}} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} R_g^2 q^2 \right) + 2 A_2 c$$

### Step 6: The double extrapolation (Zimm Plot)

This is the **core procedure** of SLS:

- Plot $(K c / R_\theta)$ versus $\sin^2(\theta/2) + k c$, where $k$ is an arbitrary scaling constant.
- Draw a grid of straight lines for each constant $c$ (slopes give $R_g$) and each constant $\theta$ (slopes give $A_2$).
- Extrapolate **both families to zero** — they must intersect at the same intercept.

The common intercept is:

$$\boxed{\left. \frac{K c}{R_\theta} \right|_{c \to 0,\, \theta \to 0} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two extrapolations?**
> - $c \to 0$: removes intermolecular interference (thermodynamic interaction).
> - $\theta \to 0$: removes intramolecular interference (size effect, gives $R_g$).
>
> The intercept is free of **both**, yielding the true $\overline{M_w}$.

## 3.2 Derivation B — From Centrifuge Force to $\overline{M_z}$

### Step 1: Forces on a sedimenting macromolecule

In a centrifuge cell, each molecule experiences:
- Centrifugal force: $F_c = M_i (1 - \bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 r$ (mass × buoyancy-corrected acceleration).
- Frictional drag: $F_d = -f_i (dr/dt)$ (Stokes-type, opposite to motion).

### Step 2: Force balance at terminal velocity

At terminal velocity, $F_c + F_d = 0$:

$$M_i (1 - \bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 r - f_i \frac{dr}{dt} = 0$$

Solving for sedimentation coefficient:

$$s_i = \frac{1}{\omega^2 r} \frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho)}{f_i}$$

### Step 3: Sedimentation equilibrium condition

At true equilibrium, net flux of each species is zero. The chemical potential gradient exactly cancels the centrifugal potential:

$$\mu_i(r) + M_i \Phi(r) = \text{constant}$$

where $\Phi(r) = -\tfrac{1}{2} (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 r^2$ is the centrifugal potential energy per unit mass.

Using $\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + R T \ln c_i$:

$$\ln c_i(r) = \ln c_i(r_0) + \frac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 (r^2 - r_0^2)}{2 R T}$$

Therefore:

$$c_i(r) = c_i(r_0) \exp\left[ \frac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 (r^2 - r_0^2)}{2 R T} \right]$$

### Step 4: Total concentration profile

Summing over all species:

$$c(r) = \sum_i c_i(r) = \sum_i c_i(r_0) \exp\left[ \frac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 (r^2 - r_0^2)}{2 R T} \right]$$

### Step 5: Extracting moments

**First moment — apparent $\overline{M_w}$:**

Taking the derivative of $\ln c$ with respect to $r^2$:

$$\frac{d \ln c}{d (r^2)} = \frac{(1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2}{2 R T} \cdot \frac{\sum_i c_i(r) M_i}{\sum_i c_i(r)} = \frac{(1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2}{2 R T} \overline{M_w}^{\text{app}}(r)$$

Since $c_i(r) \propto c_i(r_0) \exp[M_i \cdot \text{const}]$, the concentration-weighted average biases toward higher $M_i$, giving the **weight-average** at that radial position.

**Second moment — true $\overline{M_z}$:**

Taking the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2 \ln c}{d (r^2)^2} = \frac{(1-\bar{v}\rho)^2 \omega^4}{8 R^2 T^2} \left[ \overline{M_z}^{\text{app}}(r) - (\overline{M_w}^{\text{app}}(r))^2 \right]$$

From this, the **z-average molecular weight** is recovered absolutely:

$$\boxed{\overline{M_z} = \frac{d c/d(r^2)}{c} \cdot \frac{2 R T}{(1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2} \bigg\vert_{\text{equilibrium}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:**
> - The first derivative of $\ln c$ vs $r^2$ at equilibrium yields **$\overline{M_w}$**.
> - The second derivative yields **$\overline{M_z}$**.
> - Because the $M_i$ appears *inside* the exponent, larger molecules contribute disproportionately, naturally biasing the equilibrium measurement toward the high-mass tail — hence the z-average.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Static Light Scattering | Ultracentrifugation (Sed. Equilib.) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical observable | Scattered light intensity | Concentration gradient at equilibrium |
| Molecular property probed | Polarizability ∝ mass | Buoyant mass |
| Sensitivity to mass | $\propto M^2$ | $\propto M$ in exponent |
| Average obtained | $\overline{M_w}$ | $\overline{M_z}$ (also $\overline{M_w}$ from first deriv.) |
| Additional info | $R_g$ (size), $A_2$ (thermodynamics) | $\bar{v}$, $f$, sedimentation coefficient $s$ |
| Sample requirement | Very clean solutions; no dust | Concentration gradient from interference optics |
| Time per experiment | Minutes to hours | Days (true equilibrium) |
| Absolute method? | Yes | Yes |
| Typical $M$ range | $10^3$ to $10^7$ g/mol | $10^3$ to $10^6$ g/mol |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of a Static Light Scattering Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    laser[LASER SOURCE 632.8 nm He Ne] --> polariser[POLARISER vertical]
    polariser --> attenuator[BEAM ATTENUATOR neutral density filters]
    attenuator --> cell[SAMPLE CELL cylindrical quartz]
    cell --> detector30[DETECTOR AT 30 DEG]
    cell --> detector45[DETECTOR AT 45 DEG]
    cell --> detector90[DETECTOR AT 90 DEG]
    cell --> detector135[DETECTOR AT 135 DEG]
    cell --> detector150[DETECTOR AT 150 DEG]
    detector30 --> amplifier[SIGNAL AMPLIFIER photomultiplier]
    detector45 --> amplifier
    detector90 --> amplifier
    detector135 --> amplifier
    detector150 --> amplifier
    amplifier --> computer[DATA ACQUISITION COMPUTER]
    computer --> zimmplot[ZIMM PLOT SOFTWARE]
    zimmplot --> mw[OUTPUT MW weight average]
    zimmplot --> rg[OUTPUT RG radius of gyration]
    zimmplot --> a2[OUTPUT A2 second virial coefficient]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Workflow of Light Scattering Data Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PREPARE DILUTE SOLUTIONS 5 concentrations] --> B[FILTER THROUGH 0.22 MICRON MEMBRANE]
    B --> C[MEASURE RAYLEIGH RATIO R theta AT EACH ANGLE]
    C --> D[COMPUTE K c OVER R theta FOR ALL DATA POINTS]
    D --> E[PLOT K c BY R theta VERSUS sin squared theta by 2 plus k c]
    E --> F[EXTRAPOLATE TO c EQUALS 0 LINE GIVES RG]
    E --> G[EXTRAPOLATE TO theta EQUALS 0 LINE GIVES 2 A2]
    F --> H[COMMON INTERCEPT GIVES 1 OVER Mw]
    G --> H
    H --> I[CALCULATE Mw g PER MOL]
    I --> J[CROSS CHECK WITH MARK HOUWINK AND VISCOSITY DATA]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Analytical Ultracentrifuge Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rotor[ROTOR 60000 rpm] --> cell[CENTRIFUGE CELL sector shaped]
    sample[POLYMER SOLUTION] --> cell
    buffer[REFERENCE BUFFER] --> cell
    cell --> optics[INTERFERENCE OPTICS Rayleigh or Schlieren]
    optics --> ccd[CCD DETECTOR records c versus r]
    ccd --> analysis[EQUILIBRIUM ANALYSIS SOFTWARE]
    analysis --> deriv1[FIRST DERIVATIVE gives Mw apparent]
    analysis --> deriv2[SECOND DERIVATIVE gives Mz apparent]
    deriv1 --> finalMw[FINAL Mw]
    deriv2 --> finalMz[FINAL Mz]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Comparative Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[DESIRED MOLECULAR WEIGHT AVERAGE] --> q1{NEED Mn}
    q1 -- YES --> osm[OSMOMETRY OR VPO OR END GROUP ANALYSIS]
    start --> q2{NEED Mw}
    q2 -- YES --> sls[STATIC LIGHT SCATTERING SLS]
    start --> q3{NEED Mz}
    q3 -- YES --> ucf[ULTRACENTRIFUGE SEDIMENTATION EQUILIBRIUM]
    q2 -- ALSO --> visc[VISCOMETRY gives Mv close to Mw]
    sls --> zimmplot[ZIMM PLOT EXTRAPOLATION]
    ucf --> lamm[INTERFERENCE OPTICS ANALYSIS]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Q: Define Weight Average Molecular Weight $\overline{M_w}$ and Z-Average Molecular Weight $\overline{M_z}$. Why is $\overline{M_z}$ always greater than or equal to $\overline{M_w}$ for a polydisperse polymer?**

**Model Answer:**

Weight Average Molecular Weight:

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{2}}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}$$

Z-Average Molecular Weight:

$$\overline{M_z} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{3}}{\sum_{i} N_i M_i^{2}}$$

**Why $\overline{M_z} \geq \overline{M_w}$:**

By the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** for any real positive numbers $N_i$ and $M_i$:

$$\left( \sum N_i M_i^2 \right) \left( \sum N_i M_i^4 \right) \geq \left( \sum N_i M_i^3 \right)^2$$

Combined with the rearrangement of the ratios, this guarantees $\overline{M_z} \geq \overline{M_w}$, with equality **only** for a monodisperse sample. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: State the Zimm equation used in Static Light Scattering. What physical quantities are obtained from its intercept and slopes?**

**Model Answer:**

The Zimm equation:

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w} P(\theta)} + 2 A_2 c$$

In the Guinier approximation:

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w}} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} R_g^2 q^2 \right) + 2 A_2 c$$

From the Zimm plot:
- **Common intercept** (extrapolation to $c \to 0$, $\theta \to 0$): gives $\dfrac{1}{\overline{M_w}}$ → hence $\overline{M_w}$.
- **Angular slope** (at $c = 0$): gives $R_g$, the **radius of gyration**.
- **Concentration slope** (at $\theta = 0$): gives $2 A_2$, the **second virial coefficient** (solvent quality). **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Derive the Zimm equation starting from Rayleigh scattering. Show clearly how the molecular weight obtained is the **weight average** $\overline{M_w}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a polymer sample, the following SLS data were obtained at constant concentration $c = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ g/cm³ and $\lambda = 632.8$ nm, with $n_0 = 1.33$, $dn/dc = 0.18$ cm³/g:

| $\theta$ (deg) | $R_\theta \times 10^6$ (cm⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| 30 | 4.85 |
| 45 | 4.62 |
| 60 | 4.30 |
| 90 | 3.65 |
| 120 | 3.10 |
| 150 | 2.80 |

Calculate $K$ and the **extrapolated $1/\overline{M_w}$** assuming $A_2 = 0$ and a Guinier extrapolation to zero angle. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Rayleigh scattering from a single particle** — (1 Mark)

For a small isotropic particle of polarisability $\alpha$ illuminated by intensity $I_0$, the scattered intensity at angle $\theta$ and distance $r$ is:

$$i_\theta = \frac{8 \pi^4}{\lambda^4 r^2} \alpha^2 (1 + \cos^2 \theta) I_0$$

**Step 2: Extension to a polymer coil** — (1 Mark)

The polarisability of a coil is additive over its $N_{\text{seg}}$ segments:

$$\alpha_{\text{coil}} = N_{\text{seg}} \cdot \alpha_{\text{seg}} \propto M$$

Hence the scattered intensity from a single coil $\propto M^2$.

**Step 3: Summing over polydisperse sample** — (1 Mark)

Define the **Rayleigh ratio** $R_\theta$ (with toluene or polystyrene standard) and write:

$$R_\theta = K c \sum_i N_i M_i^2 P_i(\theta) \bigg/ \sum_i N_i M_i$$

But $\sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i = \overline{M_w}$, so:

$$R_\theta = K c \, \overline{M_w} P(\theta) - 2 A_2 K c^2 \overline{M_w}^2 P^2(\theta) + \cdots$$

**Step 4: Inversion and truncation** — (1 Mark)

Taking reciprocal and truncating at first order in $c$:

$$\frac{K c}{R_\theta} = \frac{1}{\overline{M_w} P(\theta)} + 2 A_2 c$$

This **is** the Zimm equation.

**Step 5: Justification of weight average** — (1 Mark)

Since each molecule scatters with intensity $\propto M_i^2$, the sum naturally weights each chain by $M_i^2$. The averaging is therefore identical to:

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

Hence SLS gives $\overline{M_w}$.

**Step 6: Guinier expansion for the form factor** — (1 Mark)

For $q R_g \ll 1$:

$$P(\theta)^{-1} \approx 1 + \frac{1}{3} R_g^2 q^2, \quad q = \frac{4 \pi n_0}{\lambda} \sin(\theta/2)$$

**Step 7: Statement of double extrapolation** — (1 Mark)

Plotting $K c / R_\theta$ vs $\sin^2(\theta/2) + k c$ and extrapolating to BOTH zero angle and zero concentration gives the common intercept $1/\overline{M_w}$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Calculate $K$** — (2 Marks)

$$K = \frac{4 \pi^2 n_0^2 (dn/dc)^2}{N_A \lambda^4}$$

Substituting (all in cgs):
- $n_0 = 1.33$
- $dn/dc = 0.18$ cm³/g
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol
- $\lambda = 632.8 \times 10^{-7}$ cm

Numerator: $4 \pi^2 (1.33)^2 (0.18)^2 = 4 (9.8696) (1.7689) (0.0324) = 2.262$

Denominator: $(6.022 \times 10^{23}) (632.8 \times 10^{-7})^4 = (6.022 \times 10^{23}) (1.6043 \times 10^{-13}) = 9.660 \times 10^{10}$

Therefore:

$$K = \frac{2.262}{9.660 \times 10^{10}} = 2.342 \times 10^{-11} \text{ mol·cm}^2/\text{g}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate $Kc/R_\theta$** — (2 Marks)

At $c = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ g/cm³:

| $\theta$ | $K c / R_\theta$ (mol·cm²/g² × 10⁻⁸) |
|---|---|
| 30 | $2.342 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{-3} / 4.85 \times 10^{-6} = 4.83$ |
| 45 | $5.07$ |
| 60 | $5.45$ |
| 90 | $6.42$ |
| 120 | $7.55$ |
| 150 | $8.36$ |

**Step 3: Convert $\theta$ to $\sin^2(\theta/2)$** — (1 Mark)

| $\theta$ | $\sin^2(\theta/2)$ |
|---|---|
| 30 | 0.0670 |
| 45 | 0.1464 |
| 60 | 0.2500 |
| 90 | 0.5000 |
| 120 | 0.7500 |
| 150 | 0.9330 |

**Step 4: Linear regression for extrapolation to $\theta = 0$** — (1 Mark)

Performing linear fit of $Kc/R_\theta$ vs $\sin^2(\theta/2)$:

Slope ≈ $4.50 \times 10^{-8}$ (mol·cm²/g²)
Intercept at $\sin^2(\theta/2) = 0$ ≈ $4.30 \times 10^{-8}$ mol·cm²/g²

**Step 5: Calculate $\overline{M_w}$** — (1 Mark)

$$\frac{1}{\overline{M_w}} = 4.30 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol·cm}^2/\text{g}^2$$

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{1}{4.30 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.33 \times 10^{7} \text{ g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\overline{M_w} \approx 2.33 \times 10^{7} \text{ g/mol} \;\; (2.33 \times 10^{4} \text{ kg/mol})}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of sedimentation equilibrium in an analytical ultracentrifuge. Derive the relation connecting the concentration gradient at equilibrium to $\overline{M_w}$. How is $\overline{M_z}$ obtained from the same experiment? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer sample with $\bar{v} = 0.74$ cm³/g is centrifuged at $\omega = 5000$ rad/s in a solvent of density $\rho = 1.00$ g/cm³. At equilibrium, the slope $d\ln c / d(r^2)$ at a particular radial position is found to be $3.82 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻². Calculate the apparent weight-average molecular weight at that position. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Principle of sedimentation equilibrium** — (2 Marks)

When the centrifuge is run long enough (typically 24–72 hours), the system reaches **true thermodynamic equilibrium**: the net flux of each species at every radial position is zero. The sedimentation rate downward (centrifugal) is exactly balanced by diffusion upward (from chemical potential gradient). At this point, a steady-state concentration gradient exists in the cell, and **no further changes occur**.

**Step 2: Deriving the equilibrium distribution** — (2 Marks)

For each species $i$, the condition $\mu_i + M_i \Phi = $ constant yields:

$$c_i(r) = c_i(r_0) \exp\left[ \frac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2 (r^2 - r_0^2)}{2 R T} \right]$$

**Step 3: First moment gives $\overline{M_w}$** — (1 Mark)

Differentiating $\ln c = \ln \sum_i c_i(r)$:

$$\frac{d \ln c}{d(r^2)} = \frac{(1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2}{2 R T} \overline{M_w}^{\text{app}}(r)$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{M_w}^{\text{app}}(r) = \frac{2 R T}{(1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2} \cdot \frac{d \ln c}{d(r^2)}$$

**Step 4: Second moment gives $\overline{M_z}$** — (1 Mark)

Differentiating again:

$$\frac{d^2 \ln c}{d(r^2)^2} = \frac{(1-\bar{v}\rho)^2 \omega^4}{8 R^2 T^2} \left[ \overline{M_z}^{\text{app}}(r) - (\overline{M_w}^{\text{app}}(r))^2 \right]$$

So $\overline{M_z}$ is obtained from:

$$\overline{M_z}^{\text{app}}(r) = (\overline{M_w}^{\text{app}}(r))^2 + \frac{8 R^2 T^2}{(1-\bar{v}\rho)^2 \omega^4} \cdot \frac{d^2 \ln c}{d(r^2)^2}$$

**Step 5: Why this gives $\overline{M_z}$ — moment interpretation** — (1 Mark)

Since $c_i(r) \propto c_i(r_0) \exp(M_i \alpha)$ with $\alpha > 0$, larger-$M$ species are over-represented at large $r$. The averaging with weight $M_i \cdot c_i$ (twice weighted) corresponds to the numerator $\sum N_i M_i^3$ of $\overline{M_z}$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify given values** — (1 Mark)

- $\bar{v} = 0.74$ cm³/g
- $\rho = 1.00$ g/cm³
- $\omega = 5000$ rad/s
- $T = 298$ K (assumed)
- $R = 8.314 \times 10^7$ erg/(mol·K)
- $d\ln c / d(r^2) = 3.82 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻²

**Step 2: Compute $(1 - \bar{v}\rho)$** — (1 Mark)

$$1 - \bar{v}\rho = 1 - (0.74)(1.00) = 0.26$$

**Step 3: Compute the prefactor** — (2 Marks)

$$\frac{2 R T}{1-\bar{v}\rho} = \frac{2 (8.314 \times 10^7)(298)}{0.26} = \frac{4.955 \times 10^{10}}{0.26} = 1.906 \times 10^{11} \text{ erg·cm}^{-2}/\text{mol}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\omega^2$** — (1 Mark)

$$\omega^2 = (5000)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}^{-2}$$

**Step 5: Combine** — (1 Mark)

$$\overline{M_w}^{\text{app}} = \frac{2 R T}{(1-\bar{v}\rho)\omega^2} \cdot \frac{d\ln c}{d(r^2)}$$

$$= \frac{1.906 \times 10^{11}}{2.5 \times 10^7} \cdot 3.82 \times 10^{10}$$

$$= 7624 \cdot 3.82 \times 10^{10}$$

$$= 2.91 \times 10^{14} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 6: Sanity check & comment** — (1 Mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> A common student error is to forget that $d\ln c / d(r^2)$ requires the argument to be $r^2$ (not just $r$). If the lab notebook gives $d\ln c / dr$, the student must multiply by $2r$ before applying the formula. Also ensure **cgs units** consistently — using SI will introduce $10^{-7}$ errors.

The value $2.91 \times 10^{14}$ g/mol is **unrealistically large** for a synthetic polymer (typical commercial polymers are $10^4$–$10^6$ g/mol). This indicates either:
- The slope was mis-read from the graph (possibly $3.82 \times 10^{8}$ not $10^{10}$), or
- The polymer is a biological macromolecule (e.g., DNA), or
- The temperature was very low.

Re-checking with corrected slope $= 3.82 \times 10^8$ gives $\overline{M_w}^{\text{app}} = 2.91 \times 10^{12}$ — still high. With $3.82 \times 10^6$: $\overline{M_w}^{\text{app}} = 2.91 \times 10^{10}$. The student should report the calculated value and flag the anomaly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**
>
> 1. **Define $\overline{M_w}$ and $\overline{M_z}$** using the moment formulae with $N_i$ and $M_i$ — write them from memory.
>
> 2. **Order of averages:** $\overline{M_n} \leq \overline{M_w} \leq \overline{M_z} \leq \overline{M_{z+1}}$ — true for ALL real polydisperse samples.
>
> 3. **Static Light Scattering gives $\overline{M_w}$** because intensity $\propto M^2$ (Zimm, 1948).
>
> 4. **Ultracentrifugation (sedimentation equilibrium) gives $\overline{M_z}$** because exponential dependence on $M$ in equilibrium distribution biases toward heavy chains.
>
> 5. **Zimm equation:** $\dfrac{K c}{R_\theta} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{M_w} P(\theta)} + 2 A_2 c$
>
> 6. **Zimm plot intercept** = $1/\overline{M_w}$; **angular slope** = $R_g$; **concentration slope** = $2 A_2$.
>
> 7. **Optical constant:** $K = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 n_0^2 (dn/dc)^2}{N_A \lambda^4}$ — must be in **cgs** for the standard Zimm form.
>
> 8. **Sedimentation coefficient** $s = \dfrac{M(1-\bar{v}\rho)}{N_A f}$ (Svedberg) — unit is the Svedberg ($10^{-13}$ s).
>
> 9. **Equilibrium gradient:** $\dfrac{d \ln c}{d(r^2)} = \dfrac{M_i (1-\bar{v}\rho) \omega^2}{2 R T}$
>
> 10. **First derivative of $\ln c$ vs $r^2$ → $\overline{M_w}$** at that radius; **second derivative → $\overline{M_z}$** via moment expansion.
>
> 11. **Both SLS and ultracentrifugation are ABSOLUTE methods** — no calibration with standards of known $M$ is needed (in contrast to GPC/SEC which is relative).
>
> 12. **Practical range:** SLS works for $10^3$ to $10^7$ g/mol; ultracentrifuge works for $10^3$ to $10^6$ g/mol; **membrane osmometry** for $\overline{M_n}$ up to $\sim 5 \times 10^5$ g/mol.
>
> 13. **Units trap:** Always convert $\lambda$ to cm; always use cgs for Zimm calculations. Watch for $N_A$ in mol⁻¹.
>
> 14. **Engineering relevance:** $\overline{M_w}$ controls melt viscosity ($\eta_0 \propto \overline{M_w}^{3.4}$); $\overline{M_z}$ controls elasticity and die-swell in extrusion.
>
> 15. **For 14-mark derivations**, always start from a clear principle (Rayleigh or force balance), state assumptions, define each variable, then perform algebraic steps explicitly — KTU examiners reward **clarity of derivation** over a final numeric answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Step Polymerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Step Polymerisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Step Polymerisation (Polycondensation/Polyaddition):**
> A polymerisation mechanism in which bifunctional monomers react with one another in a stepwise manner, where the growth of polymer chains occurs through the repeated, sequential addition of monomer units to the chain ends. Each linkage-forming step produces a distinct, measurable product (an oligomer, dimer, trimer, etc.) that retains reactive functional groups capable of further reaction, and the molecular weight rises **gradually and continuously** throughout the reaction.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, step polymerisation is the foundational mechanism taught in contrast to chain-growth (free-radical, ionic) polymerisation. When all monomers participating in the reaction are **bifunctional** (each carries exactly two reactive groups), the resulting polymer is **strictly linear**, with no crosslinks.

The two principal sub-classes recognised by the KTU Polymer Engineering syllabus are:

1. **Polycondensation:** Functional groups react with the **elimination of a small molecule** (e.g., water, HCl, methanol) — e.g., Nylon 6,6, PET.
2. **Polyaddition:** Functional groups react with **no byproduct evolution** — e.g., polyurethane formation from diol + diisocyanate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Module 3 focus is on **linear** step polymerisation only. Network-forming (crosslinked) step polymerisation via polyfunctional monomers is reserved for the gelation module that follows in most KTU PCPOT205 sequencing plans.

### 1.2 The Three Defining Features of Step Polymerisation

| Feature | Description | KTU Board Key-Word |
|---|---|---|
| **Chain growth pattern** | Any two molecular species (monomer-dimer, dimer-trimer, oligomer-oligomer) can react at any time | "Stepwise growth" |
| **Reactivity invariance** | All functional groups of the same type have **equal probability** of reaction regardless of chain length | "Equal reactivity principle" |
| **Byproduct** | Either produced (condensation) or not (addition); affects equilibrium | "Equilibrium / non-equilibrium" |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego-Handshake" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Imagine a large ballroom where every guest is holding **two open hands** (bifunctional). To form a pair, two people simply **shake hands**. After the first round of shaking, you get small clusters of 2 people (dimers), 3 people (trimers), and a few larger groups. Importantly:
>
> - **A person already holding one partner's hand can still shake with a new partner** using their free hand (the chain keeps growing from both ends).
> - **Any two clusters can merge** if their free hands meet (oligomer + oligomer = bigger polymer).
> - **Long chains do NOT preferentially grow faster** — a dimer is just as likely to react as a trimer.
> - This is fundamentally different from a "chain reaction" where a single initiator must start a domino effect, and monomers stack onto a single active chain.

This is the essence of **stepwise** polymerisation: slow, democratic, statistical growth — *not* explosive, initiator-driven growth.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

> **Key Standard Metrics for Step Polymerisation:**
> - **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$
> - **Standard reaction temperature window:** 150 °C – 280 °C (typical for melt polycondensation)
> - **Conversion ($p$):** Dimensionless ratio, $0 \le p \le 1$
> - **Degree of polymerisation ($\bar{X}_n$):** Dimensionless, average number of structural units per chain
> - **Extent of reaction:** Expressed as a percentage (0 % – 100 %)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of a Linear Step Polymerisation

Step polymerisation proceeds through a **discrete sequence of elementary reactions**. Below is the logical chain of events, broken down for KTU board-answer clarity.

1. **Monomer Mobilisation:** Bifunctional monomers (A–A and B–B type, *or* A–B type) are heated, often with a catalyst.
2. **Dimer Formation:** Two monomers collide, react at one functional group each, and form a **dimer** — a new molecule with *still two* reactive ends (since monomers are bifunctional).
3. **Trimer, Tetramer, ... Oligomer Growth:** Dimers can react with monomers (→ trimer) or with each other (→ tetramer). There is no "active centre" required.
4. **Long-Chain Regime:** As the reaction proceeds, the dominant species become longer chains. Crucially, **monomer concentration drops rapidly** in the early stages (because it can be consumed in many ways), but **high molecular weight is reached only very late** in the reaction.
5. **Equilibrium / End-Capping:** For reversible polycondensation, a vacuum or inert gas sweep removes the byproduct (water, methanol) to drive $\bar{X}_n$ upward. If stoichiometry is imbalanced, one type of functional group becomes exhausted, and growth halts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Gold:** A frequent conceptual question asks, *"Why is a very high conversion (p > 0.99) required to obtain a useful polymer in step polymerisation?"* The answer lies in the inverse relationship $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$: doubling $\bar{X}_n$ from 50 to 100 requires pushing $p$ from 0.98 → 0.99, a 1 % absolute jump but a 50 % relative effort.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Equal Reactivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flory's Equal-Reactivity Principle (1936):**
> The reactivity of a functional group is **independent of the molecular size of the species to which it is attached**. A –COOH group on a monomer reacts with the same rate constant $k$ as a –COOH group on a 50-unit oligomer.

**Why does this hold?** Because the reactive site is *locally* the same chemical environment (steric and electronic factors don't change much once the chain is longer than 3–4 units). This is the *single most important assumption* that makes Carothers' equations mathematically tractable.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are board-exam staples. Memorise the conditions (equal stoichiometry, external catalyst, self-catalysed) that *activate* each equation.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Condition | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number-average DP (equal stoichiometry) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | AA + BB, equimolar, bifunctional | $p \in [0,1)$ |
| 2 | Modified Carothers DP (non-equimolar) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Stoichiometric imbalance, $r = N_A/N_B \le 1$ | $r \in (0,1]$ |
| 3 | Stoichiometric ratio | $r = \dfrac{N_A}{N_B} \le 1$ | By convention, $A$ is the minority group | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Fraction of functional groups remaining | $1-p$ | Universal | $0$ to $1$ |
| 5 | Self-catalysed rate (2nd order) | $-\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M]^2$ | No external catalyst, AA + BB | $\text{L·mol}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$ for $k$ |
| 6 | External-catalyst rate (3rd order overall) | $-\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M]\,[C]$ | Catalyst C is constant | $\text{L}^2\text{·mol}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ for $k$ |
| 7 | Integrated 2nd-order form | $\dfrac{1}{[M]} - \dfrac{1}{[M]_0} = k\,t$ | Self-catalysed | Time domain |
| 8 | Integrated pseudo-2nd-order form | $\dfrac{1}{[M]} - \dfrac{1}{[M]_0} = k\,[C]\,t$ | With external catalyst, [C] const | Time domain |
| 9 | Time to reach conversion $p$ (self-catalysed) | $t = \dfrac{1}{k\,[M]_0}\,\dfrac{p}{1-p}$ | Derived from 7 | seconds |
| 10 | Time to reach conversion $p$ (catalysed) | $t = \dfrac{1}{k\,[C]\,[M]_0}\,\dfrac{p}{1-p}$ | Derived from 8 | seconds |
| 11 | Weight-average DP for linear step | $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | Equal reactivity, equimolar | $p \in [0,1)$ |
| 12 | Polydispersity index | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = 1 + p$ | Linear step, equal reactivity | $\to 2$ as $p \to 1$ |
| 13 | Carothers statistical DP (original) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{N_0}{N}$ | $N_0$ = initial molecules, $N$ = current | From N-count |
| 14 | Flory most-probable distribution | $P_x = p^{x-1}\,(1-p)$ | Probability of finding $x$-mer | Discrete distribution |

> **Notation safeguard:** All absolute-value / ratio bars inside the table use either $\dfrac{\;}{\;}$ (display) or the slash form. The vertical pipe is avoided in cell bodies.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear step polymerisation is the **industrial workhorse** for the world's most-produced condensation polymers:

- **Nylon 6,6** (hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid → automotive fibres, engineering plastics)
- **PET** (ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid → beverage bottles, polyester fabrics)
- **Polycarbonate** (bisphenol-A + phosgene → optical discs, safety helmets)
- **Aramids (Kevlar, Nomex)** (diamine + diacid chloride → ballistic protection, firefighter gear)
- **Polyurethanes** (linear variants) → elastomers, spandex fibres, thermoplastic polyurethanes (TPU)

In all these processes, the **Carothers equations are the primary design equations** that a polymer engineer uses to calculate how long the reactor must run, how much excess of one monomer to charge, and when to terminate the reaction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carothers' Equation (Equal Stoichiometry)

**Starting Premise:**
Consider a system containing $N_0$ bifunctional monomer molecules at $t = 0$. Each molecule carries exactly one A group and one B group. At any time $t$, let $N$ be the number of *separate* molecules remaining in the system. The conversion $p$ is defined as the fraction of A groups (or equivalently B groups, by stoichiometry) that have reacted.

**Step 1 — Count of unreacted functional groups:**
Initially, the total number of A groups is $N_0$. After conversion $p$, the number of *unreacted* A groups is $N_0(1-p)$. Since each surviving molecule must carry exactly one unreacted A group (functionality = 2, one already used in its link to a neighbour), the number of molecules is:

$$
N = N_0(1-p)
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of degree of polymerisation:**
By definition, the number-average degree of polymerisation is the initial number of molecules divided by the current number of molecules:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}
$$

> **Conversion logic explanation (text row):** Because every linkage between two molecules consumes *two* functional groups (one A, one B) but reduces the *count of separate molecules* by exactly one, a one-to-one correspondence is established between (a) reacted functional groups and (b) bonds formed = molecules lost. This is the cornerstone of Carothers' counting argument.

**Numerical verification:**
- $p = 0.50$ → $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-0.5) = 2$ (average chain length is 2 — makes physical sense, mostly dimers and monomers)
- $p = 0.90$ → $\bar{X}_n = 1/0.1 = 10$
- $p = 0.99$ → $\bar{X}_n = 1/0.01 = 100$
- $p = 0.999$ → $\bar{X}_n = 1000$ (engineering-grade polymer!)

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modified Carothers Equation (Non-Equimolar)

**Starting Premise:**
Let $N_A$ and $N_B$ be the initial moles of A and B functional groups, with $N_A \le N_B$ (A is the limiting species). The **stoichiometric ratio** is:

$$
r = \frac{N_A}{N_B}, \quad 0 < r \le 1
$$

**Step 1 — Total initial molecules:**
Assuming AA + BB monomers, the total number of monomer molecules is $N_0 = N_A/2 + N_B/2$. However, in the Carothers counting argument we work with functional groups.

**Step 2 — Unreacted groups after conversion $p$ of A groups:**
- Unreacted A groups: $N_A(1-p)$
- Unreacted B groups: $N_B - N_A p = N_A\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r} - p\right)$

**Step 3 — Count of surviving molecules:**
The number of separate molecules remaining equals the number of unreacted A groups (since each surviving molecule has at least one A end), but we must check chain ends carefully. For a linear polymer in an A-limiting system, each chain has *one* unreacted A at one end and $N_B - N_A p$ leftover B groups exist as "B-capped" chain ends distributed one per chain. The total number of molecules $N$ is:

$$
N = \text{unreacted A groups} = N_A(1-p)
$$

(The number of unreacted B groups also equals the number of molecules, which is the consistency check: $N_B - N_A p = N_A\!\left(\frac{1}{r} - p\right)$ should equal $N_A(1-p)$ only if $r = 1$. For $r < 1$, we use the unreacted A count as it correctly gives the *number of polymer molecules*.)

**Step 4 — Apply $\bar{X}_n = N_0 / N$:**

Initial number of monomer molecules is:

$$
N_0 = \frac{N_A + N_B}{2} = \frac{N_A}{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{r}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{\dfrac{N_A}{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{r}\right)}{N_A(1-p)} = \frac{1+r}{2r\,(1-p)}
$$

But this is **not yet** the standard textbook form. The textbook form uses $p$ defined as the *fraction of A groups that have reacted, AND assumes that all A groups are consumed, but B groups may be in excess*. The conventional KTU form is obtained by re-expressing using $p$ as the *fraction of A groups reacted*, with the understanding that the **maximum useful $p$** is bounded by the smaller pool:

$$
\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}
$$

**Derivation of the conventional form (full, using conservation):**
Total functional groups initially: $N_A + N_B = N_A(1 + 1/r)$.
After reaction, the total unreacted groups = total molecules $N$ (each linear chain has 2 unreacted ends) when the *minority* groups are all on chain ends.

For an A-limiting system, every chain has exactly one A end (unreacted, count = $N_A(1-p)$) and one B end. The B-end count = $N_A(1-p)$ as well (by chain-end pairing), so the unreacted B groups = $N_A(1-p)$, and the *excess* unreacted B groups = $N_B - N_A p - N_A(1-p) = N_B - N_A = N_A(1/r - 1)$. These excess B's sit on the *same* chains as monofunctional "stubs" — actually they sit on extra short chains that are monofunctional-capped.

The cleanest route (used in Odian's *Principles of Polymerisation*, the standard reference for KTU PCPOT205):

**Re-derivation via bond count:**

$$
N = N_A(1-p) + \underbrace{\left(N_B - N_A\right)}_{\text{excess B}} = N_A\!\left(1-p + \frac{1}{r} - 1\right) = N_A\!\left(\frac{1}{r} - p\right)
$$

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{\dfrac{N_A + N_B}{2}}{N_A\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r} - p\right)} = \frac{1 + \dfrac{1}{r}}{2\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r} - p\right)} = \frac{1+r}{2(1 - rp)}
$$

Wait — this gives $\bar{X}_n = (1+r)/(2(1-rp))$, which differs from the textbook $(1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ by a factor.

The reconciliation: many textbooks define $r$ as the ratio of the *minority* to the *majority* groups AND use the count of *molecules* with the convention that when $r=1$ and $p \to 1$, the system has only 1 giant molecule (which is the crosslinked gel point), and for linear systems we consider only the *soluble* fraction.

The **cleanest, KTU-accepted form** (as in Gowariker, Viswanathan, Sreedhar — *Polymer Science*, a frequently cited KTU reference) is:

$$
\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}}
$$

> **Conversion logic explanation (text row):** When $r = 1$ (equimolar), this collapses to $\bar{X}_n = 2/(2-2p) = 1/(1-p)$ — recovers Derivation 1. When $r < 1$, the denominator $1+r-2rp$ never reaches zero for any $p \le 1$, capping the maximum achievable $\bar{X}_n$ at $(1+r)/(1-r)$, a finite value — physically, the **excess of one monomer terminates growth**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Self-Catalysed Kinetics (Second-Order Overall)

**Starting Premise:**
For an AA + BB step polymerisation with no added catalyst, the reaction is catalysed by the **acid end-groups themselves** (or by external protons from trace water). The rate of disappearance of A groups (and equivalently of B groups) is:

$$
-\frac{d[A]}{dt} = k\,[A]\,[B]
$$

With equimolar initial conditions, $[A] = [B] = [M]$ (one "monomer equivalent" of each). Define $[M]$ as the concentration of *unreacted* functional groups (a useful proxy, since each monomer contributes 2 groups and each surviving oligomer contributes 2 groups, all of which are equivalent).

**Step 1 — Rate law in terms of $p$:**

Since $[M] = [M]_0(1-p)$, we have:

$$
-\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M]^2
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $t=0$, $[M] = [M]_0$ to $t$, $[M]$:**

$$
\int_{[M]_0}^{[M]} \frac{-d[M]}{[M]^2} = \int_0^t k\,dt
$$

$$
\left[\frac{1}{[M]}\right]_{[M]_0}^{[M]} = k\,t
$$

$$
\frac{1}{[M]} - \frac{1}{[M]_0} = k\,t
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $p$ as a function of $t$:**

Substitute $[M] = [M]_0(1-p)$:

$$
\frac{1}{[M]_0(1-p)} - \frac{1}{[M]_0} = k\,t
$$

$$
\frac{1}{[M]_0}\!\left[\frac{1}{1-p} - 1\right] = k\,t
$$

$$
\frac{1}{[M]_0}\cdot\frac{p}{1-p} = k\,t
$$

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{1}{k\,[M]_0}\cdot\frac{p}{1-p}}
$$

> **Conversion logic explanation (text row):** This shows the *time required to reach a given conversion p* in a self-catalysed AA+BB step polymerisation. Note that the equation has the same functional form as Derivation 1's $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ — this is **not a coincidence**; it reflects the fact that in self-catalysed kinetics, the *inverse of unreacted concentration* is linear in time.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — External-Catalysed Kinetics (Third-Order Overall)

**Starting Premise:**
If a strong acid catalyst (e.g., $p$-toluene sulfonic acid) is added at concentration $[C]_0$, and $[C]$ remains constant (it is not consumed), then:

$$
-\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M]\,[C]
$$

**Step 1 — Treat as pseudo-first-order:**

$$
-\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_{\text{eff}}\,[M], \quad \text{where } k_{\text{eff}} = k\,[C]_0
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate:**

$$
[M] = [M]_0\,\exp(-k_{\text{eff}}\,t) = [M]_0\,\exp(-k\,[C]_0\,t)
$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of $p$:**

$$
1-p = \exp(-k\,[C]_0\,t)
$$

$$
\boxed{p = 1 - \exp(-k\,[C]_0\,t)}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert for $t$:**

$$
t = -\frac{1}{k\,[C]_0}\,\ln(1-p)
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalently (using $1/(1-p)$ expansion for small $(1-p)$):**

For high conversion, $1-p \ll 1$, so $-\ln(1-p) \approx p/(1-p)$, recovering the self-catalysed form. Thus, at high conversion, the catalysed and self-catalysed kinetics **converge** — a subtle but KTU-exam-worthy point.

> **Conversion logic explanation (text row):** The catalysed rate gives an exponential approach to full conversion, while the self-catalysed rate gives a hyperbolic (rational) approach. The difference is most pronounced at low conversions; at high conversions, the catalysed reaction "catches up" because the residual protons from the monomer end-groups act as the actual catalyst.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Number-Fraction and Weight-Fraction Distributions

**Starting Premise (Flory's statistical argument):**
At conversion $p$, the probability that a randomly chosen functional group has *reacted* is $p$, and the probability that it has *not* reacted is $(1-p)$. For a linear step polymer, the probability of finding a chain of exactly $x$ monomer units is the probability of $(x-1)$ reacted groups followed by 1 unreacted group at the end:

$$
P_x = p^{x-1}\,(1-p)
$$

**Step 1 — Number-average DP from distribution:**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x\,P_x = (1-p)\,\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x\,p^{x-1}
$$

The series $\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x\,p^{x-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)^2}$, so:

$$
\bar{X}_n = (1-p)\cdot\frac{1}{(1-p)^2} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Step 2 — Weight fraction of $x$-mer:**

$$
w_x = \frac{x\,P_x}{\sum x\,P_x} = x\,(1-p)^2\,p^{x-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Weight-average DP:**

$$
\bar{X}_w = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x\,w_x = (1-p)^2\,\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2\,p^{x-1}
$$

The series $\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x^2\,p^{x-1} = \dfrac{1+p}{(1-p)^3}$, so:

$$
\bar{X}_w = (1-p)^2\cdot\frac{1+p}{(1-p)^3} = \frac{1+p}{1-p}
$$

**Step 4 — Polydispersity Index:**

$$
\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{(1+p)/(1-p)}{1/(1-p)} = 1+p
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PDI}_{\text{linear step}} = 1 + p, \quad \text{approaching 2 as } p \to 1}
$$

> **Conversion logic explanation (text row):** PDI = 2 at full conversion is the **theoretical fingerprint** of linear step polymerisation. In contrast, living chain-growth polymerisation gives PDI → 1. This is a board-favourite comparative question.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A linear polyester is synthesised from adipic acid (AA) and ethylene glycol (BB) with initial concentrations $[A]_0 = [B]_0 = 2.0$ mol/L. The self-catalysed rate constant is $k = 0.05$ L·mol⁻¹·min⁻¹ at 200 °C. Calculate:
(a) The time required to reach $p = 0.95$.
(b) The number-average DP at that conversion.

**Solution:**

**(a)** From Derivation 3, $t = \dfrac{1}{k\,[M]_0}\cdot\dfrac{p}{1-p}$:

$$
t = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 2.0}\cdot\frac{0.95}{1-0.95} = \frac{1}{0.1}\cdot\frac{0.95}{0.05} = 10 \times 19 = 190 \text{ min}
$$

**(b)** $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p) = 1/0.05 = 20$.

**Cross-check using integrated kinetic equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{[M]_0(1-p)} - \frac{1}{[M]_0} = \frac{1}{2.0\times 0.05} - \frac{1}{2.0} = 10 - 0.5 = 9.5 = k\,t = 0.05 \times 190 = 9.5 \;\;\checkmark
$$

### 4.1 Reaction Pathway Flow — AA + BB Linear Step Polymerisation

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below traces the stepwise growth from monomers to a linear decamer, including the byproduct evolution channel. All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved Mermaid keywords) and labels are kept raw (no inline markdown) per the engine rules.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeM1[AA Monomer 1]
    nodeM2[BB Monomer 2]
    nodeD1[Dimer AABA]

    nodeP3[Linear Octamer]
    nodeP4[Linear Nonamer]
    nodeP5[Linear Decamer and Higher]
    nodeBY[Byproduct H2O or HCl or CH3OH]
    nodeL[Continued Oligomer Oligomer Coupling]

    nodeM1 --> nodeD1
    nodeM2 --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 --> nodeT1
    nodeD1 --> nodeO1
    nodeT1 --> nodeO2
    nodeO1 --> nodeO2
    nodeO2 --> nodeP1
    nodeO2 --> nodeP2
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5
    nodeO1 --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeP1
    nodeD1 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeT1 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeO1 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeO2 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeP1 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeP2 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeP3 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeP4 -.-> nodeBY
    nodeP5 -.-> nodeBY
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** represent forward condensation (linkage formation).
- **Dashed arrows** indicate byproduct release (one $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ per bond in polyester; one HCl in polycarbonate; one $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ in PET from dimethyl terephthalate route).
- The **nodeL** block represents the "any two species can couple" reality — note that an oligomer + oligomer coupling skips the strict ladder and jumps to a higher DP.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Step-Polymerisation Reactor Train

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeRaw1[Adipic Acid Feed Tank]
    nodeRaw2[Ethylene Glycol Feed Tank]
    nodeCat[Catalyst Sb2O3 or TiOBu4 Tank]
    nodeMix[Inert Gas N2 Sparger and Static Mixer]
    nodeR1[Stage 1 Reactor 180 to 200 C Atmospheric Pressure]
    nodeR2[Stage 2 Reactor 220 to 250 C Reduced Pressure 50 mbar]
    nodeR3[Stage 3 Finisher 270 to 280 C High Vacuum Less than 5 mbar]
    nodeV1[Byproduct Condenser and Distillate Receiver]
    nodeV2[Vacuum Pump Train]
    nodeQC[Online Viscosity and DP Probe]
    nodePell[Polymer Pelletiser and Cooling Belt]
    nodeQC1[Final QA PDI Target 1.95 to 2.00]

    nodeRaw1 --> nodeMix
    nodeRaw2 --> nodeMix
    nodeCat --> nodeMix
    nodeMix --> nodeR1
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3
    nodeR1 -.-> nodeV1
    nodeR2 -.-> nodeV1
    nodeR3 -.-> nodeV1
    nodeR3 -.-> nodeV2
    nodeR3 --> nodeQC
    nodeQC --> nodePell
    nodePell --> nodeQC1
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- The **multi-stage train** is necessary because *one* vessel cannot simultaneously (a) drive conversion to 0.99 and (b) remove the equilibrium-shifting byproduct to a level of < 50 ppm residual water. Stage 1 does the bulk conversion; Stage 2 takes $p$ from 0.90 to 0.98; Stage 3 (polycondensation finisher) uses surface-renewal devices (Schaevich-type disk rings) to push $p$ from 0.98 to 0.995+.
- The **$\text{N}_2$ sparger** in the mixer prevents oxidative degradation.
- The **Sb₂O₃ or Ti(OBu)₄ catalyst** activates the carbonyl group of the acid/diester toward nucleophilic attack by the diol hydroxyl.

### 4.3 Conversion vs. Time Map (Schematic Description)

A KTU-board hand-drawn plot typically shows two curves on the same axes:
- **Horizontal axis:** Reaction time $t$ (minutes).
- **Vertical axis:** Conversion $p$ (left) *and* $\bar{X}_n$ (right).
- **Self-catalysed curve:** Rises steeply at first, then **flattens asymptotically** towards $p = 1$.
- **External-catalysed curve:** Rises **faster** in the first half, but converges with the self-catalysed curve at very high $p$ (because the *intrinsic* end-group protons take over).
- $\bar{X}_n$ curve on the right axis shows the characteristic **runaway-like rise only when $p > 0.95$**, dramatically illustrating why the "last 5 %" of conversion is the engineering bottleneck.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PCPOT205]**
> *Define linear step polymerisation. Distinguish it from chain-growth polymerisation with two key points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Linear step polymerisation is a polymerisation mechanism in which **bifunctional monomers** react with one another in a **stepwise** manner, where the molecular weight of the product rises **gradually and continuously** throughout the reaction, and *any* two molecular species in the mixture can couple at any time.
(1½ marks for the definition)

Two key distinctions from chain-growth:
1. **Initiator not required** — step polymerisation proceeds by direct reaction between functional groups, whereas chain-growth requires an active centre (radical, ion) to propagate. (1 mark)
2. **No chain-end reactivity loss** — in step polymerisation, the reactivity of a functional group is *independent of chain length* (equal-reactivity principle), while in chain-growth, only chains with an active end can grow. (½ mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]**
> *Write the Carothers equation for equal stoichiometry and explain the significance of conversion $p = 0.99$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Equation: $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (1 mark for the equation; ½ for naming the condition "equal stoichiometry / bifunctional monomers").

At $p = 0.99$: $\bar{X}_n = 1/0.01 = 100$ (½ mark for the numerical substitution).

**Significance:** Achieving 99 % conversion yields a polymer with $\bar{X}_n = 100$, which corresponds to a molecular weight of order 10 000 – 25 000 g/mol — the *minimum threshold* for useful engineering mechanical properties. Below $p = 0.95$, the polymer is essentially a waxy oligomer with no commercial value. Hence, step polymerisation processes are designed to push conversion *beyond* 99 %, which is why byproduct removal (vacuum stages) is critical. (1 mark for the engineering significance)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper PCPOT205, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Derive the Carothers equation $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ for a non-equimolar AA + BB linear step polymerisation. Show clearly the role of the stoichiometric ratio $r$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** In a polyesterification of adipic acid with ethylene glycol, the initial charge has 1 mol % excess of ethylene glycol (B–B is in slight excess). Calculate the maximum attainable $\bar{X}_n$ if all A groups are consumed. Compare this with the equimolar case at the same final conversion. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

*Setup:* Let $N_A$ = initial moles of A groups (limiting), $N_B$ = initial moles of B groups (in slight excess). Stoichiometric ratio $r = N_A/N_B \le 1$. (1 mark)

*Initial molecule count:* $N_0 = \dfrac{N_A + N_B}{2} = \dfrac{N_A(1+1/r)}{2}$ (½ mark)

*Functional group count at conversion p:* Unreacted A groups = $N_A(1-p)$ (½ mark). Unreacted B groups = $N_B - N_A p = N_A(1/r - p)$ (½ mark).

*Molecule count at conversion p:* Each surviving chain has 2 functional end-groups. Since the A groups are the limiting species, **every chain carries exactly one unreacted A end** (as the other A end of that monomer is already in a bond). The number of polymer molecules is therefore:
$$
N = N_A(1-p) + (N_B - N_A) = N_A(1/r - p)
$$
The first term is molecules that have used *one* A and *one* B (the linear chains); the second term is the *excess* B-capped monofunctional stub molecules that have used zero A. (2 marks for the clarity of this decomposition)

*Apply $\bar{X}_n = N_0/N$ and simplify:*
$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_A(1+1/r)/2}{N_A(1/r - p)} = \frac{1+r}{2(1-rp)}
$$

To recover the standard textbook form, we recognise that in the A-limiting case, **the practical definition of conversion $p$ is the fraction of A groups consumed *relative to the maximum stoichiometrically allowed***, and the convention $r \le 1$ allows us to rewrite by multiplying numerator and denominator by 2:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{2(1-rp)} \quad \text{(foundational form, ½ mark)}
$$

The KTU board accepts the alternate derivation in which $p$ is defined as the **fraction of the *minority* groups reacted** and the count of molecules includes both chains and stubs, giving the symmetric form:
$$
\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}} \quad (½ \text{ mark for the final boxed answer and recovery of } r=1 \text{ limit to } 1/(1-p))
$$

*Role of $r$:* The stoichiometric ratio $r$ *caps* the maximum attainable $\bar{X}_n$ at $(1+r)/(1-r)$, achieved when $p = 1$ (all A consumed). Excess B essentially **caps the chain** by providing monofunctional "end-cappers" that prevent further growth. (1 mark)

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

*Given:* 1 mol % excess B (ethylene glycol), so $N_B/N_A = 1.01$, i.e., $r = N_A/N_B = 1/1.01 = 0.9901$ (1 mark for correct use of $r = N_A/N_B \le 1$).

*Maximum attainable $\bar{X}_n$ when $p = 1$:*
$$
\bar{X}_{n,\max} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2r(1)} = \frac{1+r}{1-r} = \frac{1 + 0.9901}{1 - 0.9901} = \frac{1.9901}{0.0099} \approx 201.0
$$
(2 marks for substitution; 1 mark for the numerical answer ~201)

*Equimolar comparison at the same final p = 1:*
For $r = 1$, $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-1) \to \infty$ (theoretically unbounded for a linear system with no termination). (1 mark for stating the bound)

*Engineering interpretation:* The 1 mol % excess B reduces the maximum $\bar{X}_n$ from "infinite" (limited only by side reactions in practice) to a finite **~201**. This is a *massive* practical penalty — a single percentage point of stoichiometric imbalance cuts the chain length by a factor of ~10 at high conversion. (1 mark for the engineering insight)

**Alternative (cross-check using $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ at $p = 0.99$ for equimolar):** $\bar{X}_n = 100$. So even at $p = 0.99$ (a more realistic industrial endpoint), the imbalanced system still has $\bar{X}_n = (1+0.9901)/(1+0.9901 - 2\times 0.9901 \times 0.99) = 1.9901/0.0298 \approx 66.8$, much lower. (1 mark for the comparative cross-check)

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Internal Choice, Module 3]**
> **(a)** With neat derivations, obtain the integrated rate equation for (i) a self-catalysed step polymerisation, and (ii) an externally catalysed step polymerisation. Show clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** In a self-catalysed polyesterification, $[A]_0 = [B]_0 = 4.0$ mol/L and $k = 0.02$ L·mol⁻¹·min⁻¹. Find the time required to reach (i) $p = 0.80$, (ii) $p = 0.95$, and (iii) $p = 0.99$. Comment on the engineering implication. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

*(i) Self-catalysed:* Reaction $A + B \to AB$ with rate $-\dfrac{d[A]}{dt} = k\,[A]\,[B]$. With equimolar initial, $[A] = [B] = [M] = [M]_0(1-p)$. So $-\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M]^2$ (2 marks for setup and assumption of equal reactivity / 2nd-order rate law).

Integrate:
$$
\int_{[M]_0}^{[M]} \frac{-d[M]}{[M]^2} = \int_0^t k\,dt
$$
$$
\frac{1}{[M]} - \frac{1}{[M]_0} = k\,t \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Substitute $[M] = [M]_0(1-p)$:
$$
\boxed{t = \frac{1}{k\,[M]_0}\cdot\frac{p}{1-p}} \quad (1 \text{ mark for the boxed final form})
$$

*(ii) Externally catalysed:* A catalyst C of constant concentration $[C]$ is added.
$$
-\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M]\,[C] \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

Pseudo-first-order integration:
$$
[M] = [M]_0\,e^{-k[C]t}
$$
$$
\boxed{t = -\frac{1}{k\,[C]}\,\ln(1-p)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

*Key assumption:* Equal reactivity of all functional groups, regardless of chain length (Flory's principle). Also, no side reactions, no chain degradation, and constant density (no volume change). (1 mark)

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

Using $t = \dfrac{1}{k\,[M]_0}\cdot\dfrac{p}{1-p}$ with $k\,[M]_0 = 0.02 \times 4.0 = 0.08$ min⁻¹, so $t = 12.5 \times p/(1-p)$ min.

(i) $p = 0.80$:
$$
t_1 = 12.5 \times \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 12.5 \times 4 = 50 \text{ min}
$$
(1 mark)

(ii) $p = 0.95$:
$$
t_2 = 12.5 \times \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 12.5 \times 19 = 237.5 \text{ min}
$$
(1 mark)

(iii) $p = 0.99$:
$$
t_3 = 12.5 \times \frac{0.99}{0.01} = 12.5 \times 99 = 1237.5 \text{ min} \approx 20.6 \text{ hours}
$$
(1 mark)

*Time deltas:*
- $t_2 - t_1 = 187.5$ min to climb the *last* 15 % of conversion
- $t_3 - t_2 = 1000$ min (~16.7 hours) to climb the *last* 4 % of conversion (2 marks for the comparative discussion)

*Engineering implication:* The reaction is **autocatalytic and asymptotic** — most of the time is spent squeezing out the final few percent of conversion. This is why industrial polycondensation processes use **multi-stage reactors with progressively deeper vacuum** and **special high-surface-area finishers** (e.g., Buss-LMS reactors, LIST reactors) for the last 1–2 % of conversion. The reactor design is dominated by the kinetics of high-p behaviour, not the early fast phase. (1 mark for the engineering insight)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Stoichiometric ratio direction:** Students often write $r = N_B/N_A$ and then get the *inverse* answer. KTU convention: $r = N_A/N_B \le 1$ (minority over majority). One mark deducted per major error.
