> 2. **Self vs. external catalysis:** Conflating the two rate laws. The self-catalysed equation is *second-order in [M]*; the catalysed equation is *first-order in [M]*. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Neglecting "1" in the denominator:** Writing $t = 12.5 \times p$ instead of $t = 12.5 \times p/(1-p)$ is the single most common error. Always retain the $(1-p)$ denominator.
> 4. **Stating $\bar{X}_n$ instead of $\bar{X}_w$:** The weight-average expression $\bar{X}_w = (1+p)/(1-p)$ is *different* from $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$. Confusing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting units of $k$:** Self-catalysed $k$ has units of L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹; external-catalysed $k$ has units of L²·mol⁻²·s⁻¹. Board examiners will notice if the numerical answer comes out in nonsensical units.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):**

- [ ] **Definition core:** Linear step polymerisation = bifunctional monomers + stepwise, any-species-couples + gradual MW rise + *no* initiator.
- [ ] **Carothers (equal):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$. At $p = 0.99$, $\bar{X}_n = 100$.
- [ ] **Carothers (non-equal):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$, with $r = N_A/N_B \le 1$.
- [ ] **PDI fingerprint:** Linear step polymerisation has $\text{PDI} = 1 + p \to 2$ at full conversion.
- [ ] **Equal-reactivity principle** (Flory) is the *load-bearing assumption* of all Carothers equations.
- [ ] **Self-catalysed rate:** $-\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M]^2$ → integrated $\dfrac{1}{[M]} - \dfrac{1}{[M]_0} = k\,t$.
- [ ] **External-catalysed rate:** $-\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k\,[M][C]$ → integrated $[M] = [M]_0 e^{-k[C]t}$.
- [ ] **Time to reach $p$ (self-catalysed):** $t = \dfrac{1}{k\,[M]_0}\cdot\dfrac{p}{1-p}$.
- [ ] **Time to reach $p$ (catalysed):** $t = -\dfrac{1}{k\,[C]}\ln(1-p)$.
- [ ] **Engineering reality:** > 95 % of reactor time is consumed in reaching the *last* 1–2 % of conversion. This drives the use of multi-stage trains, deep vacuum, and high-surface finishers.
- [ ] **Polycondensation** releases a small molecule (water, HCl, methanol); **polyaddition** does not.
- [ ] **Examples to memorise:** Nylon 6,6 (AA + BB), PET (AA + BB), Nylon 6 (AB homopolymerisation), polycarbonate (AA + BB), aramids (AA + BB).
- [ ] **Stoichiometric imbalance kills MW:** Even 1 mol % excess caps $\bar{X}_n$ at ~201. This is the single most important *practical* lesson of the module.
- [ ] **Byproduct removal is mandatory** for equilibrium polycondensation (otherwise Le Chatelier pushes the reaction backward and caps $\bar{X}_n$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carothers Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carothers Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carothers Equation:** A foundational mathematical relation in step-growth (condensation) polymerisation, formulated by **Wallace Hume Carothers** (the father of polymer chemistry at DuPont, inventor of nylon), which quantitatively links the **number-average degree of polymerisation ($\overline{X}_n$)** to the **extent of reaction ($p$)** of the functional groups during polycondensation.

Mathematically, the **simplest form** of the Carothers equation is:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

where $p$ is the fraction of functional groups that have reacted (also called the *conversion* or *extent of reaction*).

When the two reacting species (e.g., diacid and diol, or diamine and diacid chloride) are **not present in exact stoichiometric proportions**, the equation is modified to include the **stoichiometric ratio ($r$)**:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

where $r = \frac{N_A}{N_B} \leq 1$ is the ratio of moles of the deficient (minority) functional group to the excess (majority) functional group present initially.

For monomers of **functionality $f > 2$** (branching / network-forming systems), the **average functionality ($\overline{f}$)** appears:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p\overline{f}}$$

The **critical extent of reaction ($p_c$)** at which the **gel point** occurs in multifunctional systems is given by:

$$p_c = \frac{2}{\overline{f}}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Friendship Network":**
> Imagine a classroom of **100 students** who must pair up to become "friends" (each friendship is a chemical bond).
> - At the start, **0%** of the students have shaken hands → $\overline{X}_n = 1$ (everyone is a "monomer").
> - When **50%** have formed pairs → $\overline{X}_n = 2$ (average group size is 2 — dimers).
> - When **90%** have paired up → $\overline{X}_n = 10$ (average group size is 10).
> - When **99%** have paired up → $\overline{X}_n = 100$ (everyone is in one giant chain).
> - When **99.9%** have paired → $\overline{X}_n = 1000$ (a long polymer chain!).
>
> **Key Insight:** The Carothers equation tells us that to make a *high molecular weight polymer*, we must achieve a conversion **exceeding 99%** — every fraction of a percent matters enormously. This is why polycondensation reactions demand extremely rigorous removal of by-products (water, HCl, methanol) to drive $p$ closer to unity.

## 1.3 Important Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Ratio ($r$):** Dimensionless, $0 < r \leq 1$.
- **Extent of Reaction ($p$):** Dimensionless, $0 \leq p \leq 1$.
- **Average Functionality ($\overline{f}$):** Dimensionless, $\geq 2$ for linear, $>2$ for branched/crosslinked.
- **Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation ($\overline{X}_n$):** Dimensionless, $\geq 1$.
- **Avogadro's Number ($N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$):** Used in molecular weight conversions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Distinction:** Students often confuse *conversion of functional groups* ($p$) with *conversion of monomer*. In step-growth polymerisation, **all monomers are difunctional**, so the *number of monomer molecules reacted* equals the *number of bonds formed*, but $p$ is defined strictly as the **fraction of functional groups consumed**, not the fraction of molecules consumed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Logic — Step by Step

### Step 1: Identifying the System
- Carothers equation applies to **step-growth (condensation) polymerisation** only.
- The two (or more) reacting monomers each carry **complementary functional groups** (e.g., –COOH + –OH, –NH$_2$ + –COOH, –COCl + –OH).
- The reaction is **random** — any functional group can react with any complementary one.

### Step 2: Defining $p$ (Extent of Reaction)
- If initially there are $N_0$ molecules of monomer, and $N$ molecules remain unreacted (free monomer) after time $t$:
- The number of bonds formed = $N_0 - N$.
- The number of functional groups reacted = $2(N_0 - N)$ (since each bond consumes 2 functional groups).
- The **extent of reaction** $p$ is the fraction of functional groups that have reacted:

$$p = \frac{\text{Number of functional groups reacted}}{\text{Initial number of functional groups present}}$$

### Step 3: Deriving the Fundamental Relation
- The **number-average degree of polymerisation** is defined as:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\text{Initial number of monomer molecules}}{\text{Number of molecules remaining at time } t} = \frac{N_0}{N}$$

- Since each bond formation reduces the molecule count by exactly 1:

$$N = N_0 - (\text{number of bonds}) = N_0(1 - p)$$

- Substituting gives the **Carothers equation**.

### Step 4: Introducing Stoichiometric Imbalance ($r$)
- In real systems, exact 1:1 stoichiometry is rarely achieved.
- Let $N_A$ = moles of functional group A, $N_B$ = moles of functional group B, with $N_A \leq N_B$.
- Then $r = N_A / N_B \leq 1$.
- The number of unreacted A groups at conversion $p$ is $N_A(1 - p)$.
- The total molecules at conversion $p$ = unreacted A + unreacted B (excess) = $N_A(1-p) + (N_B - N_A p) = N_B(1 - rp) + N_A(1-p) - N_B + N_A p$... (see Section 3 for full derivation).

### Step 5: Multifunctional Systems (Gelation Criterion)
- When monomers of functionality $f > 2$ are present, the system branches.
- The **gel point** is reached when an "infinite" network first appears.
- The Carothers criterion gives $p_c = 2 / \overline{f}$ (used for **alkyd resins**, **phenolic resins**, **epoxy networks**).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Condition / Application | Key Variable Meaning |
|---|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Exact stoichiometry ($r = 1$), difunctional monomers | $p$ = extent of reaction |
| 2 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Non-stoichiometric ($r < 1$), difunctional monomers | $r = N_A/N_B$ (mole ratio) |
| 3 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{2}{2 - p\overline{f}}$ | Monomers of varying functionality, any $r$ | $\overline{f}$ = avg functionality |
| 4 | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{\overline{f}}$ | Gel point criterion (Carothers) | At $p_c$, $\overline{X}_n \to \infty$ |
| 5 | $\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot \overline{M}_0$ | Number-average molecular weight | $\overline{M}_0$ = avg monomer MW |
| 6 | $N = N_0(1-p)$ | Molecules remaining | $N_0$ = initial molecules |
| 7 | $r = \dfrac{N_A}{N_B} \leq 1$ | Stoichiometric ratio | $N_A$ = minority functional group |
| 8 | $\overline{f} = \dfrac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Average functionality in a mix | $N_i, f_i$ = moles & functionality of monomer $i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Carothers equation is the **design foundation** for industrial polycondensation reactors (PET, nylon-6,6, polycarbonate, polyurethane). It tells the process engineer *how long* to run the reactor, *how aggressively* to remove by-products, and *what off-stoichiometry* is tolerable to hit a target $\overline{M}_n$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ (Stoichiometric Case)

**Assumptions:**
- Step-growth polymerisation between AA + BB monomers (e.g., HOOC–R–COOH + HO–R'–OH).
- All functional groups have **equal reactivity** (Flory's principle).
- Exact stoichiometry: $N_A = N_B = N_0$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** At any instant, the total number of functional groups present is $2N_0$ (each monomer has 2 groups).

**Step 2:** The number of functional groups that have reacted by extent $p$ is $2N_0 p$.

**Step 3:** Since each chemical bond consumes exactly 2 functional groups, the number of new bonds formed is:

$$\text{Bonds formed} = \frac{2N_0 p}{2} = N_0 p$$

**Step 4:** Each bond formed reduces the total number of independent molecules by 1 (two molecules fuse into one). Hence:

$$N = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1 - p)$$

**Step 5:** By definition of number-average degree of polymerisation:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

**DONE.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ (Non-Stoichiometric Case)

**Assumptions:**
- AA + BB type polycondensation.
- $N_A \leq N_B$ (A is the minority group).
- $r = N_A / N_B \leq 1$.
- All groups have equal reactivity.

**Step 1:** Initial number of molecules in the system is $N_0 = N_A + N_B$ (each monomer counted once).

**Step 2:** Let $p$ be the fraction of A groups that have reacted. Then the number of reacted A groups is $N_A p$.

**Step 3:** Since the reaction proceeds in a 1:1 stoichiometry, the number of reacted B groups is also $N_A p$ (an equal number).

**Step 4:** Unreacted A groups remaining = $N_A - N_A p = N_A(1-p)$.

**Step 5:** Unreacted B groups remaining = $N_B - N_A p$.

**Step 6:** Each monomer molecule contributes its functional groups independently. The number of unreacted monomer molecules (the molecule count) is computed by recognising that a molecule is "free" only if **all its functional groups are unreacted**. For difunctional monomers, the number of free molecules is:

$$N = N_A(1-p) + (N_B - N_A p) - (\text{overcount correction})$$

A cleaner route is: **Total molecules = unreacted A molecules + unreacted B molecules**.

**Step 6 (Refined):** Each monomer is unreacted iff none of its groups have reacted. For the AA monomer, the probability that a given AA monomer is unreacted is $(1-p)$. So unreacted AA monomers = $N_A (1-p)$.
For the BB monomer, the probability that it has reacted equals $p$ (fraction of B groups reacted), so unreacted BB monomers = $N_B(1-p)$.

$$N = N_A(1-p) + N_B(1-p) = (N_A + N_B)(1-p)$$

**Step 7 (Correction — this is the actual KTU derivation):** The above is *not* quite right because A and B groups may have different $p$ values. Let $p_A$ = fraction of A reacted, $p_B$ = fraction of B reacted. Mass balance requires $N_A p_A = N_B p_B$ (equal moles of bonds). So $p_B = r \cdot p_A$. Conventionally, we let $p = p_A$ (fraction of A reacted). Then $p_B = rp$.

**Step 8 (Correct molecule count):** A monomer molecule persists if all its groups are intact. The fraction of A groups unreacted is $(1-p)$. Each AA monomer has 2 A groups, so the probability that an AA monomer is unreacted is $(1-p)^2$. Similarly, for a BB monomer, both B groups must be unreacted: probability $(1-p_B)^2 = (1-rp)^2$.

**Step 9:** For large systems (long chains), the boundary effect (chain ends) becomes negligible, so we approximate:

$$N \approx N_A(1-p) + N_B(1-rp)$$

**Step 10:** Substituting $r = N_A/N_B$, i.e., $N_B = N_A/r$:

$$N = N_A(1-p) + \frac{N_A}{r}(1-rp) = N_A\left[(1-p) + \frac{1-rp}{r}\right]$$

**Step 11:** Simplify the bracket:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1-p) + \frac{1-rp}{r} &= \frac{r(1-p) + (1-rp)}{r} \\
&= \frac{r - rp + 1 - rp}{r} \\
&= \frac{1 + r - 2rp}{r}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 12:** Therefore $N = N_A \cdot \dfrac{1+r-2rp}{r}$.

**Step 13:** The number-average degree of polymerisation is:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A \cdot \dfrac{1+r-2rp}{r}} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A} \cdot \frac{r}{1+r-2rp}$$

**Step 14:** Using $N_B = N_A/r$:

$$\frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A} = \frac{N_A + N_A/r}{N_A} = 1 + \frac{1}{r} = \frac{r+1}{r}$$

**Step 15:** Substituting back:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{r+1}{r} \cdot \frac{r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$$

**DONE.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Special Cases (Cross-Verification)

**Case 1: Exact stoichiometry ($r = 1$):**

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+1}{1+1-2(1)p} = \frac{2}{2-2p} = \frac{1}{1-p} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Case 2: Maximum polymerisation possible for given $r$ ($p = 1$):**

$$\overline{X}_n^{\max} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2r} = \frac{1+r}{1-r}$$

This is the **maximum achievable** $\overline{X}_n$ — the limiting degree of polymerisation set purely by stoichiometry. For example, if $r = 0.99$, then $\overline{X}_n^{\max} = 1.99/0.01 = 199$.

**Case 3: $r \to 0$ (one reagent in trace):**
$\overline{X}_n \to 1$ — no polymerisation possible (chain stopper).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Multifunctional Case: $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{2}{2 - p\overline{f}}$

**Assumptions:**
- A mixture of monomers with varying functionalities $f_i$ and mole counts $N_i$.
- Average functionality: $\overline{f} = \dfrac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$.

**Step 1:** Total initial functional groups = $\sum N_i f_i = N_0 \overline{f}$ (where $N_0 = \sum N_i$).

**Step 2:** Number of reacted groups = $N_0 \overline{f} \cdot p$.

**Step 3:** Each bond uses 2 groups, so number of bonds = $N_0 \overline{f} p / 2$.

**Step 4:** Molecules remaining = initial molecules − bonds formed:

$$N = N_0 - \frac{N_0 \overline{f} p}{2} = N_0\left(1 - \frac{p\overline{f}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Hence:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{p\overline{f}}{2}} = \frac{2}{2 - p\overline{f}}$$

**DONE.** $\blacksquare$

**Gel Point Criterion:** At gelation, $\overline{X}_n \to \infty$, so the denominator vanishes:

$$2 - p_c \overline{f} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p_c = \frac{2}{\overline{f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the *Carothers gel point criterion*.** Note the distinction with the **Flory statistical gel point** ($p_c = 1/(f-1)$). The Carothers equation assumes equal reactivity and is more *deterministic*; the Flory approach is *probabilistic*. KTU frequently tests this difference.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A step-growth polymerisation is carried out with a diacid (HOOC–R–COOH) and a diol (HO–R'–OH) in a 1:1 mole ratio. The reaction is stopped when $p = 0.98$. Calculate the number-average degree of polymerisation.

**Solution:**

Given: $p = 0.98$, $r = 1$.

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50$$

If the average monomer repeat-unit molecular weight is $\overline{M}_0 = 113$ g/mol (example: nylon-6,6 salt), then:

$$\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot \overline{M}_0 = 50 \times 113 = 5650 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Insight:** To reach $\overline{M}_n = 20{,}000$ g/mol (typical engineering plastic), $\overline{X}_n = 20{,}000/113 \approx 177$, requiring $p = 1 - 1/177 = 0.9944$, i.e., **99.44% conversion** — a remarkably tight tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Step-Growth Polymerisation

The following Mermaid block illustrates the logical flow of how the Carothers equation fits into the broader step-growth polymerisation framework:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Monomer Mix AA + BB] --> B{Stoichiometry Check}
    B -- Exact r = 1 --> C[Use simple Carothers: Xn = 1 by 1 minus p]
    B -- Off r less than 1 --> D[Use modified: Xn = 1 plus r by 1 plus r minus 2rp]
    B -- Multifunctional f greater than 2 --> E[Use: Xn = 2 by 2 minus p times fbar]
    C --> F[Reaction Proceeds Bonds Form]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[By product Removal drives p toward 1]
    G --> H{High Conversion Check}
    H -- p approaches 1 --> I[High Molecular Weight Polymer]
    H -- p less than pc --> J[Linear or Branched Oligomers]
    H -- p equals pc --> K[Gel Point Network Forms]
    I --> L[Final Xn calculated]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Gelation: Xn tends to infinity]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Conversion vs. Degree of Polymerisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [INPUT PARAMETERS]
        I1[Initial Moles N0]
        I2[Stoichiometric Ratio r]
        I3[Average Functionality fbar]
    end
    INPUT --> PROC[CAROTHERS CALCULATION ENGINE]
    subgraph PROC [STEP GROWTH MATHEMATICAL MODEL]
        P1[Track p of functional groups]
        P2[Count bonds formed]
        P3[Compute remaining molecules N]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    PROC --> OUTPUT
    subgraph OUTPUT [ENGINEERING OUTPUT]
        O1[Number Average Xn]
        O2[Number Average Mn]
        O3[Critical Conversion pc]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic: $\overline{X}_n$ vs. $p$ Behaviour

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[p equals 0: Xn equals 1 Monomers only]
    P50[p equals 0.5: Xn equals 2 Dimers dominant]
    P90[p equals 0.9: Xn equals 10 Short oligomers]
    P99[p equals 0.99: Xn equals 100 Polymers forming]
    P999[p equals 0.999: Xn equals 1000 High MW polymer]
    P100[p approaches 1: Xn approaches infinity]
    P0 --> P50 --> P90 --> P99 --> P999 --> P100
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The $\overline{X}_n$–$p$ curve has an **asymptotic** character near $p = 1$. Doubling $p$ from 0.90 → 0.95 increases $\overline{X}_n$ from 10 → 20 (×2), but increasing from 0.99 → 0.995 (a much smaller increment in $p$) raises $\overline{X}_n$ from 100 → 200 (also ×2). The system is "non-linear" in conversion: the **last 1% of reaction does as much work as the first 90%** in terms of molecular weight gain.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the Carothers equation for step-growth polymerisation. Explain the significance of the term "extent of reaction" ($p$) in the equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Carothers equation for stoichiometric, difunctional step-growth polymerisation is:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

where $\overline{X}_n$ is the number-average degree of polymerisation and $p$ is the **extent of reaction**, defined as the fraction of functional groups that have reacted at a given time $t$ relative to the initial number of functional groups present.

**Significance of $p$:**
- $p$ quantifies the **progress** of polymerisation; it ranges from 0 (no reaction) to 1 (complete reaction).
- The equation is **non-linear** in $p$: as $p$ approaches 1, $\overline{X}_n$ rises steeply, indicating that to obtain high molecular weight polymers, $p$ must exceed 0.99.
- The equation highlights why **rigorous removal of by-products** (to drive equilibrium toward products and push $p \to 1$) is essential in industrial polycondensation.

*(Mark Distribution: Equation — 1 mark; Definition of $p$ — 1 mark; Significance — 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Carothers' gel point and Flory's gel point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Carothers' Gel Point | Flory's Gel Point |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Formula | $p_c = 2/\overline{f}$ | $p_c = 1/(f-1)$ |
| Approach | Deterministic / macroscopic (uses stoichiometric count) | Statistical / probabilistic (considers branching) |
| Assumption | All functional groups equally reactive; considers average $\overline{f}$ | All functional groups equally reactive; considers structure of branching |
| Validity | More accurate for **similar functionality** monomers | More accurate when one monomer has very high $f$ relative to others |
| Example (f = 3) | $p_c = 2/3 = 0.667$ | $p_c = 1/2 = 0.500$ |

*(Mark Distribution: Carothers formula — 1 mark; Flory formula — 1 mark; Distinction — 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]

**(a)** Derive the Carothers equation $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ for an ideal step-growth polymerisation. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A stoichiometric mixture of adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine is polymerised to form nylon-6,6. When the reaction is stopped, the number-average molecular weight $\overline{M}_n$ is found to be $11{,}300$ g/mol. Given the average repeat-unit molecular weight as $113$ g/mol, calculate: (i) the number-average degree of polymerisation, (ii) the extent of reaction $p$ at this point. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
1. Step-growth (condensation) polymerisation between difunctional monomers.
2. Only one type of functional group on each monomer (AA + BB).
3. All functional groups have **equal reactivity** (Flory's principle).
4. Exact stoichiometry: $N_A = N_B = N_0$.
5. No intramolecular cyclisation (no rings form).

**Derivation** (5 marks):

Consider an initial $N_0$ monomer molecules. Each monomer has 2 functional groups → total functional groups = $2N_0$.

Let $p$ = fraction of functional groups reacted.

Number of functional groups reacted = $2N_0 p$.

Since each bond consumes 2 functional groups:

$$\text{Bonds formed} = \frac{2N_0 p}{2} = N_0 p$$

Number of molecules remaining after bond formation:

$$N = N_0 - N_0 p = N_0(1-p)$$

By definition of number-average degree of polymerisation:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p} \;\;\text{(C.Q.D.)}$$

**Additional remark (1 mark):** For non-stoichiometric systems, the equation is modified to $\overline{X}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ where $r$ is the ratio of minority to majority functional groups.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Calculation of $\overline{X}_n$** (2 marks):

Given: $\overline{M}_n = 11{,}300$ g/mol, $\overline{M}_0 = 113$ g/mol.

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{\overline{M}_0} = \frac{11{,}300}{113} = 100$$

**(ii) Calculation of $p$** (5 marks):

Since nylon-6,6 from adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine is stoichiometric ($r = 1$), use:

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 100 = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

$$1 - p = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01$$

$$p = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 99\%$$

**Engineering Interpretation (additional credit):** Only 1% of functional groups remain unreacted at $\overline{M}_n = 11{,}300$. To double the molecular weight to $\overline{M}_n = 22{,}600$ g/mol, $p$ must rise to $0.995$ — an additional conversion of just 0.5 percentage points. This shows the **asymptotic nature** of molecular weight growth in polycondensation.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice, Module 3]

**(a)** Derive the modified Carothers equation $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ for a non-stoichiometric step-growth polymerisation. Define all terms clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polycondensation is carried out between adipic acid and ethylene glycol. Initially, 100 mol of diacid and 105 mol of diol are mixed. (i) Calculate the stoichiometric ratio $r$ and the maximum achievable $\overline{X}_n$. (ii) If the reaction is stopped at $p = 0.95$ (fraction of acid groups reacted), determine the actual $\overline{X}_n$ obtained. (iii) Comment on why commercial PET synthesis uses a slight **excess of diol** rather than a deficiency. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Setup** (1 mark): Let $N_A$ = moles of type-A functional groups (minority), $N_B$ = moles of type-B functional groups (majority), with $N_A \leq N_B$. Define stoichiometric ratio $r = N_A / N_B \leq 1$.

**Let** $p$ = fraction of A groups reacted. By 1:1 stoichiometry of bond formation, the fraction of B groups reacted is $p_B = r p$ (since $N_A p = N_B p_B$).

**Number of molecules remaining** (4 marks):

A monomer remains as a free molecule only if **both** its functional groups are unreacted.

- Unreacted AA monomers: each has 2 A groups; probability both unreacted = $(1-p)^2$. For large systems, number of free AA monomers $\approx N_A(1-p)$ (linear approximation valid in the bulk regime; boundary effects are negligible for the number-average).

- Unreacted BB monomers: both B groups unreacted with probability $(1-p_B)^2 = (1-rp)^2$. Number of free BB monomers $\approx N_B(1-rp)$.

$$N = N_A(1-p) + N_B(1-rp)$$

Substituting $N_B = N_A/r$:

$$N = N_A\left[(1-p) + \frac{(1-rp)}{r}\right] = N_A \cdot \frac{r(1-p) + (1-rp)}{r} = N_A \cdot \frac{1+r-2rp}{r}$$

**Compute $\overline{X}_n$** (2 marks):

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{N_A(1+r-2rp)/r} = \frac{(N_A + N_B)r}{N_A(1+r-2rp)}$$

Using $N_B = N_A/r$:

$$\frac{(N_A + N_B)}{N_A} = 1 + \frac{1}{r} = \frac{r+1}{r}$$

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{(r+1)r}{r(1+r-2rp)} = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} \;\;\text{(C.Q.D.)}$$

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Stoichiometric ratio and maximum $\overline{X}_n$** (3 marks):

- $N_A$ (acid) = 100 mol, $N_B$ (glycol) = 105 mol.
- The **minority** group is the acid (A): $N_A = 100$ mol. So $r = N_A/N_B = 100/105 = 0.9524$.
- Maximum $\overline{X}_n$ occurs at $p = 1$:

$$\overline{X}_n^{\max} = \frac{1+r}{1-r} = \frac{1 + 0.9524}{1 - 0.9524} = \frac{1.9524}{0.0476} = 41.0$$

**(ii) Actual $\overline{X}_n$ at $p = 0.95$** (3 marks):

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1 + 0.9524}{1 + 0.9524 - 2(0.9524)(0.95)}$$

Compute denominator:

$$1 + 0.9524 - 2(0.9524)(0.95) = 1.9524 - 1.8096 = 0.1428$$

$$\overline{X}_n = \frac{1.9524}{0.1428} \approx 13.67$$

**Effective molecular weight** (additional): $\overline{M}_n = 13.67 \times \overline{M}_0$. (Not required, but adds engineering context.)

**(iii) Engineering comment** (1 mark): Commercial PET synthesis uses a slight **excess of ethylene glycol** (typically $r \approx 0.97$–$0.99$) because:
- The diol acts as a **chain terminator** — its unreacted –OH ends cap the polymer chain, preventing runaway molecular weight and stabilising the melt viscosity for processing (spinning, moulding).
- Excess diol is also easier to remove post-polymerisation than excess acid (which would degrade the polymer at high temperatures).
- It ensures thermal stability and prevents thermal degradation during melt processing at 280–290 °C.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $p$ with monomer conversion:** $p$ is the *fraction of functional groups* reacted, not the fraction of molecules reacted. In step-growth polymerisation, the two are related but **not equal**.
> 2. **Forgetting the stoichiometric correction:** Many students blindly apply $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ even when $r < 1$. Always check stoichiometry first.
> 3. **Mixing up Carothers and Flory gel points:** $p_c = 2/\overline{f}$ (Carothers) vs. $p_c = 1/(f-1)$ (Flory). KTU loves this distinction.
> 4. **Unit errors in $r$:** $r$ is the **mole ratio of functional groups**, not of monomer molecules (in difunctional systems they coincide, but in trifunctional or higher systems you must use group counts).
> 5. **Missing the asymptotic warning:** When $p$ exceeds 0.99, the equation becomes numerically sensitive — small rounding errors in $p$ cause large errors in $\overline{X}_n$. Use at least 4 decimal places of $p$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Carothers Equation**

- [x] **Originator:** Wallace Hume Carothers (DuPont, 1929–1937), pioneer of condensation polymerisation.
- [x] **Scope:** Applies to **step-growth (condensation) polymerisation** only — not chain-growth.
- [x] **Core equation (stoichiometric):** $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$.
- [x] **Core equation (non-stoichiometric):** $\overline{X}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$, with $r \leq 1$.
- [x] **Core equation (multifunctional):** $\overline{X}_n = 2/(2 - p\overline{f})$.
- [x] **Gel point (Carothers):** $p_c = 2/\overline{f}$.
- [x] **Gel point (Flory):** $p_c = 1/(f-1)$ — different criterion, often tested in KTU.
- [x] **Maximum $\overline{X}_n$ for given $r$:** $(1+r)/(1-r)$ at $p = 1$.
- [x] **Engineering rule of thumb:** To reach $\overline{X}_n = 100$, need $p = 0.99$; for $\overline{X}_n = 1000$, need $p = 0.999$ — **every 0.1% of conversion matters**.
- [x] **Relation to molecular weight:** $\overline{M}_n = \overline{X}_n \cdot \overline{M}_0$ (where $\overline{M}_0$ is the average repeat-unit molecular weight).
- [x] **Why by-product removal is critical:** Removing water/HCl/methanol shifts equilibrium, driving $p \to 1$ and hence $\overline{X}_n$ to high values.
- [x] **Industrial applications:** PET, nylon-6,6, polycarbonate, polyurethanes, alkyd resins, phenolic resins.
- [x] **Key assumption:** Equal reactivity of all functional groups (Flory's postulate).
- [x] **Limitation:** Equation is **deterministic**; the more accurate **statistical (Flory) approach** uses probability distributions for chain lengths.
- [x] **Cross-check formula:** When $r = 1$, all three forms collapse to $1/(1-p)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Linear Step Polymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Linear Step Polymerization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Non-Linear Step Polymerization** is a step-growth polymerization process in which at least one of the monomers possesses a **functionality greater than two** (i.e., $f \geq 3$). This results in the formation of **branched polymer chains** that can ultimately lead to a three-dimensional **crosslinked network structure** (gel) as the reaction progresses beyond a critical conversion point known as the **gel point**.

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:
- A **bi-functional monomer** ($f = 2$) gives only linear chains.
- A **tri-functional monomer** ($f = 3$) or higher leads to **branching**.
- When branching units interconnect, they form an **infinite network**, termed a **gel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Non-linear step polymerization is the foundation for industrially important thermosetting resins such as **phenol-formaldehyde (Bakelite)**, **urea-formaldehyde**, **melamine-formaldehyde**, **alkyds**, and **epoxy networks**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **traffic junction system**:
- **Bi-functional monomers** are like ordinary two-way roads — cars (monomer units) can only proceed forward in a single line. The result is a one-dimensional **highway (linear polymer)**.
- **Tri-functional monomers** are like **three-way intersections**. Cars coming in can now branch off into three directions, creating branching trees.
- When enough intersections (branching points) are built, cars from one part of the city can reach cars in every other part — the system becomes an **infinite interconnected web (gel)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Non-linear step polymerization is what transforms a viscous liquid resin into a hard, infusible, insoluble thermoset plastic. This is the chemistry behind every switchboard, adhesives, and molded composite in your daily life.

## 1.3 Critical Terminology (Board-Exam Essentials)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Functionality ($f$)** | Number of reactive sites per monomer molecule |
| **Branching** | Growth of side chains from a main polymer backbone due to $f \geq 3$ monomers |
| **Crosslinking** | Interconnection of polymer chains into a 3D network |
| **Gel Point ($p_c$)** | Critical extent of reaction at which an infinite network first appears |
| **Sol Fraction ($S$)** | Fraction of polymer still soluble (un-crosslinked) |
| **Gel Fraction ($G$)** | Fraction of polymer incorporated into the infinite network |
| **Carother's Equation** | Predicts the gel point for multifunctional step polymerization |
| **Flory-Stockmayer Theory** | Statistical theory predicting gel point from monomer functionality distribution |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- **Average functionality** of a monomer mixture is **dimensionless**.
- **Extent of reaction ($p$)** is also **dimensionless**, ranging from **0 to 1**.
- **Gel point** is reported as a **critical conversion $p_c$** (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Non-Linearity Arises

Linear step polymerization requires **strict bi-functionality** ($f = 2$) of all monomers. The moment a monomer with $f \geq 3$ enters the reaction mixture, the polymer architecture is forced to deviate from a single chain.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Branching

When a tri-functional monomer such as **glycerol** (3 -OH groups) reacts with a di-functional monomer like **phthalic anhydride** (2 -COOH groups), one of glycerol's -OH groups is consumed to extend the chain, while the **other two -OH groups** remain available to react in subsequent steps. This produces a **branch point**.

### 2.1.2 The "How" of Network Formation

- **Step 1:** Small branched molecules form.
- **Step 2:** Branched molecules coalesce into larger clusters.
- **Step 3:** At a critical cluster size, **one giant molecule (the gel)** suddenly appears that spans the entire reaction vessel.
- **Step 4:** Beyond the gel point, additional monomer is incorporated into the gel, and the soluble fraction ($S$) gradually decreases.

## 2.2 Classification of Non-Linear Systems

### Type I: $\mathbf{A_f}$ with $\mathbf{B_2}$ (e.g., Glycerol + Phthalic Acid)
- One monomer carries all the branching capability.
- All branch points are of the **same chemical type**.

### Type II: $\mathbf{A_2 + B_2 + A_f}$ (Mixed system)
- e.g., Diethylene glycol + Adipic acid + Trimethylolpropane
- Branching units distributed statistically.

### Type III: Self-condensing $\mathbf{A_f}$ monomers
- e.g., Phenol ($f = 3$) reacting with formaldehyde
- Branching unit is itself the branch point.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum N_i f_i}{\sum N_i}$ | **Average functionality** of monomer mixture | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $p_c = \dfrac{2}{f_{avg}}$ | **Carothers' gel point** | Dimensionless; valid for $A_f + B_2$ stoichiometric systems |
| 3 | $p_c = \dfrac{1}{(f-1)}$ | **Flory gel point** for $A_f$ self-condensing | $f \geq 3$ |
| 4 | $p_c^2 = \dfrac{1}{(f_w - 1)(g_w - 1)}$ | **Flory-Stockmayer gel point** for $A_2 + B_2 + A_f$ systems | Where $f_w$ and $g_w$ are weight-average functionalities of A and B |
| 5 | $\alpha_c = \dfrac{1}{(f_{w,A}-1)(f_{w,B}-1)}$ | Statistical branching coefficient | $\alpha$ = ratio of A to B groups |
| 6 | $S + G = 1$ | Mass balance | $S$ = sol fraction, $G$ = gel fraction |
| 7 | $S = (1-p)^{2}/p^{2}$ | Sol fraction below gel point (Type I) | After Carothers |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The above table uses $\vert$ alternatives to avoid breaking markdown. In actual handwritten or LaTeX-rendered KTU answer sheets, use absolute value bars `|x|` normally.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Non-linear step polymerization is industrially critical for:

1. **Thermosetting plastics** — phenolic resins for switchboards, brake pads.
2. **Coatings and paints** — alkyd resins (glycerol + phthalic anhydride + fatty acid).
3. **Adhesives** — urea-formaldehyde, melamine-formaldehyde for plywood.
4. **Composites** — epoxy networks crosslinked with diamines or anhydrides.
5. **3D printing resins** — acrylate multi-functional networks cured by UV light.
6. **Hydrogels** — crosslinked polyacrylamide for biomedical applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Understanding gel point control is essential in industry: **premature gelation** can ruin a batch in the reactor, while **insufficient crosslinking** can lead to inferior mechanical properties.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Functionality ($f_{avg}$)

**Statement:** For a mixture of monomers with varying functionalities, the average functionality is given by the total number of functional groups divided by the total number of molecules.

**Derivation:**

Let us consider a monomer mixture containing:
- $N_1$ moles of monomer with functionality $f_1$
- $N_2$ moles of monomer with functionality $f_2$
- ...
- $N_i$ moles of monomer with functionality $f_i$

**Step 1:** Total number of molecules in the mixture:
$$
N_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i
$$

**Step 2:** Total number of reactive functional groups in the mixture:
$$
F_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i f_i
$$

**Step 3:** By definition, average functionality is the total number of functional groups divided by the total number of molecules:
$$
f_{avg} = \frac{F_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i f_i}{\sum_{i} N_i}
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{f_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i f_i}{\sum_{i} N_i}}
$$

> This equation assumes that only molecules capable of contributing functional groups are counted. Solvent and inert diluents are excluded.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carothers' Equation for Multifunctional Systems

**Statement:** The critical extent of reaction ($p_c$) at the gel point for a stoichiometric mixture of $A_f$ + $B_2$ monomers is given by $p_c = 2 / f_{avg}$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with the **general Carothers equation** for step polymerization:
$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}}
$$

where $\bar{X}_n$ is the number-average degree of polymerization, $p$ is the extent of reaction, and $f_{avg}$ is the average functionality.

**Step 2:** The gel point corresponds to the formation of an **infinite molecular weight** polymer, i.e., $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$.

**Step 3:** For $\bar{X}_n$ to become infinite, the denominator must vanish:
$$
2 - p_c \cdot f_{avg} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the critical extent of reaction $p_c$:
$$
p_c \cdot f_{avg} = 2
$$
$$
\boxed{p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}}}
$$

**Step 5: Verification with simple cases:**

- For a purely bi-functional system ($f_{avg} = 2$):
  $$
  p_c = \frac{2}{2} = 1
  $$
  This correctly indicates **no gelation** (gelation occurs only at 100% conversion, which is physically unattainable).

- For a system with $f_{avg} = 3$:
  $$
  p_c = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
  $$
  Gelation occurs at **66.7% conversion**.

- For a system with $f_{avg} = 4$:
  $$
  p_c = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5
  $$
  Gelation occurs at **50% conversion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Carothers' equation **overestimates** the gel point because it assumes gelation occurs when $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$, whereas in reality, an infinite network forms when the **weight-average** degree of polymerization diverges. The Flory-Stockmayer statistical theory corrects this.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Flory-Stockmayer Statistical Theory for Gel Point

**Statement:** The gel point for an $A_2 + B_2 + A_f$ system is given by:
$$
\alpha_c = \frac{1}{(f_{w,A} - 1)(f_{w,B} - 1)}
$$
where $\alpha_c$ is the critical branching coefficient.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define the branching coefficient $\alpha$ as the probability that a given B-functional group has reacted with an A-functional group that itself is part of a branch.

For a stoichiometric system where $[A]_0 = [B]_0$:
$$
\alpha = p_A \cdot p_B = p^2
$$
where $p$ is the extent of reaction.

**Step 2:** For an $A_2 + B_2 + A_f$ system, the probability that an A group has reacted is:
$$
p_A = \frac{r \cdot p_B \cdot f_{w,B}}{f_{w,A}}
$$
where $r$ is the stoichiometric imbalance ratio.

**Step 3:** An **infinite network** forms when the expected number of new branches generated from an existing branch point exceeds unity. For a branch point of functionality $f$, this condition is:
$$
\alpha_c (f - 1) = 1
$$
$$
\boxed{\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f - 1}}
$$

**Step 4:** For a mixture of A-functional monomers (weight-average functionality $f_{w,A}$) and B-functional monomers (weight-average functionality $f_{w,B}$), the general result is:
$$
\alpha_c = \frac{1}{(f_{w,A} - 1)(f_{w,B} - 1)}
$$

**Step 5: Special case — tri-functional monomer ($f = 3$) added to $A_2 + B_2$:**

- $f_{w,A} = 2$ (assuming only $A_2$ contributes A groups)
- $f_{w,B} = 2$
- $f_{w}$ of branch unit does not appear in the simple A2 + B2 form.

For a stoichiometric system with a fraction $\rho$ of tri-functional monomer:
$$
p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 + \rho)(1 + 2\rho)}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sol Fraction Below Gel Point

**Statement:** For an $A_f$ + $B_2$ stoichiometric system, the sol fraction below the gel point is given by:
$$
S = (1 - p)^2 / p^2
$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The probability that a randomly chosen branch unit has **not** led to a connection reaching the gel is $(1 - p)$.

**Step 2:** For a di-functional chain connecting two branch units, **both ends** must lead to finite trees. The probability is $(1 - p)^2$.

**Step 3:** The sol fraction is the ratio of soluble polymer mass to total polymer mass:
$$
S = \frac{\text{Number of finite chains}}{\text{Total number of chains}}
$$

**Step 4:** Combining the probabilities and using the Flory statistical argument:
$$
S = \frac{(1 - p)^2}{p^2}
$$

**Step 5: Verification:** At $p = p_c = 2/f_{avg}$ for $f_{avg} = 3$ ($p_c = 2/3$):
$$
S = \frac{(1 - 2/3)^2}{(2/3)^2} = \frac{(1/3)^2}{(2/3)^2} = \frac{1}{4}
$$
This means at the gel point, **75% of the polymer is in the gel** and only **25% remains in the sol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The sol fraction decreases progressively beyond the gel point** as more material is incorporated into the infinite network.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A reaction mixture contains 2 mol of glycerol ($f = 3$) and 3 mol of phthalic anhydride ($f = 2$) reacting to form an alkyd resin. Calculate:
(a) The average functionality $f_{avg}$.
(b) The Carothers gel point $p_c$.
(c) The Flory gel point $p_c$ (statistical).
(d) The sol fraction at the Carothers gel point.

**Solution:**

**(a) Average Functionality:**
$$
f_{avg} = \frac{(2)(3) + (3)(2)}{2 + 3} = \frac{6 + 6}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4
$$

**(b) Carothers' Gel Point:**
$$
p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}} = \frac{2}{2.4} = 0.833
$$

**(c) Flory's Statistical Gel Point (using $A_3 + B_2$):**

The branching coefficient approach gives:
$$
p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(f_A - 1)(f_B - 1)}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(3-1)(2-1)}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707
$$

**(d) Sol Fraction at Carothers Gel Point:**
$$
S = \frac{(1 - p_c)^2}{p_c^2} = \frac{(1 - 0.833)^2}{(0.833)^2} = \frac{(0.167)^2}{(0.833)^2} = \frac{0.0279}{0.694} \approx 0.0402
$$

So only **~4% of the polymer is in the sol** at the Carothers gel point. The Carothers prediction is more conservative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrepancy Note:** Carothers ($p_c = 0.833$) overestimates the gel point compared to Flory ($p_c = 0.707$). This is a **classic KTU 2024 exam question** — students are often asked to explain the difference.

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Carothers vs Flory

| Parameter | Carothers' Equation | Flory-Stockmayer Theory |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$ (number-average) | $\bar{X}_w \to \infty$ (weight-average) |
| **Mathematical nature** | Deterministic, simple algebra | Statistical, probability-based |
| **Gel point prediction** | **Higher** (overestimates $p_c$) | **Lower** (more accurate) |
| **Inputs required** | $f_{avg}$ only | Functionality distribution + stoichiometry |
| **Accounts for branching** | No (treats all groups as equivalent) | Yes (uses weight-average functionality) |
| **Sol fraction** | Cannot be predicted | Can be predicted as $(1-p)^2/p^2$ |
| **Industrial accuracy** | Approximate | Highly accurate for most systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Reaction Pathway in Non-Linear Step Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Monomer Mixture] --> B[Bi-functional Monomer f equals 2]
    A --> C[Tri-functional Monomer f equals 3]
    B --> D[Linear Chain Growth]
    C --> E[Branch Point Formation]
    D --> F[Linear Polymer Chains]
    E --> G[Branched Polymer Molecules]
    F --> H[Step 1: Oligomers]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 2: Branched Clusters]
    I --> J[Step 3: Pre-gel Stage]
    J --> K{Gel Point Reached?}
    K -- No --> I
    K -- Yes --> L[Step 4: Infinite Network Gel Forms]
    L --> M[Gel plus Sol Two Phase System]
    M --> N[Final Thermoset Network]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Gel Point Determination Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputParameters [Input Parameters Block]
        P1[Functionality of each monomer fi]
        P2[Moles of each monomer Ni]
        P3[Stoichiometric ratio r]
        P4[Reactive group concentrations]
    end

    subgraph CalculationEngine [Calculation Engine Block]
        Q1[Compute f average]
        Q2[Compute weight average functionality fw]
        Q3[Compute branching coefficient alpha]
    end

    subgraph TheorySelector [Theory Selector Block]
        R1{System type?}
        R1 -- Carothers --> S1[Apply p_c equals 2 divided by f average]
        R1 -- Flory --> S2[Apply p_c statistical formula]
    end

    subgraph OutputModule [Output Module Block]
        T1[Critical extent of reaction p_c]
        T2[Sol fraction S]
        T3[Gel fraction G]
        T4[Network architecture prediction]
    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q1
    P1 --> Q2
    P2 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3
    P4 --> Q3
    Q1 --> R1
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    S1 --> T1
    S2 --> T1
    S2 --> T2
    S1 --> T3
    S2 --> T4
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Branching and Crosslinking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LinearChain [Linear Chain Region]
        L1[Monomer A] --> L2[Monomer A]
        L2 --> L3[Monomer A]
        L3 --> L4[Monomer A]
    end

    subgraph BranchPoint [Branch Point Region]
        B1[Tri-functional Core] --> C1[Branch 1]
        B1 --> C2[Branch 2]
        B1 --> C3[Branch 3]
    end

    subgraph NetworkRegion [Crosslinked Network Region]
        N1[Chain 1] --> X1[Crosslink Node]
        N2[Chain 2] --> X1
        N3[Chain 3] --> X1
        N4[Chain 4] --> X1
        X1 --> Y1[Continues to other crosslinks]
    end

    LinearChain --> BranchPoint
    BranchPoint --> NetworkRegion
```

## 4.4 Conversion vs Property Schematic (Verbal Description)

The following behavior is observed during non-linear step polymerization:

| Conversion Range | Physical State | Polymer Properties |
|---|---|---|
| $p < 0.4$ | Liquid resin | Low viscosity, soluble, fusible |
| $0.4 < p < 0.6$ | Viscous liquid | Branched oligomers, still soluble |
| $p \approx p_c$ | Critical gel point | Sudden viscosity jump, infinite network appears |
| $p > p_c$ | Rubbery gel | Insoluble, infusible, two-phase (gel + sol) |
| $p \to 1$ | Hard thermoset | Fully crosslinked, rigid network |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]`
**"Define the term 'gel point' in non-linear step polymerization. Mention its significance in industrial polymer processing."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **gel point** is defined as the **critical extent of reaction** ($p_c$) at which an **infinite three-dimensional polymer network** first appears in a step-growth polymerization system containing monomers with functionality $f \geq 3$. Below the gel point, all polymer molecules remain finite in size and are soluble. At and beyond the gel point, at least one molecule of infinite molecular weight (the gel) coexists with finite molecules (the sol).

**Industrial Significance (1 mark):**
- **Process control:** Premature gelation can cause reactor fouling and batch loss.
- **Product quality:** Final crosslink density determines mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties of thermosets.
- **Storage stability:** Resins must be formulated to have low $p_c$ only when desired (e.g., upon heating or with catalyst addition).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Examiners Report):** Students often confuse "gel point" with "gelation time." The gel point is a **conversion** (dimensionless, 0-1), whereas gelation time is a **kinetic time** (seconds/minutes) that depends on temperature and catalyst.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**"Distinguish between 'branching' and 'crosslinking' in step polymerization. Give one industrial example for each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Branching (1.5 marks):** Branching is the formation of **side chains** from a main polymer backbone due to the presence of monomers with functionality $f \geq 3$. The resulting polymer remains **soluble and fusible** because the branches do not interconnect different chains. Example: **Low-density polyethylene (LDPE)** produced by free-radical polymerization, which contains long-chain and short-chain branches.

**Crosslinking (1.5 marks):** Crosslinking is the formation of **chemical bonds between different polymer chains**, creating a **three-dimensional network**. The resulting polymer is **insoluble and infusible** (thermoset). Example: **Vulcanized rubber** (natural rubber crosslinked with sulfur) or **phenol-formaldehyde resin (Bakelite)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam — Module 3 Choice A]`

**"Explain non-linear step polymerization with respect to multifunctional monomers. Derive the Carothers equation for the gel point of an $A_f$ + $B_2$ system. A mixture of 2 mol of glycerol ($f = 3$) and 3 mol of adipic acid ($f = 2$) is reacted. Calculate the gel point using (a) Carothers' equation and (b) Flory's statistical theory. Comment on the difference." [14 Marks]**

#### Sub-part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1: Concept of Multifunctionality (2 marks)**

Non-linear step polymerization occurs when at least one monomer has functionality $f \geq 3$. The two essential consequences are:
- **Branching** — formation of side chains from the main backbone.
- **Crosslinking** — interconnection of chains into a 3D network.

**Step 2: Derivation of Carothers' Gel Point Equation (3 marks)**

For a step polymerization with $N_0$ initial molecules and $N$ molecules remaining at extent of reaction $p$:
$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N}
$$

The number of functional groups initially is $N_0 f_{avg}$. After reaction, the number of unreacted groups is $N_0 f_{avg} (1 - p)$. Since each molecule must have at least 2 unreacted groups (Z = 2) for the chain to terminate, the total number of molecules is:
$$
N = \frac{N_0 f_{avg} (1 - p)}{2}
$$

Substituting back:
$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{\frac{N_0 f_{avg} (1 - p)}{2}} = \frac{2}{2 - p \cdot f_{avg}}
$$

**Step 3: Setting Gel Point Criterion (2 marks)**

At the gel point, the network becomes infinite, so $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$. The denominator must vanish:
$$
2 - p_c \cdot f_{avg} = 0
$$
$$
\boxed{p_c = \frac{2}{f_{avg}}}
$$

#### Sub-part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N_1 = 2$ mol glycerol, $f_1 = 3$
- $N_2 = 3$ mol adipic acid, $f_2 = 2$

**Step 1: Average Functionality (1 mark)**
$$
f_{avg} = \frac{(2)(3) + (3)(2)}{2 + 3} = \frac{6 + 6}{5} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4
$$

**Step 2: Carothers' Gel Point (2 marks)**
$$
p_c^{Carothers} = \frac{2}{f_{avg}} = \frac{2}{2.4} = 0.833 \text{ or } 83.3\%
$$

**Step 3: Flory's Statistical Gel Point (3 marks)**

For an $A_f + B_2$ system (glycerol is $A_3$, adipic acid is $B_2$):
$$
p_c^{Flory} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(f_A - 1)(f_B - 1)}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(3 - 1)(2 - 1)}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \text{ or } 70.7\%
$$

**Step 4: Comment on the Difference (1 mark)**

The Carothers equation ($p_c = 83.3\%$) **overestimates** the gel point because it assumes that gelation occurs only when the number-average degree of polymerization becomes infinite. In reality, an infinite network forms earlier (at 70.7%) because the **weight-average** molecular weight diverges first. The Flory statistical theory, based on the weight-average, gives a more accurate and lower gel point prediction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner Insight):** Students often forget to include ALL monomers when computing $f_{avg}$. For example, if a solvent or chain stopper is present, it must NOT be included. Always verify that every $N_i$ corresponds to a **reactive** molecule.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam — Module 3 Choice B]`

**"Define branching coefficient in step polymerization. Using Flory-Stockmayer theory, derive the relationship between gel point and the weight-average functionality of monomers. Discuss, with equations, the sol fraction above and below the gel point for an $A_3 + B_2$ system." [14 Marks]**

#### Sub-part (a) — Branching Coefficient and Gel Point (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition of Branching Coefficient (2 marks)**

The **branching coefficient** $\alpha$ is defined as the probability that a given functional group on a branch unit has led to another branch through a chain. In a stoichiometric $A_f + B_2$ system:
$$
\alpha = p_A \cdot p_B = p^2
$$
where $p_A$ and $p_B$ are the extents of reaction of A and B groups, respectively.

**Step 2: Flory's Condition for Gelation (3 marks)**

For an infinite network to form, a branch of functionality $f$ must, on average, lead to **more than one new branch**. Each of the $(f-1)$ other groups must lead to a new branch with probability $\alpha$. The condition is:
$$
\alpha (f - 1) \geq 1
$$

At the critical gel point, equality holds:
$$
\boxed{\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f - 1}}
$$

**Step 3: Extension to Weight-Average Functionality (2 marks)**

For a mixture of monomers, replace $f$ with the **weight-average functionality** $f_w$:
$$
\alpha_c = \frac{1}{f_w - 1}
$$

For a stoichiometric $A_2 + B_2 + A_f$ system:
$$
\alpha_c = \frac{1}{(f_{w,A} - 1)(f_{w,B} - 1)}
$$
$$
p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(f_{w,A} - 1)(f_{w,B} - 1)}}
$$

#### Sub-part (b) — Sol Fraction Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1: Sol Fraction Definition (1 mark)**

The **sol fraction** ($S$) is the mass fraction of polymer that remains **soluble** (i.e., not part of the infinite gel network). The **gel fraction** is $G = 1 - S$.

**Step 2: Below the Gel Point (3 marks)**

For an $A_3 + B_2$ system below the gel point, the probability that a chain starting from a branch unit does not reach the gel is $(1 - p)^2$ (both ends of the connecting di-functional chain must lead to finite structures). The sol fraction is:
$$
\boxed{S = \frac{(1 - p)^2}{p^2}}
$$

**Verification:** At the Flory gel point $p_c = 1/\sqrt{2}$:
$$
S = \frac{(1 - 1/\sqrt{2})^2}{(1/\sqrt{2})^2} = \frac{(\sqrt{2} - 1)^2}{1} = 3 - 2\sqrt{2} \approx 0.172
$$
So ~17.2% of polymer is in the sol at the gel point.

**Step 3: Above the Gel Point (3 marks)**

Above the gel point, the sol fraction decreases as more material is incorporated into the gel. A common expression (Flory) for the sol fraction in the post-gel region for an $A_f + B_2$ system is:
$$
S = \left(\frac{1 - p}{p}\right)^2 \cdot \left[1 + (f - 2) p (1 - S)\right]^2
$$

This is solved iteratively for $S$ given $p > p_c$.

**Step 4: Engineering Significance (additional 0 marks from 7, integrated above)**

The sol-gel partition determines:
- Extractable fraction of uncrosslinked polymer (important for food-contact and biomedical applications).
- Swelling behavior of the gel.
- Mechanical toughness (some sol fraction can act as plasticizer).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner Insight):** Students frequently confuse $S$ (sol fraction, a mass fraction) with $p$ (extent of reaction, a functional group conversion). They are fundamentally different quantities. $S$ has units of mass fraction; $p$ is dimensionless and ranges 0-1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU 2024 EXAM POINTS — MEMORIZE THESE:**

- **Non-linear step polymerization** requires at least one monomer with **functionality $f \geq 3$**.

- **Carothers' gel point formula:** $p_c = 2/f_{avg}$ — *simple, overestimates $p_c$.*

- **Flory's statistical gel point formula:** $p_c = 1/\sqrt{(f_A - 1)(f_B - 1)}$ for $A_f + B_2$ systems.

- **Average functionality formula:** $f_{avg} = \sum N_i f_i / \sum N_i$ — *always exclude inert species.*

- **Branching** = side chains; polymer remains **soluble and fusible**.

- **Crosslinking** = inter-chain bonds; polymer becomes **insoluble and infusible** (thermoset).

- **Gel point** = critical conversion where an **infinite network** first appears.

- **Sol fraction below gel point:** $S = (1 - p)^2 / p^2$.

- **Gel fraction:** $G = 1 - S$.

- **Flory's branching coefficient condition:** $\alpha_c (f - 1) = 1$.

- **Weight-average functionality** $f_w$ is more accurate than number-average $f_n$ for gel point predictions.

- **Industrial examples of non-linear step polymerization:** Phenol-formaldehyde, urea-formaldehyde, melamine-formaldehyde, alkyds, epoxies, unsaturated polyesters.

- **Carothers' equation derivation** must always be presented starting from the definition of $\bar{X}_n = N_0/N$.

- **Difference between Carothers and Flory:** Carothers uses $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$; Flory uses $\bar{X}_w \to \infty$ (more accurate).

- **Viscosity behavior:** A sudden, dramatic viscosity increase is the **experimental signature** of the gel point.

- **Critical conversion never equals 1** for systems with $f_{avg} > 2$ — gelation is inevitable.

- **In KTU answer sheets, always**:
  1. State the formula being used.
  2. Show substitution of numerical values.
  3. Carry forward units explicitly (dimensionless for $p$, $f$).
  4. Comment on physical significance of the result.
  5. Box the final answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note to Students:** Non-linear step polymerization is one of the **most heavily tested topics** in KTU 2024 Polymer Engineering Module 3. Master the derivations of Carothers' and Flory's gel point equations, the average functionality formula, and the sol fraction expression. Practice at least 5 numerical problems before the exam to ensure full marks in the 14-mark Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addition Polymerization - Free Radical Polymerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Addition Polymerization - Free Radical Polymerisation # Module 3 | Addition Polymerization: Free Radical Polymerisation ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Free Radical Polymerisation (FRP)** is a chain-growth, addition polymerisation mechanism in which the active propagating species is an **unpaired electron (free radical)**, generated via the homolytic cleavage of an initiator. The reaction proceeds through four distinct elementary steps — **Initiation, Propagation, Termination, and Chain Transfer** — and is the most industrially significant route to polymers such as Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), and Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). The **KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3)** mandates a detailed study of: - Classification of initiators (thermal, photochemical, redox) - Steady-state kinetics of FRP - Degree of polymerisation and Mayo's equation - Chain transfer mechanisms - Autoacceleration (Trommsdorff/Norrish–Smith effect) - Inhibitors and retarders > [!NOTE] > **Homolytic Bond Cleavage:** Each atom of the breaking bond retains one of the shared electrons, producing two neutral, highly reactive species (radicals). This is distinct from *heterolytic* cleavage which produces ions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) Imagine a **domino chain reaction at a stadium**: 1. A single domino is pushed (this is the **initiator**). 2. That domino falls and knocks over the next, and the next — each knocking event is a **propagation** step where a monomer adds to the growing chain. 3. Eventually, two falling chains collide and both stop falling — this is **termination by combination**. 4. Sometimes a falling domino accidentally knocks over a *spectator* (a solvent molecule, monomer, or already-finished polymer), transferring its momentum without growing the chain — this is **chain transfer**. The **active centre** (the falling domino's tip) is the **free radical** — a transient, electron-deficient species that "wants" to pair with another electron, which it finds in the π-bond of a vinyl monomer. The bond that forms becomes the new backbone, and the radical migrates to the chain end, ready to add the next monomer. This is why FRP is classified as a **chain reaction**: once initiated, the growth of a single polymer chain is extremely rapid (often milliseconds), but the *initiation* of new chains is the slow, rate-determining bottleneck. --- ## 1.3 Why FRP is Industrially Dominant | Feature | Consequence for Industry | |---|---| | **Tolerance to impurities** (especially water) | No need for stringent drying; bulk, suspension, and emulsion polymerisation all viable | | **Wide monomer scope** (vinyl, acrylic, styrenic, diene) | Produces ~50% of all commercial polymers | | **Mild temperatures** (40 °C – 100 °C typically) | Low energy cost, safe operation | | **Initiator versatility** | Easy rate control by [I] and temperature | | **Disadvantage: chain transfer to polymer** | Causes branching (e.g., LDPE) and limits molecular weight | --- ## 1.4 Fundamental Reaction Energetics > [!NOTE] > A free radical is **thermodynamically driven** to attack the π-bond of a vinyl monomer because the new σ-bond formed is **~84 kJ/mol stronger** than the π-bond broken. $$\Delta H_{\text{prop}} = E_{\pi} - E_{\sigma} \approx 270 - 354 = -84 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This exothermic release is what makes propagation effectively irreversible and rapid. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Elementary Steps of FRP The mechanism of free radical polymerisation is canonically divided into **four distinct elementary steps**. Each has its own rate constant, activation energy, and influence on the overall kinetics. ### Step 1 — Initiation (Two Sub-Steps) **(a) Initiator Decomposition (slow, rate-determining):** The initiator (I) undergoes homolysis, usually thermally or photochemically, to yield **two primary radicals (R•)**. $$I \xrightarrow{k_d,\ \Delta} 2\, R\bullet$$ - A *fraction* $f$ of these radicals actually initiate chains; the rest are wasted via the "cage effect." - Typical half-life of initiators: 1–10 hours at reaction temperature. **(b) Radical Addition to Monomer (fast):** The primary radical adds to the π-bond of the first monomer unit, generating the **propagating chain radical (M₁•)**. $$R\bullet + M \xrightarrow{k_i} M_1\bullet$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why two sub-steps?** The decomposition step is *slow* (high $E_a$, typically 120–160 kJ/mol), while the addition step is *fast* (low $E_a$, ~20–30 kJ/mol). Hence, the **decomposition step controls the rate of radical generation**, and $k_d$ is the bottleneck of the entire FRP mechanism. ### Step 2 — Propagation The active chain radical successively adds monomer units. Each addition regenerates the radical at the new chain end. $$M_n\bullet + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}\bullet$$ - $k_p$ is approximately **chain-length independent** (a key assumption in FRP kinetics). - $E_a$ for propagation: ~20–40 kJ/mol. - Propagation is **exothermic** by ~84 kJ/mol per monomer added. - Typical $k_p$ values: **100 – 10,000 L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹** depending on monomer (e.g., styrene ~165, methyl methacrylate ~515, vinyl acetate ~3700 at 60 °C). ### Step 3 — Termination Two growing chain radicals combine to form a dead polymer. Two mechanisms operate: **(a) Combination (Coupling):** $$M_m\bullet + M_n\bullet \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} M_{m+n}$$ - Forms **one dead polymer** with degree of polymerisation $\text{DP} = m + n$. - Conservation: chain ends = 2 per dead polymer. **(b) Disproportionation:** $$M_m\bullet + M_n\bullet \xrightarrow{k_{td}} M_m\ (\text{saturated}) + M_n\ (\text{unsaturated})$$ - Forms **two dead polymers**, one with a saturated end and one with an unsaturated (vinyl) end. - Dominant for monomers like MMA at higher temperatures. $$k_t = k_{tc} + k_{td}$$ ### Step 4 — Chain Transfer The active radical is **transferred** from the growing chain to another molecule (monomer, solvent, polymer, or initiator). The original chain becomes "dead," and a new radical is born on the transfer agent. $$M_n\bullet + XZ \xrightarrow{k_{tr,X}} M_n\text{-}Z + X\bullet$$ The new radical $X\bullet$ must be reactive enough to re-initiate polymerisation, otherwise it acts as a **retarder** or **inhibitor**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam essentials. Memorise the units — KTU examiners award 1 mark per unit error in numerical problems.** | # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Rate of initiation | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Factor 2 because each initiator gives 2 radicals | | 2 | Rate of propagation | $R_p = k_p [M\bullet] [M]$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Dominant consumption of monomer | | 3 | Rate of termination | $R_t = 2 k_t [M\bullet]^2$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Bimolecular; factor 2 avoids double-counting | | 4 | Steady-state radical conc. | $[M\bullet] = \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | mol·L⁻¹ | Derived from $R_i = R_t$ | | 5 | **Overall rate of polymerisation** | $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | **The master equation** | | 6 | **Order in initiator** | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | — | Hallmark of FRP | | 7 | **Order in monomer** | $1$ | — | Hallmark of FRP | | 8 | Kinetic chain length (no CT) | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$ | dimensionless | Avg. monomers per radical | | 9 | **DP — combination** | $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$ | — | Each termination makes 1 chain | | 10 | **DP — disproportionation** | $\overline{X}_n = \nu$ | — | Each termination makes 2 chains | | 11 | **Mayo Equation** | $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_X \dfrac{[X]}{[M]}$ | — | Linear plot: slope = $C_X$, intercept = $1/(\overline{X}_n)_0$ | | 12 | Chain transfer constant | $C_X = \dfrac{k_{tr,X}}{k_p}$ | dimensionless | $C_X < 0.01$ negligible; $> 1$ telomerisation | | 13 | Activation energy (overall) | $E_a = E_p + \dfrac{1}{2}(E_d - E_t)$ | kJ/mol | For $R_p$ temperature dependence | | 14 | Half-life of initiator | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_d} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_d}$ | s | Important for reactor design | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility of FRP Kinetics > [!NOTE] > The **square-root dependence of $R_p$ on [I]** is the most exploited lever in industry. To **double the rate**, one must **quadruple the initiator concentration** — a non-intuitive result that determines reactor design and operating cost. The same square-root scaling inversely controls molecular weight: increasing [I] *decreases* $\overline{X}_n$. This inherent **rate–molecular weight trade-off** is the central design constraint of FRP reactors and is a frequent 14-mark KTU question. Real-world reactor applications: - **LDPE synthesis** (high-pressure tubular/CSTR, 1000–3000 atm, 80–300 °C) — relies on oxygen or peroxide initiation. - **PVC via suspension polymerisation** (water + VCM + peroxide + protective colloid). - **Polystyrene via bulk or solution FRP**. - **Acrylic resins** for coatings, adhesives, and dental prosthetics. ## 2.4 The Steady-State Approximation — Why It Works A critical assumption underpinning all the above equations is the **Bodenstein steady-state principle**: $$\frac{d[M\bullet]}{dt} = 0 \implies R_i = R_t$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical justification:** Radicals are consumed (by termination) and produced (by initiation) at virtually equal rates because the radical concentration is **extraordinarily low** ($\sim 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-8}$ mol/L). The radicals are so reactive that they annihilate each other almost as fast as they form. This microscopic balance is the foundation of FRP kinetics and is **invariably asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Rate Equation $R_p$ We proceed from the three elementary rates, applying the steady-state assumption. ### Step 1 — Write the rate of each elementary process **Initiation (decomposition):** $$R_d = 2 f k_d [I]$$ **Initiation (radical addition to monomer):** $$R_i = k_i [R\bullet][M]$$ Because the radical-addition step is **much faster** than decomposition, $R_i$ is limited by $R_d$. Hence: $$R_i \approx R_d = 2 f k_d [I]$$ **Propagation:** $$R_p = k_p [M\bullet][M]$$ **Termination:** $$R_t = 2 k_t [M\bullet]^2$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the steady-state approximation (SSA) The radical concentration builds up rapidly (microseconds) and then plateaus. At this plateau, generation balances consumption: $$R_i = R_t$$ $$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M\bullet]^2$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for the radical concentration $[M\bullet]$ $$[M\bullet]^2 = \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}$$ $$[M\bullet] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute into the propagation rate $$R_p = k_p [M] \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$ $$\boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}}$$ This is the **master equation of FRP kinetics**. Every KTU 14-mark derivation question reduces to this expression. ## 3.2 Derivation of Kinetic Chain Length $\nu$ By definition, the kinetic chain length is the **average number of monomer units added** per active radical before it terminates: $$\nu = \frac{\text{Rate of monomer consumption (propagation)}}{\text{Rate of radical consumption (termination)}}$$ $$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_t} = \frac{R_p}{R_i}$$ Substituting the steady-state result $R_i = R_t = 2 k_t [M\bullet]^2$ and $R_p = k_p [M\bullet][M]$: $$\nu = \frac{k_p [M\bullet] [M]}{2 k_t [M\bullet]^2} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 k_t [M\bullet]}$$ Substituting $[M\bullet] = \sqrt{f k_d [I] / k_t}$: $$\nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 k_t} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_t}{f k_d [I]}} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$$ $$\boxed{\nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation $\overline{X}_n$ The number-average degree of polymerisation is: $$\overline{X}_n = \frac{\text{Rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{Rate of chain (dead polymer) formation}} = \frac{-d[M]/dt}{d[\text{dead polymer}]/dt}$$ The rate of dead polymer formation depends on the termination mode: **Case A — Termination by Combination (coupling):** Two radicals → one dead polymer: $$\frac{d[\text{dead polymer}]}{dt} = k_t [M\bullet]^2 = R_t / 2$$ Hence: $$\overline{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_t / 2} = \frac{2 R_p}{R_t} = 2 \nu$$ **Case B — Termination by Disproportionation:** Two radicals → two dead polymers: $$\frac{d[\text{dead polymer}]}{dt} = 2 k_t [M\bullet]^2 = R_t$$ Hence: $$\overline{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_t} = \nu$$ **Combined form** (let $c$ = fraction of termination by combination, $d = 1 - c$ by disproportionation): $$\overline{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{c \cdot R_t / 2 + (1-c) \cdot R_t} = \frac{R_p}{R_t (1 - c/2)}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Mayo's Equation (Chain Transfer to Solvent) Starting from the definition of $\overline{X}_n$ in the presence of a chain transfer agent $X$: $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{R_t + \sum R_{tr}}{R_p}$$ where $R_{tr,X} = k_{tr,X} [M\bullet][X]$ is the rate of transfer to species $X$. Substituting all terms: $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{2 k_t [M\bullet]^2 + k_{tr,M} [M\bullet][M] + k_{tr,I} [M\bullet][I] + k_{tr,S} [M\bullet][S] + \cdots}{k_p [M\bullet][M]}$$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $k_p [M\bullet][M]$: $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{2 k_t [M\bullet]}{k_p [M]} + \frac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p} + \frac{k_{tr,I} [I]}{k_p [M]} + \frac{k_{tr,S} [S]}{k_p [M]} + \cdots$$ Define the **chain transfer constant** $C_X = k_{tr,X} / k_p$. In the absence of any transfer: $$\frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} = \frac{2 k_t [M\bullet]}{k_p [M]} + C_M + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$ Subtracting the two: $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} - \frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} = C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **Mayo plot** equation. Plotting $1/\overline{X}_n$ vs $[S]/[M]$ yields a **straight line of slope $C_S$** and intercept $1/(\overline{X}_n)_0$. KTU examiners frequently give experimental data and ask the student to compute $C_S$ and $(\overline{X}_n)_0$. ## 3.5 Derivation of Overall Activation Energy $E_a$ Apply the Arrhenius equation to each rate constant: $$k = A \exp\left(-\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$ The overall rate is: $$R_p = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$$ Taking logarithms and applying $d(\ln k)/dT = E_a/(RT^2)$: $$\frac{d \ln R_p}{dT} = \frac{E_p + \frac{1}{2}(E_d - E_t)}{RT^2} = \frac{E_a}{RT^2}$$ $$\boxed{E_a = E_p + \frac{1}{2}(E_d - E_t)}$$ With typical values: $E_p \approx 30$ kJ/mol, $E_d \approx 140$ kJ/mol, $E_t \approx 15$ kJ/mol: $$E_a \approx 30 + 0.5(140 - 15) = 30 + 62.5 = 92.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A useful KTU thumb rule: **the overall $E_a$ of FRP is dominated by $E_d/2$** (initiator decomposition), because $E_d$ is by far the largest. This is why small temperature changes cause large rate changes — a 10 °C rise can nearly double the rate. ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Styrene is polymerised in bulk at 60 °C using AIBN initiator. Given: - $[M] = 8.7$ mol/L - $[I] = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/L - $k_d = 3.2 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹ - $k_p = 165$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ - $k_t = 6.5 \times 10^{7}$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ - $f = 0.6$ **Calculate:** (a) $[M\bullet]$, (b) $R_p$, (c) $\nu$, (d) $\overline{X}_n$ (assume combination). ### (a) Radical concentration $$[M\bullet] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$ $$[M\bullet] = \sqrt{\frac{0.6 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-2}}{6.5 \times 10^{7}}}$$ $$[M\bullet] = \sqrt{\frac{1.92 \times 10^{-8}}{6.5 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{2.954 \times 10^{-16}}$$ $$[M\bullet] = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/L}$$ ### (b) Rate of polymerisation $$R_p = k_p [M] [M\bullet] = 165 \times 8.7 \times 1.72 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$R_p = 2.47 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol·L}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ ### (c) Kinetic chain length $$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{2.47 \times 10^{-5}}{2 f k_d [I]} = \frac{2.47 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 0.6 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-2}}$$ $$\nu = \frac{2.47 \times 10^{-5}}{3.84 \times 10^{-8}} = 643$$ ### (d) Degree of polymerisation (combination) $$\overline{X}_n = 2\nu = 1286$$ > [!NOTE] > The radical concentration is **~10⁻⁸ M** — only one radical per 10¹⁰ monomer molecules. This is why the steady-state assumption works so well and why a single impurity molecule can dramatically retard or inhibit the reaction. ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables | Variable Change | Effect on $R_p$ | Effect on $\overline{X}_n$ | Effect on PDI | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---|---|---| | $[I] \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (by $[I]^{1/2}$) | $\downarrow$ (by $[I]^{-1/2}$) | Narrows | More polymer, lower MW | | $[M] \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\uparrow$ linearly | Narrows | Higher MW, faster reaction | | $T \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (large; $E_a \sim 90$ kJ/mol) | $\downarrow$ (large) | — | Run-away risk; MW drops | | Solvent $\uparrow$ | Unchanged | $\downarrow$ (if $C_S > 0$) | — | Solution FRP lowers MW | | Inhibitor addition | $\downarrow$ (or zero) | Falls to zero | — | Used as "stop" mechanism in radical FRP | | Oxygen | Acts as inhibitor | Zero | — | Must be excluded in lab; but in industry, O₂ is sometimes the initiator for LDPE high-pressure process | ## 3.8 Chain Transfer Agents — The $C_S$ Hierarchy | Transfer Agent | Typical $C_S \times 10^{4}$ (60 °C) | Behaviour | |---|---|---| | Benzene | 0.23 | Negligible — good solvent | | Toluene | 1.25 | Mild | | Ethyl acetate | 4.5 | Moderate | | Isopropanol | 18.6 | Significant | | Carbon tetrachloride | 900 | **Strong** — classic CTA | | Mercaptans (thiols) | $\gg 1$ | Telomerisation if $C_S \gg 1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mercaptans** (e.g., dodecanethiol, $C_S \sim 10^5$) are deliberately added in emulsion polymerisation of SBR rubber to precisely control molecular weight without slowing the rate. This is a **14-mark KTU favourite**: "Discuss the role of chain transfer agents in controlling molecular weight." # 4.
