KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the term "kinetic chain length" in free radical polymerisation. How is it related to the degree of polymerisation under combination and disproportionation termination?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **kinetic chain length ($\nu$)** is defined as the *average number of monomer molecules added to a growing chain radical from its initiation to its termination*. Mathematically: $$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$$ - Under **combination** termination, two radicals fuse to form **one** dead polymer. Hence, the number-average degree of polymerisation is: $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$. - Under **disproportionation** termination, two radicals yield **two** dead polymers. Hence: $\overline{X}_n = \nu$. **(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark relation to DP)** ✓ --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between an "inhibitor" and a "retarder" in free radical polymerisation, giving one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Inhibitor | Retarder | |---|---|---| | **Action** | Completely stops polymerisation; reacts with primary radicals to form stable, non-propagating species | Slows the rate but does not stop polymerisation; chain transfer to retarder is slow but not zero | | **Chain transfer constant $C_X$** | $C_X \to \infty$ (radical never re-initiates) | $C_X$ moderate (radical *may* re-initiate slowly) | | **Effect on $R_p$** | $R_p = 0$ (induction period observed) | $R_p$ reduced but finite | | **Example** | **Hydroquinone**, **1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH)**, oxygen, benzoquinone | **Nitrobenzene**, **nitroso compounds** | Hydroquinone is the **industry-standard inhibitor** added to monomers (e.g., styrene, MMA) for safe transport and storage. **(1 mark distinction, 1 mark action mechanism, 1 mark examples)** ✓ > [!NOTE] > Both inhibitors and retarders **do not affect $k_p$** — they reduce $R_p$ by lowering the steady-state radical concentration. This subtlety is often tested. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Module 3 Choice]` **(a) Derive the steady-state rate expression for free radical polymerisation, clearly stating the assumptions used. Show that the rate is proportional to $[M]$ and to $[I]^{1/2}$. (7 marks)** **(b) A monomer is polymerised by FRP at 60 °C with $[M] = 5.0$ mol/L, $[I] = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, $f = 0.7$, $k_d = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹, $k_p = 1000$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹, $k_t = 5.0 \times 10^{7}$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹. Calculate (i) the rate of polymerisation, (ii) the kinetic chain length, and (iii) the number-average degree of polymerisation assuming combination. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Steady-state approximation applies: $R_i = R_t$. 2. $k_p$ is independent of chain length. 3. Termination is exclusively by combination (or by disproportionation — to be specified). 4. Chain transfer is negligible. 5. The radical-addition step is fast compared to initiator decomposition, so $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$. **Elementary steps and their rates (1 mark):** | Step | Equation | Rate | |---|---|---| | Decomposition | $I \to 2 R\bullet$ | $R_d = 2 f k_d [I]$ | | Initiation (addition) | $R\bullet + M \to M_1\bullet$ | $R_i = k_i [R\bullet][M]$ | | Propagation | $M_n\bullet + M \to M_{n+1}\bullet$ | $R_p = k_p [M\bullet][M]$ | | Termination (combination) | $M_m\bullet + M_n\bullet \to M_{m+n}$ | $R_t = 2 k_t [M\bullet]^2$ | **Apply SSA (1 mark):** $R_i = R_t \Rightarrow 2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M\bullet]^2$ $$[M\bullet] = \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Substitute into $R_p$ (1 mark):** $$R_p = k_p [M] [M\bullet] = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}}$$ **Order in monomer (1 mark):** $R_p \propto [M]^1$ — first order. **Order in initiator (1 mark):** $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2}$ — half order. This half-order dependence is the **kinetic fingerprint** of FRP. **Theoretical significance (1 mark):** The half-order in [I] is experimentally verified and is a direct consequence of the bimolecular termination step. A deviation from half-order suggests chain transfer, primary radical termination, or thermal (spontaneous) initiation. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $[M] = 5.0$ mol/L, $[I] = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, $f = 0.7$, $k_d = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹, $k_p = 1000$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹, $k_t = 5.0 \times 10^{7}$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹. **(i) Rate of polymerisation (3 marks):** $$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$ Inside the square root: $$\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t} = \frac{0.7 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0 \times 10^{7}} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{-7}}{5.0 \times 10^{7}} = 2.8 \times 10^{-15}$$ $$[M\bullet] = \sqrt{2.8 \times 10^{-15}} = 5.29 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/L}$$ $$R_p = 1000 \times 5.0 \times 5.29 \times 10^{-8} = 2.65 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol·L}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ **(ii) Kinetic chain length (2 marks):** $$R_i = 2 f k_d [I] = 2 \times 0.7 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.8 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol·L}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{2.65 \times 10^{-4}}{2.8 \times 10^{-7}} = 946$$ **(iii) Number-average DP (combination) (2 marks):** $$\overline{X}_n = 2 \nu = 2 \times 946 = 1892$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgot the factor 2 in $R_i$** — examiners deduct 1 full mark. The 2 arises because each initiator molecule yields *two* radicals. > 2. **Forgot the factor 2 in $R_t$** — this is a *statistical* factor to avoid double-counting radical pairs, NOT a chemistry factor. Many students incorrectly omit it. > 3. **Wrong DP formula** — Students often write $\overline{X}_n = \nu$ for all cases. It is **$2\nu$ for combination and $\nu$ for disproportionation**. > 4. **Units of $k_p$ and $k_t$ must match** — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per unit mismatch. > 5. **Mixing up $R_p$ with $-d[M]/dt$** — By convention, $R_p$ is the rate of *monomer consumption by propagation* (which equals $-d[M]/dt$ for short chains, but is *not* the rate of polymer chain formation). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Module 3 Choice]` **(a) Explain the phenomenon of chain transfer in free radical polymerisation. Derive Mayo's equation and discuss its significance in determining chain transfer constants experimentally. (7 marks)** **(b) Discuss the Trommsdorff (Norrish–Smith) effect in free radical polymerisation, its origin, consequences on molecular weight, and industrial relevance. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Definition of chain transfer (1 mark):** Chain transfer is the process in which an active growing chain radical $M_n\bullet$ abstracts an atom (commonly H or Cl) from a *non-radical* species $XZ$, producing a *dead* polymer and a *new* radical $X\bullet$: $$M_n\bullet + XZ \xrightarrow{k_{tr,X}} M_n\text{-}Z + X\bullet$$ The new radical $X\bullet$ may or may not re-initiate polymerisation. **Types of chain transfer (1.5 marks):** - **Transfer to monomer** ($C_M$): reduces $\overline{X}_n$; for styrene $C_M \sim 10^{-4}$. - **Transfer to solvent** ($C_S$): crucial in solution polymerisation; chloroform ($C_S \sim 6 \times 10^{-4}$) and CCl₄ ($C_S \sim 10^{-3}$) are strong transfer agents. - **Transfer to initiator** ($C_I$): wastes initiator; significant for peroxides. - **Transfer to polymer** ($C_P$): causes *branching*; dominant in LDPE synthesis. **Derivation of Mayo equation (3 marks):** The DP in presence of transfer agent $S$: $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{R_t + R_{tr,S} + R_{tr,M} + R_{tr,I} + \cdots}{R_p}$$ $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{2 k_t [M\bullet]^2 + k_{tr,M}[M\bullet][M] + k_{tr,S}[M\bullet][S] + \cdots}{k_p [M\bullet][M]}$$ $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{2 k_t [M\bullet]}{k_p [M]} + C_M + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]} + \cdots$$ Define $(\overline{X}_n)_0$ as the DP in the absence of $S$ (with only monomer and intrinsic transfer): $$\frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} = \frac{2 k_t [M\bullet]}{k_p [M]} + C_M + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$ Subtracting: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]}}$$ **Experimental significance (1.5 marks):** - A plot of $1/\overline{X}_n$ vs $[S]/[M]$ gives a **straight line** (Mayo plot). - **Slope** = $C_S$ (chain transfer constant of solvent $S$). - **Intercept** = $1/(\overline{X}_n)_0$ (intrinsic DP without transfer). - This method is the standard tool to characterise new solvents/regulators for FRP. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Definition (1 mark):** The **Trommsdorff effect** (also called the **Norrish–Smith effect** or **gel effect**) is the **autoacceleration** of free radical polymerisation rate observed at intermediate to high monomer conversions, accompanied by an **increase in molecular weight**. **Origin — physical mechanism (2 marks):** - As conversion increases, the reaction mixture viscosity rises dramatically. - Diffusion of *long* chain radicals becomes rate-limiting. - Termination is bimolecular and requires two chain radicals to **diffuse together**; hence $k_t$ **decreases sharply**. - Propagation, however, requires only the *small monomer* to diffuse to the chain end; $k_p$ is **relatively unchanged** (or decreases only slightly). - Net effect: $R_p \propto k_p / \sqrt{k_t}$ **increases** as $k_t$ falls, while $\overline{X}_n \propto k_p/\sqrt{k_t}$ also **increases**. **Quantitative description (1.5 marks):** $$R_p = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_t \downarrow \Rightarrow R_p \uparrow \text{ and } \overline{X}_n \uparrow$$ **Distinction from glass effect (0.5 mark):** At very high conversion (~80–90%), the system can vitrify (glass effect); here even $k_p$ drops and the reaction effectively stops. **Consequences (1 mark):** 1. Sudden rise in $R_p$ — risk of **thermal runaway** (large exotherm). 2. Broadened molecular-weight distribution (high PDI). 3. Loss of molecular-weight control at high conversion. 4. Reactor hot-spots; potential autoignition in industrial reactors (e.g., bulk styrene polymerisation). **Industrial relevance and mitigation (1 mark):** - **Bulk polymerisation** of styrene and MMA exhibits strong Trommsdorff effect; reactors require **aggressive cooling** and temperature control. - **Solution, suspension, and emulsion polymerisation** dilute the system and **reduce the Trommsdorff effect** — a key reason these heterogeneous methods dominate commercial FRP. - Chain transfer agents (CTAs) such as dodecanethiol limit chain length, reducing viscosity buildup and thereby mitigating the gel effect. - Reactor design uses **intensive mixing, reflux condensers, and staged feeds** to control exotherms. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Trommsdorff Effect:** > 1. Students often claim $R_p$ **decreases** at high conversion — this is **wrong**; the defining feature is **autoacceleration** ($R_p$ *increases*) due to falling $k_t$. > 2. Students confuse the **Trommsdorff effect** with the **glass effect** — they are different. Trommsdorff = $k_t$ falls; glass effect = even $k_p$ falls. > 3. Trommsdorff is **conversion-dependent**, not temperature-dependent directly. > 4. Do NOT confuse it with "diffusion-controlled termination" terminology used for low-molecular-weight radicals at low conversion — Trommsdorff specifically refers to *long* radicals at *high* conversion. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 | Free Radical Polymerisation** - **FRP is a chain-growth mechanism** with four elementary steps: Initiation, Propagation, Termination, Chain Transfer. - **Initiation is in two sub-steps:** slow decomposition ($k_d$) followed by fast radical addition ($k_i$). The decomposition is the **rate-determining bottleneck**. - **Master rate equation:** $$R_p = k_p [M] \left(\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}$$ - **First order in [M]**, **half order in [I]** — the kinetic fingerprint of FRP. - **Steady-state approximation:** $R_i = R_t$ — valid because $[M\bullet]$ is extremely low ($10^{-7}$–$10^{-8}$ M). - **Kinetic chain length:** $$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$$ - **Degree of polymerisation:** - Combination: $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$ - Disproportionation: $\overline{X}_n = \nu$ - **Mayo equation** (chain transfer): $$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]}$$ - **Chain transfer constant:** $C_X = k_{tr,X} / k_p$. Mercaptans have $C_S \gg 1$ (used as molecular-weight regulators in SBR rubber synthesis). - **Overall activation energy:** $E_a = E_p + \tfrac{1}{2}(E_d - E_t) \sim 90$ kJ/mol — dominated by $E_d$. - **Trommsdorff (Norrish–Smith) effect:** At high conversion, viscosity rises, $k_t$ drops, $R_p$ and $\overline{X}_n$ both increase — **autoacceleration**. Distinguish from glass effect at very high conversion. - **Inhibitor** (e.g., hydroquinone, DPPH) stops polymerisation completely ($C_X \to \infty$). **Retarder** (e.g., nitrobenzene) only slows it. - **Cage effect:** Fraction $f$ of primary radicals escapes the solvent cage to initiate chains; typically $f = 0.5$–$0.7$. - **Half-order in initiator** ⇒ to double $R_p$, quadruple $[I]$ — non-intuitive, but central to FRP reactor design. - **Rate–MW trade-off:** Increasing $[I]$ increases $R_p$ but decreases $\overline{X}_n$. This is the **fundamental design constraint** of FRP. - **Three common initiators to remember:** 1. **AIBN** (azobisisobutyronitrile) — 60–80 °C, $E_d \approx 130$ kJ/mol. 2. **Benzoyl peroxide (BPO)** — 60–90 °C, $E_d \approx 140$ kJ/mol. 3. **Potassium persulfate (K₂S₂O₈)** — water-soluble; used in emulsion polymerisation. - **Photoinitiators** (e.g., benzoin ethers) — RT reactions, important in UV-curable coatings and dental composites. - **Redox initiators** (e.g., Fe²⁺/H₂O₂, peroxides + amines) — operate at very low temperatures (0–50 °C); critical for low-temperature FRP of certain dienes. - **Industrially produced by FRP:** LDPE, PVC, PS, PMMA, polyacrylonitrile, SBR rubber, polyacrylamide, polytetrafluoroethylene (via special initiators).

topics.title: Cationic Polymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cationic Polymerization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cationic Polymerization** is a sub-class of **chain-growth (addition) polymerization** in which the active propagating centre is a **carbocation** (positively charged carbon species). The reaction proceeds via electrophilic attack of a cationic initiator–co-catalyst complex onto an electron-rich vinyl monomer, opening the π-bond and generating a positively charged chain end that adds successive monomer units.

The general chain-carrying species can be represented as:

$$\sim\sim \text{C}^{\oplus} \text{X}^{\ominus} \quad \text{or} \quad \sim\sim \text{CH}_2 - \overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{H}(\text{R})\ \text{Y}^{\ominus}$$

where **X⁻ (or Y⁻)** is the counter-ion from the initiator/co-catalyst system, and **R** is an electron-donating substituent that stabilises the carbocation (e.g., –OR, –C₆H₅, alkyl groups).

**Key chemical requirement:** Cationic polymerization is only feasible with monomers whose double bond is **electron-rich** — i.e., substituted with +I (inductive donating) or +M (mesomeric donating) groups. Such substituents stabilise the developing positive charge on the growing carbocation through **hyperconjugation** and **resonance delocalisation**.

**Standard Initiator Systems (Lewis Acid + Co-catalyst):**

| Lewis Acid (Initiator) | Co-catalyst (Proton donor) | Active Species Generated |
|------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| BF₃ | H₂O, ROH, RCOOH | H⁺ (BF₃OH⁻) |
| AlCl₃ | H₂O, HCl | H⁺ (AlCl₃OH⁻) |
| SnCl₄ | H₂O | H⁺ (SnCl₄OH⁻) |
| TiCl₄ | H₂O, CCl₃COOH | H⁺ (TiCl₄OH⁻) |
| SbCl₅, SbF₅ | H₂O | Super-acidic cation |

> [!NOTE]
> The actual initiator is **NOT** the Lewis acid alone — it is the **protonated complex** formed by the reaction of the Lewis acid with a trace of protogenic co-catalyst (water, alcohol, acid). This is a hallmark feature of cationic polymerisation and is unique to it.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **magnet attracting iron filings**:
- The **magnet (Lewis acid)** is not active on its own — it needs to be **energised** (the protogenic co-catalyst, like water, acts as the "switch" that powers it up).
- Once energised, the magnet **pulls in a single iron filing first (initiation)**, but that filing itself becomes a temporary magnet (the carbocation), which **pulls in the next filing (propagation)**, and so on — building a chain.
- The chain stops when two magnets **clash and neutralise (termination)**, or when a stray iron piece (chain transfer agent) diverts the chain.

Similarly, in cationic polymerisation:
- A **trace of water** reacts with BF₃ to form a proton (H⁺), which is the actual "magnet" that grabs the first electron-rich monomer.
- That monomer becomes positively charged and grabs the next, growing the chain.
- The chain stops via combination with a counter-ion, or by transferring its positive charge to another molecule.

## 1.3 Why Cationic Polymerization Matters (Engineering Significance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cationic polymerisation is the **only commercial route** to several technologically vital polymers:
> - **Polyisobutylene (PIB)** — used in fuel additives, adhesives, sealants, chewing gum base.
> - **Butyl Rubber (IIR)** — copolymer of isobutylene with small amount of isoprene; used in **inner tubes of tyres**, pharmaceutical stoppers.
> - **Poly(vinyl ethers)** — used in pressure-sensitive adhesives, coatings.
> - **Poly(N-vinylcarbazole)** — used in electrophotography (photoconductors).
> - **Coumarone-indene resins** — used in printing inks, floor tiles, rubber tackifiers.

The process is also the foundation of modern **Living Cationic Polymerization (LCP)**, which enables precise synthesis of block copolymers, telechelics, and end-functional polymers (developed by Kennedy, Sawamoto, Higashimura).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanistic Steps — The Four-Stage Cascade

Cationic polymerisation follows the **classical chain-reaction scheme**: Initiation → Propagation → Termination (or Chain Transfer).

### Stage 1: INITIATION (Two Sub-steps)

**Step (i) — Formation of the active initiating cation:**

The Lewis acid (Mx) reacts with a co-catalyst (RH) to generate a protonated complex that dissociates into a carbocation and a counter-ion.

$$\text{M}_x\text{R} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}^{\oplus}\ [\text{M}_x\text{R}]^{\ominus}$$

Example with BF₃ + H₂O:

$$2\ \text{BF}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^{\oplus}\ [\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus}$$

**Step (ii) — Electrophilic addition of the cation to the first monomer:**

The active cation attacks the π-bond of the electron-rich monomer, opening it to form a new carbocation.

$$\text{H}^{\oplus}[\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CHR} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{HR}\ [\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus}$$

**Why is this energetically favourable?** The H⁺ (a small, highly electrophilic species) seeks electrons. Vinyl monomers substituted with –OR, –C₆H₅, –CH₃ have **electron-rich double bonds** (high π-electron density), so the H⁺ attacks the C=C readily. The resulting carbocation is stabilised by the +M or +I effect of R.

### Stage 2: PROPAGATION

The newly formed carbocation adds successive monomers in a head-to-tail fashion, with the positive charge migrating to the chain end each time.

$$\sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{HR}\ [\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CHR} \xrightarrow{k_p} \sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CHR}-\text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{HR}\ [\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus}$$

**Key feature:** Each propagation step regenerates a carbocation of the **same nature** at the chain end — i.e., the chain end remains "alive" until a termination or chain-transfer event occurs. This is what makes **living cationic polymerisation** possible when termination/transfer are suppressed.

**Regioselectivity:** The cation attacks the **less-substituted carbon** of the monomer (Markovnikov-like) to place the positive charge on the more substituted (more stable) carbon — a 1,2- or 1,1-addition preference.

### Stage 3: TERMINATION

Unlike free-radical polymerisation, **true termination by combination is rare** in cationic systems. The dominant termination modes are:

**(a) Spontaneous termination (collapse with counter-ion):**

$$\sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{HR}\ [\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus} \longrightarrow \sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CHR}-\text{OH} + \text{BF}_3$$

This is a **unimolecular** process and is often called the "**ion-pair collapse**" or "**spontaneous termination**".

**(b) Combination with a counter-ion from another chain** (rare, only at high initiator concentrations):

$$\sim\sim \text{C}^{\oplus} + \text{X}^{\ominus} \longrightarrow \sim\sim \text{C}-\text{X}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because termination by ion-pair collapse is dominant and unimolecular, the **rate of termination does NOT depend on monomer concentration** — a critical difference from free-radical polymerisation that affects the kinetic order of the overall rate.

### Stage 4: CHAIN TRANSFER

A more common fate of a cationic chain is **chain transfer to monomer (CTM)**, where the active centre transfers a proton to a fresh monomer molecule, creating a new initiating species but stopping the original chain.

$$\sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{HR}\ [\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CHR} \xrightarrow{k_{tr,M}} \sim\sim \text{CH}=\text{CHR} + \text{CH}_3-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{HR}\ [\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus}$$

A new chain is started, and the original chain ends in an **unsaturated (allylic/vinylic) dead polymer**. Chain transfer can also occur **to solvent, to polymer, or deliberately to a chain transfer agent (CTA)** such as an ether or an alcohol.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All the formulas below are **frequently asked in KTU End Semester Examinations** under Module 3. Memorise these and the conditions under which they apply.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Definition of Terms | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Rate of Initiation ($R_i$) | $R_i = k_i [\text{C}][\text{M}]$ | $k_i$ = initiation rate constant; [C] = co-catalyst conc.; [M] = monomer conc. | Often written as $R_i = 2 f k_d [\text{I}]$ in radical — don't confuse. |
| 2 | Rate of Propagation ($R_p$) | $R_p = k_p [\text{M}^+][\text{M}]$ | $[\text{M}^+]$ = active chain cation conc. | KTU expects the **assumed steady-state form** in derivations. |
| 3 | Rate of Termination ($R_t$) | $R_t = k_t [\text{M}^+]$ | Unimolecular ion-pair collapse | Note: $R_t$ is **independent of [M]** — a cationic hallmark. |
| 4 | Steady-state assumption (SSA) | $R_i = R_t$ | Gives $[\text{M}^+] = \dfrac{k_i[\text{C}][\text{M}]}{k_t}$ | Apply ONLY in classic cationic with spontaneous termination. |
| 5 | Overall Rate of Polymerisation ($R_p$ in terms of [M], [C]) | $R_p = \dfrac{k_p k_i}{k_t} [\text{C}] [\text{M}]^2$ | Second order in [M], first order in [C] | **Distinctive signature** — different from radical ($R_p \propto [\text{M}]^{1.5}$). |
| 6 | Kinetic Chain Length ($\nu$) | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [\text{M}]}{k_t}$ | Average monomers added per active centre | Use SSA to simplify. |
| 7 | Number-average DP ($\bar{X}_n$, no transfer) | $\bar{X}_n = \nu = \dfrac{k_p [\text{M}]}{k_t}$ | Pure termination case | KTU frequently asks for this. |
| 8 | Number-average DP with chain transfer to monomer | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{\nu} + \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$ | $k_{tr,M}$ = CTM rate constant | **MOST ASKED FORMULA** in Module 3. |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n}$ in full (Mayo-like form) | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{k_t}{k_p [\text{M}]} + \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p} + \dfrac{k_{tr,S}[\text{S}]}{k_p [\text{M}]} + \dfrac{C_\text{CTA}[\text{CTA}]}{[\text{M}]}$ | Includes transfer to solvent, CTA | Used in Mayo-plot analysis. |
| 10 | Chain transfer constant ($C_M$) | $C_M = \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$ | Dimensionless | Slope of Mayo plot. |
| 11 | Cationic Activation Energy ($E_a$) | Typically **negative** or very low (4–8 kJ/mol) | Lower than radical ($E_a \sim$ 80–100 kJ/mol) | Justifies low-T cationic polymerisation. |
| 12 | Number of active chains ($N^*$) | $N^* = R_i \cdot \tau$ or $[M^+]$ from SSA | $\tau$ = average lifetime of chain | Useful in catalyst efficiency problems. |
| 13 | Catalytic efficiency (when CTA present) | $f = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i}$ (corrected) | Often < 1 | Frequently combined with transfer equations. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use `|` (vertical pipe) in your written exam answers when denoting absolute value or modulus.** KTU evaluators mark this as ambiguous. Use `mod` or `abs` or, in LaTeX, `\vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Process is Indispensable

| Polymer | Monomer | Initiator | Industrial Use |
|---------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Polyisobutylene (PIB) | Isobutylene | BF₃ + H₂O at –80 to –100 °C | Adhesives, fuel additives |
| Butyl rubber (IIR) | Isobutylene + 1–3% isoprene | AlCl₃ at –100 °C | Tyre inner tubes, pharma closures |
| Poly(vinyl ether)s | Methyl/ethyl vinyl ether | BF₃·OEt₂ | Pressure-sensitive adhesives |
| Coumarone–indene resins | Coumarone + indene | BF₃, AlCl₃ | Inks, rubber tackifiers |
| Petroleum resins | C₅/C₹ cracked fractions | AlCl₃, BF₃ | Paints, road marking |

The **ability to operate at very low temperatures (–40 to –100 °C)** is exploited to **suppress chain transfer** and obtain high molecular weight polymers — a regime where radical or anionic chemistry is impractical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Active Centre Concentration $[\text{M}^+]$

In classical cationic polymerisation with **spontaneous (unimolecular) termination**, we apply the **steady-state assumption (SSA)**: the rate of formation of active centres equals the rate of their destruction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate of Initiation: } & R_i = k_i\,[\text{C}]\,[\text{M}] \\[6pt]
\text{Rate of Termination: } & R_t = k_t\,[\text{M}^+] \\[6pt]
\text{At steady state: } & R_i = R_t \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow\ & k_i\,[\text{C}]\,[\text{M}] = k_t\,[\text{M}^+] \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow\ & [\text{M}^+]_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{k_i\,[\text{C}]\,[\text{M}]}{k_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this form?** The unimolecular ion-pair collapse makes $R_t$ depend only on $[\text{M}^+]$, not on $[\text{M}]$. This is a kinetic signature of cationic systems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall Rate of Polymerisation $R_p$

The rate of polymerisation (monomer consumption in propagation) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= k_p\,[\text{M}^+]\,[\text{M}]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute the SSA expression for $[\text{M}^+]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= k_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{k_i\,[\text{C}]\,[\text{M}]}{k_t}\right) \cdot [\text{M}] \\[6pt]
R_p &= \dfrac{k_p\,k_i}{k_t}\,[\text{C}]\,[\text{M}]^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $R_p \propto [\text{M}]^2$ — **second order in monomer**, first order in co-catalyst. This is a **diagnostic test** to confirm a cationic mechanism: plotting $\log R_p$ vs $\log [\text{M}]$ gives slope ≈ **2.0** (whereas radical gives 1.5 and anionic gives 1.0).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kinetic Chain Length $\nu$ and Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation $\bar{X}_n$

The **kinetic chain length** $\nu$ is defined as the average number of monomer units added per active centre during its lifetime:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu &= \dfrac{\text{Rate of Propagation}}{\text{Rate of Termination (or Initiation at SSA)}} \\[6pt]
\nu &= \dfrac{R_p}{R_t} = \dfrac{k_p\,[\text{M}^+]\,[\text{M}]}{k_t\,[\text{M}^+]} \\[6pt]
\nu &= \dfrac{k_p\,[\text{M}]}{k_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

When **chain transfer is negligible**, the number-average DP equals the kinetic chain length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_n = \nu = \dfrac{k_p\,[\text{M}]}{k_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: $\bar{X}_n$ in the Presence of Chain Transfer to Monomer (CTM)

In the presence of chain transfer to monomer, a propagating chain may be stopped by:
1. Normal termination (rate $R_t = k_t[\text{M}^+]$), or
2. Transfer to monomer (rate $R_{tr,M} = k_{tr,M}[\text{M}^+][\text{M}]$).

The number-average DP is the **total number of monomer molecules consumed** per **chain-stopping event**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} &= \dfrac{\text{Rate of chain stopping events}}{\text{Rate of monomer consumption in propagation}} \\[8pt]
\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} &= \dfrac{R_t + R_{tr,M}}{R_p} \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{k_t\,[\text{M}^+] + k_{tr,M}\,[\text{M}^+]\,[\text{M}]}{k_p\,[\text{M}^+]\,[\text{M}]} \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{k_t}{k_p\,[\text{M}]} + \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

Or equivalently, defining the **chain transfer constant** $C_M = k_{tr,M}/k_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{k_t}{k_p\,[\text{M}]} + C_M
\end{aligned}
$$

**Generalised Mayo equation** (chain transfer to monomer, solvent, and CTA simultaneously):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{k_t}{k_p\,[\text{M}]} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[\text{S}]}{[\text{M}]} + C_{\text{CTA}} \dfrac{[\text{CTA}]}{[\text{M}]}
\end{aligned}
$$

This equation is the foundation of experimental **Mayo plots** (1/$\bar{X}_n$ vs 1/[M]) used to extract $k_{tr,M}/k_p$ for cationic monomers.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Why Cationic Polymerisation is Performed at LOW Temperature

The overall activation energy of cationic polymerisation can be derived from the Arrhenius combination of the three rate constants:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p = \dfrac{k_p\,k_i}{k_t}\,[\text{C}]\,[\text{M}]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the Arrhenius equation $k = A\,e^{-E/RT}$ to each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_p &= A_p\, e^{-E_p/RT} \\
k_i &= A_i\, e^{-E_i/RT} \\
k_t &= A_t\, e^{-E_t/RT}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{k_p\,k_i}{k_t} &= \dfrac{A_p A_i}{A_t}\, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-(E_p + E_i) + E_t}{RT}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{A_p A_i}{A_t}\, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_t - (E_p + E_i)}{RT}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **effective activation energy** is:

$$
E_a^{\text{overall}} = E_t - (E_p + E_i)
$$

**Why is $E_t$ large and $(E_p + E_i)$ small?**
- $E_t$ is the energy barrier to **collapse of the ion-pair** to a covalent bond — this is a unimolecular process with significant solvent rearrangement.
- $E_p$ is low because the carbocation is highly reactive (no spin restriction, no activation by bond homolysis).
- $E_i$ is small because the co-catalyst/proton formation is a fast, low-barrier reaction.

Thus, $E_a^{\text{overall}}$ is **positive but small** (typically **4–8 kJ/mol**), so $R_p$ increases only mildly with $T$. However, **side reactions (chain transfer)** have *higher* activation energies, so lowering $T$ **selectively suppresses chain transfer** and gives higher $\bar{X}_n$.

**Numerical illustration (typical isobutylene values):**
- $E_p \approx$ 8 kJ/mol
- $E_i \approx$ 4 kJ/mol
- $E_t \approx$ 16 kJ/mol
- $E_a^{\text{overall}} = 16 - (8 + 4) = 4$ kJ/mol

Lowering $T$ from 0 °C to –100 °C (ΔT = 100 K) reduces $R_p$ by a factor of only $e^{-4000/(8.314 \times 173)} \approx 0.06$ (a 16× reduction), but reduces $R_{tr,M}$ (with $E_{tr,M} \approx$ 20 kJ/mol) by $e^{-20000/(8.314 \times 173)} \approx 8 \times 10^{-6}$ (a $10^{5}$× reduction). This is why **super-low-T cationic polymerisation** is industrially used to make high-MW polyisobutylene.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Variables on $R_p$ and $\bar{X}_n$

| Variable Increased | $R_p$ Effect | $\bar{X}_n$ Effect | Engineering Implication |
|--------------------|--------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| [M] (monomer) | ↑↑ (quadratic) | ↑ (with no transfer) | Higher [M] → faster + longer chains |
| [C] (co-catalyst) | ↑ (linear) | ↓ (more chains, shorter) | More catalyst = more nuclei = lower MW |
| Temperature | ↑ (slight) | ↓ (transfer dominates) | **Lower T → higher MW** (industrial rule) |
| Solvent polarity (ε) | ↑ (faster propagation) | Variable | More polar → better ion separation → faster |
| Lewis Acid strength | ↑ (faster initiation) | Variable (may ↑ transfer) | Stronger acid = more activity, but less control |

## 3.7 Comparison Matrix: Cationic vs Radical vs Anionic Polymerisation

| Feature | Cationic | Radical | Anionic |
|---------|----------|---------|---------|
| Active centre | Carbocation (C⁺) | Radical (•) | Carbanion (C⁻) |
| Monomer polarity | Electron-rich (+I, +M R) | Wide range | Electron-poor (–I, –M R) |
| Initiator | Lewis acid + co-catalyst | Peroxides, AIBN | Strong bases, alkyl lithiums |
| $R_p$ order in [M] | 2 | 1.5 | 1 |
| Termination | Unimolecular (ion collapse) | Bimolecular (comb/disprop) | None (living) |
| $E_a$ (kJ/mol) | 4–8 | 80–100 | ≈ 0 (ΔH very small) |
| Temperature | Very low (–80 to –100 °C) | Moderate (50–80 °C) | Low to ambient |
| Chain transfer | **Very significant** | Significant | Negligible |
| Living character | Possible with additives | ATRP, RAFT | Inherent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Overall Mechanism of Cationic Polymerisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Lewis Acid Mx_n e.g. BF3"] --> B["+ Co-catalyst RH e.g. H2O"]
    B --> C["Active Initiating Cation H plus BF3OH minus"]
    C --> D["Initiation: Attack on first monomer CH2=CHR"]
    D --> E["Carbocation 1 formed CH3-CHR plus counter ion"]
    E --> F["Propagation: Add monomer kp step"]
    F --> G["Carbocation n formed chain growing"]
    G --> H{"Chain fate?"}
    H --> I["Spontaneous Termination kt ion pair collapse"]
    H --> J["Chain Transfer to Monomer ktrM new chain started"]
    H --> K["Chain Transfer to Solvent ktrS"]
    H --> L["Chain Transfer to CTA ktrCTA controlled MW"]
    I --> M["Dead Polymer Covalent end"]
    J --> N["Dead unsaturated polymer plus new active chain"]
    K --> O["Dead polymer plus new active chain"]
    L --> P["Dead polymer plus new active chain"]
    N --> F
    O --> F
    P --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Cationic Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Feed & Activation Stage"]
        A1["Monomer Reservoir Electron rich vinyl"]
        A2["Lewis Acid Reservoir BF3 liquid"]
        A3["Co-catalyst Reservoir Trace H2O"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Reaction Stage Cryogenic  minus 80 to  minus 100 C"]
        B1["CSTR or Plug Flow Reactor with agitator"]
        B2["Inert atmosphere N2 or Ar blanket"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Quench & Recovery Stage"]
        C1["Quench with alcohol or base kills active centres"]
        C2["Solvent strip and coagulation"]
        C3["Drying and pelletising"]
    end
    subgraph S4["Quality Control"]
        D1["GPC for molecular weight distribution"]
        D2["NMR for tacticity and end groups"]
        D3["Viscosity for process control"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Energy Profile Diagram (Qualitative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Reactants Monomer plus Initiator"] -->|Ea initiation| P2["Transition State 1 Initiation"]
    P2 -->|downhill exothermic| P3["Carbocation Intermediate"]
    P3 -->|Ea propagation small| P4["Transition State 2 Propagation"]
    P4 -->|downhill| P5["Extended Carbocation Chain"]
    P5 -->|Ea termination moderate| P6["Transition State 3 Termination"]
    P6 --> P7["Dead Polymer Products"]
    style P2 fill:#ffd1d1
    style P4 fill:#d1e0ff
    style P6 fill:#d1ffd1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Red barrier (P2)**: Initiation barrier — moderate height.
- **Blue barrier (P4)**: Propagation barrier — small height, frequent crossings = fast chain growth.
- **Green barrier (P6)**: Termination barrier — moderate; occurs less frequently at low T.
- The chain progresses through many propagation cycles (P3→P4→P5) for every one initiation (P1→P2→P3) and termination (P5→P6→P7) cycle — this is what makes long polymer chains possible.

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Living Cationic Polymerisation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Initiator
    participant M1 as Monomer 1
    participant M2 as Monomer 2
    participant A as Active Centre Dormant Ion Pair
    participant N as Nucleophilic Additive
    Note over I,A: Stage 1 Initiation
    I->>A: H plus plus BF3OH minus
    Note over A,N: Stage 2 Dormancy Equilibrium
    A->>M1: Covalent dormant species exchange
    M1->>A: Activated cation grows
    A->>M2: Add second monomer
    Note over A,N: Stage 3 Controlled Propagation
    A->>A: Repeated dormant active cycles
    Note over A,N: Stage 4 Quench
    A->>N: Termination with nucleophile
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between cationic and free-radical polymerisation with respect to (i) active centre, (ii) suitable monomers, and (iii) effect of temperature on molecular weight."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cationic | Free-Radical |
|---------|----------|--------------|
| (i) Active centre | Carbocation (C⁺) with counter-ion (X⁻) | Carbon radical (•) — neutral |
| (ii) Suitable monomers | Electron-rich (vinyl ethers, isobutylene, styrene, NVC) | Wide range (styrene, MMA, vinyl chloride) |
| (iii) Effect of T on MW | **Lower T → Higher MW** (chain transfer suppressed more than propagation) | Lower T → Higher MW (less transfer, fewer radicals) — but kinetic order in [M] differs |

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"What is a chain transfer constant? Write the Mayo equation for degree of polymerisation in cationic polymerisation."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Chain transfer constant** ($C_X$) is the ratio of the rate constant for chain transfer to species X to the rate constant for propagation:

$$C_X = \dfrac{k_{tr,X}}{k_p}$$

It is dimensionless and indicates the **propensity of a chain to transfer** its active centre to species X. Higher $C_X$ means lower $\bar{X}_n$.

**Mayo equation for cationic polymerisation** (with transfer to monomer, solvent, CTA, and unimolecular termination):

$$\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{k_t}{k_p[\text{M}]} + C_M + C_S\dfrac{[\text{S}]}{[\text{M}]} + C_{\text{CTA}}\dfrac{[\text{CTA}]}{[\text{M}]}$$

This equation is the basis of the **Mayo plot** ($1/\bar{X}_n$ vs $1/[\text{M}]$) used to extract $C_M$ experimentally.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, every Module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice (either/or)**. Below are two fully-worked alternative questions (Question A or Question B), each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — "Cationic Polymerisation: Kinetics & Mechanism"
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Describe the mechanism of cationic polymerisation of styrene using BF₃–H₂O as initiator. Write the kinetic expressions for the rates of initiation, propagation, and termination. Apply the steady-state assumption and derive the overall rate of polymerisation."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Initiation**

(i) Formation of active cation (BF₃ + H₂O):

$$\text{BF}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^{\oplus}[\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus}$$

(ii) Electrophilic attack on styrene:

$$\text{H}^{\oplus}[\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CHC}_6\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{H}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)[\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus}$$

The carbocation is stabilised by **resonance** with the phenyl ring (benzylic stabilisation).

**Step 2: Propagation**

$$\sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{H}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)[\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus} + \text{CH}_2=\text{CHC}_6\text{H}_5 \xrightarrow{k_p} \sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{H}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)[\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus}$$

**Step 3: Termination (spontaneous, ion-pair collapse)**

$$\sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}\text{H}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)[\text{BF}_3\text{OH}]^{\ominus} \xrightarrow{k_t} \sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{OH} + \text{BF}_3$$

**Step 4: Rate expressions**

$$R_i = k_i [\text{C}][\text{M}] \quad ; \quad R_p = k_p [\text{M}^+][\text{M}] \quad ; \quad R_t = k_t [\text{M}^+]$$

**Step 5: Steady-state assumption** $R_i = R_t$:

$$k_i [\text{C}][\text{M}] = k_t [\text{M}^+] \Rightarrow [\text{M}^+] = \dfrac{k_i [\text{C}][\text{M}]}{k_t}$$

**Step 6: Overall $R_p$**

$$R_p = k_p [\text{M}^+][\text{M}] = \dfrac{k_p k_i}{k_t} [\text{C}][\text{M}]^2$$

**Conclusion:** $R_p$ is **first order in [C]** and **second order in [M]**, a diagnostic feature of cationic polymerisation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Derive the expression for the number-average degree of polymerisation ($\bar{X}_n$) in cationic polymerisation, (i) without chain transfer, and (ii) with chain transfer to monomer. What is the Mayo plot and how is it used to determine the chain transfer constant $C_M$?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Case (i): No chain transfer**

A chain is terminated only by spontaneous ion-pair collapse. Hence, the **kinetic chain length** equals $\bar{X}_n$:

$$\bar{X}_n = \nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t} = \dfrac{k_p [\text{M}^+][\text{M}]}{k_t [\text{M}^+]} = \dfrac{k_p [\text{M}]}{k_t}$$

**Case (ii): With chain transfer to monomer (CTM)**

The chain stops via either $R_t$ (termination) or $R_{tr,M} = k_{tr,M}[\text{M}^+][\text{M}]$ (transfer). The number of chain-stopping events per unit time is $R_t + R_{tr,M}$, and monomers consumed in propagation per unit time is $R_p$. Thus:

$$\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{R_t + R_{tr,M}}{R_p} = \dfrac{k_t[\text{M}^+] + k_{tr,M}[\text{M}^+][\text{M}]}{k_p[\text{M}^+][\text{M}]}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{k_t}{k_p[\text{M}]} + \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p} = \dfrac{k_t}{k_p[\text{M}]} + C_M$$

**Mayo Plot:**

A graph of $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n}$ (y-axis) vs $\dfrac{1}{[\text{M}]}$ (x-axis) is plotted using experimental data at varying monomer concentrations.

- **Slope** = $\dfrac{k_t}{k_p}$ → from which $k_{tr,M}$ can be found if $k_t$ is known.
- **Intercept** = $C_M = \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$ → directly gives the chain transfer constant to monomer.

The Mayo plot is therefore a **linear diagnostic tool** to extract two fundamental kinetic ratios from a single set of $\bar{X}_n$ measurements.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — "Industrial Cationic Polymerisation: Butyl Rubber"
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Describe the industrial cationic polymerisation of isobutylene to produce polyisobutylene (PIB) and butyl rubber. Discuss the role of co-catalyst, the choice of temperature, and the influence on molecular weight."**

**Model Solution:**

**Polyisobutylene (PIB):**
- Monomer: isobutylene, $\text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2$ (highly electron-rich, gives tert-carbocation).
- Initiator system: **BF₃ + trace H₂O** (or alcohol).
- Temperature: **–80 to –100 °C** (in liquid ethylene or propane diluent).
- Mechanism: classic cationic with very low $T$ to suppress chain transfer to monomer.

**Butyl rubber (IIR):**
- Monomer: isobutylene (95–99%) + isoprene (1–5%) as a comonomer (provides unsaturation for sulphur vulcanisation).
- Initiator: **AlCl₃ + trace HCl** in CH₃Cl (methyl chloride) solvent.
- Temperature: **–95 to –100 °C** (slurry polymerisation in liquid ethylene).
- Product: a copolymer with random placement of isoprene units (~1 double bond per 100 repeat units), enabling vulcanisation.

**Role of co-catalyst:**
The Lewis acid alone (BF₃, AlCl₃) is **inactive**. A trace of protogenic co-catalyst (H₂O, HCl, ROH) is **absolutely essential** to form the protonated initiating complex. Without it, no polymerisation occurs — this is the diagnostic feature of cationic polymerisation.

**Choice of low temperature:**
- $R_p$ is only weakly temperature-dependent (low $E_a$), so reducing T from 0 °C to –100 °C reduces $R_p$ modestly.
- Chain transfer to monomer has a **higher** $E_a$, so its rate drops **dramatically** at low T.
- Net effect: very high $\bar{X}_n$ (typically 50,000 to 500,000 for PIB).

**Effect on molecular weight:**
- Low T + high [M] + low [C] → highest $\bar{X}_n$.
- Industrial PIB grades span MW 10,000 (lubricant additives) to >500,000 (chewing gum base), tuned by T and catalyst loading.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Explain with suitable equations the phenomenon of chain transfer to monomer in cationic polymerisation. How does it affect $\bar{X}_n$? Discuss a method to suppress chain transfer in the industrial manufacture of high-MW polyisobutylene."**

**Model Solution:**

**Chain transfer to monomer (CTM):**

The growing carbocation abstracts a hydride (or a proton) from a fresh monomer molecule, terminating itself but generating a new active centre:

$$\sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}(\text{CH}_3)_2 + \text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2 \longrightarrow \sim\sim \text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3 + \text{CH}_3-\overset{\oplus}{\text{C}}(\text{CH}_3)_2$$

The **original chain** ends with an internal double bond (a dead unsaturated polymer) and the **new active centre** starts a fresh chain.

**Effect on $\bar{X}_n$:**

Each CTM event **caps the growth of the original chain** without consuming a propagating species, so the **average chain length decreases**:

$$\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \underbrace{\dfrac{k_t}{k_p[\text{M}]}}_{\text{termination}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}}_{C_M}$$

As temperature rises, $k_{tr,M}/k_p$ increases (because $E_{tr,M} > E_p$ typically), so $\bar{X}_n$ **falls sharply** with T.

**Methods to suppress CTM (industrial practice for high-MW PIB):**

1. **Very low polymerisation temperature** (–80 to –100 °C): preferentially reduces $k_{tr,M}$ relative to $k_p$ due to the higher activation energy of CTM.

2. **Use of highly active Lewis acid + carefully controlled co-catalyst**: minimises the number of initiating centres, slowing initiation to a rate comparable with slow propagation, allowing each chain to grow long before being capped.

3. **High monomer concentration**: increasing [M] raises $R_p$ faster than $R_{tr,M}$ (since $R_p \propto [\text{M}]^2$ and $R_{tr,M} \propto [\text{M}]$), shifting the Mayo equation in favour of longer chains.

4. **Use of solvent with high dielectric constant and strong cation solvation** (e.g., CH₂Cl₂, CH₃Cl): this stabilises the carbocation as a more separated ion pair, reducing its tendency to abstract from monomer.

5. **Living cationic polymerisation (LCP)** by Kennedy, Sawamoto, Higashimura: addition of a **nucleophilic additive** (e.g., dioxane, ethyl acetate, DMSO) reversibly caps the active centre as a "dormant" covalent species, dramatically suppressing both termination and CTM. This enables synthesis of **narrow-polydispersity, end-functional, and block** polyisobutylenes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall / Mark Loss Zones (KTU 2024):**
> 1. Forgetting to mention that the **Lewis acid alone is not the initiator** — the co-catalyst is essential. Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 2. Writing $R_t = k_t[\text{M}^+]^2$ (radical-type bimolecular termination) instead of $R_t = k_t[\text{M}^+]$ (unimolecular ion collapse) — this changes the entire kinetic order.
> 3. Confusing **chain transfer** with **termination** — they are different: CTM *regenerates* an active centre; termination *destroys* it.
> 4. Skipping the steady-state assumption step in derivations — KTU valuators explicitly check for the phrase "applying SSA, $R_i = R_t$" and the subsequent substitution.
> 5. Not using proper `mod` or `\vert` notation for absolute value / modulus in exam scripts.
> 6. Forgetting the **regioselectivity** (Markovnikov) when drawing the propagation step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cationic Polymerization (Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Definition:** Chain-growth polymerisation with a **carbocation** as the active centre, initiated by a **Lewis acid + protogenic co-catalyst** complex.
- **Suitable monomers:** Electron-rich vinyl monomers — isobutylene, styrene, vinyl ethers (methyl, ethyl), N-vinyl carbazole, coumarone, indene, 1,3-dienes. Monomer order of reactivity: vinyl ethers > isobutylene > styrene > butadiene.
- **Initiator systems:** BF₃, AlCl₃, SnCl₄, TiCl₄, SbCl₅ — each with a co-catalyst (H₂O, HCl, ROH, RCOOH).
- **Co-catalyst is mandatory** — Lewis acid alone is inactive.
- **Mechanism stages:** Initiation → Propagation → Termination (spontaneous, unimolecular) OR Chain transfer (to monomer, solvent, polymer, CTA).
- **Kinetics:** $R_p = \dfrac{k_p k_i}{k_t}[\text{C}][\text{M}]^2$ — diagnostic **second order in [M]**.
- **Termination order:** $R_t = k_t[\text{M}^+]$ — **unimolecular** ion-pair collapse (NOT bimolecular like radical).
- **$\bar{X}_n$ (no transfer):** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p[\text{M}]}{k_t}$.
- **$\bar{X}_n$ (with CTM):** $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{k_t}{k_p[\text{M}]} + C_M$ — **Mayo equation**.
- **Mayo plot:** $1/\bar{X}_n$ vs $1/[\text{M}]$ — slope gives $k_t/k_p$, intercept gives $C_M$.
- **Temperature effect:** $E_a$ is small (4–8 kJ/mol); chain transfer has higher $E_a$, so **lower T → higher MW**.
- **Industrial examples:** PIB (–100 °C, BF₃/H₂O), Butyl rubber (AlCl₃/HCl, –100 °C), poly(vinyl ether)s, coumarone-indene resins, petroleum resins.
- **Regioselectivity:** Markovnikov addition — cation goes to **less-substituted** carbon, positive charge on **more substituted** (more stable) carbon.
- **Solvent:** Polar, non-nucleophilic (CH₂Cl₂, CH₃Cl, liquid SO₂, toluene); polar solvents help ion separation, increasing $k_p$.
- **Counter-ion role:** Tight ion pair → slow propagation; loose/solvent-separated ion pair → fast propagation; very loose (free ion) → fastest but less controllable.
- **Living cationic polymerisation (LCP):** Use of nucleophilic additives (dioxane, EtOAc) to make dormant covalent species, enabling narrow MWD and block copolymers. Pioneers: **Kennedy, Sawamoto, Higashimura**.
- **Diagnostic tests for cationic mechanism:** (a) $R_p \propto [\text{M}]^2$, (b) Inhibition by bases/nucleophiles (water, pyridine, ethers), (c) acceleration by electron-donating solvents, (d) sensitivity to trace co-catalyst.
- **Comparison:** Cationic ↔ Anionic are mirror images; Cationic uses electron-rich monomers, anionic uses electron-poor monomers; both are ionic chain polymerisations.
- **Activation energy order:** Cationic (≈ 4–8) < Anionic (≈ 0–10) ≪ Radical (≈ 80–100) kJ/mol.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — CATIONIC POLYMERIZATION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Anionic Polymerization** is a sub-class of **chain-growth (addition) polymerization** in which the active propagating chain-end carries a **negative charge (carbanion)**. The chain is initiated by electron transfer or by a strong nucleophile/anionic species, and propagation occurs by the nucleophilic attack of the carbanion on the carbon–carbon double bond of an electron-deficient vinyl monomer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim standard):**
> *"Anionic polymerization is a chain polymerization in which the active centre is a carbanion generated by the action of an anionic initiator (such as organolithium, alkali metal amide, or alkali metal/naphthalene complex) on a vinyl monomer bearing an electron-withdrawing substituent."*

Key identifying features:
- **Active centre:** Carbanion (R–CH₂⁻ M⁺) — strongly nucleophilic.
- **Co-catalyst / counter-ion:** Alkali metal cation (Li⁺, Na⁺, K⁺, Cs⁺).
- **Monomer requirement:** Electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) such as –C₆H₅, –CN, –COOR, –COR, –CH=CH₂ (conjugated dienes).
- **Solvent:** Aprotic, non-protic (THF, benzene, toluene, hexane, dioxane) — **protic solvents (water, alcohols, amines) terminate the chain instantly.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Relentless Sniper"

Imagine a **sniper** (the initiator anion) loaded with a magazine of bullets (monomer molecules):

1. The sniper **locks onto one vinyl monomer** and fires — the bullet (carbanion) opens the C=C double bond, embedding itself into the target.
2. The bullet becomes a *new* sniper aimed at the *next* monomer.
3. Each shot is **deliberate, controlled, and never misses** — because no termination reaction is occurring (in living anionic systems).
4. The moment a "civilian" (impurity like water or oxygen) walks into the firing range, the chain is **killed instantly**.

This is exactly what an anionic polymerization does: it propagates in a **living** manner because, in the absence of impurities, the carbanion end **never stops hunting for monomers**.

> [!NOTE]
> The landmark discovery of **"Living Anionic Polymerization"** was made by **M. Szwarc (1956)** at Syracuse University. He demonstrated that, in rigorously purified systems, polymer chains do not terminate and can resume propagation upon addition of fresh monomer — opening the door to **block copolymers, star polymers, and narrow-polydispersity materials**.

## 1.3 Classification of Anionic Polymerization

| Class | Initiator Type | Medium | Solubility | Example |
|-------|---------------|--------|------------|---------|
| **Heterogeneous** | Bulk metal (e.g., Na, K) | Bulk / Slurry | Insoluble metal | Na in styrene |
| **Homogeneous (Organometallic)** | R–Li, RMgX, R₂Zn | Solution | Soluble | n-BuLi in THF |
| **Homogeneous (Complex / Electron Transfer)** | Na/Naphthalene, K/Naphthalene | THF, DME | Soluble radical anion | Na⁺[C₁₀H₈]•⁻ |

## 1.4 Why It Matters — Engineering Utility

- **Telechelic polymers** (end-functionalized) for polyurethane and epoxy networks.
- **Styrene–Butadiene–Styrene (SBS) and SBR rubbers** — industrially the largest tonnage anionic product.
- **Kraton® thermoplastic elastomers** (Shell Chemicals).
- **Living anionic polymerization** is the *only* method capable of producing polymers with **Polydispersity Index (PDI) ≈ 1.0**, making it indispensable for advanced material design.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** Always mention "**living character**" and "**absence of termination/chain transfer**" in any 14-mark anionic question — this is the **single most important KTU board keyword**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Mechanism (The Three Canonical Stages)

### Stage 1 — Initiation

The initiator (I) reacts with one monomer unit, generating the first carbanion.

**Pathway A — Direct Nucleophilic Addition (Organometallic Initiators):**

$$\text{R–M} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHX} \longrightarrow \text{R–CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{C}}\text{HX}^{-}\,\text{M}^{+}$$

**Pathway B — Electron Transfer (Alkali Metal/Aromatic Complexes):**

$$\text{Na} + \text{Naphthalene} \longrightarrow \text{Na}^{+}[\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8}]^{\bullet -}$$

$$\text{Na}^{+}[\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8}]^{\bullet -} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHX} \longrightarrow \text{Na}^{+-}\text{CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{C}}\text{HX} + \text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Electron-transfer initiation** is the *only* route that allows polymerization of monomers whose substituent X is **mildly electron-withdrawing** (e.g., styrene, butadiene). It also gives rise to **"dianion"** initiators that propagate from **both ends** — a clever Szwarc trick.

### Stage 2 — Propagation

The carbanion end of the growing chain attacks the C=C of the next monomer. The negative charge **resides on the chain end at all times** — a "living" carbanion.

$$\text{R–[CH}_2{-}\text{CHX]}_n{-}\text{CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{C}}\text{HX}^{-}\,\text{M}^{+} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHX}$$

$$\longrightarrow \text{R–[CH}_2{-}\text{CHX]}_{n+1}{-}\text{CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{C}}\text{HX}^{-}\,\text{M}^{+}$$

**Why no termination?**
- No free radicals to combine.
- No chain transfer to monomer (carbanion is too nucleophilic, monomer cannot abstract).
- **Provided the medium is rigorously free of O₂, H₂O, CO₂, and protic impurities.**

### Stage 3 — Termination (Intentional, by Design)

The chemist **deliberately kills** the chain using a proton donor (e.g., methanol, water) or a coupling agent (e.g., CO₂, epichlorohydrin, SiCl₄ — for star polymers).

$$\text{R–[CH}_2{-}\text{CHX]}_n{-}\text{CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{C}}\text{HX}^{-}\,\text{M}^{+} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{R–[CH}_2{-}\text{CHX]}_n{-}\text{CH}_2{-}\text{CH}_2\text{X} + \text{CH}_3\text{O}^{-}\text{M}^{+}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In industrial anionic synthesis (e.g., SBR), an **external terminator is NOT used** — instead the living carbanion is reacted with a coupling agent (SiCl₄, epoxidized soy-bean oil) to form a **multi-arm star polymer** which dramatically improves processability.

## 2.2 Solvent Polarity and Ion-Pairing — The KTU Favourite

The **ion pair** between the carbanion and the counter-cation can exist in three forms:

| Form | Symbol | Nature | Solvent |
|------|--------|--------|---------|
| **Contact ion pair** | R⁻/M⁺ | Tight, no solvent interposed | Non-polar (hexane, toluene) |
| **Solvent-separated ion pair** | R⁻ ∥ M⁺ | Loosely associated | Moderately polar (THF, Et₂O) |
| **Free ions** | R⁻ + M⁺ | Completely dissociated | Highly polar + high dielectric |

Each species has its **own propagation rate constant**: $k_{p,\text{free}} > k_{p,\text{ssip}} > k_{p,\text{cp}}$.

- In **non-polar solvents** (hexane): propagation is slow, but **stereoregular (e.g., 1,4-cis-PBD)**.
- In **polar solvents** (THF): propagation is fast, but **stereochemistry is lost (mix of 1,2/1,4)**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Why is THF used for anionic polymerization of styrene despite its high chain-transfer constant to solvent?"* — Answer: In anionic polymerization, **there is no chain transfer to solvent** because the carbanion does not abstract a proton from THF. THF is chosen purely for its **high dielectric constant (ε = 7.6)** which promotes ion-pair dissociation and faster propagation.

## 2.3 Monomer Suitability

A monomer is polymerizable anionically **only if** its vinyl group is **electron-deficient** (i.e., the substituent stabilizes the carbanion by resonance and/or induction).

| Monomer | Substituent X | Stabilization Mechanism |
|---------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Styrene | –C₆H₅ | Resonance (benzylic carbanion) |
| α-Methylstyrene | –C(CH₃)= | Resonance + Hyperconjugation |
| 1,3-Butadiene | –CH=CH₂ | Allylic carbanion (resonance) |
| Isoprene | –C(CH₃)=CH– | Allylic carbanion |
| Methyl methacrylate | –COOCH₃ | Inductive + Resonance |
| Acrylonitrile | –CN | Inductive (strong –I) |
| Vinylidene cyanide | –(CN)₂ | Strong inductive |
| Vinyl ethers (CH₂=CH–OR) | –OR | Resonance (oxocarbenium) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often claim vinyl chloride or ethylene *can* be polymerized anionically. **They cannot** — the substituents (–Cl, –H) are either weakly deactivating or weakly activating, and the carbanion is not sufficiently stabilized. Such monomers are exclusively **radical or Ziegler-Natta** polymerized.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Conditions / Units |
|---|----------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Rate of initiation | $R_i = k_i[I][M]$ | $k_i$ in L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 2 | Rate of propagation | $R_p = k_p[M^-][M]$ | $M^-$ = living carbanion conc. |
| 3 | Rate of polymerization | $R_p = k_p [M] \sum [M^-]$ | Quasi-steady state on $M^-$ |
| 4 | Number-average DP (no termination) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0}$ | Living system, no chain transfer |
| 5 | Number-average DP (with chain transfer) | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0 + [T]}$ | T = chain-transfer agent |
| 6 | Molecular weight (Szwarc equation) | $\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{m_{[M]}\cdot[M]_0}{[I]_0}$ | $m_{[M]}$ = molar mass of monomer repeat unit |
| 7 | Polydispersity index (ideal living) | $\text{PDI} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n}$ | Approaches **1.0** as $\bar{X}_n \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Activation energy (styrene/n-BuLi) | $E_a \approx 60\text{–}75$ kJ mol⁻¹ | THF, 25 °C |
| 9 | Average lifetime of carbanion | Hours to months | Under vacuum, sealed system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Szwarc equation** ($\bar{M}_n = m \cdot [M]_0 / [I]_0$) is the **MOST frequently tested formula** in KTU ESE. It is the direct mathematical proof of living character — molecular weight can be **predetermined stoichiometrically**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why Anionic Polymerization is Industrial

1. **Narrow molecular-weight distribution** (PDI ≈ 1.01–1.10) — critical for calibration standards (NIST-grade PS, PE).
2. **Block copolymer synthesis** — sequential monomer addition creates SBS, SIS, SEBS, and PS-b-PMMA.
3. **Functional end-groups** — by using functional initiators (e.g., 3-(tert-butyldimethylsilyloxy)-1-propyllithium), one can place a **protected hydroxyl** at the chain end.
4. **Diene rubbers (SBR, BR, IR)** — over **5 million tonnes/year** are made using anionic catalysis.
5. **Low temperature operation** (–78 °C to +50 °C) — no thermal degradation of sensitive monomers.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Number-Average Degree of Polymerization ($\bar{X}_n$)

**Given:** Living anionic polymerization of a monomer M with initiator I. No chain transfer, no termination.

**Assumptions:**
1. Every initiator molecule gives **one** living chain.
2. Every monomer molecule is consumed in propagation (no side reactions).
3. Conversion = 100% (in the idealized living case).

**Step 1 — Number of moles of growing chains**

Each initiator I generates one active chain $M^-$. The total number of growing chains = number of initiator molecules consumed.

$$N_{\text{chains}} = [I]_0 \cdot V$$

where $V$ is the reaction volume in litres and $[I]_0$ is the initial initiator concentration (mol L⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Number of moles of monomer consumed**

If conversion is $p$ (fraction of monomer reacted):

$$N_{\text{monomer consumed}} = p \cdot [M]_0 \cdot V$$

**Step 3 — Definition of $\bar{X}_n$**

By definition, the number-average degree of polymerization equals the number of monomer units per chain (averaged over all chains):

$$\bar{X}_n \equiv \dfrac{\text{moles of monomer consumed}}{\text{moles of chains}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting**

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{p \cdot [M]_0 \cdot V}{[I]_0 \cdot V}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = p \cdot \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0}}$$

**Step 5 — Special case: Complete conversion ($p \to 1$)**

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Szwarc Living Polymerization Equation**. The molar ratio $[M]_0/[I]_0$ is therefore called the **stoichiometric DP ratio** — a designer knob that the chemist can dial in *before* the reaction.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Molecular Weight $\bar{M}_n$ from Stoichiometry

**Step 1 — Mass of polymer formed**

Assuming $p = 1$ (complete conversion):

$$m_{\text{polymer}} = [M]_0 \cdot V \cdot m_0$$

where $m_0$ is the molar mass of one monomer repeat unit (e.g., 104.15 g mol⁻¹ for styrene).

**Step 2 — Number of moles of chains**

Same as above: $N_{\text{chains}} = [I]_0 \cdot V$.

**Step 3 — Number-average molecular weight (definition)**

$$\bar{M}_n \equiv \dfrac{\text{total mass of polymer}}{\text{total moles of chains}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{polymer}}}{N_{\text{chains}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting**

$$\bar{M}_n = \dfrac{[M]_0 \cdot V \cdot m_0}{[I]_0 \cdot V}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{M}_n = m_0 \cdot \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

Styrene ($m_0 = 104.15$ g mol⁻¹) is polymerized anionically at –30 °C in THF using n-BuLi as initiator. Given:
- $[M]_0 = 1.0$ mol L⁻¹
- $[I]_0 = 0.005$ mol L⁻¹
- $V = 2.0$ L
- Conversion $p = 1.0$

Compute $\bar{X}_n$, $\bar{M}_n$, and the mass of polymer.

**Solution:**

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1.0}{0.005} = 200$$

$$\bar{M}_n = 104.15 \times 200 = 20{,}830 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

$$m_{\text{polymer}} = 1.0 \times 2.0 \times 104.15 = 208.3 \text{ g}$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Rate of Polymerization Under Steady-State

**Assumption:** After a short initiation period, all initiator is consumed and the concentration of active centres $[M^-]$ is constant and equal to $[I]_0$ (in the absence of termination/chain transfer).

**Step 1 — Definition of $R_p$**

$$R_p \equiv -\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M^-][M]$$

**Step 2 — Quasi-steady-state (or simply "no termination")**

$$[M^-] = [I]_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3 — Integrated form**

$$\int_{[M]_0}^{[M]} \dfrac{d[M]}{[M]} = -k_p [I]_0 \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln \dfrac{[M]_0}{[M]} = k_p [I]_0 \, t$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $[M]$**

$$[M](t) = [M]_0 \exp(-k_p [I]_0 \, t)$$

**Step 5 — First-order kinetics in monomer**

The plot of $\ln\left([M]_0/[M]\right)$ vs $t$ is a **straight line with slope $k_p[I]_0$** — a hallmark of living anionic polymerization (and indeed of any "no-termination" system).

> [!TIP]
> This **linear semi-log plot** is what experimentally confirms the "living" character. If the plot curves or plateaus, either termination or chain transfer has occurred.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Block Copolymer Formation

A **diblock copolymer A-b-B** is synthesized by sequential anionic polymerization.

**Step 1 — Synthesize block A**

Initiate monomer A (e.g., styrene) with initiator I. After complete conversion, the chain is $I-A_n^-$.

$$\bar{X}_n^{(A)} = \dfrac{[A]_0}{[I]_0}$$

**Step 2 — Add monomer B (e.g., isoprene)**

The living carbanion of block A nucleophilically attacks monomer B, propagating into block B.

$$\bar{X}_n^{(B)} = \dfrac{[B]_0}{[I]_0}$$

**Step 3 — Overall DP and $M_n$**

$$\bar{X}_n^{(\text{total})} = \bar{X}_n^{(A)} + \bar{X}_n^{(B)} = \dfrac{[A]_0 + [B]_0}{[I]_0}$$

$$\bar{M}_n^{(\text{total})} = m_0^{(A)} \cdot \bar{X}_n^{(A)} + m_0^{(B)} \cdot \bar{X}_n^{(B)}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Synthesis of PS-b-PI using s-BuLi in cyclohexane at 40 °C.
- $[I]_0 = 0.01$ mol L⁻¹
- Styrene added: $[A]_0 = 1.0$ mol L⁻¹, $m_0^{(A)} = 104.15$ g mol⁻¹
- Isoprene added: $[B]_0 = 1.5$ mol L⁻¹, $m_0^{(B)} = 68.12$ g mol⁻¹

$$\bar{X}_n^{(A)} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.01} = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_A = 10{,}415 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

$$\bar{X}_n^{(B)} = \dfrac{1.5}{0.01} = 150 \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_B = 10{,}218 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

$$\bar{M}_n^{(\text{total})} = 10{,}415 + 10{,}218 = 20{,}633 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Anionic vs. Radical vs. Cationic (KTU Comparison Table)

| Feature | Anionic | Radical | Cationic |
|---------|---------|---------|----------|
| Active centre | Carbanion R⁻ | Radical R• | Carbanion R⁺ |
| Initiator | n-BuLi, Na/Naph | AIBN, BPO | BF₃·OEt₂, H₂SO₄ |
| Monomer substituent | **EWG** (–CN, –Ph) | Wide range | **EDG** (–OR, –CH₃) |
| Termination | **None (living)** | Combination/Disproportionation | Combination with counter-ion |
| PDI achievable | **≈ 1.0–1.1** | 1.5–2.0 | 1.5–3.0 |
| Living character | **Yes (Szwarc)** | No (ATRP, RAFT — pseudo-living) | No |
| Temp. range | –80 to +50 °C | 50–100 °C | –80 to 0 °C |
| Solvent | Aprotic | Wide | Polar, low $T$ |
| Block copolymer? | **Yes (sequential)** | Difficult (SFRP) | Limited |
| Industrial example | **SBS, SBR** | LDPE, PS | Butyl rubber, polyisobutylene |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most condensed high-yield revision tool** for any anionic question that comes paired with radical/cationic in Part B.

## 4.3 Schematic — Ion-Pair Equilibria in Anionic Polymerization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cp[Contact Ion Pair: R-M]:::cpBox
    ssip[Solvent-Separated Ion Pair: R || M]:::ssipBox
    free[Free Ions: R- and M+]:::freeBox

    solvent2[Moderately Polar: THF Et2O]:::solBox
    solvent3[Highly Polar with high dielectric]:::solBox

    solvent1 --> cp
    solvent2 --> ssip
    solvent3 --> free

    cp <-->|Equilibrium K1| ssip
    ssip <-->|Equilibrium K2| free

    kp1[Small kp, high stereoregularity]:::rateBox
    kp2[Medium kp]:::rateBox

    cp --> kp1
    ssip --> kp2
    free --> kp3

    classDef cpBox fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c

```

> [!NOTE]
> The **three states of the ion pair** are in **dynamic equilibrium**, and the observed $k_p$ is a weighted average: $k_p^{\text{obs}} = \alpha k_{p,\text{cp}} + \beta k_{p,\text{ssip}} + \gamma k_{p,\text{free}}$, where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ are the mole fractions of each species.
