# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define anionic polymerization. Why are vinyl ethers polymerizable by cationic but not anionic mechanisms?*

**Model Answer:**

Anionic polymerization is a chain-growth polymerization in which the active centre is a carbanion generated by the action of a strong nucleophile (initiator) on a vinyl monomer bearing an electron-withdrawing substituent.

Vinyl ethers (CH₂=CH–OR) have an **alkoxy group (–OR)**, which is a strong **electron-donating group (EDG)** by resonance. This EDG **destabilizes a carbanion** (would localize extra negative charge on an already electron-rich carbon) but **stabilizes a carbocation** (forms a resonance-stabilized oxocarbenium ion). Therefore, vinyl ethers are cationic-monomers and **cannot** be polymerized anionically.

*(Examiner's note: 3-mark answer should fit in 1 page; write definition in 2 lines, justification in 4–5 lines with structure.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is meant by "living" polymerization? Mention any two characteristics.*

**Model Answer:**

A polymerization is said to be **"living"** when, after complete monomer consumption, the active chain ends remain **dormant yet reactive** — they neither terminate nor undergo chain transfer, and resume propagation upon addition of fresh monomer.

**Two characteristics:**
1. The polydispersity index (PDI = $\bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$) is very low, approaching **1.0** for high-DP systems.
2. The number-average molecular weight can be **predetermined** by the stoichiometric ratio $\bar{M}_n = m_0 \cdot [M]_0 / [I]_0$ (Szwarc equation), and increases **linearly** with monomer conversion.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a suitable example, explain the mechanism of anionic polymerization initiated by an organolithium compound. Discuss the role of solvent polarity on the active centre and on the stereochemistry of the resulting polymer.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Derive the Szwarc equation $\bar{M}_n = m_0 \cdot [M]_0 / [I]_0$ for a living anionic polymerization. Using this equation, calculate $\bar{M}_n$ and $\bar{X}_n$ for the polymerization of styrene (molar mass = 104.15 g mol⁻¹) initiated with n-BuLi at $[M]_0 = 2.0$ mol L⁻¹ and $[I]_0 = 0.01$ mol L⁻¹.*

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Mechanism with n-BuLi (organolithium initiator):**

*Step 1 — Initiation:* n-Butyllithium is a strong nucleophile. It attacks the β-carbon of the vinyl group of styrene, opening the C=C and forming a benzylic carbanion paired with Li⁺:

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_9{-}\text{Li} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CH}{-}\text{C}_6\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_9{-}\text{CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{CH}}{-}\text{C}_6\text{H}_5^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+}$$

The carbanion is **resonance-stabilized** by the phenyl ring.

*Step 2 — Propagation:* The carbanion end attacks another styrene monomer, shifting the active centre to the new chain end:

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_9{-}\text{PS}_n^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHPh} \longrightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_9{-}\text{PS}_{n+1}^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+}$$

*Step 3 — Termination (intentional):* Addition of methanol quenches the chain:

$$\text{C}_4\text{H}_9{-}\text{PS}_n^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_4\text{H}_9{-}\text{PS}_n{-}\text{H} + \text{CH}_3\text{O}^{-}\text{Li}^{+}$$

**Role of Solvent Polarity:**

The active centre exists as an **ion pair** R⁻/Li⁺, and the polarity of the solvent controls how tightly the Li⁺ is held:

- **Non-polar solvents (hexane, toluene):** Li⁺ remains in **contact ion pair** state. The active centre is highly aggregated (e.g., (RLi)₆ in hydrocarbon). Polymerization is slow but the **stereoregularity is excellent** (e.g., 1,4-cis-polybutadiene up to 95% in non-polar medium with Li⁺ counter-ion).
- **Polar aprotic solvents (THF):** The ion pair is **solvent-separated**, dissociating to free ions. Polymerization is fast (10²–10⁴ times faster), but the **stereoregularity drops** (random 1,2/1,4 placements).

This is industrially exploited: anionic SBR synthesized in non-polar media has a high cis-1,4 content (rubbery), while in THF the microstructure is mixed.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

*Derivation:* Refer to **Section 3.1 and 3.2** of these notes.

$$\bar{X}_n = p \cdot \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0} \quad ; \quad \bar{M}_n = m_0 \cdot \dfrac{[M]_0}{[I]_0}$$

*Numerical calculation:*

Given $m_0 = 104.15$ g mol⁻¹, $[M]_0 = 2.0$ mol L⁻¹, $[I]_0 = 0.01$ mol L⁻¹, $p = 1$.

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{2.0}{0.01} = 200$$

$$\bar{M}_n = 104.15 \times 200 = 20{,}830 \text{ g mol}^{-1} = 20.83 \text{ kg mol}^{-1}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Szwarc electron-transfer mechanism for the anionic polymerization of styrene using sodium/naphthalene initiator. What is the advantage of this initiator over n-BuLi?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Discuss in detail the synthesis of a styrene–isoprene–styrene (SIS) triblock copolymer using living anionic polymerization. Sketch the GPC and DSC outputs expected for such a polymer.*

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Szwarc Electron-Transfer Mechanism:**

*Step 1 — Formation of the radical anion of naphthalene:*

$$\text{Na}^{\circ} + \text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8 \longrightarrow \text{Na}^{+}\,[\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8]^{\bullet -}$$

The single electron from sodium is transferred to the LUMO of naphthalene, creating the **deep-green** naphthalenide radical anion in THF.

*Step 2 — Electron transfer to monomer:*

$$\text{Na}^{+}\,[\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8]^{\bullet -} + \text{CH}_2{=}\text{CHPh} \longrightarrow \text{Na}^{+-}\text{CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{CH}}\text{Ph}^{\bullet} + \text{C}_{10}\text{H}_8$$

The monomer radical anion is generated, and naphthalene is **regenerated** (regenerating the green color).

*Step 3 — Radical coupling (dianion formation):*

$$2\,\text{Na}^{+-}\text{CH}_2{-}\overset{..}{\text{CH}}\text{Ph}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{Na}^{+-}\text{CH}_2{-}\text{CHPh}{-}\text{CHPh}{-}\text{CH}_2^{-}\text{Na}^{+}$$

Two radical anions **couple** to form a **dianion** (a dicarbanion) — the *signature* of the Szwarc method. The dianion has **two active ends** and propagates in **both directions** simultaneously.

*Step 4 — Propagation:*

$$\text{^{\-}CH}_2{-}\text{[CHPh]}_n{-}\text{CH}_2^{-} + \text{more monomer} \longrightarrow \text{growing dianion}$$

*Step 5 — Termination (protonation):*

$$\text{^{\-}chain}^{\,-} + 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{H{-}chain{-}H} + 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{O}^{-}\text{Na}^{+}$$

**Advantage of Na/Naphthalene over n-BuLi:**

- A **dianion** is produced in a single step, leading to **simultaneous growth from both chain ends** — this **doubles the molecular weight** at a given initiator concentration and gives a **narrow PDI** of 1.0–1.2.
- The method is **oxygen- and moisture-tolerant** to a slightly greater degree than free organolithiums, although both still require inert atmosphere.
- Allows polymerization of monomers that are **difficult to initiate** with n-BuLi (e.g., 1,1-diphenylethylene derivatives, vinyl naphthalene).
- n-BuLi, being monofunctional, gives a *mono*-carbanion, and produces one chain per initiator; Na/Naphthalene is **bifunctional**, and produces one chain per **half** initiator — i.e., $[I]_0$ effectively equals half the moles of Na/Naphthalene used.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Synthesis of SIS Triblock Copolymer:**

**Stage 1 — Initiation:**

Cyclohexane (an inert, non-polar solvent) is purified and degassed. s-BuLi (sec-butyllithium) is injected as initiator. The flask is sealed under high vacuum.

$$\text{s-BuLi} \longrightarrow \text{s-Bu}^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+}$$

**Stage 2 — First block (Polystyrene, PS):**

Styrene monomer is added. The s-Bu⁻ carbanion attacks styrene repeatedly. After complete conversion, the chain is:

$$\text{s-Bu}{-}\text{PS}_n^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+}$$

For a target $M_n$ of 15,000 g mol⁻¹ for PS block, with $m_0 = 104.15$:

$$\bar{X}_n^{(S)} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{104.15} \approx 144 \text{ units per PS block}$$

**Stage 3 — Middle block (Polyisoprene, PI):**

Isoprene is added. The PS–Li⁺ carbanion initiates isoprene polymerization. After full conversion:

$$\text{s-Bu}{-}\text{PS}_{144}{-}\text{PI}_m^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+}$$

For PI target $M_n$ of 50,000 g mol⁻¹, with $m_0 = 68.12$:

$$\bar{X}_n^{(I)} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{68.12} \approx 734 \text{ units of isoprene}$$

Microstructure in cyclohexane: ~93% 1,4-cis + 7% 3,4 — the high cis-1,4 content makes the PI block **elastomeric** (rubbery at room temperature).

**Stage 4 — Third block (Polystyrene, PS):**

A second charge of styrene is added. The chain grows into the second PS block:

$$\text{s-Bu}{-}\text{PS}_{144}{-}\text{PI}_{734}{-}\text{PS}_{144}^{-}\,\text{Li}^{+}$$

**Stage 5 — Termination:**

Methanol is added to terminate the chain:

$$\text{s-Bu}{-}\text{S}_{144}{-}\text{I}_{734}{-}\text{S}_{144}{-}\text{H} + \text{CH}_3\text{O}^{-}\text{Li}^{+}$$

Final $\bar{M}_n \approx 15{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 80{,}000$ g mol⁻¹.

**Expected GPC Trace:**

A single, **narrow, monomodal peak** (PDI = 1.05–1.10) centred at $M_n = 80{,}000$ g mol⁻¹. **No** low-molecular-weight tail and no high-molecular-weight shoulder (which would indicate premature termination or coupling, respectively).

**Expected DSC Trace:**

Two distinct glass transitions, characteristic of microphase-separated block copolymers:
- $T_g^{(PS)} \approx +100 \,^\circ\text{C}$ (PS end blocks)
- $T_g^{(PI)} \approx -60 \,^\circ\text{C}$ (PI middle block, rubbery)

The two $T_g$'s prove that the PS and PI segments are **microphase-separated** into separate nanodomains — PS acts as a hard physical crosslink, and PI as a soft rubbery matrix. This is what gives the **thermoplastic elastomer** behaviour of Kraton-type SIS.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in anionic polymerization questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing initiator types** — Writing "n-BuLi is a free-radical initiator" — **lose 2 marks immediately**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Living" keyword** — Any 14-mark question on anionic polymerization *must* mention that the polymerization is **living**, **without termination**, and that **$\bar{M}_n$ is stoichiometrically controlled** by $[M]_0/[I]_0$.
> 3. **Wrong solvent** — Writing that water/alcohol is used as solvent. These are **terminators**, not solvents. The carbanion is *instantly* killed by protic species.
> 4. **Wrong monomer example** — Giving ethylene or propylene as anionic monomers. These are **Ziegler-Natta** monomers. Use styrene, butadiene, isoprene, MMA, acrylonitrile.
> 5. **Forgetting the Szwarc equation in derivations** — The line $\bar{M}_n = m_0 \cdot [M]_0/[I]_0$ is **non-negotiable** for 7-mark derivation parts.
> 6. **Not drawing arrow-pushing (curly arrows)** — KTU examiners expect at least one fully drawn mechanism with **curved arrows** showing nucleophilic attack. Without arrows, 1–2 marks are typically docked.
> 7. **Polydispersity confusion** — PDI for ideal living is **1.0 + 1/$\bar{X}_n$**, not exactly 1.0. For $\bar{X}_n$ = 200, PDI = 1.005 — a subtle but common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation):**
>
> - **Anionic polymerization = chain-growth, carbanion active centre, electron-withdrawing monomer, aprotic solvent.**
> - **Szwarc (1956) discovered living anionic polymerization.**
> - **Initiators:** n-BuLi (homogeneous, monofunctional); Na/Naphthalene (homogeneous, bifunctional, dianion); Na/K metal (heterogeneous).
> - **Solvents:** Aprotic — THF, dioxane, benzene, toluene, hexane. **Never** water, alcohol, amine.
> - **Monomers:** Styrene, butadiene, isoprene, α-methylstyrene, MMA, acrylonitrile, vinyl ketones, vinylidene cyanide.
> - **No termination step in living systems** ⇒ **narrow PDI** (≈ 1.0–1.1).
> - **Szwarc equation:** $\bar{M}_n = m_0 \cdot [M]_0/[I]_0$ and $\bar{X}_n = [M]_0/[I]_0$.
> - **Ion pair forms:** Contact (R⁻/M⁺) ⇌ Solvent-separated (R⁻∥M⁺) ⇌ Free ions (R⁻ + M⁺).
> - **Solvent effect:** Non-polar → slow + high stereoregularity; Polar → fast + low stereoregularity.
> - **Block copolymers:** Sequential monomer addition to the living chain (SBS, SIS, SEBS, PS-b-PMMA).
> - **Termination:** Intentional addition of CH₃OH (proton donor) or coupling agent (SiCl₄ for star polymers).
> - **Industrial products:** SBR (styrene-butadiene rubber), BR (polybutadiene rubber), IR (polyisoprene), SBS/SIS thermoplastic elastomers (Kraton®).
> - **Anionic vs. Radical:** Anionic needs EWG monomer; radical works for both EWG and EDG monomers; anionic is living; radical is not.
> - **Anionic vs. Cationic:** Anionic needs EWG monomer; cationic needs EDG monomer; anionic is living; cationic is not.
> - **Kinetics:** $R_p = k_p [M^-][M]$, first-order in monomer, $\ln([M]_0/[M]) = k_p [I]_0 t$ (linear).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Co-Ordination Polymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Co-Ordination Polymerization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Co-Ordination Polymerization** is a specialized form of chain-growth (addition) polymerization in which the monomer molecule is first **chemisorbed (coordinated)** onto a vacant orbital site of a **transition metal complex catalyst**, followed by **insertion** into the growing polymer chain via a metal–carbon (M–C) bond. This process enables exceptional **stereochemical control** over the polymer microstructure.

The reaction is catalyzed by **heterogeneous multi-site catalysts** (e.g., Ziegler–Natta systems) or **homogeneous single-site catalysts** (e.g., metallocenes), and is the industrial method of choice for producing **stereoregular polyolefins** such as isotactic polypropylene (iPP) and linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights**
> - **Active Site (C\*)**: The coordinatively unsaturated transition metal center where chain growth occurs.
> - **Cocatalyst (Activator)**: An organoaluminum compound (e.g., AlEt₃, MAO) that alkylates and reduces the transition metal salt to form the active species.
> - **Stereospecificity**: The ability of the catalyst to orient each successive monomer in a defined geometric configuration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **molecular tailor's workstation**:

- The **transition metal atom** (e.g., Ti, Zr) is the **master tailor** sitting at a fixed workbench.
- The **workbench** has a fixed slot (the *vacant coordination site*) and a moving pin (the *growing polymer chain* attached to the metal).
- A **monomer molecule** (e.g., propylene) walks in, gets temporarily seated on the vacant slot, gets **stitched** to the moving pin (insertion step), and then the **pin moves back** to the vacant slot, regenerating the workbench for the next monomer.

> [!TIP]
> Think of it like a **"lock-and-key"** system: the catalyst's geometry (lock) decides which face of the monomer (key) gets inserted, producing either *isotactic*, *syndiotactic*, or *atactic* polymers.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Catalyst Loading**: typically **0.1–10 mg of Ti per gram of polymer**.
- **Activity (Productivity)**: measured as **kg of polymer per gram of transition metal (g-Ti)**.
- **Reaction Conditions**: Temperature **30–100 °C**, Pressure **1–40 atm** for slurry/gas-phase processes.
- **Cossee–Arlman Rate Constant (kₚ)**: Order of magnitude **10² to 10⁴ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹** for propylene insertion.
- **Alkylaluminum Cocatalyst**: Usually **Al(i-Bu)₃** or **AlEt₃** in Ti/Al molar ratio of **1:1 to 1:10**.

### 1.4 Classification of Co-ordination Polymerization

| Type | Catalyst Example | Polymerization Medium | Tacticity Control |
|------|------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Ziegler–Natta (Heterogeneous)** | TiCl₃ / AlEt₃ | Slurry / Gas phase | Isotactic PP |
| **Ziegler–Natta (Soluble)** | VOCl₃ / AlEt₂Cl | Solution | Ethylene–Propylene rubbers |
| **Metallocene (Single-Site)** | Cp₂ZrCl₂ / MAO | Solution / Gas phase | Isotactic / Syndiotactic (tunable) |
| **Post-Metallocene** | Constrained Geometry (CGC) | Solution | Ethylene–α-olefin copolymers |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Context & Natta's Contribution

- **Karl Ziegler (1953)**: Discovered that TiCl₄ + AlR₃ polymerizes ethylene at low pressure to give linear, high-density polyethylene (HDPE).
- **Giulio Natta (1954)**: Extended the system to **propylene** and discovered that the resulting polymer was **stereoregular (isotactic)**. Natta coined the terms *isotactic*, *syndiotactic*, and *atactic* based on X-ray diffraction patterns.
- Together they received the **Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1963)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Ziegler–Natta catalyst system marked the birth of the modern polyolefin industry. It enabled synthesis of polymers whose **stereochemistry, crystallinity, density, and mechanical properties** could be tailored precisely.

### 2.2 The Cossee–Arlman Monometallic Mechanism (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The **Cossee–Arlman mechanism** (1964) is the most widely accepted model for stereospecific insertion on heterogeneous Ziegler–Natta catalysts.

**Pre-requisites:**
1. The active site is a **surface-exposed transition metal ion** (e.g., Ti³⁺ on the lateral cleavage faces of α-TiCl₃).
2. The metal is in a **d¹ or d² electronic configuration** with at least **one vacant coordination site**.
3. The site is **alkylated** (M–R bond) by the cocatalyst.
4. The geometry around Ti is **octahedral** (coordination number = 6), but one position is vacant.

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. **Alkylation of Active Site**:
   - The cocatalyst AlR₃ transfers an alkyl group (R) to the surface Ti ion, forming **Ti–R** (active site, C\*).
   - Coordination number: 5 (one vacancy).

2. **Monomer Coordination (π-complexation)**:
   - The olefin (e.g., CH₂=CHR) approaches and binds to the **vacant site** via its **π-electrons**.
   - A **π-complex** is formed: Ti ← ‖ C=C
   - Coordination number returns to 6.

3. **Insertion (Migration/Cis-insertion)**:
   - The Ti–R bond and the coordinated olefin undergo a **four-centered transition state**.
   - A new **C–C bond** forms between the alkyl group (R) and the *less substituted* carbon of the olefin.
   - Simultaneously, the *more substituted* carbon attaches to the metal.
   - This is a **1,2-insertion** (for propylene, the CH₂ inserts into Ti–R).

4. **Site Regeneration**:
   - After insertion, the **vacant site migrates** to the position previously occupied by the alkyl group.
   - The polymer chain is now "one unit longer" and ready to accept the next monomer.
   - The cycle repeats.

### 2.3 Stereochemistry of Insertion

| Polymer Type | Monomer Insertion Geometry | Catalyst Requirement |
|--------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| **Isotactic** | Same enantioface every time (*e.g.*, *re,re,re…*) | **Chiral, non-racemic** active site |
| **Syndiotactic** | Alternating enantioface (*re,re,si,si…*) | Site alternation or oscillating geometry |
| **Atactic** | Random enantioface | Non-stereospecific site |

> [!TIP]
> **Chiral Site Control**: In heterogeneous Ziegler–Natta, the asymmetry of the crystal surface around Ti creates a *chiral pocket* that forces each incoming monomer to present the same face. In metallocenes, the **chirality of the substituted Cp rings** (e.g., *C₂-symmetric ansa-Zirconocene*) does the job.

### 2.4 Metallocene Catalysts — The Single-Site Revolution

- **General Formula**: Cp₂MX₂ (M = Zr, Ti, Hf; X = Cl, Me)
- **Cocatalyst**: **Methylaluminoxane (MAO)** — an oligomeric [–O–Al(CH₃)–]ₙ.
- **Activation**: MAO abstracts X⁻, forming the cationic [Cp₂M–R]⁺ active species.
- **Advantages**:
  1. **Single active site** → narrow MWD (M_w/M_n ≈ 2).
  2. **Tunable stereochemistry** by ligand design (e.g., *rac*-Et(Ind)₂ZrCl₂ → isotactic PP).
  3. **High activity** (up to 10⁴ kg PP / g-Zr·h).
  4. Incorporates **α-olefins, cycloolefins, styrene**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R_p = k_p [C^*] [M]$ | Rate of polymerization (propagation) | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr} + R_{spont}}$ | Number-average degree of polymerization | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k_t [C^*]}$ | Average chain lifetime on active site | s |
| 4 | $N_{chains} = R_i \cdot \tau$ | Number of polymer chains produced per unit time | chains·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $M_n = M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n$ | Number-average molecular weight | g·mol⁻¹ |
| 6 | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n} \rightarrow 2$ (ideal) | Polydispersity index (≈ 2 for ideal chain growth) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_p [C^*]}$ | Half-life of monomer in coordination step | s |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{kg\ poly}{g\ metal \cdot h}$ | Catalyst productivity / activity | kg·g⁻¹·h⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\Delta G^\ddagger = \Delta H^\ddagger - T\Delta S^\ddagger$ | Activation free energy of insertion | J·mol⁻¹ |
| 10 | $[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$ | Intrinsic viscosity (Mark–Houwink) | dL·g⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** All `|` symbols in formulas above use the **\vert** LaTeX command to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In handwritten exam sheets, students may use plain `|`.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Polypropylene (PP)**: 70+ million tonnes/year, used in packaging, automotive, textiles.
2. **Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE)**: Co-polymerization of ethylene with 1-butene/1-octene using metallocenes gives film-grade materials.
3. **Ethylene–Propylene Diene Rubber (EPDM)**: Soluble vanadium-based catalysts enable random copolymerization.
4. **Syndiotactic Polystyrene (sPS)**: Metallocene catalysts with specific ligand design yield high-melting (270 °C) engineering plastic.
5. **Cyclic Olefin Copolymers (COC)**: Used in medical packaging, optical lenses.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rate of Polymerization & Chain Lifetime on Active Sites

The **Cossee–Arlman mechanism** yields a kinetic framework in which propagation and chain transfer compete. We derive the key rate expression.

**Step 1: Set up elementary steps**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initiation:} \quad & C^* + M \xrightarrow{k_i} C^*-M_1 \\
\text{Propagation:} \quad & C^*-M_n + M \xrightarrow{k_p} C^*-M_{n+1} \\
\text{Chain Transfer to Cocatalyst:} \quad & C^*-M_n + AlR_3 \xrightarrow{k_{tr}} P_n + C^*-R \\
\text{Spontaneous Termination:} \quad & C^*-M_n \xrightarrow{k_t} P_n + C^*-H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the quasi-steady-state approximation (QSSA) on active sites**

Assume $[C^*]$ (vacant active sites) and $[C^*-M_n]$ (occupied active sites) are in steady state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d[C^*]}{dt} &= 0 \\
\dfrac{d[C^*-M_n]}{dt} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The total active site concentration is:

$$
[C_{total}] = [C^*] + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} [C^*-M_n]
$$

For long chains ($n \gg 1$), the propagation step dominates, and the total is dominated by growing chains:

$$
[C_{total}] \approx [C^*-P] \quad \text{(growing chain ends)}
$$

**Step 3: Write propagation rate**

The rate of monomer consumption (i.e., rate of polymerization) is:

$$
R_p = k_p [C^*-P] [M]
$$

Since most sites are occupied, $[C^*-P] \approx [C_{total}]$, so:

$$
\boxed{R_p = k_p [C_{total}] [M]}
$$

**Step 4: Average chain lifetime (τ)**

The mean time a chain spends growing on a site before termination or transfer is the **chain lifetime**:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{[C^*-P]}{R_t + R_{tr}}
$$

Substituting $R_t = k_t [C^*-P]$ and $R_{tr} = k_{tr} [C^*-P]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \dfrac{[C^*-P]}{(k_t + k_{tr}) [C^*-P]} \\
\tau &= \dfrac{1}{k_t + k_{tr}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **characteristic time** independent of site concentration.

**Step 5: Number-average degree of polymerization (X̄_n)**

By definition, $\overline{X}_n$ is the number of monomer units added per chain initiated:

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{Rate of propagation}}{\text{Rate of chain termination/transfer}}
$$

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr}} = \dfrac{k_p [C^*-P] [M]}{(k_t + k_{tr}) [C^*-P]}
$$

The site concentration cancels:

$$
\boxed{\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{k_t + k_{tr}}}
$$

**Step 6: Number-average molecular weight**

$$
M_n = M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n
$$

where $M_0$ is the molar mass of the repeat unit (e.g., 28 g·mol⁻¹ for –CH₂–CH₂–).

**Step 7: Polydispersity Index for ideal chain growth**

For a system with **constant rate of chain growth and termination** (most probable distribution / Schulz–Flory):

$$
\dfrac{M_w}{M_n} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n} \rightarrow 2 \quad \text{as} \quad \overline{X}_n \rightarrow \infty
$$

Single-site metallocene catalysts approach this limit; heterogeneous Ziegler–Natta typically shows $M_w/M_n = 3$ to $10$ due to multiple active site types.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A Ziegler–Natta polymerization of propylene is carried out at 50 °C with the following data:

- Active site concentration: $[C^*] = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol·L⁻¹
- Propylene concentration (dissolved): $[M] = 1.2$ mol·L⁻¹
- $k_p = 850$ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹
- $k_t = 0.08$ s⁻¹
- $k_{tr} = 0.02$ s⁻¹

**Calculate:** (a) Rate of polymerization, (b) $\overline{X}_n$, (c) $M_n$, (d) Average chain lifetime.

**Solution:**

**(a) Rate of polymerization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p &= k_p [C^*] [M] \\
&= 850 \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1.2 \\
&= 850 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.51 \ \text{mol} \cdot L^{-1} \cdot s^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Number-average degree of polymerization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{k_p [M]}{k_t + k_{tr}} \\
&= \dfrac{850 \times 1.2}{0.08 + 0.02} \\
&= \dfrac{1020}{0.10} \\
&= 10{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Number-average molecular weight:**

Propylene repeat unit (–CH₂–CH(CH₃)–) has $M_0 = 42$ g·mol⁻¹:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_n &= M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n \\
&= 42 \times 10{,}200 \\
&= 4.28 \times 10^{5} \ g \cdot mol^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Average chain lifetime:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \dfrac{1}{k_t + k_{tr}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10} \\
&= 10 \ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mechanistic Comparison

| Feature | Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₃/AlEt₃) | Metallocene (Cp₂ZrCl₂/MAO) |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Active Sites** | Multiple (heterogeneous surface) | Single (homogeneous) |
| **Stereocontrol Mechanism** | Chiral crystal surface | Chiral ligand framework |
| **Typical PDI** | 3 – 10 | 2 – 2.5 |
| **Activity (kg/g-Metal·h)** | 1 – 50 | 1000 – 10,000 |
| **Comonomer Incorporation** | Limited | Excellent |
| **Polymer Morphology Control** | Replicating (spherical catalyst) | Requires support |
| **Cost** | Low | High (MAO expensive) |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cossee–Arlman Mechanism — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the four-stage catalytic cycle of monomer coordination–insertion on a Ziegler–Natta active site. It is a **functional architecture** rendering of the chemical process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Octahedral Ti Surface Site with Vacancy"]
    B["STEP 1 ALKYLATION: Ti-Cl plus AlEt3 gives Ti-Et bond and Active Site Cstar"]
    C["STEP 2 PI-COORDINATION: Propylene pi-bonds to Vacant Site of Ti"]
    D["STEP 3 INSERTION: Four-centered TS, 1,2-insertion of CH2 into Ti-Et, New Ti-CH2CHMe-R bond"]
    E["STEP 4 SITE MIGRATION: Vacant Site returns to original Position, Site Regenerated"]
    F["LOOP BACK: Next Propylene Molecule coordinates to Fresh Vacancy"]
    G["END: Polypropylene Chain extended by one Unit"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> D
    F -.->|after n insertions| G
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Heterogeneous vs Single-Site Catalysts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HET["HETEROGENEOUS ZIEGLER-NATTA"]
        H1["TiCl3 Crystal Surface"]
        H2["Multiple Active Sites: Type-I, Type-II, Type-III"]
        H3["Broad MWD: 3 to 10"]
        H4["Isotactic PP by Chiral Surface"]
    end

    subgraph HOM["HOMOGENEOUS METALLOCENE"]
        M1["Cp2ZrCl2 plus MAO Cocatalyst"]
        M2["Single Identical Active Site"]
        M3["Narrow MWD: near 2"]
        M4["Tacticity by Ligand Design"]
    end

    H1 --> H2 --> H3
    H1 --> H4
    M1 --> M2 --> M3
    M1 --> M4
```

### 4.3 Stereoregularity Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Polypropylene Tacticity Outcomes"]
    S --> ISO["ISOTACTIC: All CH3 groups on Same Side, Helical 3-1 conformation, Crystalline, Tm = 165 C"]
    S --> SYN["SYNDIOTACTIC: CH3 groups Alternating, Crystalline, Tm = 130 C"]
    S --> ATA["ATACTIC: Random CH3 placement, Amorphous, Soft, Tacky"]

    ISO -.->|Catalyst: TiCl3 plus AlEt3| ISO
    SYN -.->|Catalyst: VCl4 plus Ansa-Metallocene| SYN
    ATA -.->|Catalyst: Free radical or non-specific| ATA
```

### 4.4 Schematic of a Single Active Site (Geometry)

```
         Cl
          |
    Cl -- Ti -- R(growing chain)
          |
         Cl  ←  (one Cl removed = vacant site)
          |
         (surface lattice of TiCl3 crystal)
```

**Geometry**: Distorted octahedral, with the **alkyl chain (R)** cis to the **vacant site** — a critical requirement for cis-insertion (Cossee–Arlman postulate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Mechanistic Insight**: The vacant site and the M–R bond **must be cis** to each other. After insertion, the vacancy migrates so that the new M–polymer bond is *trans* to the original vacancy — this re-orients the geometry for the next monomer's *same-face* coordination, producing isotacticity.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define co-ordination polymerization. Name the catalyst system that revolutionized the stereospecific polymerization of α-olefins.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Co-ordination polymerization is a chain-growth polymerization in which the monomer is first **coordinated (chemisorbed)** to a transition metal complex catalyst via its π-electrons and then **inserted** into the growing polymer chain through a metal–carbon bond (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for coordination step, 1 for insertion step).
>
> The **Ziegler–Natta catalyst** system (e.g., TiCl₃ + Al(C₂H₅)₃), discovered by Karl Ziegler and Giulio Natta (Nobel Prize 1963), revolutionized the stereospecific polymerization of propylene and other α-olefins (awarded full marks if named correctly).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What are isotactic, syndiotactic, and atactic polymers? Give one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> - **Isotactic polymers**: All substituent groups (e.g., –CH₃ in PP) lie on the **same side** of the polymer backbone. Highly **crystalline**, high melting point. *Example*: Isotactic polypropylene (Tm ≈ 165 °C). (1 mark)
> - **Syndiotactic polymers**: Substituents **alternate** regularly on either side of the backbone. Also crystalline but with a different crystal form. *Example*: Syndiotactic polystyrene. (1 mark)
> - **Atactic polymers**: Substituents are arranged **randomly** along the chain. **Amorphous** and soft. *Example*: Atactic polypropylene (used in adhesives, sealants). (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students write the Ziegler–Natta discovery as "Ziegler discovered isotactic PP" — this is **WRONG**. Ziegler discovered HDPE; **Natta** discovered stereospecific polymerization of propylene. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing the roles of catalyst and cocatalyst. The transition metal salt (e.g., TiCl₄) is the **catalyst precursor**; the alkylaluminum (e.g., AlEt₃) is the **cocatalyst/activator**. (Lose 1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake #3**: Omitting the **vacancy** in the Cossee–Arlman mechanism. The vacant coordination site is **mandatory** for monomer π-coordination. (Lose 2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake #4**: Writing "free radical" for co-ordination polymerization. Co-ordination polymerization is **NOT free radical** — it is a **coordinated insertion** mechanism. (Lose 2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake #5**: Forgetting units in numerical problems. Always state $M_n$ in g·mol⁻¹, $R_p$ in mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹.

---

#### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Describe the **Cossee–Arlman mechanism** for co-ordination polymerization with a neat sketch of the catalytic cycle. Discuss the role of the **vacant coordination site** and the **stereochemistry of insertion**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Ziegler–Natta catalysts** and **metallocene catalysts** in terms of active site uniformity, polydispersity, stereocontrol mechanism, and activity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Cossee–Arlman Mechanism (7 marks):**

The **Cossee–Arlman mechanism** (proposed by Cossee and Arlman, 1964) explains the stereospecific insertion of α-olefins on a **surface-exposed Ti³⁺** ion of a heterogeneous Ziegler–Natta catalyst. The mechanism consists of **four cyclic steps** (1 mark for naming):

**Step 1 — Alkylation of the Active Site (1 mark):**
The cocatalyst Al(C₂H₅)₃ transfers an alkyl group (–C₂H₅) to a surface Ti atom that bears a chloride ligand and a vacant site. This creates the **active site (C\*)** with a Ti–C bond.

$$
Ti-Cl \ (\text{surface}) + Al(C_2H_5)_3 \longrightarrow Ti-C_2H_5 \ (C^*) + Al(C_2H_5)_2Cl
$$

**Step 2 — π-Coordination of the Monomer (1 mark):**
The olefin (e.g., propylene, CH₂=CH–CH₃) approaches the **vacant coordination site** of the octahedral Ti and binds through its π-orbitals. A **π-complex** is formed. The vacant site is filled; coordination number becomes 6.

**Step 3 — Insertion (Cis-Insertion) (2 marks):**
A **four-centered transition state** is formed in which:
- The Ti–R bond and the C=C of the coordinated olefin are **cis**.
- The *less hindered* carbon (CH₂ end) of the olefin forms a new C–C bond with the alkyl group (R).
- The *more substituted* carbon (CHMe) forms a new bond with Ti.

This is a **1,2-insertion** (Markovnikov). The net result: the polymer chain grows by one monomer unit, and a new Ti–polymer bond is formed.

**Step 4 — Site Migration and Regeneration (1 mark):**
After insertion, the **vacancy migrates** from its previous position to where the original Ti–R bond was. This step is **critical for isotacticity**: the geometry of the site is preserved, ensuring that the *next* monomer will be inserted with the *same enantioface* preference (chiral site control).

**Role of the Vacant Site (1 mark):**
The vacant site is the **gateway** for monomer entry. Without it, the olefin cannot approach the metal. The site is created by surface defects, alkylation, or chloride abstraction. The *cis* relationship between the vacancy and the M–R bond is the geometric prerequisite for the four-centered insertion TS.

---

**(b) Ziegler–Natta vs Metallocene Catalysts (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Ziegler–Natta | Metallocene |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| **Active Sites** | Multiple non-equivalent sites on crystal surface | Single, well-defined site |
| **PDI (M_w/M_n)** | Broad: 3–10 | Narrow: ~2 |
| **Stereocontrol Source** | Chiral crystal lattice environment | Chiral ligand (e.g., *rac*-Et(Ind)₂) |
| **Activity** | Low–Moderate (kg/g-M·h) | Very High (10³–10⁴ kg/g-M·h) |
| **Comonomer Incorporation** | Limited (poor C₈/C₁₂) | Excellent (uniform LLDPE) |
| **Catalyst Cost** | Low | High (MAO is expensive) |
| **Polymer Morphology** | Replicates catalyst (spherical) | Requires support (e.g., silica) |

(Allocate 1 mark per major comparison point × ~6 points + 1 mark for overall summary statement = 7 marks)

**Concluding statement (1 mark):**
Metallocenes offer superior precision in polymer architecture but at higher cost; Ziegler–Natta remains the industrial workhorse for commodity polyolefins.

---

#### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [Alternative to Question A]

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the **kinetics of co-ordination polymerization** and derive an expression for the **number-average degree of polymerization (X̄_n)** in terms of rate constants and monomer concentration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **metallocene catalysis**? Explain the role of **MAO (methylaluminoxane)** and discuss how **ligand design** controls the **tacticity** of polypropylene. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Kinetics of Co-ordination Polymerization (7 marks):**

The kinetics are governed by **four elementary steps** (1 mark):

1. **Initiation** (rate constant $k_i$): $C^* + M \to C^*–M_1$
2. **Propagation** (rate constant $k_p$): $C^*–M_n + M \to C^*–M_{n+1}$
3. **Chain transfer to cocatalyst** (rate constant $k_{tr}$)
4. **Spontaneous β-hydride elimination** (rate constant $k_t$)

**Quasi-Steady-State Approximation (QSSA):** (1 mark)
Apply QSSA to active sites. Total active site concentration:
$$
[C_{total}] = [C^*] + \sum_{n \geq 1} [C^*-M_n]
$$
For long chains, $[C^*-M_n] \gg [C^*]$, so $[C_{total}] \approx [C^*-P]$ (growing chain ends).

**Rate of Polymerization:** (1 mark)
$$
R_p = k_p [C^*-P] [M] = k_p [C_{total}] [M]
$$

**Average Chain Lifetime:** (1 mark)
$$
\tau = \dfrac{[C^*-P]}{R_t + R_{tr}} = \dfrac{1}{k_t + k_{tr}}
$$

**Derivation of X̄_n:** (2 marks)
$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{Rate of monomer addition to chains}}{\text{Rate of chain termination + transfer}}
$$
$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr}} = \dfrac{k_p [C^*-P] [M]}{(k_t + k_{tr}) [C^*-P]}
$$
Canceling $[C^*-P]$:
$$
\boxed{\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{k_t + k_{tr}}}
$$

**Engineering Implication (1 mark):**
- Increasing $[M]$ increases $M_n$.
- Higher $k_t$ (e.g., via H₂ as chain transfer agent in industrial HDPE/PP) decreases $M_n$ — this is how molecular weight is controlled in gas-phase Ziegler–Natta reactors.

---

**(b) Metallocene Catalysis and MAO (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Metallocenes are **sandwich-like organometallic compounds** with the general formula $(C_p)_2 MX_2$ where $C_p$ = cyclopentadienyl (η⁵-C₅H₅), M = Zr, Ti, or Hf, and X = Cl, Me, etc. The most common is **Cp₂ZrCl₂** (zirconocene dichloride).

**Structure:** Two η⁵-bonded Cp rings above and below the metal, plus two chloride/alkyl ligands in a "pseudotetrahedral" arrangement. (1 mark)

**Role of MAO (Methylaluminoxane) (2 marks):**
MAO is an oligomeric compound [–O–Al(CH₃)–]ₙ used as the **cocatalyst/activator**. It performs three critical functions:
1. **Alkylation**: Replaces Cl with –CH₃ on Zr.
2. **Halide abstraction**: Forms the cationic active species $[Cp_2Zr–CH_3]^+$ and $[MAO–Cl]^-$.
3. **Stabilization**: The bulky MAO anion stabilizes the highly electrophilic cation and prevents bimolecular decomposition.

**Ligand Design and Tacticity (3 marks):**

| Ligand | Symmetry | Tacticity of PP |
|--------|----------|-----------------|
| Unsubstituted Cp₂ZrCl₂ | $C_{2v}$ | Atactic |
| *rac*-Et(Ind)₂ZrCl₂ (ansaturated) | $C_2$ (chiral) | **Isotactic** |
| *meso*-Et(Ind)₂ZrCl₂ | $C_s$ | Atactic |
| Me₂C(Cp)(Fluorenyl)ZrCl₂ | $C_s$ | **Syndiotactic** |

The **chirality of the ligand environment** at the active site dictates the face of the monomer that coordinates. *C₂-symmetric* ansa-metallocenes with two identical substituted indenyl ligands create a chiral pocket that consistently presents the *same enantioface* of propylene → **isotactic**. *C_s-symmetric* (Cp,Flu) catalysts have a geometry that alternates preference → **syndiotactic**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Co-Ordination Polymerization**

- [x] **Definition**: Chain-growth polymerization via monomer coordination to a transition metal, followed by insertion into M–C bond.
- [x] **Ziegler–Natta Catalyst**: Combination of a transition metal halide (TiCl₃, TiCl₄) with an alkylaluminum (AlEt₃, Al(i-Bu)₃).
- [x] **Natta's contribution**: Discovery of stereoregular (isotactic) polypropylene; coined *iso/syndio/atactic* terminology.
- [x] **Cossee–Arlman mechanism**: 4-step cycle — Alkylation → π-Coordination → Insertion → Site Migration.
- [x] **Vacant coordination site**: Mandatory for monomer π-coordination. *Cis* to M–R bond for 4-centered TS.
- [x] **Stereochemistry of PP**: Iso (same side) = Crystalline, Tm ≈ 165 °C; Syndio (alternating) = Crystalline, Tm ≈ 130 °C; Atactic = amorphous, soft.
- [x] **Metallocene catalysts**: Cp₂MX₂ + MAO → single-site, narrow MWD (~2), high activity.
- [x] **MAO function**: Alkylation, halide abstraction, stabilization of cationic active species.
- [x] **Ligand–tacticity relationship**: *C₂*-symmetric chiral ansa-metallocene → isotactic; *C_s*-symmetric → syndiotactic.
- [x] **Key Rate Equation**: $R_p = k_p [C^*][M]$.
- [x] **Degree of polymerization**: $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{k_t + k_{tr}}$.
- [x] **Chain lifetime**: $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k_t + k_{tr}}$.
- [x] **PDI**: ~2 for single-site (ideal Schulz–Flory); 3–10 for heterogeneous ZN.
- [x] **Industrial uses**: PP (isotactic, 70+ Mt/yr), LLDPE, EPDM, sPS, COC.
- [x] **Common Pitfall**: Ziegler = HDPE, Natta = isotactic PP (do not mix up!).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Co-Polymerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Co-Polymerisation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Co-Polymerisation** is a polymerisation process in which two or more chemically distinct monomer species (monomer units) are incorporated into a single polymer chain backbone, producing a macromolecule known as a **copolymer**. The process involves the simultaneous chain propagation of two (or more) different monomers, M₁ and M₂, leading to a polymer chain whose composition and microstructure depend on the relative concentrations and reactivities of the monomers used. The general representation of a binary copolymerisation is: $$[M_1 - M_2] \xrightarrow{\text{Initiation / Propagation}} \sim M_1 - M_2 - M_1 - M_1 - M_2 - M_2 - M_1 \sim$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Co-polymerisation is treated as a critical extension of chain-growth polymerisation. Students must master the **Mayo-Lewis copolymer composition equation**, **reactivity ratios (r₁, r₂)**, and the **classification of copolymer types** (random, alternating, block, graft). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are making a **hybrid fruit salad** (the copolymer) by mixing two fruits — diced mangoes (monomer M₁) and sliced pineapples (monomer M₂). If you toss them randomly, you get a *random* copolymer. If you deliberately alternate one mango, then one pineapple, then one mango... you have an *alternating* copolymer. If you first prepare a full bowl of mangoes, then later add a full bowl of pineapples, you get a *block* copolymer. Finally, if you spear pieces of mango onto a single long skewer of pineapple chunks (or vice-versa), you produce a *graft* copolymer. In every case, the **final product's taste (properties)** is determined not just by the ingredients (monomers), but also by **how they are arranged** — which is exactly what co-polymerisation kinetics studies. ## 1.3 Classification of Copolymers (KTU High-Yield Classification) | Type | Architecture | Property Distinction | |------|--------------|----------------------| | **Random / Statistical** | M₁ and M₂ units follow Bernoulli statistics (no long-range order) | Properties are **weighted averages** of both homopolymers | | **Alternating** | Strict –M₁–M₂–M₁–M₂– sequence | Highly regular; behaves like a *new* homopolymer | | **Block** | Long runs of M₁ joined to long runs of M₂ | Retains properties of **both** parent homopolymers; thermoplastic elastomers | | **Graft** | Main chain of M₁ with side chains of M₂ | Combines surface chemistry of graft with bulk of backbone | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **reactivity ratios** r₁ and r₂ control the *type* of copolymer formed, even when the same two monomers are used. For example, styrene–maleic anhydride gives a random copolymer, but styrene–acrylonitrile gives an alternating copolymer under specific conditions. ## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Constants & Units) - **Reactivity ratio** (r): **dimensionless** ratio of rate constants. - **Compositions** f₁, f₂ (monomer feed mole fractions) and F₁, F₂ (copolymer mole fractions): **dimensionless**, ranging from **0 to 1**. - **Monomer concentration** [M]: typically expressed in **mol·L⁻¹ (mol/L)**. - **Mayo-Lewis constants** r₁ = k₁₁/k₁₂ and r₂ = k₂₂/k₂₁: **dimensionless**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Chain-Propagation Steps in Binary Copolymerisation When two monomers **M₁** and **M₂** react in the presence of a radical initiator (or ionic/catalytic species), the propagating chain-end can be of two types: a radical ending in M₁ (denoted **~M₁•**) or ending in M₂ (denoted **~M₂•**). Each of these chain-end radicals can add either monomer, yielding **four propagation rate constants**: 1. **~M₁• + M₁ → ~M₁M₁•**, governed by rate constant **k₁₁** 2. **~M₁• + M₂ → ~M₁M₂•**, governed by rate constant **k₁₂** 3. **~M₂• + M₂ → ~M₂M₂•**, governed by rate constant **k₂₂** 4. **~M₂• + M₁ → ~M₂M₁•**, governed by rate constant **k₂₁** The **rates of monomer consumption** are therefore: $$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$ $$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]$$ ## 2.2 Reactivity Ratios — The Heart of Copolymerisation The **reactivity ratios** are the cornerstone parameters that quantitatively describe the preference of a propagating radical for its "own" monomer versus the "other" monomer. $$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} \quad \text{... preference of } \sim M_1^{\bullet} \text{ for } M_1 \text{ over } M_2$$ $$r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} \quad \text{... preference of } \sim M_2^{\bullet} \text{ for } M_2 \text{ over } M_1$$ **Physical Interpretation of Special Cases:** - **r₁ = r₂ = 1** → *Ideal* copolymerisation. The two monomers are incorporated purely by chance (no preference). Produces a **random copolymer**. - **r₁ × r₂ = 1** → *Strict ideal* copolymerisation. The reactivity ratios are reciprocally related. - **r₁ = r₂ = 0** → Alternating copolymerisation. Neither radical adds its own monomer. - **r₁ > 1, r₂ > 1** → Tendency toward **block** copolymer formation (each radical prefers its own monomer). - **r₁ × r₂ → 0** → Strongly alternating tendency. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Engineering Utility | |---|--------------|----------|----------------------|---------------------| | 1 | Mayo-Lewis Differential Equation | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{r_2[M_2] + [M_1]}$ | [M₁],[M₂] = instantaneous monomer conc. | Predicts instantaneous copolymer composition | | 2 | Copolymer Composition (Mole Fraction) | $F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$ | f₁,f₂ = feed mole fractions; F₁ = M₁ fraction in copolymer | Used in industrial reactor design | | 3 | Reactivity Ratio r₁ | $r_1 = \dfrac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}$ | k₁₁, k₁₂ = propagation rate constants (L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹) | Quantifies monomer selectivity | | 4 | Reactivity Ratio r₂ | $r_2 = \dfrac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$ | k₂₂, k₂₁ = propagation rate constants (L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹) | Quantifies monomer selectivity | | 5 | Azeotropic Condition | $F_1 = f_1 \iff \dfrac{r_1 - 1}{r_2 - 1} = \dfrac{f_1}{1 - f_1}$ | When feed and copolymer have identical composition | Single-reactor production without composition drift | | 6 | Alfrey-Price Q-e Equation | $k_{12} = P_1 Q_2 \exp(-e_1 e_2)$ | P = reactivity of radical; Q = monomer reactivity; e = polarity | Predicts reactivity ratios a priori | | 7 | Fineman-Ross Linear Form | $\dfrac{f_1(2F_1 - 1)}{(1 - f_1)F_1} = r_1 \cdot \dfrac{f_1^2}{(1 - f_1)^2} - r_2$ | Linearised form; enables r₁,r₂ from experiments | Experimental determination of reactivity ratios | | 8 | Instantaneous Number-Average Composition | $\bar{F}_1 = \dfrac{M_1^0 - M_1}{M_1^0 - M_1^0 + M_2^0 - M_2}$ | Superscript 0 = initial | Used in batch reactor design | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pitfall Avoidance:** In the KTU exam, always use the **backslash-styled vertical bar** `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values in LaTeX, never the pipe `\|` symbol inside markdown tables, as it breaks table formatting. ## 2.4 The Q-e Scheme (Alfrey-Price) When no experimental reactivity ratios are available, the **Q-e scheme** offers a semi-empirical prediction. The cross-propagation rate constant k₁₂ is decomposed as: $$k_{12} = P_1 \, Q_2 \, \exp(-e_1 e_2)$$ - **P₁**: A measure of the general **reactivity** of radical ~M₁•. - **Q₂**: A measure of the general **reactivity** of monomer M₂. - **e₁, e₂**: Polar factors (e) describing the polarity of the double bond (electron-withdrawing → positive e; electron-donating → negative e). The reactivity ratios can then be expressed as: $$r_1 = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \exp\left[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)\right]$$ $$r_2 = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \exp\left[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)\right]$$ This scheme is widely used in **industrial polymer design** for predicting new monomer pairings before expensive experimentation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Mayo-Lewis Copolymer Composition Equation ### Step 1: Write the rate of disappearance of each monomer The consumption of monomer M₁ occurs via two pathways: addition to a ~M₁• radical (with rate constant k₁₁) and addition to a ~M₂• radical (with rate constant k₂₁). $$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$ The consumption of monomer M₂ similarly: $$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Steady-State Approximation (SSA) to radical concentrations The SSA states that the concentration of each chain-end radical is approximately constant over time: $$\frac{d[\sim M_1^{\bullet}]}{dt} \approx 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]}{dt} \approx 0$$ This implies that the **interconversion** between ~M₁• and ~M₂• radicals is balanced: $$k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] = k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$ Solving for the radical concentration ratio: $$[\sim M_1^{\bullet}] = \frac{k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$$ ### Step 3: Form the differential ratio d[M₁]/d[M₂] Divide the rate of M₁ consumption by the rate of M₂ consumption: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute the SSA result Insert the expression for [~M₁•] in terms of [~M₂•] from Step 2: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11} \cdot \frac{k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} \cdot [M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{12} \cdot \frac{k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} \cdot [M_2]}$$ ### Step 5: Cancel common factors Both numerator and denominator share the factor k₂₁[~M₂•]. Cancel: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{\dfrac{k_{11} k_{21}[M_1]^2}{k_{12}[M_2]} + k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{22}[M_2] + \dfrac{k_{21}[M_1][M_2]}{[M_2]}}$$ ### Step 6: Simplify and introduce reactivity ratios The terms k₂₁[M₁] in the numerator and k₂₁[M₁] in the denominator cancel after factoring. Dividing numerator and denominator by k₁₂[M₂]: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{\dfrac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} \cdot \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot [M_1] + [M_1]}{[M_2] + [M_1] \cdot \dfrac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}}$$ Rearranging with **r₁ = k₁₁/k₁₂** and **r₂ = k₂₂/k₂₁**: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{r_2 [M_2] + [M_1]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **This is the Mayo-Lewis Equation** (1944). It expresses the **instantaneous** ratio of monomers entering the copolymer in terms of monomer concentrations and the two reactivity ratios. ## 3.2 Conversion to the Mole-Fraction Form (Copolymer Composition Equation) ### Step 1: Define mole fractions Let f₁ = [M₁]/([M₁] + [M₂]) and f₂ = 1 − f₁. Also, let F₁ = d[M₁]/(d[M₁] + d[M₂]) be the mole fraction of M₁ units in the copolymer. ### Step 2: Express d[M₁]/d[M₂] in terms of f₁ Divide numerator and denominator of the Mayo-Lewis equation by [M₂]: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1 \cdot \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} + 1}{r_2 + \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]}} = \frac{r_1 f_1 / f_2 + 1}{r_2 + f_1 / f_2}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by f₂: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1 f_1 + f_2}{r_2 f_2 + f_1}$$ ### Step 3: Convert to F₁ form Since F₁ = d[M₁]/(d[M₁] + d[M₂]) and F₂ = 1 − F₁: $$F_1 = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{d[M_2]}{d[M_1]}} = \frac{\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]}}{1 + \dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]}}$$ Substituting: $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1 + f_2}{(r_1 f_1 + f_2) + (r_2 f_2 + f_1)}$$ Expanding the denominator using f₁ + f₂ = 1: $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1 + f_2}{r_1 f_1 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2}$$ ### Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator by f₁ $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the KTU-board-favoured form** of the copolymer composition equation. You must memorise this for direct 7-mark derivations. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Problem Solving Style) **Problem:** A free-radical copolymerisation of styrene (M₁) and methyl methacrylate (M₂) is conducted with a feed mole fraction f₁ = 0.40. The reactivity ratios are r₁ = 0.52 and r₂ = 0.46. Calculate the instantaneous copolymer composition F₁. **Given:** - f₁ = 0.40, therefore f₂ = 0.60 - r₁ = 0.52, r₂ = 0.46 **Step 1:** Compute f₁², f₂², and f₁ f₂. $$f_1^2 = 0.16, \quad f_2^2 = 0.36, \quad f_1 f_2 = 0.24$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the copolymer composition equation: $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$ $$F_1 = \frac{(0.52)(0.16) + (0.24)}{(0.52)(0.16) + 2(0.24) + (0.46)(0.36)}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate numerator and denominator: $$\text{Numerator} = 0.0832 + 0.24 = 0.3232$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 0.0832 + 0.48 + 0.1656 = 0.7288$$ **Step 4:** Compute F₁: $$F_1 = \frac{0.3232}{0.7288} \approx 0.4435$$ **Result:** The copolymer contains approximately **44.35 mol% styrene** (M₁) units, even though the feed had only 40% styrene. This shows that styrene is being preferentially incorporated (composition drift). > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Insight:** This is exactly why industrial batch copolymerisations require **semi-batch feeding strategies** — to maintain a constant F₁ in the product despite the drift in f₁ as the reaction proceeds. ## 3.4 Azeotropic Composition — Analytical Matrix An **azeotrope** occurs when F₁ = f₁, i.e., the copolymer composition equals the feed composition (no drift). Setting the Mayo-Lewis equation to satisfy this: $$f_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$ Solving (the full derivation is in standard texts): $$f_{1, \text{azeotrope}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}$$ **Condition for azeotrope existence:** The product **r₁ × r₂ ≤ 1** must be satisfied. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Copolymerisation Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Tank
Monomer M1 and M2] --> B[Mixing Vessel
Set f1 and f2] B --> C[Reactor
Initiator and Temperature] C --> D{Chain End Type} D -- Radical ends in M1 --> E[Sim M1 radical adds M1
k11] D -- Radical ends in M1 --> F[Sim M1 radical adds M2
k12] D -- Radical ends in M2 --> G[Sim M2 radical adds M2
k22] D -- Radical ends in M2 --> H[Sim M2 radical adds M1
k21] E --> I[Polymer Chain Growth] F --> I G --> I H --> I I --> J{Determine r1 and r2} J --> K[Mayo Lewis Equation] K --> L[Predict F1 Composition] L --> M{Composition Check} M -- F1 equals f1 --> N[Azeotropic Point] M -- F1 differs from f1 --> O[Composition Drift] O --> P[Semi Batch Feeding Strategy] N --> Q[Industrial Scale Up] P --> Q Q --> R[Final Copolymer Product] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Copolymers ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Two Comonomers M1 and M2] --> B{Copolymer Type} B -- r1 equals r2 equals 1 --> C[Random Copolymer
M1 M2 M1 M1 M2 M2 M1] B -- r1 times r2 equals 0 --> D[Alternating Copolymer
M1 M2 M1 M2 M1 M2] B -- r1 greater than 1
r2 greater than 1 --> E[Block Copolymer
MMMM BBBB MMMM] B -- Side chain grafting --> F[Graft Copolymer
Main chain M1 with M2 branches] C --> G[Properties: Average of both homopolymers] D --> H[Properties: Highly regular, distinct from parents] E --> I[Properties: Retains both parent homopolymer traits] F --> J[Properties: Surface differs from bulk] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Process Block — Q-e Scheme Engineering Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Monomer Pair] --> B[Look up Q1, Q2, e1, e2 values] B --> C[Calculate k11 from Q and e] B --> D[Calculate k12 from Q and e] B --> E[Calculate k22 from Q and e] B --> F[Calculate k21 from Q and e] C --> G[Compute r1 equals k11 over k12] D --> G E --> H[Compute r2 equals k22 over k21] F --> H G --> I[Apply Mayo Lewis Equation] H --> I I --> J[Predict Copolymer Composition F1] J --> K[Match with Experimental Data] K -- Match --> L[Reactor Design] K -- Mismatch --> M[Run Fineman Ross or Kelen Tudos] M --> I ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1] **Define the term "reactivity ratio" in copolymerisation. What is its significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The reactivity ratio, denoted r₁ (or r₂), is defined as the ratio of the rate constant for a propagating radical adding its own monomer to the rate constant for it adding the other monomer: $$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}, \quad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$$ Reactivity ratios are **dimensionless** quantities that quantify the relative preference of a propagating radical for its own monomer versus the comonomer. Their significance lies in: 1. **Determining copolymer type** — r₁, r₂ values dictate whether the copolymer will be random, alternating, or blocky. 2. **Predicting composition** — through the Mayo-Lewis equation, they allow prediction of copolymer composition F₁. 3. **Designing reactors** — high r₁r₂ products require semi-batch feeding to control composition drift. ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Q2] **Differentiate between block and graft copolymers with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Block Copolymer | Graft Copolymer | |---------|-----------------|-----------------| | Architecture | Two or more homopolymer blocks joined end-to-end | Main chain of one monomer with side chains of another | | Synthesis | Living anionic polymerisation, ATRP | Chain transfer to polymer, macromonomer technique | | Example | **SBS rubber** (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene) | **Styrene grafted onto polybutadiene** (HIPS — High Impact Polystyrene) | | Property | Thermoplastic elastomer | Surface modified bulk polymer | ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 3] **(a)** Derive the **Mayo-Lewis copolymer composition equation**, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a radical copolymerisation of styrene (M₁) and butadiene (M₂), the feed contains 35 mol% styrene. Given r₁ = 0.64 and r₂ = 1.38, determine the **instantaneous copolymer composition** F₁. Comment on whether the system shows an **azeotrope**. **(7 marks)** ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3, Alternative] **(a)** Explain the **classification of copolymers** with neat structural diagrams. Discuss how reactivity ratios control the type of copolymer formed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Alfrey-Price Q-e scheme** for predicting reactivity ratios. Given that for styrene, Q₁ = 1.0 and e₁ = −0.8, and for acrylonitrile, Q₂ = 0.6 and e₂ = 1.2, calculate the reactivity ratios r₁ and r₂. **(7 marks)** ## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — Derivation of Mayo-Lewis Equation (7 marks) **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Steady-state radical concentration (long-chain approximation). 2. Chain-end reactivity depends only on the **terminal** monomer unit (penultimate unit effect neglected). 3. Reactivity ratios r₁ and r₂ are constant (independent of conversion). 4. Only propagation reactions are considered (termination, chain transfer neglected for composition derivation). **Derivation (5 marks):** Write the rate of disappearance of M₁ and M₂: $$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$ $$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]$$ Apply steady-state approximation: $$k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] = k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$ Taking the ratio of consumption rates and substituting the SSA: $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]}$$ After substituting [~M₁•] from the SSA and simplifying (cancelling k₂₁[~M₂•]): $$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{r_2[M_2] + [M_1]}$$ This is the **Mayo-Lewis equation**. #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** f₁ = 0.35, f₂ = 0.65, r₁ = 0.64, r₂ = 1.38. **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the required products. $$f_1^2 = 0.1225, \quad f_2^2 = 0.4225, \quad f_1 f_2 = 0.2275$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the copolymer composition equation: $$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$ $$F_1 = \frac{(0.64)(0.1225) + 0.2275}{(0.64)(0.1225) + 2(0.2275) + (1.38)(0.4225)}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate: $$F_1 = \frac{0.0784 + 0.2275}{0.0784 + 0.455 + 0.5831} = \frac{0.3059}{1.1165}$$ $$F_1 \approx 0.2740 \quad \text{(27.40 mol% styrene in copolymer)}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Check azeotrope condition. For an azeotrope, r₁ × r₂ must be ≤ 1, but here r₁ × r₂ = 0.64 × 1.38 = 0.8832 ≤ 1. However, compute the azeotropic feed composition: $$f_{1,\text{azeotrope}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} = \frac{1 - 1.38}{2 - 0.64 - 1.38} = \frac{-0.38}{-0.02} = 19$$ Since this exceeds 1, **no azeotrope exists** for this pair in the physical composition range. Hence the system always shows **composition drift**. ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) — Classification of Copolymers (7 marks) **Classification (4 marks):** 1. **Random Copolymer** — Monomer units distributed along the chain in a random (Bernoullian) sequence. Formed when r₁ ≈ r₂ ≈ 1. *Example:* Styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) made by free-radical polymerisation. 2. **Alternating Copolymer** — Strict –M₁–M₂–M₁–M₂– sequence. Formed when r₁ × r₂ → 0. *Example:* Styrene–maleic anhydride copolymer. 3. **Block Copolymer** — Long sequences of one monomer followed by long sequences of the other. Formed when r₁ > 1 and r₂ > 1, or synthesised by living polymerisation. *Example:* SBS thermoplastic elastomer. 4. **Graft Copolymer** — Branches of one monomer grafted onto a backbone of the other. *Example:* High-impact polystyrene (HIPS), where polybutadiene is grafted with styrene. **Reactivity Ratio Control (3 marks):** - r₁, r₂ < 1, r₁r₂ → 0 → alternating - r₁ = r₂ = 1 → random - r₁, r₂ > 1 → block tendency - Q-e scheme helps predict r-values a priori. #### Part (b) — Q-e Scheme Calculation (7 marks) **Theory (3 marks):** The Alfrey-Price Q-e scheme relates reactivity ratios to monomer properties: $$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \exp\left[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)\right]$$ $$r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \exp\left[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)\right]$$ **Calculation (4 marks):** Given: Q₁ = 1.0, e₁ = −0.8, Q₂ = 0.6, e₂ = 1.2. **Compute r₁:** $$r_1 = \frac{1.0}{0.6} \exp\left[-(-0.8)(-0.8 - 1.2)\right]$$ $$r_1 = 1.667 \cdot \exp\left[-(-0.8)(-2.0)\right]$$ $$r_1 = 1.667 \cdot \exp\left[-1.6\right] = 1.667 \cdot 0.2019 \approx 0.337$$ **Compute r₂:** $$r_2 = \frac{0.6}{1.0} \exp\left[-(1.2)(1.2 - (-0.8))\right]$$ $$r_2 = 0.6 \cdot \exp\left[-(1.2)(2.0)\right]$$ $$r_2 = 0.6 \cdot \exp\left[-2.4\right] = 0.6 \cdot 0.0907 \approx 0.054$$ **Result:** r₁ ≈ 0.337, r₂ ≈ 0.054. The product r₁ × r₂ ≈ 0.018, indicating a **strong alternating tendency** in the styrene–acrylonitrile system. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the steady-state assumption** in the Mayo-Lewis derivation (1 mark lost). > 2. **Sign errors in the Q-e exponential** — students often write e₁(e₂ − e₁) instead of e₁(e₁ − e₂). Always double-check the sign convention. > 3. **Unit confusion** — r₁ and r₂ are dimensionless, but rate constants (kᵢⱼ) carry units of L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹. Don't write units for r. > 4. **Azeotrope misinterpretation** — saying "azeotrope exists" without checking that the calculated f₁,azeotrope lies between 0 and 1. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Co-Polymerisation** - **Core Definition:** Co-polymerisation = polymerisation of two or more distinct monomers, producing a single copolymer chain with mixed monomer units. - **Four Propagation Rate Constants:** k₁₁, k₁₂, k₂₁, k₂₂ — these four constants govern all binary copolymerisation kinetics. - **Reactivity Ratios:** r₁ = k₁₁/k₁₂ and r₂ = k₂₂/k₂₁ — dimensionless parameters quantifying radical selectivity. - **Mayo-Lewis Equation:** d[M₁]/d[M₂] = (r₁[M₁] + [M₂])/(r₂[M₂] + [M₁]) — gives the *instantaneous* copolymer composition. - **Copolymer Composition (Mole Fraction Form):** F₁ = (r₁f₁² + f₁f₂)/(r₁f₁² + 2f₁f₂ + r₂f₂²) — used in all numerical KTU problems. - **Azeotrope Condition:** F₁ = f₁ occurs when r₁ × r₂ ≤ 1 and f₁,azeotrope = (1 − r₂)/(2 − r₁ − r₂) lies in (0, 1). - **Copolymer Types & r-values:** - **Random:** r₁ ≈ r₂ ≈ 1 - **Alternating:** r₁ × r₂ → 0 - **Block tendency:** r₁ > 1, r₂ > 1 - **Graft:** requires specific synthetic techniques, not directly predicted by r-values. - **Q-e Scheme:** k₁₂ = P₁Q₂ exp(−e₁e₂); r₁ = (Q₁/Q₂) exp[−e₁(e₁−e₂)]. Allows a priori prediction of reactivity ratios. - **Assumptions in Mayo-Lewis Derivation:** (i) Steady-state radical concentration, (ii) terminal model, (iii) long-chain approximation, (iv) constant r-values. - **Composition Drift:** F₁ differs from f₁ during batch reaction → requires **semi-batch feeding** in industry. - **Experimental Determination of r₁, r₂:** Use **Fineman-Ross** linearisation or **Kelen-Tudos** method with low-conversion data. - **Industrial Examples:** SBR (random), SBS (block), HIPS (graft), styrene-MAn (alternating), styrene-acrylonitrile (SAN) for engineering plastics. - **Common Exam Keywords:** "terminal model," "penultimate effect," "azeotropic composition," "instantaneous vs. cumulative composition," "semi-batch strategy," "reactor design," "composition drift."

topics.title: Different Types of Copolymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Copolymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **copolymer** is a macromolecule derived from more than one species of monomer, in which each type of monomer is incorporated into the same polymer chain. The process by which two or more different monomers polymerize together to form such macromolecules is called **copolymerisation**. The resulting polymer is termed a **heteropolymer** or, more commonly, a **copolymer**, and the constituent monomers are called **comonomers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, students must distinguish between the *architectural arrangement* of comonomers along the chain (sequence distribution) and the *chemical composition* of the copolymer. The type of copolymer is determined by how comonomer units are sequenced along the polymer backbone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train of Coaches" Model

Imagine a long passenger train. Each coach represents a monomer unit.

- If the train has **only Red coaches** → it is a *homopolymer* (poly(methyl methacrylate), polystyrene, etc.).
- If the train has **Red, Blue, Red, Blue, Red, Blue…** alternating coaches → *alternating copolymer*.
- If the coaches appear as **Red, Red, Blue, Red, Blue, Blue, Blue, Red…** with no pattern → *random/statistical copolymer*.
- If a **block of Red coaches** is hitched to a **block of Blue coaches** → *block copolymer*.
- If on a long Red train, short **Blue side-trailers** are attached at random points → *graft copolymer*.

This "train-of-coaches" mental picture makes the *architecture* of the copolymer — which is the heart of this KTU topic — instantly clear.

### 1.3 Why Copolymers Matter in Engineering

Copolymers are not merely academic curiosities. They form the backbone of **engineering thermoplastics** (ABS, SAN, SBR rubber), **impact modifiers** (EVA in PVC), **surfactants** (Pluronics, a triblock of PEG–PPG–PEG), **biomedical hydrogels**, and **pressure-sensitive adhesives**. The brilliance of copolymerisation lies in the fact that the engineer can **fine-tune properties** (glass transition, crystallinity, modulus, solubility, impact strength) *without synthesising new monomers* — only by mixing existing ones in different ratios and architectures.

> [!NOTE]
> A copolymer's properties depend on THREE independent variables:
> 1. **Composition** (mole fraction of each comonomer)
> 2. **Sequence distribution** (random, alternating, block, graft)
> 3. **Chain architecture** (linear, branched, star, comb, network)

This KTU module focuses predominantly on variable (2) and (3) — the *types* of copolymers.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $r_1, r_2$ | Monomer reactivity ratios | dimensionless |
| $f_1, f_2$ | Mole fraction of monomer in the **feed** | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $F_1, F_2$ | Mole fraction of monomer in the **copolymer** | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $Q_1, Q_2$ | Reactivity parameter (resonance) | dimensionless |
| $e_1, e_2$ | Polarity parameter | dimensionless (typically −2 to +2) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Principal Types of Copolymers

A systematic taxonomy, ordered by **increasing architectural complexity**, is presented below. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "list and briefly describe the types" — this ordered taxonomy is the most examination-friendly.

#### Type 1 — Statistical (Random) Copolymers
- **Definition:** The two comonomer units follow **no definite order**; the sequence follows a statistical (Markovian) distribution.
- **Formation condition:** Reactivity ratios $r_1 \approx r_2 \approx 1$. Each propagating chain-end shows no preference for either monomer.
- **Engineering example:** Styrene–butadiene rubber (SBR, before vulcanisation, has random sequences).
- **Property signature:** Single, broad glass transition; properties are an *average* of the two homopolymers.

#### Type 2 — Alternating Copolymers
- **Definition:** Comonomers strictly alternate: **–A–B–A–B–A–B–**.
- **Formation condition:** $r_1 \cdot r_2 \to 0$ (ideally $r_1 = r_2 = 0$). Each chain-end strongly prefers the *other* monomer.
- **Engineering example:** Styrene–maleic anhydride (SMA), styrene–acrylonitrile at equimolar feed can drift toward alternation.
- **Property signature:** Highly regular, often crystallisable, predictable Tg.

#### Type 3 — Periodic Copolymers
- **Definition:** A *short, regular* sequence that repeats, e.g. **–A–A–B–A–A–B–** corresponding to $(AAB)_n$.
- **Formation condition:** Requires specialised catalysts; not common in free-radical copolymerisation. KTU may test this only at a definitional level.
- **Significance:** Bridges the gap between strictly alternating and statistical copolymers.

#### Type 4 — Block Copolymers
- **Definition:** A linear copolymer composed of *long* uninterrupted sequences (blocks) of one monomer, followed by a long block of the second monomer: **–AAAA…AAAA–BBBB…BBBB–**.
- **Formation condition:** Living/controlled polymerisation (anionic, ATRP, RAFT, NMP). NOT accessible by simple free-radical copolymerisation.
- **Engineering examples:**
    - **SBS (Styrene–Butadiene–Styrene):** Thermoplastic elastomer (the soles of your shoes).
    - **Pluronic (PEO–PPO–PEO):** Non-ionic surfactant used in pharmaceuticals.
- **Property signature:** **Microphase separation** into nanodomains (spheres, cylinders, lamellae) — a unique phenomenon not seen in any other copolymer class.

#### Type 5 — Graft Copolymers
- **Definition:** A **main (backbone) chain** of monomer A has **branches (side-chains)** of monomer B grafted onto it, usually at random points along the backbone.
- **Formation conditions:**
    1. *Grafting-from:* Polymerise B initiated from active sites on an existing A-chain.
    2. *Grafting-onto:* Pre-formed B-chains are coupled onto an A-backbone.
    3. *Macromonomer method:* B is polymerised as a macromonomer with A.
- **Engineering example:** **High-impact polystyrene (HIPS)** — polybutadiene backbone with polystyrene grafts; **ABS** (Acrylonitrile–Butadiene–Styrene) — SAN grafted onto polybutadiene.
- **Property signature:** Combines toughness of one phase with rigidity of the other.

#### Type 6 — Gradient Copolymers
- **Definition:** The composition of the comonomers **changes gradually** along the chain length — at one end the chain is rich in A, at the other end it is rich in B.
- **Formation condition:** Reactivity ratios $r_1 \gg 1$ and $r_2 \ll 1$ (or vice-versa), combined with living polymerisation in a *batch* reactor.
- **Property signature:** Single broad Tg, but no microphase separation — intermediate between statistical and block.

#### Type 7 — Star, Comb, and Network (Cross-linked) Copolymers
- **Definition:** Architecturally complex systems where multiple copolymer chains radiate from a central core (star) or are interconnected (network).
- **Engineering example:** Cross-linked SBR (vulcanised rubber), hydrogels, superabsorbents.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **minimum competency set** for any KTU question on copolymer types and reactivity. Memorise them with their boundary conditions.

| # | Equation / Relation | Significance / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}$ | **Instantaneous copolymer composition (Mayo–Lewis, differential form)** |
| 2 | $F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$ | **Mole fraction of M1 in copolymer** (most used in problems) |
| 3 | $F_1 - f_1 = \dfrac{f_1(1-f_1)(r_1 - r_2)}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$ | **Drift equation** — how far copolymer composition lags behind feed |
| 4 | $r_1 = \dfrac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}, \quad r_2 = \dfrac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$ | **Definition of reactivity ratios** (terminal model) |
| 5 | $r_1 r_2 = \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right]$ (ideal case $r_1 r_2 = 1$) | **Ideal copolymerisation** (no penultimate effect) |
| 6 | $r_1 = \dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} \exp\!\big[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)\big]$ | **Alfrey–Price Q–e equation for r1** |
| 7 | $r_2 = \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} \exp\!\big[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)\big]$ | **Alfrey–Price Q–e equation for r2** |
| 8 | $T_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_p}{\Delta S_p^0 + R \ln [M]_e}$ | **Ceiling temperature** (thermodynamic limit) |

> **Boundary-condition alerts for KTU:**
> - $r_1 = r_2 = 1 \implies$ **Random (ideal) copolymer**, $F_1 = f_1$ always.
> - $r_1 = r_2 = 0 \implies$ Perfectly **alternating** copolymer.
> - $r_1 > 1$ and $r_2 > 1 \implies$ **Tendency toward block** (heterogeneous).
> - $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$ with $r_1 \neq r_2 \implies$ **Ideal azeotropic** behaviour; $F_1 = f_1$ at one specific feed only.

> **Note on notation:** In the formula sheet, $f_1 f_2$ is written without the vertical pipe; for absolute-value or magnitude notation, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in your answer script, **never** the bare pipe inside a table cell.

### 2.3 How Reactivity Ratios Dictate the Type of Copolymer

The Mayo–Lewis terminal model predicts that the *probability* of placing a particular next unit depends on the unit at the *active chain-end*. This is captured by the reactivity ratios. Their product and magnitude collectively dictate the *type* of copolymer formed:

| $r_1$ | $r_2$ | $r_1 r_2$ | Resulting Copolymer Architecture |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\approx 1$ | $\approx 1$ | $\approx 1$ | Random (ideal) |
| $= 0$ | $= 0$ | $= 0$ | Perfectly alternating |
| $> 1$ | $> 1$ | $> 1$ | Block-like (tendency to homopolymerise) |
| $\ll 1$ | $\gg 1$ | $\ll 1$ | Gradient / strong drift |
| $> 1$ | $< 1$ | $= 1$ | Ideal; azeotrope at $f_1 = \dfrac{1-r_2}{2-r_1-r_2}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **type of copolymer** in a free-radical batch reaction can also be tuned by *conversion control*: stopping at low conversion gives near-random; stopping at high conversion gives a *mixture* of chains with drifting composition (effectively gradient-like).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Block copolymers (SBS, SEBS):** Thermoplastic elastomers that can be melt-processed like plastics yet behave like vulcanised rubber — recyclable, no vulcanisation step. Used in **tyre tread compounds, footwear, asphalt modification**.
- **Graft copolymers (HIPS, ABS):** Convert a brittle polymer (PS, SAN) into a tough, impact-resistant one — used in **helmets, Lego bricks, automotive dashboards**.
- **Alternating copolymers (Styrene–Maleic Anhydride):** Used as **sizing agents** in paper, **compatibilisers** in polymer blends, and **precursors** for high-heat polymers.
- **Statistical copolymers (EVA):** Adjusting vinyl-acetate content moves the material from rigid plastic to flexible film — **food packaging, solar-cell encapsulants**.
