## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Mayo–Lewis Copolymer Composition Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** in the KTU Module-3 syllabus. The student must be able to derive it on paper.

#### 3.1.1 Set-up of the Kinetic Scheme (Terminal Model)

Consider two monomers $M_1$ and $M_2$ and a propagating chain-end. The **terminal model** assumes that the reactivity of the chain-end depends *only* on the *last* monomer unit added. There are four possible propagation steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} \sim M_1 M_1^{\bullet} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} \sim M_1 M_2^{\bullet} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} \sim M_2 M_1^{\bullet} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} \sim M_2 M_2^{\bullet}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Define the Reactivity Ratios

The relative preference of a chain-end is encoded in two ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} \\
r_2 &= \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical meaning:** $r_1$ is the tendency of a chain ending in $M_1$ to add $M_1$ rather than $M_2$.

#### 3.1.3 Rate of Consumption of Each Monomer

The rate at which $M_1$ disappears from the reactor is the sum of Steps 1 and 3 (both add $M_1$):

$$
-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]
$$

Similarly, $M_2$ disappears through Steps 2 and 4:

$$
-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]
$$

#### 3.1.4 Apply the Steady-State Approximation on Chain-Ends

At steady-state, the concentration of $\sim M_1^{\bullet}$ and $\sim M_2^{\bullet}$ are constant, and the rates of interconversion balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] &= k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1] \\
\Rightarrow \frac{[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]}{[\sim M_1^{\bullet}]} &= \frac{k_{12}[M_2]}{k_{21}[M_1]}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.5 Form the Ratio of Disappearance Rates

Take the ratio $-\dfrac{d[M_1]/dt}{-d[M_2]/dt} = \dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} &= \frac{k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]}
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]$ and substitute the steady-state relation $[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]/[\sim M_1^{\bullet}] = (k_{12}[M_2])/(k_{21}[M_1])$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} &= \frac{\dfrac{k_{11}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} + \dfrac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{21}[M_1]} \cdot \dfrac{k_{12}[M_2]}{k_{12}[M_2]}}{1 + \dfrac{k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]}{k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]}} \\
&= \frac{\dfrac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}\dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} + 1}{1 + \dfrac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}\dfrac{[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]}{[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]} \cdot \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_1]}} \\
&= \frac{r_1 \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} + 1}{1 + r_2 \dfrac{[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]}{[\sim M_1^{\bullet}]}\dfrac{[M_2]}{[M_2]} \cdot \text{...}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

A cleaner final form, after full substitution, is the **Mayo–Lewis equation**:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}\;}
$$

#### 3.1.6 Convert to Mole Fractions

Let $f_1 = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_1]+[M_2]}$ and $f_2 = 1 - f_1$. Then $[M_1]/[M_2] = f_1/f_2$. The instantaneous copolymer composition $F_1$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_1] + d[M_2]} \\
&= \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** This single equation tells the chemical engineer *what copolymer composition $F_1$ will be produced* for a given *feed* $f_1$. In a batch reactor, $f_1$ drifts as monomers are consumed; the integrated form (Skeist equation) gives the cumulative composition.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Azeotropic Composition

The **azeotrope** is the special feed composition at which $F_1 = f_1$. Setting $F_1 = f_1$ in the composition equation and solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2} \\
\text{with } f_2 &= 1 - f_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-multiplying and simplifying:

$$
f_{1,\text{azeo}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}
$$

> **Existence condition:** The azeotrope exists (i.e., $0 \le f_{1,\text{azeo}} \le 1$) **only if** $r_1 < 1$ AND $r_2 < 1$. If both reactivity ratios are less than unity, the system naturally drifts toward alternation.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Identify the Copolymer Type from Reactivity Ratios

| Observation | Inferred Architecture | Example System |
|---|---|---|
| $r_1 = r_2 = 1$ | Ideal random | Styrene–p-methylstyrene (early estimates) |
| $r_1 = r_2 = 0$ | Strictly alternating | Cannot occur in practice; theoretical limit |
| $r_1 r_2 \approx 0$ (both small) | Alternating | Styrene–maleic anhydride |
| $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$, $r_1 \neq r_2$ | Ideal; azeotrope exists at $f_{1,\text{azeo}} = (1-r_2)/(2-r_1-r_2)$ | Styrene–acrylonitrile (reported near-ideal) |
| $r_1 > 1$, $r_2 > 1$, $r_1 r_2 > 1$ | Block-like in FRP; gives heterogenous mix | Vinyl acetate–acrylonitrile |
| $r_1 \gg 1$, $r_2 \ll 1$ | Gradient (in living polymerisation) | Designed via controlled feed |
| Living polymerisation used | Explicit block, graft, or star | SBS, Pluronic, HIPS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU confusion clarified:** Random, statistical, and ideal are *not* always synonyms. A **random** copolymer has zero short-range order; a **statistical** copolymer follows a first-order Markov chain (some short-range order may exist); an **ideal** copolymer has $r_1 r_2 = 1$, regardless of whether it is random or not. In most KTU problems, "random" and "statistical" are used interchangeably — but be careful in higher-mark questions.

### 4.1 Architectural Block Diagram of the Seven Copolymer Types

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *sequence arrangement* of comonomers A and B for each copolymer type. This serves as a single-glance visual summary that the KTU student can reproduce in the answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Two monomers: A and B]) --> T1
    T1["Type 1: STATISTICAL / RANDOM"]:::arch --> t1ex["A B A A B B A A B B A B"]
    T2["Type 2: ALTERNATING"]:::arch --> t2ex["A B A B A B A B A B"]
    T3["Type 3: PERIODIC"]:::arch --> t3ex["A A B A A B A A B A A B"]
    T4["Type 4: BLOCK (diblock shown)"]:::arch --> t4ex["A A A A A A A A B B B B B B B"]
    T5["Type 5: GRAFT (backbone + branches)"]:::arch --> t5ex["A A A A A A A A ; branches of B at points"]
    T6["Type 6: GRADIENT"]:::arch --> t6ex["A A A A A B B B A A B B B B A B A"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: From Reactivity Ratios to Copolymer Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Determine r1 and r2 from experiment]) --> B{r1 r2 product?}
    B -- r1 r2 approaches 0 --> C[ALTERNATING copolymer]
    B -- r1 r2 equals 1 --> D{r1 equals r2?}
    B -- r1 r2 greater than 1 --> E[BLOCK-LIKE tendency]
    B -- r1 r2 less than 1 but nonzero --> F[NON-IDEAL random with drift]
    D -- YES --> G[IDEAL RANDOM, F1 always equals f1]
    D -- NO --> H[IDEAL AZEOTROPIC; azeotrope at f1 equals 1-r2 over 2-r1-r2]
    E --> I{Living polymerisation?}
    I -- YES --> J[True BLOCK or GRAFT architecture]
    I -- NO --> K[Heterogeneous mixture of A-rich and B-rich chains in FRP]
    F --> L[GRADIENT in living, broad-distribution random in FRP]
```

### 4.4 Composition-Drift Curve (F1 versus f1) — Sketcher's Guide

Although a true plotted curve is best drawn by hand in the answer book, here is a Mermaid representation of how the F1 vs f1 curve **bends** depending on the reactivity ratios. The diagonal is the line $F_1 = f_1$ (azeotrope/ideal random line).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    note["F1 vs f1 shape guide"]:::note
    R[Random: curve = straight diagonal line]
    A[Alternating: S-shaped curve crossing diagonal at f1 = 0.5]
    BL[Block tendency: curve bows far above diagonal for f1 small, far below for f1 large]
    AZ[Azeotrope: curve touches diagonal at one specific f1]

    note --> R
    note --> A
    note --> BL
    note --> AZ

    classDef note fill:#FFFBEA,stroke:#A67C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#3D2C00;
```

> **Exam-room tip:** When asked to sketch the F1-vs-f1 curve, always:
> 1. Draw the **diagonal** ($F_1 = f_1$) first.
> 2. Mark the **azeotropic point** (if it exists) where the curve meets the diagonal.
> 3. Sketch the deviation (S-shape for alternating, convex bow for block tendency) — **and label both axes** with units (dimensionless mole fractions).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

#### Q1. Differentiate between a block copolymer and a graft copolymer with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **block copolymer** is a linear copolymer in which two or more long, uninterrupted sequences (blocks) of one monomer are covalently linked to a block of another monomer. Example: **SBS (Styrene–Butadiene–Styrene)** triblock, an important thermoplastic elastomer.

A **graft copolymer** has a *main backbone* of one monomer with *branches* of a second monomer covalently attached at various points along the backbone. Example: **HIPS (High-Impact Polystyrene)**, where polystyrene branches are grafted onto a polybutadiene backbone.

| Feature | Block | Graft |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Linear A–B (or A–B–A) | Comb-like, branched |
| Synthesis | Living / controlled polymerisation | Grafting-from, grafting-onto, macromonomer |
| Phase behaviour | Microphase separation into ordered nanodomains | Less ordered, often impact-toughened blend-like |

*(Award 1 mark each for definition, ½ mark for example, ½ mark for distinguishing feature.)*

---

#### Q2. What are the conditions on the reactivity ratios for the formation of (i) an ideal random copolymer, and (ii) an alternating copolymer? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Ideal random copolymer:** $r_1 = r_2 = 1$, so that $r_1 r_2 = 1$ and the chain-end has no preference for either comonomer. The Mayo–Lewis equation reduces to $F_1 = f_1$ for all feeds. *(1.5 marks)*

(ii) **Alternating copolymer:** $r_1 \to 0$ and $r_2 \to 0$, giving $r_1 r_2 \to 0$. Each chain-end strongly prefers the *other* monomer, forcing strict alternation. An azeotrope exists at $f_1 = 0.5$ (equimolar feed). *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Mayo–Lewis copolymer composition equation starting from the four propagation steps of the terminal model. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a free-radical copolymerisation of styrene ($M_1$) and acrylonitrile ($M_2$) at 60 °C, $r_1 = 0.40$ and $r_2 = 0.04$. A feed mixture has $f_1 = 0.30$ (mole fraction styrene in feed). Calculate (i) the instantaneous copolymer composition $F_1$, and (ii) whether the system has an azeotrope. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions** *(1 mark)*:
1. Steady state on active chain-end concentrations.
2. Long-chain approximation (propagation only; initiation/termination negligible to composition).
3. Terminal model — reactivity depends only on the last unit of the chain-end.
4. Equal reactivity of all chains of a given type (no chain-length dependence).

**Four propagation steps** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{11}} \sim M_1 M_1^{\bullet} \\
\sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{12}} \sim M_1 M_2^{\bullet} \\
\sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{21}} \sim M_2 M_1^{\bullet} \\
\sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{22}} \sim M_2 M_2^{\bullet}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rate of disappearance of monomers** *(1 mark)*:

$$
-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]
$$

$$
-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]
$$

**Steady state on chain-ends** *(1 mark)*:

$$
k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] = k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]
$$

**Take the ratio of disappearance rates and simplify** *(2 marks)*:

$$
\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}
$$

**Convert to mole fractions** *(1 mark)*: with $f_1 = [M_1]/([M_1]+[M_2])$,

$$
F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.40$, $r_2 = 0.04$, $f_1 = 0.30$, $f_2 = 0.70$.

**(i) Instantaneous copolymer composition** *(3 marks)*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2} \\
&= \frac{(0.40)(0.30)^2 + (0.30)(0.70)}{(0.40)(0.30)^2 + 2(0.30)(0.70) + (0.04)(0.70)^2} \\
&= \frac{0.0360 + 0.2100}{0.0360 + 0.4200 + 0.0196} \\
&= \frac{0.2460}{0.4756} \\
&= 0.5173
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $F_1 \approx 0.517$ — the copolymer is richer in styrene than the feed. *(1 mark for concluding sentence)*

**(ii) Azeotrope check** *(3 marks)*:

An azeotrope exists only if both $r_1 < 1$ **and** $r_2 < 1$. Here $r_1 = 0.40 < 1$ and $r_2 = 0.04 < 1$. ✔

Compute the azeotropic feed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{1,\text{azeo}} &= \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} \\
&= \frac{1 - 0.04}{2 - 0.40 - 0.04} \\
&= \frac{0.96}{1.56} \\
&= 0.6154
\end{aligned}
$$

The azeotrope lies at $f_1 \approx 0.615$. Our feed $f_1 = 0.30$ is **below** the azeotrope, so the copolymer is richer in $M_1$ (styrene) than the feed — consistent with $F_1 = 0.517 > 0.30$. *(1 mark for interpretation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, describe the **seven principal types of copolymers** based on the arrangement of comonomers. Give one engineering example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Alfrey–Price Q–e scheme** for predicting reactivity ratios. How are the parameters $Q$ and $e$ obtained experimentally, and what are their physical interpretations? Compute $r_1$ and $r_2$ for a pair of monomers with $Q_1 = 1.0$, $e_1 = 0.8$, $Q_2 = 0.5$, $e_2 = -0.5$. State the type of copolymer that would result. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Seven Types of Copolymers (7 marks)** *(1 mark per type — ½ for diagram, ½ for example)*:

1. **Statistical / Random:** No definite order. Example: SBR rubber (raw, pre-vulcanisation).
2. **Alternating:** Strict ABABAB pattern. Example: Styrene–maleic anhydride (SMA).
3. **Periodic:** Short repeating sequence like $(AAB)_n$. Example: Special metallocene-catalysed ethylene–propylene.
4. **Block:** Long sequences AAAA…BBBB. Example: SBS thermoplastic elastomer.
5. **Graft:** Backbone of A with side-chains of B. Example: HIPS, ABS.
6. **Gradient:** Composition changes gradually along the chain. Example: Designed by living polymerisation with strong drift.
7. **Star / Network:** Multiple arms from a core, or cross-linked chains. Example: Vulcanised SBR, hydrogels.

*(Diagrams are mandatory; ½ mark may be deducted for missing block diagram.)*

**Part (b) — Q–e Scheme (7 marks)**

**Origin and physical meaning** *(2 marks)*:
The Alfrey–Price (1947) Q–e scheme semi-empirically predicts reactivity ratios from molecular properties of monomers. Two parameters are introduced per monomer:
- **$Q$** — a measure of the *resonance stabilisation* of the radical (and hence its general reactivity). Higher $Q$ = more reactive radical.
- **$e$** — a measure of the *polarity* of the double bond (and the radical). Positive $e$ = electron-poor, negative $e$ = electron-rich.

**The Q–e equations** *(2 marks)*:

$$
r_1 = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \exp[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)]
$$

$$
r_2 = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \exp[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)]
$$

**How Q and e are obtained** *(1 mark)*: Styrene is chosen as the **reference monomer** with $Q = 1.0$ and $e = -0.8$. A series of copolymerisation experiments with different comonomers is performed; $r_1$ and $r_2$ are determined by the Fineman–Ross or Kelen–Tüdös methods, and the Q–e values are back-calculated and tabulated.

**Numerical calculation** *(1.5 marks)*: With $Q_1 = 1.0$, $e_1 = 0.8$, $Q_2 = 0.5$, $e_2 = -0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \frac{1.0}{0.5} \exp[-(0.8)(0.8 - (-0.5))] \\
&= 2.0 \cdot \exp[-(0.8)(1.3)] \\
&= 2.0 \cdot \exp[-1.04] \\
&= 2.0 \cdot 0.3535 \\
&= 0.707
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_2 &= \frac{0.5}{1.0} \exp[-(-0.5)(-0.5 - 0.8)] \\
&= 0.5 \cdot \exp[(-0.5)(-1.3)] \\
&= 0.5 \cdot \exp[0.65] \\
&= 0.5 \cdot 1.9155 \\
&= 0.958
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation** *(0.5 mark)*: $r_1 r_2 = 0.707 \times 0.958 = 0.677$. Since $r_1 r_2 < 1$ and both $r$ values are near unity, the copolymerisation is **non-ideal but tends toward random with mild drift**; with this strong polarity difference, slight alternation tendency may also be present.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in copolymer-type questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing *random* and *statistical*.** Examiners dock 1 mark if these are used interchangeably in a 7-mark question where the distinction matters. Use "statistical" by default.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the steady-state assumption.** The Mayo–Lewis equation *requires* steady state on chain-end concentrations. If you derive it without stating this assumption, expect to lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Writing $r_1 r_2 = 0$ for alternating without acknowledging it as a *limit*.** True alternation requires $r_1 = r_2 = 0$; in practice $r_1 r_2$ is small but nonzero. Wording matters.
>
> 4. **In numerical problems: forgetting to verify the azeotrope existence condition** ($r_1 < 1$ and $r_2 < 1$). A student often calculates the azeotropic feed even when the condition is not met — leading to a negative or invalid $f_1$.
>
> 5. **Calling *gradient* copolymerisation the same as *drift in a batch reactor*.** They are not the same. Drift in FRP gives a *blend* of chains with different compositions; a true gradient copolymer is a *single chain* with continuously varying composition, which requires living polymerisation.
>
> 6. **Omitting the diagram in "describe the types" questions.** A 7-mark descriptive question in KTU without a block diagram almost always loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Copolymer** = polymer made from two (or more) different monomers on the *same* chain.
- **Seven principal types:** Statistical/Random, Alternating, Periodic, Block, Graft, Gradient, Star/Network.
- **Sequence distribution** is governed by the **reactivity ratios** $r_1$ and $r_2$ of the **terminal model**.
- **Key equation to remember by heart:** Mayo–Lewis instantaneous composition:
  
  $$ F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2} $$

- **Boundary conditions** (must memorise):
  - $r_1 = r_2 = 1$ → random
  - $r_1 = r_2 \to 0$ → alternating
  - $r_1 r_2 = 1$ → ideal (azeotrope exists if $r_1 \neq r_2$)
  - $r_1, r_2 > 1$ → block-tendency
- **Block and graft copolymers** require *living / controlled* polymerisation; **random and alternating** can be made by ordinary free-radical polymerisation.
- **Engineering examples (high-yield, must know):**
  - SBS → thermoplastic elastomer (block)
  - HIPS / ABS → impact-modified plastic (graft)
  - SBR → tyre rubber (random)
  - SMA → high-heat polymer / compatibiliser (alternating)
  - Pluronic → pharmaceutical surfactant (block)
- **Alfrey–Price Q–e scheme:** $Q$ = resonance/reactivity, $e$ = polarity. Styrene is the **reference monomer** ($Q=1.0$, $e=-0.8$).
- **Azeotrope composition formula:** $f_{1,\text{azeo}} = (1 - r_2) / (2 - r_1 - r_2)$, valid only when $r_1 < 1$ **and** $r_2 < 1$.
- **Gradient ≠ drift in FRP:** true gradient requires living polymerisation; batch-FRP drift gives a *mixture* of chains.
- **Always draw** a labelled block-diagram of the copolymer architecture in descriptive answers — examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Alternating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternating Copolymerisation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Alternating copolymerisation is a specific mode of **chain-growth copolymerisation** in which two structurally distinct comonomers (M₁ and M₂) are incorporated into the growing polymer chain in a strictly regular, alternating sequence — producing a polymer backbone of the form **-(M₁-M₂-M₁-M₂-M₁-M₂)-**. This is mathematically characterised by the reactivity ratio product **r₁ · r₂ → 0** (where r₁ = k₁₁/k₁₂ and r₂ = k₂₂/k₂₁), and is the limiting case of the **Mayo–Lewis terminal model** when the cross-propagation rate constants dominate the homo-propagation rate constants.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Polymers & Polymerisation Principles*, this topic sits within **Module 3 (Copolymerisation)** and forms a critical bridge between the terminal model kinetics and the **Q-e scheme** developed by Alfrey and Price.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Dance Partner" Analogy:**
> Imagine a ballroom where two types of dancers exist — **Tall dancers (M₁)** and **Short dancers (M₂)**. A **Tall dancer (M₁)** will *refuse* to dance with another Tall dancer; a **Short dancer (M₂)** will *refuse* to dance with another Short dancer. However, every Tall dancer *loves* dancing with a Short dancer, and vice-versa. The result? A perfectly alternating line: **Tall–Short–Tall–Short…** with no two same-type dancers ever standing next to each other.
>
> This is exactly what happens in **alternating copolymerisation** — the active chain-end radical (or ion) is highly selective and will only add the *opposite* monomer to its own end.

A more chemically accurate analogy: when the two monomers have strongly opposing **electron-donor and electron-acceptor** character (e.g., styrene = donor, maleic anhydride = acceptor), they form a **Charge-Transfer Complex (CTC)** in the feed, which then propagates as a single "monomer unit" — enforcing strict alternation.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Strict Alternation) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactivity ratio of M₁ | r₁ | ≈ 0 | dimensionless |
| Reactivity ratio of M₂ | r₂ | ≈ 0 | dimensionless |
| Reactivity ratio product | r₁ · r₂ | → 0 | dimensionless |
| Ceiling temperature | T_c | Often low (especially for CTCs) | K or °C |
| Q-value (Alfrey–Price) | Q | Spans 0.1 – 15 | dimensionless |
| Polarity parameter | e | Range −2 to +2 | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The condition **r₁ · r₂ = 0** (or approaching 0) is the *defining mathematical fingerprint* of an alternating copolymer. Be prepared to derive and explain this from the Mayo–Lewis terminal model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mayo–Lewis Terminal Model — Foundation

In radical copolymerisation of two monomers M₁ and M₂, four propagation rate constants exist:

- **k₁₁** = rate of M₁ adding to a chain ending in M₁ (homo-propagation)
- **k₁₂** = rate of M₂ adding to a chain ending in M₁ (cross-propagation)
- **k₂₂** = rate of M₂ adding to a chain ending in M₂ (homo-propagation)
- **k₂₁** = rate of M₁ adding to a chain ending in M₂ (cross-propagation)

The **reactivity ratios** are defined as:

$$ r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} \qquad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} $$

## 2.2 Deriving the Alternation Condition

The instantaneous copolymer composition (Mayo–Lewis equation) is:

$$ \frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]\,(r_1[M_1] + [M_2])}{[M_2]\,(r_2[M_2] + [M_1])} $$

**Logical Steps for Strict Alternation:**

1. **Goal:** We want the instantaneous mole fraction of M₁ in the copolymer **F₁ → 0.5** regardless of feed composition.
2. **Setting r₁ = 0:** The chain end bearing M₁ can *never* add another M₁; it *must* add M₂. So r₁ = k₁₁/k₁₂ = 0 implies k₁₁ = 0.
3. **Setting r₂ = 0:** Symmetrically, chain ends bearing M₂ can *only* add M₁. So k₂₂ = 0.
4. **Substitution check:** If r₁ = r₂ = 0, the Mayo–Lewis equation reduces to:

$$ \frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]\,[M_2]}{[M_2]\,[M_1]} = 1 $$

5. **Result:** The molar ratio of monomers in the polymer is **exactly 1:1** — perfect alternation, independent of feed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Even if the feed is 90% M₁ and 10% M₂, the copolymer that forms is still 50:50. The "leftover" monomer accumulates. This makes alternating copolymerisation **composition-invariant** in the polymer product.

## 2.3 Mechanistic Origins of Alternation

There are **three recognised mechanisms** (KTU syllabus emphasis):

### Mechanism A — Strongly Differentiated Reactivity (Penultimate + Terminal Effects)
- One monomer (e.g., a 1,1-disubstituted electron-poor olefin) is sterically/electronically unable to homopolymerise, so k₁₁ ≈ 0.
- Example: **Maleic anhydride** alone does **not** homopolymerise readily under radical conditions.

### Mechanism B — Charge-Transfer Complex (CTC) Polymerisation
- The two monomers form a **1:1 ground-state donor–acceptor complex** in the feed:

$$ M_1 + M_2 \rightleftharpoons [M_1 \cdots M_2]_{CTC} $$

- This complex then polymerises as a *single* "super-monomer" with a single, well-defined propagation step.
- The CTC behaves as a pre-organised unit, enforcing alternation even when free-radical homopolymerisation of either monomer is fast.

### Mechanism C — Zwitterionic / Charge-Alternation Mechanism
- A spontaneous electron transfer from donor monomer to acceptor monomer creates **zwitterionic species** that propagate alternately. Important for some specialty systems (e.g., cyclopolymerisation of dienes with electrophiles).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1 = \dfrac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}$ | Reactivity ratio of M₁ | Always |
| 2 | $r_2 = \dfrac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$ | Reactivity ratio of M₂ | Always |
| 3 | $r_1 \cdot r_2 \to 0$ | **Strict alternation condition** | KTU exam key |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1](r_1[M_1]+[M_2])}{[M_2](r_2[M_2]+[M_1])}$ | Mayo–Lewis composition | Copolymerisation general |
| 5 | $r_1 = \dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2}\exp\bigl[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)\bigr]$ | Q-e relation for r₁ | Alfrey–Price scheme |
| 6 | $r_1 \cdot r_2 = \exp\bigl[-(e_1-e_2)^2\bigr]$ | Product r₁·r₂ from Q-e | Never exceeds 1 |
| 7 | $F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$ | Fineman–Ross form | where f₁, f₂ = feed mole fractions |
| 8 | $r_1 r_2 = 1$ | Ideal (random) copolymerisation | Boundary case |
| 9 | $r_1 = r_2 = 1$ | Azeotropic random | Special case |
| 10 | $r_1 r_2 < 1$ | Tendency toward alternation | **Alternating region** |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** In the Q-e scheme, the product **r₁·r₂ = exp[−(e₁−e₂)²]**. This product is **always ≤ 1** and **approaches 0** only when the polarity difference |e₁ − e₂| is large. Memorise this — it is the cleanest way to *predict* alternation from the Q-e table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Alternation? |
|---|---|
| **Styrene–Maleic Anhydride (SMA) copolymers** | High-Tg, used as compatibilisers in polymer blends |
| **Vinyl ether–Maleic anhydride** (e.g., Gantrez®) | Bioadhesive, dental, pharmaceutical binder |
| **Isobutylene–Isoprene (Butyl rubber)** | Near-alternating, excellent gas barrier (tyre inner liners) |
| **Charge-transfer polymerised resists** (microelectronics) | Regular structure → uniform etch rate in photoresists |
| **Drug-delivery hydrogels** | Alternating maleic acid copolymers are biodegradable and pH-responsive |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Strict Alternation from Mayo–Lewis

**Starting Point — Mayo–Lewis Equation:**

$$ \frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]\,(r_1[M_1] + [M_2])}{[M_2]\,(r_2[M_2] + [M_1])} $$

**Step 1 — State the physical requirement.**
For perfect alternation, the rate of M₁ incorporation must equal the rate of M₂ incorporation at all times. This means:

$$ \frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = 1 $$

**Step 2 — Set the right-hand side equal to 1.**

$$ \frac{[M_1](r_1[M_1] + [M_2])}{[M_2](r_2[M_2] + [M_1])} = 1 $$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply.**

$$ [M_1](r_1[M_1] + [M_2]) = [M_2](r_2[M_2] + [M_1]) $$

**Step 4 — Expand both sides.**

$$ r_1[M_1]^2 + [M_1][M_2] = r_2[M_2]^2 + [M_1][M_2] $$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common term [M₁][M₂] from both sides.**

$$ r_1[M_1]^2 = r_2[M_2]^2 $$

**Step 6 — Rearrange.**

$$ r_1 \cdot [M_1]^2 = r_2 \cdot [M_2]^2 $$

**Step 7 — For this to hold for *any* feed ratio [M₁]:[M₂], we must have r₁ = 0 and r₂ = 0 (or, more generally, r₁·r₂ → 0 with k₁₁, k₂₂ → 0).**

$$ \boxed{r_1 = 0 \;\; \text{and} \;\; r_2 = 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Strict Alternation}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the single most-likely 14-mark question in the KTU ESE from this topic. Be ready to reproduce it line by line.

## 3.2 Derivation: r₁·r₂ from the Q-e Scheme

The Alfrey–Price Q-e scheme assigns to each monomer a **Q-value** (resonance stability) and an **e-value** (polarity, electron density). The cross- and homo-propagation rate constants are assumed to scale as:

$$ k_{ij} = P_i \, Q_j \, \exp(-e_i e_j) $$

where **Pᵢ** is a parameter characteristic of the radical *i* and **Qⱼ**, **eⱼ** are parameters of monomer *j*.

**Step 1 — Express k₁₁, k₁₂, k₂₁, k₂₂ in this form.**

$$ k_{11} = P_1 Q_1 \exp(-e_1^2) \quad;\quad k_{12} = P_1 Q_2 \exp(-e_1 e_2) $$
$$ k_{22} = P_2 Q_2 \exp(-e_2^2) \quad;\quad k_{21} = P_2 Q_1 \exp(-e_1 e_2) $$

**Step 2 — Form the reactivity ratio r₁.**

$$ r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} = \frac{P_1 Q_1 \exp(-e_1^2)}{P_1 Q_2 \exp(-e_1 e_2)} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2}\exp\bigl[e_1 e_2 - e_1^2\bigr] = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2}\exp\bigl[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)\bigr] $$

**Step 3 — Symmetrically form r₂.**

$$ r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\exp\bigl[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)\bigr] $$

**Step 4 — Multiply r₁ and r₂.**

$$ r_1 r_2 = \left(\frac{Q_1}{Q_2}\exp\bigl[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)\bigr]\right)\left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\exp\bigl[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)\bigr]\right) $$

**Step 5 — Note that Q₁/Q₂ × Q₂/Q₁ = 1 and the exponents combine.**

$$ r_1 r_2 = \exp\bigl[-e_1(e_1-e_2) - e_2(e_2-e_1)\bigr] = \exp\bigl[-(e_1^2 - e_1 e_2 + e_2^2 - e_1 e_2)\bigr] $$

**Step 6 — Simplify.**

$$ r_1 r_2 = \exp\bigl[-(e_1^2 - 2e_1 e_2 + e_2^2)\bigr] = \exp\bigl[-(e_1 - e_2)^2\bigr] $$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result.**

$$ \boxed{r_1 r_2 = \exp\bigl[-(e_1 - e_2)^2\bigr]} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key consequence:** Since the argument of the exponential is *always non-positive*, the product r₁·r₂ is **always ≤ 1**. Alternation (r₁·r₂ → 0) is achieved when **|e₁ − e₂|** is large — i.e., when the two monomers have very different polarities (one strongly electron-donating, the other strongly electron-accepting).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU University Exam - May 2024 style numerical**

**Problem:**
For the copolymerisation of styrene (M₁) with maleic anhydride (M₂), the reactivity ratios are reported as **r₁ = 0.097** and **r₂ = 0.001**. Calculate the product r₁·r₂ and interpret the copolymer structure.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the product.**

$$ r_1 \cdot r_2 = 0.097 \times 0.001 = 9.7 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0 $$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.** Since r₁·r₂ ≪ 1 (and approaches 0), the system exhibits **strong alternating tendency**. The resulting SMA copolymer is nearly perfectly alternating.

**Step 3 — Compute F₁ from the Mayo–Lewis equation for f₁ = 0.5** (equimolar feed):

$$ F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2} $$

$$ F_1 = \frac{0.097 \times 0.25 + 0.5 \times 0.5}{0.097 \times 0.25 + 2 \times 0.25 + 0.001 \times 0.25} $$

$$ F_1 = \frac{0.02425 + 0.25}{0.02425 + 0.5 + 0.00025} = \frac{0.27425}{0.5245} \approx 0.523 $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** F₁ ≈ 0.5 confirms **~50:50 incorporation** — perfect alternation in the polymer backbone, even though the *r*-values are not exactly zero (real-world deviation due to minor homo-propagation events).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Alternation Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Two comonomers M1 and M2 mixed] --> checkQ{Do they form a CTC?}
    checkQ -- YES --> ctcPath[Charge-Transfer Complex M1-M2 forms in feed]
    checkQ -- NO --> checkE{Is e1 - e2 large?}
    checkE -- YES --> polarityPath[Strong polarity mismatch forces alternation]
    checkE -- NO --> checkK{Are k11 and k22 near zero?}
    checkK -- YES --> homoBlockPath[Steric/electronic block prevents homopolymerisation]
    checkK -- NO --> randomPath[Random or blocky copolymer forms]

    ctcPath --> altOut[Strictly alternating copolymer -M1-M2-M1-M2-]
    polarityPath --> altOut
    homoBlockPath --> altOut
    randomPath --> nonAltOut[Non-alternating copolymer]

    altOut --> finOut[KTU condition: r1 r2 approaches 0]
    nonAltOut --> finOut2[KTU condition: r1 r2 approaches 1]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mayo–Lewis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed[Feed composition f1 and f2] --> mlEq[Mayo-Lewis equation]
    r1in[Reactivity ratio r1] --> mlEq
    r2in[Reactivity ratio r2] --> mlEq
    mlEq --> fOut[Instantaneous copolymer composition F1]
    fOut --> judge{r1 times r2 near zero?}
    judge -- YES --> altPoly[Alternating polymer chain]
    judge -- NO --> randPoly[Random or blocky polymer chain]
    altPoly --> qeCheck{Q-e scheme confirms polarity gap}
    qeCheck -- large e1 minus e2 --> confirmed[Alternation confirmed by both models]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Alternating Chain Architecture

```
        Radical Chain End
              |
              v
  ... - M1  - M2  - M1  - M2  - M1  - M2  - M1*  (active end)
        ^     ^     ^     ^     ^     ^     ^
        |     |     |     |     |     |     |
      donor  accept donor accept donor accept donor
      (D)    (A)    (D)    (A)    (D)    (A)   (D*)

  Donor monomer M1 (e.g., styrene, vinyl ether)
  Acceptor monomer M2 (e.g., maleic anhydride, fumaronitrile)
  Active radical always at the donor end - it will only add an acceptor M2 next.
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Propagation Cycle in Alternating System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Rad1: Initiation produces M1*
    Rad1 --> Rad2: k12 - M2 adds to M1 radical
    Rad2 --> Rad1: k21 - M1 adds to M2 radical
    Rad1 --> Rad1: k11 = 0 - FORBIDDEN
    Rad2 --> Rad2: k22 = 0 - FORBIDDEN
    Rad1 --> [*]: Termination
    Rad2 --> [*]: Termination

    note right of Rad1: k11 equals zero
    note right of Rad2: k22 equals zero
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 style]**

### Q1. Define an alternating copolymer. What is the mathematical condition on the reactivity ratios for strict alternation? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
An alternating copolymer is one in which the two comonomers (M₁ and M₂) are incorporated in a strictly regular, alternating sequence along the polymer chain — i.e., the structure is of the form -(M₁-M₂-M₁-M₂)-. The mathematical condition for strict alternation is **r₁ · r₂ → 0** (with r₁ = r₂ = 0 in the ideal limit), which arises from the Mayo–Lewis terminal model. Under this condition, the chain-end radical of type M₁ can *only* add M₂, and vice-versa, producing a perfectly 1:1 composition irrespective of the feed ratio. **[3 Marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 1 for r₁·r₂ = 0, 1 for the consequence]**

### Q2. What is a charge-transfer complex (CTC)? How does its formation lead to alternating copolymerisation? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A **Charge-Transfer Complex (CTC)** is a 1:1 ground-state association complex formed between an **electron-donor monomer (M_D)** and an **electron-acceptor monomer (M_A)** through partial electron transfer, often visible as a new absorption band in UV-Vis spectroscopy. In the feed, the equilibrium M_D + M_A ⇌ [M_D···M_A]_CTC holds. This CTC acts as a single, pre-organised "super-monomer" during propagation, so that only one donor and one acceptor enter the chain per propagation step. The result is **strictly alternating** enchainment. Examples: styrene (donor) with maleic anhydride (acceptor). **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 style — Module Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define the reactivity ratios r₁ and r₂ in radical copolymerisation. Starting from the Mayo–Lewis terminal model equation, **derive the condition for strict alternation**. Show all intermediate steps. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the **Alfrey–Price Q-e scheme**, derive the expression for the product **r₁ · r₂** in terms of the polarity parameters e₁ and e₂. Using this result, explain why the Q-e scheme predicts that **alternation is favoured when the two monomers have very different polarities (large |e₁ − e₂|)**. Provide **two real examples** of alternating copolymer systems and state their typical Q and e values. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark):**

$$ r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}, \qquad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} $$

These are dimensionless ratios quantifying the tendency of a propagating chain-end radical to add its *own* type of monomer (homo-propagation) versus the *opposite* type (cross-propagation).

**Step 2 — Write the Mayo–Lewis equation (1 Mark):**

$$ \frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1](r_1[M_1] + [M_2])}{[M_2](r_2[M_2] + [M_1])} $$

**Step 3 — Set the composition ratio to 1 for alternation (1 Mark):**

$$ \frac{[M_1](r_1[M_1] + [M_2])}{[M_2](r_2[M_2] + [M_1])} = 1 $$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply and expand (1 Mark):**

$$ r_1[M_1]^2 + [M_1][M_2] = r_2[M_2]^2 + [M_1][M_2] $$

**Step 5 — Cancel and rearrange (1 Mark):**

$$ r_1[M_1]^2 = r_2[M_2]^2 $$

**Step 6 — Conclude (2 Marks):**

For this to hold for **any** feed composition, both r₁ and r₂ must be zero. Hence the strict alternation condition is:

$$ \boxed{r_1 = 0 \;\;\text{and}\;\; r_2 = 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; r_1 r_2 = 0} $$

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the Q-e rate expressions (1 Mark):**

$$ k_{ij} = P_i Q_j \exp(-e_i e_j) $$

**Step 2 — Form r₁ and r₂ (2 Marks):**

$$ r_1 = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2}\exp\bigl[-e_1(e_1 - e_2)\bigr], \qquad r_2 = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\exp\bigl[-e_2(e_2 - e_1)\bigr] $$

**Step 3 — Multiply and simplify (2 Marks):**

$$ r_1 r_2 = \exp\bigl[-(e_1 - e_2)^2\bigr] $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**
Since (e₁ − e₂)² ≥ 0, the exponential ≤ 1. As |e₁ − e₂| grows, the product r₁·r₂ → 0, indicating stronger alternation. Large polarity mismatch is therefore the **physical driver** of alternation in the Q-e model.

**Step 5 — Two examples with values (1 Mark):**

| System | M₁ (Q₁, e₁) | M₂ (Q₂, e₂) | r₁·r₂ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Styrene (M₁) + Maleic Anhydride (M₂) | Q₁ ≈ 1.0, e₁ ≈ −0.8 | Q₂ ≈ 0.23, e₂ ≈ +2.25 | ≈ 0.003 |
| Vinyl Ether (M₁) + Maleic Anhydride (M₂) | Q₁ ≈ 0.07, e₁ ≈ −1.2 | Q₂ ≈ 0.23, e₂ ≈ +2.25 | < 0.01 |

Both systems have large |e₁ − e₂| and exhibit **near-perfect alternation**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **three mechanistic origins** of alternating copolymerisation: (i) strongly differentiated reactivity, (ii) charge-transfer complex (CTC) formation, and (iii) zwitterionic propagation. For each mechanism, provide **one example system**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A copolymerisation of **styrene (M₁)** and **acrylonitrile (M₂)** is performed with the feed mole fraction **f₁ = 0.6** and **f₂ = 0.4**. The reactivity ratios are **r₁ = 0.40** and **r₂ = 0.04**. **(i)** Calculate the **instantaneous copolymer composition F₁**. **(ii)** Calculate the **product r₁·r₂** and comment on the tendency toward alternation. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Mechanism (i) — Strongly Differentiated Reactivity (≈ 2.5 Marks):**
When one monomer is sterically or electronically incapable of homopolymerisation (e.g., **1,2-disubstituted olefins like maleic anhydride or fumaronitrile**), k₁₁ ≈ 0, so r₁ ≈ 0. The chain-end bearing this monomer can *only* add the other monomer. **Example:** Maleic anhydride + stilbene (both cannot homopolymerise radically, but they strictly alternate).

**Mechanism (ii) — Charge-Transfer Complex Formation (≈ 2.5 Marks):**
Donor and acceptor monomers form a 1:1 ground-state complex [M_D···M_A] with a measurable equilibrium constant **K_CTC**. The CTC then polymerises as a *single* unit, enforcing alternation. **Example:** **Styrene (donor) + Maleic Anhydride (acceptor)** — UV-Vis shows a new CTC band; the resulting SMA copolymer is highly alternating.

**Mechanism (iii) — Zwitterionic Propagation (≈ 2 Marks):**
Spontaneous electron transfer from donor to acceptor creates **zwitterionic intermediates** (+M_D−M_A−) that propagate by mutual ion-pair addition. **Example:** Certain **vinyl sulphide + acrylonitrile** systems show this behaviour. Less common but documented in specialty polymer literature.

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Part (b)(i) — Compute F₁ (4 Marks):**

Using the Mayo–Lewis rearranged form:

$$ F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2} $$

Substituting f₁ = 0.6, f₂ = 0.4, r₁ = 0.40, r₂ = 0.04:

**Numerator:**

$$ N = r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2 = (0.40)(0.36) + (0.6)(0.4) = 0.144 + 0.240 = 0.384 $$

**Denominator:**

$$ D = r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2 = 0.144 + 2(0.6)(0.4) + (0.04)(0.16) $$
$$ D = 0.144 + 0.480 + 0.0064 = 0.6304 $$

**Result:**

$$ F_1 = \frac{0.384}{0.6304} \approx 0.609 \;\;(\text{i.e., } 60.9\% \text{ styrene in copolymer}) $$

**Part (b)(ii) — Compute r₁·r₂ and interpret (3 Marks):**

$$ r_1 \cdot r_2 = (0.40)(0.04) = 0.016 $$

Since r₁·r₂ = 0.016 is **much less than 1** (and closer to 0 than to 1), the system has a **strong tendency toward alternation**. The SAN (Styrene–Acrylonitrile) copolymer is therefore a **highly alternating** copolymer in practice, although not perfectly so. The product 0.016 is consistent with the large |e₁ − e₂| for styrene (e = −0.8) and acrylonitrile (e = +1.20), giving |e₁ − e₂| = 2.0, so r₁·r₂ = exp(−4) ≈ 0.018, in close agreement with the experimentally fitted value 0.016.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "feed independence" property:** Many students state r₁·r₂ = 0 but fail to mention the key consequence — that alternating copolymers have **composition F₁ = 0.5 regardless of feed**. This is worth 2 marks in the 7-mark derivation.
> 2. **Sign error in Q-e derivation:** The exponent in r₁ = (Q₁/Q₂) exp[−e₁(e₁ − e₂)] is *minus* e₁(e₁ − e₂), *not* e₁·e₂. A sign error loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing CTC with radical complex:** The CTC is a *ground-state equilibrium* between two monomers — **not** a radical. Writing "CTC radical" or "radical complex" is a definitional error and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Numerical rounding:** Do not round F₁ to one decimal too early. Keep 4 significant figures through the numerator/denominator and only round at the end.
> 5. **Writing r₁·r₂ = 1 for alternation:** This is the **ideal random** case, not alternating. A common and heavily penalised error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Alternating Copolymerisation**

- **Definition:** Strictly alternating enchainment —M₁–M₂–M₁–M₂– in the polymer backbone.
- **Master condition:** **r₁ · r₂ → 0** (strict); r₁·r₂ ≪ 1 (strong tendency).
- **Mayo–Lewis equation** is the terminal model; for alternation, set d[M₁]/d[M₂] = 1.
- **Three mechanisms:** Differentiated reactivity, CTC (most common in industry), Zwitterionic.
- **Q-e scheme result:** r₁·r₂ = exp[−(e₁ − e₂)²] — always ≤ 1, → 0 when |e₁ − e₂| is large.
- **Styrene (e ≈ −0.8) + Maleic Anhydride (e ≈ +2.25)** is the textbook example of an alternating system via CTC.
- **SAN (Styrene–Acrylonitrile)** is a near-alternating copolymer widely used in plastics and SAN rubber.
- **Composition invariance:** For perfect alternation, the *polymer* composition is 50:50 **regardless of the feed ratio** — a unique and exam-favoured property.
- **Boundary cases to distinguish:**
  - r₁·r₂ = 0 → Alternating
  - r₁·r₂ = 1 → Ideal (random) copolymer
  - r₁ = r₂ = 1 → Azeotropic random
  - r₁·r₂ > 1 → Blocky / heterogeneous
- **Industrially important alternating copolymers:** SMA, Gantrez® (vinyl ether–maleic anhydride), butyl rubber (isobutylene–isoprene).
- **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) checklist for derivation:** Be ready to reproduce the 6-step proof of r₁·r₂ = 0 and the 7-step Q-e derivation of r₁·r₂ = exp[−(e₁ − e₂)²] from memory.
- **Units:** All r-values and Q-e parameters are **dimensionless**; CTC equilibrium constant K_CTC has units of **M⁻¹**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Random Copolymerization (Statistical Copolymerization)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **RANDOM COPOLYMERIZATION (Statistical Copolymerization)** is a copolymerization process in which two or more chemically distinct monomer units (M₁ and M₂) are incorporated into a single polymer chain in a purely **statistical / probabilistic** sequence, governed by the relative concentrations and the kinetic preference (reactivity ratios) of the growing chain-end radical for the available monomers. The resulting macromolecule is termed a **Random Copolymer** (or **Statistical Copolymer**).

In formal KTU 2024 terminology, random copolymerization is the chain-growth copolymerization where the probability of finding a monomer M₁ adjacent to M₂ depends solely on the feed composition and the monomer reactivity ratios **r₁** and **r₂**, and the sequence distribution follows a first-order Markov chain statistical model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Random copolymerization is distinguished from *alternating* (r₁·r₂ → 0), *block* (r₁·r₂ ≫ 1 with high chain transfer), and *graft* copolymerization. It is a sub-class of chain copolymerization along with alternating, block, and graft mechanisms.

### 1.2 The Four Bi-Types of Copolymer Architecture

| Type | Sequence Motif | Driving Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Random (Statistical)** | –M₁–M₂–M₂–M₁–M₁–M₁–M₂– | r₁·r₂ ≈ 1 |
| **Alternating** | –M₁–M₂–M₁–M₂–M₁–M₂– | r₁·r₂ → 0 |
| **Block** | –(M₁)ₙ–(M₂)ₘ–(M₁)ₚ– | r₁·r₂ ≫ 1, living / controlled |
| **Graft** | Main chain M₁ with M₂ side branches | Post-polymerization grafting |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Commuter Train" Model

Imagine a railway platform where two types of commuters — engineers (M₁) and doctors (M₂) — board a train in a probabilistic, non-ordered fashion. If the train has equal preference for both (r₁ = r₂ = 1), the seating arrangement is purely random: you may see "Engineer-Doctor-Engineer-Engineer-Doctor-Doctor-Engineer…". 

This is **random copolymerization** — the chain radical has no chemical preference for either monomer, so incorporation is dictated only by the *concentration* of monomers in the reactor. If, however, the train conductor (radical) consistently prefers engineers (r₁ ≫ 1), the train becomes enriched with engineers at the start and drifts toward doctors only as engineers are depleted — this is **composition drift** in a real random copolymerization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A true "ideal random copolymer" is one where **r₁ = r₂ = 1**. Any deviation from this ideal shifts the system toward a *non-random* statistical copolymer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mayo–Lewis Copolymerization Equation (Differential Form)

The cornerstone equation for ALL chain copolymerizations (including random) was derived by **Mayo and Lewis (1944)** and **Alfrey and Goldfinger (1944)** independently. It is universally accepted in KTU board examinations.

For a binary copolymerization of M₁ and M₂, considering the four propagation reactions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{11}} \sim M_1 M_1^{\bullet} \\
\sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{12}} \sim M_1 M_2^{\bullet} \\
\sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{21}} \sim M_2 M_1^{\bullet} \\
\sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{22}} \sim M_2 M_2^{\bullet}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying the **steady-state assumption** to the ratio [M₁•]/[M₂•] and the **long-chain hypothesis**, the instantaneous mole fraction of M₁ in the copolymer (F₁) is given by:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$

where:
- $f_1$ = mole fraction of M₁ in the **feed (reactor)**
- $f_2 = 1 - f_1$ = mole fraction of M₂ in the feed
- $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ = reactivity ratio of M₁
- $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$ = reactivity ratio of M₂

### 2.2 Reactivity Ratios — The "Personality Index" of Monomers

| Parameter | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **r₁** | $k_{11}/k_{12}$ | Tendency of M₁-ended radical to add M₁ vs M₂ |
| **r₂** | $k_{22}/k_{21}$ | Tendency of M₂-ended radical to add M₂ vs M₁ |
| **r₁ · r₂** | Product | Determines the *type* of copolymerization |
| **1/r₁** | — | Tendency of M₁-ended radical to add M₂ vs M₁ |

### 2.3 Conditions for Random Copolymerization (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The defining condition for random copolymerization is r₁ · r₂ = 1.**
> When r₁·r₂ = 1, the Mayo–Lewis equation simplifies dramatically, and monomer incorporation becomes purely **azeotropic / statistical**.

Three sub-cases exist under the umbrella "r₁·r₂ = 1":

1. **Ideal Random Case (r₁ = r₂ = 1):** Both radicals are completely non-selective. The chain composition equals the feed composition at all times.
2. **General Random Case (r₁·r₂ = 1, r₁ ≠ 1):** Random incorporation but with a slight bias; the system exhibits a **composition drift** as conversion proceeds.
3. **Azeotropic Composition:** A specific feed composition where F₁ = f₁, eliminating drift completely. This occurs when $f_1 = (1 - r_2)/(2 - r_1 - r_2)$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Use / Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ | Reactivity ratio of M₁ |
| 2 | $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$ | Reactivity ratio of M₂ |
| 3 | $F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$ | Mayo–Lewis differential equation |
| 4 | $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$ | **Random copolymerization condition** |
| 5 | $f_{1,\text{az}} = \dfrac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}$ | Azeotropic feed composition |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \dfrac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$ | Reactivity-ratio form |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d M_1}{d M_2} = \dfrac{M_1}{M_2} \cdot \dfrac{r_1 M_1 + M_2}{r_2 M_2 + M_1}$ | Integrated (Mole form) |
| 8 | $\overline{P}_n \approx \overline{P}_{n,0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - \alpha}$ | Number-average degree of polymerization |
| 9 | $F_1 = f_1$ (at azeotrope) | Azeotropy identity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** Reactivity ratios $r_1, r_2$ are **dimensionless** since they are ratios of identical-order rate constants (L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹). Mole fractions $f_1, f_2, F_1, F_2$ are also dimensionless (mol/mol). The KTU valuation key accepts dimensionless answers in numerical problems.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Random Copolymerization

Random copolymers dominate the **commercial polymer industry** because of their tunable property spectrum:

- **SBR (Styrene–Butadiene Rubber):** Random copolymer used in tire treads. (r₁·r₂ ≈ 1)
- **NBR (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber):** Random copolymer for oil seals. (Acrylonitrile + Butadiene)
- **EVA (Ethylene–Vinyl Acetate):** Random copolymer for films and adhesives.
- **SAN (Styrene–Acrylonitrile):** Random copolymer for transparent housings.
- **Vinyon, Dynel:** Vinyl chloride / Vinyl acetate random copolymers.

By tuning the feed ratio, polymer engineers can dial in glass transition temperature (T_g), crystallinity, modulus, and solvent resistance — making random copolymerization a **workhorse industrial process**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Mayo–Lewis Equation for Random Copolymerization

**Step 1: Define the four elementary propagation steps**

Consider a binary free-radical copolymerization of monomers M₁ and M₂. The growing chain-end radical can be of two types: $\sim M_1^{\bullet}$ and $\sim M_2^{\bullet}$. Each can add either monomer. The four propagation reactions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)} \quad &\sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} \sim M_1 - M_1^{\bullet} \\
\text{(b)} \quad &\sim M_1^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} \sim M_1 - M_2^{\bullet} \\
\text{(c)} \quad &\sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} \sim M_2 - M_1^{\bullet} \\
\text{(d)} \quad &\sim M_2^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} \sim M_2 - M_2^{\bullet}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Write the rates of consumption of M₁ and M₂**

M₁ is consumed by reactions (a) and (c); M₂ is consumed by reactions (b) and (d):

$$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$

$$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio d[M₁]/d[M₂]**

Dividing the first equation by the second to eliminate time:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{12}[\sim M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[\sim M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]}$$

**Step 4: Apply the steady-state assumption on radical chain ends**

Assuming the concentrations of the two radical chain-end types are in dynamic equilibrium (steady state):

$$\frac{[\sim M_1^{\bullet}]}{[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$$

**Step 5: Substitute and simplify**

Let the ratio of the two radical types be $R = [\sim M_1^{\bullet}]/[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]$. Substituting back:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{k_{11} R + k_{21}}{k_{12} R + k_{22}}$$

Using $R = k_{21}[M_1]/k_{12}[M_2]$ and dividing numerator and denominator by $k_{12}$:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$$

where $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$.

**Step 6: Convert to mole fractions**

Let $f_1 = [M_1]/([M_1] + [M_2])$ and $F_1 = d[M_1]/(d[M_1] + d[M_2])$. After algebraic manipulation:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$

**This is the Mayo–Lewis copolymerization equation** — the differential form. It is valid for ALL chain copolymerizations (random, alternating, block-feed conditions) and is the SINGLE most important equation in KTU Module 3.

### 3.2 Specialization to the Random Case (r₁·r₂ = 1)

When $r_1 r_2 = 1$, we can write $r_2 = 1/r_1$. Substituting into the Mayo–Lewis equation:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + (1/r_1) f_2^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $r_1$:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1^2 f_1^2 + r_1 f_1 f_2}{r_1^2 f_1^2 + 2 r_1 f_1 f_2 + f_2^2} = \frac{r_1 f_1 (r_1 f_1 + f_2)}{(r_1 f_1 + f_2)^2} = \frac{r_1 f_1}{r_1 f_1 + f_2}$$

**Beautiful result for random copolymerization:**

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1}{r_1 f_1 + f_2}$$

This is the **simplified Mayo–Lewis form for random copolymerization** and is a KTU-favorite formula.

### 3.3 Worked Example (Model KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** In the free-radical random copolymerization of styrene (M₁) and butadiene (M₂) at 60 °C, the reactivity ratios are r₁ = 0.82 and r₂ = 1.38. The feed contains f₁ = 0.30 mole fraction styrene. Calculate the instantaneous copolymer composition F₁ and determine whether composition drift occurs.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Verify the random condition**

$$r_1 \cdot r_2 = 0.82 \times 1.38 = 1.1316 \approx 1$$

✓ Confirms random (ideal-statistical) copolymerization.

**Step 2: Calculate f₂**

$$f_2 = 1 - f_1 = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70$$

**Step 3: Apply the random-case Mayo–Lewis equation**

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1}{r_1 f_1 + f_2} = \frac{0.82 \times 0.30}{(0.82 \times 0.30) + 0.70}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{0.246}{0.246 + 0.700} = \frac{0.246}{0.946} = 0.260$$

**Step 4: Compare F₁ and f₁**

$$F_1 = 0.260 < f_1 = 0.300$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result**

Since F₁ < f₁, the copolymer is **depleted in styrene** relative to the feed. This means styrene is being consumed *slower* than butadiene, so as conversion proceeds, the feed will become richer in styrene — a classic **composition drift**. To obtain a uniform composition, one would feed the reactor continuously with the azeotropic composition.

**Step 6: Compute azeotropic composition**

$$f_{1,\text{az}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} = \frac{1 - 1.38}{2 - 0.82 - 1.38} = \frac{-0.38}{-0.20} = 1.90$$

Since $f_{1,\text{az}} > 1$, **no true azeotrope exists** in the conventional feed range — the system will always drift. (In real styrene–butadiene systems, polymerization is stopped at <70% conversion to control drift.)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reactivity Ratio × Architecture Map

| r₁ | r₂ | r₁·r₂ | Architecture | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | **Alternating** (perfect) | Strict alternation |
| 0.01 | 0.01 | ≈ 0 | Alternating tendency | Styrene–Maleic Anhydride |
| 0.5 | 2.0 | 1.0 | **Random** | General random |
| 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | **Ideal Random** | No drift |
| 2.0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | **Random (mirror)** | Drift toward M₂ |
| 10 | 0.1 | 1.0 | Random with strong bias | Pseudo-blocky |
| 100 | 0.01 | 1.0 | Tendency toward **block** | Controlled drift |
| 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.25 | Tendency to alternating | Partial alternation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Random Copolymerization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEED REACTOR: f1, f2] --> B[RADICAL CHAIN END]
    B --> C{WHICH RADICAL TYPE}
    C --> D[M1 ENDED RADICAL]
    C --> E[M2 ENDED RADICAL]
    D --> F{k11 vs k12 SELECTION}
    E --> G{k22 vs k21 SELECTION}
    F -->|k11 preferred| H[ADD M1 HOMOPROPAGATION]
    F -->|k12 preferred| I[ADD M2 CROSSPROPAGATION]
    G -->|k22 preferred| J[ADD M2 HOMOPROPAGATION]
    G -->|k21 preferred| K[ADD M1 CROSSPROPAGATION]
    H --> L[GROWING CHAIN]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{RANDOM SEQUENCE BUILDS}
    M --> N[F1 INSTANTANEOUS COMPOSITION]
    N --> O[COPOLYMER PRODUCT]
    O --> P{CONVERSION CHECK}
    P -->|< 60 percent| Q[CONTINUE REACTION]
    P -->|> 60 percent| R[STOP TO LIMIT COMPOSITION DRIFT]
    Q --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Decision Tree for Identifying Copolymerization Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: GIVEN r1 AND r2] --> B{COMPUTE r1 times r2}
    B -->|r1 r2 approx 0| C[ALTERNATING COPOLYMERIZATION]
    B -->|r1 r2 approx 1| D[RANDOM COPOLYMERIZATION]
    B -->|r1 r2 much greater than 1| E[BLOCK OR GRAFT TENDENCY]
    B -->|r1 r2 between 0 and 1| F[INTERMEDIATE STATISTICAL]
    D --> D1{r1 equals 1 AND r2 equals 1}
    D1 -->|YES| D2[IDEAL RANDOM NO DRIFT]
    D1 -->|NO| D3[REAL RANDOM WITH COMPOSITION DRIFT]
    D3 --> D4{COMPUTE f1 AZEOTROPE}
    D4 -->|f1 az in range 0 to 1| D5[AZEOTROPIC FEED EXISTS]
    D4 -->|f1 az outside range| D6[NO AZEOTROPE DRIFT UNAVOIDABLE]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Building a Random Copolymer Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[START RADICAL INITIATOR] --> P1[ADD M1 HOMOPROPAGATION k11]
    P1 --> P2[CHAIN END IS M1 RADICAL]
    P2 --> P3[ADD M2 CROSSPROPAGATION k12]
    P3 --> P4[CHAIN END IS M2 RADICAL]
    P4 --> P5[ADD M2 HOMOPROPAGATION k22]
    P5 --> P6[CHAIN END IS M2 RADICAL]
    P6 --> P7[ADD M1 CROSSPROPAGATION k21]
    P7 --> P8[CHAIN END IS M1 RADICAL]
    P8 --> P9[ADD M1 HOMOPROPAGATION k11]
    P9 --> P10[FINAL RANDOM SEQUENCE M1 M1 M2 M2 M1 M1]
    P10 --> P11[TERMINATION BY COMBINATION OR DISPROPORTIONATION]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Industrial Random Copolymerization Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[M1 STORAGE TANK] --> B[M1 METERING PUMP]
    C[M2 STORAGE TANK] --> D[M2 METERING PUMP]
    B --> E[STATIC MIXER FEEDLINE]
    D --> E
    E --> F[REACTOR VESSEL CSTR OR BATCH]
    G[INITIATOR DOSING UNIT] --> F
    F --> H[TEMPERATURE CONTROL JACKET]
    F --> I[ONLINE FTIR SAMPLING]
    I --> J{COMPOSITION MONITOR}
    J -->|F1 deviates from f1| K[ADJUST FEED RATIO]
    K --> B
    K --> D
    J -->|within tolerance| L[CONTINUE POLYMERIZATION]
    L --> M[POLYMER WORKUP COAGULATION DRYING]
    M --> N[FINAL RANDOM COPOLYMER PELLETS]
```

### 4.5 Q–e Map Position (Conceptual Schematic)

| Monomer Pair | r₁ | r₂ | r₁·r₂ | Q₁ | e₁ | Q₂ | e₂ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Styrene (1) + Butadiene (2) | 0.82 | 1.38 | 1.13 | 1.00 | -0.80 | 2.39 | -0.50 |
| Styrene (1) + Acrylonitrile (2) | 0.41 | 0.04 | 0.016 | 1.00 | -0.80 | 0.48 | 1.23 |
| Vinyl Chloride (1) + Vinyl Acetate (2) | 1.68 | 0.23 | 0.39 | 0.056 | 0.16 | 0.026 | -0.88 |

> [!NOTE]
> The Q–e scheme (Alfrey–Price) gives a deeper thermodynamic interpretation: **Q** measures the *resonance stabilization* (general reactivity) and **e** measures the *polarity* of the monomer. Random copolymerization typically arises when both monomers have similar Q values but *opposite* or *comparable* e values, allowing statistical incorporation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define random copolymerization. What is the mathematical condition for an ideal random copolymerization in terms of reactivity ratios?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Random copolymerization is a chain-growth copolymerization process in which two or more different monomers (M₁ and M₂) are incorporated into a polymer chain in a purely statistical sequence, governed by their relative concentrations in the feed and the kinetic preference of the growing chain-end radical.

The mathematical condition for an **ideal random copolymerization** is:

$$r_1 = r_2 = 1 \quad \text{or more generally,} \quad r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$$

Under this condition, the Mayo–Lewis equation simplifies to $F_1 = (r_1 f_1)/(r_1 f_1 + f_2)$, and the chain composition depends only on the feed composition. The product $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$ implies that the two monomers have an equal tendency for homo-propagation and cross-propagation, producing a true statistical copolymer.

**(1 mark) — Definition**
**(1 mark) — Condition r₁ = r₂ = 1**
**(1 mark) — Equation simplification and statistical meaning**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Distinguish between random copolymerization and alternating copolymerization with respect to (i) reactivity ratio product, (ii) sequence distribution, and (iii) one industrial example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Random | Alternating |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) r₁·r₂** | = 1 | → 0 |
| **(ii) Sequence** | Statistical, no order; drift possible | Strict ABABAB… pattern |
| **(iii) Example** | SBR (Styrene–Butadiene Rubber) | Styrene–Maleic Anhydride (SMA) |

**(1 mark) — Reactivity ratio distinction**
**(1 mark) — Sequence distribution**
**(1 mark) — Industrial examples**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Mayo–Lewis copolymerization equation for a binary free-radical copolymerization starting from the four propagation steps. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A random copolymer of styrene (M₁) and methyl methacrylate (M₂) is synthesized with r₁ = 0.52, r₂ = 0.46. For a feed of f₁ = 0.60:
   (i) Verify whether the system exhibits true random behavior.
   (ii) Calculate the instantaneous copolymer composition F₁.
   (iii) Comment on the existence of an azeotrope. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 (1 mark): Write the four elementary propagation steps with rate constants k₁₁, k₁₂, k₂₁, k₂₂.

Step 2 (1 mark): Write the rates of disappearance of M₁ and M₂.

Step 3 (1 mark): Form the ratio d[M₁]/d[M₂] to eliminate time.

Step 4 (1 mark): Apply the **steady-state assumption** on the two radical chain-end types:

$$\frac{[\sim M_1^{\bullet}]}{[\sim M_2^{\bullet}]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$$

Step 5 (1 mark): Substitute the radical ratio back into the rate ratio and simplify to obtain:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}$$

Step 6 (1 mark): Convert to mole fractions $f_1, f_2$ (feed) and $F_1, F_2$ (copolymer):

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$

Step 7 (1 mark): State the two main assumptions: (i) **steady state** for radical chain ends, (ii) **long-chain hypothesis** (only propagation is rate-controlling; initiation and termination do not affect composition).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Verify random behavior — 2 marks:**

$$r_1 \cdot r_2 = 0.52 \times 0.46 = 0.2392$$

Since $r_1 \cdot r_2 \neq 1$, this is **NOT a true random copolymerization**. It is a **statistical copolymer with a strong alternating tendency** because $r_1 \cdot r_2$ is closer to 0 than to 1.

**(ii) Calculate F₁ — 3 marks:**

Using the full Mayo–Lewis equation with f₁ = 0.60 and f₂ = 0.40:

$$F_1 = \frac{(0.52)(0.60)^2 + (0.60)(0.40)}{(0.52)(0.60)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40) + (0.46)(0.40)^2}$$

Numerator: $(0.52)(0.36) + 0.24 = 0.1872 + 0.24 = 0.4272$

Denominator: $0.1872 + 0.48 + 0.0736 = 0.7408$

$$F_1 = \frac{0.4272}{0.7408} = 0.5767 \approx 0.577$$

**(iii) Azeotrope — 2 marks:**

$$f_{1,\text{az}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} = \frac{1 - 0.46}{2 - 0.52 - 0.46} = \frac{0.54}{1.02} = 0.529$$

Since $0 \leq f_{1,\text{az}} = 0.529 \leq 1$, **an azeotrope exists at f₁ = 0.529**. At this feed composition, the copolymer composition matches the feed exactly, and no composition drift occurs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of random copolymerization. Derive the simplified form of the Mayo–Lewis equation when r₁·r₂ = 1, and discuss the phenomenon of composition drift with a neat sketch of the composition–conversion diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In the free-radical copolymerization of vinyl chloride (M₁) and vinyl acetate (M₂), r₁ = 1.68 and r₂ = 0.23. For a feed containing 80 mol% vinyl chloride:
   (i) Compute F₁.
   (ii) Determine the azeotropic feed composition.
   (iii) State whether the copolymer is richer or poorer in vinyl chloride than the feed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

(1 mark) **Definition:** Random copolymerization is the statistical incorporation of two or more monomers along a polymer chain with no fixed sequence pattern. Condition: $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$.

(2 marks) **Derivation of simplified Mayo–Lewis:** Start with the full equation and substitute $r_2 = 1/r_1$. Multiply numerator and denominator by $r_1$:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1^2 f_1^2 + r_1 f_1 f_2}{r_1^2 f_1^2 + 2 r_1 f_1 f_2 + f_2^2} = \frac{r_1 f_1 (r_1 f_1 + f_2)}{(r_1 f_1 + f_2)^2} = \frac{r_1 f_1}{r_1 f_1 + f_2}$$

(2 marks) **Composition Drift Discussion:** As the polymerization proceeds, the more reactive monomer is consumed preferentially, so the feed composition $f_1$ changes with conversion. Consequently, the instantaneous copolymer composition $F_1$ also drifts. The **composition–conversion diagram** shows F₁ as a function of overall conversion C; in a random system with $r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$ but $r_1 \neq 1$, the curve starts at the initial F₁(0) and bends as C increases, asymptoting toward the final feed composition.

(2 marks) **Sketch (verbal):** Plot F₁ (y-axis) vs. conversion C (x-axis, 0 to 1). The curve typically starts above or below the diagonal (azeotrope line F₁ = f₁) and drifts toward f₁(∞). At the **azeotropic feed** ($f_{1,\text{az}}$), the curve is a horizontal straight line at F₁ = f₁, indicating zero drift.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Compute F₁ — 3 marks:**

$$r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1.68 \times 0.23 = 0.3864$$

This is NOT exactly 1, so the simplified random equation does not strictly apply. We must use the full Mayo–Lewis equation.

$$F_1 = \frac{(1.68)(0.80)^2 + (0.80)(0.20)}{(1.68)(0.80)^2 + 2(0.80)(0.20) + (0.23)(0.20)^2}$$

Numerator: $1.68 \times 0.64 + 0.16 = 1.0752 + 0.16 = 1.2352$

Denominator: $1.0752 + 0.32 + 0.0092 = 1.4044$

$$F_1 = \frac{1.2352}{1.4044} = 0.8795 \approx 0.880$$

**(ii) Azeotropic feed — 2 marks:**

$$f_{1,\text{az}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} = \frac{1 - 0.23}{2 - 1.68 - 0.23} = \frac{0.77}{0.09} = 8.56$$

Since $f_{1,\text{az}} = 8.56 \gg 1$, **no real azeotrope exists** in the physical range. The system will always exhibit drift.

**(iii) Compare F₁ and f₁ — 2 marks:**

$$F_1 = 0.880 > f_1 = 0.80$$

The copolymer is **richer in vinyl chloride** than the feed. This means VC is consumed faster than VAc, and the feed will become progressively depleted in VC — typical of a system where $r_1 > 1$ (VC-ended radical prefers VC).

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS IN RANDOM COPOLYMERIZATION QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the full Mayo–Lewis equation** when $r_1 r_2 \neq 1$ — students wrongly use the simplified form. Always check the product first.
> 2. **Confusing $f_1$ (feed) with $F_1$ (copolymer)** — these are NEVER the same in a drifting system. The examiner specifically looks for both symbols in your answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the long-chain hypothesis** in the derivation — the examiner allocates 1–2 marks specifically for stating this assumption.
> 4. **Miscalculating the azeotrope** — the formula $f_{1,\text{az}} = (1-r_2)/(2-r_1-r_2)$ only applies when the result lies in [0, 1]; otherwise, state that no azeotrope exists.
> 5. **Mixing up r₁ and r₂ definitions** — always define them explicitly as $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$ before using them.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — RANDOM COPOLYMERIZATION**
>
> ✅ Random copolymerization condition: **$r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$**
>
> ✅ Ideal (drift-free) random case: **$r_1 = r_2 = 1$**
>
> ✅ Mayo–Lewis (full) equation: $F_1 = (r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2)/(r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2)$
>
> ✅ Simplified random equation: $F_1 = (r_1 f_1)/(r_1 f_1 + f_2)$
>
> ✅ Azeotropic feed: $f_{1,\text{az}} = (1-r_2)/(2-r_1-r_2)$
>
> ✅ Key assumption 1: **Steady state** on chain-end radicals.
>
> ✅ Key assumption 2: **Long-chain hypothesis** (initiation and termination negligible for composition).
>
> ✅ Industrial examples: **SBR, NBR, EVA, SAN** — all random copolymers.
>
> ✅ Composition drift occurs in ALL non-azeotropic random copolymerizations.
>
> ✅ Reactivity ratios are **dimensionless**; mole fractions are **dimensionless**.
>
> ✅ r₁·r₂ → 0 ⇒ alternating; r₁·r₂ ≈ 1 ⇒ random; r₁·r₂ ≫ 1 ⇒ block-tendency.
>
> ✅ The Q–e scheme (Alfrey–Price) gives a thermodynamic underpinning: random behavior arises when Q₁ ≈ Q₂ (similar resonance stabilization) and the cross-termination is favorable.
>
> ✅ **Always verify** whether r₁·r₂ = 1 BEFORE choosing the simplified equation in KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block and Graft Copolymer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Block and Graft Copolymer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Copolymers (Foundational Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copolymers** are polymers derived from two or more distinct monomer species (termed *comonomers*). When the number of distinct monomer types is **two**, the resulting polymer is called a **bipolymer** or simply a **copolymer**; when **three** distinct monomers are used, the product is a **terpolymer** (e.g., ABS — Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene).

### 1.2 Definition of Block Copolymers

> [!NOTE]
> A **Block Copolymer** is a macromolecule composed of two or more discrete *homopolymer* segments (blocks) of distinct chemical composition joined together linearly at their chain ends via covalent bonds. The general representation is **(A)ₘ – (B)ₙ**, where the subscripts denote the degree of polymerisation of each block.

The structural taxonomy of block copolymers is as follows:

| Sub-Type | Architecture | Example (Commercial) |
|---|---|---|
| **Diblock** | A–B | PS-b-PMMA (Polystyrene-b-Poly(methyl methacrylate)) |
| **Triblock (linear)** | A–B–A or A–B–C | **SBS** (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene), SIS |
| **Multiblock** | (A–B)ₙ | Polyurethane elastomers (soft-hard segments) |
| **Star-block** | (A–B)ₙ radiating from a core | S–(B–A)ₙ star polymers |

### 1.3 Definition of Graft Copolymers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Graft Copolymer** (also known as a *comb copolymer*) is a branched macromolecule in which one or more side chains (the *grafts*) of one chemical composition are covalently attached to a linear or branched *main chain* (the *backbone*) of a different chemical composition. The notation is typically **P(A)-g-P(B)**, where the "g" denotes the grafting connection.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — "The Train" (Block Copolymer):** Imagine a freight train. The locomotive (Block A) and the cargo wagons (Block B) are manufactured from completely different materials (steel vs. wood) but are firmly **bolted end-to-end**. They travel as one unit. This is essentially a **diblock** copolymer. A **triblock (ABA)** is like a freight train with matching engines on both ends. A **multiblock (ABAB)** is a long train alternating between steel and wooden cars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — "The Tree" (Graft Copolymer):** Think of a *Mango Tree*. The trunk is one type of wood (Backbone A). From the trunk, branches of a different wood type (Graft B) grow out laterally and perpendicularly. The whole tree is a single living entity, just as a graft copolymer is a single macromolecule with two chemically distinct domains — one continuous (the trunk) and one pendant (the branches).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — "The Necklace" (Statistical/Random Copolymer — for contrast):** If a **block copolymer** is a train with segregated cars, a **random copolymer** is like a bead necklace where coloured beads (different monomers) appear in a **statistical sequence** without order. **Graft copolymers** are unique because the two chemistries are segregated by *topology* (where they sit) rather than by *sequence* (their order along the chain).

### 1.5 Position in the KTU Copolymer Classification Hierarchy

Copolymers are broadly classified into **four architectural families**:

1. **Statistical / Random Copolymers** — monomer sequence follows probability (Markov/terminal models).
2. **Alternating Copolymers** — strict ABABAB alternation.
3. **Block Copolymers** — long, contiguous runs (blocks) of each monomer.
4. **Graft Copolymers** — one monomer forms the backbone, the other forms pendant side chains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The distinction between **Block** and **Graft** is fundamentally **topological**:
> - **Block** = sequences of different monomers joined **end-to-end** (1D linear).
> - **Graft** = sequences of different monomers joined **perpendicularly** (branching architecture).

### 1.6 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Variables)

- **Total Molecular Weight (Mₙ,total):** Sum of all block/graft molecular weights.
- **Composition (f):** Volume or weight fraction of one block in the copolymer.
- **Number of Blocks (n):** Distinct contiguous sequences.
- **Polydispersity Index (PDI = M_w/Mₙ):** Should ideally approach **1.0** for well-controlled block copolymers.
- **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC):** The threshold concentration above which block copolymers self-assemble into micelles in a selective solvent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Copolymers — In-Depth Operational Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Why do Block Copolymers Self-Assemble? (The Thermodynamic "Why")

> [!NOTE]
> A block copolymer chain is held together by covalent bonds, but the **A and B blocks are usually immiscible** (analogous to oil and water). When the A-B interaction energy (described by the **Flory-Huggins interaction parameter, χ**) exceeds a critical threshold, the system minimises its free energy by **microphase separation** — the blocks segregate into nanodomains (spheres, cylinders, lamellae, gyroids) with periodicities of typically **10–100 nm**. This is the foundation of *Block Copolymer Lithography* and *Nanopatterning*.

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Diblock Copolymer Microphase Separation

1. **Chain Synthesis:** A and B monomers are polymerised sequentially to form an A–B diblock with controlled block lengths.
2. **Chain Stretching Penalty:** When the blocks try to phase separate, the covalent junction forces them to remain connected, **stretching** the chain.
3. **Interfacial Energy:** The A/B interface has a positive energy proportional to **χ**.
4. **Equilibrium Morphology:** The chain reaches a balance, forming periodic nanodomains. The morphology is dictated by the volume fraction **f_A**:
   - f_A < 0.20 → **B spheres in A matrix** (S)
   - 0.20 < f_A < 0.35 → **B cylinders in A matrix** (C)
   - 0.35 < f_A < 0.65 → **Lamellae** (L) — alternating A-B sheets
   - 0.65 < f_A < 0.80 → **A cylinders in B matrix** (C')
   - f_A > 0.80 → **A spheres in B matrix** (S')
5. **Domain Spacing (d):** Scales as **d ∝ a · N^(2/3)**, where *a* is the monomer size and *N* is the total degree of polymerisation.

#### 2.1.3 Synthesis Methods for Block Copolymers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Block copolymers require **"living" or "controlled" polymerisation** techniques, because classical free-radical polymerisation produces dead chains that cannot be extended into blocks.

| Method | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Anionic (Living) Polymerisation** | Sequential addition of monomers to a carbanion | SBS rubber (industrial scale) |
| **Cationic Living Polymerisation** | Sequential carbocationic addition | Polyisobutylene-b-PMMA |
| **ATRP** (Atom Transfer Radical Polymerisation) | Cu-catalysed controlled radical | PS-b-PBA |
| **RAFT** (Reversible Addition-Fragmentation Chain Transfer) | Thiocarbonyl-thio chain transfer | PS-b-PAA |
| **NMP** (Nitroxide-Mediated Polymerisation) | Stable nitroxide radical capping | PS-b-PMMA |
| **Ring-Opening Polymerisation (ROP)** | Coordinative mechanism | PEG-b-PLA |
| **Click Coupling** | Post-polymerisation azide-alkyne cycloaddition | Modular coupling of pre-formed blocks |

### 2.2 Graft Copolymers — In-Depth Operational Analysis
