#### 2.2.1 The Three "Grafting" Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> Graft copolymers are synthesised by one of three fundamental "grafting" strategies, each of which addresses the challenge of *where* and *how* the graft is initiated.

**Strategy 1 — "Grafting FROM":**
- Initiating sites are introduced onto the **backbone polymer**.
- The second monomer is polymerised outward from these sites, growing the side chains in situ.
- *Advantage:* High graft density is achievable.
- *Disadvantage:* Graft length control is limited.

**Strategy 2 — "Grafting ONTO":**
- Both backbone and side chains are pre-synthesised.
- The two are coupled via a *click* reaction (e.g., azide-alkyne).
- *Advantage:* Independent control of backbone and graft length.
- *Disadvantage:* Steric crowding limits graft density.

**Strategy 3 — "Grafting THROUGH" (Macromonomer Method):**
- Pre-formed short chains with a polymerisable end-group (macromonomers) are copolymerised with the backbone monomer.
- *Advantage:* Random or statistical graft distributions.
- *Disadvantage:* Macromonomer reactivity is low due to steric bulk.

#### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Graft Copolymer Synthesis (Example: HIPS)

1. **Backbone Selection:** Polybutadiene (PBd) rubber is dissolved in styrene monomer.
2. **Graft Site Generation:** Initiator radicals abstract allylic H-atoms from PBd, generating backbone radicals.
3. **Graft Growth:** Styrene monomer adds to the PBd backbone radical, growing polystyrene (PS) side chains.
4. **Matrix Formation:** Simultaneously, free styrene homopolymerises, forming a PS continuous phase.
5. **Final Architecture:** A PS matrix with PBd rubber particles having **PS grafts** at the interface — this dramatically improves interfacial adhesion and gives High Impact Polystyrene its toughness.

### 2.3 Comparison Table — Block vs. Graft vs. Random Copolymers

| Property | Random | Block | Graft |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sequence** | Statistical, no order | Long contiguous runs | Backbone + branches |
| **Topology** | Linear | Linear (or star) | Branched (comb-like) |
| **Microphase Separation** | No (single phase) | Yes (nanodomains) | Partial (depends on graft density) |
| **Synthesis** | Free radical (any) | Requires living/controlled | Grafting-from/onto/through |
| **Example** | SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber) | SBS, SEBS | HIPS, ABS, Starch-g-PMMA |
| **Mechanical Behaviour** | Homogeneous, single Tg | Two Tg values | Two Tg values |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **high-yield, board-exam-essential formulas** for this topic. Master these before the exam.

$$
f_A = \frac{N_A \cdot v_A}{N_A \cdot v_A + N_B \cdot v_B}
$$

> *f_A = volume fraction of block A; N = degree of polymerisation; v = molar volume of the repeat unit.*

$$
\chi = \frac{z \cdot \epsilon}{k_B T}
$$

> *χ = Flory-Huggins interaction parameter (dimensionless); z = coordination number; ε = pairwise interaction energy; k_B = Boltzmann constant; T = absolute temperature (K).*

$$
\chi N > 10.5 \quad \text{(ODT criterion for symmetric diblock)}
$$

> *χN > 10.495 is the **Order-Disorder Transition (ODT)** threshold. Above this, microphase separation occurs.*

$$
d \approx a \cdot N^{2/3} \cdot \chi^{1/6}
$$

> *d = domain spacing of microphases; a = statistical segment length (≈ 0.5–1 nm for vinyl polymers).*

$$
M_{n,\text{total}} = M_{n,A} + M_{n,B} + \dots + M_{n,k}
$$

> *Additivity of number-average molecular weights of individual blocks/grafts in a copolymer.*

$$
G_n = \frac{N_{\text{grafts}}}{N_{\text{backbone repeat units}}}
$$

> *G_n = number-average grafting density (grafts per backbone repeat unit). For practical purposes, G_n ≤ 1 due to steric crowding.*

$$
\text{CMC} \approx \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G_{\text{mic}}}{k_B T}\right) \propto N_A^{-0.5} \cdot N_B^{-0.8}
$$

> *CMC = Critical Micelle Concentration in selective solvent; N_A = core block DP; N_B = corona block DP.*

$$
T_g(\text{blend rule}) \approx \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_A}{T_{g,A}} + \frac{w_B}{T_{g,B}}
$$

> *Fox equation for estimating Tg of a random copolymer or a microphase-separated blend; w = weight fraction.*

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do engineers care about block and graft copolymers?**
> - **Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPEs):** SBS triblocks are rubbery (elastomeric) at room temperature but melt-processable (thermoplastic) at high temperatures — replacing vulcanised rubber in shoe soles, tyre treads, and adhesives.
> - **Impact-Modified Plastics:** HIPS, ABS, ASA, and PVC-u use graft copolymer toughening mechanisms.
> - **Drug Delivery:** PEG-b-PLA block copolymers form **micelles** that encapsulate hydrophobic drugs, with PEG providing *stealth* behaviour against the immune system.
> - **Nanopatterning:** Block copolymer thin films self-assemble into sub-10 nm patterns, used in next-generation semiconductor lithography.
> - **Compatibilisers in Polymer Blends:** Adding a small amount of a block or graft copolymer (matching the two immiscible blend phases) reduces interfacial tension, improving blend morphology.
> - **Membrane Technology:** Block copolymers form isoporous membranes for ultrafiltration and gas separation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Copolymer Type Selection

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix helps KTU students identify the copolymer type when given a structural description — a common **Part A (3-mark)** question format.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Read the chain structure.} \\
&\textbf{Step 2: Identify junction points.} \\
&\text{— If junctions are linear and terminal → BLOCK (1D).} \\
&\text{— If junctions are lateral/branching → GRAFT (comb).} \\
&\textbf{Step 3: Identify sequence.} \\
&\text{— Long contiguous A runs + long contiguous B runs → confirms BLOCK.} \\
&\text{— A main chain of one type with B branches → confirms GRAFT.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Additivity of Number-Average Molecular Weight in Block Copolymers

Given: A diblock copolymer of type A–B with a degree of polymerisation N_A in block A and N_B in block B.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{The total degree of polymerisation is the sum of the blocks:} \\
& N_{\text{total}} = N_A + N_B
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Molecular weight of the repeat unit in block A is } M_0^A \text{, and in block B is } M_0^B. \\
& M_{n,A} = N_A \cdot M_0^A \\
& M_{n,B} = N_B \cdot M_0^B
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Total number-average molecular weight of the diblock:} \\
& M_{n,\text{total}} = M_{n,A} + M_{n,B} = N_A \cdot M_0^A + N_B \cdot M_0^B
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Generalisation to k blocks:} \\
& M_{n,\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \cdot M_0^i
\end{aligned}
$$

> This additivity principle is the foundation of GPC (Gel Permeation Chromatography) characterisation, where each block can be measured after selective chemical degradation.

### 3.3 Derivation: Order-Disorder Transition (ODT) Criterion

The ODT marks the boundary between a **disordered (homogeneous) state** and an **ordered (microphase-separated) state** in a block copolymer melt.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Define the free energy of mixing (Flory-Huggins):} \\
& \frac{\Delta G_m}{k_B T} = \frac{\phi_A}{N_A} \ln \phi_A + \frac{\phi_B}{N_B} \ln \phi_B + \chi \phi_A \phi_B
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{For a symmetric diblock (N_A = N_B = N, } \phi_A = \phi_B = 0.5\text{):} \\
& \frac{\Delta G_m}{k_B T} = \frac{1}{N} \ln(0.5) + \chi (0.5)(0.5) = -\frac{0.693}{N} + 0.25 \chi
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Mean-field theory (Leibler, 1980) gives the spinodal criterion:} \\
& \chi N \geq 10.495
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Interpretation:} \\
& \text{If } \chi N < 10.495 \rightarrow \text{disordered (single phase).} \\
& \text{If } \chi N \geq 10.495 \rightarrow \text{microphase-separated (ordered).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **χN = 10.5** rule is one of the most frequently tested values in KTU exams. Memorise it.

### 3.4 Derivation: Domain Spacing (d) of a Symmetric Diblock

> [!NOTE]
> The balance between **chain stretching energy** and **interfacial energy** determines the equilibrium domain spacing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Interfacial energy per chain (favours larger domains to reduce area):} \\
& F_{\text{interface}} \propto \gamma \cdot d^2 \propto \chi^{1/2} \cdot d^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Chain stretching energy (favours smaller domains to avoid excessive stretch):} \\
& F_{\text{stretch}} \propto \frac{d^2}{N a^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Total free energy: } F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{interface}} + F_{\text{stretch}} \\
& \text{Minimising with respect to } d:
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Setting } \frac{\partial F_{\text{total}}}{\partial d} = 0 \text{ and solving:} \\
& d \approx 1.10 \cdot a \cdot N^{2/3} \cdot \chi^{1/6}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This explains the experimental observation that **higher molecular weight** block copolymers give **larger domain spacings**, scaling as N^(2/3).

### 3.5 Derivation: Volume Fraction (f_A) from Synthesis Stoichiometry

> [!NOTE]
> In controlled polymerisation, the volume fraction of block A in a diblock is set by the **monomer conversion** and **monomer feed ratio**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Moles of monomer A converted:} \\
& n_A = [M]_0 \cdot X_A \cdot V
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Volume of polymer A formed:} \\
& V_A = \frac{n_A \cdot M_0^A}{\rho_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Total copolymer volume:} \\
& V_{\text{total}} = V_A + V_B
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Volume fraction of block A:} \\
& f_A = \frac{V_A}{V_A + V_B} = \frac{n_A M_0^A / \rho_A}{n_A M_0^A / \rho_A + n_B M_0^B / \rho_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Simplification (equal densities ρ_A = ρ_B = ρ):
> f_A = (n_A · M_0^A) / (n_A · M_0^A + n_B · M_0^B)

### 3.6 Worked Example: Identifying a Copolymer from a Structural Description

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem (3 marks):** "A polymer consists of a polybutadiene main chain with polystyrene side chains. Identify the copolymer type and give one industrial example."

**Solution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Structure has a main chain (PBd) and side chains (PS).} \\
& \text{This is a BRANCHED architecture, not end-to-end.} \\
& \rightarrow \textbf{Graft Copolymer (PBd-g-PS)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{This is exactly the structure of HIPS} \\
& \text{(High Impact Polystyrene) — used in refrigerator liners,}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{disposable cups, and CD cases. The graft copolymer} \\
& \text{provides toughening of the brittle PS matrix.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Model Answer (3 marks):** Graft copolymer; example — HIPS; mechanism — grafting-from during bulk polymerisation of styrene in the presence of dissolved polybutadiene.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Copolymer Classification Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid flowchart is a **board-exam revision aid** — students should be able to draw a simplified version of this hierarchy from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Copolymers"] --> B1["Linear Topology"]
    A0 --> B2["Branched Topology"]
    B1 --> C1["Statistical or Random"]
    B1 --> C2["Alternating"]
    B1 --> C3["Block Copolymer"]
    B2 --> C4["Graft Copolymer"]
    C3 --> D1["Diblock AB"]
    C3 --> D2["Triblock ABA"]
    C3 --> D3["Multiblock ABABn"]
    C3 --> D4["Star Block"]
    C4 --> E1["Regular Graft"]
    C4 --> E2["Irregular Graft"]
    C4 --> E3["Comb like Graft"]
    C1 --> F1["Example SBR"]
    C2 --> F2["Example Maleic Anhydride Styrene"]
    C3 --> F3["Example SBS SEBS"]
    C4 --> F4["Example HIPS ABS"]
```

### 4.2 Block Copolymer Architectures — Detailed Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Diblock AB"
        node1["A block<br/>n repeating units"] --> node2["Covalent junction"] --> node3["B block<br/>m repeating units"]
    end
    subgraph "Triblock ABA"
        node4["A block"] --> node5["B block"] --> node6["A block"]
    end
    subgraph "Multiblock ABABn"
        node7["A"] --> node8["B"] --> node9["A"] --> node10["B"]
    end
    subgraph "Star Block"
        node11["Central Core"] --> node12["Arm AB"]
        node11 --> node13["Arm AB"]
        node11 --> node14["Arm AB"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Graft Copolymer Architecture — Detailed Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Backbone of Polymer A"
        b1["A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A-A"]
    end
    subgraph "Grafts of Polymer B"
        g1["B-B-B-B-B"]
        g2["B-B-B"]
        g3["B-B-B-B-B-B"]
    end
    b1 --- g1
    b1 --- g2
    b1 --- g3
    note["G_n = 3 grafts per 10 backbone A units = 0.3"]
```

### 4.4 Microphase Separation Morphology Map (Block Copolymers)

> [!NOTE]
> The morphology is a function of the volume fraction **f_A** of one block. The following Mermaid block is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow showing the morphology decision tree.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Diblock Copolymer AB"] --> B{"What is fA?"}
    B -->|"fA less than 0.20"| C["B spheres<br/>cubic packing in A matrix"]
    B -->|"0.20 to 0.35"| D["B cylinders<br/>hexagonal in A matrix"]
    B -->|"0.35 to 0.65"| E["Lamellae<br/>alternating A and B sheets"]
    B -->|"0.65 to 0.80"| F["A cylinders<br/>hexagonal in B matrix"]
    B -->|"fA greater than 0.80"| G["A spheres<br/>cubic packing in B matrix"]
    B -->|"very high chiN"| H["Gyroid network<br/>bicontinuous"]
    C --> I["Domain spacing d proportional to N power 2 over 3"]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.5 Synthesis Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need to make a block or graft copolymer"] --> q1{"Is living<br/>polymerisation feasible?"}
    q1 -->|"Yes, anionic"| r1["Sequential anionic polymerisation<br/>Example SBS"]
    q1 -->|"Yes, controlled radical"| r2["ATRP or RAFT or NMP<br/>Example PS-b-PMMA"]
    q1 -->|"No"| q2{"Are preformed chains available?"}
    q2 -->|"Yes, coupling desired"| r3["Click chemistry grafting onto<br/>Example azide alkyne"]
    q2 -->|"Yes, initiator on backbone"| r4["Grafting from<br/>Example HIPS"]
    q2 -->|"Macromonomer route"| r5["Grafting through<br/>Example comb polymers"]
    r1 --> end1["Block or Graft Copolymer"]
    r2 --> end1
    r3 --> end1
    r4 --> end1
    r5 --> end1
```

### 4.6 Comparative Topology Diagram — Block vs. Graft vs. Random

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Random"
        r1["A"] --> r2["B"] --> r3["A"] --> r4["B"] --> r5["A"] --> r6["A"] --> r7["B"] --> r8["A"]
    end
    subgraph "Block AB diblock"
        bl1["A"] --> bl2["A"] --> bl3["A"] --> bl4["A"] --> bl5["A"] --> bl6["B"] --> bl7["B"] --> bl8["B"] --> bl9["B"] --> bl10["B"]
    end
    subgraph "Graft"
        gr1["A"] --> gr2["A"] --> gr3["A"] --> gr4["A"] --> gr5["A"] --> gr6["A"]
        gr1 --- gr7["B-B-B-B-B"]
        gr3 --- gr8["B-B-B"]
        gr5 --- gr9["B-B-B-B-B-B"]
    end
```

### 4.7 Schematic: Grafting Strategies (From / Onto / Through)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Grafting FROM"
        gf1["Backbone A with initiator sites X"] --> gf2["X"]
        gf2 --> gf3["B-B-B-B-B (graft grows)"]
    end
    subgraph "Grafting ONTO"
        go1["Backbone A with functional group Y"] --- go2["Preformed B-B-B-B with group Z"]
        go1 --- go3["Coupling via Y plus Z reaction"]
    end
    subgraph "Grafting THROUGH"
        gt1["Monomer A in reactor"] --> gt2["Macromonomer B-B-B-B- with vinyl end"]
        gt2 --> gt3["Copolymerised into backbone"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
**Distinguish between a block copolymer and a graft copolymer with a suitable example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **block copolymer** is a linear macromolecule composed of two or more homopolymer segments joined **end-to-end** through covalent bonds. Example: **SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene)** — a triblock used as a thermoplastic elastomer.

> A **graft copolymer** is a branched macromolecule in which side chains of one monomer are covalently attached to a main chain of a different monomer. Example: **HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene)** — a polybutadiene backbone with polystyrene side chains, used in refrigerator liners and disposable cups.

> **Key Difference:** Block copolymers have an *end-to-end* (linear) topology, whereas graft copolymers have a *branching* (comb-like) topology. Both exhibit microphase separation due to immiscibility between the chemically distinct blocks/grafts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
**List any three methods of synthesising block copolymers. Why is free radical polymerisation unsuitable for block copolymer synthesis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Synthesis methods for block copolymers (3):**
> 1. **Anionic living polymerisation** (industrial SBS synthesis).
> 2. **Controlled radical polymerisation (ATRP/RAFT/NMP)** — versatile for vinyl monomers.
> 3. **Ring-opening polymerisation (ROP)** — for polyesters, polyethers like PEG-b-PLA.

> **Why free radical polymerisation is unsuitable:** Classical free radical polymerisation produces "dead" chains with no active chain-end after the first monomer conversion is complete. The reactive chain end is consumed by termination/biradical coupling, so a second monomer cannot be added sequentially to extend the chain into a block. Living/controlled methods are required to maintain an active chain end.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the mechanism of microphase separation in block copolymers. Discuss the various morphologies obtained with a neat sketch. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** When the A and B blocks of a block copolymer are chemically immiscible, the system minimises its free energy by undergoing **microphase separation** — segregating into nanodomains of order 10–100 nm, while the covalent junction between blocks prevents macroscopic phase separation.

> **Mechanism (3 marks):**
> 1. **Driving Force — Flory-Huggins χ parameter:** The A–B repulsion energy is captured by the **χ parameter**. A higher χ means greater immiscibility.
> 2. **ODT Criterion:** Microphase separation occurs when **χN > 10.5** (for symmetric diblock). Below this value, the copolymer remains disordered.
> 3. **Chain Stretching Penalty:** The covalent A–B junction forces chain stretching, which opposes macroscopic separation.
> 4. **Equilibrium Balance:** The equilibrium morphology balances interfacial energy (favours larger domains) and chain stretching energy (favours smaller domains), leading to periodic structures.

> **Morphologies (3 marks) — As a function of f_A:**
> - **Spheres (S):** f_A < 0.20 or f_A > 0.80
> - **Cylinders (C):** 0.20 < f_A < 0.35 or 0.65 < f_A < 0.80
> - **Lamellae (L):** 0.35 < f_A < 0.65
> - **Gyroid (G):** Very high χN
> - **Domain spacing d ≈ a · N^(2/3) · χ^(1/6)**

> **Sketch (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4):** A neat labelled sketch showing the morphology map with f_A on the x-axis and the corresponding structures drawn for each region.

---

**(b) Describe the synthesis, structure, properties, and applications of SBS triblock copolymer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Synthesis (2 marks):** SBS is synthesised by **sequential anionic living polymerisation** using an organolithium initiator (e.g., *n*-butyllithium). The order is:
> 1. Styrene is polymerised first → forms a "living" PSLi block.
> 2. Butadiene is added → forms a PBd block (PS-b-PBdLi).
> 3. A coupling agent (e.g., SiCl₄ or a difunctional silane) joins two PS-b-PBdLi chains to form the **S–B–S** triblock.
> (Alternatively, a three-step sequential addition can yield a linear S-B-S.)

> **Structure (1 mark):** Linear triblock architecture:
> Polystyrene (rigid, glassy, Tg ≈ 100 °C) — Polybutadiene (flexible, rubbery, Tg ≈ −90 °C) — Polystyrene (rigid, glassy).
> The two PS end-blocks form hard nanodomains dispersed in a soft PBd matrix.

> **Properties (2 marks):**
> - **Two glass transition temperatures** (Tg_PS ≈ 100 °C, Tg_PBd ≈ −90 °C) — confirmed by DSC.
> - **Thermoplastic Elastomer (TPE):** Behaves as a cross-linked rubber at room temperature (PS domains act as physical crosslinks) but flows as a thermoplastic above the PS Tg.
> - Excellent elasticity, reversible deformation, solvent resistance, and processability.

> **Applications (2 marks):**
> 1. **Footwear** — sports shoe soles.
> 2. **Adhesives** — hot-melt and pressure-sensitive adhesives.
> 3. **Bitumen modification** — roofing and road construction.
> 4. **Toys and automotive parts** — flexible yet tough components.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

**(a) Discuss the various methods of synthesising graft copolymers with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** Graft copolymers have a main chain (backbone) of one monomer and side chains (grafts) of another. The three primary synthesis strategies are:

> **1. Grafting-FROM (2 marks):**
> - Initiating groups are chemically installed onto a pre-formed backbone.
> - The second monomer is polymerised outward from these sites, growing the grafts *in situ*.
> - **Example:** **HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene)** — backbone radicals on polybutadiene are generated by H-abstraction, and styrene grows out as PS side chains. The graft copolymer at the PBd-PS interface toughens the PS matrix.
> - *Advantage:* High graft density possible.
> - *Disadvantage:* Limited control over graft length distribution.

> **2. Grafting-ONTO (2 marks):**
> - Both the backbone and grafts are pre-synthesised separately.
> - They are coupled via a "click" reaction (e.g., azide-alkyne cycloaddition, esterification, or amide coupling).
> - **Example:** PEG side chains grafted onto a poly(L-lysine) backbone via NHS-ester chemistry, used in biomedical applications.
> - *Advantage:* Independent control of backbone and graft length.
> - *Disadvantage:* Steric crowding limits graft density.

> **3. Grafting-THROUGH (Macromonomer Method) (1.5 marks):**
> - Short polymer chains bearing a polymerisable end-group (macromonomers) are copolymerised with a low-MW comonomer.
> - **Example:** Poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA) macromonomer copolymerised with methyl methacrylate to form PMMA-g-PEG comb copolymers.
> - *Advantage:* Well-defined graft distribution.
> - *Disadvantage:* Reduced macromonomer reactivity.

> **Other Methods (0.5 marks):** Radiation-induced grafting (used for membrane modification), plasma-induced grafting, and photografting.

---

**(b) Explain the synthesis, structure, properties, and applications of ABS (Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene) graft copolymer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** ABS is a **terpolymer** of three monomers — Acrylonitrile (A), Butadiene (B), and Styrene (S) — and is the most commercially important graft copolymer, produced globally in millions of tonnes annually.

> **Synthesis (2 marks):**
> ABS is produced by a **two-step emulsion graft copolymerisation**:
> 1. **Step 1 — Polybutadiene latex:** A polybutadiene (PBd) latex is first prepared by emulsion polymerisation of butadiene.
> 2. **Step 2 — Grafting:** Styrene and acrylonitrile are added to the PBd latex along with an initiator. The initiator radicals abstract allylic H from PBd, generating backbone radicals. Styrene-co-acrylonitrile (SAN) grafts grow from these sites, forming **PBd-g-SAN** graft copolymer.
> 3. Free styrene-acrylonitrile also homopolymerises to form a continuous SAN matrix.

> **Structure (1 mark):** A **SAN (Styrene-Acrylonitrile) continuous matrix** with dispersed **PBd rubber particles**, where the PBd particle surface is decorated with **SAN grafted chains**. The grafts act as *compatibilisers* between the rubber and the matrix.

> **Properties (2 marks):**
> - **Tough and rigid** — high impact strength (notched Izod ≈ 200–400 J/m).
> - **Two Tg values** (Tg_PBd ≈ −85 °C, Tg_SAN ≈ 110 °C).
> - **Heat resistance** (from acrylonitrile units).
> - **Chemical resistance** (from acrylonitrile).
> - **Glossy surface finish** (from styrene).
> - **Processable** by injection moulding, extrusion, and 3D printing.

> **Applications (1 mark):**
> 1. **Automotive:** Dashboard components, interior trims, bumper covers.
> 2. **Consumer electronics:** Laptop and mobile phone housings, keyboard keys.
> 3. **Pipes and fittings:** Drainage systems, pressure pipes.
> 4. **LEGO bricks** and toys.
> 5. **Medical devices** (sterilisable grade).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing **block copolymer** with **graft copolymer** in the introduction — this immediately costs 1–2 marks. Always begin by stating the **topology** (linear end-to-end vs. branched comb-like).
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to mention that block copolymers require **living/controlled** polymerisation; free radical polymerisation gives **random** copolymers, not block copolymers. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** For **morphology questions**, students often forget to label the **volume fraction range** for each morphology on the diagram. Always annotate f_A ranges on the sketch.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** For the **HIPS/ABS question**, students forget to mention the **grafting-FROM mechanism** explicitly. Saying "styrene is mixed with polybutadiene" is not enough — describe the **radical abstraction + graft growth** mechanism.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to state the **industrial applications** — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for real-world applications. Always end with 2–3 specific applications.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** In numerical questions involving **block copolymer morphology**, students forget the **χN > 10.5 ODT criterion**. Always check whether the system is in the ordered or disordered regime.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this 2 hours before the exam:**

- ☐ A **copolymer** contains ≥ 2 distinct monomer types. Block and graft are two of the four main architectural families (alongside random and alternating).
- ☐ **Block** = linear, end-to-end topology. **Graft** = branched, comb-like topology with side chains perpendicular to the backbone.
- ☐ Block copolymer sub-types: **di (AB), tri (ABA), multi (ABABₙ), star**.
- ☐ Industrial example of block copolymer: **SBS** (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene) — thermoplastic elastomer.
- ☐ Industrial example of graft copolymer: **HIPS** (PBd-g-PS), **ABS** (PBd-g-SAN).
- ☐ Block copolymers require **living or controlled polymerisation** — anionic, ATRP, RAFT, NMP, ROP. Classical free-radical polymerisation is **not suitable**.
- ☐ Three grafting strategies: **Grafting-FROM** (HIPS), **Grafting-ONTO** (click chemistry), **Grafting-THROUGH** (macromonomer).
- ☐ Block copolymers undergo **microphase separation** when **χN > 10.5** (ODT criterion for symmetric diblock — Leibler 1980).
- ☐ Morphology depends on volume fraction f_A: **Spheres (f<0.20) → Cylinders (0.20–0.35) → Lamellae (0.35–0.65) → Cylinders (0.65–0.80) → Spheres (f>0.80)**.
- ☐ **Gyroid** morphology appears at very high χN.
- ☐ Domain spacing: **d ≈ 1.10 · a · N^(2/3) · χ^(1/6)** — higher MW gives larger domains.
- ☐ Block copolymers form **micelles** in selective solvents above the **CMC**.
- ☐ Number-average molecular weights are **additive** across blocks: M_n,total = Σ N_i · M_0^i.
- ☐ Grafting density **G_n ≤ 1** in practice due to steric crowding.
- ☐ Both block and graft copolymers typically show **two Tg values** (DSC evidence of microphase separation).
- ☐ **Flory-Huggins χ parameter** quantifies the A–B interaction energy; high χ = strong immiscibility.
- ☐ Key applications to memorise: **TPEs (SBS, SEBS)**, **toughened plastics (HIPS, ABS)**, **drug delivery micelles (PEG-b-PLA)**, **block copolymer lithography (sub-10 nm patterning)**, **compatibilisers in polymer blends**, **nanoporous membranes**.
- ☐ **Difference between random and block:** Random = statistical sequence, single phase. Block = long contiguous runs, two phases (microphase-separated).
- ☐ **Mnemonic for morphologies (S → C → L → C → S):** **"Some Cute Little Cats Sit"** — Spheres, Cylinders, Lamellae, Cylinders, Spheres as f_A increases.
- ☐ Block copolymer research was pioneered by **Leibler (1980)** — mean-field theory of microphase separation.
- ☐ **SBS** is a **thermoplastic elastomer (TPE)** — physical crosslinks from PS domains, not chemical crosslinks.
- ☐ **Grafting-FROM** is the most industrially scalable strategy; **grafting-ONTO** gives the best control.
- ☐ Always distinguish **block copolymer** (linear) from **graft copolymer** (branched) in the very first line of your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copolymerization Drift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Copolymerization Drift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — COPOLYMERIZATION DRIFT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copolymerization Drift (also termed Composition Drift):** The progressive, time-dependent change in the instantaneous composition of both the **unreacted monomer feed** and the **copolymer being formed** during a copolymerization reaction, arising from the preferential (unequal) consumption of the two comonomers due to their differing relative reactivities.

In a free-radical (or ionic) copolymerization of two monomers **M₁** and **M₂**, the four propagation rate constants are:

$$k_{11},\ k_{12},\ k_{21},\ k_{22}$$

The **reactivity ratios** are defined as:

$$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}, \qquad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}$$

Because $r_1 \neq r_2$ in most practical systems, one monomer is incorporated into the growing chain faster than the other. As polymerization proceeds, the feed is enriched in the *less reactive* monomer, and the **instantaneous copolymer composition shifts continuously**. This phenomenon is *copolymerization drift*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Ingredient Recipe"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are cooking a dish requiring **2 cups of rice** and **1 cup of lentils**, but you discover mid-cooking that the rice absorbs water 3× faster than the lentils. By the time the rice is done, there are still plenty of raw lentils left, and the *flavor of the dish changes* from start to finish. The "taste" (copolymer composition) **drifts** because the two ingredients are depleted at different rates.

In a copolymer reactor:
- **Rice (faster monomer)** → depleted early; the first chains formed are **rice-rich**.
- **Lentils (slower monomer)** → left behind; the last chains formed are **lentil-rich**.
- The **overall (cumulative) copolymer** is a heterogeneous blend of chains with a *distribution* of compositions.

### 1.3 Why Drift Matters — Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Copolymerization drift directly impacts:
> 1. **Product consistency** — physical, mechanical, and thermal properties vary across chains.
> 2. **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** — composition heterogeneity broadens the $T_g$ transition.
> 3. **Process control** — requires semi-batch (feed policies) or azeotropic composition selection.
> 4. **End-use performance** — optical clarity, impact strength, and chemical resistance depend on uniform composition.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Reactivity ratio of M₁ | $r_1$ | $0.01 - 25$ |
| Reactivity ratio of M₂ | $r_2$ | $0.01 - 25$ |
| Product $r_1 \cdot r_2$ | $r_1 r_2$ | $0 - 1$ (ideal range) |
| Azeotropic feed (M₁) | $f_{1,az}$ | $0 - 1$ |
| Conversion | $X$ | $0 - 1$ |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The drift phenomenon unfolds in **four sequential stages** during a batch copolymerization:

1. **Initial mixing** — Feed composition $f_{1,0}$, $f_{2,0}$ established in the reactor.
2. **Early propagation** — Reactive monomer consumed preferentially; chains formed are rich in the faster monomer.
3. **Intermediate stage** — Feed becomes depleted in the fast monomer; the instantaneous composition of newly-formed chains shifts (the *drift*).
4. **Late stage** — Slow monomer dominates the residual feed; final chains are nearly homopolymeric in the slow monomer.

> [!NOTE]
> The cumulative copolymer is a **composite** of all chains formed at different times — meaning drift produces a *compositionally heterogeneous* product, which broadens physical properties.

## 2.2 The Mayo–Lewis (Instantaneous Composition) Equation

The **instantaneous** mole fraction of M₁ in the copolymer, $F_1$, is related to the feed mole fraction $f_1$ by:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^{2} + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^{2}}$$

Equivalent in differential form (consumption):

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{r_2 [M_2] + [M_1]}$$

**Drift is absent only when** $F_1 = f_1$ for *all* conversions — this occurs at the **azeotropic composition**.

### 2.3 The Azeotropic Composition

Setting $F_1 = f_1$ in the Mayo–Lewis equation and solving for $f_1$:

$$f_{1,az} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Azeotrope exists **only if** $r_1 < 1$ and $r_2 < 1$. At this single feed composition, drift vanishes and the copolymer is compositionally uniform throughout conversion.

## 2.4 Skeist's Equation — Drift with Conversion

Let $M$ = total unreacted monomer concentration, $M_0$ = initial total monomer concentration, $X$ = fractional conversion = $1 - M/M_0$.

A differential material balance gives the **Skeist equation**:

$$\frac{dM}{M} = \frac{df_1}{F_1 - f_1}$$

Integrating from initial feed $(M_0, f_{1,0})$ to any state $(M, f_1)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right) = \int_{f_{1,0}}^{f_1} \frac{df_1}{F_1(f_1) - f_1}$$

This equation predicts **how the feed composition $f_1$ changes as conversion proceeds** — the mathematical heart of drift analysis.

## 2.5 Meyer–Lowry Equation — Cumulative Copolymer Composition

The **cumulative (average)** composition of all polymer formed up to conversion $X$ is:

$$\langle F_1 \rangle = \frac{f_{1,0} - (1 - X) f_1}{X}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\langle F_1 \rangle = \frac{f_{1,0} - (M/M_0) f_1}{1 - (M/M_0)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At low conversion ($X \to 0$), $\langle F_1 \rangle \to F_1$ (the instantaneous value). At high conversion, $\langle F_1 \rangle$ deviates significantly due to drift.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ | Reactivity ratio of M₁ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$ | Reactivity ratio of M₂ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_1 = (r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2)/(r_1 f_1^{2} + 2f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^{2})$ | Mayo–Lewis (instantaneous) | mole fraction |
| 4 | $f_{1,az} = (1 - r_2)/(2 - r_1 - r_2)$ | Azeotropic feed composition | mole fraction |
| 5 | $\ln(M/M_0) = \int_{f_{1,0}}^{f_1} df_1 / (F_1 - f_1)$ | Skeist equation (drift) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\langle F_1 \rangle = (f_{1,0} - (1-X) f_1)/X$ | Meyer–Lowry (cumulative) | mole fraction |
| 7 | $X = 1 - M/M_0$ | Conversion | dimensionless |
| 8 | $r_1 r_2 \to 1$ | Ideal copolymerization | dimensionless |
| 9 | $r_1 r_2 \to 0$ | Alternating tendency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $r_1, r_2 > 1$ | Blocky tendency (high drift) | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Drift Behaviour vs. Reactivity Ratio Product

| Case | $r_1 r_2$ | Behaviour | Drift Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal random | $r_1 = r_2 = 1$ | Bernoullian | **None** (no drift) |
| Ideal non-random | $r_1 r_2 = 1$, $r_1 \neq r_2$ | Penultimate-independent | **Pronounced** |
| Alternating | $r_1 \to 0$, $r_2 \to 0$ | Alternating | **None at $f_1 = 0.5$** |
| Azeotropic | $r_1 < 1$, $r_2 < 1$ | Single azeotrope | **None at $f_{1,az}$** |
| Block-forming | $r_1 > 1$, $r_2 > 1$ | Blocky copolymer | **Very high** |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mayo–Lewis Equation

**Step 1 — Define the four propagation steps:**

$$M_1^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} M_1^{\bullet}$$

$$M_1^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} M_2^{\bullet}$$

$$M_2^{\bullet} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} M_1^{\bullet}$$

$$M_2^{\bullet} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} M_2^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state approximation** to the active chain-end concentrations $[M_1^{\bullet}]$ and $[M_2^{\bullet}]$:

$$\frac{d[M_1^{\bullet}]}{dt} = k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1] - k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] = 0$$

$$\frac{d[M_2^{\bullet}]}{dt} = k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] - k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1] = 0$$

Both yield the same relationship:

$$[M_1^{\bullet}] = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}\, [M_2^{\bullet}]$$

**Step 3 — Rate of consumption of each monomer:**

$$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$$

$$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio** and substitute the steady-state ratio $[M_1^{\bullet}]/[M_2^{\bullet}]$:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]}{k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k_{12}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]$:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1 \cdot \frac{[M_1^{\bullet}]}{[M_2^{\bullet}]} \cdot \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} + \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]}}{r_1 \cdot \frac{[M_1^{\bullet}]}{[M_2^{\bullet}]} \cdot \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} + 1}$$

After substitution of the steady-state ratio and simplification:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{r_2 [M_2] + [M_1]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mole fractions** using $f_1 = [M_1]/([M_1]+[M_2])$ and $F_1 = d[M_1]/(d[M_1]+d[M_2])$:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^{2} + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^{2}}$$

This is the **Mayo–Lewis equation** governing instantaneous composition. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation of Skeist's Equation (Drift Master Equation)

**Step 1 — Define total monomer concentration:**

$$M = [M_1] + [M_2], \qquad M_0 = [M_1]_0 + [M_2]_0$$

**Step 2 — Express feed composition in terms of $M$ and $f_1$:**

$$[M_1] = f_1 M, \qquad [M_2] = (1 - f_1) M$$

**Step 3 — Material balance on M₁:**

$$M_0 f_{1,0} = M f_1 + \text{(mol of M}_1 \text{ polymerized)}$$

Differentiate with respect to $M$ (where $f_1$ is now a function of $M$):

$$0 = f_1 + M \frac{df_1}{dM} + \frac{d[M_1]_{poly}}{dM}$$

**Step 4 — The amount of M₁ polymerized at any instant** per unit decrease in $M$ is exactly $F_1$ (the instantaneous copolymer composition):

$$\frac{d[M_1]_{poly}}{dM} = -F_1$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the material balance:**

$$0 = f_1 + M \frac{df_1}{dM} - F_1$$

Rearranging:

$$M \frac{df_1}{dM} = F_1 - f_1$$

Or in standard form:

$$\frac{dM}{M} = \frac{df_1}{F_1(f_1) - f_1}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate** from the initial state $(M_0, f_{1,0})$ to $(M, f_1)$:

$$\int_{M_0}^{M} \frac{dM}{M} = \int_{f_{1,0}}^{f_1} \frac{df_1}{F_1(f_1) - f_1}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right) = \int_{f_{1,0}}^{f_1} \frac{df_1}{F_1(f_1) - f_1}$$

This is the **Skeist equation**, the cornerstone for predicting drift. ✔

## 3.3 Derivation of the Meyer–Lowry Equation

**Step 1 — Total polymer mass produced** at conversion $X$ (per unit initial mass):

$$P = X = 1 - \frac{M}{M_0}$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on M₁ in the polymer phase:**

$$\langle F_1 \rangle \cdot X = f_{1,0} - (1 - X) f_1$$

The left side is the total M₁ in the polymer; the right side is initial M₁ minus residual M₁.

**Step 3 — Solve for $\langle F_1 \rangle$:**

$$\langle F_1 \rangle = \frac{f_{1,0} - (1 - X) f_1}{X}$$

In terms of $M/M_0$:

$$\langle F_1 \rangle = \frac{f_{1,0} - (M/M_0) f_1}{1 - (M/M_0)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Meyer–Lowry equation requires $f_1$ at the conversion of interest, which is found *via the Skeist equation*. The two equations together fully describe drift.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Drift Calculation

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.65$, $r_2 = 1.20$, initial feed $f_{1,0} = 0.40$, target conversion $X = 0.50$.

**Step 1 — Find azeotropic composition** (only valid if both $r < 1$; here $r_2 = 1.20 > 1$, so **no azeotrope exists** — drift is unavoidable).

**Step 2 — Find $f_1$ at 50% conversion using Skeist equation** (numerical integration).

We tabulate $F_1(f_1)$ for the relevant range:

| $f_1$ | $f_2$ | Numerator $r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2$ | Denominator | $F_1$ | $F_1 - f_1$ | $1/(F_1 - f_1)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.40 | 0.60 | $0.65(0.16) + 0.24 = 0.344$ | $0.344 + 0.48 + 1.20(0.36) = 1.256$ | 0.274 | $-0.126$ | $-7.94$ |
| 0.30 | 0.70 | $0.65(0.09) + 0.21 = 0.2685$ | $0.2685 + 0.42 + 1.20(0.49) = 1.2765$ | 0.210 | $-0.090$ | $-11.11$ |
| 0.20 | 0.80 | $0.65(0.04) + 0.16 = 0.186$ | $0.186 + 0.32 + 1.20(0.64) = 1.274$ | 0.146 | $-0.054$ | $-18.52$ |
| 0.10 | 0.90 | $0.65(0.01) + 0.09 = 0.0965$ | $0.0965 + 0.18 + 1.20(0.81) = 1.249$ | 0.0773 | $-0.0227$ | $-44.05$ |

**Step 3 — Simpson's rule integration** from $f_{1,0} = 0.40$ to $f_1 = 0.10$:

$$\int_{0.40}^{0.10} \frac{df_1}{F_1 - f_1} \approx \int_{0.40}^{0.10} -K(f_1)\, df_1$$

The integral value (in magnitude) corresponds to $\ln(M/M_0)$. With finer tabulation and Simpson's rule:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M}{M_0}\right) = \int_{0.40}^{f_1} \frac{df_1}{F_1 - f_1}$$

At $X = 0.50$, $M/M_0 = 0.50$, so $\ln(0.50) = -0.693$.

By numerical inversion, at $X = 0.50$: **$f_1 \approx 0.197$**.

**Step 4 — Compute cumulative composition (Meyer–Lowry):**

$$\langle F_1 \rangle = \frac{0.40 - (0.50)(0.197)}{0.50} = \frac{0.40 - 0.0985}{0.50} = 0.603$$

**Interpretation:** The feed started at $f_{1,0} = 0.40$ M₁. By 50% conversion, the feed has dropped to $f_1 \approx 0.20$ M₁ (depleted). The cumulative copolymer contains **60.3% M₁ units**, even though the feed was only 40% M₁ — a clear signature of **drift**.
