# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define copolymerization drift. Under what condition is drift completely absent?**

**Model Answer:**

Copolymerization drift (composition drift) is the progressive change in the **instantaneous composition of the copolymer** and the **unreacted monomer feed** as conversion proceeds, due to the unequal consumption rates of the two comonomers characterized by reactivity ratios $r_1$ and $r_2$.

**Drift is absent when the system is polymerized at the azeotropic feed composition**, given by:

$$f_{1,az} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}$$

This requires both $r_1 < 1$ and $r_2 < 1$. At this point, $F_1 = f_1$ for *all* conversions.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark condition, 1 mark formula)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: State the Mayo–Lewis equation. What does it predict?**

**Model Answer:**

The Mayo–Lewis equation relates the **instantaneous** mole fraction $F_1$ of monomer M₁ in a copolymer to the **feed** mole fraction $f_1$:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^{2} + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^{2}}$$

It predicts:
- The composition of polymer being formed at any instant.
- The **azeotropic composition** (when $F_1 = f_1$).
- The tendency toward alternation, randomness, or blockiness.

It is the **starting point** for analysing copolymerization drift.

*(3 marks — 1 mark statement, 1 mark interpretation, 1 mark link to drift)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Define reactivity ratios $r_1$ and $r_2$. Derive the Mayo–Lewis equation using the steady-state approximation for active chain-end concentrations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a copolymerization of styrene ($M_1$, $r_1 = 0.65$) and butadiene ($M_2$, $r_2 = 1.38$) at a feed composition $f_1 = 0.30$:
   (i) Calculate the instantaneous copolymer composition $F_1$.
   (ii) Determine whether an azeotropic composition exists. If yes, find it; if not, explain why.
   (iii) Comment on the expected drift behaviour. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A (a)

**Step 1:** Define reactivity ratios:
- $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ — preference of M₁-ended radical to add M₁ over M₂.
- $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$ — preference of M₂-ended radical to add M₂ over M₁.

**Step 2:** Apply steady state to active centers $[M_1^{\bullet}]$ and $[M_2^{\bullet}]$:
- Rate of formation of $[M_1^{\bullet}]$ = rate of disappearance.
- $k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1] = k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2]$.
- Therefore: $[M_1^{\bullet}]/[M_2^{\bullet}] = (k_{21}[M_1])/(k_{12}[M_2])$.

**Step 3:** Write rates of consumption:
- $-d[M_1]/dt = k_{11}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_1]$.
- $-d[M_2]/dt = k_{12}[M_1^{\bullet}][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^{\bullet}][M_2]$.

**Step 4:** Take ratio, substitute steady-state ratio, simplify to:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{r_2 [M_2] + [M_1]}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to mole fractions: $f_1 = [M_1]/M$ and $F_1 = d[M_1]/(d[M_1]+d[M_2])$:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^{2} + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^{2}}$$

*(7 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks steady-state, 2 marks ratio derivation, 2 marks conversion to mole fraction)*

#### Model Solution — Part A (b)

**(i) Instantaneous $F_1$ at $f_1 = 0.30$, $f_2 = 0.70$:**

- Numerator: $r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2 = 0.65(0.09) + (0.30)(0.70) = 0.0585 + 0.21 = 0.2685$.
- Denominator: $0.2685 + 2(0.30)(0.70) + r_2 f_2^{2} = 0.2685 + 0.42 + 1.38(0.49) = 0.2685 + 0.42 + 0.6762 = 1.3647$.
- $F_1 = 0.2685 / 1.3647 = 0.1967$.

**The instantaneous copolymer contains 19.67% M₁** even though the feed has 30% M₁. This already shows drift onset.

**(ii) Azeotrope check:**

For an azeotrope: $r_1 < 1$ (yes, 0.65) **AND** $r_2 < 1$ (no, 1.38).

Since $r_2 > 1$, **no azeotrope exists** for this system. Drift is unavoidable at any feed composition.

**(iii) Drift behaviour:**

- $r_1 r_2 = 0.65 \times 1.38 = 0.897$ (close to 1) — ideal-like random tendency.
- Because $r_2 > 1$, the M₂-ended radical prefers to add M₂, so M₂ is consumed faster.
- Feed will be **enriched in M₁** as conversion proceeds.
- $F_1$ will **increase** with conversion (cumulative $\langle F_1 \rangle$ becomes M₁-rich).
- This is a classic **drift-prone system** with no easy operational cure.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for calculation, 2 marks azeotrope, 2 marks drift analysis)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is the Skeist equation? Derive it from a material balance on the monomer mixture during copolymerization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monomer pair has $r_1 = 0.40$ and $r_2 = 0.60$. The initial feed is $f_{1,0} = 0.50$.
   (i) Calculate the azeotropic feed composition.
   (ii) If the polymerization is run to 40% conversion, use the Skeist equation (numerically or graphically) to estimate the new feed composition $f_1$.
   (iii) Calculate the cumulative copolymer composition $\langle F_1 \rangle$ using the Meyer–Lowry equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Step 1 — Statement of Skeist equation:**

The Skeist equation describes how the **feed composition drifts** as a function of conversion:

$$\frac{dM}{M} = \frac{df_1}{F_1 - f_1}$$

Integrated form: $\ln(M/M_0) = \int_{f_{1,0}}^{f_1} df_1 / (F_1 - f_1)$.

**Step 2 — Material balance derivation:**

Let $M$ = total unreacted monomer concentration, $M_0$ = initial value, $f_1$ = current feed mole fraction of M₁, $F_1$ = instantaneous copolymer composition.

Mass balance on M₁:
- Initial: $M_0 f_{1,0}$.
- Remaining in feed: $M f_1$.
- In polymer: $(M_0 - M) \langle F_1 \rangle$.

Differential form (per unit $M$ change):
- $0 = M(df_1/dM) + f_1 + F_1 \cdot (-1)$.
- The $-F_1$ accounts for the M₁ being consumed into the polymer at rate equal to the instantaneous composition.

Rearranging:
$$M \frac{df_1}{dM} = F_1 - f_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dM}{M} = \frac{df_1}{F_1 - f_1}$$

**Step 3 — Integration** yields the working form of Skeist's equation used to predict drift.

*(7 marks — 2 marks statement, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks interpretation)*

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**(i) Azeotropic composition:**

$$f_{1,az} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} = \frac{1 - 0.60}{2 - 0.40 - 0.60} = \frac{0.40}{1.00} = 0.40$$

So the azeotrope lies at $f_{1,az} = 0.40$. Since $f_{1,0} = 0.50 > 0.40$, we are **off-azeotrope** on the M₁-rich side — drift is expected.

**(ii) Skeist equation at $X = 0.40$:**

We need: $\ln(M/M_0) = \ln(1 - 0.40) = \ln(0.60) = -0.5108$.

Compute $F_1(f_1)$ for the range $0.40$ to $0.55$ (since $f_1$ will increase as M₁ is depleted):

| $f_1$ | $F_1$ (Mayo–Lewis) | $F_1 - f_1$ | $1/(F_1 - f_1)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 0.5263 | 0.0263 | 38.02 |
| 0.48 | 0.5087 | 0.0287 | 34.84 |
| 0.46 | 0.4897 | 0.0297 | 33.67 |
| 0.44 | 0.4693 | 0.0293 | 34.13 |
| 0.42 | 0.4473 | 0.0273 | 36.63 |
| 0.40 | 0.4235 | 0.0235 | 42.55 |

Note: At $f_1 = 0.40$, $F_1 = 0.4235 > 0.40$, confirming M₁ is being incorporated *faster* than its feed fraction, so the feed drifts *toward* the azeotrope (0.40).

Numerical integration using trapezoidal rule (h = 0.02):

$$\int_{0.50}^{0.42} \frac{df_1}{F_1 - f_1} \approx 0.02 \times \left[\frac{38.02 + 36.63}{2} + 34.84 + 33.67 + 34.13\right]$$

$$= 0.02 \times [37.33 + 34.84 + 33.67 + 34.13] = 0.02 \times 139.97 = -2.80$$

Wait — the sign convention: we are integrating *from* 0.50 *to* 0.42 (decreasing $f_1$):

$$\int_{0.50}^{0.42} \frac{df_1}{F_1 - f_1} = -2.80$$

This represents $\ln(M/M_0) = -2.80$, i.e., $M/M_0 = e^{-2.80} = 0.061$, corresponding to $X = 0.939$ (94% conversion) — much more than 40%!

**Correction via more careful integration:** The integral must equal exactly $\ln(0.60) = -0.511$. Iterating to find the $f_1$ where the integral equals $-0.511$:

For the small interval near 0.50:
- Average $1/(F_1 - f_1)$ between 0.50 and 0.49 ≈ 36.4.
- $\Delta f_1 = -0.511 / 36.4 = -0.0140$.

Therefore: **$f_1 \approx 0.486$ at 40% conversion**.

The feed has drifted only slightly from 0.50 to 0.486 — because we are close to the azeotrope and the integral $1/(F_1 - f_1)$ is large (small denominator), so a small change in $f_1$ produces a large change in $\ln(M/M_0)$.

**(iii) Meyer–Lowry cumulative composition:**

$$\langle F_1 \rangle = \frac{f_{1,0} - (1 - X) f_1}{X} = \frac{0.50 - (0.60)(0.486)}{0.40} = \frac{0.50 - 0.2916}{0.40} = \frac{0.2084}{0.40} = 0.521$$

So the cumulative copolymer contains 52.1% M₁. Compare to the instantaneous $F_1$ at the current feed ($F_1 \approx 0.520$ at $f_1 = 0.486$) — close because we are still near azeotrope at 40% conversion.

*(7 marks — 2 marks azeotrope, 3 marks numerical integration, 2 marks Meyer–Lowry)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in Skeist equation** — Students often write $dM/M = df_1 / (f_1 - F_1)$ with the wrong sign. The correct form is $dM/M = df_1 / (F_1 - f_1)$. *Memorize the order*.
> 2. **Azeotrope condition** — Students forget the condition $r_1 < 1$ **AND** $r_2 < 1$. A common error: applying the formula even when $r_1 r_2 > 1$ (no real azeotrope).
> 3. **Meyer–Lowry vs Instantaneous** — Examiners often ask "what is $F_1$?" expecting the **instantaneous** value (Mayo–Lewis) and students give the **cumulative** value (Meyer–Lowry) — or vice versa. Read carefully!
> 4. **Unit confusion** — Reactivity ratios are dimensionless. Conversion $X$ is dimensionless. Mole fractions are dimensionless. No units should appear in these answers.
> 5. **Neglecting conversion limit** — At very high conversion, the Mayo–Lewis equation may give non-physical results (e.g., $F_1 < 0$ or $F_1 > 1$). Always verify the range.
> 6. **Forgetting steady-state assumption** — In derivations, examiners expect the **steady-state approximation** to be *explicitly stated and applied* to both $[M_1^{\bullet}]$ and $[M_2^{\bullet}]$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Copolymerization Drift**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Copolymerization drift = change in feed & copolymer composition with conversion.
- ☐ Caused by unequal monomer reactivity (different $k_{11}, k_{12}, k_{21}, k_{22}$).
- ☐ Quantified by reactivity ratios $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$.

**Key Equations (must memorize):**
- ☐ Mayo–Lewis: $F_1 = (r_1 f_1^{2} + f_1 f_2)/(r_1 f_1^{2} + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^{2})$.
- ☐ Azeotrope: $f_{1,az} = (1 - r_2)/(2 - r_1 - r_2)$ — only valid if both $r < 1$.
- ☐ Skeist: $\ln(M/M_0) = \int df_1/(F_1 - f_1)$ — predicts *drift*.
- ☐ Meyer–Lowry: $\langle F_1 \rangle = (f_{1,0} - (1-X) f_1)/X$ — cumulative composition.
- ☐ Conversion: $X = 1 - M/M_0$.

**Drift Behaviour Rules:**
- ☐ $r_1 = r_2 = 1$ → no drift (Bernoullian ideal).
- ☐ $r_1 r_2 = 1$, $r_1 \neq r_2$ → drift pronounced.
- ☐ Azeotropic feed → no drift.
- ☐ $r_1, r_2 > 1$ → blocky, large drift.
- ☐ $r_1, r_2 \to 0$ → alternating, drift only at off-50:50 feeds.

**Engineering Solutions to Drift:**
- ☐ Choose azeotropic feed (if it exists).
- ☐ Use semi-batch with feed-on-demand.
- ☐ Use CSTR for steady-state operation.
- ☐ Stop at low conversion.
- ☐ Choose comonomer pair with $r_1 \approx r_2 \approx 1$.

**Common Exam Traps:**
- ☐ Distinguish **instantaneous** $F_1$ from **cumulative** $\langle F_1 \rangle$.
- ☐ The Mayo–Lewis equation gives $F_1$ *at a given moment*; the Skeist + Meyer–Lowry pair gives the *integrated drift history*.
- ☐ Azeotrope *eliminates* drift, not just *reduces* it.
- ☐ Always check $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ and $0 \le f_1 \le 1$ for physical validity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Copolymer Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Copolymer Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Copolymer Equation**, also known as the **Mayo–Lewis Equation** or the **Terminal Copolymerization Model**, is a fundamental kinetic expression in polymer engineering that quantitatively relates the instantaneous mole fraction of two monomers incorporated into a growing copolymer chain to the mole fraction of those same monomers present in the reaction feed. It forms the theoretical backbone of free-radical copolymerization design and is expressed through two experimentally accessible parameters called **reactivity ratios** ($r_1$ and $r_2$).

Mathematically, the copolymer equation is given as:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}$$

where:
- $[M_1]$ and $[M_2]$ are the molar concentrations of monomers 1 and 2 in the feed.
- $d[M_1]$ and $d[M_2]$ are the infinitesimal quantities of monomers 1 and 2 consumed.
- $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$ are the monomer reactivity ratios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This derivation is a **high-yield, frequently repeated 14-mark question** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) of PCPOT205. Students must memorize the four propagation steps, the steady-state assumption, and the final instantaneous composition equation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a dance floor with two types of dancers — say, **Red Caps** ($M_1$) and **Blue Caps** ($M_2$). At any moment, the *lead dancer* (the active chain-end radical) has a personal preference for which partner to grab next. If the lead is "picky" and strongly prefers Red Caps, it will keep grabbing Reds regardless of the crowd composition — leading to long stretches of red in the chain. If it is "indifferent," it grabs whatever is closest.

- The **reactivity ratio** $r_1 > 1$ means the radical ending in $M_1$ is **picky for its own type** (tendency toward homopolymerization of $M_1$).
- The **reactivity ratio** $r_1 < 1$ means the radical ending in $M_1$ **prefers the other monomer** $M_2$ (tendency toward alternation).
- The **reactivity ratio** $r_1 = r_2 = 1$ means a **completely random (ideal) copolymer**.

The Mayo–Lewis equation essentially translates these "personal preferences" into a precise mathematical prediction of the resulting copolymer chain composition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Notation (KTU Board Approved):**
> Subscript notation $k_{ij}$ always means: the rate constant for the addition of monomer $j$ to a growing chain radical ending with monomer $i$. The diagonal terms $k_{11}$ and $k_{22}$ represent **homopropagation**, while the off-diagonal $k_{12}$ and $k_{21}$ represent **cross-propagation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Foundational Assumptions of the Terminal Model

The derivation rests on **four key assumptions** that the student must explicitly state in any KTU board answer:

1. **Chain-end influence only (Terminal Model):** The reactivity of a propagating chain radical depends *solely* on the identity of the terminal monomer unit. Earlier units in the chain are assumed to exert a **penultimate effect of zero magnitude**.
2. **Steady-State Approximation:** The total concentration of active chain ends remains essentially constant during propagation; the rate of formation equals the rate of termination.
3. **Long-chain approximation:** Propagation steps are far more frequent than initiation or termination, allowing rate equations to be simplified.
4. **Equal radical reactivity regardless of chain length:** The reactivity of a chain radical is independent of the number of monomer units already added.

### The Four Propagation Steps (Logic Ladder)

In a binary copolymerization of monomers $M_1$ and $M_2$, the following four reactions are possible at the active chain end:

- **Step 1 — Homopropagation of $M_1$:** $\sim M_1^{\cdot} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} \sim M_1 M_1^{\cdot}$
- **Step 2 — Cross-propagation (add $M_2$ to radical ending in $M_1$):** $\sim M_1^{\cdot} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} \sim M_1 M_2^{\cdot}$
- **Step 3 — Homopropagation of $M_2$:** $\sim M_2^{\cdot} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} \sim M_2 M_2^{\cdot}$
- **Step 4 — Cross-propagation (add $M_1$ to radical ending in $M_2$):** $\sim M_2^{\cdot} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} \sim M_2 M_1^{\cdot}$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the complete set of equations the KTU board examiner expects you to reproduce, derive, or apply.

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Expression | Units / Range | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $r_1$ | $k_{11} / k_{12}$ | Dimensionless (0 to $\infty$) | Reactivity ratio of monomer 1 |
| $r_2$ | $k_{22} / k_{21}$ | Dimensionless (0 to $\infty$) | Reactivity ratio of monomer 2 |
| $f_1$ | $[M_1]/([M_1]+[M_2])$ | Mole fraction (0 to 1) | Mole fraction of $M_1$ in the feed |
| $F_1$ | $d[M_1]/(d[M_1]+d[M_2])$ | Mole fraction (0 to 1) | Mole fraction of $M_1$ in the copolymer |
| Mayo–Lewis (Differential Form) | $d[M_1]/d[M_2] = (r_1[M_1]+[M_2])/([M_1]+r_2[M_2])$ | — | Instantaneous composition |
| Mayo–Lewis (Copolymer Form) | $F_1 = (r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2)/(r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2)$ | — | Mole fraction in polymer |
| Q-e Scheme (Alfrey–Price) | $r_1 = (Q_1/Q_2) \cdot \exp[-e_1(e_1-e_2)]$ | — | Semi-empirical prediction |
| $r_1 \cdot r_2$ product | $r_1 r_2 \approx 1$ | Ideal random copolymer | Special cases |
| $r_1 \cdot r_2$ product | $r_1 r_2 \to 0$ | Strong tendency to alternate | Special cases |
| $r_1 = r_2 = 1$ | — | Ideal random copolymer (azeotropic) | Special case |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The copolymer equation allows polymer chemists to **pre-engineer** the composition of styrene–butadiene rubber (SBR), ethylene–vinyl acetate (EVA), acrylonitrile–butadiene–styrene (ABS), and many commercial copolymers. By adjusting the feed ratio $f_1$, manufacturers control glass transition temperature ($T_g$), mechanical strength, and chemical resistance of the resulting material.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivation is presented in **exhaustive, KTU-board-valuation-grade** detail. Each algebraic transition is explicitly written and explained. No steps have been skipped.

### Step 1 — Define the Rate of Disappearance of Each Monomer

The total rate at which monomer $M_1$ is consumed is the sum of its consumption via both chain ends ($\sim M_1^{\cdot}$ and $\sim M_2^{\cdot}$):

$$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]$$

Similarly, the rate of disappearance of monomer $M_2$ is:

$$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{22}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_2] + k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2]$$

**Explanation:** $k_{11}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_1]$ is the rate at which a radical ending in $M_1$ adds another $M_1$, while $k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]$ is the rate at which a radical ending in $M_2$ adds $M_1$. Summing both gives the total consumption of $M_1$.

### Step 2 — Divide the Two Rate Equations to Eliminate Time

To obtain a composition relationship independent of time, we divide the first equation by the second:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]}{k_{22}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_2] + k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2]}$$

**Explanation:** The negative signs cancel because both numerators and denominators are positive rates of disappearance. The instantaneous ratio $d[M_1]/d[M_2]$ represents the **molar ratio in which the two monomers enter the copolymer** at that instant.

### Step 3 — Apply the Steady-State Assumption to Active Radicals

The total concentration of chain-end radicals is constant, which means the rate of **interconversion** between $[M_1^{\cdot}]$ and $[M_2^{\cdot}]$ is balanced. In other words, the rate at which $[M_1^{\cdot}]$ is converted to $[M_2^{\cdot}]$ equals the rate at which $[M_2^{\cdot}]$ is converted back to $[M_1^{\cdot}]$:

$$k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2] = k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]$$

Rearranging for the radical concentration ratio:

$$\frac{[M_1^{\cdot}]}{[M_2^{\cdot}]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$$

**Explanation:** This is the **steady-state principle for active chain ends** — a key KTU board expectation. It expresses that the populations of the two types of radicals remain dynamically balanced.

### Step 4 — Substitute the Radical Ratio into the Composition Equation

Let $x = \frac{[M_1^{\cdot}]}{[M_2^{\cdot}]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$. We now rewrite the numerator and denominator of Step 2 by factoring out the common radical concentration:

**Numerator transformation:**

$$k_{11}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1] = [M_2^{\cdot}] \left( k_{11} x [M_1] + k_{21}[M_1] \right)$$

**Denominator transformation:**

$$k_{22}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_2] + k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2] = [M_2^{\cdot}] \left( k_{22}[M_2] + k_{12} x [M_2] \right)$$

Therefore, the common factor $[M_2^{\cdot}]$ cancels:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11} x [M_1] + k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{22}[M_2] + k_{12} x [M_2]}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute $x$ and Simplify

Insert $x = \dfrac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$ into the numerator:

$$k_{11} x [M_1] + k_{21}[M_1] = k_{11} \cdot \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} \cdot [M_1] + k_{21}[M_1]$$

$$= \frac{k_{11} k_{21}}{k_{12}} \cdot \frac{[M_1]^2}{[M_2]} + k_{21}[M_1]$$

Factor out $k_{21}[M_1]$:

$$= k_{21}[M_1] \left( \frac{k_{11}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} + 1 \right) = k_{21}[M_1] \left( \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_2]} \right)$$

since $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$.

Now substitute $x$ into the denominator:

$$k_{22}[M_2] + k_{12} x [M_2] = k_{22}[M_2] + k_{12} \cdot \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} \cdot [M_2]$$

$$= k_{22}[M_2] + k_{21}[M_1]$$

Factor out $k_{21}$:

$$= k_{21} \left( \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}[M_2] + [M_1] \right) = k_{21} \left( r_2[M_2] + [M_1] \right)$$

since $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$.

### Step 6 — Final Cancellation and the Mayo–Lewis Equation

Substituting both simplified forms back into the rate ratio:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1] \left( \dfrac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_2]} \right)}{k_{21} \left( [M_1] + r_2[M_2] \right)}$$

The common factor $k_{21}$ cancels. Multiplying numerator and denominator by $[M_2]$:

$$\boxed{\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}}$$

**This is the famous Mayo–Lewis Copolymer Equation** (1944).

### Step 7 — Convert to the Mole-Fraction Form (Copolymer Form)

Let $f_1 = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_1]+[M_2]}$ and $f_2 = 1 - f_1$ be the feed mole fractions, and $F_1 = \dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_1]+d[M_2]}$ be the instantaneous mole fraction of $M_1$ in the copolymer.

Starting from $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{F_1}{1-F_1}$ and $[M_1]/[M_2] = f_1/f_2$, we substitute and simplify:

$$\frac{F_1}{1-F_1} = \frac{r_1 f_1 + f_2}{f_1 + r_2 f_2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$F_1(f_1 + r_2 f_2) = (1-F_1)(r_1 f_1 + f_2)$$

Expanding both sides:

$$F_1 f_1 + F_1 r_2 f_2 = r_1 f_1 + f_2 - F_1 r_1 f_1 - F_1 f_2$$

Collecting $F_1$ terms on the left:

$$F_1 f_1 + F_1 r_1 f_1 + F_1 r_2 f_2 + F_1 f_2 = r_1 f_1 + f_2$$

$$F_1 \left( f_1 + r_1 f_1 + r_2 f_2 + f_2 \right) = r_1 f_1 + f_2$$

$$F_1 \left( f_1(1 + r_1) + f_2(1 + r_2) \right) = r_1 f_1 + f_2$$

After standard algebraic manipulation (using $f_1 + f_2 = 1$ and $f_1^2 + f_1 f_2 = f_1$):

$$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The differential form (Mayo–Lewis) and the integrated form (Skeist's equation) are *different* things. The differential form gives **instantaneous** composition, while the integrated form tracks **cumulative** composition as conversion proceeds. The differential form is the one being derived above.

### Analytical Matrix — Special Cases of the Copolymer Equation

| Case | Condition | Copolymer Behaviour | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal (random) | $r_1 r_2 = 1$ | Random placement; $F_1 = r_1 f_1 / (r_1 f_1 + f_2)$ | Styrene–isoprene |
| Alternating | $r_1 = r_2 = 0$ | Strict alternation; $F_1 = 0.5$ always | Maleic anhydride–stilbene |
| Azeotropic | $r_1 < 1, r_2 < 1$ and $f_1 = f_2$ | Feed and copolymer have same composition | SBR (special compositions) |
| Block tendency | $r_1 > 1, r_2 > 1$ | Long runs of each monomer | Block copolymer precursors |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Mermaid Diagram 1 — Architecture of the Copolymerization Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Monomer System M1 and M2 in Feed"] --> B["Active Chain End Radical"]
    B --> C1["Radical ending in M1"]
    B --> C2["Radical ending in M2"]
    C1 --> P1["Add M1: k11, Homopropagation"]
    C1 --> P2["Add M2: k12, Cross Propagation"]
    C2 --> P3["Add M1: k21, Cross Propagation"]
    C2 --> P4["Add M2: k22, Homopropagation"]
    P1 --> D["Steady State Assumption Applied"]
    P2 --> D
    P3 --> D
    P4 --> D
    D --> E["Substitute Radical Ratio x"]
    E --> F["Mayo Lewis Differential Equation"]
    F --> G["Convert to Mole Fraction Form"]
    G --> H["Final Copolymer Composition F1"]
    H --> I["Predict Properties: Tg, Strength, Density"]
    I --> J["Industrial Application: SBR, EVA, ABS"]
```

### Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Block Flow of the Derivation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["INPUT: Feed Composition M1 and M2"] --> A2["STEP 1: Four Propagation Rate Equations"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 2: Divide Rate Equations to Eliminate dt"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 3: Apply Steady State for Radicals"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 4: Express Radical Ratio as k21 M1 over k12 M2"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 5: Substitute into Composition Ratio"]
    A6 --> A7["STEP 6: Define Reactivity Ratios r1 and r2"]
    A7 --> A8["STEP 7: Simplify and Cancel k21"]
    A8 --> A9["OUTPUT: Mayo Lewis Equation dM1 over dM2"]
    A9 --> A10["OUTPUT: Copolymer Form F1 as function of f1"]
```

### Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Copolymer Microstructure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Given: r1 and r2 for a Monomer Pair"] --> Q1{"Is r1 r2 approximately equal to 1?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| R1["IDEAL RANDOM COPOLYMER"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| Q2{"Are r1 and r2 both much less than 1?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| R2["ALTERNATING COPOLYMER"]
    Q2 -->|"No"| Q3{"Are r1 and r2 both greater than 1?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| R3["BLOCK COPOLYMER TENDENCY"]
    Q3 -->|"No"| R4["COMPLEX MICROSTRUCTURE - Numerical Integration Needed"]
    R1 --> X["Styrene Butadiene Rubber Type"]
    R2 --> Y["Maleic Anhydride Comonomers"]
    R3 --> Z["Block Copolymer Precursors"]
    R4 --> W["Specialty Engineering Plastics"]
```

### Mermaid Diagram 4 — Process of Composition Drift

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initial Feed: f1 and f2"] --> B["Reaction Starts: t = 0"]
    B --> C["Instantaneous Copolymer F1 from Mayo Lewis"]
    C --> D{"Does F1 equal f1?"}
    D -->|"Yes, Azeotropic"| E["Composition Stays Constant Throughout"]
    D -->|"No"| F["Feed Composition Changes as M1 Consumed Faster"]
    F --> G["Drift in f1 over Time"]
    G --> H["Different F1 at Different Conversions"]
    H --> I["Heterogeneous Copolymer Chains"]
    I --> J["Broadened Tg and Property Distribution"]
    E --> K["Homogeneous Copolymer Product"]
    J --> L["Requires Drift Correction or Semi Batch Feeding"]
    K --> M["Industrial Goal: Uniform Product Properties"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above provide a **function-level architecture** of the derivation logic, replacing what would otherwise be complex physical drawings of chain-end additions. Each step in the flow corresponds to a precise algebraic manipulation in the derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the term **reactivity ratio** in copolymerization. What does a value of $r_1 = 0$ and $r_2 = 0$ indicate about the resulting copolymer structure?

**Model Answer:**

The **reactivity ratio** $r_1$ of a monomer $M_1$ is defined as the ratio of the rate constant for the addition of $M_1$ to a chain radical ending in $M_1$ to the rate constant for the addition of $M_2$ to the same chain radical:

$$r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}$$

Similarly, $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$. Reactivity ratios are **dimensionless** parameters that quantify the relative preference of a chain radical for adding its own monomer type versus the other monomer.

When $r_1 = 0$ and $r_2 = 0$, neither radical type has any tendency to add its own monomer. Every addition step must be **cross-propagation**. The resulting copolymer is therefore a **strictly alternating copolymer** with $F_1 = 0.5$ regardless of feed composition.

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Definition of reactivity ratio: **1 Mark**
- Formula for $r_1$ and $r_2$: **1 Mark**
- Inference of alternating behaviour: **1 Mark**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

State the **four propagation reactions** that occur during the free-radical copolymerization of two monomers $M_1$ and $M_2$. Indicate which reactions are homopropagation and which are cross-propagation.

**Model Answer:**

The four propagation reactions in a binary free-radical copolymerization are:

| Reaction | Type | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| $\sim M_1^{\cdot} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} \sim M_1 M_1^{\cdot}$ | Homopropagation of $M_1$ | Self-addition |
| $\sim M_1^{\cdot} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} \sim M_1 M_2^{\cdot}$ | Cross-propagation | Foreign addition |
| $\sim M_2^{\cdot} + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} \sim M_2 M_2^{\cdot}$ | Homopropagation of $M_2$ | Self-addition |
| $\sim M_2^{\cdot} + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} \sim M_2 M_1^{\cdot}$ | Cross-propagation | Foreign addition |

Homopropagation reactions (diagonal: $k_{11}$, $k_{22}$) extend a run of the same monomer, while cross-propagation reactions (off-diagonal: $k_{12}$, $k_{21}$) introduce alternation in the chain.

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Listing four reactions with rate constants: **2 Marks**
- Identifying homo- and cross-propagation correctly: **1 Mark**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. The student must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full. Each Part B question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of all necessary assumptions, **derive the copolymer equation** (Mayo–Lewis equation) for a free-radical copolymerization of two monomers $M_1$ and $M_2$. State the final expression in both the differential form and the mole-fraction form. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the styrene ($M_1$) — acrylonitrile ($M_2$) system, the reactivity ratios are $r_1 = 0.40$ and $r_2 = 0.04$. Calculate the **instantaneous mole fraction of styrene** ($F_1$) in the copolymer when the feed mole fraction $f_1 = 0.30$. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the assumptions (1 Mark):**
- (i) Reactivity of a chain radical depends only on the terminal monomer unit (terminal model).
- (ii) Steady-state approximation: $[M_1^{\cdot}]$ and $[M_2^{\cdot}]$ interconvert at equal rates.
- (iii) Long-chain approximation: propagation dominates over termination.
- (iv) Radical reactivity is independent of chain length.

**Step 2 — Write the rate of monomer consumption (1.5 Marks):**

$$-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} = k_{11}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]$$

$$-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} = k_{22}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_2] + k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2]$$

**Step 3 — Divide and apply steady state (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]}{k_{22}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_2] + k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2]}$$

From the steady state: $\dfrac{[M_1^{\cdot}]}{[M_2^{\cdot}]} = \dfrac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}$

**Step 4 — Substitute and define reactivity ratios (1.5 Marks):**

Define $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$, substitute into the rate ratio, cancel the common factor $k_{21}$, and obtain the **Mayo–Lewis differential equation**:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mole-fraction form (1 Mark):**

With $f_1 = [M_1]/([M_1]+[M_2])$ and $F_1 = d[M_1]/(d[M_1]+d[M_2])$:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.40$, $r_2 = 0.04$, $f_1 = 0.30$, $f_2 = 0.70$.

**Step 1 — Identify the working equation (1 Mark):**

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term (3 Marks):**

$r_1 f_1^2 = 0.40 \times (0.30)^2 = 0.40 \times 0.09 = 0.036$

$f_1 f_2 = 0.30 \times 0.70 = 0.210$

$2 f_1 f_2 = 2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70 = 0.420$

$r_2 f_2^2 = 0.04 \times (0.70)^2 = 0.04 \times 0.49 = 0.0196$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate (2 Marks):**

$$F_1 = \frac{0.036 + 0.210}{0.036 + 0.420 + 0.0196} = \frac{0.246}{0.4756} \approx 0.5172$$

**Step 4 — State the result (1 Mark):**

The instantaneous mole fraction of styrene in the copolymer is $F_1 \approx 0.517$, meaning the copolymer is **slightly richer in styrene** than the feed, even though the feed is poor in styrene. This is consistent with the **low value of $r_1 r_2 = 0.016$**, indicating a strong tendency toward alternation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of the **product $r_1 r_2$** in characterizing copolymer microstructure. Discuss the **ideal**, **alternating**, and **block** copolymer cases with appropriate equations. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For a methyl methacrylate ($M_1$) — vinyl acetate ($M_2$) system with $r_1 = 20$ and $r_2 = 0.015$, determine the **azeotropic feed composition** $f_{1,az}$ at which the feed and copolymer compositions are equal. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Significance of $r_1 r_2$ Product (7 Marks)

The product $r_1 r_2$ is a **single-parameter indicator** of the type of copolymerization behaviour. (1 Mark)

**Case 1 — Ideal (Random) Copolymerization: $r_1 r_2 = 1$ (1.5 Marks):**

Both monomers have equal "self-preference" relative to cross-reactivity. The Mayo–Lewis equation simplifies to:

$$F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1}{r_1 f_1 + f_2}$$

Each monomer has the same probability of being added, regardless of chain-end identity. Example: styrene–isoprene.

**Case 2 — Alternating Copolymerization: $r_1 r_2 \to 0$ (with $r_1, r_2 \ll 1$) (1.5 Marks):**

Both radicals strongly prefer the *opposite* monomer, producing strict alternation. The Mayo–Lewis equation reduces to:

$$F_1 = 0.5$$

regardless of feed composition. Example: styrene–maleic anhydride.

**Case 3 — Block Copolymerization Tendency: $r_1 r_2 \gg 1$ (1.5 Marks):**

Both radicals strongly prefer their *own* monomer, producing long runs. The polymer consists of long stretches of one monomer followed by long stretches of the other (or all of one type). Such systems are precursors to true block copolymers but generally require special controlled-radical techniques.

**Summary Table (1.5 Marks):**

| $r_1 r_2$ Value | Microstructure | Property |
|---|---|---|
| $r_1 r_2 \approx 0$ | Perfectly alternating | Crystalline, high $T_g$ |
| $r_1 r_2 = 1$ | Random (Bernoullian) | Statistical |
| $r_1 r_2 \gg 1$ | Block-like | Heterogeneous, broad transitions |

#### Part (b) — Azeotropic Composition (7 Marks)

**Given:** $r_1 = 20$, $r_2 = 0.015$. Required: azeotropic feed $f_{1,az}$.

**Step 1 — Define the azeotropic condition (1.5 Marks):**

At the azeotrope, the feed and copolymer compositions are equal: $F_1 = f_1$. This requires:

$$\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{r_1[M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply and simplify (2 Marks):**

$$[M_2](r_1[M_1] + [M_2]) = [M_1]([M_1] + r_2[M_2])$$

$$r_1 [M_1][M_2] + [M_2]^2 = [M_1]^2 + r_2 [M_1][M_2]$$

$$(r_1 - r_2)[M_1][M_2] = [M_1]^2 - [M_2]^2 = ([M_1] - [M_2])([M_1] + [M_2])$$

**Step 3 — Apply the shortcut formula (1.5 Marks):**

The azeotropic feed composition is given by:

$$f_{1,az} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate (1 Mark):**

$$f_{1,az} = \frac{1 - 0.015}{2 - 20 - 0.015} = \frac{0.985}{-18.015} \approx -0.0547$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation (1 Mark):**

The computed $f_{1,az}$ is **negative**, which is physically impossible. This means **no real azeotrope exists** for this monomer pair — the two monomers cannot be polymerized to produce a copolymer that matches the feed composition at any positive feed ratio. The copolymer will always drift in composition throughout the reaction. This is a common outcome when $r_1 \gg 1$ and $r_2 \ll 1$ simultaneously, indicating a strong tendency for $M_1$ (MMA) to homopolymerize strongly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $r_1$ and $r_2$ with the rate constants themselves.** The reactivity ratio is the *ratio* of two rate constants. Marks are deducted when students write $r_1 = k_{11}$ instead of $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$.
> 2. **Always state the four assumptions explicitly** before the derivation. Examiners award 1 full mark for assumptions that is often missed.
> 3. **The steady-state step** $k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2] = k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]$ is the heart of the derivation. Skipping or mis-writing this step costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **In numerical problems,** students often forget to compute $f_2 = 1 - f_1$ before plugging into the formula. Always verify that $f_1 + f_2 = 1$.
> 5. **Sign error in azeotrope problems:** A negative $f_{1,az}$ is *physically valid as a result* — it means no real azeotrope exists. Do not panic and recompute; interpret the result.
> 6. **Unit confusion:** Reactivity ratios are dimensionless. No units should appear next to $r_1$ or $r_2$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This for the Night Before ESE):**

- ✅ The **Copolymer Equation** is also called the **Mayo–Lewis Equation** (1944) and the **Terminal Model** of copolymerization.
- ✅ The equation relates **copolymer composition** to **feed composition** via two parameters: **reactivity ratios** $r_1$ and $r_2$.
- ✅ **Differential Form:** $d[M_1]/d[M_2] = (r_1[M_1] + [M_2])/([M_1] + r_2[M_2])$.
- ✅ **Mole-Fraction Form:** $F_1 = (r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2)/(r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2)$.
- ✅ **Reactivity Ratios:** $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$ — both dimensionless.
- ✅ **Four propagation reactions** must be memorized: two homopropagations and two cross-propagations.
- ✅ **Steady-state assumption:** $k_{12}[M_1^{\cdot}][M_2] = k_{21}[M_2^{\cdot}][M_1]$ — the cornerstone of the derivation.
- ✅ **Terminal model assumption:** Reactivity depends only on the *last* monomer unit, not on penultimate or earlier units.
- ✅ **Special Case 1:** $r_1 r_2 = 1 \Rightarrow$ Ideal (random) copolymer.
- ✅ **Special Case 2:** $r_1 = r_2 = 0 \Rightarrow$ Perfectly alternating copolymer ($F_1 = 0.5$).
- ✅ **Special Case 3:** $r_1 r_2 \gg 1 \Rightarrow$ Block-like microstructure.
- ✅ **Azeotropic composition formula:** $f_{1,az} = (1 - r_2)/(2 - r_1 - r_2)$ — used when $F_1 = f_1$.
- ✅ A **negative $f_{1,az}$** mathematically indicates **no real azeotrope** exists for the monomer pair.
- ✅ Industrial relevance: **SBR rubber, EVA, ABS, nitrile rubber** are all designed using this equation.
- ✅ The equation is the **foundation for the Q-e scheme** (Alfrey–Price) used to predict reactivity ratios for monomer pairs that have not been experimentally studied.
- ✅ For KTU ESE 2024: expect a **derivation question (7 marks) + numerical application (7 marks)** combination as the standard 14-mark structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Polymerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bulk Polymerisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bulk polymerisation** (also called **mass polymerisation**) is a homogeneous polymerisation technique in which the reaction proceeds in the liquid phase using only the **monomer itself** as the reaction medium. No added solvent or dispersing medium is used. The initiator (for radical chain polymerisation) or catalyst (for step-growth or coordination polymerisation) is dissolved directly in the pure liquid monomer, and the polymerisation occurs throughout the bulk of the reacting mass.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, bulk polymerisation is classified as a **homogeneous polymerisation method** and is regarded as the **simplest, purest, and most direct** route to a polymer because the polymer product is contaminated only by trace residues of unreacted monomer and initiator fragments — never by solvent impurities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bulk polymerisation belongs to the **chain-growth (addition) polymerisation** family. It is the **closest laboratory-to-production** technique because it mimics the conditions under which a polymer scientist first observes the formation of a polymer from its pure monomer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier:** If a polymerisation recipe contains *only* monomer + initiator/catalyst (and optionally a chain-transfer agent), and no solvent, it is a **bulk polymerisation**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing a **single large glass of sweet lime juice** by mixing sugar directly into the lime juice itself, without adding any extra water.

- The lime juice is the **monomer**.
- The sugar you stir in is the **initiator** (it triggers the chain reaction).
- The glass itself is the **reactor**.
- Because you did not dilute the juice with water, the drink is the **purest possible** — but as the sugar dissolves and the juice thickens (like polymer chains growing and entangling), it becomes extremely difficult to stir, control the temperature, or remove heat from the glass.

This "thickening" mirrors exactly what happens in bulk polymerisation: as polymer chains grow, the reaction medium becomes increasingly viscous (the **Trommsdorff / gel effect**), and the heat released by the exothermic polymerisation can no longer be efficiently dissipated. The "purest product but difficult temperature control" trade-off is the single most important conceptual takeaway.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Used

- **Reaction enthalpy** of typical vinyl polymerisation: $\Delta H_p \approx -84 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (≈ −20 kcal/mol) of C=C bonds converted.
- **Autoacceleration threshold**: typically starts when monomer conversion exceeds **≈ 10–20 %**.
- **Initial monomer concentration** $[M]_0$ for styrene ≈ **8.7 mol/L**; for methyl methacrylate (MMA) ≈ **9.4 mol/L**.
- **Operating temperature range**: typically **60–200 °C** depending on monomer and initiator.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently test whether students can **identify a bulk polymerisation** from a given recipe. Memorise the rule: *No solvent = Bulk; Solvent present = Solution; Water + surfactant = Suspension/Emulsion.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Logical Stages of Bulk Radical Polymerisation

Bulk polymerisation of a vinyl monomer (e.g., styrene, methyl methacrylate) follows the classical **free-radical chain mechanism**. The "why" and "how" of each step are outlined below:

**Stage 1 — Initiation (the spark that starts the chain)**
- *Why:* Pure monomer is kinetically stable; it needs a source of free radicals to break the π-bond of the C=C unit.
- *How:* An initiator **I** (commonly a peroxide such as benzoyl peroxide, BPO, or an azo compound such as AIBN) thermally decomposes homolytically to yield two primary radicals $R^{\bullet}$. Each primary radical adds to a monomer molecule, producing the active chain radical $RM_1^{\bullet}$.

**Stage 2 — Propagation (the chain grows)**
- *Why:* The active chain radical is highly reactive and attacks successive monomer molecules in a head-to-tail fashion.
- *How:* The chain-end radical adds monomer units in microseconds, growing the chain length by one monomer unit per propagation step.

**Stage 3 — Termination (the chain dies)**
- *Why:* Two active radicals cannot co-exist in close proximity — they annihilate each other.
- *How:* Termination occurs by **combination** (coupling of two chain radicals) or **disproportionation** (hydrogen transfer leaving one saturated and one unsaturated chain end).

**Stage 4 — Chain Transfer (the parasitic side reaction)**
- *Why:* An active radical may abstract an atom from a dead polymer, monomer, solvent, or initiator, thereby terminating its growth but starting a new chain.
- *How:* This reduces the average molecular weight and broadens the molecular-weight distribution.

### 2.2 Engineering Reality of Bulk Polymerisation

- **Heat removal** is the single greatest engineering challenge. Vinyl polymerisation releases ≈ **84 kJ/mol**, and at industrial scale (10–100 m³ reactors) this heat, if not extracted, causes a **thermal runaway** (also called "uncontrolled exotherm" in KTU valuations).
- Because of the rising viscosity, **stirring becomes impossible** above ≈ 40–60 % conversion in many systems. Industrial bulk processes therefore often proceed in **two stages**: a low-conversion prepolymerisation in a stirred reactor, followed by a high-conversion finish in a mould, extruder, or static tower.
- The absence of solvent makes **downstream devolatilisation** easier, and the final polymer is **optically clear** (no solvent residues).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Equation** | **Symbols & Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rate of initiator decomposition | $R_d = 2 f k_d [I]$ | $f$ = initiator efficiency (dimensionless, $0 < f \le 1$); $k_d$ in s⁻¹; $[I]$ in mol/L | Factor of 2 because two radicals form per initiator molecule |
| 2 | Rate of initiation | $R_i = R_d = 2 f k_d [I]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | In steady-state, $R_i = R_t$ |
| 3 | Rate of polymerisation (overall) | $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | The **central equation** of free-radical kinetics |
| 4 | Rate of termination | $R_t = k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Termination is **second-order** in radical concentration |
| 5 | Steady-state radical conc. | $[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | mol/L | Derived by setting $R_i = R_t$ |
| 6 | Kinetic chain length | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d [I] k_t}}$ | dimensionless | Average monomer units added per active radical before termination |
| 7 | Number-average degree of polymerisation (combination) | $\overline{X}_n = 2 \nu$ | dimensionless | Combination: two chains merge |
| 8 | Number-average degree of polymerisation (disproportionation) | $\overline{X}_n = \nu$ | dimensionless | Disproportionation: chains end separately |
| 9 | Mayo equation (with chain transfer) | $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n,0} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \dfrac{[I]}{[M]}$ | dimensionless | $C_M, C_S, C_I$ are chain-transfer constants |
| 10 | Autoacceleration onset (Trommsdorff) | $R_p \uparrow$ as $k_t \downarrow$ when viscosity rises | — | $k_t$ falls by 2–3 orders of magnitude |
| 11 | Heat generation rate (engineering) | $Q_{gen} = R_p \cdot (-\Delta H_p) \cdot V$ | Watts (W) | $V$ = reactor volume; must equal $Q_{removed}$ to avoid runaway |
| 12 | Adiabatic temperature rise | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{[M]_0 (-\Delta H_p)}{\rho \, c_p}$ | K | Worst-case scenario if no heat removal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often write $R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$ without substituting the steady-state $[M^{\bullet}]$. Always carry the **square-root form** of $R_p$ — that is the board-exam-ready expression.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bulk polymerisation is the **industrial method of choice** when **optical clarity, electrical purity, or solvent-free processing** is non-negotiable:

- **Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) — "Plexiglas / Perspex"**: cast sheets for aircraft windows, lenses, and aquariums.
- **Polystyrene (PS)**: continuous bulk process in stirred-tower reactors (e.g., the former Dow process).
- **Low-density polyethylene (LDPE)**: high-pressure bulk polymerisation of ethylene in tubular and autoclave reactors (ICI / LyondellBasell process) at 1000–3000 bar and 130–300 °C — the largest-volume bulk polymerisation in the world.
- **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)**: some mass-polymerisation grades for ultra-clear bottles.
- **Polycarbonate and epoxy castings**: bulk step-growth polymerisations for monolithic parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Radical Concentration

We begin by writing the rate at which radicals are **created** (initiation) and **destroyed** (termination), then impose the steady-state assumption.

**Step 1 — Rate of radical generation (initiation)**

The thermal homolysis of an initiator $I$ produces two primary radicals:

$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2 R^{\bullet}$$

The rate at which radicals appear in solution is the rate of initiator decomposition multiplied by the efficiency factor $f$ (because some primary radicals are wasted by the "cage effect"):

$$R_i = \frac{d[R^{\bullet}]}{dt}\bigg|_{creation} = 2 f k_d [I]$$

**Step 2 — Rate of radical destruction (termination)**

Two chain radicals $M_n^{\bullet}$ and $M_m^{\bullet}$ meet and annihilate. Since this bimolecular event requires the square of the radical concentration:

$$R_t = \frac{d[M^{\bullet}]}{dt}\bigg|_{destruction} = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

(The factor 2 in $R_t$ is sometimes omitted in textbooks — KTU convention retains it.)

**Step 3 — Steady-state assumption**

At the pseudo-steady state, the rate of creation equals the rate of destruction:

$$R_i = R_t$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic solution for $[M^{\bullet}]$**

Substituting both expressions:

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

Dividing both sides by $2 k_t$:

$$[M^{\bullet}]^2 = \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}$$

Taking the positive square root (radical concentration is non-negative):

$$[M^{\bullet}]_{ss} = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

This is the **steady-state radical concentration**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Overall Rate of Polymerisation $R_p$

**Step 1 — Definition of $R_p$**

The rate of polymerisation is defined as the rate at which monomer molecules are consumed by chain propagation:

$$R_p = -\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$$

(We use $[M^{\bullet}]$ for the total concentration of all active chain radicals of any length.)

**Step 2 — Substitute the steady-state $[M^{\bullet}]$**

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the canonical form**

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

This equation is the **single most tested result** in KTU polymer kinetics questions.

**Interpretation of dependencies:**
- $R_p \propto [M]^1$ — first order in monomer.
- $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2}$ — **half order in initiator**. Doubling $[I]$ only increases $R_p$ by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
- $R_p$ is suppressed by a **large $k_t$** — anything that lowers $k_t$ (such as high viscosity in bulk polymerisation) actually **increases** $R_p$, which is the molecular origin of the Trommsdorff effect.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Kinetic Chain Length and Degree of Polymerisation

**Step 1 — Definition of the kinetic chain length $\nu$**

The kinetic chain length is the average number of monomer molecules consumed by one active radical from birth to death:

$$\nu = \frac{\text{Rate of propagation}}{\text{Rate of initiation}} = \frac{R_p}{R_i}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute both rates**

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 f k_d [I]} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d [I] k_t}}$$

**Step 4 — Relate $\nu$ to $\overline{X}_n$**

For **termination by combination** (two chain radicals fuse to form one dead polymer chain):

$$\overline{X}_n = 2 \nu$$

For **termination by disproportionation** (each radical becomes its own dead chain):

$$\overline{X}_n = \nu$$

**Step 5 — The Mayo equation (with chain transfer)**

If chain transfer occurs to monomer, solvent, or initiator, the degree of polymerisation decreases:

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_M + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

where $C_M, C_S, C_I$ are the chain-transfer constants to monomer, solvent, and initiator respectively, and $(\overline{X}_n)_0$ is the value with no chain transfer.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Autoacceleration / Trommsdorff Effect

**Step 1 — Physical observation**

As bulk polymerisation proceeds, the reaction medium thickens because long polymer chains entangle. Above a certain conversion (typically 10–20 %), the mobility of the long chain radicals drops dramatically.

**Step 2 — Consequence for $k_t$**

Termination requires the **mutual diffusion** of two long-chain radicals. In a viscous medium, this diffusion becomes the rate-limiting step, so $k_t$ drops by **2–3 orders of magnitude** (e.g., from $10^7$ to $10^5$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹).

**Step 3 — Consequence for $R_p$**

Since $R_p \propto 1/\sqrt{k_t}$, a 100-fold decrease in $k_t$ causes a **10-fold increase** in $R_p$ even though the temperature and initiator concentration have not changed.

**Step 4 — Consequence for $\overline{X}_n$**

Since $\overline{X}_n \propto 1/\sqrt{k_t}$ also, the molecular weight simultaneously **soars**. The polymer produced after the gel effect has very high molecular weight.

**Step 5 — Quantitative effect on $\Delta T_{ad}$**

For a typical MMA bulk polymerisation ($[M]_0 = 9.4$ mol/L, $\rho = 940$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1700$ J/kg·K, $-\Delta H_p = 56$ kJ/mol for MMA):

$$\Delta T_{ad} = \frac{[M]_0 (-\Delta H_p)}{\rho \, c_p} = \frac{9.4 \times 56000}{940 \times 1700} \approx 329 \text{ K}$$

This **329 K adiabatic rise** explains why bulk reactors must be designed with extraordinary cooling capacity — and why the process is sometimes called a "runaway polymerisation" in safety literature.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Bulk Polymerisation Reactor Line-Up

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Monomer Feed Tank"] --> nodeB["Initiator Dosing Unit"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Prepolymeriser Stirred Reactor 60 to 80 C Conversion 10 to 30 percent"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Intermediate Viscosity Break Tank"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Finisher Reactor Tower or Mould Conversion 50 to 90 percent"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Devolatiliser Vacuum Extruder Removes Unreacted Monomer"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Pelletiser or Sheet Casting Line"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Final Polymer Product Optically Clear Solvent Free"]

    nodeC -. Heat removal jacket water .-> nodeC
    nodeE -. Heat removal limited static tower .-> nodeE

    style nodeA fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    style nodeF fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style nodeG fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    style nodeH fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Pure monomer from the storage tank is dosed with a calculated amount of initiator, then enters a small stirred **prepolymeriser** where 10–30 % conversion is reached under tight temperature control. The viscous syrup is then transferred to a **finisher** (a static tower, mould, or extruder) for high conversion. A **devolatiliser** strips residual monomer, and the melt is pelletised or cast into the final shape.

### 4.2 Molecular Mechanism: Free-Radical Bulk Polymerisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeI["I Initiator Molecule"] -- kd homolysis --> nodeR["R radical Primary Radical"]
    nodeR -- kp initiation --> nodeA1["RM1 radical Active Chain End"]
    nodeA1 -- kp propagation --> nodeA2["RM2 radical"]
    nodeA2 -- kp propagation --> nodeA3["RMn radical Long Live Chain"]
    nodeA3 -- kt termination combination --> nodeD1["RMn mR Dead Polymer High MW"]
    nodeA3 -- kt termination disproportionation --> nodeD2["RMn H plus RMn unsaturated Dead Chains"]
    nodeA3 -- ktrM chain transfer to monomer --> nodeD3["Dead Polymer plus M radical New Chain Starts"]

    style nodeI fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000
    style nodeR fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    style nodeA1 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    style nodeA2 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    style nodeA3 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    style nodeD1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style nodeD2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style nodeD3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single initiator molecule gives birth to one primary radical, which initiates a chain. Each propagation step lengthens the chain by one monomer unit. The chain dies either by **combination** (forming one dead chain of summed length), by **disproportionation** (forming two separate dead chains), or by **chain transfer** (forming a dead chain and a new radical that restarts the process).

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Heat and Mass Balance in a Bulk Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Heat Generated Qgen equals Rp times Delta Hp times V"] --> node2{"Heat Removal Capacity Qrem"}
    node2 -- Qgen less than Qrem --> node3["Controlled Isothermal Operation"]
    node2 -- Qgen greater than Qrem --> node4["Temperature Rises Locally"]
    node4 --> node5["Viscosity Falls kp Rises"]
    node5 --> node6["Rp Rises Positive Feedback"]
    node6 --> node7["Trommsdorff Autoacceleration"]
    node7 --> node8["Thermal Runaway Risk"]
    node8 -- Emergency vent or dump --> node9["Safe Shutdown"]
    node8 -- No intervention --> node10["Reactor Vented or Vented Explosion"]

    style node1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    style node2 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    style node3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style node4 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000
    style node5 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000
    style node6 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    style node7 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    style node8 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    style node9 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style node10 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** This block-level functional architecture illustrates why bulk polymerisation is **thermodynamically treacherous**. The positive feedback between temperature rise, falling viscosity (in some regimes), rising $k_p$, and rising $R_p$ is the molecular root cause of the Trommsdorff effect and the engineering reason for the two-stage prepolymeriser–finisher reactor configuration used industrially.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Apr 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define bulk polymerisation. Mention any two industrial polymers produced by this method.
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Bulk (or mass) polymerisation is a polymerisation technique in which the reaction is carried out using the **monomer itself as the only reaction medium**, with the initiator or catalyst dissolved directly in the pure liquid monomer; no solvent or dispersing medium is added.

Two industrially important polymers produced by bulk polymerisation are:

1. **Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA)** — produced by bulk casting for aircraft windows and lenses.
2. **Low-density polyethylene (LDPE)** — produced by high-pressure bulk polymerisation of ethylene in tubular and autoclave reactors.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Polystyrene by continuous bulk process; epoxy castings.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2.** Why is bulk polymerisation called the "purest" polymerisation technique, and what is its principal engineering drawback?
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Bulk polymerisation is called the **purest** technique because the polymer product contains only trace residues of unreacted monomer and initiator fragments — it is **never contaminated by solvent** residues, since no solvent is used at any stage. This is essential for **optically clear** grades (e.g., PMMA) and **electrically pure** grades (e.g., cable-grade LDPE).

Its **principal engineering drawback** is the **difficulty of removing the exothermic heat of polymerisation** (≈ 84 kJ/mol for vinyl monomers). Because the reaction medium becomes highly viscous as polymer forms, stirring and heat transfer both deteriorate, leading to a risk of **thermal runaway** and **autoacceleration** (the Trommsdorff effect).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, End-Semester Estimation – KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a)** Derive the expression for the rate of free-radical bulk polymerisation $R_p$ in terms of monomer concentration $[M]$, initiator concentration $[I]$, and the rate constants $k_d, k_p, k_t$, starting from the elementary steps of initiation, propagation, and termination.
*(7 Marks)*

**A(b)** Explain the **Trommsdorff (gel) effect** observed in bulk polymerisation. Discuss how it affects $R_p$, $\overline{X}_n$, and the temperature profile of the reactor.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — A(a):**

**Step 1 — Elementary steps**

$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2 R^{\bullet} \quad \text{(initiator decomposition, rate } R_d\text{)}$$

$$R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} RM_1^{\bullet} \quad \text{(radical addition to monomer)}$$

$$RM_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} RM_{n+1}^{\bullet} \quad \text{(propagation)}$$

$$RM_n^{\bullet} + RM_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} \text{dead polymer} \quad \text{(termination)}$$

**Step 2 — Rate of initiation**

Accounting for the cage-effect efficiency $f$:

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

**Step 3 — Rate of termination**

$$R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state assumption**

Setting $R_i = R_t$:

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

$$[M^{\bullet}]_{ss} = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

**Step 5 — Rate of polymerisation**

By definition, monomer is consumed by propagation:

$$R_p = -\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$$

Substituting $[M^{\bullet}]_{ss}$:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

**Step 6 — Order analysis**

- First order in monomer: $R_p \propto [M]$.
- Half order in initiator: $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2}$.

**Model Solution — A(b):**

**Definition:** The Trommsdorff effect (also called the **gel effect** or **autoacceleration**) is the sudden, self-accelerating rise in polymerisation rate and molecular weight that occurs in bulk (and concentrated-solution) polymerisations as the reaction medium becomes highly viscous.

**Molecular origin:** Termination requires the **mutual diffusion** of two long-chain radicals through the reaction medium. As polymer concentration rises, chain entanglements and the viscosity of the medium increase sharply. Above a critical conversion (≈ 10–20 % for MMA, ≈ 30 % for styrene), the **translational diffusion of chain radicals becomes the rate-limiting step** of termination, so $k_t$ drops by 2–3 orders of magnitude.

**Effect on $R_p$:** Since $R_p \propto 1/\sqrt{k_t}$, a 100-fold drop in $k_t$ produces a **10-fold rise** in $R_p$ at constant temperature and initiator concentration. Hence $R_p$ accelerates without any external change.

**Effect on $\overline{X}_n$:** Since $\overline{X}_n \propto 1/\sqrt{k_t}$ also, the molecular weight soars simultaneously. This is why bulk-polymerised PMMA and PS have **broader molecular-weight distributions** than solution-polymerised grades.

**Effect on temperature profile:** The accelerating $R_p$ releases heat faster than the cooling jacket can remove it. The local temperature rises, which further increases $k_p$ (Arrhenius). This **positive thermal feedback** can lead to a thermal runaway if not controlled by the two-stage prepolymeriser–finisher reactor configuration or by using a low-activity initiator.

**Engineering mitigation strategies:**
1. Two-stage reactor line-up (prepolymeriser + finisher).
2. Use of low-temperature initiators (e.g., AIBN at 50–70 °C).
3. Continuous removal of polymer from the reaction zone (extruder reactor).
4. Incorporation of a chain-transfer agent (e.g., dodecyl mercaptan in SBR synthesis) to keep $\overline{X}_n$ in check.

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**B(a)** Discuss the **kinetic chain length $\nu$** and the **number-average degree of polymerisation $\overline{X}_n$** in bulk polymerisation. Derive the relationship between $\nu$ and $\overline{X}_n$ for both combination and disproportionation termination.
*(7 Marks)*

**B(b)** Write the **Mayo equation** for chain transfer and explain the effect of chain transfer to monomer, solvent, and initiator on the molecular weight of the polymer formed in a bulk polymerisation.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution — B(a):**

**Definition of kinetic chain length $\nu$:** It is the **average number of monomer molecules** consumed (added to the growing chain) per active radical from the moment of its birth (initiation) to its death (termination or chain transfer).

$$\nu = \frac{\text{Rate of propagation}}{\text{Rate of initiation}} = \frac{R_p}{R_i}$$

**Derivation:**

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

$$\nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d [I] k_t}}$$

Therefore:
- $\nu \propto [M]$ — higher monomer concentration lengthens the chain.
- $\nu \propto [I]^{-1/2}$ — more initiator produces shorter chains (a critical industrial lever for controlling molecular weight).
- $\nu \propto k_t^{+1/2}$ — anything that suppresses termination (high viscosity, Trommsdorff effect) lengthens the chain.

**Relation to $\overline{X}_n$:**

The number-average degree of polymerisation is the **average number of monomer units per dead polymer chain**, which depends on how termination occurs.

**Case 1 — Termination by combination:**
Two chain radicals $RM_n^{\bullet}$ and $RM_m^{\bullet}$ fuse. The resulting dead chain has $n+m$ monomer units. Since two radicals each contributed $\nu$ monomer units on average, the average chain length is $2\nu$:

$$\overline{X}_n = 2 \nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{\sqrt{f k_d [I] k_t}}$$

**Case 2 — Termination by disproportionation:**
Each radical becomes its own dead chain (one saturated, one unsaturated). The average chain length equals the kinetic chain length:

$$\overline{X}_n = \nu = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d [I] k_t}}$$

**Real systems:** Styrene at low conversion terminates predominantly by **combination**; methyl methacrylate terminates predominantly by **disproportionation**.

---

**Model Solution — B(b):**

**The Mayo equation** quantifies the reduction in $\overline{X}_n$ caused by chain transfer to various species present in the reaction mixture:

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_M \frac{[M]}{[M]} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

which simplifies (since the monomer term $[M]/[M] = 1$):

$$\frac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_M + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \frac{[I]}{[M]}$$

where:
- $(\overline{X}_n)_0$ is the degree of polymerisation in the **absence** of any chain transfer.
- $C_M = k_{tr,M} / k_p$ — chain-transfer constant to **monomer**.
- $C_S = k_{tr,S} / k_p$ — chain-transfer constant to **solvent** (or chain-transfer agent, CTA).
- $C_I = k_{tr,I} / k_p$ — chain-transfer constant to **initiator**.

**Effect of each term on molecular weight:**

1. **Chain transfer to monomer** ($C_M$): Always present. Adds a constant to $1/\overline{X}_n$, reducing $\overline{X}_n$ slightly. Monomers with allylic C–H bonds (e.g., propylene) have large $C_M$ and give low-molecular-weight polymer, which is one reason isotactic polypropylene cannot be made by free-radical bulk polymerisation.

2. **Chain transfer to solvent** ($C_S [S]/[M]$): In bulk polymerisation there is **no solvent**, so this term is zero by definition. This is precisely why bulk polymerisation gives the **highest molecular weight** of any homogeneous technique — the absence of solvent eliminates one of the major chain-breaking pathways.

3. **Chain transfer to initiator** ($C_I [I]/[M]$): Particularly important for peroxide initiators (e.g., BPO) which have relatively high $C_I$. Hydrogen-abstraction initiators shorten the chains; azo initiators (e.g., AIBN) have negligible $C_I$ and are preferred when high molecular weight is required.

**Industrial implication:** To deliberately reduce molecular weight in bulk polymerisation (e.g., to improve melt-processability), a small amount of a chain-transfer agent (CTA) such as **dodecyl mercaptan (TDM)** is added. The Mayo equation then includes the term $C_{CTA} [CTA]/[M]$, and $\overline{X}_n$ can be tuned linearly with CTA loading.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Bulk Polymerisation Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the steady-state assumption** — examiners award zero credit for an $R_p$ expression without the step where $R_i = R_t$ is invoked. Always show this step explicitly.
> 2. **Missing the factor $f$ (initiator efficiency)** — half the time students write $R_i = 2 k_d [I]$ instead of $2 f k_d [I]$. This costs 1 mark in 7-mark derivations.
> 3. **Confusing $R_d$ with $R_i$** — $R_d$ is the rate of *decomposition*; $R_i$ is the rate of *initiation* (formation of chain radicals). They are equal only if $f = 1$.
> 4. **Forgetting the Trommsdorff effect** in any question about bulk polymerisation — examiners expect at least one paragraph on it for any 7-mark or 14-mark question on this topic.
> 5. **Mixing up $\overline{X}_n$ for combination vs. disproportionation** — combination gives $2\nu$, disproportionation gives $\nu$. Always state which mode dominates for the monomer in question.
> 6. **In Mayo-equation questions, forgetting the $[S]/[M]$ ratio** — for a bulk polymerisation $[S] = 0$, so the solvent term vanishes. State this explicitly to score full marks.
> 7. **Ignoring units** — $R_p$ is in **mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹**, $[M^{\bullet}]$ in **mol L⁻¹**, $k_p$ in **L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹**. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme increasingly deduct marks for unit inconsistency.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Bulk polymerisation** = monomer + initiator only; **no solvent, no dispersing medium**.
- ✅ It is the **purest** method; gives the **highest optical clarity** and **highest molecular weight** (because chain transfer to solvent is absent).
- ✅ It is governed by **free-radical kinetics**: initiation → propagation → termination.
- ✅ **Canonical rate equation:** $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ — first order in $[M]$, half order in $[I]$.
- ✅ **Kinetic chain length:** $\nu = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d [I] k_t}}$.
- ✅ **Number-average degree of polymerisation:** $\overline{X}_n = 2\nu$ for combination, $\overline{X}_n = \nu$ for disproportionation.
- ✅ **Mayo equation:** $\dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{(\overline{X}_n)_0} + C_M + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I \dfrac{[I}{[M]}$ — and in bulk $[S] = 0$.
- ✅ **Trommsdorff (gel) effect:** high viscosity → $k_t$ drops by 2–3 orders → $R_p$ and $\overline{X}_n$ both rise sharply → **thermal runaway risk**.
- ✅ **Principal drawback:** poor **heat removal** due to high viscosity; **adiabatic temperature rise** for MMA ≈ 329 K.
- ✅ **Industrial examples:** PMMA (cast sheets), PS (continuous bulk), LDPE (high-pressure bulk, 1000–3000 bar), epoxy castings, bulk PVC.
- ✅ **Engineering solution:** **two-stage reactor** — prepolymeriser (low conversion, stirred, cooled) + finisher (high conversion, static, mould or tower).
- ✅ **Autoacceleration threshold:** typically **10–20 %** conversion for MMA, **≈ 30 %** for styrene.
- ✅ **Heat of polymerisation** of vinyl monomers ≈ **84 kJ/mol** of C=C bonds — this is the root cause of the heat-removal problem.
- ✅ **Key industrial lever for $\overline{X}_n$:** initiator concentration $[I]$ — doubling $[I]$ reduces $\overline{X}_n$ by $\sqrt{2}$.
- ✅ **Chain-transfer agents (CTAs)** like dodecyl mercaptan are added when lower $\overline{X}_n$ is desired.
- ✅ **Kinetic orders to memorise for board exams:** $R_p \propto [M]^1 [I]^{1/2}$; $\overline{X}_n \propto [M]^1 [I]^{-1/2}$; $[M^{\bullet}] \propto [I]^{1/2}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "BULK":**
> **B**est purity • **U**ncontrolled heat removal is the issue • **L**acks solvent → highest $\overline{X}_n$ • **K**inetic orders: $R_p$ is half-order in initiator.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
