<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solution Polymerisation** is a homogeneous bulk-type polymerisation technique in which the **monomer**, the **initiator**, and the resulting **polymer** are all dissolved in an inert, non-reactive organic **solvent** (the reaction medium). The polymerisation proceeds as a single, continuous liquid phase, and the solvent functions simultaneously as a **heat-transfer diluent** and as a **chain-transfer agent** that moderates molecular weight.

Mathematically, the technique is defined by the condition that the polymer formed remains in solution throughout the reaction, i.e., the solvent is chosen such that its **Hildebrand solubility parameter (δ)** lies within approximately ±1.0 (cal/cm³)^½ of the polymer's solubility parameter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Cool Banquet Hall"**
> Imagine a hot, crowded kitchen where a chef is deep-frying 1000 samosas at once (this is bulk polymerisation — heat builds up, oil smokes, things burn). Now move that same frying operation into a **large, air-conditioned banquet hall** filled with cool, circulating air (the solvent). The hall **absorbs the excess heat**, the chef can work safely, the temperature stays uniform, and the samosas (polymer chains) drift around in the cool air without clumping together. The hall itself is not food, but its *quiet presence* (chain transfer to solvent) prevents any single samosa from becoming oversized. **Solution polymerisation is that banquet hall.**

## 1.3 Why a Solvent is Used (Heat & Viscosity)

| Driving Problem in Bulk Polymerisation | How Solvent Solves It |
|---|---|
| Autoacceleration (Trommsdorff/Norrish effect) | Dilution reduces radical concentration |
| Hot-spots causing runaway exotherm | Solvent acts as a **thermal ballast** |
| High viscosity (poor mixing) | Viscosity drop ⇒ better heat & mass transfer |
| Difficulty controlling MW | Chain transfer to solvent tunes $\bar{M}_n$ |

## 1.4 Key Boundary Conditions & Physical Constants

- Operating temperature range: typically **40 °C to 150 °C** (free-radical) or **−100 °C to +50 °C** (ionic).
- Monomer concentration in feed: usually **20 % to 50 % w/v** (balance is solvent).
- Solvent boiling point must exceed reaction temperature by at least **10–20 °C** to permit reflux heat removal.
- Standard Hildebrand solubility parameters for common solvents:
  - **Toluene:** $\delta = 18.2 \, (\text{MPa})^{1/2}$
  - **Ethyl acetate:** $\delta = 18.6 \, (\text{MPa})^{1/2}$
  - **Water:** $\delta = 47.8 \, (\text{MPa})^{1/2}$ (only for water-soluble monomers)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

Solution polymerisation follows the classical **free-radical chain-growth kinetics** modified by a continuous chain-transfer interaction with the solvent (S). The complete elementary scheme is:

1. **Initiation (homolytic decomposition of initiator I)**
   - $I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^{\bullet}$
   - $R^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} RM_1^{\bullet}$

2. **Propagation (chain growth by successive monomer additions)**
   - $RM_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} RM_{n+1}^{\bullet}$

3. **Chain transfer to solvent (the defining feature of solution polymerisation)**
   - $RM_n^{\bullet} + S \xrightarrow{k_{tr,S}} P_n + S^{\bullet}$
   - $S^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_i} SM_1^{\bullet}$ (re-initiation)

4. **Chain transfer to monomer**
   - $RM_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_{tr,M}} P_n + M^{\bullet}$

5. **Termination (combination or disproportionation)**
   - Combination: $RM_n^{\bullet} + RM_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} P_{n+m}$
   - Disproportionation: $RM_n^{\bullet} + RM_m^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_{td}} P_n + P_m$

## 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step

- **Why is solvent inert but still affects kinetics?** The solvent's C–H or X–H bond is weak enough to donate a hydrogen atom to an active chain radical, *terminating* that chain and *starting* a new one (a solvent radical that re-initiates). This is the molecular origin of the **chain transfer constant** $C_S$.
- **Why is temperature control superior?** The solvent's high heat capacity ($C_p$ of toluene ≈ **156 J/mol·K**, water ≈ **75.3 J/mol·K**) and large latent heat of vaporisation (when refluxed) provide a *thermal buffer* that prevents runaway exotherms characteristic of bulk polymerisation.
- **How is molecular weight controlled?** Two knobs: (i) $[M]/[S]$ ratio — more solvent ⇒ more chain transfer ⇒ lower $\bar{M}_n$; (ii) initiator concentration $[I]^{1/2}$ — more initiator ⇒ more chains ⇒ lower $\bar{M}_n$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *core* numerical toolkit for solution polymerisation. Memorise every row; it is the minimum for scoring 14-mark derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rate of initiation | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | $f$ = initiator efficiency (0.3–0.8); $k_d$ in s⁻¹; $[I]$ in mol/L |
| 2 | Rate of propagation | $R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$ | $k_p$ in L/mol·s; $[M^{\bullet}]$ steady-state radical conc. |
| 3 | Steady-state radical conc. | $[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | $k_t = k_{tc} + k_{td}$ |
| 4 | Rate of polymerisation | $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$ | Final working equation |
| 5 | Kinetic chain length | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}}$ | Average monomer units per active chain |
| 6 | Number-average DP (no transfer) | $\bar{X}_n = 2 \nu$ | Combination: factor 2; Disproportionation: factor 1 |
| 7 | Chain transfer constant to solvent | $C_S = \dfrac{k_{tr,S}}{k_p}$ | Dimensionless; toluene ≈ $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 8 | Chain transfer constant to monomer | $C_M = \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$ | Typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| 9 | Mayo equation (generalised) | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_S \dfrac{[S]}{[M]} + C_M$ | $\bar{X}_{n,0}$ = DP with zero transfer |
| 10 | Molecular weight | $\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0$ | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit (g/mol) |
| 11 | Solvent heat balance | $Q_{rem} = \dot{m}_{S} \, C_{p,S} \, \Delta T$ | $Q_{rem}$ in W; mass flow $\dot{m}_S$ in kg/s |
| 12 | Trommsdorff critical condition | $k_p \sqrt{[M^{\bullet}]} > k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$ | Defines gel-effect onset |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial resins & lacquers:** Solution polymerisation of acrylates, methacrylates, and vinyl acetate in ethyl acetate or toluene directly yields *lacquer-grade* polymer solutions ready for spraying — no re-dissolution step needed.
- **Sclairtech™ process (NOVA Chemicals):** Linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE) is produced by solution polymerisation in cyclohexane at ~300 °C and high pressure, giving superior $\bar{M}_n$ / MWD control.
- **Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC):** Modern E-PVC grades are partly made by solution polymerisation in THF/MEK, giving a fine powder after solvent stripping.
- **Pharmaceutical & biomedical polymers:** Solution polymerisation in supercritical CO₂ (scCO₂) — an "advanced solution process" — eliminates toxic solvent residues.
- **Adhesives & pressure-sensitive coatings:** Solution polymerisation of 2-ethylhexyl acrylate in heptane provides narrow-MWD polymers for hot-melt adhesives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Radical Concentration in Solution Polymerisation

We start from the elementary reactions and apply the **steady-state approximation (SSA)** to the radical concentration, valid when the rate of radical formation equals the rate of radical consumption.

**Step 1.** Write the rate of radical formation (initiation):

$$R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

**Step 2.** Write the rate of radical disappearance (termination by combination + disproportionation):

$$R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

(Factor 2 arises because two radicals are consumed per termination event; KTU convention.)

**Step 3.** Apply the steady-state approximation, $R_i = R_t$:

$$2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the radical concentration:

$$[M^{\bullet}]^2 = \frac{2 f k_d [I]}{2 k_t} = \frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

> *Note:* Some textbooks write the numerator as $2 f k_d [I]$ and the denominator as $2 k_t$, which algebraically cancel to the same result. The compact form $[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{2 f k_d [I] \,/\, k_t}$ appears when the factor-2 convention is retained on $R_i$ but not on $R_t$ — both forms are accepted in KTU scripts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rate of Polymerisation $R_p$ in Solution

**Step 1.** The propagation rate is:

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the SSA radical concentration from Section 3.1:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

**Step 3.** Square both sides to obtain the canonical KTU relation:

$$R_p^{\,2} = \frac{f k_d \, k_p^{\,2}}{k_t} [I] [M]^2$$

**Step 4.** Take the square root and write the final working equation:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2}$ and $R_p \propto [M]^1$ — the half-order dependence on initiator is the classical fingerprint of free-radical polymerisation and is *frequently asked* in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mayo Equation (Effect of Chain Transfer to Solvent on $\bar{X}_n$)

This is the most important derivation in solution polymerisation. We derive the relationship between degree of polymerisation and the solvent/monomer ratio.

**Step 1.** Define the *number-average* degree of polymerisation:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{\text{Rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{Rate of chain formation}} = \frac{R_p}{R_i + R_{tr,S} + R_{tr,M}}$$

**Step 2.** The rate of chain formation by chain transfer to solvent is:

$$R_{tr,S} = k_{tr,S} [M^{\bullet}] [S]$$

**Step 3.** The rate of chain formation by chain transfer to monomer is:

$$R_{tr,M} = k_{tr,M} [M^{\bullet}] [M]$$

**Step 4.** The rate of chain formation by termination is $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ (using SSA, $R_i = R_t$). Substitute all three:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}]}{2 f k_d [I] + k_{tr,S} [M^{\bullet}] [S] + k_{tr,M} [M^{\bullet}] [M]}$$

**Step 5.** Divide numerator and denominator by $k_p [M^{\bullet}]$:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{[M]}{\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_p [M^{\bullet}]} + \dfrac{k_{tr,S}}{k_p} [S] + \dfrac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p} [M]}$$

**Step 6.** Recognise that $\dfrac{2 f k_d [I]}{k_p [M^{\bullet}]} = \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}}$ (the DP that would be obtained in the absence of any chain transfer). Define the *chain transfer constants* $C_S = k_{tr,S}/k_p$ and $C_M = k_{tr,M}/k_p$:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{[M]}{\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_S [S] + C_M [M]}$$

**Step 7.** Invert and rearrange into the famous **Mayo equation**:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_M$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mayo equation is *the* defining kinetic result of solution polymerisation. It is the single most-tested equation in KTU Polymer Engineering board exams for Module 4.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Each Operational Variable Affects Key Outputs

| Variable increased | $R_p$ | $\bar{X}_n$ | $\bar{M}_n$ | PDI |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $[I]$ (initiator conc.) | $\uparrow$ (half-order) | $\downarrow$ sharply | $\downarrow$ | broadens |
| $[M]$ (monomer conc.) | $\uparrow$ (first-order) | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | narrows |
| $[S]$ (solvent conc.) | slight $\uparrow$ (dilution effect) | $\downarrow$ (more $C_S$ transfer) | $\downarrow$ | may broaden |
| Temperature $T$ | $\uparrow$ (Arrhenius) | $\downarrow$ ($C_S$ rises) | $\downarrow$ | widens |
| Solvent $C_S$ (e.g., CCl₄ vs toluene) | minor | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | broadens |
| Stirring rate (Reynolds No.) | no kinetic effect | no effect | no effect | narrows (better mixing) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style, 7-mark standard)

> **Problem.** Methyl methacrylate (MMA) is polymerised in ethyl acetate at 60 °C. The following data are given:
> - $[M]_0 = 2.0$ mol/L, $[S] = 6.0$ mol/L, $[I] = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L (AIBN, $f = 0.6$)
> - $k_d = 3.2 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹, $k_p = 1.5 \times 10^{3}$ L/mol·s, $k_t = 4.5 \times 10^{7}$ L/mol·s
> - $C_S = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ for ethyl acetate at 60 °C
> - $C_M \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ (negligible, but included)
> - $M_0$ (MMA) = 100 g/mol
>
> **Find (a)** the steady-state radical concentration, **(b)** the rate of polymerisation, and **(c)** the number-average molecular weight.

**Solution.**

**(a) Steady-state radical concentration:**

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.6 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4.5 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{1.92 \times 10^{-9}}{4.5 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{4.267 \times 10^{-17}}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = 6.53 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{mol/L}$$

**(b) Rate of polymerisation:**

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = 1.5 \times 10^{3} \times 2.0 \times 6.53 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$R_p = 1.96 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{mol/(L·s)}$$

**(c) Number-average degree of polymerisation using the Mayo equation:**

First, $\bar{X}_{n,0}$ from the no-transfer case:

$$\bar{X}_{n,0} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{3} \times 2.0}{2 \sqrt{0.6 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.5 \times 10^{7} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

Denominator: $2 \sqrt{8.64 \times 10^{-2}} = 2 \times 0.2940 = 0.5880$

$$\bar{X}_{n,0} = \frac{3000}{0.5880} = 5102$$

Now the Mayo equation:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1}{5102} + (2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times \frac{6.0}{2.0} + 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = 1.96 \times 10^{-4} + 7.50 \times 10^{-5} + 1.00 \times 10^{-5} = 2.81 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\bar{X}_n = 3559$$

Finally, the number-average molecular weight:

$$\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0 = 3559 \times 100 = 3.559 \times 10^{5} \; \text{g/mol}$$

> **Result:** $\bar{M}_n \approx 3.56 \times 10^{5}$ g/mol — a typical value for solution-polymerised PMMA at moderate conversion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solution Polymerisation Reactor — Block Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional architecture* of an industrial solution-polymerisation train. It identifies each unit, the streams that connect them, and the process control loops.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED["FEED SECTION"]
        monTANK["MMA MONOMER TANK"]
        solTANK["SOLVENT TANK"]
        iniTANK["INITIATOR SLURRY TANK"]
    end

    subgraph RXN["REACTION TRAIN"]
        jackR["JACKETED CSTR REACTOR 60 C"]
        condR["TOTAL CONDENSER REFLUX"]
        coolR["PRECOOLER HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["PROCESS CONTROL LOOPS"]
        tctrl["TEMP CONTROLLER PID"]
        pctrl["PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE"]
        lvlctrl["LEVEL TRANSMITTER LT"]
    end

    subgraph DOWN["DOWNSTREAM RECOVERY"]
        devap["SOLVENT DEVOLATILISER"]
        condCOL["CONDENSER COLUMN"]
        recTANK["RECOVERED SOLVENT TANK"]
        extPOL["POLYMER EXTRUSION PELLETISER"]
    end

    monTANK -->|M METERED| jackR
    solTANK -->|S METERED| jackR
    iniTANK -->|I SLURRY| jackR

    jackR -->|VAPOUR REFLUX| condR
    condR -->|LIQUID RETURN| jackR
    jackR -->|POLYMER SOLUTION| coolR

    jackR -.->|T SENSOR FEEDBACK| tctrl
    tctrl -.->|STEAM OR CW VALVE| jackR
    jackR -.->|PRESSURE TRANSMITTER| pctrl
    jackR -.->|LEVEL SIGNAL| lvlctrl

    coolR -->|WARM SOLUTION| devap
    devap -->|VAPOUR S RECOVERY| condCOL
    condCOL -->|LIQUID S| recTANK
    recTANK -->|RECYCLE S| solTANK
    devap -->|MOLTEN POLYMER| extPOL
    extPOL -->|PELLET PRODUCT| prodSTK["PRODUCT SILO"]
```

## 4.2 Kinetic Flow of Solution Polymerisation — Elemental Step Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["INITIATOR I 2 f k d"] -->|kd| rad1["2 PRIMARY RADICALS R"]
    rad1 -->|ki| rad2["MONOMER RADICAL M1"]
    rad2 -->|kp chain growth| rad3["OLIGOMERIC RADICAL Mn"]
    rad3 -->|kp propagation| rad4["POLYMER RADICAL Mn PLUS 1"]
    rad4 -->|kp continued| grow["GROWING CHAIN LARGE N"]

    grow -->|ktr S chain transfer to solvent| term1["DEAD POLYMER Pn"]
    term1 -->|PLUS SOLVENT RADICAL S| reinit["REINITIATION WITH MONOMER"]

    grow -->|ktr M chain transfer to monomer| term2["DEAD POLYMER Pn PLUS M RADICAL"]
    term2 -->|REINITIATION| reinit

    grow -->|kt combination| term3["DEAD POLYMER Pn PLUS m LARGER"]
    grow -->|kt disproportionation| term4["TWO DEAD POLYMERS Pn AND Pm"]
```

## 4.3 Mayo Equation — Graphical Determination of $C_S$ (Plot Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis1["X AXIS RATIO S TO M"] --> plot1["1 OVER Xn  OBSERVED"]
    axis2["Y AXIS 1 OVER Xn"] --> plot1
    plot1 -->|"LINEAR FIT"| fit1["STRAIGHT LINE"]
    fit1 -->|"INTERCEPT AT 0"| inter["1 OVER Xn 0"]
    fit1 -->|"SLOPE OF LINE"| slope["C S CHAIN TRANSFER CONSTANT"]
    slope -->|"NUMERICAL VALUE"| result["C S OBTAINED"]
```

> **Reading the graph:** A plot of $1/\bar{X}_n$ against $[S]/[M]$ yields a straight line whose **slope is $C_S$** and whose **intercept** is $1/\bar{X}_{n,0} + C_M$. This is the standard KTU-expected experimental route to determining $C_S$ in a 7-mark problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define solution polymerisation. List any four advantages it offers over bulk polymerisation.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution polymerisation** is a homogeneous polymerisation technique in which the monomer, initiator, and the resulting polymer are all dissolved in an inert solvent that serves as the reaction medium.
>
> **Advantages over bulk polymerisation:**
> 1. Excellent **temperature control** because the solvent acts as a thermal ballast.
> 2. **Low viscosity** of the reaction mass, ensuring good mixing and heat transfer.
> 3. **Easy molecular-weight control** by varying the solvent-to-monomer ratio.
> 4. **Direct use** of the polymer solution (e.g., in lacquers and coatings) without re-dissolution.
> 5. Reduced risk of **runaway exotherm** (autoacceleration suppressed).
> 6. Convenient for **kinetic studies** because all components are in one phase.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the chain transfer constant $C_S$? State the Mayo equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

> The **chain transfer constant to solvent**, $C_S = k_{tr,S}/k_p$, is the dimensionless ratio of the rate constant for chain transfer from an active polymer radical to the solvent, to the rate constant for propagation. It quantifies the *efficiency* of a solvent in terminating a growing chain.
>
> **Mayo equation:**
>
> $$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_M$$
>
> where $\bar{X}_{n,0}$ is the DP in the absence of any transfer, $[S]/[M]$ is the solvent-to-monomer concentration ratio, and $C_M$ is the chain transfer constant to monomer. The equation is significant because it allows experimental determination of $C_S$ and predicts $\bar{X}_n$ as a function of solvent concentration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question must offer an **internal choice**. Question A and Question B are *completely independent* alternatives — you may answer *either*.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Mayo equation** for degree of polymerisation in solution polymerisation, clearly stating the steady-state approximation and the definition of the chain transfer constant.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the free-radical solution polymerisation of styrene in toluene at 60 °C, the following data are obtained:

| $[M]$ (mol/L) | $[S]$ (mol/L) | $\bar{X}_n$ (observed) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2.00 | 2.00 | 4500 |
| 2.00 | 6.00 | 2200 |
| 2.00 | 10.00 | 1500 |

Determine (i) the chain transfer constant $C_S$ of toluene, and (ii) $\bar{X}_{n,0}$ (the DP that would be obtained with no chain transfer).

**Model Solution.**

**(a)** *See Section 3.3 of these notes for the full derivation.* The key steps are:

1. Write $\bar{X}_n = R_p / (R_i + R_{tr,S} + R_{tr,M})$.
2. Apply steady-state: $R_i = 2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$.
3. Substitute $R_{tr,S} = k_{tr,S}[M^{\bullet}][S]$ and $R_{tr,M} = k_{tr,M}[M^{\bullet}][M]$.
4. Divide numerator and denominator by $k_p [M^{\bullet}]$.
5. Identify $C_S = k_{tr,S}/k_p$ and $C_M = k_{tr,M}/k_p$ and obtain the Mayo equation:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1}{\bar{X}_{n,0}} + C_S \frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_M$$

**(b)** Compute $1/\bar{X}_n$ and $[S]/[M]$ for each row:

| $[S]/[M]$ | $1/\bar{X}_n$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1.00 | $2.222 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 3.00 | $4.545 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 5.00 | $6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ |

(i) **Slope** by linear regression (or two-point) = $\dfrac{6.667 - 2.222}{5.00 - 1.00} \times 10^{-4} = \dfrac{4.445 \times 10^{-4}}{4.00}$

$$\boxed{C_S = 1.111 \times 10^{-4}}$$

(ii) **Intercept** = $1/\bar{X}_n$ at $[S]/[M] = 0$:

$$1/\bar{X}_{n,0} + C_M = 2.222 \times 10^{-4} - 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.00 = 1.111 \times 10^{-4}$$

If $C_M \ll 1/\bar{X}_{n,0}$ (typical for styrene, $C_M \approx 6 \times 10^{-6}$):

$$\bar{X}_{n,0} \approx \frac{1}{1.11 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 9000$$

$$\boxed{\bar{X}_{n,0} \approx 9000 \quad (\bar{M}_{n,0} \approx 9.36 \times 10^{5} \text{ g/mol})}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labelled schematic (or block diagram), describe the **process flow** of an industrial solution polymerisation plant for the production of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). Highlight the role of the solvent, the reflux condenser, and the devolatiliser.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solution polymerisation of vinyl acetate is carried out at 60 °C with the following kinetic parameters: $k_p = 2.3 \times 10^{3}$ L/mol·s, $k_t = 6.0 \times 10^{7}$ L/mol·s, $k_d = 4.0 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹, $f = 0.5$, $[I] = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L, $[M] = 1.5$ mol/L. Calculate (i) the steady-state radical concentration, (ii) the rate of polymerisation, and (iii) the kinetic chain length $\nu$.

**Model Solution.**

**(a)** *Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.* The expected descriptive points (≥ 5 are needed for full marks):

1. **Monomer + solvent + initiator** are metered from their respective feed tanks into a **jacketed CSTR** (or stirred batch reactor).
2. The **solvent** (e.g., ethyl acetate) dissolves monomer, initiator, and the polymer product, keeping viscosity low and providing a heat sink.
3. The **reflux condenser** at the top of the reactor returns evaporated solvent, removes reaction heat through latent heat of vaporisation, and maintains a constant reaction temperature.
4. **Temperature, pressure, and level controllers** monitor the reactor; cooling water/steam in the jacket provides fine temperature trimming.
5. The **polymer solution** is cooled and sent to a **devolatiliser**, where solvent is stripped under vacuum and recovered in a condenser column.
6. The **molten polymer** is fed to an **extruder / pelletiser** to give the final PMMA pellets; recovered solvent is recycled.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

(i) **Steady-state radical concentration:**

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{6.0 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{6.0 \times 10^{7}}} = \sqrt{1.667 \times 10^{-17}}$$

$$\boxed{[M^{\bullet}] = 4.08 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{mol/L}}$$

(ii) **Rate of polymerisation:**

$$R_p = k_p [M] [M^{\bullet}] = 2.3 \times 10^{3} \times 1.5 \times 4.08 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\boxed{R_p = 1.41 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{mol/(L·s)}}$$

(iii) **Kinetic chain length:**

$$\nu = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}}$$

Compute denominator:

$$2 \sqrt{0.5 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.0 \times 10^{7} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 2 \sqrt{6.0 \times 10^{-2}} = 2 \times 0.2449 = 0.4899$$

$$\nu = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{3} \times 1.5}{0.4899} = \frac{3450}{0.4899} \approx 7043$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 7043 \text{ monomer units per active chain}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Factor of 2 in the radical concentration equation:** Many students write $[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{2 f k_d [I] / k_t}$ *and* $R_t = 2 k_t [M^{\bullet}]^2$ *simultaneously* — that double-counts the factor of 2 and produces a $[M^{\bullet}]$ value **higher by $\sqrt{2}$** (~41 % error). Pick **one** convention and stick to it.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_M$ in the Mayo equation:** The full Mayo equation includes $C_M$, not just $C_S$. Examiners *specifically test* whether you include both terms.
> 3. **Mixing termination modes:** In the relation $\bar{X}_n = 2\nu$ you must use factor 2 only for *combination*; for *disproportionation* the factor is 1. KTU scripts often deduct 1 mark for this slip.
> 4. **Units of $R_p$:** Always quote $R_p$ in **mol/(L·s)**. Writing just "mol/s" without the per-litre volume basis loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Plot of Mayo equation:** The slope of $1/\bar{X}_n$ vs. $[S]/[M]$ is $C_S$ — *not* the intercept. The intercept is $1/\bar{X}_{n,0} + C_M$. Examiners mark this distinction carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Solution polymerisation** is a *homogeneous* technique: monomer, initiator, **and polymer** are all soluble in the chosen solvent.
- [x] The **solvent is the thermal ballast**; it is *inert* to monomer but *active* in chain transfer.
- [x] Three elementary steps in addition to bulk kinetics: **chain transfer to solvent ($k_{tr,S}$), chain transfer to monomer ($k_{tr,M}$), and re-initiation** by the solvent radical.
- [x] The **rate equation** is $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{f k_d [I]/k_t}$ with $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2}$ and $R_p \propto [M]^1$.
- [x] The **chain transfer constant** $C_S = k_{tr,S}/k_p$ is *the* material property of a solvent in this context.
- [x] The **Mayo equation** is the central 14-mark result: $1/\bar{X}_n = 1/\bar{X}_{n,0} + C_S [S]/[M] + C_M$.
- [x] A plot of $1/\bar{X}_n$ vs. $[S]/[M]$ is linear with **slope $C_S$** and **intercept $1/\bar{X}_{n,0} + C_M$**.
- [x] **Kinetic chain length** $\nu = R_p/R_i = k_p[M]/(2\sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]})$.
- [x] For *combination* termination, $\bar{X}_n = 2\nu$; for *disproportionation*, $\bar{X}_n = \nu$.
- [x] **Molecular weight control knobs:** (1) $[S]/[M]$ ratio, (2) $[I]$ concentration, (3) temperature, (4) choice of solvent (low-$C_S$ solvents give high MW).
- [x] **Industrial examples:** PMMA lacquers, Sclairtech LLDPE, E-PVC, pressure-sensitive adhesives, biomedical polymers in scCO₂.
- [x] **Advantages:** temperature control, low viscosity, direct-use solutions, MW tunability.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** chain transfer reduces MW, solvent recovery cost, flammability/toxicity hazards, lower reactor productivity per volume.
- [x] **Trommsdorff (gel) effect** is *suppressed* in solution polymerisation because solvent dilution keeps the viscosity low.
- [x] The reaction is **endothermic-controlled**; temperature uniformity is the single biggest engineering benefit.
- [x] **Solvent selection criteria:** (i) solubility of monomer and polymer, (ii) low $C_S$ for high-MW products, (iii) boiling point ≥ reaction temperature, (iv) chemical inertness toward initiator, (v) low toxicity and easy recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mark Strategy for KTU Module 4:** Always start a 14-mark answer with a one-line *definition*, follow with a clearly-numbered *derivation*, and end with a *boxed final answer* carrying the correct **units**. Examiners reward presentation as much as content.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suspension Polymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suspension Polymerization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Suspension Polymerization** (also called **Pearl Polymerization** or **Bead Polymerization**) is a heterogeneous radical chain-growth polymerization technique in which one or more water-immiscible (hydrophobic) liquid monomers are mechanically dispersed as discrete droplets (typically **50 µm – 2 mm** in diameter) within a continuous aqueous phase. Each monomer droplet is stabilized against coalescence by the addition of small quantities of a **protective colloid** (water-soluble polymer) or a **fine inorganic dispersant**, and polymerization is initiated within the droplets using an **oil-soluble (monomer-soluble) free-radical initiator**. Since each droplet functions as an independent miniature bulk-polymerization microreactor, the resulting polymer is obtained in the form of discrete spherical beads that can be filtered, washed, and dried directly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Suspension polymerization is one of the **three principal industrial heterogeneous polymerization techniques**, the others being **emulsion** and **precipitation** polymerization. It is the principal route for commercial **PVC, expanded polystyrene (EPS), ion-exchange resins, and acrylic beads**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making **laddus (Indian sweet balls)** at home:

- The **oil/ghee** in the pan is your **monomer** (e.g., styrene).
- The **water** in the surrounding vessel is your **continuous aqueous phase**.
- As you stir vigorously with a ladle (**mechanical agitation**), the oil breaks into tiny spherical droplets.
- To prevent the droplets from merging back into one big pool, you sprinkle a little **flour/maida** (this is your **stabilizer/dispersant**).
- Now, you drop a pinch of **baking soda** (the **oil-soluble initiator**) — but ONLY into each droplet, not into the water.
- The baking soda triggers a reaction **inside** each tiny oil droplet, and they all individually turn into solid little **pearls (beads)**.
- Finally, you **strain the pearls out of the water**, rinse them, and dry them.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three Coexisting Phases" in Suspension Polymerization:**
> 1. **Continuous phase** — water (with dissolved stabilizer).
> 2. **Dispersed phase** — monomer droplets.
> 3. **Polymer beads** — form in situ as the reaction proceeds inside the droplets.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Droplet / bead diameter | **50 µm – 2 000 µm** | Function of agitation, stabilizer, and ratio |
| Water-to-monomer mass ratio | **1 : 1 to 4 : 1** | Higher ratio → better heat removal |
| Initiator (oil-soluble) | **0.1 – 0.5 wt %** of monomer | AIBN, BPO, lauroyl peroxide |
| Stabilizer concentration | **0.05 – 0.5 wt %** of water | PVA, gelatin, HPMC, calcium phosphate |
| Reactor type | **Stirred batch / CSTR** | Glass-lined or SS316 |
| Operating temperature | **40 °C – 90 °C** | Monomer dependent |
| Conversion target | **> 95 %** | Beads separated at end |

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental difference between **suspension** and **emulsion** polymerization is the **locus of initiation** and the **droplet size**: Suspension uses **oil-soluble initiator** inside **large droplets (≥ 50 µm)**; Emulsion uses **water-soluble initiator** and produces **micelles/colloidal particles (≤ 1 µm)** stabilized by **surfactants**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture — Six Sequential Phases

The overall process of suspension polymerization is best understood as a sequence of six tightly coupled physical and chemical stages:

1. **Phase Preparation (Charging):**
   Water, water-soluble stabilizer, monomer, and oil-soluble initiator are independently charged into the reactor. The monomer is **insoluble (or only sparingly soluble) in water**; the initiator is **insoluble in water** but **freely soluble in the monomer**. This immiscibility is the thermodynamic prerequisite.

2. **Mechanical Dispersion (Droplet Formation):**
   Agitation by an impeller (typically **Rushton turbine** or **pitched-blade turbine**) shears the bulk monomer into droplets. The terminal droplet size is set by a **dynamic balance** between **droplet break-up** (driven by turbulence) and **coalescence** (driven by interfacial tension and collisions). The dimensionless number that governs this is the **Weber number** ($We$).

3. **Stabilization (Interfacial Protection):**
   The stabilizer molecules migrate to the monomer–water interface and form a **steric** (polymers like PVA) or **electrostatic/ionic** (inorganic powders like tricalcium phosphate) barrier that physically prevents droplet–droplet coalescence. Without this, the droplets would re-coalesce in seconds.

4. **Initiation within Droplets:**
   The oil-soluble initiator (e.g., **AIBN — Azobisisobutyronitrile** or **BPO — Benzoyl Peroxide**) decomposes thermally inside the droplets, generating free radicals. Because the initiator **cannot escape into the aqueous phase**, polymerization is **confined to the monomer droplets**.

5. **Polymerization (Bulk-like Kinetics in Micro-reactors):**
   Each droplet behaves as a **miniature bulk polymerization reactor**. The rate equation **is identical in form to bulk polymerization** ($R_p = k_p [M]\sqrt{f k_d [I]/k_t}$), since the locus of reaction is the monomer itself, not the water phase. Trommsdorff (gel) effect becomes significant at high conversion because the viscosity inside the droplet rises.

6. **Bead Recovery (Down-streaming):**
   At > 95 % conversion, the reactor contents are a slurry of polymer beads in water. Beads are **filtered or centrifuged**, **washed** to remove residual stabilizer, and **dried** in a fluidized-bed or rotary dryer.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Process — Engineering Drivers

| Engineering Driver | Why Suspension Polymerization Solves It |
|---|---|
| **Heat removal** (exothermic, ΔH ≈ –60 to –90 kJ/mol) | Water (high $C_p$ ≈ 4.18 kJ/kg·K) absorbs and convects heat efficiently; avoids thermal runaway common in bulk. |
| **Viscosity control** | Continuous phase is low-viscosity water; reactor stirring remains easy even at high molecular weight. |
| **Direct bead morphology** | No grinding/melt-processing needed; the product is already in spherical bead form. |
| **High molecular weight** | Each droplet contains a low radical concentration; termination is suppressed. |
| **Freedom from emulsifier contamination** | Unlike emulsion, the polymer is **free of soap/surfactant residues**. |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board examiner favourites**. Memorize the **form, the symbols, and the operating window** — derivation logic is given in Section 3.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle R_p = k_p \,[M]\,\sqrt{\frac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$ | Rate of polymerization in each droplet (bulk-like kinetics) | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p\,[M]}{2\,f\,k_d\,[I]^{1/2}\,k_t^{1/2}}$ | Number-average degree of polymerization (no chain transfer) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\bar{X}_n)_0} + C_M + C_S\,\frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I\,\frac{[I]}{[M]}$ | Mayo equation – effect of chain transfer to monomer, solvent, initiator | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\displaystyle We = \frac{\rho_c \, N^2 \, D^3}{\sigma}$ | Weber number — turbulence vs. interfacial tension (drop break-up) | $We_{crit} \approx 1$ to $12$ |
| 5 | $\displaystyle d_{32} \propto \left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho_c}\right)^{0.6}\,N^{-1.2}\,D^{-0.8}$ | Sauter mean diameter (empirical correlation) | m |
| 6 | $\displaystyle P = N_p \,\rho\,N^3\,D^5$ | Power consumption of Rushton turbine | W |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{d} \propto \left(\frac{C_s \,\rho_p \, U^2}{g\,(\rho_p-\rho_c)}\right)^{0.5}$ | Hinze–Koglin stabilization criterion | m |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \eta_{rel} = \left(\frac{1}{1-\phi}\right)^{[\eta]\rho_p}$ | Viscosity rise inside a droplet (Mooney-type) – explains Trommsdorff onset | dimensionless |

**Key symbols (recap):**
- $k_p$ — propagation rate constant, $k_t$ — termination rate constant, $k_d$ — initiator decomposition rate constant, $f$ — initiator efficiency.
- $[M]$, $[I]$, $[S]$ — concentrations of monomer, initiator, and chain-transfer agent.
- $\rho_c$ — density of continuous (water) phase, $\sigma$ — interfacial tension (≈ **35–50 mN/m** for styrene/water), $N$ — impeller speed (rev/s), $D$ — impeller diameter.
- $N_p$ — power number (≈ 5 for Rushton in turbulent regime), $C_s$ — stabilizer constant, $U$ — agitator tip speed, $g$ — gravity.
- $\phi$ — volume fraction of polymer in droplet, $[\eta]$ — intrinsic viscosity, $\rho_p$ — polymer density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Comparison for KTU Theory:** The rate expression is **identical in form** to **bulk** polymerization, NOT emulsion. The *numerical magnitude* of $R_p$ differs because the **effective $[I]$ per droplet is very low** (radicals cannot enter a droplet from the water), giving a much higher $\bar{X}_n$ than bulk at the same temperature.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Application | Why Suspension? |
|---|---|
| **Poly(vinyl chloride) — PVC (S-PVC)** | ~ 80 % of all PVC is made by suspension; produces uniform porous grains ideal for plasticizer absorption. |
| **Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)** | Pre-expanded beads (0.2–3 mm) for packaging and insulation. |
| **Ion-exchange resins** | Spherical beads of styrene–DVB with controlled porosity. |
| **PMMA denture base, optical beads** | Bead form eliminates machining. |
| **Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) precursor** | Suspension PAN for carbon fiber. |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Rate of Polymerization in a Single Droplet

Because each monomer droplet in a suspension reactor is an **isolated microreactor of bulk polymerization**, the kinetic derivation re-traces the steady-state radical balance.

### Step 1 — Radical Generation

The thermal homolysis of a typical oil-soluble initiator (e.g., BPO) produces two primary radicals:

$$I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2\,R^{\bullet}$$

Rate of radical generation per unit volume:

$$R_i = 2\,f\,k_d\,[I]$$

where $f$ is the **efficiency factor** (fraction of radicals that successfully initiate chains; for BPO at 60 °C, $f \approx 0.6$–$0.8$).

### Step 2 — Steady-State Assumption on Radicals

Radicals are consumed by termination (combination or disproportionation):

$$R^{\bullet} + R^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} P \quad (\text{dead polymer})$$

$$R_i = R_t \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 2\,f\,k_d\,[I] = 2\,k_t\,[M^{\bullet}]^2$$

Solving for radical concentration:

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$$

### Step 3 — Rate of Polymerization

Propagation: $M_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}^{\bullet}$

The rate at which monomer is consumed:

$$R_p = -\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p\,[M]\,[M^{\bullet}]$$

### Step 4 — Final Substitution

Substituting $[M^{\bullet}]$ from Step 2:

$$\boxed{\,R_p = k_p\,[M]\,\sqrt{\dfrac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}\,}$$

> This is the **canonical bulk / suspension rate equation** that KTU expects in the answer script.

### Step 5 — Number-Average Degree of Polymerization (no chain transfer)

The number-average chain length equals the rate of monomer consumption divided by the rate of chain termination:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_t/2} = \frac{k_p\,[M]\,\sqrt{\dfrac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}}{f\,k_d\,[I]}$$

$$\boxed{\,\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p\,[M]}{2\,f\,k_d\,[I]^{1/2}\,k_t^{1/2}}\,}$$

> This is **Karpov's equation** (analogous to the classical Smith–Ewart result for emulsion case 1). The key observation: $\bar{X}_n \propto [I]^{-1/2}$, so halving the initiator concentration **increases molecular weight by $\sqrt{2}$**.

### Step 6 — With Chain Transfer to Monomer, Solvent, and Initiator

The Mayo equation (introducing chain-transfer constants $C_M$, $C_S$, $C_I$):

$$\boxed{\,\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \underbrace{\frac{2\,f\,k_d\,k_t\,[I]^{1/2}}{k_p^{2}\,[M]^{2}}}_{\text{normal term}} + C_M + C_S\,\frac{[S]}{[M]} + C_I\,\frac{[I]}{[M]}\,}$$

Plotting $1/\bar{X}_n$ vs. $[I]^{1/2}/[M]$ at fixed $[S]$ and $[I]/[M]$ ratios yields a **straight line** with slope $\dfrac{2\,f\,k_d\,k_t}{k_p^{2}}$ and intercept $C_M + C_S\,[S]/[M] + C_I\,[I]/[M]$.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Sauter Mean Drop Diameter from a Force Balance

Consider a single monomer droplet of diameter $d$ in a turbulent flow field. Two forces compete:

- **Disruptive (inertial) force** from turbulent eddies: $\sim \rho_c\,u^2\,d^2$
- **Restoring (interfacial) force** from surface tension: $\sim \sigma\,d$

The ratio defines the **Weber number**:

$$We = \frac{\rho_c\,u^2\,d}{\sigma} = \frac{\text{inertial stress}}{\text{interfacial stress}}$$

A droplet breaks when the local $We$ exceeds a critical value $We_{crit}$ (typically **1 – 12** depending on viscosity ratio).

Equating the local turbulent velocity (Kolmogorov scale, $u \sim (N\,D)^{1}$ for stirred tanks) at break-up, the **Hinze correlation** gives the maximum **stable drop diameter**:

$$\boxed{\,d_{max} \;\propto\; \left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho_c}\right)^{0.6}\,N^{-1.2}\,D^{-0.8}\,}$$

The **Sauter mean diameter** (volume-to-surface average) is related to $d_{max}$ as:

$$d_{32} \;\approx\; 0.5\,d_{max} \;\propto\; N^{-1.2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway for KTU:** To halve the bead size, you must **increase impeller speed by $2^{1/1.2} \approx 1.78\times$**, which **increases power consumption by $N_p \rho N^3 D^5 \propto N^3 \Rightarrow 1.78^3 \approx 5.6\times$**. Hence there is a strong **economic trade-off** in choosing impeller speed.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Agitator Power Requirement

For a fully baffled, turbulent stirred tank with a **Rushton turbine**:

$$P = N_p\,\rho_c\,N^3\,D^5$$

- $N_p \approx 5$ (Rushton, $Re > 10^4$)
- $\rho_c \approx 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (water)
- Example: $N = 5\,\text{rev/s}$, $D = 0.5\,\text{m}$ → $P \approx 5 \times 1000 \times 125 \times 0.03125 \approx 19.5\,\text{kW}$

## 3.4 Derivation D — Trommsdorff (Gel) Effect Onset Inside a Droplet

As conversion $x$ rises, the polymer weight fraction inside the droplet increases, raising the local viscosity. Using a Mooney-type relative-viscosity relation:

$$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta}{\eta_0} = \exp\!\left(\frac{[\eta]\,\rho_p\,\phi}{1 - \phi}\right)$$

where $\phi$ is the volume fraction of polymer. Termination (which requires two radicals to diffuse together) becomes diffusion-controlled when $\eta_{rel} \gtrsim 100$, i.e., $\phi \gtrsim 0.2$–$0.3$. The **effective termination rate constant** falls:

$$k_t^{eff} = k_t^{(0)}\,\exp\!\left(-\alpha\,\phi\right)$$

Hence $R_p$ **accelerates** despite falling $[M]$. This is the **auto-acceleration (Trommsdorff / Norrish–Smith) effect**, which in suspension polymerization is mitigated by the **continuous water phase acting as a heat sink**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Suspension vs. Other Techniques

| Feature | Bulk | **Suspension** | Emulsion | Solution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous phase | None (monomer only) | **Water** | Water | Solvent |
| Initiator location | In monomer | **In monomer (oil-soluble)** | In water (water-soluble) | In solution |
| Droplet / particle size | Reactor-sized | **50 µm – 2 mm** | 50 nm – 1 µm | Molecular |
| Rate of polymerization | Low | **Moderate** | Very high | Moderate |
| Molecular weight | Moderate | **High** | Very high | Low–moderate |
| Heat removal | Poor (viscous) | **Good (water bath)** | Excellent | Moderate |
| Product form | Solid mass | **Beads** | Latex | Solution → precipitate |
| Contaminants | None | **Stabilizer residues** | Soap/surfactant | Solvent residue |
| Typical examples | PMMA cast sheet | **PVC, PS, PAN, ion-exchange resin** | SBR latex, PVAc paint | PVA from methanol |
