# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (Model Answer Style):** Two-sentence crisp definitions followed by the missing detail. Total 3 marks = **1 mark definition + 1 mark component + 1 mark key feature**.

### Q1. Define suspension polymerization. List any two stabilizers used. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Suspension polymerization is a heterogeneous chain-growth polymerization in which a water-immiscible monomer is dispersed as micrometer-sized droplets in a continuous aqueous phase using mechanical agitation and a stabilizer; polymerization is initiated within the droplets by an oil-soluble free-radical initiator, yielding spherical polymer beads.
Two common stabilizers are **(i) Poly(vinyl alcohol) — PVA** (steric stabilizer) and **(ii) Tricalcium phosphate — $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$** (inorganic fine-powder stabilizer).
The key feature is that **each droplet behaves as an independent bulk-polymerization microreactor**, while the surrounding water phase efficiently removes the heat of polymerization.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between suspension and emulsion polymerization in any three aspects. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| # | Suspension Polymerization | Emulsion Polymerization |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuous phase: **water**; droplet size **50 µm – 2 mm** | Continuous phase: water; particle size **50 nm – 1 µm** |
| 2 | Initiator: **oil-soluble** (BPO, AIBN) | Initiator: **water-soluble** (KPS, APS) |
| 3 | Stabilizer: protective colloid / fine powder (no soap) | Stabilizer: **surfactant / soap** (micelles form) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (either–or). Both choices below carry **7 + 7 = 14 marks** split across sub-parts (a) and (b). Model answers are exhaustive so you can write *exactly the same structure* in the exam hall.

---

### Q3(A). (a) Explain the mechanism of suspension polymerization with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of stabilizers. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism (5 steps):**
1. The **water-immiscible monomer** (e.g., vinyl chloride, styrene) is charged into a stirred, baffled reactor containing water and a dissolved/fine-powder stabilizer.
2. The **impeller (Rushton turbine)** shears the bulk monomer into discrete spherical droplets of **50 µm – 2 mm**, stabilized at the interface by adsorbed stabilizer molecules.
3. An **oil-soluble initiator** (AIBN, BPO, lauroyl peroxide) is added — it is **insoluble in water** and partitions exclusively into the droplets.
4. Thermal decomposition of the initiator inside each droplet generates **free radicals** that initiate chain propagation. Because radicals cannot cross the water–monomer interface, **polymerization is confined to the droplets**; each droplet is a **miniature bulk reactor**.
5. As conversion exceeds 95 %, the slurry is cooled, filtered/centrifuged, washed to remove stabilizer residues, and dried to yield **spherical polymer beads**.

**Role of stabilizers (3 functions, 1.5 marks each sub-bullet):**
- **Prevent coalescence** of monomer droplets during polymerization by forming a **steric barrier** (PVA, gelatin, HPMC) or an **electrostatic/mechanical barrier** (tricalcium phosphate, talc, $\text{BaSO}_4$).
- **Control bead size distribution** — higher stabilizer concentration → smaller, more uniform beads; this is governed by the **Hinze–Koglin stabilization criterion**.
- **Modify surface properties** of the final beads (porosity, plasticizer absorption in PVC) and prevent fouling of reactor walls.

**Diagram:** See the Mermaid block in Section 4.2 — the student may draw a circle labelled "monomer droplet" containing initiator + radical + growing chain, surrounded by water + stabilizer interface.

---

### Q3(A). (b) Derive the rate expression for suspension polymerization and discuss the effect of initiator concentration on the molecular weight of the polymer. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Apply the steady-state radical balance to a single monomer droplet:

$$R_i = 2\,f\,k_d\,[I], \qquad R_t = 2\,k_t\,[M^{\bullet}]^2$$

At steady state $R_i = R_t$, so:

$$[M^{\bullet}] = \sqrt{\frac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$$

The rate of polymerization (rate of monomer consumption) is:

$$R_p = k_p\,[M]\,[M^{\bullet}] = k_p\,[M]\sqrt{\frac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}}$$

Number-average degree of polymerization (no chain transfer):

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_i/2} = \frac{k_p\,[M]}{2\,\sqrt{f\,k_d\,k_t\,[I]}}$$

**Effect of initiator concentration on molecular weight (3 marks):**
- $\bar{X}_n \propto [I]^{-1/2}$ — **halving the initiator concentration increases the molecular weight by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$**.
- This is a **universal result for radical polymerization** (bulk, suspension, solution).
- In industrial practice, suspension polymerization uses a **low initiator concentration (0.1 – 0.5 wt %)** precisely to obtain **high molecular weight** (e.g., PVC $\bar{M}_w$ ≈ 50 000 – 150 000 g/mol).
- **Trommsdorff (gel) effect** at high conversion further enhances molecular weight by suppressing $k_t$ inside the viscous droplet.

---

### Q3(B). (a) What are the criteria for a monomer to be polymerized by suspension technique? Discuss the different types of stabilizers with examples. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Criteria for monomer (1 mark each):**
1. **Insoluble (or sparingly soluble) in water** — otherwise it diffuses out of the droplets and the system reverts to solution polymerization.
2. **Capable of free-radical polymerization** — must contain a vinyl, acrylic, or diene group.
3. **Should not be highly water-reactive** (no hydrolysis under reactor conditions).
4. **Sufficient interfacial tension with water** (≥ 25 mN/m) to allow droplet formation.
5. **Monomer viscosity** not too high initially (≤ 10 mPa·s) to allow droplet break-up.
6. Examples satisfying all the above: **vinyl chloride (VCM), styrene, methyl methacrylate (MMA), vinylidene chloride, acrylonitrile (for PAN precursor)**.

**Types of stabilizers (6 marks, 2 marks each):**

**Type 1 — Water-soluble polymers (steric / protective colloids):**
- Form a viscous adsorbed layer at the droplet interface that physically prevents coalescence.
- Examples: **Poly(vinyl alcohol) — PVA**, **gelatin**, **hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC)**, **sodium polyacrylate**, **carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC)**.
- Used at 0.05 – 0.5 wt % based on water; can leave a thin residue on bead surface (important for medical-grade polymers).

**Type 2 — Inorganic fine powders (electrostatic / mechanical barrier):**
- Fine particles (1 – 10 µm) adsorb at the interface, creating a **Pickering-type** barrier.
- Examples: **Tricalcium phosphate — $\text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$**, **talc**, **barium sulfate**, **calcium carbonate**, **hydroxyapatite**.
- **Advantage:** cheap, easily removed by dilute acid wash; preferred for rigid PVC where residue is unacceptable.

**Type 3 — Surfactants (rarely used alone):**
- Anionic (sodium dodecyl sulfate), cationic (CTAB) — but these produce small particles and contaminate the polymer, so they are not preferred in pure suspension. Used in **hybrid suspension-emulsion** systems.

---

### Q3(B). (b) Compare suspension polymerization with bulk and emulsion polymerization. Explain the engineering advantages of suspension polymerization for the commercial production of PVC. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Bulk | Suspension | Emulsion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase | Single | Two (water + monomer droplet) | Three (water + micelle + monomer droplet) |
| Initiator | Oil-soluble | Oil-soluble | Water-soluble |
| Particle/droplet size | Reactor | 50 µm – 2 mm | 50 nm – 1 µm |
| Heat removal | Poor (viscous) | Excellent (water bath) | Excellent |
| Product purity | High | Moderate (stabilizer residue) | Low (soap residue) |
| Typical $R_p$ | Low | Moderate | Very high |
| Typical $\bar{M}_w$ | Moderate | High | Very high |
| Product form | Solid mass | Beads | Latex |
| Example | PMMA cast sheet | PVC, EPS, ion-exchange resin | SBR, PVAc latex |

**Why suspension is preferred for commercial PVC (4 marks):**
1. **Thermal runaway control** — PVC polymerization is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ –96 kJ/mol of VCM) and the reaction is autocatalytic; the water phase absorbs ~ 80 % of the heat and keeps the temperature within ± 0.5 °C, ensuring uniform $M_w$.
2. **Plasticizer absorption** — suspension-grade PVC (S-PVC) beads are **porous** with high surface area, enabling rapid absorption of phthalate plasticizers during compounding (impossible with emulsion-grade E-PVC, which has soap-filled pores).
3. **Purity** — stabilizer residues are removed by simple water wash; no soap or surfactant is required, so S-PVC is the **food-grade and medical-grade standard**.
4. **Direct bead morphology** — no grinding required; bead size (50 – 250 µm) is controlled by agitation speed and stabilizer concentration.
5. **Scale-up** — suspension reactors scale linearly to **> 200 m³** in commercial plants (e.g., Vinnolit, Formosa Plastics), making it the most economically viable route for the > 50 million tonnes/year global PVC market.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing suspension with emulsion.** Examiners **deduct 1 – 2 marks** if you say *"the initiator is water-soluble"* or *"micelles are formed"* — these are EXCLUSIVELY for emulsion polymerization.
> 2. **Writing the rate equation with surfactant concentration** — that is the **Smith–Ewart Case 1** for emulsion, NOT suspension. Suspension uses the **bulk kinetics expression** $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{f k_d [I] / k_t}$.
> 3. **Omitting the role of the aqueous phase as a heat sink** — this is THE single most important engineering reason for choosing suspension; examiners expect it in any 7-mark answer.
> 4. **Confusing stabilizers with surfactants** — protective colloids and Pickering-type powders are NOT surfactants; they do not form micelles.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify initiator concentration range** in any derivation or numerical — always quote **0.1 – 0.5 wt %** of monomer.
> 6. **Skipping the "droplet-as-mini-reactor" analogy** — examiners use it as a check-marker for conceptual clarity.
> 7. **Drawing poor diagrams** — always show: monomer droplet ↔ continuous water phase ↔ stabilizer at interface ↔ radical inside droplet.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ Suspension polymerization = **monomer droplets dispersed in water**, stabilized by **protective colloids or fine powders**, polymerized by **oil-soluble initiators**.
- ✅ Each droplet = **miniature bulk polymerization reactor** → bulk-like kinetics apply.
- ✅ Rate equation: $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{f k_d [I] / k_t}$ — **NOT** Smith–Ewart.
- ✅ $\bar{X}_n \propto [I]^{-1/2}$ — **lower initiator = higher molecular weight**.
- ✅ **Weber number** controls droplet break-up; $d_{32} \propto N^{-1.2}$.
- ✅ **Power consumption** of Rushton turbine: $P = N_p \rho N^3 D^5$.
- ✅ Three classes of stabilizers: **(i) Water-soluble polymers (PVA, gelatin, HPMC)**; **(ii) Inorganic fine powders (TCP, talc)**; **(iii) Surfactants (rare)**.
- ✅ **Stabilizer function**: prevent coalescence + control bead size + modify bead surface.
- ✅ **Monomer criteria**: water-immiscible + radical-polymerizable + low water-reactivity.
- ✅ **Trommsdorff (gel) effect** occurs inside the droplet at high conversion; mitigated by water as heat sink.
- ✅ **Major industrial products**: **S-PVC (~ 80 % of global PVC)**, **EPS**, **PMMA beads**, **ion-exchange resins**, **PAN precursor**.
- ✅ **Advantages**: good heat removal, no soap contamination, direct spherical beads, high $\bar{M}_w$, easy scale-up.
- ✅ **Disadvantages**: stabilizer residue (requires washing), batch process, water-treatment cost.
- ✅ **Distinguishing test for examiners**: "Suspension uses **oil-soluble initiator + protective colloid + large droplets**; Emulsion uses **water-soluble initiator + surfactant + micelles**."



<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emulsion Polymerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Emulsion Polymerisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emulsion Polymerisation** is a heterogeneous chain-growth (addition) polymerisation technique in which a water-immiscible vinyl monomer is dispersed as droplets in an **aqueous continuous phase** with the help of a **surfactant (emulsifier)**, and the polymerisation is initiated by a **water-soluble (thermal or redox) initiator**. The locus of polymerisation is not the macroscopic monomer droplets, but the **sub-microscopic surfactant micelles** swollen with monomer, and later the growing polymer particles.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, emulsion polymerisation is classified as one of the four principal commercial methods of free-radical chain polymerisation, the others being **bulk, solution, and suspension** polymerisation. It is the **only industrial technique that simultaneously produces high molecular weight polymer at very high rates of polymerisation** — a combination unattainable in the other three methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Surfactant (Emulsifier):** An amphiphilic molecule with a hydrophilic head and a hydrophobic tail (e.g., **Sodium Dodecyl Sulphate / SDS, C₁₂H₂₅SO₄Na⁺**). Above the **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)**, these molecules self-assemble into **micelles** (typically 4–10 nm diameter).

### 1.2 Standard Recipe of an Emulsion Polymerisation

| Component | Function | Typical Example | Typical Wt % |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monomer** | Building block | Styrene, Vinyl Acetate, MMA, Butadiene | 30 – 50 % |
| **Water** | Continuous medium | Distilled/DI water | 50 – 70 % |
| **Surfactant** | Micelle & particle stabilisation | SDS, SLS, non-ionic surfactants | 0.5 – 3 % |
| **Water-soluble Initiator** | Generates free radicals in water | K₂S₂O₈, (NH₄)₂S₂O₄, Redox pair | 0.1 – 0.5 % |
| **Buffer / Electrolyte** | Controls pH | NaHCO₃, Na₃PO₄ | 0.1 – 0.3 % |
| **Chain Transfer Agent** (optional) | Molecular weight control | n-Dodecyl Mercaptan (TDM) | trace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **protective colloid** (e.g., polyvinyl alcohol, polyacrylic acid) is sometimes added to improve colloidal stability against shear, freezing, and electrolyte addition.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Mayonnaise Analogy"

Imagine preparing mayonnaise at home. You vigorously whisk tiny droplets of **oil** (analogous to the hydrophobic **monomer**) into **water** (the continuous phase). A spoonful of **mustard/egg-yolk** (analogous to the **surfactant**) sits at the surface of every oil droplet, preventing the droplets from merging back into one bulk oil layer. The droplets remain individually suspended — an **emulsion**.

Now, instead of a whisk, imagine the system is gently heated and a **water-soluble peroxide** (the initiator) is added. The peroxide decomposes in the *water* phase, but the polymerisation chain reaction actually proceeds **inside the surfactant-stabilised monomer droplets (micelles)**. The result is a milky **latex** — a colloidal dispersion of polymer particles in water, exactly the product you get in emulsion polymerisation.

> [!NOTE]
> The "oil" in the analogy corresponds to monomer; the "mustard" corresponds to surfactant; the "heat" plus initiator corresponds to the radical generation step; and the final product (mayonnaise) is analogous to the **latex** — a stable colloidal dispersion of polymer particles (typically 50–500 nm in diameter).

### 1.4 Why Emulsion Polymerisation is Industrially Important

1. **High Rate AND High Molecular Weight simultaneously** — Smith-Ewart Case 2 kinetics.
2. **Excellent heat dissipation** — water (high specific heat, ~4.18 kJ/kg·K) absorbs reaction exotherm, preventing runaway / "hot spots" that plague bulk polymerisation.
3. **Low viscosity product** — even at >50 % solids, the latex remains water-thin, enabling easy stirring, pumping, and heat transfer.
4. **Direct use as latex** — paints, adhesives, coatings, textile finishes, carpet backing, gloves, etc.
5. **Environmental advantage** — water replaces organic solvents (low VOC, non-toxic, non-flammable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Over **5 million metric tonnes** of emulsion-polymerised polymers (SBR rubber, PVC latex, acrylic emulsions, PVA emulsions) are produced globally each year, making it the **single most industrially important polymerisation process** by volume.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Harkins Qualitative Theory (1945–1947)

W. D. Harkins proposed a qualitative picture of emulsion polymerisation, later quantified by Smith and Ewart (1948). The reaction mixture contains **three distinct physical loci**, each with a very different surface area:

| Locus | Size | Number / cm³ water | Total surface area | Monomer concentration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Monomer droplets** (1 – 10 µm) | Macroscopic | ~10⁸ | Small (~10⁴ cm²) | Bulk monomer (~ 5 M for styrene) |
| **Surfactant micelles** (4 – 10 nm) | Sub-microscopic | ~10¹⁷ – 10¹⁸ | HUGE (~10⁶ cm²) | Swollen with monomer (~ 1 M) |
| **Aqueous phase (true solution)** | Molecular | — | — | Very low (~10⁻³ M for styrene in water) |

**Harkins' Central Insight:** Although the **macroscopic monomer droplets** are visually dominant, they possess negligible surface area. The **micelles**, although invisible, have a surface area that is **~10⁶ times larger** than that of the droplets. Therefore, the **oligomeric radicals generated in the water phase are captured almost exclusively by micelles**, not by droplets. Polymerisation effectively occurs in micelles, which then transform into **polymer particles** (latex particles).

### 2.2 The Three Stages of Emulsion Polymerisation (Harkins–Smith–Ewart)

#### **STAGE I — Particle Nucleation (Conversion ≈ 0 – 10 %)**

* Initiator (e.g., K₂S₂O₈) decomposes in the aqueous phase:

$$\text{S}_2\text{O}_8^{\,2-} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \;\text{SO}_4^{\,-\bullet}$$

* A sulphate radical adds monomer units dissolved in the water phase (very low [M] in water, so oligomer growth is slow) until the oligomer becomes surface-active.
* This surface-active oligomer enters a **micelle** (much more probable than entering a monomer droplet because of enormous surface area).
* Inside the micelle, the oligomer encounters **swollen monomer** and grows rapidly, propagating to a long chain.
* The micelle is converted into a **monomer-swollen polymer particle** (~10 nm).
* New micelles continue to be consumed; eventually micelles disappear (Stage I ends when surfactant concentration falls **below CMC**).
* **Rate of polymerisation accelerates** in Stage I as N (number of particles) increases.

> [!NOTE]
> The locus of polymerisation is the **micelle/polymer particle** — NOT the monomer droplets. Monomer droplets are merely **reservoirs** that feed monomer by diffusion into the particles.

#### **STAGE II — Particle Growth (Conversion ≈ 10 – 50 %, sometimes up to 80 %)**

* All micelles have been converted to polymer particles. **No new particles form.**
* Monomer diffuses from the large droplets (~10⁸ cm⁻³) through the water phase into the polymer particles.
* Polymer particles remain saturated with monomer at a concentration **[M]_p** (≈ bulk monomer concentration, e.g., 5.5 M for styrene in polystyrene particles at 50 °C).
* **Constant rate period** — [M]_p is constant; N is constant; therefore Rp is constant.
* Monomer droplets gradually shrink and **disappear at the end of Stage II**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Stage II is the **engine room** of emulsion polymerisation. Most commercial reactors are designed to operate predominantly in this stage, where Smith-Ewart Case 2 kinetics apply.

#### **STAGE III — Completion (Conversion ≈ 50 – 100 %)**

* Monomer droplets are gone; only polymer particles containing residual monomer remain.
* [M]_p inside the particles **decreases** as polymerisation consumes the remaining monomer.
* Rate of polymerisation **declines** (Trommsdorff / Norrish–Smith effect may set in at high conversion due to reduced termination rate — see also bulk polymerisation).
* Final latex contains solid polymer particles (50 – 500 nm) dispersed in water.

### 2.3 Smith–Ewart Quantitative Kinetics (1948)

Smith and Ewart wrote a population balance for the number of particles containing *n* free radicals. They defined:

* **Nᵢ** = number of particles per unit volume containing *i* radicals
* **N** = total number of particles per unit volume
* **ρ** = rate of radical entry into a single particle (radicals · particle⁻¹ · s⁻¹)
* **ρᵢ** = rate of initiation in water (radicals · cm⁻³ · s⁻¹)
* **k₀** = rate constant for radical exit (desorption) from a particle
* **k_t** = rate constant for bimolecular termination inside a particle

The general Smith-Ewart recursion:

$$\frac{dN_i}{dt} = \rho\, N_{i-1} - \rho\, N_i + (i+1)k_0 N_{i+1} - i k_0 N_i + \frac{k_t}{v}\,(i+2)(i+1) N_{i+2} - \frac{k_t}{v}\,i(i-1) N_i$$

where *v* is the swollen volume of a particle.

### 2.4 Smith-Ewart Case 2 (The Industrially Important Case)

Assumptions:
1. **ρ is small** (low rate of radical entry per particle, ~10⁻³ – 10⁻¹ s⁻¹).
2. **Termination inside a particle is instantaneous** as soon as a second radical enters (i.e., the entry of a radical finds another radical already inside ⇒ they terminate immediately).
3. **No radical exit** (k₀ ≈ 0).
4. **Particles contain either 0 or 1 radical at any instant** — the "0/1" or "zero-one" assumption.

Under Case 2, the **average number of radicals per particle, n̄ = 0.5**. This is the **single most important result** of Smith-Ewart theory.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of polymer particles per unit volume of water | $$N = 0.208 \cdot \rho_i^{\,0.4} \cdot a_s^{\,0.6} \cdot \left(\frac{k_d f [I]}{k_w}\right)^{0.4}$$ | Smith-Ewart Case 2; aₛ = [Surfactant] above CMC |
| 2 | Simplified form (commonly used) | $$N = k \cdot \rho^{\,0.4} \cdot S^{\,0.6} \cdot I^{\,0.4}$$ | k is a constant |
| 3 | Average radicals per particle (Case 2) | $$\bar{n} = 0.5$$ | Foundational result |
| 4 | Total radical concentration | $$[\text{M}^\bullet] = \frac{N}{2}$$ | Since n̄ × N = 0.5 × N = N/2 |
| 5 | Rate of polymerisation (Rₚ) | $$R_p = k_p\,[M]_p\,\frac{N}{2}$$ | Constant in Stage II |
| 6 | Rate of initiation in water | $$\rho_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$ | Factor 2 for 2 radicals per initiator |
| 7 | Rate of radical entry per particle | $$\rho = \frac{\rho_i}{N}$$ | Radicals entering one particle per second |
| 8 | Instantaneous number-average DP | $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_i + R_{tr}} \approx \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{2 \rho_i} = \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{4 f k_d [I]}$$ | **Key KTU result — high M.W. AND high Rₚ** |
| 9 | Degree of polymerisation (Case 2) | $$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N \cdot k_p [M]_p}{2 \rho_i}$$ | Inverse dependence on ρᵢ |
| 10 | Critical Micelle Concentration (SDS, 25 °C) | **CMC ≈ 8 × 10⁻³ mol/L = 0.008 M** | Standard value for SDS |
| 11 | Micelle aggregation number (SDS) | **N_agg ≈ 60 – 70** | Number of surfactant molecules per micelle |
| 12 | Particle diameter range | **50 – 500 nm** | Typical latex particle size |
| 13 | Heat of polymerisation absorbed by water | Heat capacity of water **Cₚ = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)** | Explains thermal safety |

> [!NOTE]
> Formula 8 is the **board-favourite** derivation. Memorise that $R_p \propto N \propto [S]^{0.6}[I]^{0.4}$ and $\bar{X}_n \propto N/[I]$. Doubling surfactant thus increases molecular weight more strongly than doubling initiator.

### 2.6 Comparison with Other Chain Polymerisation Methods

| Property | Bulk | Solution | Suspension | Emulsion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat removal | Poor | Good | Good | **Excellent** |
| Molecular weight achievable | Low–Med | Low | Med | **Very high** |
| Rate of polymerisation | High | Low | Med | **Very high** |
| Product form | Polymer melt/solid | Polymer solution | Beads/granules | **Latex** |
| Solvent issues | None | Yes (toxic, costly) | None (water) | **None (water)** |
| Reactor fouling | High | Low | Low | **Low** |
| Solid content in product | 100 % | 5 – 30 % | 100 % | **30 – 60 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **simultaneous achievement of high molecular weight and high rate** is the unique signature of emulsion polymerisation — possible because Rp ∝ N (∝ [S]⁰·⁶) while X̄ₙ ∝ N/[I]. One can increase N (and hence both Rp AND X̄ₙ) by adding more surfactant, without increasing initiator.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

* **Paints & Coatings:** Acrylic, PVA, styrene-acrylic emulsions for interior/exterior wall paints.
* **Adhesives:** EVA, acrylic pressure-sensitive adhesives.
* **SBR Rubber:** Styrene–Butadiene emulsion copolymer for tyres (cold emulsion SBR).
* **NBR Rubber:** Acrylonitrile–Butadiene latex for gloves, gaskets, hoses.
* **PVC Paste Resin:** Emulsion PVC used in plastisols for synthetic leather.
* **Paper Coatings:** SB-latex for glossy printability.
* **Biomedical / Drug Delivery:** Latex particles as carriers for controlled release.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Smith-Ewart Case-2 Number of Particles (N)

**Starting assumption:** During Stage I, every entering oligomeric radical is captured by a micelle. Once a micelle captures a radical, it becomes a "live" polymer particle. The probability that a micelle has captured a radical by time *t* is governed by Poisson statistics if the capture rate is *ρ* per micelle per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactor-level input parameters:** rate of radical generation in the water phase $\rho_i$ (radicals · cm⁻³ · s⁻¹), total surfactant concentration $S_T$ (mol · cm⁻³), volume of water $V_w$ (cm³), and volume of a swollen micelle $v_m$ (cm³).

**Step 1 — Rate of radical entry per micelle (ρ).**

The total number of micelles per cm³ of water is approximately $M = (S_T - \text{CMC})/N_{agg}$ per cm³. The rate of radical entry per micelle is:

$$\rho = \frac{\rho_i}{M} = \frac{\rho_i \, N_{agg}}{S_T - \text{CMC}}$$

This expresses how often, on average, a single micelle is hit by a radical.

**Step 2 — Number of micelles activated in time dt.**

Each micelle is activated with rate ρ. The number of fresh micelles becoming active in the interval *dt* is:

$$dM_{active} = \rho\, M_{unactivated}\, dt$$

This is a first-order kinetic equation in the population of "still-empty" micelles.

**Step 3 — Integration.**

Let $M_0$ be the initial number of micelles and $M_u$ the unactivated ones at time $t$:

$$\frac{dM_u}{dt} = -\rho\, M_u$$

Separating variables and integrating from $M_0$ to $M_u$:

$$\int_{M_0}^{M_u} \frac{dM_u}{M_u} = -\rho \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{M_u}{M_0}\right) = -\rho\, t$$

$$M_u(t) = M_0\, e^{-\rho\, t}$$

**Step 4 — Number of active particles formed (N, in time *t*).**

Number of activated micelles = number of particles formed:

$$N(t) = M_0 - M_u = M_0\left(1 - e^{-\rho t}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Micelles are exhausted at time τ when 99 % of micelles are activated.**

$$e^{-\rho \tau} = 0.01 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \rho \tau \approx 4.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \tau \approx \frac{4.6}{\rho}$$

**Step 6 — Final particle number (long-time limit).**

All micelles eventually become particles, so the *final* number of particles is the original number of micelles:

$$N = M_0 = \frac{(S_T - \text{CMC})\, N_{agg}}{V_w}$$

Substituting ρ from Step 1 and assuming the surfactant is well above CMC, one obtains the **Smith–Ewart result**:

$$\boxed{\,N = 0.208\;\rho_i^{\,0.4}\; a_s^{\,0.6} \cdot \left(\frac{k_d f\,[I]}{k_w}\right)^{0.4}\,}$$

where $a_s$ is the concentration of surfactant *above the CMC* and $k_w$ is a kinetic constant capturing the competition between radical capture and growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key qualitative conclusions:**
> - $N \propto [S]^{0.6}$ — more surfactant, more (smaller) particles.
> - $N \propto [I]^{0.4}$ — more initiator, more (smaller) particles.
> - $N \propto (\text{temperature})^{0.4}$ — via $k_d$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Rate of Polymerisation in Stage II (Smith-Ewart Case 2)

**Step 1 — Radical concentration in the aqueous phase.**

The rate of formation of free radicals in the water phase is:

$$\rho_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

with $f$ = initiator efficiency, $k_d$ = first-order decomposition rate constant, [I] = initiator concentration. Units: radicals · L⁻¹ · s⁻¹ (or per cm³).

**Step 2 — Total number of radicals in all particles.**

In Case 2, the average number of free radicals in one particle is $\bar{n} = 0.5$. Hence the total radical population per cm³ of water is:

$$[\text{M}^\bullet] = \bar{n}\, N = \frac{N}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Propagation rate within a single particle.**

Inside a particle, monomer concentration is the *swollen-particle* monomer concentration, $[M]_p$ (essentially the bulk monomer concentration, e.g. 5.5 M for styrene). The local propagation rate is:

$$R_{p,\text{local}} = k_p\,[M]_p\,[\text{M}^\bullet]_\text{particle}$$

**Step 4 — Total rate per unit volume of water.**

Summing over all particles:

$$R_p = N \cdot k_p\,[M]_p\,[\text{M}^\bullet]_\text{particle} = k_p\,[M]_p \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_p = \frac{1}{2}\, k_p\,[M]_p\,N\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is **the** canonical KTU result for Stage II. It is the rate of monomer consumption per unit volume of water (or per unit volume of *total* reactor, depending on convention).

**Step 5 — Substituting the Smith-Ewart expression for N:**

$$R_p = \frac{1}{2}\, k_p\,[M]_p \cdot 0.208\;\rho_i^{\,0.4}\,a_s^{\,0.6}\left(\frac{k_d f [I]}{k_w}\right)^{0.4}$$

Simplifying:

$$R_p = 0.104\, k_p\,[M]_p\,\rho_i^{\,0.4}\,a_s^{\,0.6}\left(\frac{k_d f [I]}{k_w}\right)^{0.4}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation $\bar{X}_n$

**Step 1 — General definition.**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{\text{Rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{Rate of chain formation}}$$

The rate of chain formation is the rate at which a "living" radical is destroyed — i.e. either by **termination** (rate $R_t$) or by **chain transfer** (rate $R_{tr}$):

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr}} = \frac{R_p}{\rho_i}\quad(\text{if chain transfer is negligible})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Smith-Ewart Case 2.**

$$R_p = \tfrac{1}{2} k_p [M]_p N \quad\text{and}\quad R_t = \rho_i$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} k_p [M]_p N}{\rho_i} = \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{2\rho_i}$$

If $R_{tr} \neq 0$, modify the denominator:

$$\boxed{\,\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{2\rho_i + \sum_i R_{tr,i}}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\bar{X}_n$ in terms of measurable quantities.**

Using $\rho_i = 2 f k_d [I]$:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{4 f k_d [I]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **paradoxical, board-favourite result**: $\bar{X}_n$ is proportional to $N$ (which itself depends on $[I]^{0.4}$). Therefore $\bar{X}_n \propto [I]^{-0.6}$ — **increasing the initiator DECREASES the molecular weight**, exactly as expected. Yet Rp $\propto N \propto [I]^{0.4}$ — **rate still INCREASES** with initiator. This is the unique kinetic feature of emulsion polymerisation that bulk and solution methods cannot replicate.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Key Variables on $N$, $R_p$, $\bar{X}_n$

| Change | Effect on N | Effect on Rₚ | Effect on X̄ₙ | Industrial implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ [Surfactant] S** | N ∝ S⁰·⁶ ↑↑ | Rₚ ∝ S⁰·⁶ ↑↑ | X̄ₙ ∝ S⁰·⁶ ↑↑ | Smaller, more numerous, higher M.W. particles |
| **↑ [Initiator] I** | N ∝ I⁰·⁴ ↑ | Rₚ ∝ I⁰·⁴ ↑ | X̄ₙ ∝ I⁻⁰·⁶ ↓↓ | Higher rate but lower molecular weight |
| **↑ Temperature T** | N ∝ (k_d)⁰·⁴ ↑ (k_d ↑ with T) | Rₚ ↑ (k_p ↑, N ↑) | X̄ₙ ↓ (more radicals, ρᵢ ↑) | Faster, lower M.W. polymer |
| **↑ [Monomer]** | Small effect on N | Rₚ ∝ [M] ↑ | X̄ₙ ∝ [M] ↑ | Higher M.W. at fixed rate |
| **↑ Chain Transfer Agent** | No effect | No effect | X̄ₙ ↓ | TDM / mercaptan — MW regulator |
| **↑ Stirring / agitation** | Possible coagulation | Indirect | Indirect | Stability control |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> **Problem.** Styrene is emulsion polymerised at 50 °C using 0.5 wt % SDS (above CMC), 0.1 wt % K₂S₂O₈, 50 wt % styrene, water balance. The following data are reported: $k_p = 2.5 \times 10^{3}$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹, $k_d = 4.5 \times 10^{-6}$ s⁻¹, $f = 0.6$, $[M]_p = 5.0$ mol L⁻¹ (inside particles), $[I] = 3.7 \times 10^{-3}$ mol L⁻¹, $N = 2.4 \times 10^{14}$ particles per mL of water. Compute (a) the rate of polymerisation $R_p$, (b) the number-average degree of polymerisation $\bar{X}_n$, and (c) the rate of initiation $\rho_i$.

**Solution.**

**(a) Rate of polymerisation (Smith-Ewart Case 2):**

$$R_p = \tfrac{1}{2} k_p [M]_p N$$

$$R_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (2.5 \times 10^{3}) \times (5.0) \times (2.4 \times 10^{14})\;\text{L mol}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1}\,\cdot\text{mol L}^{-1}\,\cdot\,\text{mL}^{-1}$$

$$R_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{3} \times 5.0 \times 2.4 \times 10^{14}\;\text{mol s}^{-1}\,\text{mL}^{-1}$$

$$R_p = 1.5 \times 10^{18}\;\text{mol s}^{-1}\,\text{mL}^{-1}\;\text{(water)}$$

Equivalent volumetric units: convert to mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ by dividing by 1000 mL/L:

$$R_p = 1.5 \times 10^{15}\;\text{mol L}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**(b) Rate of initiation:**

$$\rho_i = 2 f k_d [I] = 2 \times 0.6 \times (4.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.7 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\rho_i = 2.0 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{mol L}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**(c) Number-average degree of polymerisation:**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{\rho_i} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{15}}{2.0 \times 10^{-8}} = 7.5 \times 10^{22}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical sanity check:** The astronomical value of $\bar{X}_n$ in this calculation is *unphysical* and signals that the *units* of $N$ (per mL vs. per L) and the *basis* (per volume water vs. per volume reactor) have been mixed. Always **state the basis explicitly**: N per cm³ water, Rₚ per cm³ water, [M]ₚ in mol per cm³ of particles. A typical KTU-style answer would carry all units, and the board examiner deducts marks for unit inconsistency. A realistic $\bar{X}_n$ for emulsion polystyrene is **10⁴ – 10⁶**, not 10²².

The above example is therefore most useful as a **dimensional-analysis exercise**. For a clean KTU-grade answer, the standard expression is presented symbolically:

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{2 f k_d [I]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The examiner will NOT demand that you compute the final number — they will award full marks for **stating the formula, identifying the basis, and applying the Case 2 assumption $\bar{n} = 0.5$**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure A — Micelle and Particle Architecture (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph water[ "AQUEOUS PHASE  water" ]
        init[ "K2S2O8 in water" ]
        rad[ "SO4 radical" ]
        oligo[ "Oligomeric radical  surface active" ]
    end

    subgraph micelle[ "SURFACTANT MICELLE  diameter 4 to 10 nm" ]
        tail1[ "hydrophobic tail inward" ]
        head1[ "hydrophilic head outward" ]
        mon1[ "monomer swollen inside" ]
    end

    subgraph particle[ "POLYMER PARTICLE  diameter 50 to 500 nm" ]
        polym[ "polymer chains" ]
        mon2[ "monomer dissolved" ]
        surr[ "surfactant shell" ]
    end

    subgraph drop[ "MONOMER DROPLET  diameter 1 to 10 micrometer" ]
        monbulk[ "bulk liquid monomer" ]
    end

    init --> rad
    rad --> oligo
    oligo -->|"captured by surface area"| micelle
    micelle -->|"particle nucleation Stage I"| particle
    drop -->|"diffusion of monomer Stage II"| particle
    particle -->|"growth"| polym
```

### 4.2 Figure B — The Three-Stage Reaction Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage1["STAGE I  Particle Nucleation\nConversion 0 to 10 %\nMicelles consumed\nN increases\nRp accelerates"]
    stage2["STAGE II  Particle Growth\nConversion 10 to 50 %\nNo micelles, no new particles\nMonomer diffuses from droplets\nN constant, M_p constant\nRp CONSTANT  plateau"]
    stage3["STAGE III  Completion\nConversion 50 to 100 %\nDroplets exhausted\nM_p falls\nRp declines\nTrommsdorff may set in"]

    start(["Begin initiator decomposition\nand monomer addition"]) --> stage1
    stage1 -->|"micelles depleted\nat CMC"| stage2
    stage2 -->|"monomer droplets\ndisappear"| stage3
    stage3 --> finish(["Final latex\nsolid content 30 to 60 %\nparticle size 50 to 500 nm"])

    style stage1 fill:#fff4e6
    style stage2 fill:#e6ffe6
    style stage3 fill:#e6e6ff
```

### 4.3 Figure C — Rate of Polymerisation vs. Conversion Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["0 %"] --> b["10 %  Stage I ends\nmicelles gone\nN reaches final value"]
    b --> c["50 %  Stage II ends\ndroplets gone"]
    c --> d["100 %  Stage III ends\nfinal polymer"]

    note1["Rp rises  N increasing\nall micelles active"]:::n1
    note2["Rp CONSTANT\nN constant\nM_p constant\nSmith Ewart Case 2"]:::n2
    note3["Rp falls\nM_p decreasing\nresidual monomer used up"]:::n3

    b -.- note1
    c -.- note2
    d -.- note3

    classDef n1 fill:#ffe6cc
    classDef n2 fill:#ccffcc
    classDef n3 fill:#ccccff
```

### 4.4 Figure D — Comparative Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Recipe[ "EMULSION POLYMERISATION RECIPE" ]
        M[ "Monomer  Styrene / VAc / MMA / Butadiene" ]
        W[ "Water  50 to 70 %" ]
        S[ "Surfactant  SDS / SLS" ]
        I[ "Initiator  K2S2O8 / Redox" ]
        B[ "Buffer pH control" ]
        T[ "Chain Transfer Agent  optional" ]
    end

    subgraph Reactor[ "STIRRED TANK REACTOR  50 to 90 C" ]
        R[ "Latex  colloidal dispersion" ]
    end

    subgraph Product[ "PRODUCT FORMS" ]

        P2[ "Coagulated crumb  SBR" ]

    end

    M --> Reactor
    W --> Reactor
    S --> Reactor
    I --> Reactor
    B --> Reactor
    T --> Reactor
    Reactor --> P1
    Reactor --> P2
    Reactor --> P3
```

### 4.5 Figure E — Radical Entry / Termination Lifecycle in a Particle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r0["State 0  dormant particle\nno free radical"]
    r1["State 1  live particle\nONE free radical inside\nPROPAGATING"]
    r2["State 2  terminator event\nsecond radical enters\nBIMOL TERMINATION\nreturns to State 0"]

    r0 -->|"radical enters\nrate rho"| r1
    r1 -->|"radical enters\nrate rho\ntermination immediate"| r2
    r2 -->|"instantaneous\nin Case 2"| r0

    note1["Time-averaged occupancy\nn_bar = 0.5"]:::n
    r1 -.- note1

    classDef n fill:#fff2cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The particle flips between the dormant State 0 and the active State 1. Because the entry rate *ρ* is small and termination is instantaneous, the particle spends ~50 % of its life in each state. Hence $\bar{n} = 0.5$, the **Smith–Ewart Case 2 result**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** List any **three essential components** of an emulsion polymerisation recipe and state the function of each.

**Model Answer (3 × 1 mark):**

1. **Monomer** (e.g., styrene) — the polymerisable vinyl compound, dispersed as droplets and solubilised in micelles; provides the building block of the polymer. (1 mark)
2. **Surfactant / Emulsifier** (e.g., SDS, sodium dodecyl sulphate) — an amphiphilic molecule that, above the CMC, self-assembles into micelles which act as the locus of polymerisation. (1 mark)
3. **Water-soluble initiator** (e.g., K₂S₂O₈, potassium persulphate) — decomposes in the aqueous phase to give sulphate radicals that initiate polymerisation inside the micelles. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> A 3-mark answer should contain exactly three items, each with role + example. Adding a fourth (e.g., buffer) earns no extra credit; lacking one loses a mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]** Differentiate between **emulsion** and **suspension** polymerisation in any **three respects**.

**Model Answer (3 × 1 mark):**

| S.No. | Emulsion Polymerisation | Suspension Polymerisation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initiator is **water-soluble** (e.g., K₂S₂O₈) | Initiator is **monomer-soluble** (e.g., AIBN, BPO) |
| 2 | Polymerisation locus: **micelles** (sub-microscopic) | Polymerisation locus: **monomer droplets** (macroscopic) |
| 3 | Product is a **colloidal latex** (50 – 500 nm particles) | Product is **granular beads / pearls** (10 µm – 1 mm) |
| 4 | Surfactant is **essential** | Stabiliser (PVA, gelatin) is used, not true surfactant |
| 5 | Very high M.W. + high Rₚ simultaneously (Smith-Ewart) | Molecular weight similar to bulk polymerisation |

Any three rows (1 mark each).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most-asked KTU short-answer question on this topic. Memorise all five rows.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE-WISE KTU INTERNAL CHOICE RULE (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B sub-question carries 7 marks. Students answer **either OR**.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Model Paper Set 3]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Harkins–Smith–Ewart theory**, describe the **mechanism of emulsion polymerisation**. Discuss the **three stages** with suitable sketches. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Smith-Ewart equation for the rate of polymerisation** under Case 2 assumptions. Show that the **number-average degree of polymerisation** is $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]_p N}{2 \rho_i}$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

*(1 mark for the recipe / system description; 1 mark each for the three stages; 1 mark for locus explanation; 1 mark for kinetics essence; 1 mark for diagram/schematic.)*

**System description (1 mark):**
Emulsion polymerisation is a heterogeneous chain-growth process in which a water-immiscible monomer (e.g., styrene) is dispersed in water with the help of a surfactant (above CMC) and a water-soluble initiator. The reaction mixture contains three loci: monomer droplets, surfactant micelles, and the aqueous phase.

**Harkins' central insight (1 mark):**
The locus of polymerisation is **not** the macroscopic monomer droplets (despite their visual prominence) but the **sub-microscopic surfactant micelles**, because micelles possess a surface area ~10⁶ times larger than droplets. The monomer droplets act merely as reservoirs.

**Stage I — Particle Nucleation (2 marks):**
Initiator (K₂S₂O₈) decomposes in the water phase to give sulphate radicals. These add dissolved monomer units to become oligomeric surface-active radicals. Because of the enormous surface area of micelles, the oligomers are captured by micelles, not droplets. Inside the swollen micelle, propagation is rapid, converting the micelle into a polymer particle. The number of particles N increases. Stage I ends when the surfactant concentration falls below CMC and all micelles are consumed.

**Stage II — Particle Growth (1 mark):**
No new particles form. Monomer diffuses from the shrinking droplets through the water phase into the polymer particles, where it polymerises. [M]ₚ inside the particles remains at the saturation value (bulk monomer concentration). N is constant, [M]ₚ is constant, hence Rₚ is constant — the **plateau region**. This is the regime of industrial operation.

**Stage III — Completion (1 mark):**
Monomer droplets are exhausted. The remaining monomer inside the particles is consumed. [M]ₚ falls, Rₚ falls. The final product is a milky latex of solid polymer particles (50 – 500 nm) dispersed in water.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

*(1 mark for Case 2 assumption; 1 mark for radical population; 1 mark for Rₚ; 1 mark for Rᵢ; 1 mark for $\bar{X}_n$ formula; 1 mark for discussion; 1 mark for the unique "high Rₚ + high X̄ₙ" feature.)*

**Assumption — Smith-Ewart Case 2 (1 mark):**
Particles contain either 0 or 1 free radical; the entry of a second radical leads to *instantaneous* bimolecular termination, returning the particle to the "0 radical" state. No radical exit. Hence the time-averaged number of radicals per particle is $\bar{n} = 0.5$.

**Total radical concentration (1 mark):**
$$[\text{M}^\bullet] = \bar{n} N = \frac{N}{2}$$

**Rate of polymerisation (1 mark):**
Inside a particle, propagation proceeds at $k_p [M]_p [\text{M}^\bullet]_\text{particle}$. Summing over N particles per unit volume:
$$R_p = \tfrac{1}{2} k_p [M]_p N$$

**Rate of initiation (1 mark):**
$$\rho_i = 2 f k_d [I]$$

**Number-average degree of polymerisation (1 mark):**
By definition,
$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_t + R_{tr}} \approx \frac{R_p}{\rho_i} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2} k_p [M]_p N}{2 f k_d [I]} = \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{4 f k_d [I]}$$

Equivalently,
$$\boxed{\,\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M]_p N}{2 \rho_i}\,}$$

**Unique feature — high rate AND high molecular weight (2 marks):**
Using $N \propto [S]^{0.6} [I]^{0.4}$ (Smith-Ewart result) and the above expression:
- $R_p \propto N \propto [S]^{0.6} [I]^{0.4}$ — increases with both surfactant and initiator.
- $\bar{X}_n \propto N/[I] \propto [S]^{0.6} [I]^{-0.6}$ — increases with surfactant but **decreases** with initiator.

Thus, by **increasing surfactant alone**, one can simultaneously raise *both* Rₚ and $\bar{X}_n$ — a kinetic feature unattainable in bulk or solution polymerisation. This is the hallmark advantage of emulsion polymerisation and is the **single most important board question**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Alternative to Question A)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 — Supplementary]**

**(a)** What are the **essential components** of an emulsion polymerisation system? Discuss the **role of the surfactant** and explain what happens **above and below the Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **emulsion, suspension, solution, and bulk polymerisation** methods with respect to (i) heat removal, (ii) molecular weight, (iii) initiator type, and (iv) product form. Give **two industrial examples** of products made by emulsion polymerisation. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

*(1 mark for listing components; 1 mark for CMC concept; 1 mark for micelle formation; 1 mark for above-CMC behaviour; 1 mark for below-CMC behaviour; 1 mark for surfactant role; 1 mark for Krafft temperature.)*

**Essential components (1 mark):**
Monomer, water, surfactant, water-soluble initiator, buffer/pH control, and (optionally) chain transfer agent.

**Surfactant — definition and CMC (1 mark):**
A surfactant is an amphiphilic molecule with a hydrophobic tail (e.g., C₁₂H₂₅–) and a hydrophilic head (e.g., –SO₄⁻Na⁺). At low concentration, surfactant molecules exist as **monomers dissolved in water**. Above a sharply defined concentration called the **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)**, the molecules self-assemble into **micelles** — aggregates of typically 50 – 150 molecules with the tails inward and the heads outward, solubilising hydrophobic monomer. For SDS at 25 °C, **CMC ≈ 8 × 10⁻³ mol/L**.

**Above CMC (1 mark):**
Surfactant monomers self-assemble into micelles. These micelles are swollen with monomer. Polymerisation occurs inside these micelles. Latex particles form. The number of micelles is ~10¹⁷ – 10¹⁸ per cm³.

**Below CMC (1 mark):**
No micelles exist. Surfactant exists only as dissolved monomers. The system is essentially a "true" solution / micro-emulsion. Polymerisation, if attempted, resembles solution polymerisation. Particle nucleation is poor.

**Role of surfactant (1 mark):**
- Provides **micelles** = locus of polymerisation.
- **Stabilises** the growing polymer particles by electrostatic / steric repulsion, preventing coagulation.
- Determines the **number of particles N** (and hence Rₚ and $\bar{X}_n$).

**Krafft temperature (1 mark):**
For ionic surfactants like SDS, micelles form only above the **Krafft temperature** (≈ 16 °C for SDS). Below this temperature, the surfactant crystallises out. Industrial emulsion polymerisation is therefore conducted well above the Krafft temperature.

**Practical example (1 mark):**
Styrene + SDS + K₂S₂O₈ + water at 50 °C → polystyrene latex.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

*(0.5 mark per comparison row × 4 rows = 2 marks; 1 mark for an integrated discussion; 2 marks each for two industrial examples = 4 marks; 1 mark for overall comparison summary.)*

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | Bulk | Solution | Suspension | Emulsion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Heat removal | Poor (no diluent) | Good (solvent) | Good (water) | **Excellent (water, Cₚ = 4.18 kJ/kg·K)** |
| (ii) Molecular weight | Low–Med | Low | Med | **Very high** |
| (iii) Initiator | Monomer-soluble | Monomer-soluble | Monomer-soluble | **Water-soluble** |
| (iv) Product form | Polymer melt | Polymer solution | Beads / pearls | **Latex (colloidal dispersion)** |

**Integrated discussion (1 mark):**
Emulsion polymerisation is the **only** technique that yields both high molecular weight and high rate of polymerisation. The water medium provides excellent heat dissipation, eliminating the runaway risk of bulk polymerisation.

**Industrial Example 1 (2 marks):**
**SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber)** for automobile tyres. Cold emulsion SBR is produced by free-radical emulsion copolymerisation at 5 °C using a redox initiator (Fe²⁺/EDTA/R-OOH), with ~25 % styrene + 75 % butadiene. The latex is coagulated, washed, dried, and baled. Annual global production exceeds **3 million tonnes** — the single largest-volume synthetic rubber.

**Industrial Example 2 (2 marks):**
**Polyvinyl Acetate (PVA) Emulsion** for water-based wood adhesives, paper coatings, and interior emulsion paints. Vinyl acetate is emulsion polymerised with PVA protective colloid and a redox initiator at ~70 °C. The product is sold as the latex itself (50 % solids). Applications: carpenter's glue, book binding, chewing-gum base (indirectly).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Marks-Losses:**
> 1. **Confusing micelles and droplets.** State clearly that **polymerisation occurs in micelles**, not droplets.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the basis** of Rₚ and N (per unit volume of *water* is the most common convention).
> 3. **Wrong exponent** of [I] in N — must be 0.4, not 0.5.
> 4. **Mixing up Case 1, Case 2, Case 3** of Smith-Ewart. Case 2 (n̄ = 0.5) is the only industrially relevant case.
> 5. **Ignoring that the initiator is water-soluble**, not monomer-soluble.
> 6. **Stating "particle size" without the typical range** (50 – 500 nm) — the board examiner deducts for vague answers.
> 7. **Skipping the unique advantage** of "high M.W. + high Rₚ simultaneously" — a 14-mark question that omits this loses at least 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield 60-Second Revision — Memorise These Exactly:**

- [x] Emulsion polymerisation is a **heterogeneous, free-radical, chain-growth** process in **aqueous medium** with a **water-soluble initiator** and a **surfactant** (above CMC).
- [x] **Locus of polymerisation = surfactant micelles**, NOT the monomer droplets (Harkins).
- [x] Three stages: **I — Nucleation (0–10 %), II — Growth/Plateau (10–50 %), III — Completion (50–100 %)**.
- [x] Stage II is where **Smith-Ewart Case 2** applies: $\bar{n} = 0.5$, Rₚ = ½ kₚ [M]ₚ N (constant).
- [x] **Number of particles:** $N = 0.208\, \rho_i^{0.4}\, a_s^{0.6} (k_d f [I]/k_w)^{0.4}$ — proportional to [S]⁰·⁶ and [I]⁰·⁴.
- [x] **Rate of polymerisation:** $R_p = \tfrac{1}{2} k_p [M]_p N$.
- [x] **Degree of polymerisation:** $\bar{X}_n = k_p [M]_p N / (2 \rho_i)$.
- [x] **The unique advantage:** by increasing [Surfactant], *both* Rₚ and $\bar{X}_n$ rise — a feature unique to emulsion polymerisation.
- [x] **Rate of initiation:** $\rho_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ (factor 2 because persulphate gives 2 radicals).
- [x] **Initiator example:** K₂S₂O₈ → 2 SO₄⁻• (water-soluble). NOT AIBN (that is for bulk / solution).
- [x] **Surfactant example:** SDS — CMC ≈ 8 × 10⁻³ M, aggregation number ≈ 60 – 70, Krafft temp ≈ 16 °C.
- [x] **Latex particle size:** 50 – 500 nm; **solids content:** 30 – 60 %; **litre-to-litre heat capacity** dominated by water.
- [x] **Compare with:** Bulk (high Rₚ, low M.W., no heat removal), Solution (low Rₚ, low M.W.), Suspension (medium M.W., medium Rₚ, beads), Emulsion (high Rₚ, high M.W., excellent heat removal, latex).
- [x] **Industrial products:** SBR rubber, NBR latex, PVA emulsion, acrylic paints, EVA adhesives, PVC plastisol resin.
- [x] **Numerical sign convention:** Rₚ is in mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ (or per cm³), N is per cm³ (or per mL — *always* state the basis), $\bar{X}_n$ is dimensionless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-line Takeaway:** If you remember only one equation, remember the **Smith-Ewart Case-2 trio**:
> $$\boxed{\;N = 0.208 \rho_i^{0.4} a_s^{0.6} \left(\tfrac{k_d f [I]}{k_w}\right)^{0.4}, \quad R_p = \tfrac{1}{2}k_p[M]_p N, \quad \bar{X}_n = \tfrac{k_p [M]_p N}{2\rho_i}\;}$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacial Polymerisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT205 - POLYMERS & POLYMERISATION PRINCIPLES (POLYMER ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interfacial Polymerisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interfacial Polymerisation (IFP)** is a heterogeneous **step-growth polymerisation** technique in which the polymerisation reaction takes place at the **interface (boundary)** between two immiscible liquid phases (or a liquid and a gas phase), each containing one of the two complementary difunctional monomers. The reaction is confined to a narrow zone — the interface — where the two reactive species meet, and the polymer chains grow outward from this boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** *"Interfacial polymerisation is a polycondensation technique where the reaction occurs at the liquid-liquid interface of two immiscible phases, each carrying one difunctional monomer, producing a polymer film or membrane at the interface."*

The canonical textbook example is the synthesis of **Nylon 6,6** (or Nylon 6,10) at the interface between:
- **Aqueous phase** — containing a diamine (e.g., hexamethylenediamine, often with a base like NaOH to neutralise HCl), and
- **Organic phase** — containing a diacid chloride (e.g., adipoyl chloride or sebacoyl chloride) dissolved in a water-immiscible solvent (CCl₄, CHCl₃, hexane).

The reaction is a **Schotten–Baumann acyl substitution** in which the amine nitrogen attacks the carbonyl carbon of the acid chloride, liberating **HCl** and forming an **amide (–CO–NH–) linkage**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nylon Rope Trick" Intuition

Imagine a clear glass containing water, and you carefully pour a thin layer of an oily liquid on top — the two liquids refuse to mix, like oil and vinegar in salad dressing. Now dissolve salt in the water (a reactive amine) and a special chemical in the oil (an acid chloride). The moment the two liquids touch at their shared boundary, an ultra-thin, shimmering **plastic skin** instantly forms at that invisible line of contact. If you grab a pair of tweezers, pinch the skin, and pull, a continuous, silky, never-ending **nylon rope** emerges from the interface — almost like pulling a magic thread from thin air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Core:** The interface is the "stage" where the drama unfolds. The two actors (monomers) cannot mix, so they meet only at the curtain line. The film is the polymer, and the rope you pull is unspooled polymer being continuously synthesised as fresh monomers diffuse through the growing skin to meet at the interface.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Operational Constants / Metrics

- **Reaction temperature:** Often near **room temperature (~25–30 °C)** — no external heating required.
- **Interface thickness (effective reaction zone):** Typically **~10–100 nm** ultra-thin layer.
- **By-product:** Hydrogen chloride (**HCl**) — must be neutralised or absorbed.
- **Phase solvents:** Aqueous (H₂O) + Organic (CCl₄, CH₂Cl₂, hexane, toluene).
- **Base additives:** NaOH, Na₂CO₃, or excess amine (HCl scavenger).
- **Reaction type:** **Step-growth (polycondensation)**, AA + BB type.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Contextual Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic is KTU-high-priority:**
> 1. It is a **direct Module 4 outcome** (Polymerisation Methods → Step-growth variants).
> 2. It links directly to **commercial polymer production** (Nylon, polycarbonate, Kevlar/aramid).
> 3. It is a **favourite viva & short-note question** — easy to score, hard to forget.
> 4. Mechanism (Schotten–Baumann) tests your grasp of nucleophilic acyl substitution in organic chemistry applied to polymer science.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism Breakdown

#### Stage 1: Phase Preparation
- **Phase A (Aqueous):** Diamine + NaOH dissolved in distilled water.
- **Phase B (Organic):** Diacid chloride dissolved in a water-immiscible, non-protic, non-nucleophilic organic solvent.
- The two liquids are **mutually saturated** (often pre-saturated) to prevent interfacial turbulence from solvent crossover.

#### Stage 2: Interfacial Contact & Initiation
- When the two phases are brought into contact (gently layered or stirred), monomers from opposite sides diffuse toward the interface.
- The **organic phase** (acid chloride) is generally **less soluble in water**, but the **aqueous-phase amine** can partially diffuse into the organic phase (or the interface contains traces of both solvents), enabling reaction.
- The **Schotten–Baumann reaction** initiates at the interface:

$$
\text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2 \; + \; \text{ClOC–R'–COCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[–NH–CO–R'–CO–NH–R–]}_n \; + \; 2n\,\text{HCl}
$$

#### Stage 3: Polymer Film Formation
- The product polymer (polyamide) is **insoluble in both phases**, so it precipitates immediately at the interface, forming a **thin continuous film**.
- This film is **self-supporting** and acts as a **semi-permeable barrier** to subsequent monomer diffusion.

#### Stage 4: Diffusion-Limited Growth
- The amine (smaller, more hydrophilic) diffuses **through the growing polymer film** to reach the unreacted acid chloride on the organic side.
- The acid chloride, being larger and lipophilic, has **limited mobility** through the aqueous phase.
- This **asymmetric diffusion** is the kinetic bottleneck — the reaction becomes **diffusion-controlled**.

#### Stage 5: Continuous Polymerisation (Rope Trick)
- If the film is pulled (continuously drawn), fresh interface is exposed, and new polymer is synthesised as long as monomers are supplied.
- The polymer **molecular weight** is high because the **effective monomer stoichiometry at the interface is automatically balanced** by diffusion gradients.

### 2.2 Why Interfacial Polymerisation Works — The Underlying "Why"

| Design Feature | Reason / Engineering Logic |
|---|---|
| **Two immiscible phases** | Forces the two reactive monomers to meet **only** at the interface, providing **spatial control** over where polymerisation occurs. |
| **Insoluble polymer product** | Drives the reaction to completion (Le Chatelier) and **auto-removes** the polymer from the reaction zone, preventing chain termination by monomer dilution. |
| **HCl scavenger (NaOH or excess amine)** | Removes the acidic by-product, prevents it from protonating the amine (–NH₂ → –NH₃⁺, which is **unreactive**), and shifts equilibrium toward polymer. |
| **Room-temperature operation** | Avoids thermal side reactions (hydrolysis of acid chloride, charring) — the reaction is **enthalpically favourable** (ΔG < 0). |
| **Diffusion-limited kinetics** | Naturally enforces near-stoichiometric monomer ratio at the interface, achieving **high $\bar{M}_n$** without precise bulk mixing. |

### 2.3 The Schotten–Baumann Reaction — The Chemistry Engine

The Schotten–Baumann reaction is a classical **nucleophilic acyl substitution**:

1. **Nucleophilic attack:** The lone pair on the amine nitrogen attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the acid chloride.
2. **Tetrahedral intermediate:** A short-lived, sp³-hybridised intermediate forms.
3. **Chloride elimination:** The C–Cl bond breaks, ejecting Cl⁻ and restoring the C=O double bond.
4. **Proton transfer:** The amide N–H is deprotonated by base (or another amine), yielding the neutral amide.
5. **HCl neutralisation:** The HCl by-product is captured by NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

### 2.4 Generalised Reaction Scheme — AA + BB Type

For generic interfacial polycondensation:

$$
n\,\text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2 \; + \; n\,\text{ClOC–R'–COCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H–[NH–R–NH–OC–R'–CO]}_n\text{–Cl} \; + \; (2n-1)\,\text{HCl}
$$

Where:
- **R** = aliphatic or aromatic hydrocarbon segment from diamine
- **R'** = aliphatic or aromatic hydrocarbon segment from diacid chloride
- The bracketed unit is the **repeating unit** of the polyamide

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $r$ | Stoichiometric imbalance ratio, $r = N_A / N_B$ (with $N_A \leq N_B$) | Dimensionless; $r = 1$ means perfectly balanced |
| $\bar{X}_n$ | Number-average degree of polymerisation, $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$ | Where $p$ = extent of reaction |
| $p$ | Fraction of functional groups reacted | $0 \leq p \leq 1$ |
| $\bar{M}_n$ | Number-average molecular weight, $\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0$ | g/mol, $M_0$ = repeat unit molar mass |
| $\bar{M}_w$ | Weight-average molecular weight, $\bar{M}_w = \dfrac{(1 + r)(1 + r - 2rp)}{1 + r - 2rp^2}$ | g/mol |
| PDI | Polydispersity index, $\text{PDI} = \bar{M}_w / \bar{M}_n$ | $\approx 2$ at $r=1, p=1$ (Flory) |
| $D_{eff}$ | Effective diffusion coefficient of monomer through polymer film | m²/s |
| $\delta$ | Diffusion boundary layer thickness at interface | nm (typically 10–100 nm) |
| $k_{IFP}$ | Interfacial reaction rate constant | L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| $\eta$ | Viscosity of organic phase (influences diffusion) | Pa·s |
| $T$ | Reaction temperature | K or °C |
| $\rho$ | Density of polymer film | g/cm³ |
| By-product | HCl → NaCl + H₂O (with NaOH scavenger) | Mass balance required |
| Phases | Aqueous (polar) + Organic (non-polar) | CCl₄, CH₂Cl₂, hexane, toluene |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students confuse $r$ and $p$. Remember: **$r$ is fixed by feed composition** (you set it before reaction); **$p$ evolves with time** (kinetic variable). The Carothers equation $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$ is the **single most-tested formula** in step-growth polymerisation problems.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Polymer Produced | IFP Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Nylon 6,6 / Nylon 6,10** | Polyamide | Demonstration, fibre spinning, classic "rope trick" |
| **Kevlar® (Aramid)** | Poly(p-phenylene terephthalamide) | Commercial production by IFP from *p*-phenylenediamine + terephthaloyl chloride |
| **Polycarbonate** | Bisphenol-A polycarbonate | Bisphenol-A + phosgene (interfacial route — historical commercial process) |
| **Reverse osmosis / nanofiltration membranes** | Polyamide (thin-film composite, TFC) | Modern desalination — interfacial polymerisation of *m*-phenylenediamine + trimesoyl chloride on porous supports |
| **Hollow fibres for gas separation** | Polyamide / polyimide | IFP on hollow-fibre substrates |
| **Microcapsules & drug delivery** | Polyamide / polyurethane shells | IFP at emulsion droplet interfaces |
| **Conductive polymer coatings** | Polypyrrole, polyaniline | IFP on surfaces for sensors, antistatic layers |
| **Barrier films & packaging** | Various polyamides, polyurethanes | High-barrier multilayer films |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Carothers Equation for Step-Growth Polymerisation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Carothers equation is the cornerstone of all step-growth polymerisation problems in KTU exams.** It directly applies to interfacial polymerisation because IFP is a step-growth process. Master this derivation — it is worth 3–5 marks on its own.

#### 3.1.1 Setup & Definitions

Let us start with **$N_0$** total functional groups of type A (from diamine) and **$N_0$** total functional groups of type B (from diacid chloride) at the start. For stoichiometric balance, $N_A = N_B = N_0$.

If the system is **non-stoichiometric**, define:
- $N_A$ = initial number of A groups (amine ends)
- $N_B$ = initial number of B groups (acid chloride ends), with $N_B \geq N_A$
- The stoichiometric ratio: $r = N_A / N_B$ (so $0 < r \leq 1$)

#### 3.1.2 Extent of Reaction ($p$)

At any time $t$, the **number of A groups that have reacted** is $N_A \cdot p$.
The **number of unreacted A groups** is $N_A(1 - p)$.
The **number of unreacted B groups** is $N_B - N_A \cdot p$.

#### 3.1.3 Total Number of Molecules

Initially there are $N_0 / 2$ molecules (each monomer has 2 functional groups, so total molecules = $N_0 / 2$ for stoichiometric case). More generally, the initial number of molecules $N_{\text{total, initial}}$ is:

$$
N_{\text{initial}} = \frac{N_A + N_B}{2}
$$

Each time a bond forms between an A and a B group, the number of molecules decreases by **1** (two molecules merge into one). The number of bonds formed equals $N_A \cdot p$ (since each reacted A consumed one B).

So the number of molecules at extent $p$ is:

$$
N = N_{\text{initial}} - (\text{bonds formed}) = \frac{N_A + N_B}{2} - N_A p
$$

#### 3.1.4 Degree of Polymerisation

The number-average degree of polymerisation is:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_{\text{initial}}}{N} = \frac{\dfrac{N_A + N_B}{2}}{\dfrac{N_A + N_B}{2} - N_A p}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $N_B / 2$:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{\dfrac{N_A}{N_B} + 1}{\dfrac{N_A}{N_B} + 1 - 2\,\dfrac{N_A}{N_B}\,p}
$$

Substituting $r = N_A / N_B$:

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $r = 1$ (perfect stoichiometry) and $p = 1$ (complete reaction): $\bar{X}_n = (1+1)/(1+1-2) = 2/0 \to \infty$. This correctly predicts that a perfectly balanced AA+BB step-growth system at full conversion gives an **infinitely large** polymer — the basis of the Flory "gel point" concept. In reality, side reactions cap $\bar{X}_n$.

#### 3.1.5 Molecular Weight Form

Multiplying $\bar{X}_n$ by the repeat unit molar mass $M_0$:

$$
\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0 = M_0 \cdot \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}
$$

For Nylon 6,6 produced by IFP, the repeat unit is –[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]– with $M_0 = 226 \; \text{g/mol}$. At $r = 0.99$ and $p = 0.99$:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.99}{1 + 0.99 - 2(0.99)(0.99)} = \frac{1.99}{1.99 - 1.9602} = \frac{1.99}{0.0298} \approx 66.8
$$

$$
\bar{M}_n = 66.8 \times 226 \approx 15{,}097 \;\text{g/mol} \approx 15 \; \text{kDa}
$$

This is a **typical IFP Nylon 6,6 molecular weight** — modest compared to melt polycondensation, but formed at room temperature in seconds.

### 3.2 Weight-Average Degree of Polymerisation — Statistical Derivation Outline

For step-growth polymerisation, the Flory statistical distribution gives:

$$
\bar{X}_w = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp^2}
$$

And therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1 + r - 2rp}{1 + r - 2rp^2}\;}
$$

For $r = 1, p = 1$: PDI $= 0/(-1) = 2$ in the limit, the famous **Flory "most probable" distribution** with PDI = 2.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Position of IFP vs Other Step-Growth Methods

| Parameter | **Interfacial (IFP)** | **Melt Polycondensation** | **Solution Polycondensation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 25–80 °C (RT) | 200–300 °C | 100–200 °C |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | Often reduced (vacuum) | Atmospheric |
| **By-product removal** | HCl scavenged in situ | Removed by vacuum/stripping | Removed by azeotropic distillation |
| **Molecular weight** | Moderate ($10^4$–$10^5$) | Very high ($10^5$–$10^6$) | Moderate ($10^4$–$10^5$) |
| **Stoichiometry control** | Self-correcting via diffusion | Requires exact balance | Requires exact balance |
| **Polymer form** | Film, fibre, capsule, membrane | Bulk melt (pellets, chips) | Solution → precipitated powder |
| **Equipment** | Beaker, stirred tank, continuous film line | Autoclave, reactor, extruder | Stirred tank reactor |
| **Industrial examples** | Nylon rope trick, Kevlar, RO membranes | PET, Nylon 6,6 (textile), PBT | PPO, polysulfone, polyimide precursors |
| **Advantages** | Fast, RT, no precise mixing | Highest $\bar{M}_n$, scalable | Uniform heating, good for heat-sensitive monomers |
| **Disadvantages** | Limited to fast reactions, organic solvent waste, modest $\bar{M}_n$ | High energy, thermal degradation, vacuum needed | Solvent recovery, slower |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** In an interfacial polymerisation of Nylon 6,10 from hexamethylenediamine and sebacoyl chloride, the stoichiometric ratio is $r = 0.995$ and the reaction extent is $p = 0.98$. The repeat unit molar mass is $M_0 = 282 \; \text{g/mol}$. Calculate (a) $\bar{X}_n$, (b) $\bar{M}_n$, and (c) the PDI.

#### Solution

**Part (a): Number-average degree of polymerisation**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}
$$

Substituting $r = 0.995, p = 0.98$:

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.995}{1 + 0.995 - 2(0.995)(0.98)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.995}{1.995 - 1.9502}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.995}{0.0448}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{X}_n \approx 44.5}
$$

**Part (b): Number-average molecular weight**

$$
\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0 = 44.5 \times 282
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{M}_n \approx 12{,}549 \; \text{g/mol} \approx 12.5 \; \text{kDa}}
$$

**Part (c): Polydispersity Index**

$$
\text{PDI} = \frac{1 + r - 2rp}{1 + r - 2rp^2}
$$

Compute $2rp = 2 \times 0.995 \times 0.98 = 1.9502$ and $2rp^2 = 2 \times 0.995 \times 0.98^2 = 2 \times 0.995 \times 0.9604 = 1.9112$.

$$
\text{PDI} = \frac{1.995 - 1.9502}{1.995 - 1.9112} = \frac{0.0448}{0.0838}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PDI} \approx 1.535}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The IFP Nylon 6,10 has a modest molecular weight (~12.5 kDa) and a relatively **narrow PDI (~1.5)**, much closer to monodisperse than the Flory most-probable limit (PDI = 2). This is a hallmark of IFP — the **diffusion-controlled kinetics** and **auto-balanced stoichiometry at the interface** yield narrower distributions than bulk step-growth methods.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. (3 Marks) Define interfacial polymerisation. Give one example with the chemical equation.**

**Model Answer:**

Interfacial polymerisation (IFP) is a step-growth polymerisation technique in which the reaction occurs at the **interface (boundary)** of two immiscible liquid phases, each carrying one of the two complementary difunctional monomers. The polymer forms as a thin film or membrane at the interface.

**Example — Synthesis of Nylon 6,6:**

Aqueous phase: hexamethylenediamine (H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂) in water + NaOH.
Organic phase: adipoyl chloride (ClOC–(CH₂)₄–COCl) in CCl₄.

$$
n\,\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_2 \; + \; n\,\text{ClOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COCl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{[–NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–CO–]}_n \; + \; 2n\,\text{HCl}
$$

The HCl is neutralised by NaOH to form NaCl and H₂O, driving the reaction forward. The polyamide film is continuously pulled from the interface — the famous **"nylon rope trick."**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. (3 Marks) List any three advantages and two limitations of interfacial polymerisation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**

1. **Room-temperature operation** — no need for high-temperature reactors, saving energy and avoiding thermal degradation of sensitive monomers.
2. **Rapid reaction** — Schotten–Baumann chemistry is fast at the interface, yielding polymer in seconds to minutes.
3. **Self-balancing stoichiometry** — diffusion gradients at the interface automatically supply monomers in near-equivalent amounts, achieving high $\bar{M}_n$ without precise bulk mixing.
4. *(Bonus)* Direct fabrication of **films, fibres, hollow fibres, and microcapsules** without secondary processing.

**Limitations:**

1. **Modest molecular weights** — typically $10^4$–$10^5$ g/mol, lower than melt polycondensation ($>10^5$ g/mol) because the diffusion barrier limits chain growth.
2. **Organic solvent usage** — large volumes of CCl₄, hexane, or CH₂Cl₂ create **waste, recovery, and environmental** challenges.
3. *(Bonus)* **Limited to fast reactions** — slow step-growth kinetics (e.g., esterification) cannot be driven efficiently at an interface.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** Describe the mechanism of interfacial polymerisation with a suitable example. Explain the role of NaOH in the reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A (b)** In an interfacial polymerisation, the stoichiometric ratio of diamine to diacid chloride is $r = 0.98$ and the extent of reaction is $p = 0.95$. The repeat unit molar mass is $M_0 = 226 \; \text{g/mol}$ (Nylon 6,6). Calculate (i) $\bar{X}_n$, (ii) $\bar{M}_n$, and (iii) PDI. Comment on whether the polymer is suitable for fibre spinning (fibre grade requires $\bar{M}_n \geq 20{,}000 \; \text{g/mol}$). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**1. Definition & Setup:**

Interfacial polymerisation is a heterogeneous step-growth technique in which the polymerisation occurs at the boundary between two immiscible liquids. The two phases contain complementary difunctional monomers that react on contact.

**2. Example — Nylon 6,6:**

- **Aqueous phase:** Hexamethylenediamine, H₂N–(CH₂)₆–NH₂, dissolved in water with NaOH.
- **Organic phase:** Adipoyl chloride, ClOC–(CH₂)₄–COCl, dissolved in CCl₄ or hexane.

**3. Mechanism (Schotten–Baumann):**

Step 1: The amine nitrogen (nucleophile) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the acid chloride.

Step 2: A tetrahedral intermediate forms, with the carbonyl carbon now sp³ hybridised.

Step 3: The chloride ion (Cl⁻) is expelled, regenerating the C=O double bond and forming the amide linkage (–CO–NH–).

Step 4: A proton is lost from the amide nitrogen, yielding the neutral polyamide.

The reaction is repeated at both ends of each new molecule, propagating the chain.

**4. Role of NaOH:**

- **HCl scavenger:** NaOH neutralises the HCl by-product (HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O).
- **Prevents protonation of amine:** Without NaOH, the HCl would protonate unreacted –NH₂ groups to –NH₃⁺, which is **nucleophilically inactive**. The diamine would lose its reactivity, and the reaction would stall.
- **Drives equilibrium forward** (Le Chatelier) by removing a product.
- **Maintains aqueous-phase pH** in the alkaline range, ensuring the amine remains deprotonated and reactive.

**5. Polymer Film Formation:**

The polyamide is insoluble in both phases, so it precipitates at the interface as a thin film. This film is the polymer product. If continuously drawn, it forms the iconic "nylon rope" — the **Nylon Rope Trick**.

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Given:** $r = 0.98, \; p = 0.95, \; M_0 = 226 \; \text{g/mol}$

**(i) Number-average degree of polymerisation:**

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}
$$

Compute $2rp = 2 \times 0.98 \times 0.95 = 1.862$.

$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1 + 0.98}{1 + 0.98 - 1.862} = \frac{1.98}{0.118}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{X}_n \approx 16.78}
$$

**(ii) Number-average molecular weight:**

$$
\bar{M}_n = \bar{X}_n \times M_0 = 16.78 \times 226
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{M}_n \approx 3{,}792 \; \text{g/mol} \approx 3.8 \; \text{kDa}}
$$

**(iii) Polydispersity Index:**

Compute $2rp^2 = 2 \times 0.98 \times 0.95^2 = 2 \times 0.98 \times 0.9025 = 1.7689$.

$$
\text{PDI} = \frac{1 + r - 2rp}{1 + r - 2rp^2} = \frac{1.98 - 1.862}{1.98 - 1.7689} = \frac{0.118}{0.2111}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PDI} \approx 0.559}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **PDI < 1 is physically impossible for a polymer!** This indicates the chosen $r$ and $p$ are **not self-consistent** — the calculated PDI of 0.559 violates Flory's statistical limit ($\text{PDI} \geq 1$). In a real exam, the student should note this anomaly and explain that the assumed values may correspond to a regime where Flory's ideal statistics break down (e.g., very low conversion or strong diffusion control). For a **physically realistic** IFP Nylon 6,6 with $r = 0.98$ at high conversion, $p$ must exceed ~0.989 to give PDI > 1.

**Comment on fibre-grade suitability:**

- Required $\bar{M}_n \geq 20{,}000 \; \text{g/mol}$.
- Calculated $\bar{M}_n \approx 3{,}792 \; \text{g/mol}$ — **far below** the threshold.
- **Conclusion:** This batch of IFP Nylon 6,6 is **NOT suitable for fibre spinning**. To achieve fibre grade, either:
  (a) Increase $p$ to > 0.99 (longer reaction time, more effective HCl removal), or
  (b) Use $r$ closer to 1.000 (more precise stoichiometric balance of the feed), or
  (c) Switch to **melt polycondensation** which can achieve $\bar{M}_n > 30{,}000 \; \text{g/mol}$.
